trgt: XHFO-FM is a radio station on 92.1 MHz in Mexico City. The station is owned by Grupo Siete and carries the Radio Disney pop format.
mgt: XHFO-FM (92.1 MHz) is a radio station in Mexico City owned by Grupo Siete Comunicación. It airs a contemporary hit radio format under the Radio Disney brand. Established in 1979, the station has undergone multiple format changes, including grupera, classic hits, and pop, before adopting its current format in 2020.

page_title: Eric Sharp, Baron Sharp of Grimsdyke
trgt: Eric Sharp, Baron Sharp of Grimsdyke, CBE (17 August 1916 – 2 May 1994) was a British businessman and Member of the House of Lords.
mgt: Eric Sharp, Baron Sharp of Grimsdyke (17 August 1916 – 2 May 1994), was a British businessman and life peer. He held prominent roles in industry, including chairman and chief executive of Cable & Wireless plc, and served in various capacities at Monsanto and ICI Fibres. Sharp began his career in government, contributing to energy policy through the Ministry of Power and the Organisation for European Economic Co-operation. He was appointed a Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE) in 1980, knighted in 1984, and created a life peer in 1989. Married to Marion Freedman, he had three children, including Richard Sharp and Victoria Sharp, the President of the King’s Bench Division.

trgt: Thomas Grantham (1634–1692) was an English General Baptist minister, and theologian. He had access to Charles II of England, and made petitions on behalf of Baptist beliefs.
mgt: Thomas Grantham (1634–1692) was an English General Baptist minister, theologian, and advocate for religious liberty. Born in Lincolnshire, he became a prominent leader among the General Baptists, known for his Arminian theology and efforts to establish congregations across England. Grantham frequently faced persecution, including multiple imprisonments, for his nonconformist beliefs. He served as a "messenger," an office dedicated to evangelism and church oversight, and engaged in theological debates with Anglicans, Presbyterians, Quakers, and Roman Catholics. Grantham was a prolific writer, defending Baptist doctrines and religious freedom, and his works were widely read in the 17th century.

trgt: 56 Field Regiment (Jitra) is part of the Regiment of Artillery of the Indian Army.
mgt: The **56 Field Regiment** is an artillery unit of the Indian Army, raised in 1964.

trgt: Newry railway station (Lang-ga) serves Newry and Bessbrook in Northern Ireland. The station is located in the northwest of Newry, County Armagh on the Dublin-Belfast line close to the Craigmore Viaduct.
mgt: Newry railway station, located in Newry, Northern Ireland, is operated by NI Railways and serves as a key stop on the Belfast–Dublin Enterprise service. Originally opened in 1855 as Newry Main Line, it underwent several name changes and closures before reopening in 1984. A modern station building was constructed in 2009 to replace deteriorating facilities. The station offers regular Enterprise services to Belfast and Dublin, as well as additional peak-time services operated by Iarnród Éireann.

trgt: Fatma Nur Serter (born 10 November 1948) is a female Turkish academic and politician.
mgt: Fatma Nur Serter (born November 10, 1948) is a Turkish academic, politician, and writer.

trgt: The Forging of a Rebel is an autobiographical trilogy of books written by the Spanish author, broadcaster and journalist Arturo Barea. They were first published during the author's exile in the United Kingdom after the Spanish Civil War.
mgt: *The Forging of a Rebel* is a semi-autobiographical trilogy by Spanish writer Arturo Barea, originally published in English between 1941 and 1946. The work, comprising *The Forge*, *The Track*, and *The Clash*, chronicles Barea's life from his childhood in Madrid to his experiences in the Rif War and the Spanish Civil War, culminating in his exile to the United Kingdom. The trilogy explores themes of class struggle, political upheaval, and personal transformation, employing a realist style to depict early 20th-century Spanish society. Praised by critics such as George Orwell and Gabriel García Márquez, it was later translated into Spanish and adapted into a 1990 television miniseries by RTVE.

trgt: Ben Christo (born 22 March 1980) is an English rock musician, guitarist, songwriter, vocalist, lyricist and session musician, he is best known for his tenures with The Sisters of Mercy, Night By Night and Diamond Black.
mgt: Ben Christo (born March 22, 1980) is an English musician, songwriter, and guitarist, best known as a member of the gothic rock band The Sisters of Mercy since 2006. He has also founded and performed with bands such as Night by Night and Diamond Black, achieving critical acclaim for his songwriting and performances. Christo has collaborated with numerous artists across genres, contributed to chart-topping albums, and toured extensively worldwide. In addition to his band work, he is a session musician, composer, and musical director, with credits in film, television, and live performances.

trgt: Ruby is a color that is a representation of the color of the cut and polished ruby gemstone and is a shade of red or pink.
mgt: Ruby is a vivid shade of red, first recorded as a color name in English in 1572. Variations include antique ruby, deep ruby, and ruby red, with uses in systems like Crayola and RAL.

trgt: Uptown New York is a 1932 American Pre-Code film directed by Victor Schertzinger and starring Jack Oakie, Shirley Grey, and Leon Ames. It is based on the story by Vina Delmar.
mgt: *Uptown New York* is a 1932 American drama film directed by Victor Schertzinger and written by Viña Delmar and Warren Duff. Starring Jack Oakie, Shirley Grey, and Leon Ames, the film follows a love triangle complicated by societal expectations and personal sacrifices. Released by Sono Art-World Wide Pictures, it received mixed reviews, with praise for its performances and local color but criticism for its narrative motivations.

page_title: Cardinal Newman High School (Columbia, South Carolina)
trgt: Cardinal Newman High School is a diocesan, Roman Catholic middle and high school outside the city limits of Columbia, South Carolina. It is part of the Roman Catholic Diocese of Charleston.
mgt: Cardinal Newman High School is a Catholic school in Columbia, South Carolina, serving grades 7–12. Established in 1858 by the Ursuline Sisters, it moved to a 50-acre campus in 2016.

trgt: Southfield Football Club, occasionally referred to as Birmingham Southfield, was an English football club based in Birmingham, then in Warwickshire.
mgt: Southfield Football Club was an amateur football team based in Birmingham, England, active from around 1881 to 1893. Primarily a working-class club, it played at Bournbrook and occasionally at Calthorpe Park. Southfield competed in local competitions, including the Birmingham Senior Cup, with limited success, and briefly entered the FA Cup in 1887, suffering a heavy defeat to Burton Swifts. The club dissolved after retreating to junior football, with its last recorded match in 1892. A notable incident in its history was the death of player Charles Bache in 1885 due to a football-related injury.

trgt: Simon Brooke Mackay, Baron Tanlaw (born 30 March 1934) is a former member of the House of Lords.
mgt: Simon Mackay, Baron Tanlaw (born 30 March 1934), is a British businessman, politician, and horologist. A life peer since 1971, he served in the House of Lords until 2017, focusing on energy conservation and environmental issues. Known for advocating lighter evenings through time zone changes, he introduced the Lighter Evenings Bill in 2005. Tanlaw has significant business interests, particularly in the Far East, and served as Chancellor of the University of Buckingham from 2010 to 2013.

trgt: Diana Nemiroff (born May 1, 1946) is a Canadian curator and art historian in the field of contemporary art.
mgt: Diana Nemiroff is a Canadian curator and writer known for groundbreaking exhibitions and receiving the 2012 Governor General's Award.

trgt: The Pearl Carpet of Baroda was a carpet commissioned by the Maharaja of Baroda.
mgt: The Pearl Carpet of Baroda is a 19th-century treasure adorned with pearls, diamonds, rubies, and emeralds, originally commissioned by Maharaja Khande Rao Gaekwad of Baroda in 1865. Intended as a gift for the Prophet's Tomb in Medina, the carpet remained with the Gaekwad family after the maharaja's death in 1870. It was first publicly displayed at the 1903 Delhi Durbar and later owned by Sita Devi, who took it to Monaco in 1946. The carpet reappeared in 1985 at the Metropolitan Museum in New York and was sold at a Sotheby’s auction in Doha in 2009 for $5.5 million. It is now housed in the National Museum of Qatar. Measuring 264 by 173 centimeters, the carpet is made of deerskin and silk, showcasing intricate craftsmanship with its lavish embellishments.

trgt: Nicholas Wadley (30 April 1935 - 1 November 2017) was a British art critic, art historian, biographer, cartoonist and illustrator.
mgt: Nicholas Wadley (1935–2017) was a British art historian, educator, and illustrator, known for his writings on Stefan and Franciszka Themerson.

trgt: The Belgium–Korea Treaty of 1901 was negotiated between representatives of the Kingdom of Belgium and the Korean Empire.
mgt: The Belgium–Korea Treaty of 1901 established diplomatic relations and trade provisions, mirroring agreements with other Western nations.

trgt: Microsoft DNS is the name given to the implementation of domain name system services provided in Microsoft Windows operating systems.
mgt: Microsoft DNS is a suite of Domain Name System (DNS) services included in Microsoft Windows operating systems. It comprises a DNS client, a Dynamic DNS update client, and a DNS server, with the server software available only on Windows Server editions. The DNS client supports local caching and communication with multiple DNS servers, while the Dynamic DNS client enables automatic registration of machine names and IP addresses. The DNS server, introduced in Windows NT 3.51, supports features like caching, Dynamic DNS updates, and integration with Active Directory. It has evolved to improve compatibility with other DNS implementations and offers management through graphical, command-line, and PowerShell interfaces.

trgt: Ze’ev Raban (22 September 1890 – 19 January 1970), born Wolf Rawicki (Ravitzki), was a leading painter, decorative artist, and industrial designer of the Bezalel school style, and was one of the founders of the Israeli art world.
mgt: Ze'ev Raban (born Wolf Rawicki; 1890–1970) was a Polish-born Israeli artist and designer, renowned as a leading figure of the Bezalel school art style. Influenced by European Art Nouveau and Middle Eastern traditions, Raban's work blended biblical and Zionist themes with eclectic artistic elements. After studying in Europe, he moved to Ottoman Palestine in 1912, joining the Bezalel School of Art as a student and later as a faculty member. Raban's contributions spanned various mediums, including book illustration, graphic design, ceramics, architectural decoration, and Judaica. His works, such as illustrated biblical texts and ceramic murals, remain iconic in Israeli art and culture.

trgt: Konanakunte, formerly a census town, is a suburb of Bangalore in the Indian state of Karnataka.The MP of this area is DK Suresh (brother of DK Shivakumar, former karnataka minister),mla for this area is M.Krishnappa, Corporator is Shashirekha Jayaram.
mgt: Konanakunte is a neighborhood in Bangalore, Karnataka, India, located in the Bommanahalli Zone. Once a secluded village known for farming and underemployment, it has transformed into a semi-urban residential area with booming real estate opportunities. As of the 2001 census, it had a population of 13,262 with a literacy rate of 77%. Despite urbanization and improved accessibility through the Namma Metro project, the area faces challenges such as inadequate civic amenities, including poor infrastructure and limited water supply.

trgt: Disarmament, demobilisation and reintegration (DDR), or disarmament, demobilisation, repatriation, reintegration and resettlement (DDRRR) are strategies used as a component of peace processes, and is generally the strategy employed by all UN Peacekeeping Operations following civil wars.
mgt: Disarmament, demobilization, and reintegration (DDR) is a process aimed at transitioning ex-combatants from armed conflict to civilian life. It involves disarmament (removal of weapons), demobilization (disbanding armed groups), and reintegration (returning combatants to society). Successful DDR requires trust, peace agreements, and addressing underlying grievances, while challenges include societal reintegration and communal tensions.

trgt: Q.S. Serafijn (born February 19, 1960) is a Dutch conceptual artist and author, who is working as sculptor, photographer, and installation artist.
mgt: Q.S. Serafijn (born in Roosendaal en Nispen) is a Dutch artist and educator known for his interactive sculptures and public art projects.

trgt: Songs of the Pixies was composed by Samuel Taylor Coleridge during 1793. The poem describes Coleridge's summer vacation and his childhood home. It also incorporates Coleridge's own view of himself as a young poet.
mgt: "Songs of the Pixies" is a poem by Samuel Taylor Coleridge, written in 1793 during a summer vacation in Devon. Inspired by local folklore and the Pixies' Parlour near Ottery, the poem blends themes of fairies, mythology, and poetic self-reflection. It was first published in Coleridge's 1796 *Poems on Various Subjects* but later dismissed by the poet himself. The work draws on influences from Milton, Shakespeare, and other literary figures, showcasing Coleridge's early imaginative style. While receiving mixed critical responses, it is noted for its inventive imagery and its reflection of Coleridge's evolving poetic identity.

trgt: Legislative elections were held in Taiwan on 19 December 1992.
mgt: The 1992 Taiwanese legislative election saw the Kuomintang (KMT) secure a majority, winning 95 of 161 seats in the Legislative Yuan. Held on December 19, 1992, this was the first full re-election of the Legislative Yuan following constitutional reforms during Taiwan's democratization in the late 1980s. These reforms allowed direct elections in the free area of Taiwan after decades of restricted electoral practices due to the Chinese Civil War and the Temporary Provisions against the Communist Rebellion. Voter turnout was 72.02%.

trgt: Jasmina Obradović (Serbian Cyrillic: Јасмина Обрадовић; born 29 January 1961) is a politician in Serbia. She has served in the National Assembly of Serbia since 2012 as a member of the Serbian Progressive Party.
mgt: Jasmina Obradović is a Serbian economist and politician serving as a member of the National Assembly of Serbia since 2012 as a member of the Serbian Progressive Party. She has held various committee roles, including in defense, cultural affairs, and agriculture, and has been active in parliamentary friendship groups with several countries. Previously, she served in the Novi Sad city assembly from 2012 to 2016.

trgt: Frederick Eugene Crawford (July 27, 1910 – March 5, 1974) was an American football player.
mgt: Fred Crawford (July 27, 1910 – March 5, 1974) was an American football player, coach, and World War II veteran. A standout tackle and end for Duke University, he became the first football player from North Carolina to earn consensus All-America honors in 1933. Known for his exceptional play, he helped lead Duke to a Southern Conference championship and was later inducted into the College Football Hall of Fame in 1973. Crawford briefly played professionally for the Chicago Bears before serving in the United States Air Force during World War II. After the war, he worked for the Florida State Motor Vehicle Department until his death.

page_title: Association football during World War II
trgt: When World War II was declared in 1939, it had a negative effect on association football; competitions were suspended and players signed up to fight, resulting in the deaths of many players.
mgt: Association football during World War II was significantly disrupted, with many domestic leagues and international competitions suspended or altered. In countries like England, Scotland, and Germany, regional leagues and unofficial matches replaced regular tournaments, while players often served in the military, with some careers interrupted or ended. Football was used as a morale booster for soldiers and civilians, though the sport also faced tragedies, including the deaths of Jewish players during the Holocaust and the destruction of clubs. Despite the challenges, some players were decorated for military service, while others collaborated with occupying forces.

trgt: The Democratic Jewish Union (Lang-lt, ZDS) was a political coalition in inter-war Lithuania.
mgt: The Democratic Jewish Union (ZDS) was a political party in Lithuania active during the interwar period, participating in the 1920 and 1926 elections.

trgt: Rod Bradley (born 29 March 1983) is an English player of American football, as well an artist and entrepreneur.
mgt: Roderick Bradley (born March 29, 1983) is a British athlete, American football player, Gladiators TV personality, and sports entrepreneur.

trgt: Middletown Valley, also historically known as Catoctin Valley, is a valley in western Frederick County in the state of Maryland.
mgt: Middletown Valley, located in Frederick County, Maryland, is a 14-mile-long valley bordered by South Mountain, Catoctin Mountain, and the Potomac River.

trgt: KNIN-FM (92.9 MHz), "Wichita Falls' #1 Hit Music Station," is a radio station broadcasting a Top 40 (CHR) format. The station serves the Wichita Falls, Texas area. The station is currently owned by Townsquare Media.
mgt: KNIN-FM (92.9 MHz), branded as "92.9 NIN," is a Top 40 (CHR) radio station serving the Wichita Falls, Texas area. Established in 1975, it is owned by Townsquare Media and operates with 100,000 watts.

trgt: Software was a German electronic duo active from 1984 to 1999, originally composed of Peter Mergener [de] and Michael Weisser [de]. The duo formerly used the name Mergener & Weisser.
mgt: Software was a German electronic music duo active from the 1980s to 1999, known for releasing albums under the IC label. Founders Peter Mergener and Michael Weisser pursued separate careers post-dissolution.

trgt: Iberia Líneas Aéreas de España, S.A. (Iberia Airlines of Spain in English), usually shortened to Iberia, is the largest airline of Spain, based in Madrid.
mgt: Iberia is the flag carrier airline of Spain, founded in 1927. It played a key role in connecting Spain with Europe and Latin America, evolving through nationalization, privatization, and a 2010 merger with British Airways to form the International Airlines Group.

trgt: Two Paddocks is a wine producer based in Central Otago, New Zealand. It is owned and operated by actor Sam Neill.
mgt: Two Paddocks is a boutique winery in Central Otago, New Zealand, founded in 1993 by actor Sam Neill. Specializing in Pinot Noir, the winery operates several vineyards, including the original First Paddock in Gibbston and additional sites in the Alexandra district. Known for its Proprietor's Reserve Pinot Noir and second label Picnic, Two Paddocks also produces Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, and lavender oil.

trgt: Talent is a city in Jackson County, Oregon, United States. The population was 6,282 at the 2020 census.
mgt: Talent, Oregon, is a city in Jackson County, incorporated in 1910, with a population of 6,282 (2020).

trgt: Sheila Elias (born in Chicago) is an American artist. Her works have been featured in exhibitions across North America and at the Liberty show at the Louvre Museum in Paris.
mgt: Sheila Elias is an American artist and art historian known for exploring the intersection of art aesthetics and social consciousness. Her work spans painting, digital media, sculpture, and performance.

trgt: Alan Brian Carter (born 1952, Lincolnshire, England) is Emeritus Professor of Moral Philosophy at the University of Glasgow.
mgt: Alan Brian Carter (born 1952) is a British philosopher specializing in political, moral, and environmental philosophy.

trgt: Thomas Hourston (February 12, 1854 – April 11, 1905) was a politician from Alberta, Canada and a municipal councillor in Edmonton.
mgt: Thomas Hourston (February 12, 1854 – April 11, 1905) was a Scottish-born Canadian merchant, farmer, and alderman on Edmonton's Town Council from 1896 to 1898.

trgt: Dominic Michaelis (1938 – 22 December 2015) was an Anglo-French architect, inventor, and solar energy advocate.
mgt: Dominic Michaelis (1938 – December 22, 2015) was a French architect, inventor, and solar energy pioneer.

trgt: Odysseus is a role-playing game published by Fantasy Games Unlimited in 1980.
mgt: *Odysseus* is a role-playing game set in ancient Greece, blending heroic and classical elements. Designed by Marshall T. Rose and published by Fantasy Games Unlimited in 1980, the game features rules for character creation, combat, naval warfare, and divine intervention. While praised for its design and appeal to historical gamers, it received mixed reviews for its rule clarity and historical accuracy.

trgt: The Roland RE-201, commonly known as the Space Echo, is an audio effects unit that produces delay and reverb effects. It was produced by the Roland Corporation from 1974 to 1990.
mgt: The Roland Space Echo is a series of tape echo and delay effects units produced by Roland Corporation from 1973. Renowned for their warm, analog sound and innovative design, the Space Echo models, including the iconic RE-201, utilized a continuous tape loop system to create echo and reverb effects. Introduced by Roland founder Ikutaro Kakehashi, the series evolved from earlier tape echo designs, offering features like adjustable delay, reverb, and sound-on-sound recording. Later models, such as the RE-301 and RE-501, incorporated additional effects like chorus and improved noise reduction. The Space Echo remains highly regarded for its distinctive sound and influence on music production.

trgt: Hanauer Straße in Munich is an arterial road about one and a half kilometers long extending from south to north in the Moosach district. It was named after the Hesse city of Hanau.
mgt: Hanauer Straße is a street in Munich, Germany, extending from Dachauer Straße near Westfriedhof and crossing Georg-Brauchle-Ring. Historically, it housed the Moosach gasworks and the 1972 Olympic press city. Today, it features cultural monuments, major retail centers like the Olympia-Einkaufszentrum, and public transport connections, including underground stations and tram stops. Future developments include residential neighborhoods and office spaces. Hanauer Straße was the site of the 2016 Munich shooting, commemorated by a memorial honoring the victims.

trgt: Paul, Baron De Keersmaeker (born 1929) is a Belgian businessman, politician and chairman of PA Europe.
mgt: Paul De Keersmaeker is a Belgian lawyer, politician, and businessman who held prominent roles in politics, European affairs, and the brewing industry.

trgt: The National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL), established in 1975, is a "nonpartisan public officials’ association composed of sitting state legislators" from the states, territories and commonwealths of the United States.
mgt: The National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL), founded in 1975, is a non-governmental organization representing U.S. state legislatures. Headquartered in Denver and Washington, D.C., it supports state legislatures through research, policy development, and advocacy, particularly opposing federal preemption of state authority. NCSL operates eight standing committees and task forces addressing diverse legislative issues, organizes annual events like the Legislative Summit, and publishes resources for legislative professionals.

trgt: Ghislaine Dupont (IPA-fr; 13 January 1956 – 2 November 2013) was a French journalist who specialised in African issues.
mgt: Ghislaine Dupont (13 January 1956 – 2 November 2013) was a French journalist for Radio France Internationale, renowned for her investigative reporting on African conflicts. She contributed to the creation of Radio Okapi in the Democratic Republic of Congo and was recognized for her editorial rigor and political analysis. Dupont was tragically killed in Mali in 2013, alongside technician Claude Verlon, after being abducted. Their deaths inspired the establishment of the Ghislaine Dupont and Claude Verlon scholarships and the UN's International Day to End Impunity.

trgt: Komsomolets Ukrainy was the lead ship of Kashin-class destroyer of the Soviet Navy.
mgt: The *Komsomolets Ukrainy* was a Soviet Kashin-class destroyer, active from 1962 to 1991.

trgt: Marley Sianto Sikawa (born c. 1990), is a Kenyan communications and marketing professional, who serves as the tourism marketing coordinator for Narok County, one of the 47 administrative Kenyan counties.
mgt: Sianto Sikawa (born 1990) is a Kenyan tourism coordinator, fashion model, and philanthropist. She serves as the Tourism Marketing Coordinator for Narok County and promotes solar energy access for local women.

trgt: Wigstan (died c.840 AD), also known as Saint Wystan, was the son of Wigmund of Mercia and Ælfflæd, daughter of King Ceolwulf I of Mercia.
mgt: Wigstan (died 839), also known as Wystan or Wistan, was a Mercian prince and Christian martyr. The son of Wigmund and Ælfflæd, he declined the kingship of Mercia to pursue a religious life. He was murdered by Beorhtfrith, reportedly over a familial dispute, and became venerated as a saint. His relics were moved to Evesham Abbey, which became a pilgrimage site until its destruction during the Dissolution of the Monasteries.

trgt: George Petrakos (born c. 1988 in Essex, England) is a rugby union strength and conditioning coach with Glasgow Warriors.
mgt: George Petrakos (born c. 1988) is an English strength and conditioning coach, academic, and researcher with experience in rugby, football, and hockey.

trgt: The Harold Peary Show is a radio situation comedy broadcast in the United States September 17, 1950-June 13, 1951 on CBS. Some sources refer to the program as Honest Harold or The Hal Peary Show.
mgt: *The Harold Peary Show* was an American radio situation comedy that aired on CBS from 1950 to 1951. Starring Harold Peary as "Honest Harold" Hemp, the show struggled to replicate the success of *The Great Gildersleeve*, with which it shared notable similarities. Despite its humor and ensemble cast, the program failed to gain a significant audience and ended after one season.

page_title: Christian Albert, Burgrave and Count of Dohna
trgt: Christian Albert, Burgrave and Count of Dohna (also known as Christoph Albert, 10 December 1621 in Küstrin – 14 December 1677 in Gartz) was a German nobleman from the House of Dohna.
mgt: Christian Albert, Burgrave and Count of Dohna (10 December 1621 – 14 December 1677), was a German nobleman and general in the army of Brandenburg. He served as privy councillor, governor of Küstrin, and stadtholder of Halberstadt. Known for his military career, he participated in campaigns in Pomerania, expanded fortresses in Berlin and Küstrin, and commanded a regiment named after him. He married Countess Sophie Theodore of Brederode-Vianen, with whom he had ten children.

trgt: Sergio Ferrer Marrero (born January 29, 1951) is a former Major League Baseball shortstop.
mgt: Sergio Ferrer (born January 29, 1951) is a former Puerto Rican professional baseball shortstop who played in Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Minnesota Twins and New York Mets from 1974 to 1979.

page_title: Sisters of the Reparation of the Holy Face
trgt: The Pontifical Congregation of the Benedictine Sisters of the Reparation of the Holy Face is a Roman Catholic order whose focus is providing Acts of Reparation to Jesus Christ.
mgt: The Sisters of the Reparation of the Holy Face, a Pontifical Congregation founded in 1950 by Venerable Abbot Hildebrand Gregori, promote devotion to the Holy Face of Jesus in reparation for His Passion.

trgt: Todd Merlin Compton (born 1952) is an American historian in the fields of Mormon history and classics. Compton is a respected authority on the plural wives of the LDS Church founder, Joseph Smith.
mgt: Todd Compton is an American historian, author, and independent researcher specializing in Mormon history and classical studies. A member of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, he is best known for his award-winning book *In Sacred Loneliness: The Plural Wives of Joseph Smith*. Compton has also published extensively on Mormon history, classical mythology, and popular culture, contributing to journals such as *Dialogue: A Journal of Mormon Thought* and *Sunstone Magazine*. His biography of Jacob Hamblin, *A Frontier Life*, received multiple awards, and he has served on editorial boards for prominent Mormon history publications.

trgt: The French Covered Court Championships its original name also known as the French Covered Court Open Championships and the French Indoors was a tennis event held from 1895 through 1971 in Paris, France and Lyon, France.
mgt: The French Covered Court Championships was a defunct indoor tennis tournament held from 1895 to 1971, primarily at the Tennis Club de Paris. Played on wood courts, it hosted 69 editions and was a predecessor to the Paris Masters.

trgt: Lunella undulata, common name the common warrener or the lightning turban, is a species of sea snail, marine gastropod mollusk in the family Turbinidae.
mgt: *Lunella undulata* is a species of marine gastropod mollusk in the family Turbinidae. Endemic to Australia, it features a bright green, umbilicate shell and is found off New South Wales, South Australia, Tasmania, Victoria, and Western Australia. The species is notable for its presence in archaeological shell middens in southeast Australia.

trgt: Frank S Cherry (circa 1875-1963) was the founder and leader of one of the early Hebrew Israelite groups in the United States.
mgt: Frank Cherry (born in the Southern United States) was a religious leader and founder of the Church of the Living God, the Pillar Ground of Truth for All Nations, established in 1886. He preached that African Americans were descendants of the ancient Israelites, espoused Black Nationalism, and supported Marcus Garvey. Cherry also promoted controversial beliefs, including antisemitism, the inherent evil of white people, and the idea that the earth is square. After his death, his son Prince Benjamin F. Cherry succeeded him as the church's leader.

trgt: Jean Garrabé (11 August 1931 – 13 September 2020) was a French psychiatrist.
mgt: Jean Garrabé (11 August 1931 – 13 September 2020) was a French psychiatrist and historian of psychiatry.

trgt: The Villarreal Offensive (Lang-es) was an offensive of the Spanish Civil War which lasted from 30 November to 24 December 1936. Eusko Gudarostea's 4,300 men fought 600 men of the insurgent forces.
mgt: The Villarreal Offensive (November 30 – December 24, 1936) was a failed Republican military operation during the Spanish Civil War aimed at capturing Villarreal near Vitoria in the Basque Country. Despite initial advances, the Basque forces, part of the Republican Army, were repelled by Nationalist troops, suffering heavy casualties. The offensive sought to reduce Nationalist pressure on Madrid but ultimately ended in a Nationalist victory.

trgt: Dar Hammuda Pasha is an old palace in the medina of Tunis. It is considered one of the oldest and biggest palaces of the medina that kept their original architecture.
mgt: Dar Hammuda Pasha is a historic palace in Tunis, located on Sidi Ben Arous Street. Built in 1630 by Hammuda Pasha Bey, it served as a residence for Muradid and Husainid rulers before later being converted into a restaurant.

trgt: FOX Arena (formerly Arena) is an Australian general entertainment cable and satellite channel available on Foxtel, Austar, and Optus Television's subscription platforms.
mgt: Fox Arena, formerly known as Arena, is an Australian television channel owned by Foxtel Networks. Launched on 22 April 1995, it initially featured a mix of programming, including UK serials and cult films, before shifting focus to talk shows and celebrity content. The channel underwent several rebrands, including a partnership with Universal Networks International in 2008, adopting elements of the American Bravo network. In 2020, it was rebranded as Fox Arena with the tagline "Live Out Loud," and it now operates as part of Foxtel's LifeStyle suite.

trgt: The Taylor Titch is a British fixed-wing homebuilt aircraft, developed in the 1960s by J.F. Taylor. As of 2015, examples are still being built and flown.
mgt: The Taylor Titch is a British single-seat, low-wing monoplane designed by John Taylor in the 1960s as a high-performance aircraft for amateur construction. Developed from the Taylor Monoplane, it was created for the 1964 Midget Racer Design Competition, where it placed second. The prototype first flew in 1967, but Taylor tragically died in a crash later that year. Despite this, plans for the Titch were marketed by his family, and as of 2011, 40 examples had been completed and flown. The Titch is aerobatic, capable of cross-country touring, and can be powered by either Continental or Lycoming engines.

trgt: Raquel Torres Cerdán, also Raquel Torres (born 19 November 1948) is a Mexican anthropologist and restauranteur, who has worked to record, preserve and showcase the cuisines of the indigenous peoples of Veracruz, through her restaurants and food writing.
mgt: Raquel Torres Cerdán (born November 19, 1948) is a Mexican chef, restaurateur, and researcher known for preserving and promoting the indigenous cuisine of Veracruz. She operates two restaurants and teaches workshops on traditional Mexican cooking.

trgt:
Leif Carlsen was Danish football forward. During his career he played for Boldklubben Frem, Hvidovre IF, and Landskrona BoIS.

Carlsen was born in 1943 in Denmark, and died in 2018.
mgt: Leif Carlsen (4 December 1943 – 14 September 2018) was a Danish footballer who played as a forward for Boldklubben Frem, Hvidovre IF, and Sweden's Landskrona BoIS.

trgt: Manuel Sager is a Swiss diplomat who served as the Ambassador of Switzerland to the United States from 2010 to 2014. He was appointed to the position in December 2010.
mgt: Manuel Sager (born 1955) is a Swiss diplomat and lawyer who served as the Swiss Ambassador to the United States (2010–2014) and Director of the Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (2014–2020).

trgt: The Fury of the Aquabats! is the second studio album by American rock band the Aquabats, released on October 28, 1997 by Goldenvoice Records and Time Bomb Recordings.
mgt: *The Fury of the Aquabats!* is the second studio album by American band The Aquabats, released on October 28, 1997. Blending ska, punk, and surf rock with comedic and pop culture-inspired themes, the album marked a commercial breakthrough during the 1990s ska revival, peaking at number 172 on the *Billboard* 200. It introduced Jimmy the Robot and featured drummer The Baron von Tito, later known as Travis Barker of Blink-182. The lead single, "Super Rad!", gained airplay and MTV rotation, while the album received positive reviews for its energetic and eclectic style.

trgt: The Mali–Netherlands relations refers to the current diplomatic and military ties between the Republic of Mali and the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
mgt: Mali–Netherlands relations are marked by diplomatic representation, Dutch contributions to U.N. peacekeeping in Mali, and controversies over military safety incidents.

trgt: Civil Hospital, Aizawl is a state-owned hospital in Aizawl, Mizoram, India. Founded as a 'Coolie Dispensary' in Colonial India in 1896, it grew up to the form of a hospital in 1906.
mgt: Civil Hospital, Aizawl, established in 1896, is a 300-bed public hospital located in Aizawl, Mizoram, India. It provides specialized medical services, including surgery, oncology, pediatrics, and radiology, and houses a Regional Cancer Centre.

page_title: Cleveland Board of Education v. LaFleur
trgt: Cleveland Board of Education v. LaFleur, 414 U.S. 632 (1974), found that overly restrictive maternity leave regulations in public schools violate the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment and the Fourteenth Amendment.
mgt: *Cleveland Board of Education v. LaFleur*, 414 U.S. 632 (1974), was a landmark United States Supreme Court case that ruled overly restrictive maternity leave policies in public schools unconstitutional under the Due Process Clauses of the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments. The Court found such policies arbitrary and discriminatory, marking a significant step in protecting the rights of female teachers and advancing workplace equality. This decision also influenced the development of family leave laws, such as the Family and Medical Leave Act of 1993, and addressed longstanding biases against women in the teaching profession.

trgt: Malcolm Neville Lockyer (5 October 1923 – 28 June 1976) was a British film composer and conductor.
mgt: Malcolm Lockyer (1923–1976) was an English composer, conductor, and musician known for his film scores, television themes, and orchestral work.

trgt: John Leslie Sissons (born 30 September 1945) is an English former footballer who played for West Ham United, Sheffield Wednesday, Norwich City and Chelsea.
mgt: John Leslie Sissons (born 30 September 1945) is an English former professional footballer who played as a forward. He began his career at West Ham United, becoming the youngest player to score in an FA Cup Final in 1964. Sissons later played for Sheffield Wednesday, Norwich City, Chelsea, Tampa Bay Rowdies, and Cape Town City, achieving success in both England and abroad.

trgt: The Puzzle Mansion is a museum in Tagaytay, Cavite, Philippines which hosts a collection of jigsaw puzzle which from 2012 to 2017 was recognized to be the biggest in the world according to Guinness.
mgt: The Puzzle Mansion is a museum and bed-and-breakfast in Tagaytay, Philippines, established in 2012. Founded by Georgina Gil-Lacuna, it housed her extensive jigsaw puzzle collection, which once held a Guinness World Record.

trgt: Louis François Auguste Cauchois-Lemaire (August 28, 1789 in Paris – August 9, 1861 in Paris) was a French journalist.
mgt: Louis François Auguste Cauchois-Lemaire (1789–1861) was a French journalist, historian, and political activist known for his liberal and oppositional writings during the Restoration and July Monarchy.

trgt: Thomas Anthony Lawton (born Darwin, 1 November 1962) is a former Australian rugby union player. He played as a hooker.
mgt: Thomas Anthony Lawton (born 27 November 1962) is a former Australian rugby union player who played as a hooker. A member of a prominent rugby family, his grandfather, Tom Lawton Sr., captained the Wallabies in the 1920s. Lawton represented Queensland 42 times and earned 41 caps for Australia, debuting in 1983 and participating in the 1984 Grand Slam tour and the 1987 Rugby World Cup. He concluded his international career in 1992 and later played for Natal in South Africa, winning the Currie Cup. Post-retirement, Lawton coached Souths to two Queensland championships and works in retirement fund management.

trgt: Abderahmane Hachoud (Lang-ar; born July 2, 1988, in El Attaf) is an Algerian professional football player.
mgt: Abderahmane Hachoud (born July 2, 1988) is an Algerian footballer who plays as a defender.

trgt: Gilad Efrat (Hebrew: גלעד אפרת; born 1969) is a leading contemporary Israeli painter and a professor at the Shenkar College of Engineering and Design, Israel.
mgt: Gilad Efrat (born 1969) is an Israeli painter known for his innovative techniques that explore the intersection of abstraction and figuration, often addressing themes of identity, memory, and geopolitical landscapes. A graduate of the Bezalel Academy of Arts and Design and the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Efrat has exhibited internationally and held teaching positions at institutions such as Shenkar College and Rice University. His works, which include landscapes, portraits, and abstract compositions, often employ a unique method of layering and erasing paint to reveal images. Efrat's notable exhibitions include a large-scale solo show at The Israel Museum in 2019.

trgt: HMS Ardent was a Royal Navy 27 knot torpedo boat destroyer ordered from John I Thornycroft & Company under the 1893 – 1894 Naval Estimates. She was the sixth ship to carry this name.
mgt: HMS *Ardent* was a Royal Navy torpedo boat destroyer launched in 1894 and built by Thornycroft in Chiswick. Designed as part of the Ardent-class, she served primarily in the Mediterranean, performing duties such as training stokers and participating in fleet exercises. *Ardent* achieved notable speeds during trials, exceeding 27 knots. After a collision in 1906 and subsequent service, she was placed in reserve in 1911 and sold for breaking later that year.

page_title: Hong Kong Film Award for Best Action Choreography
trgt: The Hong Kong Film Award for Best Action Choreography is an annual Hong Kong industry award presented to a choreographer or a group of choreographers for the best achievement in action choreography.
mgt: The Hong Kong Film Award for Best Action Choreography is presented annually by the Hong Kong Film Awards to honor excellence in action choreography. Established in 1983, it recognizes the integral role of action scenes in Hong Kong cinema.

trgt: Dimitar Dobrev Dobrev was a 20th-Century Bulgarian naval officer and an I-Class Admiral. He was a participant in the Serbo-Bulgarian War, Russo-Japanese War, First Balkan War and the Second Balkan War.
mgt: Dimitar Dobrev (February 12, 1868 – April 11, 1944) was a Bulgarian naval officer and lawyer who served in the Bulgarian Navy, Russian Imperial Navy, and participated in key conflicts, including the Serbo-Bulgarian War, Russo-Japanese War, and Balkan Wars.

trgt: Donkeyboy (occasionally stylised as donkeyboy) is a synthpop band from Drammen, Norway, formed in 2005. The band consists of Cato Sundberg (vocals, rhythm guitar), Kent Sundberg (synth, vocals), Peter Michelsen (guitar, backing vocals) and Thomas Drabløs (drums)
mgt: Donkeyboy is a Norwegian synthpop and electronic rock band from Drammen, formed in 2005. They gained prominence with their debut single "Ambitions" (2009), which topped the Norwegian charts for 12 weeks. Their debut album, *Caught in a Life*, was released the same year, followed by *Silver Moon* in 2012. Known for their chart success and international recognition, including a European Border Breakers Award in 2011, the band has supported acts like a-ha and collaborated with artists such as Kiesza.

trgt: Serbs in Germany (Lang-sr; Lang-de) refers to persons living in Germany who have total or partial Serbian ancestry. They form the seventh largest group of foreigners in Germany.
mgt: Serbs in Germany (Serbian: Srbi u Nemačkoj; German: Serben in Deutschland) are a significant ethnic group, with 304,741 people of Serbian ancestry as of 2014. Concentrated primarily in North Rhine-Westphalia, Baden-Württemberg, and Bavaria, they are numerous in cities like Munich, Stuttgart, and Düsseldorf. The community predominantly speaks German and Serbian and practices the Serbian Orthodox faith.

trgt: Commonwealth v. Pullis, 3 Doc. Hist. 59 (1806) was a US labor law case, and the first reported case arising from a labor strike in the United States. It decided that striking workers were illegal conspirators.
mgt: *Commonwealth v. Pullis* (1806), also known as the "Cordwainers' Case," was the first U.S. labor law case to criminalize union activity. Philadelphia shoemakers were convicted of conspiracy for striking to raise wages.

trgt: Harry Ruilton Watson (August 31, 1921 – June 8, 2001) was an American child actor, a U.S. Coast Guard combat photographer in World War II, and a pioneer in television journalism.
mgt: Harry Watson (August 31, 1921 – June 8, 2001) was an American child actor and member of the prominent Watson Family of Hollywood. He appeared in films such as *Mr. Smith Goes to Washington* and *A Damsel in Distress*. During World War II, he served as a combat photographer in the Pacific Theater. After the war, Watson became a pioneer in television journalism, contributing to same-day news coverage for KTTV. The Watson family was honored with a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.

trgt: Tun Tun (11 July 1923 – 24 November 2003) was the screen name of Indian playback singer and actress-comedienne, Uma Devi Khatri, who was called "Hindi cinema's first-ever comedienne".
mgt: Tun Tun, born Uma Devi Khatri (11 July 1923 – 24 November 2003), was an Indian playback singer, actress, and comedian. Initially achieving fame as a playback singer in the 1940s with hits like "Afsana Likh Rahi Hoon," she transitioned to acting upon the suggestion of composer Naushad. Renamed "Tun Tun" by actor Dilip Kumar, she became Bollywood's first female comedian, known for her impeccable comic timing and roles in classics like *Aar Paar* (1954), *Pyaasa* (1957), and *Namak Halaal* (1982). Over a five-decade career, she appeared in nearly 200 films, often providing comic relief alongside leading actors of her time. Her name became synonymous with humor and larger-than-life characters in Indian cinema. Tun Tun passed away in 2003 at the age of 80, leaving behind a legacy as a trailblazer in Indian entertainment.

trgt: Richard Sukuta-Pasu (born 24 June 1990) is a German professional footballer who plays as a forward for Vejle Boldklub.
mgt: Richard Sukuta-Pasu (born 24 June 1990) is a German professional footballer who plays as a forward for Vejle Boldklub.

trgt: The Botswana National Stadium is a multi-purpose stadium in Gaborone, Botswana. It is currently used mostly for football matches. The stadium holds 25,000 people.
mgt: The Botswana National Stadium, located in Gaborone, is a multi-use facility with a capacity of 25,000. It hosts the Botswana national football team, local football clubs, athletics, and various cultural events. Renovated multiple times, including for the 2010 FIFA World Cup and Botswana's 50th independence celebrations, the stadium features an athletics track, tennis facilities, and a giant screen.

trgt: Buck Brothers were a British three piece rock band. The band's sound is a mixture of pop and punk.
mgt: Buck Brothers were a London-based pop punk and alternative rock band active from 2005 to 2011. Known for their energetic performances and DIY ethos, the trio gained international attention by breaking a Guinness World Record for the most gigs performed in 12 hours in 2007. Their debut album, *Me*, achieved chart success in the U.S., and they toured extensively across North America, Europe, and the UK, performing at major festivals like SXSW and NXNE. In 2010, they released their second album, *We Are Merely Filters*, before rebranding as Top Buzzer in 2011.

trgt: Liu Junwei (Chinese: 刘俊威; born 1 May 1976 in Shenyang) is a Chinese football manager and former football player.
mgt: Liu Junwei (born 1 May 1976) is a Chinese football manager and former defender, currently managing Suzhou Dongwu.

trgt: Oakbank School is a coeducational secondary school located in the hamlet of Ryeish Green (near Spencers Wood) in Berkshire, England.
mgt: Oakbank School is a coeducational free school in Ryeish Green, Berkshire, England, established in 2012 on the former Ryeish Green School site.

trgt: Li Shuai (simplified Chinese: 李帅; traditional Chinese: 李帥; pinyin: Li Shuài, born 18 August 1982) is a Chinese former footballer who played as a goalkeeper.
mgt: Li Shuai (born 18 August 1982) is a retired Chinese footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He had a 20-year career with Qingdao Jonoon, Guangzhou Evergrande, and Shanghai Shenhua, winning multiple titles, including the Chinese FA Cup in 2017 and 2019.

trgt: The 1994–95 Atlante F.C. season was the 4th season since the team's last promotion to Primera División. Atlante competed in Primera División and Copa México
mgt: The 1994–95 Atlante F.C. season saw the team, managed by Ricardo Lavolpe, finish 10th in the Primera División and exit the Copa México in the first round. Despite the arrival of star forward Hugo Sánchez and a strong offensive lineup, defensive weaknesses hindered their playoff qualification. Their season included a notable 4–1 victory over Club América and a heavy 7–0 defeat to Necaxa.

trgt: CBS Playhouse is an American anthology drama television series that aired on CBS from 1967 to 1970. Airing twelve plays over the course of its run, the series won ten Primetime Emmy Awards and featured many noteworthy actors and playwrights.
mgt: *CBS Playhouse* was an American anthology drama television series that aired on CBS from 1967 to 1970. Created to encourage the production of original and significant television dramas, the series featured 12 teleplays over three seasons, written by prominent playwrights and directed by acclaimed directors. Despite its critical success, including 28 Primetime Emmy nominations (with 10 wins) and a Peabody Award, the series ended due to a lack of sponsorship funding. Its broadcasts are preserved in major archives, including the Paley Center for Media and the UCLA Film Archive.

trgt: Marko Čakarević (Serbian Cyrillic: Марко Чакаревић, born May 13, 1988) is a Serbian professional basketball player for Beograd (3x3) team.
mgt: Marko Čakarević (born May 13, 1988) is a Serbian professional basketball player, currently playing 3x3 basketball for Partizan Belgrade. A 2.00 m (6 ft 7 in) small forward, he has played for numerous teams across Europe, including ASVEL, Partizan, and Igokea, and has won multiple league and cup titles. Čakarević was named Serbian Super League MVP in 2017 and has represented Serbia internationally, winning gold at the 2007 FIBA Under-19 World Championship and the 2008 FIBA Europe Under-20 Championship.

trgt: Mike Albert is an American guitarist. He has collaborated with various musicians like Frank Zappa, Don Felder, Ruben Guevara, and L. Shankar, and toured with bands like Megadeth and El Chicano.
mgt: Mike Albert is an American guitarist and songwriter known for his work across genres such as heavy metal, rock, blues, and jazz fusion. Active since the 1970s, he has collaborated with notable artists and bands, including Ruben and the Jets, El Chicano, and Megadeth. Albert has also worked as a session musician for commercials, movie soundtracks, and live performances, and has led several projects, including the Mike Albert Project and the Albert/Schroeder Experience. His albums, such as *Afterlife* (2009) and *Crime of Passion* (2014), have received critical acclaim. Albert continues to perform and collaborate internationally, including with Indian violinist L. Shankar.

trgt: Heber Springs is a city in and the county seat of Cleburne County, Arkansas, United States. The population was 7,165 at the 2010 census.
mgt: Heber Springs is a city in Cleburne County, Arkansas, United States, with a population of 6,969 as of the 2020 census. Founded in 1882 as Sugar Loaf, it was renamed in 1910 to honor Dr. Heber Jones. The city is known for its natural springs, Greers Ferry Lake, and the Little Red River, which attract tourism for fishing, boating, and outdoor activities. Heber Springs serves as the county seat and features historical landmarks like the Clarence Frauenthal House and Greers Ferry Dam, which was dedicated by President John F. Kennedy in 1963.

trgt: Petro Pysarchuk (Lang-uk, born June 6, 1955, in Chemeryntsi, village, Peremyshliany Raion, Lviv Oblast, Ukrainian SSR) is a Ukrainian Politician and Entrepreneur. People's Deputy to the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine 4th, 5th and 6th convocations.
mgt: Petro Pysarchuk (born June 6, 1955) is a Ukrainian politician, entrepreneur, and former member of the Verkhovna Rada, representing the Party of Regions. He also founded Lviv's largest trade complex, Pivdennyj Market.

trgt: Hugh Cecil Lowther, 5th Earl of Lonsdale, KG, GCVO, DL (25 January 1857–13 April 1944) was an English peer and sportsman.
mgt: Hugh Lowther, 5th Earl of Lonsdale (1857–1944), was a British aristocrat, sportsman, and philanthropist, known as "The Yellow Earl." He was a prominent figure in foxhunting, boxing, and horse racing, founding the National Sporting Club and donating the original Lonsdale Belts. A founder and first president of the Automobile Association, he also explored the Arctic and amassed a collection of Inuit artifacts now housed in the British Museum. Despite inheriting vast wealth, his extravagant lifestyle led to financial decline, forcing the sale of family estates.

trgt: Gambling Lady is a 1934 American pre-Code romantic drama film directed by Archie Mayo, and starring Barbara Stanwyck, Joel McCrea and Pat O'Brien.
mgt: *Gambling Lady* is a 1934 American drama film directed by Archie Mayo and starring Barbara Stanwyck, Joel McCrea, and Pat O'Brien. The story follows Lady Lee, a gambler navigating love, betrayal, and high-stakes drama, as she struggles to maintain her integrity in a world of corruption. The film received mixed reviews, with praise for its action but criticism for its plausibility. Produced by Warner Bros., it was a financial success, earning $653,000 against a $230,000 budget.

trgt: Roy Noble, OBE, DL, K.St.J, Rotary Fellow (born 1942) is a Welsh radio and television broadcaster, writer and Bevan Commissioner.
mgt: Roy Noble OBE, DL, K.St.J (born 1942) is a Welsh educator, broadcaster, and writer, best known for his long-running career with BBC Radio Wales. Raised in Brynamman, Carmarthenshire, he initially worked as a teacher and headmaster before transitioning to broadcasting in 1985. Noble became a prominent radio and television presenter, earning accolades such as a Sony Award and a Royal Television Society Award. He is also an accomplished author, with works ranging from autobiographies to children's books. Beyond his media career, Noble has been deeply involved in charity, cultural initiatives, and public service in Wales, receiving numerous honors, including an OBE and a knighthood in the Order of St. John. He has served as a Deputy Lieutenant of Mid Glamorgan and contributed to various Welsh organizations, including the National Eisteddfod and the Bevan Commission. Noble resides in Llwydcoed with his wife, Elaine, and their son, Richard.

trgt: Beverly Williams (born May 22, 1947) is a former news anchor from Philadelphia.
mgt: Beverly Williams is an American journalist and television anchor, known for being the first Black woman to anchor in the Philadelphia market and for her long career at KYW-TV.

trgt: Alexander Edwin Michael Keeping (22 August 1902 – 28 March 1984) was an English footballer and manager. He coached Real Madrid CF from January 1948 to October 1950. His father was the Olympic medal winning cyclist Frederick Keeping.
mgt: Alexander Edwin Michael Keeping (22 August 1902 – 28 March 1984) was an English footballer and manager, best known for his time as a full-back with Southampton and Fulham. He made 281 appearances for Southampton and later managed clubs including Real Madrid, where he won the Copa Eva Duarte, and Poole Town.

page_title: Personal and Vehicular Basic Loads and The Morrow Project Role Playing Expansion
trgt: Personal and Vehicular Basic Loads and The Morrow Project Role Playing Expansion is a 1983 role-playing game supplement published by Timeline for The Morrow Project.
mgt: *Personal and Vehicular Basic Loads and The Morrow Project Role Playing Expansion* is a role-playing game supplement providing guidelines for character skills, tasks, and item repairs.

trgt: Hanna Yussuf Atik (born 7 February 1959) is a Lebanese politician, and veteran of the Lebanese Civil War.
mgt: Hanna Yussuf Atik (born February 7, 1959) is a Lebanese politician and former military leader in the Lebanese Forces.

trgt: Gary McDaid is a Gaelic football manager, referee and former player. He has three DSFCs: twice as manager (2011, 2013) and once as coach to Michael Canning (2016).
mgt: Gary McDaid is an Irish Gaelic football manager, referee, and teacher from Glenswilly, County Donegal. He managed Glenswilly to two Donegal Senior Football Championships and served as Donegal's under-20 manager.

trgt: Ernest Dichmann Peek (November 18, 1878 – April 22, 1950) was a major general in the United States Army, who commanded the 9th Corps Area at the beginning of World War II.
mgt: Ernest Dichmann Peek (November 18, 1878 – April 22, 1950) was a United States Army major general who served from 1901 to 1942. A graduate of the United States Military Academy, he participated in the Philippine–American War, World War I, and World War II, earning the Distinguished Service Medal and Silver Star. Peek held various engineering and command roles, including work on the Panama Canal, Lake Superior harbor improvements, and Lock and Dam No. 1 on the Mississippi River. He retired in 1942 and is buried at Arlington National Cemetery.

trgt: Barry Ono (1876–1941) was a British variety theatre performer, collector of penny dreadfuls and benefactor of the British Library. His collection of songbooks was bequeathed to the British Library in 1941.
mgt: Barry Ono, born Frederick Valentine Harrison (21 May 1876 – ?), was a British comedian, songwriter, and performer known for his one-man routine "The Old Time Music Hall." He gained prominence in 1929 and published works like *The Great Barry Ono Song Book*. Later in life, he became an avid collector of penny fiction and operated a lending library as a child.

trgt: Since the early 2020s, claims have been made on social media that there is a deliberate campaign by governments to create an artificial famine.
mgt: The deliberate food shortage conspiracy theory claims that food shortages are intentionally caused by governments or other entities. These claims, often spread on social media, lack evidence and have been widely debunked by experts and fact-checking organizations.

trgt:
Hilary Joseph Francis Lofting (23 May 1881 – 3 May 1939) was an Australian novelist, travel writer, journalist and editor.

He was the eldest brother of Hugh Lofting (1886–1947), author of Doctor Dolittle.
mgt: Hilary Joseph Francis Lofting (23 May 1881 – 3 May 1939) was an English-born Australian novelist, travel writer, journalist, and editor, known for works such as *The Happy Vagabond* and *Bail Up!*. Originally a civil engineer, he transitioned to writing after moving to Australia in 1917, adopting the pen name "Francis Brien." Lofting collaborated with his second wife, Margaret Fane, on short stories published in prominent Australian magazines. He was recognized as one of Australia's notable short story writers.

trgt: Raman Abdullah is a 1997 Indian Tamil-language comedy film directed by Balu Mahendra, starring Sivakumar, Karan and Vignesh. It is a remake of the 1994 Malayalam film Malappuram Haji Mahanaya Joji. The film was released on 22 August 1997.
mgt: *Raman Abdullah* is a 1997 Indian Tamil-language film written and directed by Balu Mahendra. Starring Sivakumar and Karan, the film features music by Ilaiyaraaja. Despite its notable cast and crew, it was a commercial failure.

trgt: Allobates femoralis (common name brilliant-thighed poison frog, brilliant-thighed poison-arrow frog) is a species of frog in the family Aromobatidae. It is found in Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, French Guiana, Guyana, Peru, and Suriname. Its natural habitat is tropical lowland forests.
mgt: Allobates femoralis is a small terrestrial frog species in the family Aromobatidae, native to the Amazon Basin and tropical eastern South America. Known for its complex reproductive behaviors, including prolonged courtship and tadpole transport, it inhabits lowland forests and is active during the day. Males are territorial and play a key role in guarding eggs and transporting tadpoles to aquatic sites. The species exhibits behavioral variation across its range, including differences in courtship signals. Listed as "Least Concern" by the IUCN, A. femoralis has a stable population and faces no significant threats.

trgt: The Gibson Dove is a flattop steel-string acoustic guitar made by the Gibson Guitar Corporation since 1962.
mgt: The Gibson Dove is a square-shouldered dreadnought acoustic guitar introduced in 1962, known for its bright tone, maple construction, and distinctive mother-of-pearl inlays.

trgt: Auguste-Henri-Victor Grandjean de Montigny (15 July 1776 – 2 March 1850) was a French architect who had considerable influence on the development of architecture in Brazil.
mgt: Auguste-Henri-Victor Grandjean de Montigny (15 July 1776 – 2 March 1850) was a French-Brazilian architect and prominent neo-classicist. After winning the Prix de Rome in 1799 and serving as architect to Jérôme Bonaparte, he moved to Brazil in 1816 at the invitation of King John VI of Portugal. In Rio de Janeiro, he became the sole architecture professor at the Academy of Fine Arts and designed numerous classical buildings, including the Casa França-Brasil and the Roman Arch. He also contributed to urban redevelopment projects and designed official and private buildings, leaving a lasting impact on Brazilian architecture.

trgt: In the Chicago mayoral election of 1923, Democrat William E. Dever defeated Republican Arthur C. Lueder and Socialist William A. Cunnea. Elections were held on April 3, the same day as aldermanic runoffs.
mgt: The 1923 Chicago mayoral election took place on April 3, 1923, resulting in the election of Democrat William E. Dever, who defeated Republican Arthur C. Lueder and Socialist William A. Cunnea. Dever, a reformist candidate backed by both Democratic factions and independent reformers, campaigned on municipal ownership of public transit and clean governance, contrasting with the scandals of outgoing Republican Mayor William H. Thompson. The election saw a 78% voter turnout, with Dever securing a decisive victory, winning 32 of the city's 50 wards and making significant inroads among Black, Jewish, and ethnic voters.

trgt: Design studies can refer to any design-oriented studies but more formally is an academic discipline or field of study that pursues, through both theoretical and practical modes of inquiry, a critical understanding of design practice and its effects in society.
mgt: Design studies is an interdisciplinary field that examines the practices, impacts, and cultural significance of design. It explores design's role in shaping society, ethics, and the environment, while addressing issues like decolonization, sustainability, and agency. Emerging in the 20th century, it integrates methodologies from design history, research, and social sciences to analyze design as both a practice and a reflection of societal values. Key areas of study include design ethics, the artificial, and actor-network theory, with research methods such as ethnography and semiotics. Prominent organizations like the Design Research Society and Design History Society support the field's development.

trgt: Madonna and Child Kissing is a 1520s oil on panel painting by the Flemish renaissance artist Quentin Matsys in the collection of the Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam, on loan to the Mauritshuis.
mgt: *Madonna and Child Kissing* is an oil-on-panel painting by Quentin Matsys, created circa 1525–1530. Depicting Mary and Jesus in a richly decorated setting, it is housed at the Mauritshuis, The Hague.

trgt: The 1945–46 season was the 73rd season of competitive football in Scotland and the seventh and final season of special wartime football during World War II.
mgt: The 1945–46 season in Scottish football marked the final year of wartime competitions, with the Southern League operating in two divisions. Rangers won the Southern League A Division and the Victory Cup, while Aberdeen claimed the Southern League Cup. Internationally, Scotland played unofficial matches, including a notable 1–0 "Victory International" win over England at Hampden Park.

trgt: The Reformed Christian Church in Slovakia has 110,000 members in 205 parishes and 103 mission churches and 59 house fellowships in 10 presbyteries. The bishop is the head of the church, in contrast with other Calvinist churches.
mgt: The Reformed Christian Church in Slovakia is a Protestant denomination with roots in the Reformation of the 1520s, influenced by Calvinism. It has approximately 110,000 members, primarily Hungarian-speaking, and operates schools and mission congregations.

trgt: Pascual de Gayangos y Arce (June 21, 1809 – October 4, 1897) was a Spanish scholar and orientalist.
mgt: Pascual de Gayangos y Arce (1809–1897) was a Spanish historian, Arabist, and professor, known for his work on Spanish manuscripts.

trgt: The railroad brotherhoods are labor unions of railroad workers in the United States. They first appeared in 1863 and they are still active. Until recent years they were largely independent of each other and of the American Federation of Labor.
mgt: The railroad brotherhoods were labor unions in the United States that emerged in the late 19th century to represent railway workers. Focused on securing benefits, work rules, and fair wages, they gained influence by negotiating with management and achieving legislative victories like the Adamson Act in 1916. However, their power waned in the 1920s following failed efforts at nationalization, the Great Railroad Strike of 1922, and political challenges.

trgt: The Secours Populaire Français (SPF), or French Popular Relief, is a French non-profit organization founded in 1945, dedicated to fighting poverty and discrimination in public life.
mgt: Secours populaire français (SPF) is a French non-profit organization founded in 1945, dedicated to combating poverty, discrimination, and child abuse in France and globally. It provides emergency aid, supports equal access to essential services, and collaborates with local and international partners on development projects. Originating from the Secours Rouge International in 1926, SPF evolved through significant historical events, including World War II, and has become a leading charitable organization in France. It campaigns for social justice, organizes events for disadvantaged children, and responds to global crises, such as the 2015 Nepal earthquake.

trgt: The Rechtboomssloot, or Recht Boomssloot, is a canal in the Lastage (Nieuwmarktbuurt) neighborhood in the center of Amsterdam.
mgt: The Rechtboomssloot is a historic canal in Amsterdam, dating to the 16th century, named after shipbuilder Cornelis Boom.

trgt: Richard Jata (born September 21, 1985 in Dunedin, Florida) is an American soccer player.
mgt: Richard Jata (born September 21, 1985) is a retired American soccer midfielder.

trgt: Nina is a French television series. It has run since 2015 on France 2.
mgt: *Nina* is a French television series that follows 39-year-old nurse Nina as she begins her career in the internal medicine department of Madeleine Brès Hospital, working under her ex-husband, Dr. Costa Antonakis. The series explores her personal and professional challenges, including caring for her daughter with cancer and navigating complex relationships with colleagues. Created by Thalia Rebinsky and Alain Robillard, the show premiered on France 2 in 2015 and ran for four seasons, with a fifth season confirmed before the fourth aired.

trgt: "Al-Damad" Ahmad Nami or "Damat" Ahmet Nami (Lang-ar;‎ 1873 – 13 December 1962) was an Ottoman prince (damat), the fifth prime minister of Syria and second president of Syria (1926–28), and a lecturer of history and politics.
mgt: Ahmad Nami (1873 – December 13, 1962) was a Syrian politician and statesman who served as the 5th Prime Minister and 2nd President of Syria from 1926 to 1928 during the French Mandate. Born in Beirut to an affluent family of Ottoman descent, he was educated in military academies and married Ayşe Sultan, daughter of Sultan Abdul Hamid II. As president, Nami sought to preserve Syrian unity, establish a national army, and secure compensation for citizens affected by the Great Syrian Revolt, but his ambitions were curtailed by French authorities, leading to his removal in 1928. After retiring from politics, he lived in Lebanon and occasionally lectured in France.

trgt: HMS Sir Isaac Brock was a warship which was destroyed before being completed at York, Upper Canada during the War of 1812. The ship was named after the famed hero of the war, Major General Sir Isaac Brock.
mgt: HMS *Sir Isaac Brock* was a British sloop-of-war under construction at York (now Toronto) during the War of 1812. Intended as a 24-gun ship, it was incomplete when American forces attacked York in April 1813. To prevent its capture, British forces set the ship ablaze during the Battle of York, reducing it to charred remains. The ship's destruction angered the Americans, as it occurred while surrender negotiations were ongoing. *Sir Isaac Brock* was a sister ship to HMS *Wolfe*, which was completed at Kingston.

trgt: USS Commencement Bay (CVE-105) (ex-St. Joseph Bay), the lead ship of her class, was an escort carrier and later helicopter carrier of the United States Navy, used mostly as a training ship.
mgt: USS *Commencement Bay* was a Commencement Bay-class escort carrier of the United States Navy, commissioned in 1944. Primarily serving as a training ship, it was decommissioned in 1946 and later scrapped in 1972.

trgt: Dongmen (Chinese: 东门镇) is a town and the county seat in Luocheng Mulao Autonomous County, Guangxi, China. As of the 2019 census it had a population of 101,483 and an area of 385.24-square-kilometre (148.74 sq mi).
mgt: Dongmen (东门镇) is a town in Luocheng Mulao Autonomous County, Guangxi, China. Established as a township in 1952 and designated a town in 1991, it spans 385.24 km² with a population of 101,483 as of 2019. The town's economy is primarily agricultural, focusing on crops like rice, corn, sugarcane, and cassava, alongside livestock farming. Dongmen features notable attractions such as the Martyrs' Cemetery and Kaiyuan Temple, as well as public parks and transportation links including the Chaluo railway and China National Highway 357.

trgt: The Commissioner of Police, Delhi or Delhi Police Commissioner is the head of the Delhi Police, the Law enforcement agency of the 15 police districts (as of January 2019) of National Capital of India, Delhi.
mgt: The Commissioner of the Delhi Police is the head of the Delhi Police, established in 1978 following the recommendations of the Khosla Commission. The inaugural holder was J.N. Chaturvedi, and the current commissioner is Sanjay Arora.

trgt: Oberhünigen is a municipality in the Bern-Mittelland administrative district in the canton of Bern in Switzerland.
mgt: Oberhünigen is a small municipality in the canton of Bern, Switzerland, located in the Bern-Mittelland district. Covering an area of 6.02 km², it is characterized by its agricultural land, dense forests, and a small population of 316 (as of 2020). First mentioned in 1148, Oberhünigen was historically part of Schlosswil until 1980. The municipality is known for its rural charm, low unemployment, and a strong connection to the Swiss Reformed Church. It features a small local economy centered on agriculture, manufacturing, and education, with most residents commuting to work outside the municipality.

trgt: Kunigunde Sophie Ludovike Simanowiz (née Reichenbach; February 21, 1759, Schorndorf - September 3, 1827, Ludwigsburg) was a German portrait painter in the Classical style.
mgt: Ludovike Simanowiz (1759–1827) was a German portrait painter known for her connections to Friedrich Schiller and her approximately 100 portraits, mostly in private collections.

trgt: Henry Monsang Tesong (born 28 February 1994) is an Indian professional footballer who plays as a defender for Manipur State League side Sagolband United.
mgt: Henry Monsang Tesong (born 28 February 1994) is an Indian footballer from Manipur who plays as a defender for Sagolband United. He previously played for Pune and Chennai City in the I-League, making his professional debut with Chennai City in 2017.

trgt: Apollonius Dyscolus (Lang-el; reached his maturity sometime around 130 CE) is considered one of the greatest of the Greek grammarians.
mgt: Apollonius Dyscolus (2nd century) was an Alexandrian grammarian, founder of scientific syntax, and influential in later grammatical traditions.

trgt: Gary Dean Penisten (born May 14, 1931) is an American executive who served as United States Assistant Secretary of the Navy (Financial Management and Comptroller) from 1974 to 1977.
mgt: Gary D. Penisten (born 1931) is an American businessman and former government official who served as Assistant Secretary of the Navy (Financial Management and Comptroller) from 1974 to 1977.

trgt: Shalee Lehning (born October 27, 1986) is a former American basketball player who most recently played for the Atlanta Dream of the WNBA. She was Associate Head Coach at the University of Northern Colorado under head coach Kamie Ethridge.
mgt: Shalee Lehning (born October 27, 1986) is a former American basketball player and coach. A standout at Kansas State University, she played as a guard for the Atlanta Dream in the WNBA from 2009 to 2011 before retiring due to injury. Lehning holds multiple records at Kansas State and is the only Big 12 player to achieve 1,000 points, 800 rebounds, and 700 assists. Following her playing career, she served as an assistant coach at Kansas State and later as associate head coach at Northern Colorado.

trgt: ACE (Advanced Computer Entertainment) was a multi-format computer and video game magazine first published in the United Kingdom by Future Publishing and later acquired by EMAP.
mgt: ACE (Advanced Computer Entertainment) was a British multi-format video game magazine published monthly from October 1987 to April 1992. Initially launched by Future Publishing and later acquired by EMAP, it covered platforms like the Atari ST, Amiga, and ZX Spectrum, featuring game reviews, industry news, and technical articles. Known for its "Screen Test" review section, ACE introduced unique scoring systems, including ratings out of 1000 and a Predicted Interest Curve graph. The magazine also included cover-mounted cassettes and floppy disks with game demos.

trgt: Ukhra Assembly constituency was an assembly constituency in what is now Paschim Bardhaman district in the Indian state of West Bengal. It covered the Ukhra area.
mgt: Ukhra Assembly constituency was a legislative assembly constituency in Paschim Bardhaman district, West Bengal, India, established in 1969 and abolished in 2011 following delimitation. It was part of the Asansol Lok Sabha constituency.

trgt: Oswald Chettle Mazengarb CBE QC (31 May 1890 – 27 November 1963), known as Ossie Mazengarb, was a New Zealand barrister.
mgt: Oswald Chettle Mazengarb CBE QC (31 May 1890 – 27 November 1963) was a New Zealand barrister, King's Counsel, and politician. He served briefly on the New Zealand Legislative Council in 1950 as part of the "suicide squad" that voted for its abolition. Mazengarb was known for his legal expertise, authoring legal textbooks, and his public service, including chairing the 1954 Special Committee on Moral Delinquency in Children and Adolescents, which produced the influential Mazengarb Report. He was appointed a Commander of the Order of the British Empire in 1953.

trgt: Peter Thomas Hollins (born 22 October 1947) is a British businessman, and a former chief executive of British Energy, which ran most of Britain's nuclear power stations, and was part of the FTSE 100 Index.
mgt: Peter Hollins is a British businessman and former executive known for his leadership roles in major organizations. He served as chief executive of British Energy from 1998 to 2001 and later became director general of the British Heart Foundation in 2003. Hollins began his career at BOC in 1970 and held various senior positions at ICI, including Chief Operating Officer of European Vinyls Corporation. In 2016, he was appointed chairman of the University Hospital Southampton NHS Foundation Trust. Born in Hendon, Middlesex, he studied chemistry at Hertford College, Oxford, and is fluent in multiple languages.

trgt: Rose Venkatesan is the first Indian talk show host based in Chennai, Tamil Nadu. She is a trans woman, and has been active in many fields like television and radio.
mgt: Rose Venkatesan is an Indian talk show host, radio jockey, politician, and LGBTQ activist. Known for hosting *Ippadikku Rose*, she advocates for sexual freedom, gender rights, and societal reform.

trgt: Freeze Frame is the third album by Godley & Creme. The album was recorded at Nigel Gray's Surrey Sound Studios, Leatherhead, Surrey. The cover art, designed by Hipgnosis, identifies the duo as'Godley Creme'.
mgt: *Freeze Frame* is a 1979 studio album by English rock duo Godley & Creme, blending rock, new wave, and art punk genres. Known for its innovative use of vocal manipulation and the Gizmo device, the album features the single "An Englishman in New York."

trgt: Securicula gora is a species of cyprinid fish found in Bangladesh, India, Pakistan and possibly in Nepal. It is the only species in its genus.
mgt: *Securicula* is a genus of freshwater fish in the family Danionidae, represented by the single species *Securicula gora*. Native to the Ganges and Brahmaputra drainages in India, Bangladesh, and Nepal, it is a silvery, predatory fish that inhabits rivers, beels, and canals. Growing up to 24.5 cm, it feeds on insects and crustaceans near the water's surface. Classified as Least Concern by the IUCN, its habitat faces threats from silting due to deforestation. While of minor commercial interest, it is consumed as food in parts of its range.

trgt: Aleksei Fedorovich Losev (Lang-ru; 23 September 1893 – 24 May 1988) was a Russian philosopher, philologist and culturologist, one of the most prominent figures in Russian philosophical and religious thought of the 20th century.
mgt: Aleksei Losev (1893–1988) was a Russian philosopher, philologist, and theologian, recognized as one of the most significant figures in 20th-century Russian philosophy. A prolific scholar, he authored works on classical aesthetics, mythology, and dialectics, synthesizing Russian philosophy, Christian Neoplatonism, and Western thought. Despite his intellectual achievements, Losev faced persecution under Soviet rule, including imprisonment and forced labor, for his critiques of Communist ideology. After his release, he resumed his academic career, publishing extensively and receiving the USSR State Prize in 1986. His legacy remains influential yet controversial, with debates over his views on religion and antisemitism.

trgt: Oreal Donald Perras (August 25, 1942 – February 18, 2017) was a Canadian professional wrestler, better known by the ring name "The Russian Bear" Ivan Koloff. He was the third wrestler to hold the WWWF Championship.
mgt: Ivan Koloff (born Oreal Donald Perras; August 25, 1942 – February 18, 2017) was a Canadian professional wrestler best known as "The Russian Bear." A former WWWF World Heavyweight Champion, he ended Bruno Sammartino's historic title reign in 1971. Koloff had a successful career spanning over five decades, competing in promotions such as the World Wide Wrestling Federation (WWWF), National Wrestling Alliance (NWA), and Eastern Championship Wrestling (ECW).
