He has been honored as a “Chevalier” and later “Officier” of the Ordre National du Mérite by France. Weymouth is the brother of musician Tina Weymouth and the great-grandson of Breton writer Anatole Le Braz. He has been married twice and has three children, including Katharine Weymouth.

trgt: GE Power (formerly known as GE Energy) is an American energy technology company, owned by General Electric.
mgt: GE Power is a subsidiary of General Electric, specializing in energy industry products like turbines, generators, and nuclear reactors.

trgt: Cromarty Courthouse, formerly Cromarty Town House, is a municipal building in Church Street, Cromarty, in the Highland area of Scotland. The structure, which is used as a museum, is a Category A listed building.
mgt: The Cromarty Courthouse, located on Church Street in Cromarty, Scotland, is a historic municipal building completed in 1773 in the Scottish medieval style. Originally serving as a town house and judicial venue, it later housed the burgh council. Notable events include the 1843 rescue of a prisoner during protests against a new parish priest. Restored in the early 1990s, it reopened as the Cromarty Courthouse Museum in 1991, showcasing local history and artefacts, including items related to writer Sir Thomas Urquhart. It is a Category A listed building.

trgt: Sam Patrello (October 24, 1934 – August 15, 2009) was an American nightclub and movie comedian best known as a Jerry Lewis imitator.
mgt: Sammy Petrillo (October 24, 1934 – August 15, 2009) was an American comedian and actor, best known for his striking resemblance to Jerry Lewis and his nightclub act with Duke Mitchell, which parodied the Martin and Lewis comedy team. He appeared in the cult film *Bela Lugosi Meets a Brooklyn Gorilla* (1952) and had a varied career in comedy, television, and low-budget films. Petrillo also mentored young comedians, including Richard Pryor and Dennis Miller, and remained active in entertainment until his death.

trgt: The Matt Lucas Awards is a British entertainment series hosted by comedian and television personality Matt Lucas.
mgt: *The Matt Lucas Awards* is a British comedy entertainment show created and presented by Matt Lucas. Airing on BBC One from 2012 to 2013, the series featured celebrity guests nominating winners in humorous and unconventional award categories.

trgt: Recep Burak Yılmaz (born 27 November 1995), is a Turkish professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Hatayspor.
mgt: Recep Burak Yılmaz (born 27 November 1995) is a Turkish professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Hatayspor.

trgt: Elena Vallortigara (born 21 September 1991) is an Italian high jumper who won the bronze medal at the 2022 World Athletics Championships in Eugene, Oregon.
mgt: Elena Vallortigara (born 21 September 1991) is an Italian high jumper, 2022 World Championships bronze medalist, and eight-time national champion with a personal best of 2.02 m.

trgt: Pheona Nabasa Gladys Wall, commonly known as Pheona Wall, is a Ugandan lawyer, Public Relation Officer and serves as the president of Uganda Law Society since September 2020. She served as a lecturer of Law at Kampala International University.
mgt: Pheona Nabasa Gladys Wall is a Ugandan lawyer, public relations professional, and leader, currently serving as the Commercial Officer Legal and Debt Management at National Water and Sewerage Corporation. She was elected president of the Uganda Law Society in 2020, having previously held roles as vice president and honorary secretary. Wall has extensive experience in legal risk management, regulatory compliance, and public relations, working with organizations such as Airtel Uganda and the Public Relations Association of Uganda. She has also been recognized for her contributions, including winning the Hague Institute for Innovation award in 2018.

trgt: Frank Simmons Leavitt (June 30, 1891 – May 29, 1953) was an American professional wrestler of the early 1900s, known by the ring name Man Mountain Dean.
mgt: Man Mountain Dean, born Frank Simmons Leavitt (June 30, 1891 – May 29, 1953), was an American professional wrestler, actor, and soldier. Known for his imposing size and signature beard, he had a successful wrestling career spanning over 6,700 matches and appeared in several films. Dean also served in both World Wars, retiring as a master sergeant, and later studied journalism at the University of Georgia. He died of a heart attack in Norcross, Georgia, in 1953.

trgt: Enrico Morin (1841–1910) was an Italian admiral and politician who held several cabinet posts.
mgt: Enrico Morin (5 May 1841 – 13 September 1910) was an Italian admiral, naval commander, and politician who served as Minister of the Navy in multiple cabinets and briefly as Foreign Minister in 1903.

trgt: Timothy Christian Hauck (born December 20, 1966) is a former American football safety in the National Football League (NFL). He most recently was the safeties coach for the Philadelphia Eagles of the National Football League (NFL).
mgt: Tim Hauck (born December 20, 1966) is a former American football safety and coach. He played 13 seasons in the NFL, notably with the Philadelphia Eagles, and later coached at both collegiate and professional levels. Hauck served as the safeties coach for the Eagles from 2016 to 2020, winning Super Bowl LII.

trgt: Hugues Alexandre Joseph Meunier (23 November 1758 or 1751 – 9 December 1831) was a French Army general during the French Revolutionary Wars and the Napoleonic Wars.
mgt: Hugues Alexandre Joseph Meunier (1768–1831) was a French general and baron of the First French Empire. He served in the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars, holding key military and administrative roles, including commanding divisions, overseeing military schools, and contributing to military reforms.

trgt: Ludvík Podéšť, pseudonym Ludvík Binovský (19 December 1921 in Dubňany – 27 February 1968 in Prague), was a Czech composer, conductor, music journalist and editor.
mgt: Ludvík Podéšť (1921–1968) was a Czech composer, conductor, and musicologist known for his operas, orchestral works, and politically engaged songs. His style blended classical influences, folklore, and later Moroccan musical elements.

trgt: Mario Adrián Vázquez (born June 15, 1977) is an American singer from The Bronx, New York City. He competed on American Idol's fourth season, which aired on the FOX network in early 2005.
mgt: Mario Vazquez (born June 15, 1977) is an American singer known for his brief stint on *American Idol* in 2005, where he withdrew as a finalist citing personal reasons. Following his departure, he signed with J Records and released his debut album in 2006, featuring the hit single "Gallery." Vazquez has collaborated with notable artists and producers and later pursued independent music projects. His career has also been marked by controversy, including a 2007 lawsuit alleging misconduct, which was ultimately sent to arbitration.

trgt: Hannah Gonzalez (born 21 November 1990, Cali, Colombia) is a Colombian disc jockey of electronic music, musical producer and model.
mgt: Hannah Gonzalez (born November 21, 1990) is a Colombian DJ and electronic music producer known for her work in house, techno, and progressive house genres. Starting her career at 15, she gained recognition performing at electronic music festivals across Colombia under the pseudonym Deejane Hannah. She released notable albums, including *Orgasmic Sessions* and *Elitech*, and has performed internationally, sharing stages with artists like Franz Ferdinand and DJ Umek. Gonzalez has also been featured on prominent Colombian radio programs and won a 2011 Twitter popularity contest organized by PremiosTwCo.

trgt: Masʿūd Zazoyo or Masʿūd of Zaz (c.1430/31 – 1509/1512) was a Syriac Orthodox author, hermit, monk and prelate.
mgt: Masʿūd II of Ṭur ʿAbdin (d. 1509 or 1512) was a Syriac Orthodox patriarch, bishop, abbot, and mystical writer.

trgt: Air Vice Marshal Christina Reid Elliot, CB, CBE, DL is a retired senior Royal Air Force officer.
mgt: Christina Elliot is a retired Royal Air Force air vice-marshal who served from 1985 to 2020.

trgt: Wat Bakong (វត្តបាគង) is a Theravada Buddhist pagoda on the precincts of the Prasat Bakong in the Ruluos archeological area of the Siemreap Province.
mgt: Wat Bakong (វត្តបាគង) is a Theravada Buddhist pagoda located in Bakong, Roluos, Siem Reap, Cambodia. Built in 1939, it is situated within the precinct of the ancient Angkorian temple Prasat Bakong. The pagoda features unique architecture, including decorated panels depicting Khmer daily life and French colonial influences, as well as interior paintings illustrating Buddhist themes. Initially criticized for its modern appearance, the pagoda's cultural significance grew over time, leading to restoration efforts in 2011. It remains an important religious and cultural site, with monks actively involved in local conservation efforts.

trgt: Tateyama Station (館山駅, Tateyama-eki) is a passenger railway station in the city of Tateyama, Chiba Prefecture, Japan, operated by the East Japan Railway Company (JR East).
mgt: Tateyama Station (館山駅) is a railway station in Tateyama, Chiba, Japan, operated by JR East. Opened in 1919, it serves the Uchibō Line and features one side and one island platform.

trgt: The FC Ingolstadt 04 II is a German association football club from the city of Ingolstadt, Bavaria. It is the reserve team of FC Ingolstadt.
mgt: FC Ingolstadt 04 II is the reserve team of FC Ingolstadt 04, founded in 2004. The team competes in the Bayernliga Süd and has previously played in the Regionalliga Bayern.

trgt: Curtis Knight and the Squires were a New York band that was fronted by singer and guitarist Curtis Knight in the mid-1960s. Both Jimi Hendrix and sax player Lonnie Youngblood were members for a while.
mgt: Curtis Knight and the Squires was an R&B band active in the 1960s, notable for featuring Jimi Hendrix as a member from 1965 to 1966. The group released several recordings, including Hendrix's earliest compositions, such as "Hornet's Nest" and "Knock Yourself Out." Their work has been revisited in later releases, including the 2015 album *You Can't Use My Name: The RSVP/PPX Sessions*. Hendrix's time with the band was marked by a controversial contract with PPX Studios, which later caused legal issues.

trgt: Ruslan Tagirovich Khuchbarov (Lang-ru; 12 November 1972 – 3 September 2004), was an Ingush terrorist presumed to be a militant nicknamed "Polkovnik" (the Russian for Colonel) notorious for his leading role in the 2004 Beslan school hostage crisis.
mgt: Ruslan Khuchbarov, also known as "Polkovnik" (Colonel), was an ethnic Ingush militant involved in several high-profile attacks in Russia, including the 2000 Galashki ambush and the 2003 bombing of the Ingushetian FSB department.

trgt: American Zombie is a 2007 American mockumentary horror film directed by Grace Lee, written by Rebecca Sonnenshine and Lee, and starring Lee and John Solomon as documentary filmmakers who investigate a fictional subculture of real-life zombies living in Los Angeles.
mgt: *American Zombie* is a 2007 mockumentary horror film directed by Grace Lee that explores the zombie subculture in Los Angeles. Blending satire and social commentary, the film follows a documentary crew investigating the lives of sentient zombies. It premiered at the Slamdance Film Festival and received mixed reviews, with praise for its originality and humor but criticism for its uneven tone and ending.

trgt: The Women's Balcony (Lang-he) is a 2016 Israeli comedy film directed by Emil Ben-Shimon.
mgt: *The Women's Balcony* is a 2016 Israeli film directed by Emil Ben-Shimon, exploring community, faith, and gender dynamics within an Orthodox Jewish congregation after a synagogue balcony collapse disrupts their lives.

trgt: The Colored Hockey League of the Maritimes (CHL) was an all-black ice hockey league founded in Nova Scotia in 1895, which featured teams from across Canada's Maritime Provinces. The league operated for several decades lasting until 1930.
mgt: The Colored Hockey League (CHL) was an all-black ice hockey league founded in 1895 in Halifax, Nova Scotia, by black Baptist leaders and intellectuals. It featured teams from across the Maritimes and sought to promote equality for Black Canadians. The league is credited with pioneering innovations in hockey, including the goaltender leaving his feet to cover the puck and the slapshot. At its peak, the CHL included over 400 players and teams from seven Nova Scotia communities and Prince Edward Island. Its legacy is commemorated through books, a documentary, and a Canada Post stamp honoring the 1906 champion Halifax Eurekas.

trgt: Arthur R. Quimby Jr. (July 1, 1933 – December 6, 2010) was an American college basketball player from the University of Connecticut.
mgt: Art Quimby (July 1, 1933 – December 6, 2010) was an American college basketball player for UConn, known for his record-breaking rebounding achievements.

trgt: Andrey Viktorovich Zayakin (Lang-ru; born 23 February 1981) is a Russian physicist, political activist and journalist. One of the founders of the volunteer community network Dissernet.
mgt: Andrey Zayakin (born February 23, 1981) is a Russian physicist, journalist, and anti-corruption activist. He co-founded Dissernet, a volunteer network exposing academic plagiarism, and has worked as a journalist for *Novaya Gazeta*, where he became chief editor of the Data department. Zayakin has been involved in anti-corruption efforts, including uncovering undeclared assets of officials, and has received the Redkollegia prize for his journalism. He has also contributed to combating scientific fraud through the Russian Academy of Sciences. In 2022, Zayakin was detained and charged with financial extremist activity for supporting Alexei Navalny's Anti-Corruption Foundation.

trgt: Ronaldo Soares Giovanelli (born 20 November 1967) is a Brazilian football pundit and retired footballer. Although as a child he was a forward, he spent his career as a goalkeeper.
mgt: Ronaldo Soares Giovanelli (born November 20, 1967) is a Brazilian former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper, primarily for Corinthians, where he became the club's third most capped player with 602 appearances. Known for his physicality and decisive performances, he was instrumental in Corinthians' 1990 Campeonato Brasileiro title and earned the Bola de Prata award twice. After leaving Corinthians in 1998, he played for several other clubs before retiring in 2005. Beyond football, Ronaldo is the lead singer of the band Ronaldo e os Impedidos and a radio host for Kiss FM.

trgt: Manilal Mohandas Gandhi (28 October 1892 – 5 April 1956) was the second son of Mohandas Gandhi and Kasturba Gandhi.
mgt: Manilal Gandhi (28 October 1892 – 5 April 1956) was the second son of Mahatma Gandhi, editor of *Indian Opinion*, and activist in South Africa.

trgt: Ingrith Johnson Deyrup-Olsen (1919 – July 25, 2004) was an American zoologist, an expert on slugs, and a science professor interested in improving science education.
mgt: Ingrith Johnson Deyrup-Olsen (died July 25, 2004) was an American zoologist and physiologist known for her research on banana slugs and mucus production. A professor at Barnard College and later the University of Washington, she contributed to women's studies, biology education, and physiological research, earning a Guggenheim Fellowship and a Fulbright Scholarship.

trgt: "Now's the Time" is a composition by Charlie Parker. He led the first recording of it, on November 26, 1945.
mgt: "Now's the Time" is a 1945 blues composition by Charlie Parker, blending bebop with Kansas City jazz influences. It inspired "The Hucklebuck."

trgt: Rafe Roys Mazzeo (born 1961) is an American mathematician working in differential geometry, microlocal analysis, and partial differential equations. He is currently a professor of mathematics and the department chair at Stanford University.
mgt: Rafe Mazzeo (born 1961) is an American mathematician and professor at Stanford University, known for his contributions to geometric analysis and partial differential equations. He has published over 150 papers, supervised 11 doctoral students, and co-founded the Stanford University Mathematics Camp. Mazzeo earned his Ph.D. from MIT in 1986 under Richard Burt Melrose and became a full professor at Stanford in 1997. He has received numerous honors, including a Sloan Research Fellowship, election as a fellow of the American Mathematical Society in 2013, and membership in the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 2022.

trgt: Birna Arnbjörnsdóttir (born 1952) is an Icelandic linguist. She is Professor of Second Language Studies at the University of Iceland, Director of the Vigdís Finnbogadóttir Institute of Foreign Languages and Dean of the Faculty of Languages and Cultures.
mgt: Birna Arnbjörnsdóttir (born 1952) is an Icelandic linguist and professor specializing in second-language acquisition. She has contributed extensively to research on Icelandic as a heritage language, language contact, and the influence of English in Iceland. A professor at the University of Iceland since 2000, she has led numerous research projects, including the development of Icelandic Online, a widely recognized free language-learning platform. Birna has also been active in international research networks and language technology initiatives. She has served in various academic and community roles, including as Chair of the Vigdís Finnbogadóttir Institute of Foreign Languages.

trgt: Ole Herman Johannes Krag (7 April 1837 – 9 December 1916) was a Norwegian officer and firearms designer.
mgt: Ole Herman Johannes Krag (April 7, 1837 – December 9, 1916) was a Norwegian inventor and military officer, best known for co-developing the Krag–Jørgensen rifle, adopted by the Danish, Norwegian, and U.S. armies.

page_title: Dr. George McLelland Middleton House and Garage
trgt: The Dr. George McLelland Middleton House and Garage is a historic building located in the central part of Davenport, Iowa, United States. The residence has been listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1982.
mgt: The Dr. George McLelland Middleton House and Garage, located in Davenport, Iowa, is a Neoclassical-style residence designed by architect Frederick G. Clausen in 1903. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1982.

trgt: Bradys Bend Township is a township in Armstrong County, Pennsylvania, United States. The population was 784 at the 2020 census, a decrease from 773 at the 2010 census.
mgt: Bradys Bend Township is a small township in Armstrong County, Pennsylvania, United States. Settled in 1784 and incorporated in 1845, it spans 12.6 square miles along the Allegheny River. The township is home to historical landmarks like the Bradys Bend Iron Company Furnaces and St. Stephen's Church, both listed on the National Register of Historic Places. As of the 2010 census, the population was 784, with a slight decline estimated in 2021. The area includes portions of Pennsylvania State Game Lands Number 105, offering recreational opportunities.

trgt: Chaos of the Senses is a novel by the novelist, Ahlam Mostaghanmi published in 1997 by Hachette Antoine publishing house. The novel was translated by Nancy Roberts.
mgt: *Chaos of the Senses* is a 1997 Arabic novel by Ahlam Mosteghanemi. Set during the Algerian Civil War, it follows Hayat, a novelist in a loveless marriage, as her writing blurs with reality, exploring themes of love, identity, and Algeria's struggles.

trgt: Stevan Mirković (Serbian Cyrillic: Стеван Мирковић; 27 October 1927 – 26 September 2015) was a Serb general of the Yugoslav People's Army (JNA).
mgt: Stevan Mirković (27 October 1927 – 26 September 2015) was a Yugoslav Colonel General, Chief of the General Staff (1987–1989), and World War II veteran.

trgt:
Wergea (Lang-nl) is a village in Leeuwarden municipality in the province of Friesland, the Netherlands. It had a population of around 1,560 in January 2017.

There are two windmills in the village, De Hempenserpoldermolen and a paaltjasker.
mgt: Wergea (Dutch: Warga) is a village in the municipality of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands, with a population of 1,830 as of 2021. Originating as a terp village in the early Middle Ages, it developed into a center of trade and shipping during the 16th and 17th centuries. Notable landmarks include the 1863 polder mill De Hempenserpoldermolen and the Roman Catholic St. Martinus Church, built between 1860 and 1862. Wergea was also the site of the Netherlands' first cooperative dairy factory, established in 1886. Previously part of Boarnsterhim municipality, it became part of Leeuwarden in 2014.

trgt: Lucius Julius L. f Vop. n. Iulus was a member of the patrician house of the Julii at ancient Rome. He was military tribune with consular powers in 401 and 397 BC.
mgt: Lucius Julius Iulus was a Roman statesman who served as consular tribune in 401 and 397 BC. During his terms, he oversaw military campaigns, domestic challenges, and significant political developments, including the election of Rome's first plebeian consular tribune.

trgt: Eleanor Spencer Stone Perényi (January 4, 1918 – May 3, 2009) was a gardener and author. She wrote several books including Green Thoughts, a collection of essays based on her own gardening experiences.
mgt: Eleanor Perenyi (January 4, 1918 – May 3, 2009) was an American author best known for *Green Thoughts: A Writer in the Garden* and her 1946 memoir *More Was Lost*, chronicling her life in Hungary.

trgt: Philipp Jakob Wilhelm von Henke (19 June 1834 in Jena – 17 May 1896 in Tübingen) was a German anatomist.
mgt: Wilhelm von Henke (1834–1896) was a German anatomist and professor, known for his work on "Henke's space" and theories about the Venus de Milo's asymmetry.

trgt: Callulops fojaensis is a species of frog in the family Microhylidae. It is only known from the Foja mountains in West Papua (Indonesia).
mgt: *Callulops fojaensis* is a species of frog in the family Microhylidae, known only from the Foja Mountains in Indonesia. Described in 2012, it inhabits wet, mossy mid-montane forests at around 1,600 meters above sea level. The species is characterized by its robust, pear-shaped body, pale brown coloration, and distinctive advertisement call of loud, harsh barking notes. It is considered rare, with only two male specimens documented to date. The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has classified its conservation status as Data Deficient.

trgt: Maria-Luise Rainer (born 23 April 1959 in South Tyrol) is an Italian luger who competed between the late 1970s and the late 1980s.
mgt: Maria-Luise Rainer (born 23 April 1959) is an Italian former luger who competed from 1976 to 1988, winning multiple international medals, including a bronze at the 1979 FIL World Luge Championships and a silver at the 1980 European Championships. She also won the overall Luge World Cup title in women's singles during the 1985–86 season. Rainer later served as race director at the 2006 Winter Olympics and has held senior technical roles with the International Luge Federation.

trgt: 116 (one hundred [and] sixteen) is the natural number following 115 and preceding 117.
mgt: 116 is a natural number, notable in mathematics for its properties as a noncototient, its factorial prime status, and its connections to combinatorics and Costas arrays.

trgt: The first USS Arizona was an iron-hulled, side-wheel merchant steamship. Seized by the Confederate States of America in 1862 during the American Civil War, she was captured later the same year by the United States Navy.
mgt: USS *Arizona* (1858) was a sidewheel steamer built in Wilmington, Delaware, for commercial use before being seized by the Confederacy during the American Civil War and renamed *Caroline* for blockade-running. Captured by the Union Navy in 1862, she was recommissioned as USS *Arizona* in 1863 and served in the West Gulf Blockading Squadron, participating in key operations such as the Battle of Grand Lake and the Red River Campaign. She also supported Union efforts at Port Hudson and Sabine Pass. The vessel was destroyed by fire in 1865 near New Orleans. Efforts to locate and preserve her wreck are ongoing through the USS Arizona Civil War Project Fund.

trgt: Matthew "Matt" Benedict Walker MBE (born 25 April 1978 in Stockport, England) is a British swimmer who has participated in four Paralympic Games, winning eleven medals. He competes in the S7 (butterfly and freestyle), SM7 (medley) and SB7 (breaststroke) classifications.
mgt: Matthew Benedict Walker MBE (born 25 April 1978) is a British Paralympic swimmer who has won 11 Paralympic medals, including three golds in relay events, across four Games from 2000 to 2012. Competing in the S7 classification, he also earned numerous medals at World, European, and Commonwealth Championships. Walker was appointed Member of the Order of the British Empire (MBE) in 2009 for his contributions to disabled sport.

trgt: Ramona Christine Jennex (born June 4, 1955) is a former Canadian educator and politician from the province of Nova Scotia.
mgt: Ramona Jennex (born June 4, 1955) is a Canadian politician and educator who served as a Nova Scotia MLA (2009–2013).

trgt: The Taylorcraft Auster was a British military liaison and observation aircraft produced by the Taylorcraft Aeroplanes (England) Limited company during the Second World War.
mgt: The Taylorcraft Auster was a British military light observation aircraft developed during World War II from the American Taylorcraft Model A. Initially adapted for civilian use as the Taylorcraft Plus C, it was later modified for Air Observation Post (AOP) roles, directing artillery fire for the British Army. Various models, including the Auster III, IV, and V, were widely used by the Royal Air Force, Royal Canadian Air Force, and other Allied forces in Europe, North Africa, and the Pacific. Postwar, the Auster evolved into civilian and military variants, with its roles eventually replaced by helicopters.

trgt: Sussex Archaeological Collections is an annual peer-reviewed academic journal covering archaeological topics. The journal is published by the Sussex Archaeological Society and was established in 1848.
mgt: The *Sussex Archaeological Collections* is an annual peer-reviewed journal published by the Sussex Archaeological Society since 1848, focusing on the archaeology and history of Sussex. It was among the earliest county-based historical journals and has contributed significantly to English county studies. The journal adopted anonymous article reviewing in 1979 and became the first county archaeological society to share content via the open-access Archaeology Data Service. In 2021, its entire catalogue was digitized and made freely available online to mark the society's 175th anniversary.

trgt: Peter Houston Kostmayer (born September 27, 1946) is a Democratic politician who served seven terms in the U.S. House of Representatives from Bucks County, Pennsylvania.
mgt: Peter H. Kostmayer (born September 27, 1946) is an American politician and former Democratic U.S. Representative for Pennsylvania's 8th district, serving from 1977–1981 and 1983–1993.

trgt: Eileen Dennes (1 February 1898 – 22 January 1991) was an Irish-born actress of the silent era.
mgt: Eileen Dennes (1898–1991) was an Irish silent film actress who appeared in British and American films.

page_title: Claude Frédéric t'Serclaes, Count of Tilly
trgt: Claude Frederic T'Serclaes, Count of Tilly, was a soldier and later general in the Dutch States Army.
mgt: Claude Frédéric t'Serclaes, Count of Tilly (July 1648 – April 1723), was a Flemish nobleman and field marshal in the Dutch States Army. Serving from 1667 to 1723, he participated in major European conflicts, including the Franco-Dutch War, Nine Years' War, and War of the Spanish Succession. Notably, he co-led the Dutch forces at the Battle of Ekeren and commanded cavalry at Malplaquet. Tilly later served as governor of Namur, 's-Hertogenbosch, and Maastricht.

trgt: Chillicothe is a city in Hardeman County, in the U.S. state of Texas. The population was 707 at the 2010 census.
mgt: Chillicothe, Texas, is a small city in Hardeman County, founded in the 1880s and named after Chillicothe, Missouri. It developed rapidly with the arrival of the Fort Worth and Denver City Railway in 1887. Known as the "Iris Village" for its abundance of irises, the city was incorporated in 1907. Chillicothe has a population of 549 as of the 2020 census and is served by the Chillicothe Independent School District. It features a semi-arid climate and is located along U.S. Route 287.

trgt: Barrington J. Bayley (9 April 1937 – 14 October 2008) was an English science fiction writer.
mgt: Barrington J. Bayley (9 April 1937 – 14 October 2008) was a British science fiction writer, influential in the New Wave movement.

trgt: Rodney Bullock Jr. (born 3 June 1994) is an American professional basketball player for Prishtina of the Kosovo Basketball Superleague. He competed in college basketball for Providence.
mgt: Rodney Bullock (born June 3, 1994) is an American professional basketball player for KB Prishtina in the Kosovo Basketball Superleague. A 6 ft 8 in small forward, he played college basketball at Providence, where he earned Second-Team All-Big East honors in 2017. After going undrafted in the 2018 NBA draft, Bullock began his professional career in Europe, playing for teams in Hungary, Kosovo, and Saudi Arabia.

trgt: Year of the Gun is a 1991 American thriller film directed by John Frankenheimer and starring Andrew McCarthy, Sharon Stone and Valeria Golino.
mgt: *Year of the Gun* is a 1991 American political thriller film directed by John Frankenheimer and based on Michael Mewshaw's novel of the same name. Set in 1978 Rome during the violent activities of the Red Brigades, the film follows an American journalist whose fictional novel about a political kidnapping inadvertently entangles him in real-life events, including the abduction of Italian Prime Minister Aldo Moro. Starring Andrew McCarthy, Sharon Stone, and Valeria Golino, the film explores themes of political unrest and personal betrayal. Despite production challenges and mixed critical reception, it was noted for its atmospheric depiction of Rome and Frankenheimer's direction.

trgt: "Douglas" Khoo Kok Hui (born 21 May 1965 in Kuala Lumpur) is a Malaysian racing driver currently competing in the TCR International Asia Series. He had previously competed in the Asia Classic Car Challenge.
mgt: Douglas Khoo (born 21 May 1965) is a Malaysian racing driver and team owner. He began his career in the Asia Classic Car Challenge in 2014 and currently competes in the TCR Asia Series.

trgt: Dan Erickson (born January 26, 1984) is an American television screenwriter, showrunner, and producer. He created the thriller television show Severance, starring Adam Scott and directed by Ben Stiller, which premiered on the Apple TV+ streaming service in February 2022.
mgt: Dan Erickson (born January 26, 1984) is an American television screenwriter, producer, and showrunner, best known as the creator of the critically acclaimed Apple TV+ series *Severance* (2022). Originally from Olympia, Washington, Erickson's pilot script for *Severance* gained recognition in 2016 and was later developed by Ben Stiller's Red Hour Productions. The series premiered to widespread acclaim, earning Erickson a spot on the 2022 *TIME100 Next* list, which highlights emerging global leaders.

trgt: Tyrone Charles Kirk (born 2 January 1984, in Scunthorpe) is an English footballer.
mgt: Tyrone Kirk (born 2 January 1984) is an English former midfielder who played for clubs including Stamford, Macclesfield Town, and Rhyl.

trgt: Workpoint TV is Thailand's television station, owned by Workpoint Entertainment, broadcasting on channel 23 of Thailand's digital television.
mgt: Workpoint TV is a Thai television network owned by Workpoint Entertainment, known for game shows, news, and entertainment.

trgt: The New Philharmonic Society was a British music society, established in 1852, giving annual series of subscription concerts of orchestral music in London until 1879. The concerts in the first season were conducted by Hector Berlioz.
mgt: The New Philharmonic Society was a London-based concert organization founded in 1852, with Hector Berlioz as its inaugural conductor. Known for its large orchestra and innovative programming, it operated until 1879.

trgt: 1. FC Kaan-Marienborn is a German association football club based in Siegen, that competes in Regionalliga West.
mgt: 1. FC Kaan-Marienborn is a German football club founded in 2007, currently competing in the Regionalliga West.

trgt: Another Mind (アナザー・マインド, Anazā Maindo) is an adventure game created by Square for the PlayStation and released on November 12, 1998, in Japan. The game is presented as live-action full-motion video sequences.
mgt: *Another Mind* is a 1998 adventure video game developed and published by Square for the PlayStation. The game features a unique narrative where the player acts as a separate consciousness within the protagonist, Hitomi Hayama, influencing her actions through constructed dialogue. Combining still photographs, live-action cutscenes, and voice acting, *Another Mind* explores themes of ego and death as Hitomi navigates a mystery involving murder and other crises. Directed by Keizo Kokubo, the game was part of Square's experimental initiative in the late 1990s. Its soundtrack, composed by Junya Nakano, received praise, though the game itself garnered mixed reviews for its pacing and unconventional mechanics.

trgt: Lawrence Sidbury, Jr. (born February 6, 1986) is a former American football defensive end. He played college football at Richmond and was drafted by the Atlanta Falcons in the fourth round of the 2009 NFL Draft.
mgt: Lawrence Sidbury Jr. (born February 6, 1986) is a former American football defensive end who played in the NFL from 2009 to 2015. A standout at the University of Richmond, where he helped win the 2008 FCS National Championship, Sidbury was selected by the Atlanta Falcons in the fourth round of the 2009 NFL Draft. He also played for the Indianapolis Colts, Houston Texans, and Tampa Bay Buccaneers during his professional career. Known for his speed and pass-rushing ability, Sidbury recorded notable achievements, including a touchdown during his rookie season.

trgt: Pottawattamie Park is a town in Michigan Township, LaPorte County, Indiana, United States. The population was 235 at the 2010 census. It is included in the Michigan City, Indiana-La Porte, Indiana Metropolitan Statistical Area.
mgt: Pottawattamie Park is a small town in LaPorte County, Indiana, United States. Incorporated in 1936 on land once inhabited by the Potawatomi people, it spans 0.25 square miles and is entirely surrounded by Michigan City. As of the 2020 census, the town had a population of 242, with a predominantly residential character and a median age of 53.1 years.

trgt: Gustavo Osvaldo Parraguez Galarce (born 24 May 1943) is the municipal secretary of Pichilemu, and was the interim Mayor of the commune of Pichilemu three different times, in 1992, 2007, and 2008.
mgt: Gustavo Parraguez Galarce (born May 24, 1943) is a Chilean civil servant and politician who served three terms as interim mayor of Pichilemu, including the shortest tenure in the commune's history.

trgt: Matthew Kauffman (born October 5, 1961 in Princeton, New Jersey) is an American investigative journalist and two-time Pulitzer Prize finalist.
mgt: Matthew Kauffman is an American investigative journalist known for his award-winning reporting at the *Hartford Courant*, including uncovering military and institutional scandals.

trgt:
A Massachusetts general election was held on November 2, 1982 in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.

The election included:

Democratic and Republican candidates were selected in party primaries held September 14, 1982.
mgt: The 1982 Massachusetts elections, part of the broader United States elections, were held on November 2, 1982. Key outcomes included the election of Democrats Michael Dukakis as Governor and John Kerry as Lieutenant Governor, alongside victories for incumbents in several statewide offices. Voters also decided on four ballot questions, addressing issues such as public funding for private schools, the death penalty, nuclear power regulations, and a beverage container deposit law.

trgt: The Fine Arts Department (Lang-th, RTGS: Krom Sinlapakon) is a government department of Thailand, under the Ministry of Culture. Its mission is managing the country's cultural heritage.
mgt: The Fine Arts Department (กรมศิลปากร) is a Thai government agency under the Ministry of Culture, established in 1912. It manages archaeological sites, national museums, archives, and cultural heritage.

trgt: "Radar's Report" was the 27th episode of M*A*S*H, and third of season two. The episode aired on September 29, 1973.
mgt: "Radar's Report" is the third episode of the second season of *M*A*S*H*, originally airing on September 29, 1973. The episode, narrated through Radar's comical voice-over report, intertwines storylines involving Hawkeye's romantic interest in a nurse, Trapper's moral conflict with an enemy soldier, and Klinger’s psychiatric evaluation by Dr. Freedman, marking the character's debut.

trgt: Goudji (born July 6, 1941) is a French sculptor and goldsmith, who was born in Georgia.
mgt: Goudji is a Georgian-born French sculptor and goldsmith known for his unique metalwork and liturgical creations.

trgt: al-Majd (Lang-ar) is a Palestinian village located eighteen kilometers south-west of Hebron. The village is in the Hebron Governorate Southern West Bank.
mgt: Al-Majd (Arabic: خربة المجد) is a Palestinian village in the Hebron Governorate, located in the southern West Bank. Governed by a village council, it had a population of 1,925 in 2007. The village has historical significance, with evidence of Byzantine-era ceramics and remnants of a ruined chapel. During the Ottoman era, it was noted for its caves, cisterns, and pillar shafts. Following the 1948 Arab–Israeli War, Al-Majd came under Jordanian rule until the Six-Day War in 1967, after which it has been under Israeli occupation.

trgt: Konstantin Ivanov Slavchev (Lang-bg) also known as Konstantin (Lang-bg) is a Bulgarian pop-folk singer. He was the winner of the Bulgarian reality show VIP Brother 1 in 2006.
mgt: Konstantin Ivanov Slavchev (born 17 July 1976), known mononymously as Konstantin, is a Bulgarian pop-folk singer. Active since 1999, he gained fame with his hit debut song "Черна роза."

trgt: Louis of Brienne, also known as Louis of Acre (died in 1297), was viscount of Beaumont-sur-Sarthe in Maine, France.
mgt: Louis of Brienne, also known as Louis of Acre, was the second son of John of Brienne and Berengaria of León. Through his marriage to Agnes of Beaumont, he became the Viscount of Beaumont-sur-Sarthe and a vassal of Charles of Anjou. Raised at the French royal court, he participated in King Louis IX's crusade against Egypt and maintained close ties with the Castilian royal court.

trgt: Aurélien Bayard Chedjou Fongang (IPA-fr; born 20 June 1985) is a Cameroonian former professional footballer who played as a centre back for LOSC Lille, Galatasaray, Bursaspor, Adana Demirspor and the Cameroon national team.
mgt: Aurélien Bayard Chedjou Fongang (born 20 June 1985) is a retired Cameroonian professional footballer who played as a centre back. He had a successful club career, notably with Lille, where he won the Ligue 1 title and Coupe de France in 2011, and Galatasaray, where he secured multiple domestic trophies, including the Süper Lig title in 2015. Chedjou also represented the Cameroon national team, participating in the 2010 Africa Cup of Nations and the 2014 FIFA World Cup.

trgt: Scrabble variants are games created by changing the normal Scrabble rules or equipment.
mgt: Scrabble variants are alternative versions of the classic word game Scrabble, featuring modified rules, gameplay mechanics, or formats. These include fast-paced games like Anagrab and Speed Scrabble, mathematical adaptations like Equate and Numble, and unique formats such as Clabbers, Duplicate Scrabble, and Super Scrabble. Variants like Bananagrams and UpWords have also gained commercial popularity.

trgt: Kaintuck' (Kentucky) is a 1935 symphonic poem for piano and orchestra by American composer William Grant Still.
mgt: *Kaintuck'* is a symphonic poem by William Grant Still, inspired by a train trip to Kentucky and featuring impressionist harmonies.

trgt: "Maximum Carnage" is a 14-part comic book crossover published in Marvel Comics'Spider-Man family of titles in 1993. It featured Spider-Man, Venom, and a host of other superheroes teaming up to face Venom's murderous offspring Carnage and his team of supervillains.
mgt: "Maximum Carnage" is a 1993 Marvel Comics crossover storyline spanning 14 issues across multiple Spider-Man titles. Written by Tom DeFalco, J.M. DeMatteis, Terry Kavanagh, and David Michelinie, it follows Spider-Man and allies as they battle Carnage and his villainous gang during a violent rampage through New York City.

trgt: John Hull Campbell (October 10, 1800 – January 19, 1868) was an American politician from Pennsylvania who served as an American Party member in the U.S. House of Representatives for Pennsylvania's 3rd congressional district from 1845 to 1847.
mgt: John Hull Campbell (October 10, 1800 – January 19, 1868) was an American lawyer and politician who served as a U.S. Representative from Pennsylvania's 3rd district from 1845 to 1847. A member of the American Party, he also served in the Pennsylvania House of Representatives in 1831. After declining renomination to Congress, he resumed his law practice in Philadelphia, where he lived until his death.

trgt: Space Under Sun is the second album by American singer, Ari Gold.
mgt: *Space Under Sun* is Ari Gold's 2004 genre-blending R&B and pop album.

trgt: The Brass era Reliance automobile was manufactured by the Reliance Automobile Manufacturing Company in Detroit, Michigan from 1904 to 1907.
mgt: Reliance was an American automobile manufacturer founded in 1904 in Detroit, Michigan, producing cars and trucks before its 1909 acquisition by General Motors.

trgt: Hristo Chernopeev (Lang-bg) (1868, Dermantsi – 6 November 1915, Krivolak) was a Bulgarian Army officer and member of the revolutionary movement in Macedonia. He was among the leaders of the Bulgarian People's Macedonian-Adrianople Revolutionary Organization.
mgt: Hristo Chernopeev (c. 1868 – November 6, 1915) was a Bulgarian military officer, revolutionary, and politician. A key figure in the Macedonian liberation movement, he participated in the Ilinden Uprising, Balkan Wars, and World War I.

trgt: Steve White (born 2 January 1959 in Chipping Sodbury) is a retired professional football forward and manager.
mgt: Stephen James White (born 2 January 1959) is an English former footballer and manager, best known for his prolific goal-scoring career as a forward with clubs including Swindon Town, Hereford United, and Cardiff City.

trgt: What Women Suffer is a 1911 Australian silent film directed by Alfred Rolfe. It is a Victorian melodrama, complete with a climax where a little child is placed on a moving saw bench and is considered a lost film.
mgt: *What Women Suffer* is a 1911 Australian silent film directed by Alfred Rolfe, based on a melodramatic play by H. G. Brandon. The film follows a tale of betrayal, murder, and redemption, culminating in a dramatic rescue. Produced by the Australian Photo-Play Company, it premiered on October 2, 1911, at Melbourne's Lyric Theatre and was praised for its cinematography and performances. The film was a commercial success, screening across Australia.

trgt: Ruth Cameron is an American record producer and jazz vocalist.
mgt: Ruth Cameron was a singer, producer, and Charlie Haden's manager.

trgt: Maestrale was the lead ship of her class of four destroyers built for the Regia Marina (Royal Italian Navy) in the early 1930s. Completed in 1934, she served in World War II.
mgt: The Italian destroyer *Maestrale* was the lead ship of the Maestrale-class destroyers, built for the Royal Italian Navy in the early 1930s. Designed to address stability issues of earlier classes, it featured improved armament, speed, and range. Launched in 1934, *Maestrale* served during World War II before being sabotaged and sunk on September 9, 1943.

trgt: General Applied Science Laboratory (GASL) is an American aerospace company, known as a pioneer of hypersonic propulsion.
mgt: General Applied Science Laboratory (GASL), founded in 1956 by Antonio Ferri, specializes in advanced propulsion systems and hypersonic research.

page_title: Crossing the Red Sea with The Adverts
trgt: Crossing the Red Sea with The Adverts is the debut studio album by English punk rock band the Adverts. It was released on 17 February 1978 by record label Bright.
mgt: *Crossing the Red Sea with The Adverts* is the 1978 debut studio album by English punk rock band The Adverts. Recorded at Abbey Road Studios, it received critical acclaim for its raw energy and is considered a landmark of the punk era. The album, featuring the single "No Time to Be 21," reached No. 38 on the UK Albums Chart and has been praised as one of the finest albums of the 1970s. It was later ranked No. 17 in *Mojo* magazine's list of the 50 greatest punk albums and included in *The Guardian*'s "1000 Albums to Hear Before You Die."

trgt: Yuda (湯田町, Yuda-machi) was a town located in Waga District, Iwate Prefecture, Japan.
mgt: Yuda (湯田町) was a town in Iwate Prefecture, Japan, merged into Nishiwaga in 2005.

trgt: Andrea Stinson (born November 25, 1967) is a retired professional basketball player from the United States, playing from 1998 to 2005 for the Charlotte Sting and the Detroit Shock.
mgt: Andrea Stinson (born November 25, 1967) is an American former professional basketball player and three-time WNBA All-Star. She played for the Charlotte Sting and Detroit Shock, earning accolades such as ACC Player of the Year (1990) and having her No. 32 jersey retired by both NC State and the Charlotte Sting. Stinson also represented the United States in international competitions, winning a bronze medal at the 1991 Pan American Games and a gold medal at the 1992 William Jones Cup. Beyond her playing career, she has participated in the U.S. Department of State's Sports Envoy program, promoting cultural understanding through basketball clinics worldwide.

trgt: Stockland Bristol (formerly Stockland Gaunts) is a village and civil parish in the Sedgemoor district of Somerset, between Bridgwater and the Steart Peninsula.
mgt: Stockland Bristol is a small village and civil parish in Somerset, England, with a population of 165 (2011). Historically recorded in the Domesday Book, it was part of the hundred of Cannington and later owned by Bristol Corporation. The village lies near the River Parrett and has faced significant flooding over the centuries. During World War II, it hosted wireless direction-finding stations. Local governance is managed by a parish council, Sedgemoor District, and Somerset County Council. The village's church, rebuilt in 1865, retains elements of the original structure.

trgt: John Paton was a Scottish soldier and Covenanter. He was executed at the Grassmarket on 9 May 1684 largely for his actions at the Battle of Bothwell Bridge.
mgt: John Paton (died 9 May 1684) was a Scottish Covenanter and military officer. Born in Fenwick, Ayrshire, he fought in several battles, including Marston Moor and Bothwell Bridge. Captured in 1684, he was executed in Edinburgh for his Covenanting activities and is commemorated with a monument in Fenwick Churchyard.

trgt: The Merchant Street Historic District in Honolulu, Hawaii, was the city's earliest commercial center.
mgt: The Merchant Street Historic District in Honolulu, Hawaii, is a seven-acre area showcasing architectural styles from the 1850s to the 1930s, including Italianate, Richardsonian Romanesque, and Mission Revival. Added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1973, it features notable buildings such as the Kamehameha V Post Office, Bishop Bank, and Judd Building, reflecting the city's commercial development.

trgt: Gaillac AOC is an Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée (AOC) in South West France in the département of Tarn, just north of Toulouse.
mgt: Gaillac AOC is a historic wine region in southwestern France, known for its ancient viticulture, diverse wine production, and renowned wineries.

trgt: Francisco Fervenza Fernández (Santander, Spain, August 15, 1907– Valencia, January 13, 1998) was a Cantabrian anarcho-syndicalist.
mgt: Francisco Fervenza Fernández (1907–1998) was a Spanish trade unionist, military commander, and Spanish Civil War participant.

trgt: Oliver Bäte (born 1 March 1965) is a German business executive who is the CEO of Allianz, a position he has held since October 2014.
mgt: Oliver Bäte is a German business executive and CEO of Allianz, having previously held roles as COO and CFO. He joined Allianz's board in 2008.

trgt: Sokratis Kokkalis (Greek: Σωκράτης Κόκκαλης; 1939) is a Greek businessman. His father, Petros Kokkalis, was a communist politician and Greek Resistance member, living in exile in East Germany after the end of the Greek Civil War.
mgt: Sokratis Kokkalis (born May 27, 1939) is a Greek businessman and philanthropist, best known as the founder of Intracom Holdings and Intralot, major multinational technology and gaming companies. He played a significant role in modernizing Greece's telecommunications infrastructure and expanding his businesses internationally. Kokkalis also owned Olympiacos FC and Olympiacos BC, leading both to numerous championships. He established the Kokkalis Foundation, promoting education, culture, and social welfare. His career has included controversies, particularly allegations of bribery and ties to East Germany, though investigations found no conclusive evidence.

trgt: Marcia Van Dyke (March 26, 1922 – November 11, 2002) was an American violinist and actress. She was featured in a cover story in the January 19, 1948, issue of Life magazine.
mgt: Marcia Evelyn Van Dyke (March 26, 1922 – November 11, 2002) was an American violinist, actress, and Broadway performer. A member of the San Francisco Symphony, she gained fame after being featured in *Life* magazine in 1947, which led to a brief Hollywood career with films such as *A Date with Judy* (1948) and *In the Good Old Summertime* (1949). She also earned acclaim on Broadway, winning a Theatre World Award for her role in *A Tree Grows in Brooklyn* (1951). Later in life, she returned to music, performing as a session musician for prominent artists.

trgt: Kate Elizabeth Russell (born 1984) is an American author. Her debut novel, My Dark Vanessa, was published in 2020 and became a national bestseller.
mgt: Kate Elizabeth Russell is an American author best known for her debut novel, *My Dark Vanessa* (2020), a national bestseller exploring themes of sexual abuse, trauma, and memory.

trgt: John Duncan (19 December 1794 – 9 August 1881) was a Scottish weaver and botanist.
mgt: John Duncan (1794–1881) was a self-taught Scottish botanist and weaver known for his extensive herbarium, which he donated to Aberdeen University. Despite living in poverty, he pursued botany, astronomy, and education, contributing significantly to the study of British flora.

trgt: The gens Tettidia, occasionally found as Tettiedia, was an obscure plebeian family at ancient Rome. No members of this gens are mentioned by Roman writers, but several are known from inscriptions.
mgt: The Tettidia gens was a Roman family of Oscan origin, likely derived from the nomen Tettius. Most inscriptions of the gens are from Samnium, and they used various common praenomina.

trgt: Maria Clara Eimmart (27 May 1676 – 29 October 1707), was a German astronomer, engraver and designer. She was the daughter and assistant of Georg Christoph Eimmart the Younger.
mgt: Maria Clara Eimmart (1676–1707) was a German astronomer and artist known for her detailed astronomical illustrations, including lunar phases and solar eclipses. Trained by her father, Georg Christoph Eimmart, she contributed significantly to early astronomical observations and depictions.

trgt: Michelle Stephenson (born 3 January 1977) is a British pop singer and television presenter. At the age of 17, she was briefly a member of the girl group that would go on to become the Spice Girls.
mgt: Michelle Stephenson (born 3 January 1977) is an English singer and television presenter, best known as an original member of the girl group Touch, which later became the Spice Girls. She left the group in 1994 after a brief tenure, citing personal reasons, including her mother's illness, though other accounts suggest differing explanations. Following her departure, Stephenson pursued a career in television presenting and music, working as a session singer and releasing an EP titled *SHESongs* in 2003.

trgt: Fernanda Frances Arribas (1862−1939) was a Spanish painter. She is known for still lifes and flower paintings. She taught at the Escuela de Artes y Oficinos in Madrid, and at the Escuela del Hogar in Madrid.
mgt: Fernanda Frances Arribas (26 February 1862 – 21 April 1939) was a Spanish still-life painter and educator. She exhibited internationally, including at the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition, and was the first woman in a Pinelo exhibition.

trgt: Olwen Williams OBE is a Consultant Physician in Genitourinary/HIV Medicine based at Betsi Cadwaladr University Health Board, North Wales. She was appointed an OBE for services to medicine in Wales in 2005. and Welsh Woman of the Year in 2000.
mgt: Olwen Williams is a Welsh physician and healthcare leader, currently serving as the Royal College of Physicians' Vice President for Wales. A Fellow of the Learned Society of Wales, she has held prominent roles in medical organizations and advised the Welsh Government.

trgt: James J. Carrigan (born April 25, 1941) is an American attorney and politician who served in the Massachusetts General Court.
mgt: James J. Carrigan (born April 25, 1941) is an American attorney, teacher, and Democratic politician who served in the Massachusetts legislature.

trgt:
The Sabre Wildcat is one of a series of American two-seat ultralight trikes that was designed by Richard Helm and produced by Sabre Aircraft. The aircraft were all supplied complete and ready-to-fly.

Sabre Aircraft ceased operations in 2008.
mgt: The Sabre Wildcat is an ultralight aircraft featuring a cable-braced high-wing, weight-shift controls, and a tandem two-seat open cockpit. Designed for affordability, its Elite model was the least expensive two-seat aircraft in 2000.

trgt: Dame Thea King DBE FRCM FGSM (26 December 1925 – 26 June 2007) was a British clarinettist.
mgt: Dame Thea King DBE FRCM FGSM (1925–2007) was a renowned English clarinettist, teacher, and chamber musician.

trgt: Jasaw Chan Kʼawiil I also known as Ruler A, Ah Cacao and Sky Rain, (before 682-734), was an ajaw of the Maya city of Tikal. He took the throne on May 3, 682 and reigned until his death.
mgt: Jasaw Chan Kʼawiil I (died 734) was an ajaw of the Maya city of Tikal, reigning from 682 to 734. Known for his military victory over Calakmul in 695, his reign marked Tikal's resurgence after a prolonged decline. He was buried in Tikal Temple I, a prominent stepped pyramid, while Tikal Temple II served as the tomb for his wife, Lady Lahan Unen Moʼ. His son, Yikʼin Chan Kʼawiil, succeeded him.

trgt: Helmut Zilk (9 June 1927, Vienna – 24 October 2008, Vienna) was an Austrian journalist and politician in the Austrian Social Democratic Party. He served as mayor of Vienna between 1984 and 1994.
mgt: Helmut Zilk (1927–2008) was an Austrian politician and journalist who served as Mayor of Vienna from 1984 to 1994. His career was marked by a letter bomb attack and allegations of collaboration with the Czechoslovak secret police.

trgt: PKP class Pm36 is a class of two express passenger (P) 4-6-2 (m) steam locomotives ordered in 1936 for the Polskie Koleje Państwowe (Polish State Railways).
mgt: The PKP class Pm36 is a Polish steam locomotive designed in 1936 by Kazimierz Zembrzuski. Two prototypes were built, one streamlined (Pm36-1) and one standard (Pm36-2). The streamlined Pm36-1 won a gold medal at the 1937 International Exposition in Paris. During World War II, both locomotives were incorporated into the German Deutsche Reichsbahn fleet, with Pm36-1 later scrapped and Pm36-2 returned to Poland post-war. Pm36-2, nicknamed "Beautiful Helen" (Piękna Helena), now serves as a tourist attraction in Wolsztyn.

trgt: Persia is a town in Cattaraugus County, New York, United States. The population was 2,203 at the 2020 census. It is in the northwest part of the county. The village of Gowanda is partially in the town.
mgt: Persia is a town in Cattaraugus County, New York, United States, with a population of 2,203 as of the 2020 census. Established in 1835, it encompasses 21 square miles and includes the village of Gowanda. The town's geography features Cattaraugus Creek and is traversed by U.S. Route 62 and New York State Route 353. Persia hosts annual events such as the Hollywood Happening motorcycle festival in June and a Christmas tree lighting in December.

trgt: Clifford Burke Harmon (July 1, 1866 – June 25, 1945) was a wealthy American real estate developer and founding partner of Wood, Harmon & Co., as well as a noted early amateur aviator.
mgt: Clifford B. Harmon (July 1, 1866 – June 25, 1945) was an American real estate developer, amateur aviator, and aviation advocate. He co-founded Wood, Harmon & Co., pioneering installment-based homeownership, and later developed properties in New York and beyond. Harmon was an early aviation enthusiast, setting records in ballooning and airplane flight, including being the first to fly across Long Island Sound in 1910. He established the Harmon Trophy to honor aviation achievements and advocated for international aviation cooperation. Harmon spent his later years in France, where he died in 1945.

trgt: Fernando Pérez Pascal (born October 3, 1964) is a Mexican former professional tennis player.
mgt: Fernando Pérez Pascal (born October 3, 1964) is a former Mexican professional tennis player, junior doubles world No. 1, and Davis Cup competitor.

page_title: Medical University of South Carolina College of Dental Medicine
trgt: The College of Dental Medicine is the dental school of the Medical University of South Carolina. It is located in the city of Charleston, South Carolina, United States. It is the only dental school in South Carolina.
mgt: The Medical University of South Carolina College of Dental Medicine, established in 1953, is a public dental school in Charleston, South Carolina. Renamed the James B. Edwards College of Dental Medicine in 2010, it features state-of-the-art facilities, competitive admissions, and a strong clinical reputation, with students excelling in national board exams and residency placements.

trgt: Paul Speratus (13 December 1484 – 12 August 1551) was a Swabian Catholic priest who became a Protestant preacher, reformer and hymn-writer. In 1523, he helped Martin Luther to create the First Lutheran hymnal, published in 1524 and called Achtliederbuch.
mgt: Paul Speratus (13 December 1484 – 12 August 1551) was a German Lutheran minister, hymn writer, and a key figure in the early Reformation. Originally a Roman Catholic priest, he became a follower of Martin Luther and contributed to the first Lutheran hymnal, the *Achtliederbuch* (1524). Speratus is best known for his hymn *Es ist das Heil uns kommen her*, which emphasizes salvation by faith alone. After facing persecution and excommunication, he served as court chaplain to Albert, Duke of Prussia, and later as the Protestant bishop of Pomesania, where he defended Lutheran theology until his death.

trgt: The Schwaben Cup (German: Schwäbischer Pokal) was a domestic cup competition in the Bavarian Regierungsbezirk of Swabia (German: Schwaben), played from 1947 to 2009.
mgt: The Schwaben Cup was a regional football competition in Bavaria, Germany, held annually from 1947 to 2009. It served as a qualifying tournament for the DFB-Pokal and later the Bavarian Cup. Open to non-professional teams from the Schwaben region, the competition featured a unique format favoring lower-tier clubs with home advantage. The cup ceased in 2009 with the expansion of the Bavarian Cup, leaving only Kreis Cup competitions as its legacy.

trgt: Saint-Denis–Porte de Paris (IPA-fr) is a station on Line 13 of the Paris Métro in the commune of Saint-Denis.
mgt: Saint-Denis–Porte de Paris is a Paris Métro station in Saint-Denis, serving the Stade de France and nearby landmarks.

trgt: Live in Boston (a.k.a. Boston Live and Jumping at Shadows) is a live album by British blues-rock band Fleetwood Mac that was first released in 1985.
mgt: *Live in Boston* is a live album by Fleetwood Mac, recorded in February 1970 at the Boston Tea Party venue and released in February 1985. Initially shelved, the recordings were later reissued under various titles, including *Jumping at Shadows* and *Boston Live*. In 1998, Snapper Music released a comprehensive three-volume set, later reissued as *Live at the Boston Tea Party* and repackaged in 2013 as *Boston*.

trgt: Howard Minion Radzely (born 1970) is an American lawyer who served as the United States Deputy Secretary of Labor from December 19, 2007 through February 2, 2009.
mgt: Howard M. Radzely (born 1970) is an American attorney who served as Acting United States Secretary of Labor in 2009 under President Barack Obama. He previously held roles as Deputy Secretary and Solicitor of Labor during the George W. Bush administration. A graduate of the University of Pennsylvania and Harvard Law School, Radzely clerked for prominent judges before entering private practice and later joining the Department of Labor. He is currently a Senior Vice President and Assistant General Counsel at The Boeing Company.

trgt: HMS Rover was a 16-gun sloop-of-war that the Royal Navy purchased in 1796, commissioned in 1798, and that was wrecked in early 1798. In her brief career she captured one French privateer.
mgt: HMS *Rover* was a 16-gun sloop-of-war of the Royal Navy, launched in 1796 and wrecked in the Gulf of St. Lawrence in 1798. Designed by Goodrich & Co. and built in Bermuda, she was initially planned as a brig but was lengthened and rigged as a full ship during construction. Commissioned in 1798 under Commander George Irwin, *Rover* captured the French privateer *Jean Bart* shortly before her loss. She was wrecked off Cape Breton Island while convoying a transport, with all crew surviving.

trgt: George Henson Estes Jr. (January 30, 1873 – July 2, 1969) was a United States Army officer in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
mgt: George Henson Estes Jr. (January 30, 1873 – July 2, 1969) was a United States Army brigadier general who served in the Spanish–American and Philippine–American Wars.

trgt: Lilian Mary Elizabeth Wyles (31 August 1885 – 13 May 1975) was an English female police officers who was among the first officers to take statements from female and juvenile assault victims, rather than relying on "assistants".
mgt: Lilian Mary Elizabeth Wyles (31 August 1885 – 13 May 1975) was a pioneering British police officer and author. She was one of the first women in the Metropolitan Police to gain attested status, enabling her to make arrests, and played a key role in establishing the practice of women officers taking statements in cases involving sexual assault. Promoted to chief inspector in 1932, Wyles served for 30 years and later authored her memoir, *A Woman at Scotland Yard*.

trgt: Lady Juliana (also known as the Lady Julian), was launched at Whitby in 1777. She transported convicts in 1789 from England to Australia.
mgt: The *Lady Juliana* was a British barque launched in 1777 or 1778, best known for transporting female convicts to Australia in 1789–1790. Chartered by the British government, the ship carried 226 female convicts, earning a reputation as a "floating brothel" due to the behavior of its crew and passengers. Despite the voyage's slow pace, the low death rate was attributed to the care of Lieutenant Thomas Edgar and surgeon Richard Alley. *Lady Juliana* was the first ship to arrive at Port Jackson after the First Fleet, though its arrival with convicts instead of provisions disappointed the struggling colony. Afterward, the ship sailed to China and later traded in the Baltic, last listed in 1829.

trgt: Europe – Simone Veil (IPA-fr) is a station on Line 3 of the Paris Métro located in the 8th arrondissement of Paris.
mgt: Europe is a station on Line 3 of the Paris Métro, located in the 8th arrondissement of Paris. Opened on 19 October 1904, it is named after Place de l'Europe, a square from which streets named for European capitals radiate. The station underwent renovations in 2000 and was culturally developed for the metro's centenary. In 2018, it was renamed Europe - Simone-Veil to honor Simone Veil, a prominent French politician and Holocaust survivor. The station features a Guimard entrance, designated a historical monument, and is served by several RATP bus and Noctilien night bus lines.

trgt: Web Therapy is an improvised online series starring Lisa Kudrow as Fiona Wallice, a therapist who has conceived of a new form of therapy, the titular "web therapy". The series debuted on LStudio.com on September 22, 2008.
mgt: *Web Therapy* is an American dark comedy web series created by and starring Lisa Kudrow. Premiering on LStudio.com in 2008, the show follows Fiona Wallice, a self-interested therapist who conducts brief therapy sessions via webcam. The series humorously explores her unconventional methods and personal flaws. It was later adapted into a television series on Showtime, running for four seasons from 2011 to 2015, and featured numerous guest stars, including Meryl Streep, Julia Louis-Dreyfus, and Courteney Cox.

trgt: Coventry RLFC was a semi-professional rugby league team based in Coventry, West Midlands, England that played in the Northern Rugby Football Union League in the 1910–11, 1911–12, and 1912–13 seasons.
mgt: Coventry RLFC was a rugby league club formed in 1910, transitioning from rugby union. It competed in the Northern Union until folding after the 1912–13 season due to financial difficulties.

trgt: Hamiota Municipality is an incorporated municipality in the Canadian province of Manitoba.
mgt: Hamiota Municipality, located in Manitoba, Canada, was incorporated on January 1, 2015.

trgt: Benjamin Hoffman (April 15, 1864 – May 20, 1922) was a Jewish-American lawyer, politician, and judge from New York.
mgt: Benjamin Hoffman (April 15, 1864 – May 20, 1922) was an American lawyer, politician, and judge. A Democrat and Tammany Hall member, he served in the New York State Assembly from 1895 to 1899 and as a Municipal Court Justice until his death.

trgt: Benton Township is one of ten townships in Adair County, Missouri, United States. As of the 2010 census, its population was 19,700. It is named for Thomas Hart Benton, one of the inaugural pair of U.S. senators from Missouri.
mgt: Benton Township is a township in Adair County, Missouri, covering 80.26 square miles (207.9 km²) with a population of 19,700 as of 2010. It includes the county seat, Kirksville, and is home to several cemeteries, streams, and educational institutions, including A.T. Still University and Truman State University. Major highways and two heliports serve the area.

trgt: "I'd Go to Jail" is a song written and recorded by Canadian country artist Dean Brody. The song was released to radio in October 2021, as the fifth single from Brody's seventh studio album Boys.
mgt: "I'd Go to Jail" is a 2021 country single by Canadian artist Dean Brody from his album *Boys*. Written by Brody and inspired by his experiences as a father, the song explores themes of love and protection. Critics praised its emotional storytelling and depiction of a father-daughter bond. The track peaked at number 73 on the Canadian Hot 100 and number five on the Billboard Canada Country chart, becoming Brody's thirty-first top ten hit.

trgt: Republic Township is a civil township of Marquette County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 1,060 at the 2010 census. The unincorporated community of Republic is located within the township.
mgt: Republic Township is a civil township in Marquette County, Michigan, United States. Covering 119.54 square miles, it had a population of 1,060 as of the 2010 census. The township includes notable features such as the Old M-95 – Michigamme River Bridge, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1999 for its historical and technological significance.

trgt: Félix Jules Méline (IPA-fr; 20 May 1838 – 21 December 1925) was a French statesman, Prime Minister of France from 1896 to 1898.
mgt: Jules Méline (20 May 1838 – 21 December 1925) was a French politician who served as Prime Minister from 1896 to 1898. A prominent advocate of protectionism, he played a key role in shaping French protectionist policies, including the Méline tariff of 1892. Méline also served as Minister for Agriculture, President of the Chamber of Deputies, and editor of *La République française*. After retiring, he authored works promoting agriculture, including *Le Retour de la terre et la surproduction industrielle* (1905).

trgt: The history of the Jews in South Africa has been marked by periods of official and unofficial antisemitism.
mgt: Antisemitism in South Africa has spanned various historical periods, including the apartheid era, with influences from Nazi ideology, discriminatory laws, and far-right movements.

trgt: Amaan Reza (Lang-bn; born 20 May 1986) is a Bangladeshi film actor.
mgt: Amaan Reza (born May 20, 1986) is a Bangladeshi actor and entrepreneur.

trgt: Tome VI is an album by composer and bandleader Gil Mellé and the Jazz Electronauts recorded in 1968 and released on the Verve label.
mgt: *Tome VI* is a 1968 jazz album by Gil Mellé, notable for being described as "the first album of electronic jazz." It features Mellé's experimentation with live electronic instruments alongside modal and free jazz styles.

trgt: August Sang (27 July 1914 – 14 October 1969) was an Estonian poet and literary translator.
mgt: August Sang (27 July 1914 – 14 October 1969) was an Estonian poet, literary translator, and member of the Arbujad literary circle.

trgt: Roy Oxlade (13 January 1929 – 15 February 2014) was an English painter, writer on art, and an art educator.
mgt: Roy Oxlade (13 January 1929 – 15 February 2014) was an English painter, writer, and educator, known for his metaphorical modernist approach to art. A student of David Bomberg, he studied at Bromley School of Art, Goldsmiths, and the Royal College of Art, where he earned a PhD. Oxlade's work, which often explored the imaginative and metaphorical potential of painting, received critical acclaim posthumously, including a 2018 solo exhibition and the inaugural exhibition at Hastings Contemporary in 2019. He was married to fellow artist Rose Wylie, with whom he had three children.

trgt: Urera is a genus of flowering plants in the nettle family, Urticaceae. It has a pantropical distribution.
mgt: Urera is a genus of lianas, shrubs, and small trees in the family Urticaceae, characterized by stinging hairs, variable leaves, and fleshy fruits. First described in 1826 by Charles Gaudichaud-Beaupré, the genus includes dioecious and monoecious species with diverse floral structures. Its taxonomy remains unclear, with estimates of valid species ranging from 17 to 75.

trgt: Stephen Soter Ortynsky Hentosh, O.S.B.M. (Lang-uk; 29 January 1866 – 24 March 1916) was the first Bishop of all Greek Catholics in the United States.
mgt: Soter Ortynsky (1866–1916) was the first bishop of the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church in the United States, known for establishing orphanages, a cathedral, and fostering church independence.

trgt: James Edgar Atwell (3 June 1946 – 12 December 2020) was an English priest who was Dean of Winchester.
mgt: James Atwell (3 June 1946 – 12 December 2020) was a British Anglican priest who served as Dean of Winchester (2006–2016) and previously as Dean of St Edmundsbury.

trgt: William Horsell (31 March 1807 – 23 December 1863) was an English hydrotherapist, publisher, and temperance and vegetarianism activist. Horsell published the first vegan cookbook in 1849.
mgt: William Horsell (31 March 1807 – 23 December 1863) was an English hydrotherapist, publisher, and activist for temperance and vegetarianism. He operated Britain’s first vegetarian hospital, co-founded the Vegetarian Society in 1847, and served as its secretary. Horsell edited several publications, including *The Truth Tester* and *The Journal of Health & Phrenological Magazine*, and published vegetarian and spiritualist literature. He also promoted phrenology and authored a hydropathic manual. Horsell died in 1863 during an anti-slavery mission to Nigeria.

trgt: Stefan Jackiw (/stɛˈfɑːn dʒæˈkiːv/; born 1985) is an American classical violinist.
mgt:
Stefan Jackiw is an American violinist known for his technical mastery and expressive performances. Born in Boston, Massachusetts, to parents who were prominent physics professors, Jackiw began playing the violin at age four and studied under renowned teachers, including Zinaida Gilels and Michèle Auclair. He attended The Roxbury Latin School and later graduated from Harvard University with a degree in music, while also earning an Artist Diploma from the New England Conservatory. 

Jackiw made his professional debut at age 12 with the Boston Pops and has since performed with major orchestras worldwide, including the Chicago Symphony Orchestra, New York Philharmonic, and Philharmonia Orchestra in London. He is also an active chamber musician and festival performer. Jackiw plays a 1704 Vincenzo Rugeri violin and has received numerous honors throughout his career. He currently resides in New York City.

trgt: Nicholas Nichols Cox (January 6, 1837 – May 2, 1912) was an American politician and a member of the United States House of Representatives for the Tennessee's 7th congressional district.
mgt: Nicholas Nichols Cox (January 6, 1837 – May 2, 1912) was an American attorney, farmer, banker, and politician who served as a Democratic U.S. Representative from Tennessee's 7th district (1891–1901). A Confederate Army colonel during the Civil War, he later practiced law, engaged in agriculture, and worked in banking. His home, the Owen-Cox House, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

trgt: Henry-Louis de La Grange (26 May 1924 – 27 January 2017) was a French musicologist and biographer of Gustav Mahler.
mgt: Henry-Louis de La Grange (1924–2017) was a French musicologist and biographer, renowned for his extensive research and definitive multi-volume biography of composer Gustav Mahler.

trgt: Agnieszka Brustman (born 31 July 1962) is a Polish chess player holding the title of woman grandmaster. She has been the Polish women's champion four times and competed in the Candidates' tournament for the Women's World Championship twice.
mgt: Agnieszka Brustman (born 31 July 1962) is a Polish chess player and Woman Grandmaster (WGM). She is a four-time Polish women's champion, a former world junior champion, and a two-time Women's World Championship Candidates' participant.

trgt: The Willard Gallery was a contemporary art gallery operating in New York City from 1940 until 1987. It was founded by Marian Willard Johnson.
mgt: Willard Gallery was an influential art gallery in New York City, founded by Marian Guthrie Willard in 1940 and active until its closure in 1987.

trgt: Brad Davis (born 30 April 1982) is a rugby league footballer who previously played for the Gold Coast Titans in the National Rugby League. He played as a half-back.
mgt: Brad Davis (born 30 April 1982) is an Australian former rugby league player who played as a halfback. He is the all-time leading points scorer for the Tweed Heads Seagulls.

page_title: R (Corner House Research) v Director of the Serious Fraud Office
trgt: R (Corner House Research) v Director of the Serious Fraud Office UKHL 60 is a UK constitutional law case, concerning the rule of law.
mgt: *R (Corner House Research) v Director of the Serious Fraud Office* [2008] UKHL 60 is a UK constitutional law case concerning the rule of law. It addressed the decision of the Serious Fraud Office (SFO) to halt an investigation into alleged bribery involving BAE Systems and Saudi Arabia's Al-Yamamah arms deal. The Divisional Court initially ruled that the SFO's decision violated the rule of law, but the House of Lords later upheld the decision, citing national security concerns as justification.

trgt: Deitingen is a municipality in the district of Wasseramt in the canton of Solothurn in Switzerland.
mgt: Deitingen is a municipality in the district of Wasseramt, in the canton of Solothurn, Switzerland.

trgt: Christmas in Spiceworld Tour was the second concert tour by British girl group the Spice Girls. The eight-show tour was launched following "solo projects, marriages, motherhood and another round of slagging in the press", as a reunion for the girls.
mgt: The *Christmas in Spiceworld Tour* was a 1999 concert tour by the Spice Girls, held exclusively in the United Kingdom from December 4 to December 15. Featuring eight shows, the tour marked the group's return after a hiatus, showcasing an elaborate stage design with Christmas-themed elements. The performances were divided into three sections, blending hits, new songs, and festive medleys. A full concert at Earl's Court was broadcast live on Sky One, including behind-the-scenes footage. The tour was marred by the death of a crew member during set dismantling.

trgt: Ruaraka is an industrial and residential suburb in the city of Nairobi. Located within the larger sub-county of Kasarani, it is approximately 8 kilometres (5.0 mi) northeast of Nairobi's central business district off Thika Road.
mgt: Ruaraka is a suburb located approximately 8 kilometers northeast of Nairobi's central business district, within the Kasarani sub-county. Established in 1922, it is a major industrial hub and home to East African Breweries Limited.

trgt: A Zirkel (German for "circle", as in "circle of friends") is a monogram used in European student societies in countries such as Germany, Austria, Switzerland, Belgium, Hungary, Latvia, Estonia (Studentenverbindungen).
mgt: A *Zirkel* is a symbol used by German-speaking student societies (*Studentenverbindungen*), consisting of intertwined letters and often an exclamation mark, signifying the society's name and active status.

trgt: Breandán Ó hEithir (18 January 1930 – 26 October 1990) was an Irish writer and broadcaster.
mgt: Breandán Ó hEithir (1930–1990) was an Irish writer, journalist, and broadcaster, known for his lively journalism.

trgt: John Becker (born April 17, 1968) is an American college basketball coach, currently the head coach of the Vermont Catamounts of the America East Conference. He replaced Mike Lonergan, who left to become the coach at George Washington University.
mgt: John Becker (born April 16, 1968) is an American college basketball coach and the head coach of the Vermont Catamounts men's basketball team since 2011.
