Known for his abstract works featuring playing cards and symbolic imagery, he rejected Socialist Realism and explored themes of chance and existentialism. A member of the Lianozovo Group, Nemukhin played a significant role in negotiating between artists and Soviet authorities during the 1960s and 1970s. His works are held in major collections worldwide, including the State Tretyakov Gallery and the Museum of Modern Art.

trgt: Steve Strome is the former head men’s tennis coach at Miami University, Louisiana State University, Duke and Army. Strome also served as the assistant men's basketball coach at Miami University from 1966 to 1969. During his coaching career Strome has received multiple awards including Coach of the Year in more than one conference, won multiple conference championships and was inducted into the Miami University Hall of Fame.
mgt: Steve Strome is an American collegiate tennis coach known for his successful tenures at Miami University, LSU, Duke, and the United States Military Academy. He led Miami to seven Mid-American Conference championships and was named MAC Coach of the Year five times. Strome also guided Army to five Patriot League titles, earning multiple Coach of the Year honors. He was inducted into the Miami University Hall of Fame in 1980.

trgt: Margaret Ransone Murray (November 16, 1901 – July 13, 1986) was an American scientist known primarily for her work on methods to establish cultures of neuronal cells. Her in vitro studies in cellular neurobiology shed light on both nerve- muscle relationships and axon myelination
mgt: Margaret Ransone Murray (November 16, 1901 – July 13, 1986) was an American biologist and pioneer in tissue culture techniques, particularly in neurobiology. She was a professor at Columbia University and made significant contributions to understanding tumorigenesis, myelination, and synaptogenesis. A founding member of the Tissue Culture Association and the American Society for Cell Biology, Murray was widely recognized for her mentorship and research, receiving numerous awards throughout her career.

trgt:
Le Sueur County is a county located in the south central portion of the U.S. state of Minnesota. As of the 2020 census, the population was 28,674. Its county seat is Le Center.

Le Sueur County is part of the Minneapolis-St. Paul-Bloomington, MN-WI Metropolitan Statistical Area.
mgt: Le Sueur County is a county in the U.S. state of Minnesota, founded on March 5, 1853, and named after French explorer Pierre-Charles Le Sueur. Its county seat is Le Center, and its largest city is Le Sueur. The county spans 474 square miles, with the Minnesota River forming its western border. Known for its rich soil and rolling hills, the area is part of Minnesota's savanna region. As of the 2020 census, the population was 28,674. Historically, the county has been predominantly Republican in national elections.

trgt: Eduard Deisenhofer (27 June 1909 – MIA 31 January 1945) was a German commander in the Waffen-SS of Nazi Germany. He was an early member in the SS, and served with the Leibstandarte SS Adolf Hitler and at the Dachau concentration camp in 1930s. During World War II, Deisenhofer served with several combat divisions on both the Eastern and Western fronts, earning the Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross. He held a PhD in political economy.
mgt: Eduard Deisenhofer (June 27, 1909 – January 31, 1945) was a German SS officer and commander during World War II, holding the rank of SS-Oberführer. A member of the Waffen-SS, he served in various units, including the SS Division Totenkopf, SS Division Wiking, and 17th SS Panzergrenadier Division Götz von Berlichingen. Deisenhofer participated in key campaigns on both the Eastern and Western Fronts, earning the Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross. He was declared missing in action near Arnswalde in 1945.

trgt: Seanad Éireann sidebar This is a list of the members of the 22nd Seanad Éireann, the upper house of the Oireachtas (legislature) of Ireland. These Senators were elected or appointed in 2002, after the 2002 general election and served until the close of poll for the 23rd Seanad at the end of July 2007.
mgt: The 22nd Seanad was the upper house of the Oireachtas (Irish legislature) from 2002 to 2007. It comprised 60 seats, with members elected through vocational panels, universities, and Taoiseach nominations. Notable changes included the replacement of deceased Senator Kate Walsh and the election of 14 senators to the 30th Dáil in 2007, leaving ten seats vacant.

trgt: Connah's Quay Power Station is a 1,420 MW gas-fired power station to the west of Connah's Quay in Flintshire in North Wales. It is next to the A548, being tightly situated between the road and the south bank of the River Dee.
mgt: Connah's Quay Power Station is a natural gas-fired combined cycle gas turbine (CCGT) power station in Flintshire, Wales. Operational since 1996, it has a capacity of 1,420 MW and is owned by Uniper. The station succeeded a coal-fired plant that operated from 1954 to 1984. Gas is supplied via a pipeline from the Point of Ayr terminal, and the station features advanced gas treatment facilities. It is Uniper's largest CCGT power station and employs around 81 people.

trgt: Horace Edward Dobbs (born 14 January 1933, in London), and commonly referred to as Horace Dobbs /ˈhɒrɪs dɒbz/ is a British scientist, researcher, author, and television producer, who is regarded as an expert on dolphins and their behaviour. In 1978, Dobbs sets up the International Dolphin Watch. He also founded the Oxford Underwater Research Group.
mgt: Horace Edward Dobbs (born January 14, 1933) is a British scientist, author, and wildlife conservationist known for his pioneering work on dolphin behavior and their therapeutic effects on humans. A former researcher at the United Kingdom Atomic Energy Authority, Dobbs later founded International Dolphin Watch and authored numerous books, including the children's series *Dilo*.

trgt: Lee Franklin Hanmer (October 24, 1871 - April 27, 1961) was the director of the recreation department of the Russell Sage Foundation from 1912 to 1937. In that capacity, he helped to found the Boy Scouts of America. He later helped to form the Campfire Girls of America in 1912.
mgt: Lee Franklin Hanmer (October 24, 1871 – April 27, 1961) was an American educator and recreation advocate. He served as a physical training supervisor in New York City schools, worked with the Playground Association of America and the Russell Sage Foundation, and directed recreation for servicemen during World War I. Hanmer received the Silver Buffalo Award in 1926.

trgt: Frederick Reimold Lehlbach (January 31, 1876 – August 4, 1937) was an American lawyer and politician. As a Republican, Lehlbach served as the U.S. representative for New Jersey's 10th congressional district from 1915 to 1933 and as the representative from New Jersey's 12th congressional district from 1933 to 1937. Lehlbach was also the nephew of Herman Lehlbach, a former U.S. Representative from New Jersey's 6th congressional district who served from 1885 to 1891.
mgt: Frederick R. Lehlbach (January 31, 1876 – August 4, 1937) was an American lawyer and Republican politician who served as a U.S. Representative from New Jersey from 1915 to 1937. Representing the 10th and later the 12th congressional districts, he chaired the Committee on Reform in the Civil Service and the Committee on Civil Service. After losing re-election in 1936, he resumed practicing law in Washington, D.C., where he died in 1937.

trgt: Vyacheslav Viktorovich Yaikov (Lang-ru; born March 1, 1974), known as The Kopeysk Strangler (Lang-ru), is a Kyrgyzstani-born Russian serial killer and rapist. He committed 6 murders on sexual grounds between 2002 and 2003. The Chelyabinsk Regional Court found Yaikov guilty and sentenced him to compulsory treatment.
mgt: Vyacheslav Viktorovich Yaikov (born March 1, 1974), known as "The Kopeysk Strangler," is a Russian serial killer who murdered six women in Chelyabinsk between 2002 and 2003. Diagnosed with an intellectual disability, he was found insane at the time of the crimes and sentenced to compulsory psychiatric treatment.

trgt:
Ali Alipour Ghara (Lang-fa; born 11 November 1995) is an Iranian international footballer who plays as a forward for Gil Vicente.

At the age of 19 years and 185 days, Alipour was the youngest-ever player to score in the Tehran derby after scoring in Persepolis' 1–0 victory on 15 May 2015. He is the only player in Tehran derby history who has scored the winning goal in two derby matches.
mgt: Ali Alipour Ghara (born 11 November 1995) is an Iranian professional footballer who plays as a forward for Portuguese club Gil Vicente. He began his career with Nassaji Mazandaran and later played for clubs including Persepolis, where he became the youngest scorer in the Tehran derby and was the Persian Gulf Pro League top scorer in 2017–18. Alipour has also represented the Iran national team, debuting in 2018.

trgt: Fort Cumberland (built 1754) was an 18th-century frontier fort at the current site of Cumberland, Maryland, USA. It was an important military and economic center during the French and Indian War (1754–63) and figured significantly in the early career of George Washington.
mgt: Fort Cumberland was a frontier fort constructed in 1754 at the confluence of Wills Creek and the Potomac River, in present-day Cumberland, Maryland. Renamed in 1755, it played a significant role in the French and Indian War, serving as a rallying point for British forces under General Braddock. The fort was also notable for its association with George Washington, who led troops back to the site after Braddock's defeat. Once the westernmost outpost of the British Empire in America, the fort is now gone, with Emmanuel Episcopal Church occupying its site, though tunnels from the original structure remain.

trgt: Romy Simpkins (born 2 November 1993) is a British actress, model, Mental health ambassador and beauty pageant titleholder who competed in several beauty pageants. She has represented her country & the United Kingdom internationally in beauty pageants. She competed at the Miss International 2016 pageant in Tokyo, Japan. Romy competed in the Miss Supranational 2018 pageant in Krynica-Zdrój, Poland.
mgt: Romy Simpkins (born 2 November 1993) is an English mental health advocate, beauty pageant titleholder, and model. She has held several titles, including Miss Dorset 2015, Miss Global International UK 2015, Miss International United Kingdom 2016, and Miss Supranational England 2018. Simpkins has also appeared in television and film productions and worked as a part-time model.

trgt: Johnson & Wales University (JWU) is a private university with its main campus in Providence, Rhode Island. Founded as a business school in 1914 by Gertrude I. Johnson and Mary T. Wales, JWU enrolled 7,357 students across its campuses in the fall of 2020. The university is accredited by the New England Commission of Higher Education.
mgt: Johnson & Wales University (JWU) is a private university founded in 1914 in Providence, Rhode Island, by Gertrude I. Johnson and Mary T. Wales. Known for its renowned culinary arts and hospitality programs, JWU also offers degrees in business, engineering, design, and more. It operates campuses in Providence and Charlotte, with former campuses in Denver and North Miami.

trgt: Panhard was a French motor vehicle manufacturer that began as one of the first makers of automobiles. It was a manufacturer of light tactical and military vehicles. Its final incarnation, now owned by Renault Trucks Defense, was formed by the acquisition of Panhard by Auverland in 2005, and then by Renault in 2012. In 2018 Renault Trucks Defense, ACMAT and Panhard combined under a single brand, Arquus.
mgt: Panhard was a French automobile and military vehicle manufacturer founded in 1887 by René Panhard and Émile Levassor. Initially known as Panhard et Levassor, the company pioneered automotive innovations, including the modern transmission and the standard car layout. It produced passenger cars until 1967, after which it focused solely on military vehicles. Acquired by Citroën and later Renault Trucks Defense, Panhard ceased operations in 2018. Its legacy includes the Panhard rod suspension system.

trgt: James McLachlan Turnbull (born 23 May 1882) was a Scottish footballer who played as a forward, most notably for Manchester United, with whom he won the Football League and the FA Charity Shield in 1908 and the FA Cup in 1909. He was the first player to net a hat-trick in a Charity Shield game.
mgt: James McLachlan Turnbull (born 23 May 1882) was a Scottish footballer who played as a forward for several clubs, including Manchester United, where he won the 1907–08 Football League title, the 1908 FA Charity Shield, and the 1908–09 FA Cup. He also played for Dundee, Rangers, Chelsea, and Bradford (Park Avenue).

trgt:
Hiroko Anzai (安西 ひろこ, Anzai Hiroko) (born February 9, 1979, in Kanagawa, Japan) is a Japanese actress, model and gravure idol, who has appeared in various magazines and photo albums.

Her first photo album was released in 1996 and titled Mystic. Fuji TV selected Hiroko as its Visual Queen of 1998. She has body measurements of 89-62-90 cm, and stands 1.57 m tall.
mgt: Hiroko Anzai (born February 9, 1979, in Kanagawa, Japan) is a Japanese actress, model, and former gravure idol. She debuted in 1996 and gained popularity in the late 1990s as a fashion model and television personality. Known for her work in teen magazines and music collaborations, Anzai briefly retired from show business before returning in 2008. She is also skilled in judo and swimming.

trgt: Mysterious Girlfriend X (Japanese: 謎の彼女X, Hepburn: Nazo no Kanojo Ekkusu) is a Japanese manga written and illustrated by Riichi Ueshiba. It was originally published as a one-shot story in 2004 before becoming a serialized comic in Kodansha's Monthly Afternoon magazine in 2006. The manga is licensed in North America by Vertical. An anime adaptation by Hoods Entertainment aired in Japan from April to June 2012, with an original video animation episode released in August 2012.
mgt: *Mysterious Girlfriend X* (Japanese: 謎の彼女X, *Nazo no Kanojo X*) is a Japanese romantic comedy manga series written and illustrated by Riichi Ueshiba. It was serialized in *Monthly Afternoon* from 2006 to 2014, with 12 volumes published. The story follows the unconventional relationship between Akira Tsubaki and Mikoto Urabe, centered around a mysterious bond involving Urabe's drool. The series was adapted into a 13-episode anime by Hoods Entertainment in 2012, along with an original video animation.

trgt:
"Flash" (stylized as "FLASH") is a single by Perfume, released on March 16, 2016. It is the fifth single from their fifth studio album Cosmic Explorer. The single reached first place in the real-time ranking of iTunes with 200,000+ downloads. It is also the band's highest selling single since "Love the World".

The single was used as the theme song for the live action adapted film Chihayafuru.
mgt: "Flash" is a song by the Japanese girl group Perfume, released as a single on March 16, 2016, and later included in their album *Cosmic Explorer*. Produced by Yasutaka Nakata, the song blends J-pop and EDM elements and features choreography inspired by martial arts and Tai chi. Its music video, directed by Yusuke Tanaka, incorporates CGI and LED light sabers, becoming Perfume's most-watched video on YouTube with over 60 million views.

trgt: New Hampshire state elections in 2018 were held on Tuesday, November 6, 2018, with the primary elections being held on June 5, 2018. Voters elected 2 members to the United States House of Representatives, the Governor of New Hampshire, all five members to the Executive Council, all 24 members to the New Hampshire Senate, and all 400 members to the New Hampshire House of Representatives, among other local elected offices.
mgt: The 2018 New Hampshire elections were held on November 6, 2018, with primary elections on June 5. Democrats gained control of the New Hampshire Executive Council, State Senate, and State House of Representatives. Both U.S. House seats were retained by Democrats, while Republican Governor Chris Sununu was reelected. No U.S. Senate election was held in the state.

trgt: Eucalyptus largiflorens, or black box or river box, is a tree that is endemic to Australia. It has rough, fibrous or flaky bark, dull greenish-grey, lance-shaped leaves, oval to club-shaped green to yellow flower buds, white flowers and hemispherical, cup-shaped or barrel-shaped fruit.
mgt: Eucalyptus largiflorens, commonly known as black box, is a tree species in the family Myrtaceae native to Australia. It grows up to 20 meters tall, with rough, dark grey bark and lance-shaped leaves. Flowering occurs in most months, especially in autumn and spring, producing white flowers. First described in 1855 by Ferdinand von Mueller, the species is widespread across Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia, particularly in grassy woodlands on the floodplains of the Murray–Darling basin. Indigenous Australians utilized its seeds as a food source, processing them to remove bitterness before consumption.

trgt: Lü is a village in the Val Müstair municipality in the district of Inn in the Swiss canton of Graubünden. In 2009 Lü merged with Fuldera, Müstair, Santa Maria Val Müstair, Tschierv and Valchava to form the municipality of Val Müstair.
mgt: Lü is a former municipality in the canton of Graubünden, Switzerland, located in the Val Müstair region. Situated at an elevation of 1,920 meters (6,300 ft), it is one of the highest villages in Europe. First mentioned in 1466, Lü covered an area of 6.8 km² and had a population of 63 as of 2008, with most residents speaking Rhaeto-Romance. The village is known for its scenic hiking trails and the Alpine Astrovillage Lü-Stailas, a public observatory inaugurated in 2009, offering favorable conditions for astronomy.

trgt: The Aboriginal South Australians are the Indigenous people who lived in South Australia prior to the British colonisation of South Australia, and their descendants and their ancestors. There are difficulties in identifying the names, territorial boundaries, and language groups of the Aboriginal peoples of South Australia, including poor record-keeping and deliberate obfuscation, so only a rough approximation can be given here.
mgt: Aboriginal South Australians are the Indigenous peoples whose traditional lands are located within the state of South Australia. These include groups such as the Kaurna, Ngarrindjeri, Pitjantjatjara, and Adnyamathanha, among others. British colonization began in 1836, with initial legal recognition of Aboriginal land rights, though conflicts and massacres occurred during settlement. Aboriginal communities were later impacted by policies such as the Stolen Generations. Today, native title rights exist in parts of the state, and efforts to preserve and revive Aboriginal languages and cultures, such as the Kaurna language, continue.

trgt: Nintendo PowerFest '94 was a 1994 Nintendo-promoted video game competition. Most of the town competitions were held in customized trailers outside of department stores. It was based on scoring points in a special Game Pak. The three Super Nintendo Entertainment System games (Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels, Super Mario Kart, and Ken Griffey, Jr. Presents Major League Baseball) were played within a time limit of six minutes.
mgt: Nintendo PowerFest '94 was a video game competition held by Nintendo in 1994, featuring challenges in *Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels*, *Super Mario Kart*, and *Major League Baseball*. Local competitions were held across the United States, culminating in the World Finals in San Diego, California. Mike Iarossi emerged as the champion after defeating Brandon Veach in a final match of *Donkey Kong Country*. Only two PowerFest '94 cartridges are known to exist today.

trgt: City Tower (formerly Sunley House) is a 30-storey skyscraper situated in the Piccadilly Gardens area of Manchester city centre in England. As of 2022, it is the tallest office building in Manchester and outside London, and the eighth-tallest building in Manchester, with a roof height of 107 metres (351 ft).
mgt: City Tower is a modernist high-rise building in Manchester, England, completed in 1965 as part of the Piccadilly Plaza complex. Standing 123 meters tall with 30 floors, it serves as an office, retail, and communication hub, hosting major radio transmitters. Owned by Schroders since 2014, it features refurbished commercial spaces and a distinctive design.

trgt:
Pilar, officially the Municipality of Pilar, is a 1st class municipality in the province of Sorsogon, Philippines. According to the 2020 census, it has a population of 75,793 people.

Pilar's economy is mainly agricultural. Despite efforts on multiplicity, this town is still dependent on the monoculture of coconut. Pilar is 55 kilometres (34 mi) from Sorsogon City and 560 kilometres (350 mi) from Manila.
mgt: Pilar is a 1st class municipality in the province of Sorsogon, Philippines, with a population of 75,793 as of the 2020 census. It is divided into 49 barangays and is known for its proximity to whale shark sightings in Pilar Bay, near San Antonio. The local government, in collaboration with WWF, has implemented guidelines to protect the sharks, though enforcement remains inconsistent.

trgt: Major-General Sir James Syme Drew KBE CB DSO MC DL (1 September 1883 – 27 June 1955) was a decorated British Army officer who saw service during both the world wars. He served as aide-de-camp to the King, commanded the 52nd (Lowland) Infantry Division, was Colonel to the Queen's Own Cameron Highlanders, and became Director-General of the Home Guard and Territorial Army.
mgt: Major-General Sir James Syme Drew KBE CB DSO MC DL (1 September 1883 – 27 June 1955) was a British Army officer who served from 1902 to 1945. A veteran of both World Wars, he commanded the 52nd (Lowland) Infantry Division during World War II and held senior roles, including Director-General of the Home Guard and Territorial Army. Knighted in 1944, Drew was highly decorated, receiving numerous honors for his service.

trgt: The Oetker Hotel Management GmbH, which also appears under the brand name Oetker Collection (OHC), is a German hotel management company based in Baden-Baden. The Oetker Collection is managed by the Oetker family. Company hospitality business first started in 1870 when Villa Soleil in Cap d'Antibes opened as a writers' retreat and later become the celebrated Hotel du Cap-Eden-Roc.
mgt: The Oetker Collection is a luxury hospitality company founded in 1870 and headquartered in Baden-Baden, Germany. Managed by the Oetker family, it operates a portfolio of prestigious hotels, including Le Bristol in Paris and Hôtel du Cap-Eden-Roc in Cap d'Antibes, as well as private villas and residences. Timo Gruenert serves as CEO.

trgt:
Otto Kirchheimer (IPA-de; 11 November 1905, Heilbronn – 22 November 1965, Washington, D.C.) was a German jurist of Jewish ancestry and political scientist of the Frankfurt School whose work essentially covered the state and its constitution.

Kirchheimer worked as a research analyst at the Office of Strategic Services (OSS), the forerunner of the CIA, starting in World War II and continuing to 1952.
mgt: Otto Kirchheimer (1905–1965) was a German-American political scientist, legal scholar, and sociologist known for his critical analyses of constitutional law, political systems, and the relationship between social structures and state power. A member of the Frankfurt School, he contributed to the study of fascism, democracy, and party systems, co-developing the concept of the "catch-all party." Kirchheimer's work remains influential in political and legal theory.

trgt: The Regional Arts & Culture Council (RACC) is an organization that administers arts grants in Multnomah, Washington, and Clackamas Counties that also do advocacy in the Portland metropolitan area in Oregon, United States. It evolved from the city’s Metropolitan Arts Commission agency in the 1990s. In 1995, the Metropolitan Arts Commission became the RACC as an independent non-profit organization.
mgt: The Regional Arts & Culture Council (RACC) is a nonprofit organization serving the Portland metropolitan area, dedicated to integrating arts and culture into community life. Funded by public and private sources, RACC provides grants, resources, and technical assistance to artists and arts organizations. It manages public art programs, including Portland's percent-for-art initiatives, and oversees arts education efforts like The Right Brain Initiative.

trgt: The former Reading Municipal Building is a historic building at 49 Pleasant Street in Reading, Massachusetts. Built in 1885, this two-story brick building was the town's first municipal structure, housing the town offices, jail, and fire station. In 1918 all functions except fire services moved out of the building. It now serves as Reading's Pleasant Street Senior Center. The building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1984.
mgt: The Reading Municipal Building, located at 49 Pleasant Street in Reading, Massachusetts, is a historic Renaissance Revival structure built in 1883. Designed by architect Horace G. Wadlin, it originally served as the town's first purpose-built municipal building, housing town offices, a fire station, and a jail. It remained a fire station until the mid-20th century and was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1984. It now serves as a senior center.

trgt: The Cóndores UNAM (English: UNAM Condors), was one of the three American football teams that represented the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM) from 1970 to 1997. The Cóndores competed in the ONEFA and won 10 National Championships, including four in a row from 1983 to 1986.
mgt: The Cóndores UNAM were a college American football team representing the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM) from 1970 to 1997. Competing in the Organización Nacional Estudiantil de Fútbol Americano (ONEFA), they won 10 national championships, becoming one of Mexico's most successful and popular teams. The team was disbanded in 1998 when UNAM merged its football programs under the Pumas name. Their legacy endures, inspiring the name and colors of the professional team Condors CDMX.

trgt: NA-36 (Hangu-cum-Orakzai) (این اے-36، ہنگو-کم-اورکزئی) is a constituency for the National Assembly of Pakistan. It covers the whole of the district Hangu. The constituency was formerly known as NA-16 (Hangu) from 1977 to 2018. The name changed to NA-33 (Hangu) after the delimitation in 2018. NA-36 (Hangu-cum-Orakzai) and Orakzai District were also included after the delimitation in 2022.
mgt: NA-36 (Hangu-cum-Orakzai) is a constituency for the National Assembly of Pakistan, representing the Hangu region with an electorate of 275,947. It was created from NA-16 (Hangu) and is currently represented by Nadeem Khayal of Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf, who won a by-election in 2022 following the death of the previous MNA, Khayal Zaman Orakzai.

trgt:
Paddleton is a 2019 American comedy-drama film, directed by Alex Lehmann, from a script by Lehmann and Mark Duplass. The film stars Duplass and Ray Romano.

The film had its world premiere at the Sundance Film Festival on February 1, 2019. It was released on February 22, 2019, by Netflix to generally favorable reviews from critics.
mgt: *Paddleton* is a 2019 American drama film directed by Alex Lehmann and written by Lehmann and Mark Duplass. Starring Duplass and Ray Romano, the film follows two middle-aged friends navigating terminal illness and the complexities of their deep platonic bond. Produced by Duplass Brothers Productions, it premiered at the Sundance Film Festival on February 1, 2019, and was later released on Netflix. The film received critical acclaim for its emotional depth and performances, holding an 89% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes.

trgt: Thomas Alexander Fraser, 12th Lord Lovat and 1st Baron Lovat, KT (17 June 1802 – 28 June 1875) was a Scottish peer. He was also the 21st MacShimidh, the traditional Gaelic Patronym for the Chiefs of the Clan Fraser of Lovat.
mgt: Thomas Alexander Fraser, 12th Lord Lovat KT (17 June 1802 – 28 June 1875), was a Scottish peer, Chief of Clan Fraser, and Lord Lieutenant of Inverness from 1853 to 1873. He restored the Lovat titles and estates, which had been forfeited after the 1747 attainder of the 11th Lord Lovat. Elevated to the Peerage of the United Kingdom as Baron Lovat in 1837, he became the 12th Lord Lovat in the Peerage of Scotland in 1854. Fraser was also a Knight of the Thistle and commissioned Strichen House in Aberdeenshire.

trgt: Vagif Mustafazadeh (Lang-az; March 16, 1940 – December 16, 1979), also known as Vaqif Mustafa-Zadeh, was a Soviet-Azerbaijani jazz pianist and composer, acclaimed for fusing jazz and the traditional Azerbaijani folk music known as mugham. According to many world famous jazz musicians, Mustafazadeh is one of the pioneers and "the architect of jazz in Azerbaijan".
mgt: Vagif Mustafazadeh (March 16, 1940 – December 16, 1979) was an Azerbaijani jazz pianist and composer, renowned as the founder of the jazz mugham genre, blending traditional Azerbaijani mugham with jazz. Despite Soviet-era restrictions on jazz, he gained international acclaim, winning awards such as the 1978 Monaco Jazz Composition Prize. His legacy endures through his recordings, influence, and a dedicated house museum in Baku.

trgt: The Venezuelan coup attempt of November 1992 was an attempt to seize control of the government of Venezuela that took place on 27 November 1992. group of young military officers who were loyal to the Hugo Chávez-led Revolutionary Bolivarian Movement-200 (MBR-200), while Chávez was in prison for the February 1992 coup d'état attempt.
mgt: The November 1992 Venezuelan coup attempt was a failed military uprising against the government of President Carlos Andrés Pérez. Led by dissident air force and navy officers, the coup sought to address widespread discontent over economic instability and corruption. Despite initial successes, the rebellion was suppressed, resulting in 172 deaths and 95 injuries.

trgt: Herald Scholarly Open Access is a publisher of various academic journals. It has a postal address in Herndon, Virginia, United States, but is actually based in Hyderabad, India. Herald Scholarly Open Access has been included on Beall's List of potential predatory open-access publishers, and has faced other criticisms of its publishing practices.
mgt: Herald Scholarly Open Access is an open-access publisher active since at least 2015. It publishes approximately 73 journals in clinical, medical, life sciences, and pharma fields, charging authors for publication. Criticized for lacking scientific editors-in-chief and email spamming, it is listed on Beall's List of potential predatory publishers.

trgt: Furcifer tuzetae is a species of chameleon that is endemic to Madagascar. It is only known from its type locality, Andrenalamivola near Befandriana Sud. It was described by Édouard-Raoul Brygoo, Robert M. Bourgat and Charles Antoine Domergue in 1972. The International Union for Conservation of Nature have rated this species as "data deficient".
mgt: *Furcifer tuzetae* is a species of chameleon endemic to Madagascar, known from a single specimen collected in Andrenalamivola, Befandriana Sud. It inhabits dry and possibly gallery forests and is oviparous. Described in 1972, it was later reclassified under the genus *Furcifer*. The species is listed as "Data Deficient" by the IUCN due to limited data, with potential threats including habitat loss from slash-and-burn agriculture and logging. The specific name honors French parasitologist Odette Tuzet.

trgt: Chenani is a town and tehsil in the Udhampur district, in the Indian union territory of Jammu and Kashmir. It is governed by a notified area council (city council). Before 1947, it was an internal jagir ruled by a Chandel (Rajput) ruler in the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir.
mgt: Chenani is a town in the Udhampur district of Jammu and Kashmir, India, located at an elevation of 1,062 meters (3,484 feet) along the Tawi River. Historically, it was a princely state ruled by the Chandel Rajputs until 1947. As of the 2011 Census, it has a population of 2,620 with a literacy rate of 88.91%. Chenani is well-connected by road, including the nearby Dr. Syama Prasad Mookerjee Tunnel, India's longest road tunnel, which reduces travel time on the Jammu–Srinagar National Highway. It serves as a gateway to nearby shrines such as Sudhmahadev and Mantalai.

trgt: Gareth James Delany (born 28 April 1997) is an Irish cricketer. He made his international debut for the Ireland cricket team in July 2019. In January 2020, he was one of nineteen players to be awarded a central contract from Cricket Ireland, the first year in which all contracts were awarded on a full-time basis.
mgt: Gareth James Delany (born 28 April 1997) is an Irish cricketer who plays as an all-rounder, batting right-handed and bowling right-arm leg break. He made his international debut for Ireland in 2019 in Twenty20 Internationals (T20Is) and in 2020 in One Day Internationals (ODIs). Domestically, he has played for Leinster Lightning, Leicestershire, and Munster Reds.

trgt: Chas Henry (born January 6, 1989) is a former American football punter. Henry played college football for the University of Florida, earned consensus All-American honors, and was recognized as the best college punter as a senior. He was signed by the Philadelphia Eagles as an undrafted free agent in 2011.
mgt: Chas Henry (born January 6, 1989) is a former American football punter. He played college football at the University of Florida, where he was a consensus All-American, won the Ray Guy Award in 2010, and was part of the 2008 BCS National Championship team. Henry played in the NFL for the Philadelphia Eagles in 2011 and 2012 and briefly joined the Tampa Bay Buccaneers in 2013.

trgt: Scott Mendes (born July 4, 1969 in Visalia, California) is an American former professional rodeo cowboy who specialized in bull riding. He won the 1997 Professional Rodeo Cowboys Association (PRCA) Bull Riding World Championship, and is the inventor of Rodeo Judge, the original rodeo card game. He was also a co-founder of the Professional Bull Riders (PBR). In 2022, he was inducted into the Bull Riding Hall of Fame.
mgt: Scott Alan Mendes (born July 4, 1969) is an American former professional bull rider and the 1997 PRCA World Champion Bull Rider. A co-founder of the Professional Bull Riders (PBR), he competed in the PRCA, PBR, and other bull riding organizations, qualifying for multiple world finals. Following his retirement, Mendes became a "born again" Christian, founding Western Harvest Ministries, producing Christian films, and serving as a pastor and motivational speaker.

trgt: The Western Sahara peace process refers to the international efforts to resolve the Western Sahara conflict. The conflict has failed so far to result in permanent peace between Morocco and the Polisario Front (including its self declared Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic). The standing issues of the peace process include Sahrawi refugees (specifically Sahrawi refugee camps in Algeria), and human rights in Western Sahara.
mgt: The Western Sahara peace process refers to ongoing efforts to resolve the conflict between the Polisario Front, representing the Sahrawi people, and Morocco over the disputed territory of Western Sahara. The conflict, rooted in the decolonization of Spanish Sahara in 1975, has involved armed conflict, ceasefires, and unarmed civil campaigns. Despite multiple peace initiatives, the region remains contested, with Morocco controlling most of the territory.

trgt: Matching logic is a formal logic mainly used to reason about the correctness of computer programs. Its operators use pattern matching to operate on the power set of states, rather than the set of states. It was created by Grigore Roșu and is used in the K Framework.
mgt: Matching logic is a formal system that operates on patterns, where statements evaluate to the set of values that match them rather than to true or false. It is used in program semantics, often in conjunction with the K Framework, to specify operational semantics and generate proofs using SMT-solvers.

trgt: Helene P. Foley is an American classical scholar. She is Professor of Classical Studies at Barnard College, Columbia University and a member of the Institute for Research on Women, Gender and Sexuality at Columbia. She specialises in ancient Greek literature (epic poetry, tragedy, and comedy), women and gender in antiquity, and the reception of classical drama.
mgt: Helene P. Foley is an American classicist and professor at Barnard College, Columbia University. She specializes in Greek tragedy, with a focus on its modern reinterpretations. A former president of the American Philological Association, she delivered the 2008 Sather Lectures, later published as *Reimagining Greek Tragedy on the American Stage* (2012).

trgt: Benjamin Brackett Downing (born September 11, 1981) is an American politician and Democratic former member of the Massachusetts State Senate. He represented the Berkshire, Hampshire and Franklin district from 2007 to 2013, before being redistricted to the Berkshire, Hampshire, Franklin, and Hampden district, which he represented until 2017.
mgt: Benjamin Brackett Downing (born September 11, 1981) is an American politician and former Democratic member of the Massachusetts Senate, representing the Berkshire region from 2007 to 2017. During his tenure, he chaired several committees and focused on energy, revenue, and public service. After leaving office, he worked in renewable energy and briefly ran for Governor of Massachusetts in 2022.

trgt: Jackson Township is one of the eighteen townships of Richland County, Ohio, United States. It is a part of the Mansfield Metropolitan Statistical Area. The 2000 census found 3,861 people in the township, 2,774 of whom lived in the unincorporated portions of the township.
mgt: Jackson Township is one of the eighteen townships of Richland County, Ohio, United States, covering an area of 24.4 square miles. As of the 2000 census, it had a population of 3,861. Part of the city of Shelby lies within its boundaries. Governed by a three-member board of trustees and an elected fiscal officer, it is one of thirty-seven Jackson Townships in Ohio.

trgt: Mélite, or The False Letters, is a comedy in five acts by Pierre Corneille. Written in 1625, it is Corneille's first play and debuted on stage in December 1629 in Berthaud’s Jeu de paume court, and was performed by the acting troupe of Montdory. Mélite represents Corneille’s creation of a new genre, the comedy of manners, which was a departure from the coarse or buffoonish farce in vogue at the time.
mgt: *Mélite* is a comedy play by Pierre Corneille, first performed in 1629. The plot revolves around misunderstandings and deception among lovers, sparked by forged letters. Éraste, in love with Mélite, becomes jealous when she falls for his friend Tircis. His scheme to disrupt their relationship leads to chaos, including heartbreak, fainting, and madness. Ultimately, the misunderstandings are resolved, with Mélite and Tircis marrying, and Éraste finding redemption by marrying Cloris, Tircis' sister.

trgt: Caledonite, whose name derives from Caledonia, the historical name of its place of discovery (Scotland), is a richly colored blue-green sulfate-carbonate mineral of lead and copper with an orthorhombic crystal structure. It is an uncommon mineral found in the oxidized zones of copper-lead deposits.
mgt: Caledonite is a rare sulfate mineral with the chemical formula Cu₂Pb₅(OH)₆CO₃(SO₄)₃, known for its blue to green color and vitreous luster. It forms in oxidized copper and lead deposits, often associated with minerals like linarite, malachite, and cerussite. Primarily a collector's mineral, caledonite is prized for its well-formed crystals, which are found in limited localities such as the Mammoth-St. Anthony Mine in Arizona and select sites in California and Chile. Its identification relies on properties like density, streak, and crystal habit.

trgt: David Peter Ryan (born 5 January 1957) is an English former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He played in the English Football League for Port Vale in 1976, on loan from Manchester United. He later played non-League football for Southport, Northwich Victoria, Hyde United and Chorley.
mgt: David Peter Ryan (born 5 January 1957) is an English former footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He began his career at Manchester United and later played for clubs including Port Vale, Southport, and Northwich Victoria. After retiring, he served as Head Goalkeeping Coach at the Manchester United Academy and worked in community development for the club from 1992 to 2013.

trgt: ESET NOD32 Antivirus, commonly known as NOD32, is an antivirus software package made by the Slovak company ESET. ESET NOD32 Antivirus is sold in two editions, Home Edition and Business Edition. The Business Edition packages add ESET Remote Administrator allowing for server deployment and management, mirroring of threat signature database updates and the ability to install on Microsoft Windows Server operating systems.
mgt: ESET NOD32 is an antivirus software developed by the Slovak company ESET, first released in 1987. Originally a DOS-based program, it evolved into a comprehensive security solution compatible with multiple operating systems, including Windows, macOS, and Linux. Known for its heuristic analysis and lightweight design, NOD32 has received numerous industry certifications and awards for malware detection and system protection.

trgt: Adoniram Judson Wells, Jr. (April 1, 1917 – February 19, 2008) was an American chemist who worked for DuPont Company for much of his career. After he retired in 1989, he began working in the field of epidemiology, and went on to publish 12 papers on the health effects of tobacco smoke, particularly passive smoking.
mgt: A. Judson Wells (1917–2008) was an American chemist and researcher known for his work on the health effects of passive smoking. After earning his doctorate from Harvard University, he worked at DuPont from 1941 to 1989, eventually becoming director of the industrial products division. He published influential studies linking secondhand smoke to heart disease and mortality.

page_title: The Show Must Go On (2007 film)
trgt:
The Show Must Go On (Korean: 우아한 세계; RR: Uahan segye; lit: Elegant World) is a 2007 South Korean action film written and directed by Han Jae-rim, starring Song Kang-ho in the lead role. The film revolves around a full-time gangster who's aspiring to be a full-time husband and dad.

The film released theatrically in South Korea on 5 April 2007.
mgt: *The Show Must Go On* is a 2007 South Korean crime drama film written and directed by Han Jae-rim. Starring Song Kang-ho, the film follows In-gu, a gangster struggling to balance his criminal career with his responsibilities as a husband and father. Released on April 5, 2007, it garnered over 1 million admissions and won Best Film and Best Actor at the 28th Blue Dragon Film Awards.

trgt: David Upthegrove (born May 3, 1971) is an American politician. He is a member of the King County Council, representing the 5th district since 2014. A member of the Democratic Party, he was a member of the Washington House of Representatives, representing the 33rd district from 2002 to 2013.
mgt: Dave Upthegrove (born May 3, 1971) is an American politician and member of the Democratic Party, serving on the King County Council since 2014. Previously, he was a member of the Washington House of Representatives from 2002 to 2013, where he chaired the Environment Committee and led environmental and education initiatives.

trgt: Hadith: A Re-evaluation is a book by Kassim Ahmad (or Ahmed). In it he writes that the hadith books in Shia and Sunni Islam were introduced without any authoritative basis. It claims that the hadith books were not endorsed by Muhammad, nor by the first four caliphs, nor by the Quran. The book makes the case for Quranism, a minority sect in Islam.
mgt: *Hadith: A Re-evaluation* is a controversial book by Malaysian author and philosopher Kassim Ahmed, published in 1985. The book argues that the Quran is complete and that the hadith are a human invention influenced by politics, often contradicting the Quran, themselves, and scientific facts. It was banned in Malaysia, and Kassim's public lectures on the topic were canceled.

trgt: Sanjog (transl. Destiny) is a 1972 Indian Hindi-language drama film directed by S. S. Balan. The film stars Mala Sinha, Amitabh Bachchan and Aruna Irani in the lead roles. It is a remake of the Tamil film Iru Kodugal directed by K. Balachander.
mgt: *Sanjog* is a 1972 Hindi-language drama film directed by S. S. Balan and produced by Ayaz Ali Shaikh. Starring Mala Sinha, Amitabh Bachchan, and Aruna Irani, the film explores themes of love, separation, and sacrifice. Its music was composed by R. D. Burman. Adapted from the Tamil film *Iru Kodugal*, the story follows Mohan (Bachchan) as his past and present relationships collide, leading to emotional turmoil and tragedy. Despite its dramatic premise, the film received criticism for its outdated screenplay and incoherent direction.

trgt: A customer data platform (CDP) is a collection of software which creates a persistent, unified customer database that is accessible to other systems. Data is pulled from multiple sources, cleaned and combined to create a single customer profile. This structured data is then made available to other marketing systems. According to Gartner, customer data platforms have evolved from a variety of mature markets, "including multichannel campaign management, tag management and data integration."
mgt: A customer data platform (CDP) is a type of software that centralizes and standardizes customer data from various sources to create a unified customer view. Unlike data management platforms (DMPs) or data lakes, CDPs collect identifiable customer data in real-time and enable personalized marketing and analytics. Emerging in 2010, CDPs evolved from customer databases and gained popularity for their integration capabilities, with the industry reaching $1.9 billion in revenue by 2022.

trgt: Thelymitra occidentalis, commonly called the western azure sun orchid or rimmed orchid, is a species of orchid in the family Orchidaceae and is endemic to the south-west of Australia. It has a single erect, fleshy, channelled, dark green leaf and up to fifteen blue flowers with darker blue veins and sometimes flushed with pink. The lobe on top of the anther has a wavy, yellow crest.
mgt: Thelymitra occidentalis, commonly known as the western azure sun orchid, is a species of orchid native to southern Australia. It is a tuberous, perennial herb with a single fleshy leaf and up to fifteen blue flowers with darker lines, blooming from September to November. Described in 2001 by Jeff Jeanes, it grows in heath and woodland habitats and is classified as "not threatened" in Western Australia.

trgt:
The eCRM or electronic customer relationship management coined by Oscar Gomes encompasses all standard CRM functions with the use of the net environment i.e., intranet, extranet and internet. Electronic CRM concerns all forms of managing relationships with customers through the use of information technology (IT).

eCRM processes include data collection, data aggregation, and customer interaction. Compared to traditional CRM, the integrated information for eCRM intraorganizational collaboration can be more efficient to communicate with customers.
mgt: Electronic customer relationship management (eCRM) is the application of internet-based technologies, such as web browsers and electronic touchpoints, to manage customer relationships and enhance customer satisfaction. Building on traditional customer relationship management (CRM), eCRM integrates information technology to personalize marketing, improve customer retention, and reduce service costs. It encompasses strategies like mobile CRM (mCRM), cloud-based solutions, and virtual CRM (vCRM), enabling businesses to deliver tailored experiences and streamline interactions. Privacy and security remain critical considerations in eCRM implementation.

trgt: Anne Ebbs (née Sinnott born 1940 or 1941) is a retired Irish Paralympic table tennis medallist. Outside of her table tennis career, Ebbs founded the Paralympic Council of Ireland in 1987 and was the organization's secretary general until 2008. Ebbs was awarded the Paralympic Order in 2010.
mgt: Anne Ebbs (born 1940) is an Irish Paralympian, sports administrator, and advocate for disability sports. She competed in multiple Paralympic Games from 1972 to 1984, winning three medals in table tennis. Ebbs played a pivotal role in developing Paralympic sports in Ireland, serving as the founding secretary general of the Paralympic Council of Ireland from 1987 to 2008. She also held leadership roles at the Irish Wheelchair Association and was Ireland's head of mission at the 1988 Summer Paralympics. Recognized for her contributions, Ebbs has received numerous honors, including the Paralympic Order and the Irish Paralympic Order.

trgt: Give Notice (foaled 18 May 1997 – after 2004) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse. He showed no discernible promise as a juvenile in 1999 but over the next two seasons he made steady progress, winning five times in handicap races over extended distances. He emerged as a top-class stayer in 2002 when he won the Sagaro Stakes and the Prix du Cadran. Apart from his victories he finished second in the Goodwood Cup and third in the Cesarewitch
mgt: Give Notice (foaled May 18, 1997) was a British-bred Thoroughbred racehorse and gelding who achieved notable success in long-distance races. Bred and owned by Ian Stewart-Brown and Michael Meacock, and trained by John Dunlop, he won seven of his 27 races, earning £185,219. His major victories included the Stubbs Stakes (2001), Sagaro Stakes (2002), and the Group 1 Prix du Cadran (2002). Known for his stamina, Give Notice competed internationally and later raced in Saudi Arabia before retiring.

trgt: The Catskill Mountain House, which opened in 1824, was a famous hotel near Palenville, New York, and in the Catskill Mountains overlooking the Hudson River Valley. In its prime, from the 1850s to the turn of the century, it was visited by three U.S. presidents (U.S. Grant, Chester A. Arthur, and Theodore Roosevelt) and the power elite of the day.
mgt: The Catskill Mountain House was a historic hotel built in 1823 atop the Catskill Mountains in New York, offering sweeping views of the Hudson Valley. A popular destination for artists, writers, and the wealthy, it played a key role in the Hudson River School art movement. Declining in prominence by the 20th century, it closed in 1941 and was demolished in 1963.

trgt: Haemulinae is a subfamily of the Haemulidae and consists of the genera of that family which are regarded as being of New World origin, although they are now widespread. The subfamily is distinguished from the Plectorhynchinae by having a short dorsal fin which contains 13-16 soft rays, as opposed to the long dorsal fin with 17-26 soft rays of the subfamily Plectorhynchinae.
mgt: Haemulinae is a subfamily of marine ray-finned fishes in the family Haemulidae, commonly known as grunts. Established in 1885, it includes several genera, though their classification has been subject to revision based on molecular studies. These studies have led to the reclassification of some species and the revival of certain genera to resolve paraphyly. Taxonomic differences exist between Catalog of Fishes and FishBase.

trgt: An amnesty bin or amnesty box is a receptacle into which items can be placed without incurring consequences related to those items. Amnesty bins have been used for various items, including drugs, weapons, fruit, invasive species, and animals. A version of an amnesty bin is also used at Amazon warehouses for damaged items.
mgt: An amnesty bin is a container used to allow individuals to anonymously dispose of prohibited or unwanted items without facing legal or other consequences. They are employed in various contexts, including drug harm reduction at music festivals, biosecurity at airports in Australia, New Zealand, and Hawaii, cannabis disposal at U.S. airports, and knife crime prevention in the UK. Amnesty bins are also used in Amazon fulfillment centers to handle damaged or unscannable items.

trgt: Facebook, Inc. v. Duguid, 592 U.S. ___ (2021), was a United States Supreme Court case related to the definition and function of auto dialers under the Telephone Consumer Protection Act of 1991 (TCPA) to send unsolicited text messages. In a unanimous decision based on statutory interpretation of the TCPA, the Supreme Court ruled that auto dialers are defined by their function to either store or produce telephone numbers from a random or sequential number generator.
mgt: *Facebook, Inc. v. Duguid* was a 2021 United States Supreme Court case concerning the definition of an "automatic telephone dialing system" (ATDS) under the Telephone Consumer Protection Act of 1991 (TCPA). The Court unanimously ruled that to qualify as an ATDS, a device must use a random or sequential number generator to store or produce phone numbers. The decision narrowed the scope of the TCPA, favoring telemarketers and raising concerns about increased unwanted calls.

trgt: "D.A.N.C.E." is the second single by Justice and the first from their album †. It includes edited and extended versions of "D.A.N.C.E", a rougher mix in the style of their earlier releases, "B.E.A.T", and the track "Phantom" which was previously issued in limited quantities twice on 12"vinyl preceding the release of"D.A.N.C.E.".
mgt: "D.A.N.C.E." is a 2007 single by French electronic music duo Justice from their debut album *†*. A tribute to Michael Jackson, the song references several of his works and features vocals by the Foundation for Young Musicians choir. Accompanied by a pop art-inspired music video directed by Jonas & François, it received critical acclaim, including a nomination for "Video of the Year" at the 2007 MTV Video Music Awards and a win at the MTV Europe Music Awards. The song was ranked number 4 on *Rolling Stone*'s list of the 100 Best Songs of 2007 and inspired numerous remixes, including one by rapper Wale.

trgt: The tie is a symbol in the shape of an arc similar to a large breve, used in Greek, phonetic alphabets, and Z notation. It can be used between two characters with spacing as punctuation, non-spacing as a diacritic, or (underneath) as a proofreading mark. It can be above or below, and reversed. Its forms are called tie, double breve, enotikon or papyrological hyphen, ligature tie, and undertie.
mgt: The tie is a typographic and phonetic symbol used in various contexts, including Cyrillic transliteration, Greek texts, vocal music scores, and phonetic alphabets. In Cyrillic transliteration, it connects letters to represent single Cyrillic characters. In Greek, the enotikon served as a hyphen in continuous script. In music, the undertie indicates syllable elision. The International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) employs ties for affricates, double articulation, and linking. It also appears in computing and proofreading.

trgt: Manitoba Centre for Health Policy (MCHP) is a public, not-for-profit research unit in the Department of Community Health Sciences within the Rady Faculty of Health Sciences at the University of Manitoba (Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada). MCHP provides and uses population-based administrative data to inform health policy, social policy and health and social service delivery provincially, nationally and internationally. MCHP also creates population-based research on health and the social determinants of health.
mgt: The Manitoba Centre for Health Policy (MCHP), established in 1991 by Drs. Noralou and Leslie Roos, is an academic research institute based at the University of Manitoba in Winnipeg, Canada. It specializes in population health and health care services research, maintaining the Manitoba Population Research Data Repository, a comprehensive de-identified database on health, social, and economic data. MCHP collaborates with government and academic partners, focusing on health determinants, Indigenous well-being, and service provision, while ensuring strict privacy and security protocols.

trgt: Error concealment is a technique used in signal processing that aims to minimize the deterioration of signals caused by missing data, called packet loss. A signal is a message sent from a transmitter to a receiver in multiple small packets. Packet loss occurs when these packets are misdirected, delayed, resequenced, or corrupted.
mgt: Error concealment refers to techniques used to mitigate the effects of data loss or corruption during transmission. These methods are categorized as receiver-based, such as waveform substitution, zero insertion, interpolation, and buffering, or transmitter-based, including retransmission, packet repetition, and interleaving. Applications span both analog and digital domains, including video, audio restoration, web browsing, and video streaming.

trgt:
Associated Equipment Company (AEC) was a British vehicle manufacturer that built buses, motorcoaches and trucks from 1912 until 1979. The name Associated Equipment Company was hardly ever used; instead it traded under the AEC and ACLO brands.

During World War One, AEC was the most prolific British lorry manufacturer; after building London's buses before the great war.
mgt: The Associated Equipment Company (AEC) was a British automotive manufacturer founded in 1912 and based in Southall, England. Specializing in lorries, buses, and motorcoaches, AEC gained prominence for its reliable vehicles, including contributions to military efforts during both World Wars. Acquired by Leyland Motors in 1962, AEC ceased production in 1979 following the closure of its Southall plant by British Leyland.

trgt: Stonestown station is a light rail station on the Muni Metro M Ocean View line, located in the median of 19th Avenue adjacent to Stonestown Galleria and the Lakeside neigborhood in San Francisco, California. It opened in 1925 with the first phase of the line and was rebuilt with a high-level island platform in 1993.
mgt: Stonestown Galleria station is a light rail stop on the Muni Metro M Ocean View line in San Francisco, California. Located at 19th Avenue and Winston Drive, it serves the nearby Stonestown Galleria mall. Originally opened in 1925, the station was rebuilt with an accessible island platform in 1993 as part of modernization efforts.

trgt: Lau is a populated area, a socken (not to be confused with parish), on the Swedish island of Gotland. It comprises the same area as the administrative Lau District, established on 1 January 2016. Originally an island, it is now part of the main Gotland island due to the isostasy. It is mostly known for the good water from the spring Lau Käldu.
mgt: Lau is a socken (district) on the eastern coast of Gotland, Sweden, encompassing the village of Lau kyrkby and the medieval Lau Church. The area is rural, featuring farms, forests, and the Lau Cove. Historically significant, Lau contains Bronze and Iron Age archaeological sites, including grave mounds, stone ships, and a hillfort. Nearby are the Laus Holmar islets, known for bird reserves and grazing.

trgt:
The three-point turn (sometimes called a Y-turn, K-turn, or broken U-turn) is the standard method of turning a vehicle around to face the opposite direction in a limited space, using forward and reverse gears. This is typically done when the road is too narrow for a U-turn.

This manoeuvre is a common requirement in driving tests.
mgt: A three-point turn, also known as a Y-turn, K-turn, or turnabout, is a driving maneuver used to reverse direction on a road. It involves a sequence of forward and reverse movements. While commonly called a "three-point turn," additional steps may be needed on narrow roads or with longer vehicles.

trgt: "You're a Big Girl Now" is a song by Bob Dylan, released on his 15th studio album, Blood on the Tracks, in 1975. It is one of five songs on the album that Dylan initially recorded in New York City in September 1974 and then re-recorded in Minneapolis in December that year. The latter recording, made on December 27, 1974, became the album track.
mgt: "You're a Big Girl Now" is a song by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan, featured on his 1975 album *Blood on the Tracks*. Recorded on December 27, 1974, in Minneapolis, the folk rock track explores themes of heartbreak and longing, with critics often linking it to Dylan's personal life, though he denied such interpretations. Praised for its emotional depth, the song has been included in several Dylan compilations and performed live over 200 times.

trgt: "Lottery" (Lang-hi, Lang-ur) is a Hindustani short story. It was written by Indian author Premchand. The story is told in narrative form from the perspective of an unnamed school teacher. The story has been adapted into play format, with theatrical performances presented by Lalit Parimoo's Natsamaj Theatre Group, as well as Mujeeb Khan's Ideal Drama and Entertainment Academy.
mgt: "Lottery" (लॉटरी) is a 1933 Hindi short story by Indian writer Premchand. First published in *Zamana*, it follows a school teacher and his friend Vikram as they pool funds to buy a lottery ticket, leading to family conflicts and moral dilemmas. The story, known for its dramatic irony and moral message, has been included in Indian textbooks, compilations, and theatrical adaptations.

trgt: Lyme is a town along the Connecticut River in Grafton County, New Hampshire, United States. The population was 1,745 as of the 2020 census. Lyme is home to the Chaffee Natural Conservation Area. The Dartmouth Skiway is in the eastern part of town, near the village of Lyme Center. The Appalachian Trail passes through the town's heavily wooded eastern end.
mgt: Lyme is a rural town in Grafton County, New Hampshire, United States, incorporated in 1761. Located along the Connecticut River, it features a historic town common, 19th-century buildings, and the longest row of contiguous horse sheds in New England. The town is home to scenic natural landmarks, including Smarts Mountain, Holts Ledge, and several ponds. As of the 2020 census, Lyme had a population of 1,745.

trgt: Dennis Knight (born December 26, 1968) is an American chef and retired professional wrestler. He is best known for his appearances with World Championship Wrestling between 1992 and 1994 under the ring name Tex Slazenger and with the World Wrestling Federation (WWF, now WWE) between 1996 and 2001 under the ring names Phineas I. Godwinn and Mideon, as well as his real name.
mgt: Mideon, born Dennis Knight on December 26, 1968, in Clearwater, Florida, is a retired American professional wrestler best known for his time in the World Wrestling Federation (WWF, now WWE). Debuting in 1989, he wrestled under various personas, including Phineas I. Godwinn, Tex Slazenger, and Mideon, a member of The Ministry of Darkness. After retiring in 2006, Knight became a chef in Clearwater, Florida.

trgt: Lake Temescal is a small reservoir in the Oakland hills, in northeastern Oakland, California. It is the centerpiece of Temescal Regional Recreation Area, also known as Temescal Regional Park (and originally, Lake Temescal Regional Park). It is a part of the East Bay Regional Park District.
mgt: Lake Temescal is a reservoir located in Oakland, California, created in 1868 by damming Temescal Creek to provide drinking water for the East Bay. Once a sag pond along the Hayward Fault, it became a public park in 1936 under the East Bay Regional Park District. Today, the lake features recreational amenities, including a beach, fishing, and trails, and receives around 200,000 visitors annually. It faces challenges such as sedimentation, blue-green algae outbreaks, and earthquake-related risks due to its location on the Hayward Fault.

trgt: Rugby League Week (frequently abbreviated to RLW) was the highest selling Australian rugby league magazine, ahead of major competitor Big League. It was published weekly (on Wednesdays) during the Australian rugby league season, which runs from March to late September (roughly corresponding to the southern hemisphere autumn and winter). The magazine was headquartered in Sydney.
mgt: *Rugby League Week* was an Australian sports magazine dedicated to rugby league, published weekly during the NRL season from 1970 to 2017. Owned by Bauer Media Group, it featured news, player profiles, match previews, and ratings. The magazine introduced the "Player of the Year" award and created "The Immortals," honoring rugby league's greatest players.

trgt: Anula Karunathilaka (Lang-si, born 23 January 1946) is a Sri Lankan film actress and theatrical performer. Her work is noted during the 70s film and cinema history in Sri Lanka. One of the most popular actresses in Sinhala cinema in 1960s, she is best known for the popular role 'Dammi' in the blockbuster film Golu Hadawatha.
mgt: Anula Karunathilaka (born January 23, 1946) is a Sri Lankan actress known for her acclaimed role as Dhammi in the iconic film *Golu Hadawatha* (Silent Heart), which earned her a Sarasaviya Award in 1969. Active from the 1960s to 2010, she contributed to the evolution of Sinhala cinema, moving away from Indianized productions.

trgt: Hans Klumbach (26 April 1904 – 14 December 1992) was a German archaeologist and scholar of classical and provincial Roman studies. He served as a curator at, and later the director of, the Romano-Germanic Central Museum. His 1973 work Spätrömische Gardehelme laid bare the relationship between the late Roman ridge helmets and the later Scandinavian and Anglo-Saxon crested helmets.
mgt: Hans Klumbach (26 April 1904 – 14 December 1992) was a German archaeologist and expert on Roman helmets. He served as director of the Romano-Germanic Central Museum in Mainz and was an honorary professor at Johannes Gutenberg University Mainz. Known for his work on late Roman ridge helmets, his 1973 publication *Spätrömische Gardehelme* established a typology for these artifacts.

trgt: Nakae Chōmin (中江 兆民, December 8, 1847 – December 13, 1901) was the pen-name of a journalist, political theorist and statesman in Meiji-period Japan. His real name was Nakae Tokusuke (中江 篤助). His major contribution was the popularization of the egalitarian doctrines of the French philosopher Jean-Jacques Rousseau in Japan. As a result, Nakae is thought to have been a major force in the development of liberalism in early Japanese politics.
mgt: Nakae Chōmin (December 8, 1847 – December 13, 1901) was a Japanese journalist, politician, and intellectual known for his contributions to liberalism and the introduction of Western democratic ideas to Japan. He translated works by Jean-Jacques Rousseau and advocated for political reform through his newspapers, which often faced government suppression. A member of the Diet of Japan, Nakae supported democratic principles and infrastructure development. He continued writing until his death from esophageal cancer.

trgt: Oxhorn Run is a tributary of Painter Run in Sullivan County, Pennsylvania, in the United States. It is approximately 1.8 miles (2.9 km) long and flows through Davidson Township. The watershed of the stream has an area of 0.87 square miles (2.3 km2). The stream is considered to be impaired by atmospheric deposition and pH. Wisconsinan Ice-Contact Stratified Drift, Wisconsinan Till, and bedrock consisting of sandstone and shale occur in its vicinity.
mgt: Oxhorn Run is a 1.8-mile-long (2.9 km) tributary of Painter Run in Davidson Township, Sullivan County, Pennsylvania. Originating on the edge of a plateau, it flows through a deepening valley before joining Painter Run. The stream's watershed spans 0.87 square miles (2.3 km²) and lies within the Upper Susquehanna-Lackawanna drainage basin. Oxhorn Run is impaired due to atmospheric deposition and pH issues, making it the only non-attaining stream in the Painter Run watershed.

trgt: Azerbaijan National Carpet Museum (Lang-az, formerly called the Azerbaijan Carpet Museum) is a museum located in Baku that displays Azerbaijani carpets and rugs with historical and modern weaving techniques and materials. It has the largest collection of Azerbaijani carpets in the world. First opened on Neftchiler Avenue in 1967, it moved to a new building on the Baku's seafront park in 2014.
mgt: The Azerbaijan Carpet Museum, established in 1967 in Baku, Azerbaijan, is dedicated to the preservation and display of Azerbaijani carpet art and cultural heritage. Initially housed in the Juma Mosque, the museum moved to a purpose-built structure resembling a rolled carpet in 2014, designed by Austrian architect Franz Janz. Its collection includes over 10,000 items, such as carpets, ceramics, metalworks, and national garments, spanning centuries. The museum also hosts international exhibitions, public lectures, and research initiatives, showcasing Azerbaijani craftsmanship globally.

trgt: Larry Weil (June 26, 1951 – April 10, 2017) was an Irish-American professional wrestler, manager and trainer better known under his ring name, "Pretty Boy" Larry Sharpe. Sharpe is perhaps most well-known for creating the Monster Factory professional wrestling school. He grew up in Castleisland, Ireland and was the original trainer of Kevin Von Erich, and many other well-known wrestlers.
mgt: Larry Sharpe (born Larry Weil; June 26, 1951 – April 10, 2017) was an Irish-American professional wrestler, trainer, and promoter. Active from 1974 to 2004, he competed in promotions such as the WWWF, Stampede Wrestling, and WWC, winning multiple championships. Sharpe co-founded The Monster Factory, one of the first public professional wrestling schools, training notable wrestlers like Bam Bam Bigelow.

trgt:
Mieczyslaw "Mickey Doyle" Kuzik is a fictional character in the HBO TV series Boardwalk Empire. He is played by Paul Sparks. Mickey Doyle is loosely based on Polish American mobster Mickey Duffy.

Mickey is a gangster in Prohibition-era Atlantic City. For the first two seasons, he is the main rival of harlem liquor kingpin Chalky White. He is known for his distinctive, nasal laugh, and serves as the series' comic relief.
mgt: Mickey Doyle, born Mieczyslaw "Mickey Doyle" Kuzik, is a fictional character from the HBO series *Boardwalk Empire*, portrayed by Paul Sparks. A Polish-American gangster and bootlegger, Mickey is a recurring figure in the series, navigating alliances and betrayals within the Prohibition-era criminal underworld. Initially working against Nucky Thompson, he later becomes a key member of Nucky's operations. Loosely based on real-life mobster Mickey Duffy, Doyle meets a violent end in the series' final season.

trgt:
The City of Lost Children (Lang-fr) is a 1995 science fantasy film directed by Marc Caro and Jean-Pierre Jeunet, written by Jeunet and Gilles Adrien, and starring Ron Perlman. An international co-production of companies from France, Germany, and Spain, the film is stylistically related to the previous and subsequent Jeunet films, Delicatessen and Amélie.

The musical score was composed by Angelo Badalamenti, with costumes designed by Jean-Paul Gaultier. It was entered into the 1995 Cannes Film Festival.
mgt: *The City of Lost Children* (French: *La Cité des enfants perdus*) is a 1995 French science fantasy film directed by Marc Caro and Jean-Pierre Jeunet. The story follows a carnival strongman, One (Ron Perlman), and a young orphan, Miette (Judith Vittet), as they attempt to rescue One's kidnapped brother from Krank (Daniel Emilfork), a malevolent being who steals children's dreams. Known for its surreal visuals and steampunk aesthetic, the film received generally favorable reviews for its design and atmosphere, though its narrative was critiqued as confusing. Despite its critical acclaim, it was a box-office bomb, grossing $11 million against an $18 million budget.

trgt: USS Romulus (ARL-22) was laid down as a United States Navy LST-542-class tank landing ship but converted to one of 39 Achelous-class repair ships that were used for repairing landing craft during World War II. Named for Romulus (along with Remus, one of the legendary twin sons of Mars and the Vestal Rhea Silvia), she was the only US Naval vessel to bear the name.
mgt: USS *Romulus* (ARL-22) was a United States Navy landing craft repair ship commissioned in 1944. Originally designated LST-962, she served in the Pacific during World War II, providing repair services in Guam, Saipan, Japan, and Okinawa. Recommissioned for the Korean War, she supported UN forces as a repair facility. Decommissioned in 1960, she was transferred to the Philippine Navy, serving as BRP *Aklan* and later *Kamagong* until 1989.

trgt:
Jabłonna [jaˈbwɔnna] is a village in Legionowo County, Masovian Voivodeship, in east-central Poland. It is the seat of the Gmina Jabłonna (administrative district). It lies approximately 4 kilometres (2 mi) south of Legionowo and 18 km (11 mi) north of Warsaw.

The village has a population of 7,365.

The town has two major landmarks: the Jabłonna Palace (Lang-pl) and the Mother of God Queen of Poland Church (Lang-pl) which are in the center of the village.
mgt: Jabłonna is a village in Legionowo County, Masovian Voivodeship, Poland, located near Warsaw. It is known for its historical landmarks, including the Jabłonna Palace, a baroque residence built in the late 18th century, now serving as a hotel and event center owned by the Polish Academy of Sciences. The village played a role in World War II and features remnants of German fortifications. Jabłonna also has a rich history of agriculture, education, and community services, including a voluntary fire brigade established in 1912.

trgt:
The 2011–12 Kategoria Superiore was the 73rd official season, or 76th season of top-tier football in Albania (including three unofficial championships of WW2) and the fourteenth season under the name Kategoria superiore. The season began on 10 September 2011 and ended on 12 May 2012. The defending champions were Skënderbeu, who won their second Albanian league championship last season and third in their history.

The size of the league was expanded from twelve to fourteen teams this season.
mgt: The 2011–12 Kategoria Superiore was the 73rd season of Albania's top-tier football league, held from 10 September 2011 to 12 May 2012. Skënderbeu won their third Albanian title and qualified for the UEFA Champions League, while Teuta, Tirana, and Flamurtari secured Europa League spots. The season featured 108 matches with 274 goals scored, averaging 2.54 per match. Roland Dervishi was the top scorer with 20 goals. Kamza, Pogradeci, and Dinamo were relegated, while the league expanded to 14 teams that season.

trgt: Gayopakhyanam (Telugu: గయోపాఖ్యానం) is a Telugu play written by Chilakamarti Lakshmi Narasimham in 1890. It is also called Prachanda Yadavam (The story of fierce Yadava King - Sri Krishna). This play is the forerunner in presenting mythical themes in the Hindu epics. The plot is based on war between Nara-Narayana, the incarnation of Arjuna and Sri Krishna induced by Gaya, a Gandharva King.
mgt: *Gayopakhyanam* (గయోపాఖ్యానం) is a Telugu play written by Chilakamarti Lakshmi Narasimham in 1890 at the age of 22. Based on a Hindu epic, it narrates the story of Gaya, a Gandharva king, and his conflict with Sri Krishna, which is ultimately resolved by Lord Shiva. The play was highly popular, selling over 100,000 copies after its publication in 1909, and was later adapted into films, including *Sri Krishnarjuna Yudham* (1962/1963).

trgt: The 2017 NHL Expansion Draft was an expansion draft conducted by the National Hockey League on June 18–20, 2017 to fill the roster of the league's expansion team for the 2017–18 season, the Vegas Golden Knights. The team's selections were announced on June 21 during the NHL Awards ceremony at T-Mobile Arena.
mgt: The 2017 NHL Expansion Draft was held on June 21, 2017, at T-Mobile Arena in Paradise, Nevada, to establish the roster of the Vegas Golden Knights, the NHL's 31st franchise. Las Vegas was awarded the team for the 2017–18 season, marking the NHL's first expansion since 2000. The draft rules allowed the Golden Knights to select one player from each of the 30 existing teams, with restrictions on protected players to ensure competitive balance. Teams submitted their protected player lists on June 17, 2017, and Vegas was required to draft a minimum of 14 forwards, nine defensemen, and three goaltenders. The Golden Knights also negotiated trades with other teams in exchange for selecting or avoiding specific players. The draft played a pivotal role in shaping the Golden Knights' inaugural roster, with some selected players later traded before the start of the season.

trgt: Culbert Levy Olson (November 7, 1876 – April 13, 1962) was an American lawyer and politician. A Democratic Party member, Olson was involved in Utah and California politics and was elected as the 29th governor of California from 1939 to 1943.
mgt: Culbert Levy Olson (November 7, 1876 – April 13, 1962) was an American politician and attorney who served as the 29th governor of California from 1939 to 1943, becoming the first Democrat to hold the office in 40 years. A staunch progressive and supporter of New Deal policies, Olson advocated for labor rights, social welfare programs, and government reform but faced significant opposition from a conservative legislature. A secular atheist, he was known for his criticism of religious influence in politics. Olson also supported Japanese American internment during World War II. After leaving office, he remained active in law and secularist advocacy, serving as president of the United Secularists of America.

trgt:
The 2013 Stadium Super Trucks Series was the inaugural season of Stadium Super Trucks competition. The series marked the revival of off-road racing in stadiums, originally created by Mickey Thompson, and reintroduced by former NASCAR driver Robby Gordon. The trucks were one of four classes available, along with Bigfoot Monster Trucks, Super Trophy Karts, and Super Buggys.

The champion was awarded the Mickey Thompson Championship Trophy. Gordon won the championship by seven points over Rob MacCachren.
mgt: The 2013 Stadium Super Trucks Series was the inaugural season of the off-road racing series founded by Robby Gordon. The season featured races at various venues, including stadiums and road courses, with notable stops at the Grand Prix of Long Beach, Crandon International Off-Road Raceway, and Caesars Palace in Las Vegas. Justin Lofton, Rob MacCachren, and Sheldon Creed were among the season's standout drivers. Robby Gordon won the championship by a narrow seven-point margin over MacCachren.

trgt: Gregory Brian Standridge (May 6, 1967 – November 16, 2017) was an American politician who served as a Republican member of the Arkansas State Senate for District 16, which includes Newton and Pope counties and parts of Boone, Carroll and Van Buren counties in the northern portion of his state.
