mgt: Greg Standridge (May 6, 1967 – November 16, 2017) was an American politician who served as a Republican member of the Arkansas Senate from the 16th district from 2015 until his death in 2017. A graduate of Arkansas Tech University, he won a special election to succeed Michael Lamoureux and passed away from cancer at age 50.

trgt: The Charleston Dirty Birds are an American professional baseball team based in Charleston, West Virginia. They are a member of the South Division of the Atlantic League of Professional Baseball, a partner league of Major League Baseball. The Dirty Birds have played their home games at GoMart Ballpark since 2005.
mgt: The Charleston Dirty Birds are a professional baseball team based in Charleston, West Virginia. Founded in 1987, they play in the Atlantic League of Professional Baseball, an independent MLB Partner League. The team, originally known as the Charleston Wheelers, has undergone several name changes and affiliations before adopting its current name in 2021. The Dirty Birds play home games at GoMart Ballpark, which opened in 2005. The name "Dirty Birds" references the canary in a coal mine, symbolizing the region's mining heritage. The franchise has a rich history of minor league baseball in Charleston, dating back to 1910.

trgt: Select Registry, Distinguished Inns of North America, is a non-profit association of more than 300 country inns, B&Bs and boutique hotels throughout the United States. Founded in 1972, the association is based in Greensboro, Georgia, United States and led by CEO Mark Reichle.
mgt: Select Registry is an association of independent innkeepers founded in 1972 by travel writer Norman Simpson, who documented unique lodging in his book *Country Inns and Back Roads*. Headquartered in Greensboro, Georgia, it includes bed and breakfasts, boutique hotels, and small resorts across North America. Members undergo quality assurance inspections and are featured in the association's travel magazine, *Inn Scene*.

trgt: Clyde Lee Chambliss Jr. (born April 16, 1969) is an American Republican politician who has served in the Alabama Senate from the 30th district since 2014. He completed his degree in Civil Engineering from the University of Alabama in 1992, after which he started his civil engineering firm. Chambliss and his wife Tara Herring Chambliss have three daughters, Kristen, Lauren, and Madison.
mgt: Clyde Lee Chambliss Jr. (born April 16, 1969) is an American politician serving as a Republican member of the Alabama Senate from the 30th district since 2014. In 2019, he sponsored the Human Life Protection Act, a controversial abortion law banning most abortions in Alabama, with exceptions only for cases where the mother’s health is endangered.

trgt: The Satanita AC 59 is an English contract law case, decided in the Court of Appeal, which concerned the formation of a contract. It is notable because it stands as an example of a case which does not fit the typical pattern of offer and acceptance that English law purports to require to find agreement.
mgt: *The Satanita* was a legal case, *Clarke v Earl of Dunraven* (1897), decided by the House of Lords. It concerned a yacht race where the *Satanita* collided with the *Valkyrie II*, causing it to sink. The court held that a contract existed between the yacht owners under the race rules, making *The Satanita*'s owner liable for damages.

trgt: The John Wescott Three-Decker, also known as the Wescott-Mulcahy Three-Decker is a historic triple decker house in Worcester, Massachusetts. It is a well-preserved example of a Queen Anne triple-decker, built about 1892. The building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1990.
mgt: The John Wescott Three-Decker is a historic triple-decker house located at 454 Pleasant Street in Worcester, Massachusetts. Built around 1892 in the Queen Anne style, it features distinctive architectural details, including alternating clapboard and shingle siding and decorative brackets. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1990.

trgt:
The Ladies of the House, also known under the working title of Stripped, is a 2014 grindhouse film and the directorial debut of actor John Stuart Wildman. The film had its world premiere on April 4, 2014 at the Dallas International Film Festival, and marks the first non-pornographic film by the former adult actress Belladonna.

Funding for The Ladies of the House was partially raised through a successful Kickstarter campaign.
mgt: *The Ladies of the House* is a 2014 American horror film directed by John Stuart Wildman. The story follows three men who, after a deadly encounter with a stripper, are hunted by her cannibalistic roommates. The film premiered at the Dallas International Film Festival and received positive reviews from horror outlets like *Dread Central* and *Bloody Disgusting*, which praised its intelligent approach to grindhouse cinema despite some imperfections.

trgt: Rajmohan's Wife (1864) is the debut novella of the Indian author Bankim Chandra Chatterjee. The English-language novella, claimed by many to be the very first Indian novella, was published by a lesser-known periodical named Indian Field edited by Kishori Chand Mitra. The novella abruptly ended for unknown reasons, and later grew more obscure, until it was rediscovered and republished.
mgt: *Rajmohan's Wife* is an 1864 English-language novella by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay, considered the first Indian novel written in English. Set in a rural Bengal village, the story explores themes of love, betrayal, and social conflict through the life of Matangini, a woman trapped in an abusive marriage. The novella is notable for its historical significance and narrative complexity.

trgt: Poot Mae Nam Khong (Lang-th, RTGS: Phut Mae Nam Khong, literally The Spirit of Mekong River) is a Thai Horror Superstition Lakorn, remade from 1990 lakorn of the same name. The lakorn starring by Weir Sukollawat and Jui Warattaya as the Main roles and Morakot Aimee Kittisara as the ghost.
mgt: *Poot Mae Nam Khong* is a Thai television drama series that aired in three iterations: 1992–1993, 2008, and 2022. Created by Sumruay Rukchart, the series follows Dr. Akkanee, who uncovers a vengeful spirit tied to the Mekong River and his past life. Combining romance, supernatural elements, and themes of reincarnation, the show explores love, betrayal, and redemption. It gained significant popularity, particularly during its 2008 run, becoming a ratings success and revitalizing the career of lead actress Jui Warattaya.

trgt: William Szathmary (October 5, 1924 – June 15, 2017), known as Bill Dana, was an American comedian, actor, and screenwriter. He often appeared on television shows such as The Ed Sullivan Show, frequently in the guise of a heavily accented Bolivian character named José Jiménez. Dana often portrayed the Jiménez character as an astronaut.
mgt: Bill Dana (born William Szathmary; October 5, 1924 – June 15, 2017) was an American comedian, actor, and screenwriter best known for creating and portraying the character José Jiménez. A prolific comedy writer, he contributed to shows like *The Steve Allen Show* and *Get Smart*. Dana also starred in *The Bill Dana Show* (1963–1965) and wrote the iconic *All in the Family* episode "Sammy's Visit." He retired the José Jiménez character in 1970 due to changing cultural sensitivities. Dana was instrumental in establishing the American Comedy Archives at Emerson College. He passed away at age 92 in Nashville, Tennessee.

trgt: Alison Mary Fiske (2 August 1943 – 26 July 2020) was an English actress, who won Actress of the Year in a New Play at the 1977 Laurence Olivier Awards for playing Fish in Dusa, Fish, Stats and Vi. She was also nominated in the 1979 Laurence Olivier Awards for Best Actress in a Supporting Role for playing Evie in For Services Rendered, and she won awards for her television performance in Helen: A Woman of Today.
mgt: Alison Fiske (2 August 1943 – 26 July 2020) was a British actress known for her work on stage and screen. She trained at the Drama Centre London and began her career with Theatre Group 20, later joining the Royal Shakespeare Company, where she performed in numerous productions. Fiske appeared in notable television series, including *The Roads to Freedom* and *Cold Blood*. She retired in 2008 and lived in East Sussex until her death from cancer in 2020.

trgt: In computer engineering, directory-based cache coherence is a type of cache coherence mechanism, where directories are used to manage caches in place of bus snooping. Bus snooping methods scale poorly due to the use of broadcasting. These methods can be used to target both performance and scalability of directory systems.
mgt: Directory-based cache coherence is a mechanism used in multiprocessor systems to maintain consistency between cached copies of shared memory. It employs a directory to track the state of cache lines across processors. Various formats, including full bit vector, coarse bit vector, sparse directory, number-balanced binary tree, chained directory, and limited pointer, optimize storage and performance by balancing scalability, memory overhead, and communication efficiency.

trgt: Klytaemnestra (minor planet designation: 179 Klytaemnestra) is a stony Telramund asteroid from the outer regions of the asteroid belt, approximately 77 kilometers (48 miles) in diameter. It was discovered on 11 November 1877, by Canadian-American astronomer James Craig Watson at the old Ann Arbor Observatory in Michigan, United States. It was his last discovery three years before his death. The transitional S-type asteroid has a rotation period of 11.17 hours. It was named after Clytemnestra from Greek mythology.
mgt: 179 Klytaemnestra is a stony asteroid in the outer region of the main asteroid belt, discovered on November 11, 1877, by James Craig Watson at the Ann Arbor Observatory. It is the largest member of the Telramund family of asteroids and orbits the Sun every 5.12 years at a distance of 2.6–3.3 AU. Classified as an S-type asteroid, it has a rotation period of 11.17 hours and a diameter ranging from 64 to 90 kilometers. The asteroid is named after Clytemnestra, a figure from Greek mythology.

trgt: Eclipse Island is a barren island in Western Australia, 17 kilometres (10.6 mi) due south of Albany, and 6.1 km (3.8 mi) south of the nearest point of the mainland coast, which is Cave Point on the southern coast of Torndirrup Peninsula. Eclipse Island Lighthouse is located near the centre of the island. Nearby South West Island contains Western Australia's southernmost landmass.
mgt: Eclipse Island is a granite island located off the south coast of Western Australia, covering 99 hectares (245 acres). Named by Captain George Vancouver in 1791 after observing a solar eclipse, it is a Class 1A Nature Reserve and an Important Bird Area, supporting significant populations of seabirds. The island features a lighthouse, weather station, and a historic landing stage.

trgt: Lewis James Emanuel (born 4 October 1983) is a former professional footballer who played as a left back or as a left midfield. He played for Bradford City, Luton Town and Brentford. He retired in 2010 having played more than 200 games. Three years after finishing his playing career, Emaunel was jailed for eight years for his part in an armed robbery.
mgt: Lewis James Emanuel (born 4 October 1983) is an English former professional footballer who played as a left-back and left-winger. He began his career at Bradford City, later playing for Luton Town and Brentford. Emanuel was part of Luton's 2009 Football League Trophy-winning team. His career ended following injuries, and in 2013, he was sentenced to eight years in prison for armed robbery.

trgt: "Stay" is a song written by Bob Khozouri and Mark Stevens, originally recorded by American singer Glenn Jones and released in 1990. It reached number six on the US Billboard Hot Black Singles chart. The song became a worldwide hit for British girl group Eternal in 1993 and 1994.
mgt: "Stay" is a 1990 R&B single by Glenn Jones from his album *All for You*, written and produced by Mark Stevens and Bobby Khozouri. In 1993, British girl group Eternal released a successful cover as their debut single, reaching number four on the UK Singles Chart and number 19 on the US Billboard Hot 100.

trgt: David Loud (born November 28, 1961, in Cincinnati, Ohio) is an American music supervisor, music director, conductor, vocal and dance arranger, pianist and actor. He is best known for his collaborations with and interpretations of the music of both Kander and Ebb and Stephen Sondheim.
mgt: David Loud (born November 28, 1961) is an American music director, conductor, vocal arranger, pianist, and actor, known for his extensive work on Broadway. He has collaborated with renowned composers such as Kander and Ebb and Stephen Sondheim, contributing to productions like *Ragtime*, *The Visit*, *Sondheim on Sondheim*, and *The Scottsboro Boys*. Loud began his career as an actor in *Merrily We Roll Along* and later transitioned to music direction, earning acclaim for his work on *And the World Goes 'Round* and *She Loves Me*. He has also created and arranged performances for venues like Carnegie Hall and Lincoln Center and currently teaches at the Manhattan School of Music.

trgt:
Hope High School is a comprehensive public secondary school in Hope, Arkansas, United States. The school serves grades 9 through 12 and is the only such school in the Hope city limits, as well as one of four high schools in Hempstead County. It is the sole high school in the Hope School District.

It serves Hope, Fulton, Oakhaven, Patmos, Perrytown, and Washington.
mgt: Hope High School is a public secondary school in Hope, Arkansas, established in 1931 to replace the earlier Garland High School. Part of the Hope School District, it serves grades 9–12 and offers a curriculum based on the Arkansas Department of Education's Smart Core program, including Advanced Placement courses. Accredited by AdvancED since 1928, its mascot is the Bobcat, and its teams compete in the 5A South Conference.

trgt: Heart of Glass (Lang-de) is a 1976 German film directed and produced by Werner Herzog, set in 18th century Bavaria. The film was written by Herzog, based partly on a story by Herbert Achternbusch. The main character is Hias, based on the legendary Bavarian prophet, Mühlhiasl.
mgt: *Heart of Glass* (German: *Herz aus Glas*) is a 1976 West German film directed by Werner Herzog. Set in 18th-century Bavaria, it follows a town's descent into madness after the secret to producing its prized ruby glass is lost. Notably, most of the actors performed under hypnosis, creating a trance-like atmosphere. The film was shot in Bavaria, Switzerland, and other global locations, with a score by Popol Vuh.

trgt: Andre Tierre Royal (born December 1, 1972) is a former professional American football player in the National Football League who played linebacker for five seasons for the Carolina Panthers and the Indianapolis Colts. Royal had 140 career tackles, 6 solo sacks, 4 forced fumbles and 2 fumble recoveries. Royal was traded to the Indianapolis Colts following several off-the-field incidents prior to the start of the season with The New Orleans Saints.
mgt: Andre Royal (born December 1, 1972) is a former American football linebacker who played in the National Football League (NFL) from 1995 to 2000. A member of the 1992 National Championship team at the University of Alabama, Royal went on to play for the Carolina Panthers, New Orleans Saints, and Indianapolis Colts. His career included All-Pro recognition, but it was marred by controversies, including a hazing lawsuit and disputes with coaches. Royal retired in 2001 due to head injuries.

trgt: The Button car plan, also known as the Button plan, was the informal name given to the Motor Industry Development Plan, an Australian federal (Labor) government initiative intended to rationalise the Australian motor vehicle industry and transition it to lower levels of protection. It took its name from Senator John Button, the then federal Minister for Commerce, Trade and Industry.
mgt: The Button car plan, introduced in 1985, was an Australian government initiative aimed at consolidating the local automotive industry to improve efficiency and competitiveness. It sought to reduce the number of locally manufactured car models and gradually lower import tariffs. The plan led to widespread badge engineering but faced criticism for its unpopularity with consumers and manufacturers. Ultimately, it failed to sustain local car production, with all major manufacturers ceasing Australian operations by 2017.

trgt: Qin Shengxiang (Chinese: 秦生祥; born February 1957) is an admiral of the Chinese People's Liberation Army Navy (PLAN) who was Political Commissar of the PLA Navy from September 2017 to January 2022. He formerly served as Director of the General Office of the Central Military Commission.
mgt: Qin Shengxiang (born February 1957) is a retired Chinese admiral and politician who served as Political Commissar of the People's Liberation Army Navy from 2017 to 2022. He previously held key roles, including Director of the General Office of the Central Military Commission from 2012 to 2017.

trgt: The 1952–53 season was Fussball Club Basel 1893's 59th season in their existence. It was their seventh consecutive season in the top flight of Swiss football after their promotion from the Nationalliga B the season 1945–46. They played their home games in the Landhof, in the Wettstein Quarter in Kleinbasel. Jules Düblin was the club's chairman for the seventh successive season.
mgt: The 1952–53 season was a successful campaign for FC Basel, as they won the Nationalliga A championship under player-coach René Bader, with Willy Dürr as his assistant. Basel played 43 matches across all competitions, winning 27 and scoring 146 goals. Josef Hügi was the season's top scorer with 38 goals. In the Swiss Cup, Basel reached the quarter-finals before being eliminated by Servette.

trgt: Nathan's Famous, Inc. is an American company that operates a chain of fast food restaurants specializing in hot dogs. The original Nathan's restaurant stands at the corner of Surf and Stillwell Avenues in the Coney Island neighborhood of the Brooklyn borough of New York City, New York. The company's headquarters are at One Jericho Plaza in Jericho, New York, part of Oyster Bay, New York.
mgt: Nathan's Famous is an American fast food company founded in 1916 by Nathan and Ida Handwerker in Coney Island, Brooklyn, New York. Known for its "kosher-style" all-beef hot dogs, the brand grew from a single stand to an international chain with locations across the United States and abroad. The company is also famous for its annual Fourth of July Hot Dog Eating Contest, held at its original location.

trgt: Koforidua Senior High Technical School, is one of the best secondary technical schools in Ghana. The school is located at Koforidua, the capital of Eastern Region in southern Ghana. Koforidua Secondary technical also called Sec-Tech, is a Senior High School located in Koforidua in the Eastern Region of Ghana. As of December 2011, the school has over 2,200 students.
mgt: Koforidua Senior High Technical School (KSTS) is a secondary technical school in Koforidua, Ghana, established in 1967 by Dr. Kwame Nkrumah. Initially an all-boys school, it began admitting girls in 1991. Known for its strong academic performance, KSTS is ranked among Ghana's Class A schools and achieved notable success in the West African Senior School Certificate Examination (WASSCE).

trgt:
New Washington, officially the Municipality of New Washington (Aklanon: Banwa it New Washington; Hiligaynon: Banwa sang New Washington; Lang-tl), is a 3rd class municipality in the province of Aklan, Philippines. According to the 2020 census, it has a population of 47,955 people.

New Washington is particularly known for being the home town of Cardinal Jaime Sin, former Archbishop of Manila.
mgt: New Washington is a municipality in the province of Aklan, Philippines, located in the Western Visayas region. Established on January 15, 1904, and named after U.S. President George Washington, it spans 66.69 square kilometers and has a population of 47,955 as of the 2020 census. Known for its fishing and rice farming industries, the town features historical landmarks, cultural festivals, and beach resorts, making it a hub for tourism and local heritage.

trgt: Eastern Macedonia and Thrace (Lang-el, IPA-el) is one of the thirteen administrative regions of Greece. It consists of the northeastern parts of the country, comprising the eastern part of the region of Macedonia along with the region of Western Thrace, and the islands of Thasos and Samothrace.
mgt: Eastern Macedonia and Thrace is an administrative region in northeastern Greece, established in 1987 and restructured under the 2010 Kallikratis plan. Its capital is Komotini, with Alexandroupolis as the largest city. The region comprises six regional units and is home to Greece's main Muslim minority.

trgt: Drapers' Academy is a secondary school for girls and boys, from years 7 to 13 sixth form with academy status, located in Harold Hill near Romford, Greater London, England. It was the first academy to be established in the London Borough of Havering.
mgt: Drapers' Academy is a mixed secondary school and sixth form located in Harold Hill, Romford, Greater London, England. Established in September 2010, it is sponsored by the Drapers' Company and Queen Mary, University of London. The academy specializes in science and mathematics.

trgt: Publius Sulpicius Quirinius (c. 51 BC – AD 21), also translated as Cyrenius, was a Roman aristocrat. After the banishment of the ethnarch Herod Archelaus from the tetrarchy of Judea in AD 6, Quirinius was appointed legate governor of Syria, to which the province of Judaea had been added for the purpose of a census.
mgt: Publius Sulpicius Quirinius (c. 51 BC – AD 21) was a Roman statesman and military leader who served as consul in 12 BC and later as governor of Syria. He is known for his military campaigns, including victories over the Marmaridae and the Homanades, and for conducting the controversial Census of Quirinius in Judea in 6 AD, which incited unrest. Quirinius was a close associate of Emperor Augustus and later Tiberius, and he was honored with a public funeral upon his death. He is also mentioned in the Gospel of Luke in connection with the nativity of Jesus.

trgt: Alan Edgar Bristow, OBE, FRAeS (3 September 1923 – 26 April 2009) founded one of the world's largest helicopter service companies, Bristow Helicopters Ltd, which prospered primarily in the international oil and mineral exploration and extraction industries, but also spread into search and rescue, peacekeeping and other fields.
mgt: Alan Bristow OBE (3 September 1923 – 26 April 2009) was a British helicopter pioneer, entrepreneur, and founder of Bristow Helicopters Ltd, one of the world's largest helicopter services companies. A decorated World War II veteran, he became the first Briton to land a helicopter on a naval frigate. Bristow's company played a key role in supporting global oil exploration and expanded operations worldwide. He was also involved in the 1985 Westland Affair, a political controversy in the UK. Beyond aviation, Bristow pursued diverse interests, including equestrian sports and innovative inventions.

trgt:
The 2014 FedEx Cup Playoffs was the series of four golf tournaments that determined the season champion on the U.S.-based PGA Tour. It was played from August 21 to September 14 and comprised the following four events:

These were the eighth FedEx Cup playoffs since their inception in 2007.

The point distributions can be seen here.
mgt: The 2014 FedEx Cup Playoffs, part of the PGA Tour, took place from August 21 to September 14 across four events: The Barclays, Deutsche Bank Championship, BMW Championship, and Tour Championship. Billy Horschel emerged as the FedEx Cup champion, securing the title with back-to-back wins in the BMW Championship and Tour Championship after starting the playoffs in 69th place.

trgt:
The New Zealand Superstock Championship is an annually contested national championship for superstocks on a rotating track schedule. The current champion is Asher Rees, registered to Kihikihi Speedway, who won his first title at Paradise Valley Raceway, near Rotorua, in January 2021.

Graham Stretch was the inaugural champion, winning the title at Palmerston North International Speedway(Central Energy Trust Arena) in the 1965/66 season. Craig Boote and Kevin Free have been the most successful winners, each winning 3 times.
mgt: The New Zealand Superstock Championship is a premier dirt track racing competition in New Zealand, first held in 1965. Featuring 132 drivers as of 2021, the championship showcases high-intensity, full-contact racing on oval dirt tracks. Superstocks racing permits aggressive tactics, including pushing competitors into walls or the infield. Cars are powered by V6 or V8 engines, limited to 248 cubic inches and producing approximately 500 horsepower. The championship is a cornerstone of New Zealand's motorsport culture, attracting significant participation and fan interest.

trgt: The Pulmonary Vascular Research Institute (PVRI) is a UK registered medical research charity. It is registered Charity in the United Kingdom (Charity No: 1127115) and a private limited company by guarantee. The company registration number in the United Kingdom is 5780068. The PVRI is a professional membership organisation for doctors and scientists from around the world who have a special interest in pulmonary hypertension (PH) or pulmonary vascular diseases (PVD).
mgt: The Pulmonary Vascular Research Institute (PVRI) is a global charitable organization founded in 2006 and based in Canterbury, UK. It focuses on advancing research, education, and awareness of pulmonary vascular disease (PVD), a rare and often fatal condition. With a network of over 7,500 members across 85 countries, PVRI organizes scientific meetings, supports regional initiatives, and publishes the peer-reviewed journal *Pulmonary Circulation*. It is funded through memberships, event fees, and grants.

trgt: Rupa Goswami (Lang-sa, Lang-bn, IAST: Rūpa Gosvāmī; 1489–1564) was a devotional teacher (guru), poet, and philosopher of the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition. With his brother Sanatana Goswami, he is considered the most senior of the six Goswamis of Vrindavan associated with Caitanya Mahaprabhu, a hidden avatar (incarnation) of Krishna in Kali Yuga.
mgt: Rupa Goswami (c. 1489–1564 CE) was a prominent theologian, scholar, and saint in the Gaudiya Vaishnavism tradition. Born into a Telugu-speaking Brahmin family, he served as a government official before becoming a disciple of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Rupa Goswami was instrumental in systematizing Gaudiya Vaishnava theology, rediscovering holy sites in Vrindavana, and composing influential devotional texts. He is regarded as the foremost follower of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu and the leader of the Rupanuga tradition. His samadhi is located at the Radha-Damodara temple in Vrindavana.

trgt: Navajo music is music made by Navajos, mostly hailing from the Four Corners region of the Southwestern United States and the territory of the Navajo Nation. While it traditionally takes the shape of ceremonial chants and echoes themes found in Diné Bahaneʼ, contemporary Navajo music includes a wide range of genres, ranging from country music to rock and rap, performed in both English and Navajo.
mgt: Navajo music encompasses a rich tradition of vocal and instrumental compositions, deeply rooted in the cultural and spiritual practices of the Navajo people. Traditional music includes sacred ceremonial songs, used to restore harmony and balance, as well as "popular" songs for public performance. Other forms include children's songs, peyote songs associated with the Native American Church, and the Shoegame, a traditional winter activity. Contemporary Navajo music spans diverse genres, including punk, metal, hip-hop, and country, blending traditional elements with modern influences.

trgt: Dan Beach Bradley (18 July 1804 – 23 June 1873) was an American Protestant missionary to Siam from 1835 until his death. He is credited with numerous firsts, including, bringing the first Thai-script printing press to Siam, publishing the first Thai newspaper and monolingual Thai dictionary, performing the first surgery in Siam, and introducing Western medicine and technology.
mgt: Dan Beach Bradley (July 18, 1804 – June 23, 1873) was an American medical missionary and educator who played a significant role in introducing Western medicine, smallpox vaccination, and the printing press to Siam (modern-day Thailand). Active from 1835 to 1873, he also founded the first newspaper in Siam, *The Bangkok Recorder*, and contributed to education and translation efforts. Bradley's medical work earned him the trust of the royal court, and his advocacy for education and equality left a lasting legacy in Thailand. He is buried in the Bangkok Protestant Cemetery.

trgt: Immunity is the debut studio album by American singer-songwriter Clairo, released on August 2, 2019, by Fader Label. The album was co-produced by Clairo and Rostam Batmanglij, formerly of Vampire Weekend. It has been described as a soft rock, bedroom pop, electropop, and indie pop record.
mgt: *Immunity* is the debut studio album by American singer-songwriter Clairo, released on August 2, 2019, through Fader. Produced by Clairo and Rostam Batmanglij, the album blends soft rock, bedroom pop, and indie pop. It received positive reviews from critics and features singles like "Bags" and "Sofia."

trgt: Margaret Cuninggim (October 15, 1914 in Nashville, Tennessee – July 4, 1986 in St. Petersburg, Florida) was an American university professor and administrator. She served as Dean of Women at the University of Tennessee from 1957 to 1966 and at Vanderbilt University from 1966 to 1973. Additionally, she served as the President of the Tennessee Association of Women's Deans from 1958 to 1960. The Margaret Cuninggim Women's Center at Vanderbilt University in named in her honor.
mgt: Margaret Louise Cuninggim (October 15, 1914 – July 4, 1986) was an American educator and university administrator. She served as Dean of Women at the University of Tennessee and Vanderbilt University, where she later became Dean of Student Services. A champion of women's education, she was involved with organizations like Alpha Lambda Delta and Mortar Board. Vanderbilt University honors her legacy through the Margaret Cuninggim Women's Center, an annual lecture series, and a fellowship named in her memory.

trgt: State Route 159 (SR 159) is a northeast-southwest state highway (signed north–south) in the south-central portion of the U.S. state of Ohio. Its southern terminus is in Chillicothe at the U.S. Route 50 and State Route 104 concurrency; its northern terminus is at U.S. Route 22 approximately 4 miles (6.4 km) southeast Lancaster.
mgt: Ohio State Route 159 (SR 159) is a 30.91-mile-long (49.74 km) north–south state highway in Ohio, maintained by the Ohio Department of Transportation (ODOT). Established in 1923, it runs from Chillicothe to near Lancaster, passing through Ross, Pickaway, and Fairfield counties. The route was fully paved by 1938 and extended to its current termini in 1971 and 1972.

trgt:
The 1950 World Snooker Championship was a snooker tournament held at the Tower Circus in Blackpool, England.

For the fourth year running the final was contested by Fred Davis and Walter Donaldson. Donaldson defeated Davis 51–46 to win his second and last world title. Donaldson won the 7th frame of the final day to lead 49–42 with only 6 frames to play. Davis made a break of 101 in frame 46 of his semi-final match against George Chenier.
mgt: The 1950 World Snooker Championship was a professional snooker tournament organized by the Billiards Association and Control Council, held from 12 December 1949 to 18 March 1950. The final took place at the Tower Circus in Blackpool, England, where Walter Donaldson of Scotland defeated England's Fred Davis 51–46 to win the championship. The qualifying rounds were held in Battersea, London, featuring notable players such as Kingsley Kennerley and Sydney Lee. This tournament followed the 1949 championship and preceded the 1951 event.

trgt:
Roll for the Galaxy is a dice game of building space empires for 2–5 players. Your dice represent your populace, whom you direct to develop new technologies, settle worlds, and ship goods. The player who best manages his workers and builds the most prosperous empire wins!

This dice version of Race for the Galaxy takes players on a new journey through the Galaxy, but with the feel of the original game.
mgt: *Roll for the Galaxy* is a dice-based strategy board game designed by Wei-Hwa Huang and Tom Lehmann, and published by Rio Grande Games. Supporting 2 to 5 players, the game involves creating a galactic civilization by recruiting workers (represented by custom dice) to settle worlds and build developments. Players strategically assign workers to phases, manage resources, and compete to earn the most victory points. The game typically lasts 30 to 60 minutes.

trgt: The National Audit Office (NAO) of Tanzania is an independent Parliamentary body in Tanzania which is responsible for auditing central government departments, government agencies and non-departmental public bodies. The NAO also carries out value for money (VFM) audits into the administration of public policy. The office is headed by the Chief Auditor General appointed by the President of Tanzania.
mgt: The National Audit Office of Tanzania (NAOT) is an independent parliamentary body responsible for auditing the government of Tanzania. Established in 2001, it succeeded the Exchequer and Audit Department, which had been in place since 1961. Headquartered in Dodoma, the NAOT operates under the Public Finance Act and is led by the Auditor General, currently Charles Kichere.

trgt: Maitreya-nātha (c. 270–350 CE) is a name whose use was pioneered by Buddhist scholars Erich Frauwallner, Giuseppe Tucci, and Hakuju Ui to distinguish one of the three founders of the Yogacara school of Buddhist philosophy, along with Asanga and Vasubandhu. Some scholars believe this Maitreya to be a historical person in India. The traditions themselves have held that it is referring to Maitreya, the future buddha.
mgt: Maitreya-nātha is a figure in Buddhist tradition whose identity is debated among scholars. Some view him as a historical teacher of Asaṅga, while others consider him the bodhisattva Maitreya. Traditional accounts hold that Asaṅga received teachings directly from Maitreya in the Tuṣita heaven through meditation. Several influential Buddhist texts, including the Five Dharmas of Maitreya, are attributed to Maitreya-nātha, Asaṅga, or both.

trgt: The Battle of Cabrita Point, also known Battle of Marbella, was a naval battle that took place while a combined Spanish-French force besieged Gibraltar on 10 March 1705 (21 March 1705 in the New Calendar) during the War of Spanish Succession. The battle ended in an allied victory (English, Portuguese and Dutch) which effectively ended the Franco-Spanish siege of Gibraltar.
mgt: The Battle of Cabrita Point, fought on 21 March 1705 during the War of the Spanish Succession, was a naval engagement off Marbella between the Grand Alliance and Franco-Spanish forces. Commanded by Sir John Leake, the Grand Alliance achieved a decisive victory, capturing or destroying several enemy ships. The battle secured Gibraltar for the Allies and forced the French to abandon their siege.

trgt:
Dulce Enemiga (English title:Sweet Enemy) is a Venezuelan telenovela written by Valentina Párraga and produced by Venevisión in 1995. This telenovela lasted 153 episodes and was distributed internationally by Venevisión International.

Gigi Zanchetta and Guillermo Dávila starred as the protagonists with Natalia Streignard as the antagonist.
mgt: *Dulce Enemiga* is a Venezuelan telenovela created by Valentina Párraga and produced by Venevisión. Airing from August 16, 1995, to March 5, 1996, it stars Gigi Zanchetta and Guillermo Dávila. The story follows Victoria Andueza, whose quest for revenge against Julio Cesar Guerrero evolves into a tumultuous love.

trgt: John Patrick Rourke FMLS (born 26 March 1942, in Cape Town) is a South African botanist, who worked at the Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden and became curator of the Compton Herbarium. He is a specialist in the flora of the Cape Floristic Region, in particular the family Proteaceae.
mgt: John Patrick Rourke (born 26 March 1942) is a South African botanist known for his work on the Cape flora and the Proteaceae family. He served as curator of the Compton Herbarium at Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden and published significant revisions of Proteacean genera. Rourke has collected approximately 2,000 plant specimens and received numerous honors, including the Linnean Society of London foreign membership in 1997 and the Cape Tercentenary Foundation's Gold Medal in 2003. Several plant species have been named in his honor.

trgt:
East Rennell is the southern portion of Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands which is designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Rennell is the largest raised coral atoll in the world and the area in East Rennell surrounding Lake Tegano contains many endemic species.

In 2013, the World Heritage Committee added East Rennell to the List of World Heritage in Danger because of the threat of logging activities to the site's outstanding universal value.
mgt: East Rennell, located on Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands, is the largest raised coral atoll in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998. Covering 37,000 hectares, it includes Lake Tegano, surrounding forests, and marine areas. Known for its rich biodiversity, it is home to numerous endemic species, including birds, reptiles, and insects. The site is managed in collaboration with local communities, but logging and environmental threats led to its inclusion on the List of World Heritage in Danger in 2013.

trgt:
Barry Took (19 June 1928 – 31 March 2002) was an English writer, television presenter and comedian. His decade-and-a-half writing partnership with Marty Feldman led to the television series Bootsie and Snudge, the radio comedy Round the Horne and other projects.

He is also remembered in the UK for presenting Points of View, a BBC Television programme featuring viewers'letters on the BBC's output, and the BBC Radio 4 programme The News Quiz.
mgt: Barry Took (19 June 1928 – 31 March 2002) was an English comedian, writer, and broadcaster, best known for his work in radio and television comedy. He co-wrote popular shows such as *The Army Game*, *Bootsie and Snudge*, and *Round the Horne*, often collaborating with Marty Feldman. Took played a key role in bringing together the performers of *Monty Python's Flying Circus* and later hosted *The News Quiz* and *Points of View*. He also authored several books, including his autobiography.

trgt: Donald Joel Aronow (March 3, 1927 – February 3, 1987) was an American designer, builder and racer of the famous Magnum Marine, Cary, Cigarette, Donzi, and Formula speedboats. He built speedboats for the Shah of Iran, Charles Keating, Robert Vesco, Malcolm Forbes, and George H. W. Bush. Retired President Lyndon B. Johnson owned several 16 ft. Donzi speedboats on his Texas ranch with which he would race his Secret Service agents.
mgt: Donald Aronow (March 1, 1927 – February 3, 1987) was an American boat designer, builder, and racer, known for founding several successful powerboat companies, including Donzi Marine, Magnum Marine, and Cigarette Racing Team. A two-time world champion and three-time U.S. champion, his boats won over 350 offshore races and were used by the U.S. Customs Service. Aronow was murdered in 1987 in a case linked to business disputes and drug smuggling.

trgt: Tobin David Stokes (born 1966) is a Canadian composer and theatre creator, notable for his work in opera, theatre, choral music and television. His works have been performed by New York City Opera, Long Beach Opera, City Opera of Vancouver, the Moscow Symphony Orchestra, and the Victoria Symphony. He also composes for television, including scores for documentaries by the BBC, ABC, and PBS.
mgt: Tobin Stokes (born 1966) is a Canadian composer from North Vancouver, British Columbia. He has composed several operas, including *Fallujah*, *Pauline*, *The Vinedressers*, and *Rattenbury*. Stokes has served as a resident composer for the Victoria Symphony and various international festivals. He holds a BMus in percussion from the University of Victoria and an MFA in creative writing from the University of British Columbia.

trgt: Fish fingers (British English) or fish sticks (American English) are a processed food made using a whitefish, such as cod, hake, haddock, shark or pollock, which has been battered or breaded. They are commonly available in the frozen food section of supermarkets. They can be baked in an oven, grilled, shallow fried, or deep-fried.
mgt: Fish fingers, also known as fish sticks, are a popular British dish made from battered or breaded whitefish. First referenced in 1900, they became widely commercialized in 1953 by Gorton's in the United States. Originally tested with herring, cod became the preferred choice. Fish fingers are now made with various fish, including salmon.

trgt: Quick couplers (also called quick hitches) are used with construction machines to allow the rapid change of buckets and attachments on the machine. They remove the need to use hammers to manually drive out and insert the mounting pins for attachments. They also bring with them additional safety risks that must be overcome by careful design and manufacture, and proper use.
mgt: A quick coupler is a device used on construction and earth-moving machinery to enable the rapid exchange of tools or attachments, such as buckets. Commonly found on hydraulic excavators, backhoe loaders, and other equipment, quick couplers come in various designs, including manual, semi-automatic, and automatic types. Safety concerns, particularly with semi-automatic couplers, have led to differing regulatory approaches worldwide. The market is supplied by numerous manufacturers, including original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and independent producers. Efforts to standardize safety guidelines have resulted in the development of international standards, such as ISO 13031.

trgt:
The 1960 Louisiana gubernatorial election was held on April 19, 1960.

Primary elections were held in two rounds on December 5, 1959, and January 9, 1960. After defeating Chep Morrison in a Democratic primary which featured some of the most racially charged campaign rhetoric in Louisiana political history, Jimmie Davis was elected to his second nonconsecutive term as governor after defeating the Republican candidate, Francis Grevemberg, in the general election.
mgt: The 1960 Louisiana gubernatorial election marked a significant turning point in the state's political history. Democrat Jimmie Davis defeated Republican Francis Grevemberg in the general election, which was notable for being the first contested gubernatorial general election in Louisiana since Reconstruction. The campaign was dominated by issues of segregation and race, with Davis adopting segregationist rhetoric to secure victory. This election also signaled the decline of the Long political dynasty, as no candidate backed by Huey or Earl Long advanced to the runoff.

trgt: The Sight is a children's fantasy novel. It was released on 24 April 2007, and it is the first installment of the Warriors: Power of Three series by Erin Hunter. The book was recommended as Children's Summer Reading by the Washington Post Children's Book Club.
mgt: *The Sight* is a 2007 children's fantasy novel by Erin Hunter and the first book in the *Warriors: Power of Three* series. It follows the adventures of three young cats, Lionpaw, Hollypaw, and Jaypaw, as they uncover a mysterious prophecy about their destiny. The novel explores themes of loyalty, identity, and power, while introducing new challenges for the ThunderClan. *The Sight* received critical acclaim, reaching #1 on major sales charts, including the *New York Times* Best Seller list, and was nominated for Amazon's Best Books of the Year in the Middle Readers category.

trgt: Route 225 is a state highway in the U.S. state of Massachusetts. The highway runs 31.100 miles (50.051 km) from Route 2A in Lunenburg in northeastern Worcester County east to Route 2 in Lexington. Route 225 serves the western Middlesex County towns of Shirley, Groton, Westford, and Carlisle. The highway also passes through Bedford along its concurrency with Route 4, which terminates at the same junction with Route 2.
mgt: Massachusetts Route 225 is a 31.1-mile (50.1 km) state highway running through Worcester and Middlesex counties. Established in 1957, it begins at Route 2A in Lunenburg and ends at Route 2 in Lexington. The route passes through several towns, including Groton, Westford, Carlisle, and Bedford, featuring notable intersections with Route 119, Route 4, and I-95. It was renumbered from Route 25 following the construction of Interstate 495.

trgt: Sanjay National Park (Guru Ghasidas National Park) is a national park in Koriya district of Chhattisgarh and Sidhi, Singrauli districts of Madhya Pradesh state, India. It covers an area of 466.657 km2 (180.177 sq mi) and is a part of the Sanjay-Dubri Tiger Reserve. It is located in the Narmada Valley dry deciduous forests ecoregion.
mgt: Sanjay National Park, also known as Sanjay Dubri National Park, is located in the Sidhi District of Madhya Pradesh, India, and spans 466.657 square kilometers. Established in 1983, it features tropical Sal forests and diverse wildlife, including Bengal tigers, Indian leopards, and over 300 bird species. It forms part of the Sanjay-Dubri Tiger Reserve.

trgt: 68th Siege Battery was a unit of Britain's Royal Garrison Artillery (RGA) formed in Wales during World War I. It served on the Western Front, including the Battles of the Somme and Passchendaele, defended against the German Spring Offensive and took part in the crushing victories of the Allied Hundred Days Offensive in 1918.
mgt: The 68th Siege Battery, Royal Garrison Artillery, was a British Army unit active from 1915 to 1919 during World War I. Formed in Pembroke Dock, Wales, it served on the Western Front, participating in major battles such as the Somme, Passchendaele, and the Hundred Days Offensive. The battery was disbanded in 1919.

trgt:
Azerbaijan—France relations are the bilateral relations between the Republic of Azerbaijan and the French Republic in the political, socio-economic, cultural, and other spheres.

Cooperation is carried out in such areas as energy (Total), environment (Suez CNIM), transport (Alstom, Thales, Iveco, SYSTRA), agriculture (Lactalis, Danone), space industry (Airbus, Thales), gas sector, security, culture, hotel business (Accor), banking (Societe Generale), aviation, etc.
mgt: Azerbaijan–France relations refer to the diplomatic, economic, cultural, and educational ties between Azerbaijan and France. Diplomatic relations were established in 1992, with embassies in Baku and Paris. The two nations collaborate in areas such as trade, energy, and education, including the establishment of the French-Azerbaijani University. High-level visits and cultural exchanges have strengthened bilateral ties, despite occasional political tensions.

trgt:
Citizens Activ is the fourth studio album by the Christian rapper Manafest. It was released on May 28, 2008 in Japan and shortly after released globally on June 24, under BEC Recordings.

The release contains the singles "4-3-2-1", "So Beautiful", and "Good Day".
mgt: *Citizens Activ* is the fourth studio album by Canadian artist Manafest, released on May 28, 2008, through BEC Recordings. Blending Christian hip hop and rap rock, the album received mixed to positive reviews and earned multiple award nominations, including at the GMA Dove Awards and GMA Canada Covenant Awards.

trgt: Annamarie Tendler (born June 9, 1985), known professionally as Anna Marie Tendler, is an American multimedia artist known for her work in photography, makeup and hairstyling, and textile crafts, with a notable specialty in handmade lampshades. She is the author of Pin It!: 20 Fabulous Bobby Pin Hairstyles and The Daily Face: 25 Makeup Looks for Day, Night, and Everything In Between!.
mgt: Anna Marie Tendler (born June 9, 1985) is an American artist, writer, designer, and former makeup artist. She gained prominence as an early beauty influencer through her Tumblr blog and has worked on various television and Broadway productions. Tendler owns Silk Parlor, a business specializing in handcrafted Victorian-style lampshades, and has exhibited her artwork. She was married to comedian John Mulaney from 2014 to 2022.

trgt: General der Nachrichtenaufklärung Training Referat was the training organization within the General der Nachrichtenaufklärung (GDNA), the military signals intelligence agency of the Wehrmacht, before and during World War II. Until 1942, the work of the Training Referat was not fully exploited and only a small beginners course was in progress.
mgt: The *General der Nachrichtenaufklärung* (GDNA) Training Referat was responsible for training German Army cryptanalysts, signal recruits, and specialists during World War II. It addressed personnel shortages and developed courses in cryptography, signals intelligence, and language interpretation. Despite successes in training cryptanalysts, field training faced challenges, particularly in the war's later years, due to resource constraints and disorganization.

trgt: Carl Froch vs. Glen Johnson was a championship fight for the WBC Super Middleweight championship. The winner of the bout went on to the Finals of the Super Six World Boxing Classic and face WBA Champion Andre Ward, after his decision win over Arthur Abraham in May 2011. It was held on June 4, 2011, at Boardwalk Hall, in Atlantic City, New Jersey, United States, and televised on Showtime.
mgt: Carl Froch vs. Glen Johnson was a professional boxing match that took place on June 4, 2011, at Boardwalk Hall in Atlantic City, New Jersey. The fight was part of the Super Six World Boxing Classic, with Froch's WBC Super Middleweight Championship on the line. Froch defeated Johnson via majority decision to advance to the tournament final. The event was broadcast on Showtime and featured a comeback bout by Mikkel Kessler.

trgt: Fuxi or Fu Hsi (伏羲 ~ 伏犧 ~ 伏戲) is a culture hero in Chinese legend and mythology, credited along with his sister and wife Nüwa with creating humanity and the invention of music, hunting, fishing, domestication, and cooking as well as the Cangjie system of writing Chinese characters around 2,000 BC. Fuxi was counted as the first of the Three Sovereigns at the beginning of the Chinese dynastic period.
mgt: Fuxi (伏羲), a prominent figure in Chinese mythology, is regarded as one of the Three Sovereigns and a cultural hero. Often depicted with a human face and a snake's body, he is said to have been born alongside his sister Nüwa and played a key role in the creation of humanity. Fuxi is credited with introducing marriage, cooking, fishing, hunting, and the I Ching trigrams. He is also associated with the invention of calligraphy and the Guqin musical instrument.

trgt:
Santiago de Cuba, also known as Santiago General Senén Casas, is the main railway station of the city of Santiago de Cuba, seat of the homonym province, Cuba. It is owned by the state company Ferrocarriles de Cuba (FFCC) and is located in the central Paseo de Martí, nearby the city harbor.

It is the second most important stations of Cuba and, along with Havana Central, Camagüey and Santa Clara, is a network's divisional headquarter.
mgt: Santiago de Cuba railway station, officially named Santiago de Cuba General Senén Casas, is a major railway terminus located in Santiago de Cuba, Cuba. Rebuilt in 1997, the station features a modern structure with two vaults, a clock tower, and facilities serving both passengers and the nearby harbor. It is a key hub for long-distance trains, including the Tren Francés, connecting Santiago with cities across the island, as well as regional services to nearby destinations like Guantánamo and Bayamo.

trgt: Richard E. Anderson (born July 29, 1941) is a former American football player and coach. He served as the head football coach at Rutgers University from 1984 to 1989, compiling a record of 28–33–4. His highlight victory during that span was 21–16 win over Penn State in 1988—Rutgers' first win over the Nittany Lions in 70 years. He played college football at Penn State, and served there as an assistant football coach for many years under Joe Paterno.
mgt: Dick Anderson (born July 29, 1941) is a former American football player and coach. He played tight end and defensive end at Penn State from 1960 to 1962 and later served as the head coach at Rutgers University from 1984 to 1989, compiling a record of 28–33–4. Anderson spent much of his coaching career at Penn State, where he was an assistant coach under Joe Paterno for over two decades, mentoring numerous All-Americans and future NFL players.

trgt: The 19th Reserve Division (19. Reserve-Division) was a unit of the Imperial German Army in World War I. The division was formed on mobilization of the German Army in August 1914 as part of X Reserve Corps. The division was disbanded in 1919 during the demobilization of the German Army after World War I. The division was raised in the Prussian Province of Hanover, the Grand Duchy of Oldenburg, and the Duchy of Brunswick.
mgt: The 19th Reserve Division (19. Reserve-Division) was an infantry division of the German Army active during World War I from 1914 to 1919. Commanded initially by Generalleutnant Max von Bahrfeldt, it participated in major engagements on the Western and Eastern Fronts, including the Great Retreat, First Battle of the Marne, Battle of Verdun, Battle of the Somme, and the Oise-Aisne Offensive. Rated as a first-class division by Allied intelligence, it underwent reorganization during the war, including triangularization in 1916.

trgt:
The Meaning of Life is an Irish television programme, broadcast on RTÉ One. Presented by Gay Byrne, each edition involves the veteran broadcaster interviewing a prominent public figure.

Interviews with former Taoiseach Bertie Ahern and actors Gabriel Byrne and Brenda Fricker during the second series attracted media attention. Ahern spoke of his religious habits, Byrne and Fricker of being sexually abused as children.
mgt: *The Meaning of Life* is an Irish television interview series hosted by Gay Byrne, which aired on RTÉ One from April 19, 2009, to October 16, 2016. Spanning 12 series and 50 episodes, the show featured in-depth interviews with notable figures, exploring their views on life, faith, and personal beliefs. It received mixed reviews, with some critics noting its religious undertones and thought-provoking discussions.

trgt: On 23 May 2020, the dead body of 13-year-old girl Angira Pasi was found hanging from a tree in Devdaha, Nepal. The day before that, she was reportedly raped by 25-year-old Birendra Bhar, after which the local residents and ward member Amar Bahadur Chaudhary held a discussion and decided that Pasi was to be married to Bhar. After her death, the police initially refused to register the case; police only registered it after public outrage.
mgt: The death of Angira Pasi, a 13-year-old Dalit girl, occurred on May 23, 2020, in Devdaha, Rupandehi District, Nepal. Following her reported rape by 25-year-old Birendra Bhar, local residents arranged for her to marry him, but Bhar's family rejected her. Pasi was later found hanging from a tree under suspicious circumstances, leading to allegations of murder. The case sparked public outrage, highlighting caste-based violence in Nepal, and prompted calls for investigation by human rights organizations.

trgt: John William Gleeson (14 March 1938 – 7 October 2016) was an Australian cricketer who played in 29 Test matches from 1967 to 1972. He is best known for his unique bowling style, which according to Cricket Australia CEO James Sutherland "bamboozled batsmen" and could "regularly dumbfound the best batsmen in any team".
mgt: John William Gleeson (14 March 1938 – 7 October 2016) was an Australian cricketer known for his unorthodox "bent-finger" bowling grip, inspired by Jack Iverson. A legbreak and googly bowler, he played 29 Test matches for Australia between 1967 and 1972, taking 93 wickets. Gleeson was particularly effective in domestic cricket, claiming 430 first-class wickets. He debuted in the Sheffield Shield in 1966–67 and later represented Australia in notable series, including the 1968 Ashes and the 1969–70 tour of India. After retiring from cricket, he worked for Telecom Australia and served on the inaugural governing committee of World Series Cricket. Gleeson passed away in 2016 in Tamworth, New South Wales, remembered for his innovative bowling style and contributions to Australian cricket.

trgt: Alba Vergés i Bosch (born 3 September 1978) is a Catalan economist and politician who is current serving as the First Vice President of the Parliament of Catalonia since 2021 and as Acting President of the Parliament of Catalonia since 2022.
mgt: Alba Vergés i Bosch (born 3 September 1978) is a Catalan economist and politician, currently serving as the acting President and First Vice President of the Parliament of Catalonia. A member of the Republican Left of Catalonia (ERC), she was previously the Minister of Health from 2018 to 2021, overseeing the region's healthcare system during significant political and social challenges. Vergés has also held various roles in the Parliament of Catalonia, including Fourth Secretary and Member of Parliament for the Province of Barcelona. Before entering politics, she worked in economics and administration, particularly in elder care management.

trgt: Kadesh, or Qadesh, was an ancient city of the Levant on or near the headwaters or a ford of the Orontes River. It was of some importance during the Late Bronze Age and is mentioned in the Amarna letters. It was the site of the Battle of Kadesh between the Hittite and Egyptian empires in the 13th century BC.
mgt: Kadesh (modern Tell Nebi Mend) was an ancient city located near the Orontes River in present-day Syria, southwest of Homs. Known for its strategic importance, it was a significant site in the Bronze Age and the location of the famous Battle of Kadesh in 1274 BC between the Egyptian and Hittite Empires, one of history's largest chariot battles. Initially under Egyptian control, it later became a Hittite stronghold before its destruction by the Sea Peoples around 1178 BC.

trgt: The Turgeon River is a tributary of the Harricana River that flows to the southern shore of James Bay. The Turgeon River is a river flowing mainly in the municipality of Eeyou Istchee Baie-James (municipality), in the administrative region of Nord-du-Québec, in Quebec, in Canada.
mgt: The Turgeon River is a 216.6 km (134.6 mi) long tributary of the Harricana River, flowing through Quebec and Ontario, Canada. Originating from Lake Turgeon in Eeyou Istchee Baie-James, it traverses forests, historical settlements, and interprovincial boundaries. Historically inhabited by Algonquins, the region saw settlement during the 1930s economic crisis. The river is notable for its covered bridges and its name, officially designated in 1968, honors Adélard Turgeon, a former Minister of Lands and Forests.

page_title: Council for the Central Laboratory of the Research Councils
trgt:
The Council for the Central Laboratory of the Research Councils (CCLRC) was a UK government body that carried out civil research in science and engineering.

On 1 April 2007 CCLRC merged with PPARC to form the Science and Technology Facilities Council.
mgt: The Council for the Central Laboratory of the Research Councils (CCLRC) was a UK non-departmental public body established in 1995 to manage large-scale scientific facilities and support research in materials, biomolecular science, space, and alternative energy. It merged with PPARC in 2007 to form the Science and Technology Facilities Council.

trgt: Abha Dawesar (born 1 January 1974) is an Indian-born novelist writing in English. Her novels include Babyji, Family Values, That Summer in Paris, and Miniplanner. Her 2005 novel Babyji won the Lambda Literary Award for Lesbian Fiction and the Stonewall Book Award.
mgt: Abha Dawesar (born January 1, 1974) is an Indian novelist, artist, and speaker based in New York City. Known for her award-winning novel *Babyji* (2005), she explores themes of identity and technology. Dawesar also creates visual and video art.

trgt: Louis Frey Jr. (January 11, 1934 – October 14, 2019) was an American lawyer and politician who served as a Republican member of the United States House of Representatives from 1969 until 1979. He represented Florida's 5th congressional district from 1969 to 1973 and the 9th district from 1973 to 1979, until he ran unsuccessfully in 1978 for the Republican nomination for governor to succeed the term-limited Democrat Reubin Askew of Pensacola.
mgt: Louis Frey Jr. (January 11, 1934 – October 14, 2019) was an American attorney, politician, and Republican member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Florida, serving from 1969 to 1979. Representing Florida's 5th and later 9th congressional districts, he was known for his fiscal conservatism and advocacy for small businesses. After leaving Congress, Frey ran unsuccessfully for governor and the U.S. Senate. He later founded the Lou Frey Institute of Politics and Government at the University of Central Florida.

trgt: Asia Aluminum is the largest aluminum extrusion group in Asia, with an annual designed capacity of 350,000 metric tons. It employed, directly and indirectly, in excess of 10,000 people, predominantly in the Asia Aluminum Industrial City which housed its primary manufacturing facilities, located in the Zhaoqing region of the People's Republic of China. Asia Aluminum made its debut on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange in 1998.
mgt: Asia Aluminum was a Hong Kong-based aluminum extrusion company founded in 1992, serving global markets. Once a major player in the industry, it faced financial difficulties following a leveraged buyout in 2006 and the global economic downturn of 2008–2009. Declared insolvent in 2009, the company entered liquidation proceedings. Its assets were ultimately sold to a management buyout consortium, leaving most investors unable to fully recover their investments.

trgt: Hilbert College is a private Franciscan college in Hamburg, New York. The college is named after Mother Colette Hilbert of the Franciscan Sisters of Saint Joseph, who founded the school in 1957 to train teachers. Hilbert College enrolls approximately 800 students and grants both undergraduate and master's degrees.
mgt: Hilbert College is a private Roman Catholic college in Hamburg, New York, founded in 1957 by the Franciscan Sisters of St. Joseph. Named after Mother Colette Hilbert, it offers 17 undergraduate and several graduate programs. The college competes in NCAA Division III athletics as the Hilbert Hawks.

trgt: The Martin PBM Mariner was an American patrol bomber flying boat of World War II and the early Cold War era. It was designed to complement the Consolidated PBY Catalina and PB2Y Coronado in service. A total of 1,366 PBMs were built, with the first example flying on 18 February 1939 and the type entering service in September 1940, with the last of the type being retired in 1964.
mgt: The Martin PBM Mariner was an American twin-engine flying boat designed and built by the Glenn L. Martin Company in 1937. Initially developed as a successor to the Martin P3M and a complement to the PBY Catalina, it served extensively during World War II in anti-submarine patrols, sinking ten U-boats, and in the Pacific Theater. Post-war, it was used by the U.S. Navy, Coast Guard, and international forces until its retirement in the late 1950s.

trgt: A temporary crown (provisional crown, interim crown) is a temporary (short-term) crown used in dentistry. Like other interim restorations, it serves until a final (definitive) restoration can be inserted. Usually the temporary crown is constructed from acrylic resins (monomethacrylate-based/polymethacrylate-based) or, chemical-cure/light cure composite (dimethacrylate-based), although alternative systems using aluminium crown forms are occasionally used. Temporary crowns function to protect the tooth, prevent teeth shifting, provide cosmetics, shape the gum tissue properly, and prevent sensitivity.
mgt: A temporary crown is a provisional dental restoration used to protect a tooth while a permanent crown is being fabricated. It serves to maintain function, aesthetics, and tooth alignment, and can be preformed or custom-made using materials like acrylic resin, composite, or metal. Temporary crowns are cemented with provisional cements, which allow for easy removal. Proper oral hygiene, including careful flossing, is essential to prevent complications such as gingival inflammation or dislodgement of the crown.

trgt: Song Bird is a studio album by American country music artist Margo Smith. It was released in July 1976 via Warner Bros. Records and was produced by Norro Wilson. Songbird was the third studio recording of Smith's music career. The album contained a total of ten tracks, including the single release, "Save Your Kisses for Me." The album would reach chart positions and the single would become a major hit.
mgt: *Song Bird* is the third studio album by American country artist Margo Smith, released in July 1976 by Warner Bros. Records. Produced by Norro Wilson, the album was recorded between January and March 1976 at Columbia Recording Studio in Nashville, Tennessee. It features ten tracks, including four written by Smith and covers of songs like "Save Your Kisses for Me." The album peaked at number 34 on the *Billboard* Top Country Albums chart, with its single "Save Your Kisses for Me" reaching the top ten on the *Billboard* Hot Country Singles chart.

trgt: Molding sand, also known as foundry sand, is a sand that when moistened and compressed or oiled or heated tends to pack well and hold its shape. It is used in the process of sand casting for preparing the mould cavity.
mgt: Molding sand, primarily used in metal casting, is a mixture of sand, bentonite clay, pulverized coal, and water, with sand as the main component. Green sand, the most common type, is used in its wet state for creating molds. This method, dating back to ancient Mesopotamia around 3200 B.C., remains one of the simplest and most cost-effective casting techniques. Modern advancements in sand quality and metallurgy have significantly improved the casting process, supporting large-scale production of various metal castings worldwide.

trgt: Chandler School is an independent, co-ed day school for students in kindergarten through grade eight located in Pasadena, California United States, founded in 1950. School enrollment is 450 students. The 4.5-acre (1.8 ha) campus overlooks the Pasadena Rose Bowl and the Arroyo Seco.
mgt: Chandler School is an independent, co-educational K-8 day school in Pasadena, California, founded in 1950 by Thomas and Catherine Chandler. Initially located in Altadena, the school moved to its current Armada Drive campus in 1958. With an enrollment of 450 students, Chandler offers a robust academic program, including a STEAM curriculum and integrated technology education, alongside a diverse athletics program featuring 11 team sports.

trgt: Fort Frederick was a fort in Albany, New York from 1676–1789. Sitting atop State Street Hill (Capitol Hill) it replaced the earlier decaying Fort Orange along the Hudson River. The fort was named for Frederick Louis, Prince of Wales, son of King George II. The fort was referred to as Fort Albany in the 1936 novel Drums Along the Mohawk. Several historical markers have been placed west of the location of the fort.
mgt: Fort Frederick was a fortification built in 1676 in Albany, New York, to replace Fort Orange and protect the city from western threats, particularly Native Americans. Initially constructed as a wooden stockade, it was rebuilt with masonry between 1702 and 1735. The fort played a significant role during the Seven Years' War but fell into disrepair afterward. It was dismantled in 1789, and its site is now marked by historical plaques near the New York State Capitol.

trgt: The Bronze Fonz is a public artwork by American artist Gerald P. Sawyer, located on the Milwaukee Riverwalk in downtown Milwaukee, Wisconsin. The Bronze Fonz depicts Henry Winkler as "The Fonz," a character in the 1970s television series Happy Days, which was set in Milwaukee.
mgt: The *Bronze Fonz* is a bronze statue by Gerald P. Sawyer, unveiled in 2008 on the Milwaukee Riverwalk in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. Depicting Henry Winkler as Arthur "Fonzie" Fonzarelli from the sitcom *Happy Days*, the statue was commissioned by Visit Milwaukee to promote tourism. Despite initial opposition from local art figures, it has become a popular attraction, encouraging visitor interaction and selfies.

trgt: Pierre Marinovitch (Lang-sr; 1 August 1898 – 2 October 1919) was a French World War I flying ace credited with 21 confirmed and 3 probable aerial victories. He was the youngest French flying ace of the war, scoring his 5th victory at age 19 years and 169 days. He was killed in an air accident following the war.
mgt: Pierre Marinovitch (1 August 1898 – 2 October 1919) was a French World War I flying ace and the youngest ace of the war, credited with 21 aerial victories. Born in Paris to Serbian and Polish parents, he joined the French military in 1916 and later served in Escadrille Spa.94. Marinovitch was awarded the Légion d'honneur and Croix de Guerre for his service. He died in a flying accident in Brussels in 1919.

trgt: The 1988 San Francisco Giants season was the Giants' 106th season in Major League Baseball, their 31st season in San Francisco since their move from New York following the 1957 season, and their 29th at Candlestick Park. The team finished in fourth place in the National League West with an 83–79 record, 111⁄2 games behind the Los Angeles Dodgers.
mgt: The 1988 San Francisco Giants season was the franchise's 106th season and their 31st in San Francisco. The team played their home games at Candlestick Park and competed in the National League's Western Division. Managed by Roger Craig and owned by Bob Lurie, the Giants' games were broadcast on KTVU and KNBR, with additional coverage on GiantsVision and KIQI.

trgt: Nick Horvath (born February 18, 1981) is an American-New Zealand former professional basketball player who played the majority of his career in the New Zealand National Basketball League (NZNBL). In 2010, he became the first person to win an NCAA championship (2001), an ANBL championship (2009) and a NZNBL championship (2010).
mgt: Nick Horvath (born February 18, 1981) is a former professional basketball player and dual American-New Zealand citizen. A 6'10" center/power forward, he played college basketball at Duke University, winning the 2001 NCAA championship. Horvath had a professional career spanning 2004 to 2015, playing in leagues across Australia and New Zealand, where he won multiple championships and individual accolades, including the 2012 NZNBL MVP. He also represented New Zealand's national team, the Tall Blacks, in 2008. Following his retirement, Horvath became a physics teacher and basketball coach at Palmerston North Boys' High School in New Zealand.

trgt: John Ricus Couperus (1816 – 1902) was a Dutch lawyer, member of the Council of Justice in Padang, member of the High Military Court of the Dutch East Indies and the landheer of Tjikopo. He was also the father of the Dutch writer Louis Couperus and knight in the Order of the Netherlands Lion.
mgt: John Ricus Couperus (24 February 1816 – 13 October 1902) was a Dutch lawyer, colonial administrator, and composer. Born in the Dutch East Indies, he held various legal and administrative positions, including membership in the High Military Court and the Council of Justice in Padang. Couperus was also a vocal critic of colonial policies, publishing influential brochures on land ownership and economic reforms. After retiring, he returned to the Netherlands, where he oversaw the construction of his family home in The Hague. He was the father of renowned Dutch author Louis Couperus.

trgt: Savakanmaindan (or Savakan Maindan, Saavanmaindan and Javakanmaindan; died 1277) was a monarch of the kingdoms of Tambralinga and Jaffna. He was the son of the Savakan king Chandrabhanu of Tambralinga of the Padmavamsa (lotus dynasty), Captured northern Sri Lankan in 1255 AD. During his rule of Jaffna, the Venetian traveller Marco Polo visited northeastern Sri Lanka.
mgt: Savakanmaindan (died 1277) was a king of Tambralinga and Jaffna, succeeding his father Chandrabhanu in 1262. Initially resisting Pandyan rule, he later submitted and retained control of Jaffna under Pandyan suzerainty. His reign saw conflicts with the Pandyas, culminating in his defeat in the late 1270s, after which the Aryacakravarti dynasty was established in Jaffna.

trgt: The Queen Sirikit Museum of Textiles is located in the Grand Palace in Bangkok Thailand. The museum replaced the 1873 Ratsadakorn-bhibhathana Building (Thai: Hor Ratsadakorn Pipat) of the Royal Treasury Ministry by a request of Queen Sirikit in 2003. The building was the original organization of the current Crown Property Bureau.
mgt: The Queen Sirikit Museum of Textiles, located in the Grand Palace, Bangkok, is a history and textile museum established in 2003 at the former Ratsadakorn-bhibhathana Building. Originally built in 1873 during King Chulalongkorn's reign for tax administration, the building was later repurposed at the request of Queen Sirikit to showcase Thailand's rich textile heritage.

trgt: Carman George Blough (November 11, 1895 – March 9, 1981) was an American accountant, professor of accounting, and civil servant. He is described as "one of the most influential 'high priests' of the profession in the Twentieth Century." He was inducted into the Accounting Hall of Fame in 1954.
mgt: Carman George Blough (1895–1979) was an American accountant, educator, and the first Chief Accountant of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. He held academic positions at institutions including the University of North Dakota and Columbia University and served as research director at the American Institute of Certified Public Accountants.

trgt: The Ministry of War (Lang-de) was a ministry for military affairs of the Kingdom of Bavaria, founded as Ministerium des Kriegswesens on October 1, 1808 by King Maximilian I Joseph of Bavaria. It was located on the Ludwigstraße in Munich. Today the building, which was built by Leo von Klenze between 1824 and 1830, houses the Bavarian public record office, Bayerisches Hauptstaatsarchiv und Staatsarchiv München.
mgt: The Ministry of War (German: Kriegsministerium) of the Kingdom of Bavaria was established as the successor to the Hofkriegsrat in 1801 and oversaw the Bavarian Army. It underwent several name changes before becoming the Kriegsministerium in 1825. The ministry held extensive responsibilities, including command of the army, until its dissolution in 1919 following the collapse of the Bavarian monarchy and the establishment of the Weimar Republic.

trgt: Walhalla-orden was a secret society founded in the early part of 1783 in the Sveaborg (today, in Finnish: Suomenlinna) fortress outside Helsinki, Finland by Johan Anders Jägerhorn along with Gustaf Adolf Reuterholm. It is thought to have been instrumental in setting in motion forces that eventually caused Finnish independence.
mgt: Valhallaorden was a secret society originating as an offshoot of the Swedish quasi-masonic La Constance, established to support the Swedish Constitution of 1772. Active primarily in the Sveaborg fortress, it adopted Gothic revivalist symbology and became a hub for officers discontented with King Gustav III. Members, bound by oaths of confidentiality, were implicated in the Anjala conspiracy, and the society is believed to have hosted early discussions of Finnish independence.

trgt: Margaret C. McEntee SR. SC., also known by her confirmation name Marita James, (born July 10, 1935) is an American Catholic religious sister and educator who is known as being the inspiration for the character of Sister James in the Pulitzer Prize and Tony Award-winning play Doubt: A Parable by John Patrick Shanley. The 2008 film adaptation is dedicated to her.
mgt: Sister Margaret McEntee, SC (born July 10, 1935), is an American Catholic religious sister and educator, known for inspiring the character Sister James in John Patrick Shanley's play *Doubt*. A member of the Sisters of Charity of New York, she has taught at various institutions in the Bronx and beyond. McEntee served as a technical advisor for the 2008 film adaptation of *Doubt*.

trgt: The Slutsk uprising (Lang-be) or the Slutsk defence (Lang-be) was an unsuccessful armed attempt to establish an independent Belarus. It took place in late 1920, near the end of the Polish-Soviet War, in the region of the town of Slutsk. It involved a series of clashes between irregular Belarusian forces loyal to the Belarusian People's Republic and the Soviet Red Army, ending in a Soviet victory.
mgt: The Slutsk uprising (November 27 – December 31, 1920) was an armed resistance by Belarusian forces against Soviet rule during the aftermath of the Soviet-Polish War and the Russian Civil War. Centered in Slutsk, Belarus, the uprising was led by local militias advocating for Belarusian independence. Despite initial successes, the poorly equipped Belarusian forces were ultimately defeated by the Red Army, forcing them to retreat into Polish territory.

trgt: Korzkiew Castle - a fourteenth-century Romanesque castle located in Korzkiew, Lesser Poland Voivodeship in Poland. The castle is part of the Trail of the Eagles' Nests tourist route. The castle complex includes the castle ruins, housing residence, a grange and park, enlisted on the register of objects of cultural heritage of the Lesser Poland Voivodeship.
mgt: Korzkiew Castle is a 14th-century Romanesque castle located in Korzkiew, Lesser Poland Voivodeship, Poland. Originally built as a defensive and residential tower by Jan z Syrokomli in 1352, it underwent several reconstructions before falling into ruin in the 19th century. Since 1997, it has been restored and now operates as a hotel.

trgt: In mathematics, particularly topology, one describes a manifold using an atlas. An atlas consists of individual charts that, roughly speaking, describe individual regions of the manifold. If the manifold is the surface of the Earth, then an atlas has its more common meaning. In general, the notion of atlas underlies the formal definition of a manifold and related structures such as vector bundles and other fiber bundles.
mgt: In topology, an atlas is a collection of charts that together describe the structure of a topological space, particularly a manifold.
