trgt: Oscar Braynon, II (born February 1, 1977) is an American Democratic politician from Miami Gardens, Florida. He served in the Florida House of Representatives from 2008 to 2011 and then in the Florida Senate from 2011 to 2020, representing parts of southern Broward and northern Miami-Dade County.
mgt: Oscar Braynon (born February 1, 1977) is an American politician and former Democratic member of the Florida Legislature. He served in the Florida House of Representatives from 2008 to 2011 and the Florida Senate from 2011 to 2020, representing districts in Miami-Dade and Broward counties. Braynon was the Minority Leader of the Florida Senate from 2016 to 2018. Before his legislative career, he served on the Miami Gardens City Council, including as Vice-Mayor.

trgt: The Rekolokomotives of DR Class 52.80 first appeared in 1960 in service with the Deutsche Reichsbahn in East Germany as extensive rebuilds of the wartime locomotives or Kriegslokomotiven of the DRB Class 52 built by Nazi Germany. This modernisation, described as 'reconstruction' (Rekonstruktion, hence Rekolokomotive), extended to almost all of the components and systems on the engine.
mgt: The DR Class 52.80 was a series of reconstructed steam locomotives derived from the wartime DRB Class 52, rebuilt between 1960 and 1967 at the Stendal Reichsbahn repair shop. Featuring upgraded boilers, welded cylinders, and other modifications, 200 locomotives were modernized for improved performance. Several examples survive today, including the extensively rebuilt 52 8055, certified for operation in Switzerland.

trgt: Patricia "Patt" Murphy Derian (née Murphy; August 12, 1929 – May 20, 2016) was an American civil rights and human rights activist who fought racism in Mississippi and went on to serve as Assistant Secretary of State for Human Rights and Humanitarian Affairs from 1977 to 1981. She was, remembered The Times of London, "a courageous champion of civil rights who took on some of the world's most brutal dictators in her role as a senior American diplomat."
mgt: Patricia Murphy Derian (August 12, 1929 – May 20, 2016) was an American civil rights and human rights activist who served as the first Assistant Secretary of State for Human Rights and Humanitarian Affairs from 1977 to 1981 under President Jimmy Carter. A former nurse and supporter of the Civil Rights Movement, she played a key role in shaping U.S. human rights policy, opposing authoritarian regimes, and advocating for political dissidents worldwide. Derian's efforts significantly influenced global human rights advocacy during and beyond her tenure.

trgt:
Northern Guilford High School is a public high school located in Greensboro, North Carolina. It is situated in proximity to NC Highway 150, which connects to Browns Summit and to Summerfield.

It opened its doors to students on January 29, 2008. The school was built because of the overcrowding of Northwest Guilford and Northeast Guilford high schools.
mgt: Northern Guilford High School is a public high school in Greensboro, North Carolina, established in 2008 as part of Guilford County Schools. Serving grades 9–12, it has a student body of 1,348 and a student-to-teacher ratio of 20:1. The school, known as the Nighthawks, has achieved notable success in athletics, winning multiple state championships across various sports, including football, basketball, baseball, and wrestling.

trgt: Gene Winstead (born 1951) is an American politician and businessman from Minnesota. Winstead was first elected Mayor of Bloomington, Minnesota in 2000. He had previously served as a city council member for 5 years. His 20 year tenure as mayor is the longest in Bloomington history.
mgt: Gene Winstead (born 1951) is an American businessman and politician who served as the 12th mayor of Bloomington, Minnesota, from 2000 to 2020. A Republican, he was the city's longest-serving mayor, overseeing significant developments such as the expansion of the Mall of America, infrastructure improvements, and the introduction of organized garbage collection. Before his mayoral tenure, Winstead held various roles in local government, including positions on the Planning Commission and Housing and Redevelopment Authority. Known for his pragmatic approach, he prioritized economic growth, affordable housing, and transportation projects. Winstead retired from public office in 2020 after 35 years of public service.

trgt: Annette Beard, also known as Annette Helton or Annette Sterling, is an American R&B and soul singer. Beard is best known for her work with Motown and as an original member of the singing group Martha and the Vandellas during the 1960s. Beard is currently known as a member of the singing group The Original Vandellas.
mgt: Annette Beard (also known as Annette Helton, Annette Sterling, and Annette Simmons) is an American singer and a founding member of the Motown girl group Martha and the Vandellas. Born in Detroit, Michigan, she began her career in 1957 with The Del-Phis, which evolved into Martha and the Vandellas. Known for her contralto vocals, Beard contributed to hits like "(Love Is Like A) Heat Wave" and "Quicksand" before leaving the group in 1964 to focus on her family. She later reunited with the group for performances and was inducted into the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame in 1995.

trgt: The National Trust for Scotland for Places of Historic Interest or Natural Beauty, commonly known as the National Trust for Scotland (Lang-gd), is a Scottish conservation organisation. It is the largest membership organisation in Scotland and describes itself as "the conservation charity that protects and promotes Scotland's natural and cultural heritage for present and future generations to enjoy".
mgt: The National Trust for Scotland, established on 1 May 1931, is a Scottish conservation charity headquartered in Edinburgh. It manages around 130 properties and 180,000 acres of land, including historic sites, gardens, and countryside. As Scotland's largest garden owner and third-largest land manager, it preserves natural and cultural heritage, including St Kilda, a dual World Heritage Site.

page_title: 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League knockout phase
trgt: The 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League knockout phase began on 9 December 2020 with the round of 32 and ended with the final on 16 May 2021 at the Gamla Ullevi in Gothenburg, Sweden, to decide the champions of the 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League. A total of 32 teams competed in the knockout phase.
mgt: The 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League knockout phase took place from December 9, 2020, to May 16, 2021, featuring 32 teams from 21 associations. Matches were played over two legs, except for the final, which was held at Gamla Ullevi in Gothenburg, Sweden. Barcelona emerged as champions, defeating Chelsea in the final.

page_title: Italian cruiser Luigi di Savoia Duca degli Abruzzi
trgt: Luigi di Savoia Duca degli Abruzzi was an Italian Duca degli Abruzzi-class light cruiser, which served in the Regia Marina during World War II. After the war, she was retained by the Marina Militare and decommissioned in 1961. She was built by OTO at La Spezia and named after Prince Luigi Amedeo, Duke of the Abruzzi, an Italian explorer and Admiral of World War I.
mgt: The *Luigi di Savoia Duca degli Abruzzi* was an Italian *Duca degli Abruzzi*-class light cruiser, commissioned in 1937 and decommissioned in 1961. Named after Prince Luigi Amedeo, Duke of the Abruzzi, it was the final and most heavily armed iteration of the *Condottieri* class. The cruiser saw action during World War II, served with the Italian Co-Belligerent Navy, and played a symbolic role in the 1954 handover of Trieste to Italy.

trgt:
Michael Joshua Oliverius (born October 10, 1981) is an American former child actor better known by his stage name Michael Oliver.

Oliver is best known for his role as "Junior" in the first two Problem Child movies. He also played Sam Dalton In Dillinger and Capone in 1995.
mgt: Michael Oliver (born Michael Joshua Oliverius; October 10, 1981) is an American former child actor and model, best known for his role as Junior in the 1990 comedy film *Problem Child* and its 1991 sequel. Beginning his career as a toddler model, Oliver gained fame for his mischievous character in *Problem Child*, which became a surprise hit. Following his brief acting career, he retired from the spotlight and now leads a private life. Oliver and his mother were involved in a legal dispute with Universal Pictures over his contract for *Problem Child 2*, which resulted in a court ruling against them.

trgt: Pieter Woortman (born 23 March 1700 – 14 April 1780) was a slave trader and an administrator of the Dutch West India Company. He was one of the longest-serving Director-General of the Dutch Gold Coast, in office between 1767 and 1769 (ad interim) and from 1769 until his death in 1780.
mgt: Pieter Woortman (23 March 1700 – 14 April 1780) was a Dutch administrator and merchant who served as Director-General of the Dutch Gold Coast from 1767 to 1780. He had a significant career with the Dutch West India Company, engaging in the slave trade and establishing influential Euro-African family lineages, including the prominent Plange family.

trgt: Kreis Schwetz was a Prussian district that existed from 1818 to 1920, with its capital at Schwetz. The district was located on the western bank of the Vistula river in the part of West Prussia that fell to Poland after the First World War through the Treaty of Versailles in 1920.
mgt: Kreis Schwetz was a district in West Prussia with a complex history of territorial changes. Established in 1818, it was part of the Kingdom of Prussia and later ceded to Poland in 1920 under the Treaty of Versailles. The district experienced German and Polish rule, reflecting its mixed population and contested political landscape.

trgt: The first season of Idol Puerto Rico premiered on August 7, 2011 and continued until November 21, 2011. It was won by Christian Pagán. The season was hosted by Jaime Augusto Mayol. The winner, Christian Pagán, signed with Universal Music Group, the label in partnership with Idol Puerto Rico, and has released two albums.
mgt: The first season of *Idol Puerto Rico* aired on WAPA-TV from August 7 to November 21, 2011, hosted by Jaime Augusto Mayol. Judges included Ricardo Montaner, Jerry Rivera, Erika Ender, and Topy Mamery. Christian Pagán won the competition, with Japhet Albert as the runner-up. The finals were held at the Centro de Bellas Artes de Caguas.

trgt: John Bullock O.S.A. (d. 1439 × 1440) was an Augustinian canon and prelate active in the 15th century Kingdom of Scotland. While earning a university degree between 1409 and 1417, Bullock gained several benefices in Scotland, and claimed the headship of St Andrews Cathedral Priory before becoming Bishop of Ross in 1418. He held the latter position until his death, which occurred in either 1439 or 1440.
mgt: John Bullock was a 15th-century Scottish cleric who served as the Bishop of Ross from 1418 until his death between 1439 and 1440. A canon lawyer and former vicar, he was appointed bishop by Pope Benedict XIII during the Western Schism. His episcopate is largely obscure, though he played a role in a 1439 contract involving the guardianship of King James II of Scotland.

trgt: USCGC Robert Yered (WPC-1104) is a Sentinel-class cutter based in Miami, Florida. She was launched on November 23, 2012, and was commissioned on February 15, 2012. Debbie Wasserman Schultz, the Congressional Representative for the district containing the vessel's base, met the ship when she arrived in Miami on January 27, 2013.
mgt: USCGC *Robert Yered* (WPC-1104) is a Sentinel-class cutter of the United States Coast Guard, commissioned on February 15, 2013. Built by Bollinger Shipyards, it is designed for coastal security, search and rescue, and interdiction missions. Named after Silver Star recipient Robert J. Yered, the cutter has participated in multiple rescue operations and migrant interdictions.

trgt: Lieutenant General Sir John Gordon Lorimer, KCB, DSO, MBE is a retired senior British Army officer, who served as the Chief of Joint Operations and the Defence Senior Adviser to the Middle East and North Africa. He was appointed Lieutenant Governor of the Isle of Man on 29 September 2021.
mgt: Lieutenant-General Sir John Lorimer KCB DSO MBE (born 1962) is a retired British Army officer and the 31st Lieutenant Governor of the Isle of Man, a position he has held since September 29, 2021. During his 40-year military career, he held senior commands, including Chief of Joint Operations, and served in Northern Ireland, Iraq, and Afghanistan.

trgt: Dinkar D. Patil (Marathi: दिनकर द. पाटील) (1915–2005) was a prominent Marathi film director, scriptwriter, and dialogue writer during the Golden Era (1950–1990) of the Marathi Film Industry. He directed, wrote scripts and dialogue for more than 60 Marathi films. He also directed two Hindi Films-Mandir and Gharbar. He wrote his famous autobiography titled as Patlache Por.
mgt: Dinkar D. Patil (6 November 1915 – 23 October 2005) was an Indian film director, scriptwriter, and author, known for his significant contributions to Marathi cinema. Active from 1948 to 1993, he directed 35 films and wrote scripts for over 60, often focusing on rural themes and social change. He introduced Marathi Lavani folk dance to cinema and played a key role in establishing the Marathi Film City in Kolhapur. Patil also authored an acclaimed autobiography, *Patlache Por*.

trgt: "Tsunagu" is the 52nd single by Japanese boy band Arashi. It was released on June 28, 2017 under their record label J Storm. "Tsunagu" is the theme song for the film Shinobi no Kuni starring Arashi member Satoshi Ohno. The single sold over 380,000 copies in its first week and topped the weekly Oricon Singles Chart. The single was certified Platinum by the Recording Industry Association of Japan (RIAJ).
mgt: "Tsunagu" is a 2017 single by Japanese boy band Arashi, released on June 28, 2017, under the J Storm label. It served as the theme song for the film *Shinobi no Kuni*, starring Arashi member Satoshi Ohno. The single was released in two editions, featuring various B-sides and bonus content. "Tsunagu" achieved commercial success, debuting at number one on the Oricon and Billboard Japan charts and earning platinum certification from the RIAJ.

trgt:
Leymus cinereus is a species of wild rye known by the common names basin wild rye, Great Basin wild rye, and Great Basin lyme grass.

It is a common native grass of western North America, including western Canada and the United States from California to Minnesota. It grows in many types of habitat, including grassland and prairie, forests, scrub, chaparral, and sagebrush.
mgt: **Leymus cinereus**, commonly known as basin wildrye, is a perennial bunchgrass native to North America. It grows in large clumps up to 2 meters tall and thrives in moist, semi-alkaline flats. The species is used in rangeland management and site reclamation, with cultivars like 'Magnar' and 'Trailhead.' Indigenous peoples utilized it for medicinal purposes, dye, bedding, and basketry.

trgt: Tom Gorman (born January 19, 1946) is a retired ATP tour American tennis player and coach. He won 7 singles and 9 doubles titles and reached semi-finals in the 3 of the 4 ATP tour grand slam events. His ATP ranking peaked at 8 in 1973.
mgt: Tom Gorman (born January 19, 1946) is a former American professional tennis player and coach. Ranked as high as world No. 8 in 1973, he won seven singles and nine doubles titles, including the 1975 Cincinnati Open and the 1971 Paris Open. Gorman reached the semifinals of Wimbledon, the US Open, and the French Open, and was a key member of the U.S. Davis Cup team, winning as both a player (1972) and captain (1990, 1992). Known for his sportsmanship, he also coached the U.S. Olympic tennis teams, leading them to a gold medal in 1988. Gorman later served as Director of Tennis at La Quinta Resort & Club and was appointed to the International Tennis Federation Davis Cup Committee.

trgt: The Kooralbyn International School (TKIS) is an independent, co-educational, boarding and day school, located in Kooralbyn, about 64 km south of Brisbane, Queensland, Australia. TKIS is the only school in Australia to have produced two Young Australian of the Year recipients (Cathy Freeman and Scott Hocknull).
mgt: The Kooralbyn International School (TKIS) is a non-denominational, co-educational P-12 school located in Kooralbyn, Queensland, Australia. Established in 1985, the school relocated after flooding in the early 1990s and reopened in 2004 following its closure in 2002 due to funding issues.

trgt: Roy Hinkley, referred to as the Professor, is one of the seven castaways from the television series Gilligan's Island (1964–67); he was played by Russell Johnson. The character was originally played by John Gabriel in the pilot episode, but the network thought he looked too young to have all the degrees attributed to the Professor.
mgt: The Professor, whose full name is Roy Hinkley, is a fictional character from the television series *Gilligan's Island*, portrayed by Russell Johnson. A high school science teacher and botanist, he joined the ill-fated voyage to write a book titled *Fun with Ferns*. Known for his ingenuity, he created numerous inventions from coconuts and bamboo to aid the castaways, though he was unable to repair their boat. The Professor was depicted as intelligent, level-headed, and asexual, often serving as a mediator among the group. His character has been referenced and parodied in popular culture, highlighting his resourcefulness and the irony of his inability to escape the island.

trgt: The Beach 90th Street station (signed as Beach 90th Street–Holland station) is a station on the IND Rockaway Line of the New York City Subway. It is served by the Rockaway Park Shuttle at all times and ten daily rush-hour only A trains.
mgt: Beach 90th Street station is an elevated station on the IND Rockaway Line of the New York City Subway, located in Rockaway Beach, Queens. Originally opened in 1880 by the Long Island Rail Road, it became a subway station in 1956. The station has two side platforms and two tracks, with exits to Rockaway Freeway and Beach 90th Street. It served as a temporary terminal for the H shuttle after Hurricane Sandy until full service was restored in 2013.

trgt: Nabû-mukin-apli, typically inscribed dAG-DU-A, “Nabû (is) establisher of a legitimate heir,” ruled c. 974–939 BC, founded Babylon’s 8th dynasty, the so-called Dynasty of E, and ruled for thirty-six years. The Synchronistic Kinglist records him as a contemporary of the Assyrian king Tiglath-Pileser II. His reign was plagued by Aramean invasions, resulting in Babylon being cut off from its agricultural hinterland for several years and consequently being unable to celebrate the new year festival.
mgt: Nabû-mukin-apli (reigned c. 974–939 BC) was a king of Babylon during the Dynasty of E, ruling in the midst of the Babylonian dark age. His reign is poorly documented, with limited historical sources. The Religious Chronicle notes interruptions to the Akitu festival due to Aramaean hostilities, and a kudurru from his reign records a legal settlement involving land disputes. He was succeeded by his sons, Ninurta-kudurri-uṣur II and Mar-biti-aḫḫe-idinna.

trgt: Stepan Petrovich Krasheninnikov (Lang-ru; November 11 [O.S. October 31] 1711 – March 8 [O.S. February 25] 1755) was a Russian explorer of Siberia, naturalist and geographer who gave the first full description of Kamchatka in the early 18th century. He was elected to the Russian Academy of Sciences in 1745. The Krasheninnikov Volcano on Kamchatka is named in his honour.
mgt: Stepan Petrovich Krasheninnikov (November 11, 1711 – March 8, 1755) was a Russian naturalist and geographer known for his extensive exploration of Kamchatka and Siberia during the Second Kamchatka Expedition (1731–1742). He documented the region's flora, fauna, geography, and indigenous cultures, publishing *Description of the Land of Kamchatka* in 1755. He later became a professor and head of the Academy's Botanic Garden in Saint Petersburg.

trgt: PKP class ET22 is a Polish six-axle electric freight locomotive built by Pafawag from 1969 to 1989. Factory designation is 201E. With 1183 units, it is one of the most numerous standard gauge electric locomotive built in Europe. Further 23 units were built for Morocco as E-1000 class.
mgt: The PKP class ET22 is a Polish electric freight locomotive developed in the late 1960s by Pafawag and the Rolling-stock Industry Central Bureau of Construction. With 1,183 units produced between 1969 and 1990, it is the most numerous standard-gauge electric locomotive built in Europe. Known for its versatility, the ET22 is capable of hauling heavy freight and passenger trains, though its design poses challenges on tight curves. Variants include prototypes for passenger service and export models for Morocco.

trgt: Bader Philanthropies Inc. is a Milwaukee, Wisconsin based foundation that consists of funds from the Helen Daniels Bader Fund and the Isabel and Alfred Bader Fund. It pledges to give away $14 million annually. The organization centers on the health of older adults and improving lives of low-income Milwaukeeans and Jewish education in Milwaukee.
mgt: Bader Philanthropies, Inc. is a private charitable foundation based in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, established in 1992. It oversees the Helen Daniels Bader Fund, focused on Alzheimer's and aging, and the Isabel and Alfred Bader Fund, supporting low-income Milwaukeeans and Jewish education. Between 1992 and 2021, it awarded approximately $400 million in grants and program-related investments.

trgt: The siege of Ypres occurred between 8 June and 8 August 1383 as part of Despenser's Crusade and the Revolt of Ghent (1379–1385). It was conducted by English forces and forces from the Flemish city of Ghent. The siege was a failure.
mgt: The Siege of Ypres (1383) was a key event during Despenser's Crusade and the Revolt of Ghent (1379–1385). Lasting from June 8 to August 8, 1383, the siege saw English forces, led by Henry le Despenser, and their Ghent allies unsuccessfully attempt to capture the Flemish city of Ypres, which remained loyal to France. Despite sustained attacks and artillery bombardments, the city's well-prepared defenses, led by John d'Oultre, withstood the siege until a French relief army forced the attackers to withdraw. The siege marked a turning point in the Revolt of Ghent and contributed to Ypres's long-term economic decline.

page_title: The Integrated Traffic and Landscape Concept for the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg
trgt: The integrated traffic and landscape concept is a spatial development plan which has been created in order to improve the spatial framework in terms of housing, economy, landscape and traffic in Luxembourg. It was introduced in the year 2004 and has a long term perspective with a horizon of 2020. This concept has been developed by several Luxembourgish governmental ministries in cooperation with other spatial planning agencies.
mgt: The Integrated Traffic and Landscape Concept (IVL) for the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg is a national strategy developed by multiple government ministries and stakeholders to guide sustainable spatial and urban development. It aims to prevent urban sprawl by focusing growth around key municipalities near major transport routes, promoting public transportation use. The IVL outlines two future scenarios and five spatial development models to address Luxembourg's evolving demographic and economic needs.

trgt:
BBC Radio Wiltshire is the BBC's local radio station serving the English county of Wiltshire. It broadcasts on FM, DAB, digital TV and via BBC Sounds from studios at Prospect Place in Swindon.

According to RAJAR, the station had a weekly audience of 64,000 and a 1.6% share as of September 2022.
mgt: BBC Radio Wiltshire is the BBC's local radio station serving the county of Wiltshire, England. Launched on 4 April 1989 as BBC Wiltshire Sound, it broadcasts local news, talk, and music from its studios in Swindon. The station operates on FM, DAB, Freeview, and online, with programming produced locally and shared regionally during off-peak hours.

trgt: Operation Northern Falcon (Lang-uk) is a joint Ukrainian–Danish operation in Greenland for the transportation of fuel from the United States Air Force's Thule Air Base to the Danish Station Nord by the military transport aircraft Ilyushin Il-76MD of the Ukrainian Air Force. It has been conducted annually since 2009.
mgt: Operation Northern Falcon is an ongoing joint operation between the Ukrainian and Danish defense ministries, initiated in 2009, to transport fuel to Greenland. Conducted annually, it involves Ukrainian Air Force personnel operating in extreme Arctic conditions to deliver fuel and cargo between Thule Air Base and Station Nord.

trgt:
The Reinhardswald (German pronunciation) is a range of hills up to 472.2 m above sea level (NN) and covering an area of over 200 km2 in the Weser Uplands in the district of Kassel, Hesse (Germany). Of this, 183 km2 are part of the unincorporated area known as Gutsbezirk Reinhardswald.

The area is known for its myths and legends, such as Grimms' Fairy Tales, and the "Sleeping Beauty" castle of Sababurg is internationally known.
mgt: The Reinhardswald is a vast, sparsely populated forest in northern Hesse, Germany, bordered by the Weser, Fulda, and Diemel rivers. Covering over 200 km², it is the largest contiguous forest in Hesse, known for its beech and oak trees, wood pastures, and historical significance as a former imperial forest. Notable attractions include Sababurg Castle, the "Sleeping Beauty" castle, and the ancient Sababurg forest. Recent plans for wind turbine development have sparked environmental and tourism-related controversies.

trgt: Charles Thomas "Chuck" Hunt III (March 2, 1954 – December 21, 2021) was an American dairy farmer, businessman in the construction industry, and physical education teacher. He served as the First Gentleman of Massachusetts from 2001 to 2003 during the tenure of his wife, acting Governor Jane Swift. He was the first man to hold the position of state First Gentleman, cementing "a place in Massachusetts history", as the Boston Globe later wrote.
mgt: Chuck Hunt (March 2, 1954 – December 21, 2021) was an American educator, coach, and the first-ever First Gentleman of Massachusetts, serving from 2001 to 2003 during the governorship of his wife, Jane Swift. A native of Adams, Massachusetts, Hunt worked as a coach and construction business owner before becoming a stay-at-home father during Swift's tenure. He died in 2021 from complications of Alport syndrome, a genetic kidney disease he had lived with throughout his life.

trgt: Substance Church is a nondenominational, Evangelical Christian, megachurch in Minneapolis–Saint Paul, Minnesota, United States. In 2010, Outreach Magazine ranked the church as the 21st fastest growing congregation in the United States (8th in terms of growth percentages). Because the church has integrated ultra-contemporary worship, cell church, and cafe church methods into a singular megachurch setting, Substance is often cited as being "trend-setting".
mgt: Substance Church is a non-denominational, evangelical megachurch founded in 2004 by Pastor Peter Haas in partnership with the Association of Related Churches. Known for its youthful congregation, with over 70% under 30, the church employs contemporary worship methods, including turntables and EDM. With permanent facilities in Spring Lake Park and downtown Minneapolis, it emphasizes church planting, creative media, and progressive methodologies.

trgt: Steven A. Levy (pronounced LEE-vee; born August 25, 1959) is an American politician and lawyer who served as the seventh County Executive of Suffolk County, New York, elected on November 4, 2003. Originally a fiscally conservative Democrat, Levy joined the Republican Party in an unsuccessful bid for the Republican nomination for governor.
mgt: Steve Levy (born August 25, 1959) is an American politician who served as the 7th County Executive of Suffolk County, New York, from 2004 to 2011. A former Democrat who switched to the Republican Party in 2010, Levy previously served in the New York State Assembly (2001–2003) and the Suffolk County Legislature (1985–2000). As County Executive, he focused on fiscal conservatism, affordable housing, environmental preservation, and economic development, including revitalizing Gabreski Airport and attracting Canon's U.S. headquarters to Suffolk County. Levy also prioritized public safety, achieving a significant reduction in violent crime, and maintained flat general fund taxes while improving the county's bond rating. In 2010, he unsuccessfully sought the Republican nomination for New York Governor.

trgt: The new areas or new districts of the People's Republic of China are new urban districts that are given special economic and development support by the Chinese Central Government or regional government. New areas are divided into two varieties: administrative or management and further divided into levels: state-level, provincial-level, and prefectural-level.
mgt: New areas (Chinese: 新区) in the People's Republic of China are special economic development zones established to promote economic growth and attract investment. State-level new areas are supported by the central government and granted preferential policies by the State Council, while provincial- and prefectural-level new areas are managed by local governments.

trgt:
Getafe Air Base (ICAO: LEGT) is a military airbase located in Getafe, 14 km south of Madrid, Spain. The air base, at an altitude of 620 m above sea level, has a single runway with a length of 3.06 km. It was one of the first military air bases in Spain and, because of that, is considered the "cradle" of Spanish aviation.

The base was inaugurated in 1911 (20 years before Madrid Barajas International Airport) and two years later the Civil Aviation School was created. It was the first air bases in Spain where the first autogyro was flown its designer Juan de la Cierva.

This air base was established permanently in 1911 (20 years before Barajas), and two years later the Civil Aviation School was created. Since then, the extensions and reforms of the same have happened to this day.
mgt:
Getafe Air Base (Base Aérea de Getafe) is a military airbase operated by the Spanish Air and Space Force, located in Getafe, Spain. Established in 1911, it serves as a key site for military aviation, hosting the "Ala 35" transport wing, which operates CASA/IPTN CN-235 and EADS CASA C-295 aircraft. The base also supports various other units, including the Cartographic Center's fleet.

In addition to its military role, the base is closely associated with the aerospace industry. Adjacent facilities, formerly belonging to CASA and now part of Airbus (EADS CASA), are used for assembling Spanish Eurofighter Typhoons and maintaining and modernizing military aircraft. The Infante de Orleans Foundation, which preserves historic aircraft, is set to relocate near the base. Proposals to convert the base into a civil airport have faced opposition, with local authorities prioritizing its military and industrial functions.

trgt:
Malice Mizer (stylized as MALICE MIZER) was a Japanese visual kei rock band active from August 1992 to December 2001. Formed by guitarists Mana and Közi, their earlier music and themes were characterized by their strong French and classical influences, later moving away from deliberate French romanticism and incorporating Gothic aspects after several difficulties befell the band. Their official fan club is named "Ma chérie".

Malice Mizer was as famous for their live shows as they were for their music, featuring lavish historical costumes and stage sets, short silent theater pieces preluding various songs, and even a particularly notable instance of the vocalist descending to Earth (the stage) as a fallen angel, only to ascend again at the end of the concert. Throughout their history, the band has gone through several different lineups and three drastic image changes. At their peak, they were considered one of "the big four of visual kei" alongside La'cryma Christi, Fanatic Crisis and Shazna. On December 11, 2001, it was announced that Malice Mizer would go on an indefinite hiatus. Mana, Közi and bassist Yu~ki have performed together several times since 2010.
mgt:
Malice Mizer was a Japanese visual kei band active from 1992 to 2001, known for their theatrical performances, elaborate costumes, and diverse musical styles, which ranged from gothic rock and neoclassical darkwave to art rock and synthpop. Founded by guitarists Mana and Közi, the band underwent three distinct eras, each defined by a different vocalist: Tetsu, Gackt, and Klaha. Their early work featured gothic and classical influences, while the Gackt era brought a more colorful, art rock style, and the Klaha era embraced a darker, gothic aesthetic.

The band achieved significant success, particularly during the Gackt era, with their major-label debut album *Merveilles* and performances at venues like Nippon Budokan. However, internal changes, including Gackt's departure and the death of drummer Kami in 1999, marked a turning point. Malice Mizer disbanded in 2001, though members pursued solo projects, with Mana founding Moi dix Mois and a fashion label.

The band has occasionally reunited for special events, such as the Deep Sanctuary concerts, celebrating their legacy. Malice Mizer remains influential in the visual kei scene and is remembered for their innovative artistry and dramatic flair.

page_title: Gymnastics at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Men's horizontal bar
trgt:
The men's horizontal bar competition at the 2016 Summer Olympics was held at the HSBC Arena on 6 and 16 August. There were 71 competitors from 34 nations. The event was won by Fabian Hambüchen of Germany, the nation's first victory in the horizontal bar since 1996 and third overall. Danell Leyva won the United States'third silver medal in four Games in the event; Nile Wilson's bronze was Great Britain's first-ever medal in the horizontal bar.

The medals were presented by Claudia Bokel IOC member, Germany and Naomi Valenzo, FIG Executive Committee Member.
mgt: The men's horizontal bar event at the 2016 Summer Olympics took place at the HSBC Arena in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, with the qualifying round on August 6 and the final on August 16. Seventy-one gymnasts from 34 nations competed, with Fabian Hambüchen of Germany winning gold with a score of 15.766. The event marked its 24th Olympic appearance, with notable returning finalists from 2012, including Epke Zonderland and Danell Leyva. Cyprus, Lithuania, Monaco, and Turkey made their event debuts, while the United States competed for the 22nd time, the most of any nation.

trgt:
"Take Me to the Pilot" is a song written by English musician Elton John and songwriter Bernie Taupin, and performed by John. It was originally released on John's eponymous second album in 1970.

It was recorded at Trident Studios in London in January 1970 and released in the United States in October 1970 as the A-side of a single, with "Your Song" as the B-side. Both songs received airplay, but "Your Song" was preferred by disc jockeys, becoming the singer's first hit (on both sides of the Atlantic) and rendering "Take Me To The Pilot" as the B-side.

The country duo Brothers Osborne recorded a remake of the song for the 2018 tribute album Restoration: Reimagining the Songs of Elton John and Bernie Taupin.
mgt:
"Take Me to the Pilot" is a song by Elton John from his 1970 self-titled album, written by John and Bernie Taupin and produced by Gus Dudgeon. Released as the B-side to "Your Song," it features a blend of art rock and R&B. The song's cryptic lyrics have puzzled listeners, with both John and Taupin admitting they do not know their meaning, likening the writing style to abstract poetry.

A staple of John's live performances, "Take Me to the Pilot" has appeared on numerous concert recordings, including *17-11-70* and *Live in Australia*. It has been covered by artists such as José Feliciano, Ben E. King, and Rick Wakeman, and featured in the soundtrack of the 2018 series *Escape at Dannemora*.

trgt: Homewood Mountain Resort is a ski area located on the west shore of Lake Tahoe in the state of California, a few miles south of Tahoe City in the town of Homewood. It has 1,260 acres (510 ha) of skiable terrain and eight lifts. While it currently does not offer lodging on site, a Draft Environmental Impact Statement has been submitted to the regional planning commission to allow for the development of a resort inclusive of a hotel, condominiums, and various other additions. Since 2006, JMA Ventures, a San Francisco Bay Area developer, has owned and operated the ski area.
mgt: Homewood Mountain Resort is a ski area located on the west shore of Lake Tahoe in California, near the unincorporated village of Homewood. Situated on Ellis Peak within the Tahoe National Forest, the resort features a vertical drop of 1,650 feet, a skiable area of 1,260 acres, and 60 trails catering to all skill levels. Known for its stunning lake views and excellent snow conditions, Homewood receives an average of 400 inches of snowfall annually and enjoys about 300 sunny days per year. In summer, the resort offers hiking and mountain biking trails, along with limited lodge and café services.

trgt:
Harewood House (/ˈhɑːrwʊd/ HAR-wuud, /ˈhɛər-/ HAIR-) is a country house in Harewood, West Yorkshire, England. Designed by architects John Carr and Robert Adam, it was built, between 1759 and 1771, for Edwin Lascelles, 1st Baron Harewood, a wealthy West Indian plantation and slave-owner. The landscape was designed by Lancelot "Capability" Brown and spans 1,000 acres (400 ha) at Harewood.

Still home to the Lascelles family, Harewood House is a member of the Treasure Houses of England, a marketing consortium for ten of the foremost historic homes in the country. The house is a Grade I listed building and a number of features in the grounds and courtyard have been listed as Grade I, II* and II.
mgt: Harewood House is a Grade I listed stately home in Harewood, England, built between 1759 and 1771 for Edwin Lascelles, 1st Baron Harewood, using wealth derived from West Indian plantations. Designed in the Palladian style by John Carr with interiors by Robert Adam, the house has undergone modifications, including expansions by Sir Charles Barry in the 1840s. It has been the family seat of the Lascelles family for generations and is currently home to David Lascelles, the 8th Earl of Harewood. Managed by the Harewood House Trust, the estate is open to the public and features extensive gardens, art collections, a bird garden, and has served as a filming location for various productions.

trgt: Middle Scots was the Anglic language of Lowland Scotland in the period from 1450 to 1700. By the end of the 15th century, its phonology, orthography, accidence, syntax and vocabulary had diverged markedly from Early Scots, which was virtually indistinguishable from early Northumbrian Middle English. Subsequently, the orthography of Middle Scots differed from that of the emerging Modern English standard. Middle Scots was fairly uniform throughout its many texts, albeit with some variation due to the use of Romance forms in translations from Latin or French, turns of phrases and grammar in recensions of southern texts influenced by southern forms, misunderstandings and mistakes made by foreign printers.
mgt: Middle Scots was the Anglic language spoken and written in the Scottish Lowlands and parts of the Northern Isles from the late 14th century to the mid-18th century, evolving into Modern Scots. It developed from Early Scots, which itself originated from Northumbrian Old English. By the early 16th century, the term "Scottis" referred to this language, distinguishing it from Gaelic, which was then called "Erse" (Irish). Middle Scots saw significant southern English influence following the Union of the Crowns in 1603 and the Union of Parliaments in 1707, leading to the adoption of Modern English as the literary standard, while Scots remained a vernacular language.

trgt: The 1979 Memorial Cup occurred May 6–13 at the Palais des Sports in Sherbrooke, Quebec, the Colisée de Trois-Rivières in Trois-Rivières, Quebec and at the Verdun Auditorium in Verdun, Quebec. It was the 61st annual Memorial Cup competition and determined the major junior ice hockey champion of the Canadian Hockey League (CHL). Participating teams were the winners of the Ontario Major Junior Hockey League, Quebec Major Junior Hockey League and Western Hockey League which were the Peterborough Petes, Trois-Rivières Draveurs and Brandon Wheat Kings. Peterborough won their first Memorial Cup, defeating Brandon in the final game.
mgt: The 1979 Memorial Cup was the 61st edition of the Canadian Hockey League's (CHL) championship tournament, held from May 6 to 13, 1979, at venues in Sherbrooke, Trois-Rivières, and Verdun, Quebec. The tournament featured three teams: the Peterborough Petes (Ontario Major Junior Hockey League), Brandon Wheat Kings (Western Hockey League), and Trois-Rivières Draveurs (Quebec Major Junior Hockey League). The Peterborough Petes won their first Memorial Cup title, defeating the Wheat Kings in the final. The tournament showcased standout players, including future NHL stars, and highlighted the dominance of each team in their respective leagues during the 1978–79 season.

trgt:
Strandflat (Lang-no) is a landform typical of the Norwegian coast consisting of a flattish erosion surface on the coast and near-coast seabed. In Norway, strandflats provide room for settlements and agriculture, constituting important cultural landscapes. The shallow and protected waters of strandflats are valued fishing grounds that provide sustenance to traditional fishing settlements. Outside Norway proper, strandflats can be found in other high-latitude areas, such as Antarctica, Alaska, the Canadian Arctic, the Russian Far North, Greenland, Svalbard, Sweden and Scotland.

The strandflats are usually bounded on the landward side by a sharp break in slope, leading to mountainous terrain or high plateaux. On the seaward side, strandflats end at submarine slopes. The bedrock surface of strandflats is uneven and tilts gently towards the sea.

The concept of a strandflat was introduced in 1894 by Norwegian geologist Hans Reusch.
mgt:
A strandflat is a coastal landform characterized by a low-relief surface that transitions from land to sea, commonly found in high-latitude regions such as Norway, Alaska, Greenland, and parts of Antarctica. It typically consists of three zones: the supramarine zone, the skerry archipelago, and the submarine zone, and may include features like skerries, embayments, and rauks (residual mountains). Strandflats vary in width, reaching up to 80 kilometers, and extend underwater to depths of 30–60 meters.

The geological origin of strandflats remains debated, with theories ranging from glacial and marine erosion during the Quaternary glaciations to deep weathering in tropical climates during the Mesozoic. In Norway, strandflats are prominent along the coast, particularly in Nordland, where they are larger and flatter than in other regions. Smaller strandflats have also been identified in Scotland, Russia, and the South Shetland Islands.

trgt:
The hôtel de la Marine (also known as the hôtel du Garde-Meuble) is an historic building located on place de la Concorde in Paris, to the east of rue Royale. It was designed and built between 1757 and 1774 by the architect Ange-Jacques Gabriel, on the newly created square first called Place Louis XV. The identical building to its west, constructed at the same time, now houses the hôtel de Crillon and the Automobile Club of France.

The Hôtel de la Marine was originally the home of the royal Garde-Mobile, the office managing the furnishing of all royal properties. Following the French Revolution it became the Ministry of the French Navy, which occupied it until 2015. It was entirely renovated between 2015 and 2021. It now displays the restored 18th century apartments of Marc-Antoine Thierry de Ville-d'Avray, the King's Intendant of the Garde-Meuble, as well the salons and chambers later used by the French Navy. A separate part displays the Al Thani Collection presenting international and inter-cultural works of art from the collection of Sheik Hamad bin Abdullah Al Thani.
mgt:
The Hôtel de la Marine is a historic neoclassical building located on the Place de la Concorde in Paris, France. Designed by Ange-Jacques Gabriel and completed in 1774, it originally served as the Garde-Meuble de la Couronne, housing royal furniture, art, and decorative objects. During the French Revolution, it became a pivotal site, with its weapons used in the storming of the Bastille. From 1789 to 2015, it served as the headquarters of the French Navy, undergoing significant modifications over the centuries.

The building is renowned for its grand facade, inspired by the Louvre Colonnade, and its richly decorated interiors, including the apartments of the Intendant and the opulent salons used for naval and state functions. Following extensive restoration completed in 2021, the Hôtel de la Marine reopened as a public monument under the Center of National Monuments. It now houses the Al Thani Collection, showcasing art and artifacts from around the world, and serves as a prominent cultural and historical landmark in Paris.

trgt: Nippu Ravva (transl. Fire Spark) is a 1993 Telugu-language action film, produced by M.V.Srinivas Prasad on Yuvarathna Arts banner and directed by A. Kodandarami Reddy. The film stars Nandamuri Balakrishna, Vijayashanti, and music composed by Bappi Lahiri & Raj–Koti with A. R. Rahman's background score. Nippu Ravva and Bangaru Bullodu, also starring Balakrishna, were released on the same day and both ran for over 100 days. It was dubbed into Tamil and Hindi as Suriya and Mawaali Raaj, respectively.
mgt: *Nippu Ravva* is a 1993 Indian Telugu-language action drama film directed by A. Kodandarami Reddy and written by the Paruchuri Brothers. Produced by M. V. Srinivas Prasad under Yuvarathna Arts, the film stars Nandamuri Balakrishna and Vijayashanti. The story follows Suryam, a trade union leader who fights against the exploitation of coal mine workers while seeking justice for his sister. The film features music composed by Bappi Lahiri and Raj–Koti, with a background score by A. R. Rahman. It was released on 3 September 1993.

trgt: Summoning is an Austrian atmospheric black metal band based in Vienna. Since their formation in 1993, they have released eight full-length albums and two EPs via Napalm Records. Their lyrics make extensive use of J.R.R. Tolkien's Middle-earth writings and mythology. Since 1995, their lineup has had a consistent lineup of Richard Lederer as "Protector" and Michael Gregor as "Silenius". The band never played live performances and has consistently maintained that they have no interest in ever doing so. Their latest album With Doom We Come was released on 5 January 2018.
mgt: Summoning is an Austrian atmospheric black metal band formed in Vienna in 1993 by Michael Gregor (Silenius), Richard Lederer (Protector), and Alexander Trondl (Trifixion). Known for their epic and atmospheric sound, the band incorporates keyboards, programmed drums, and influences from J.R.R. Tolkien's Middle-earth literature. Their early work, including the debut album *Lugburz* (1995), was rooted in traditional black metal, but subsequent releases like *Minas Morgul* (1995) and *Dol Guldur* (1997) marked a shift toward a more atmospheric style. Summoning remains active, with their latest album, *With Doom We Come*, released in 2018.

trgt: William David Arnett (born 1940) is a Regents Professor of Astrophysics at Steward Observatory, University of Arizona, known for his research on supernova explosions, the formation of neutron stars or black holes by gravitational collapse, and the synthesis of elements in stars; he is author of the monograph Supernovae and Nucleosynthesis which deals with these topics. Arnett pioneered the application of supercomputers to astrophysical problems, including neutrino radiation hydrodynamics, nuclear reaction networks, instabilities and explosions, supernova light curves, and turbulent convective flow in two and three dimensions.
mgt: W. David Arnett (born 1940) is an American astrophysicist known for his contributions to nuclear astrophysics and the study of supernovae. He earned his PhD in physics from Yale University in 1965 under the mentorship of Alastair G. W. Cameron. Arnett conducted postdoctoral research with notable scientists W. A. Fowler and Fred Hoyle before holding faculty positions at several universities, including the University of Chicago and the University of Arizona, where he became a Regents Professor. He has received prestigious awards, including the Hans Bethe Prize and the Henry Norris Russell Lectureship.

page_title: The Romance of Tiger and Rose
trgt:
The Romance of Tiger and Rose (Chinese: 传闻中的陈芊芊; pinyin: Chuánwén Zhōng De Chén Qiānqiān) is a 2020 Chinese streaming television series starring Zhao Lusi and Ding Yuxi, and tells the story of a young screenwriter who must survive after being trapped in her own script, with her character meant to die early in the story. The series airs on Tencent Video starting May 18, 2020.

The series became a hit on social media, with its hashtag garnering over 3 billion hits on Weibo. By June 2, the series received over 897 million views on Tencent Video.
mgt: *The Romance of Tiger and Rose* (传闻中的陈芊芊) is a 2020 Chinese romantic comedy and historical drama television series created and written by Nan Zhen, directed by Cha Chuanyi, and starring Zhao Lusi and Ding Yuxi. The story follows Chen Xiaoqian, a screenwriter who is transported into her own script as the doomed third princess, Chen Qianqian. To survive, she must navigate her story's plot and bring the lead characters together. The series, produced by Tencent Penguin Pictures and Orange Pictures, aired on Tencent Video from May 18 to June 1, 2020, and received positive reviews for its unique premise.

trgt:
The bullace is a variety of plum. It bears edible fruit similar to those of the damson, and like the damson is considered to be a strain of the insititia subspecies of Prunus domestica. Although the term has regionally been applied to several different kinds of "wild plum" found in the United Kingdom, it is usually taken to refer to varieties with a spherical shape, as opposed to the oval damsons.

Unlike nearly all damsons, bullaces may be either "white" (i.e. yellow or green) or "black" (i.e. blue or purple) in colour, and ripen up to six weeks later in the year. Though smaller than most damsons, bullaces are much larger than the closely related sloe. Their flavour is usually rather acidic until fully ripe.
mgt: The bullace (*Prunus domestica* var. *nigra*) is a variety of wild plum in the family Rosaceae, native to Great Britain and possibly derived from hybrids of the sloe (*Prunus spinosa*) and cherry plum (*Prunus cerasifera*). Known for its hardiness, the bullace was historically cultivated but fell out of favor as larger and sweeter plums and damsons became popular. It is distinguishable by its broader leaves, lack of spines, and late-ripening fruit, which matures in October–November. Four main varieties are recognized: Black, White, Shepherd's, and Langley Bullace, each with distinct characteristics. Bullaces are used for cooking, preserves, and fruit wine, though some varieties are palatable raw when ripe. They are often found in hedgerows, woods, or old orchards and remain available from select nurseries.

trgt:
Basuki Tjahaja Purnama (Chinese: 鐘萬學; pinyin: Zhōng Wànxué, Pha̍k-fa-sṳ: Chûng Van-ho̍k; born 29 June 1966) is an Indonesian businessman, politician, and former governor of Jakarta. He is also known by his Hakka Chinese nickname Ahok (Chinese: 阿學). He was the second minority governor of Jakarta with Chinese ancestry and also of the Evangelical faith, following Henk Ngantung, who was governor of Catholic faith from 1964–65.

Ahok was a legislator in the Indonesian People's Representative Council (DPR) and Regent of East Belitung. He was elected to the House of Representatives for the 2009–2014 term but resigned in 2012 to run for the deputy governorship of Jakarta, to which he was elected. In November 2014, he became governor of Jakarta, as his predecessor Joko Widodo had become president. Ahead of the 2017 Jakarta gubernatorial election, Ahok's political rivals aligned themselves with Islamic extremists to exploit religious and racial intolerance, resulting in Ahok being accused of blasphemy in October 2016. He then lost the election to former Education Minister Anies Baswedan and was imprisoned for blasphemy.
mgt:
Basuki Tjahaja Purnama (born 29 June 1966), also known as Ahok, is an Indonesian politician and businessman who has served as the president commissioner of Pertamina, Indonesia's state-owned oil and gas company, since 2019. He was the 15th Governor of Jakarta from 2014 to 2017, succeeding Joko Widodo. Ahok is notable for being Jakarta's first governor of Chinese descent and a Christian in over 50 years, making him a "double minority" in Indonesia. His tenure was marked by significant infrastructure projects, anti-corruption efforts, and controversial policies, including forced evictions of urban poor communities.

Ahok's political career began as the Regent of East Belitung (2005–2006) and later as a member of the People's Representative Council (2009–2012). He rose to prominence as Jakarta's deputy governor (2012–2014) before becoming governor. His governorship ended in 2017 following a blasphemy conviction, which led to a two-year prison sentence. The case drew international attention and criticism, highlighting issues of religious tolerance and minority rights in Indonesia. After his release, Ahok joined the Indonesian Democratic Party of Struggle (PDI-P).

trgt:
The Federation of State Medical Boards (FSMB) of the United States is a national non-profit organization that represents the 71 state medical and osteopathic boards of the United States and its territories and co-sponsors the United States Medical Licensing Examination. Medical boards license physicians, investigate complaints, discipline those who violate the law, conduct physician evaluations, and facilitate the rehabilitation of physicians where appropriate. The FSMB's mission calls for "continual improvement in the quality, safety and integrity of health care through the development and promotion of high standards for physician licensure and practice."

Based in Euless, Texas and Washington, D.C., the FSMB serves as the national voice for its member boards and is a recognized authority throughout the United States and internationally on issues related to medical licensure and discipline.

The United States Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE) is a three-step (three-part) professional examination for medical licensure that is sponsored by the FSMB and the National Board of Medical Examiners (NBME).
mgt:
The Federation of State Medical Boards (FSMB) is a professional association founded in 1912 that represents the 71 state and territorial medical boards in the United States, including boards of osteopathic medicine. Headquartered in Washington, D.C., the FSMB supports its member boards in regulating the medical profession to ensure public safety and uphold standards of competence and ethics. It provides services such as the Federation Physician Data Center, which tracks disciplinary actions against physicians, and the Federation Credentials Verification Service, which maintains a repository of physician credentials.

The FSMB sponsors the United States Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE) and collaborates with organizations like the Accreditation Council for Continuing Medical Education and the Educational Commission for Foreign Medical Graduates. It also publishes the *Journal of Medical Regulation* and conducts research, policy development, and public outreach. The FSMB Foundation, its philanthropic arm, funds education and research initiatives, including efforts to address the COVID-19 pandemic.

trgt:
Artificial dielectrics are fabricated composite materials, often consisting of arrays of conductive shapes or particles in a nonconductive support matrix, designed to have specific electromagnetic properties similar to dielectrics. As long as the lattice spacing is smaller than a wavelength, these substances can refract and diffract electromagnetic waves, and are used to make lenses, diffraction gratings, mirrors, and polarizers for microwaves. These were first conceptualized, constructed and deployed for interaction in the microwave frequency range in the 1940s and 1950s. The constructed medium, the artificial dielectric, has an effective permittivity and effective permeability, as intended.

In addition, some artificial dielectrics may consist of irregular lattices, random mixtures, or a non-uniform concentration of particles.

Artificial dielectrics came into use with the radar microwave technologies developed between the 1940s and 1970s. The term "artificial dielectrics" came into use because these are macroscopic analogues of naturally occurring dielectrics. The difference between the natural and artificial substance is that the atoms or molecules are artificially (human) constructed materials. Artificial dielectrics were proposed because of the need for lightweight structures and components for various microwave delivery devices.

Artificial dielectrics are a direct historical link to metamaterials.
mgt:
Artificial dielectrics are engineered materials designed to mimic the electromagnetic properties of natural dielectrics, such as electric permittivity and magnetic permeability, by using larger-scale structures to replicate the behavior of atoms and molecules. The concept was introduced by Winston E. Kock in 1948 while at Bell Laboratories, driven by the need for lightweight, low-loss materials for use in radio frequency and microwave applications. Unlike natural dielectrics, which rely on atomic or molecular interactions, artificial dielectrics achieve their properties through macroscopic structures, such as lattices of scatterers, that interact with electromagnetic fields in a controlled manner.

These materials are constructed using elements like spheres, disks, or conducting strips, whose geometric shapes determine their scattering behavior. A notable example is the rodded medium, or wire grid, a lattice of thin parallel wires studied by researchers such as J. Brown, K.E. Golden, and W. Rotman. Artificial dielectrics laid the groundwork for the development of metamaterials, which extend the concept to more complex and tunable electromagnetic responses. By synthesizing effective media properties, artificial dielectrics have become essential in advancing technologies in telecommunications, radar, and other electromagnetic applications.

trgt:
Waldorf-Astoria Hotel and Residence Tower Chicago, was a planned hotel and condominium skyscraper that would have been 1,265 feet (386 m) tall. The Prime Group was in charge of the project, which was proposed at a cost of $610 million. The building would have been located one block from the Chicago River, and north of the Sheraton Chicago in the Streeterville neighborhood of the Near North Side community area of Chicago. The building's developers, the Fordham Company, were the initial developers of the now-cancelled Chicago Spire.

The building would have been the third tallest building in Chicago as only the Willis Tower and the Trump International Hotel and Tower are taller than 1,200 feet (366 m). It would have been among the tallest buildings in the world, since only 25 buildings in the world currently exceed 1,200 feet (366 m).

Early in 2012 the approval process began for a much smaller mixed-use tower on the site.
mgt:
The Waldorf-Astoria Hotel and Residence Tower was a proposed 107-story mixed-use skyscraper in Chicago, Illinois, designed by DeStefano & Partners and developed by The Prime Group. Planned to reach a height of 1,265 feet (386 meters) with a distinctive tapering design, the building was intended to include a 325-room Waldorf-Astoria hotel managed by Hilton Hotels Corporation and 300 luxury condominiums, including six duplex penthouses. The project aimed to reflect Chicago's architectural trend favoring slender, tall buildings to preserve city views.

The tower's design featured a traditional square base transitioning to a scalpel-shaped top, with plans for a club lounge and restaurant on the 101st floor. Construction was initially scheduled to begin in 2009, with completion targeted for 2012, but the project was delayed. The development was part of a broader expansion of the Waldorf-Astoria brand, alongside other planned locations in Beverly Hills, Orlando, and Montreal.

trgt: Mar Emmanuel III Delly (Lang-syr, Lang-ar) (27 September 1927 – 8 April 2014) was the Patriarch Emeritus of Babylon of the Chaldeans and former Primate of the Chaldean Catholic Church, an Eastern Catholic sui juris particular church of the Catholic Church, and also a cardinal. He was born in Tel Keppe and was ordained a priest on 21 December 1952. He was consecrated a bishop in December 1962 at the age of 35. He was elected Patriarch of the Chaldean Church on 3 December 2003, succeeding the late Patriarch Raphael I Bidawid. He was raised to the rank of cardinal bishop on 24 November 2007.
mgt: Emmanuel III Delly (27 September 1927 – 8 April 2014) was an Iraqi cardinal of the Chaldean Catholic Church who served as Patriarch of Babylon of the Chaldeans from 2003 to 2012. Born in Tel Keppe, Iraq, he was ordained a priest in 1952 and became a bishop in 1963. As patriarch, he led the largest Christian community in Iraq and worked to foster interfaith dialogue, particularly with Muslim leaders. Elevated to the College of Cardinals in 2007 by Pope Benedict XVI, Delly contributed to the global Chaldean Church through the construction of churches and religious institutions. He retired in 2012 and died in San Diego, California, in 2014.

trgt:
The 2009 AFL season was the 113th season of the Australian Football League (AFL), the highest level senior Australian rules football competition in Australia, which was known as the Victorian Football League until 1989. The season featured sixteen clubs, ran from 26 March until 26 September, and comprised a 22-game home-and-away season followed by a finals series featuring the top eight clubs.

The premiership was won by the Geelong Football Club for the eighth time, after it defeated St Kilda by twelve points in the 2009 AFL Grand Final.
mgt: The 2009 AFL season was the 113th season of the Australian Football League (AFL), featuring 16 teams and 185 matches. Geelong claimed its eighth premiership, defeating St Kilda in the Grand Final before a crowd of 99,251. St Kilda finished as minor premiers, while Geelong also won the pre-season cup. Gary Ablett Jr. of Geelong won the Brownlow Medal, and Brendan Fevola of Carlton was the Coleman Medallist with 86 goals. Key events included Ben Cousins' recruitment by Richmond in the pre-season draft and Daniel Rich winning the AFL Rising Star award.

trgt: Yankee Dutch crossing is the name of a dance performed in a 4-by-4 square formation of 8 couples (16 dancers). The original version, called simply Dutch Crossing, was choreographed in the English country dance style by Ernst van Brakel of the Netherlands in 1990. In 2004, contra dance caller Joseph Pimentel modified the choreography in the American style, sometimes called Yankee Dutch crossing to distinguish it from the original. Any number of squares can perform the dance simultaneously. It is not normally found at monthly, bi-monthly or weekly dances. The most likely venues are multi-day dance weekends or weeks, where it is taught in sessions of one hour or longer.
mgt: The Yankee Dutch Crossing is a complex square dance involving 16 dancers arranged in four pairs of couples at the corners of a square. The dance consists of four major parts, each rotating the orientation of the couples by 90 degrees, and each major part is divided into two minor parts. Dancers alternate between two "teams"—the "X team," which traverses the diagonals of the square, and the "O team," which performs a grand right and left around the square's perimeter. The intricate choreography requires precise coordination, and the challenge of maintaining the square's structure adds to the dance's appeal.

trgt:
State Route 202 (SR 202) is a state highway in the U.S. state of Washington, serving part of the Seattle metropolitan area. It runs southeasterly for 31 miles (50 km) in the Eastside region of King County, connecting Woodinville, Redmond, Fall City, and North Bend. The highway begins at SR 522 in Woodinville, intersects SR 520 in Redmond and SR 203 in Fall City, and terminates at Interstate 90 (I-90) in North Bend. The entire highway is designated as the Cascade Valleys Scenic Byway, a state scenic and recreational highway.

Railroads and roads in the Sammamish River and Snoqualmie River valleys, where modern-day SR 202 runs, were built during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The early roads were incorporated into the Yellowstone Trail in the 1910s and became a state highway in 1925. SR 202 was established in 1964 as part of a new state highway system, running from Woodinville to Monroe; SR 522 was designated to the Woodinville–North Bend highway, continuing west to Seattle. By 1970, the designations were reversed, with SR 202 moved to the Woodinville–North Bend highway, and SR 522 moved to the Bothell–Monroe highway.
mgt:
Washington State Route 202 (SR 202) is a 30.53-mile (49.13 km) state highway in King County, Washington, connecting Woodinville and North Bend. Designated as the Cascade Valleys Scenic Byway, the route passes through the Sammamish River valley, Redmond, Fall City, Snoqualmie, and other rural and suburban areas. It begins at an interchange with SR 522 in Woodinville and terminates at Interstate 90 (I-90) in North Bend, serving as a scenic and recreational corridor.

The highway follows a historic route established in the late 19th century alongside the Seattle, Lake Shore and Eastern Railway. It was incorporated into the Sunset Highway in 1909 and later became part of the Yellowstone Trail. SR 202 was officially designated in 1964 during a statewide highway renumbering. Over the years, the route has been modernized with roundabouts, widened sections, and safety improvements to accommodate increasing traffic.

SR 202 is maintained by the Washington State Department of Transportation (WSDOT) and is a popular route for accessing wineries, parks, and attractions like Snoqualmie Falls. Traffic volumes vary, with the highest usage near Woodinville and the lowest in rural areas.

trgt: Andrew Keith Leigh (born 3 August 1972) is an Australian politician, author, lawyer and former professor of economics at the Australian National University. He currently serves as the Assistant Minister for Competition, Charities and Treasury. He briefly served as the Parliamentary Secretary to Prime Minister Julia Gillard in 2013 and then served as Shadow Assistant Treasurer from 2013 to 2019. He has been a Labor member of the Australian House of Representatives since 2010 representing the seat of Fraser until 2016 and Fenner thereafter. Leigh is not a member of any factions of the Labor Party.
mgt: Andrew Keith Leigh (born 3 August 1972) is an Australian politician, economist, and author, serving as the Assistant Minister for Competition, Charities and Treasury since 2022. A member of the Australian Labor Party, he has represented the Division of Fenner in the Australian Parliament since 2016, previously representing Fraser from 2010 to 2016. Before entering politics, Leigh was a professor of economics at the Australian National University and held various advisory and legal roles. A prolific author, he has published extensively on economics, inequality, and social capital. Leigh is also an accomplished endurance athlete and advocate for community engagement.

trgt:
The Gao Empire preceded the Songhai Empire in the region of the Middle Niger. It owes its name to the town of Gao located at the eastern Niger bend. In the ninth century CE, it was considered to be the most powerful West African kingdom.

Gao was founded in the 7th century. Because of its placement along the Niger River, it was a profitable location for fisherman to settle. Gao is one of the oldest trading centers in western Africa. Because of the placement of Gao, Gao became the capital for the Songhai (or Songhay) Empire in the early 11th century. Gao was able to flourish as the capital of the Songhai Empire, most notably via the trans-Saharan trade of gold, copper, slaves, and salt. Gao was annexed by the rulers of the kingdom of Mali in 1325, but Songhai would eventually retain control of it 40 years later.

There are no surviving indigenous written records that date from before the middle of the 17th century. Our knowledge of the early history of the town relies on the writings of external Arabic geographers living in Morocco, Egypt and Andalusia, who never visited the region.
mgt:
The Gao Empire was a prominent West African state that emerged around the 9th century and lasted until 1430, when it was succeeded by the Songhai Empire. Centered in the city of Gao along the Niger River, the empire became a vital hub for trade, fishing, and manufacturing, with its craftspeople producing highly valued carnelian beads. Gao's strategic location contributed to its prosperity and regional influence, particularly during the early Islamic period when it became a center of Muslim culture and governance.

The empire's history is primarily documented through external Arabic sources, as indigenous written records are scarce. Archaeological evidence suggests the existence of two settlements, Gao Ancien and Gao-Saney, which played significant roles in the empire's development. The introduction of Islam to Gao occurred gradually, with the Zuwa dynasty adopting the religion by the late 11th century, possibly influenced by the Almoravids.

Gao's decline began in the late 13th century when it was absorbed into the Mali Empire. However, it regained independence in the 15th century under Sunni rulers, eventually becoming the foundation for the Songhai Empire, which expanded Gao's legacy.

trgt:
Ultimate Canada is a not-for-profit organization that serves as the governing body of the sport of Ultimate (also known as "Ultimate Frisbee") in Canada. It runs the Canadian Ultimate Championships (CUC) and Canadian University Ultimate Championship (CUUC) series.

Canadian Ultimate Championships (CUC)

Each August, teams from across the country travel to the Canadian Ultimate Championships (CUC) to compete for the national title in 7 different divisions: mixed, open, women's, junior open, junior women, masters open and masters women. Teams compete at this seven-day tournament not only to determine the national champion, but also to determine who will represent Canada at the next world championships.

From 2016 to 2019, the mixed divisions were held as a separate event from the remaining divisions.

In 2022, the format was changed again with the three Masters divisions at their own event, the three senior divisions at their own event, and the two Junior divisions at their own event.

Canadian University Ultimate Championships (CUUC)

The CUUC started in 1995 and brings university teams from across the country to compete in the open & the women's division. Each fall Ultimate Canada operates two competitions for university Ultimate teams in Canada: the Canadian University Ultimate Championships (CUUC) and the Canadian Eastern University Ultimate Championships (CEUUC). The CEUUC began in 1998 and brings university teams primarily from Ontario and Quebec together to compete in the open & the women's division.
mgt:
Ultimate Canada is the national governing body for the sport of ultimate in Canada, established in 1993 and affiliated with the World Flying Disc Federation. The organization oversees the development and promotion of ultimate across the country, supporting leagues, tournaments, and player development at all levels. Canada has a rich history in ultimate, with the sport introduced in the early 1970s by Ken Westerfield, who helped establish the Toronto Ultimate Club (TUC), the country's first ultimate league. The TUC, founded in 1980, remains one of the largest and oldest leagues in Canada, with over 3,300 active members.

Ultimate Canada also organizes the Canadian Ultimate Championships, first held in 1987, and has seen Canadian teams achieve international success, including top rankings in the World Flying Disc Federation's Ultimate World Rankings. The sport has grown significantly, with major leagues and associations in cities such as Vancouver, Ottawa, Montreal, Calgary, and London. In 2013, Canada introduced its first semi-professional ultimate team, the Toronto Rush, which won the American Ultimate Disc League (AUDL) Championship in its inaugural season.

Ultimate Canada also established the Canadian Ultimate Hall of Fame in 2011 to honor pioneers and contributors to the sport. With a focus on inclusivity and growth, the organization continues to expand ultimate's reach across the country.

trgt:
Robert Mark Altman (October 10, 1944 – September 24, 2021) was an American photographer. Altman attended Hunter College at the City University of New York and studied psychology and anthropology. Initially he had no intention of becoming a photographer, and said that the camera he wore around his neck was essentially just a prop to "meet girls". However, after graduation, he opened a shop called the Electric Lotus and displayed some of his photographs on a notice board here. The reaction here was so positive that Ansel Adams ended up taking him on as a photography apprentice.

He was soon hired as a photojournalist by Rolling Stone magazine. Here he was able to capture shots of the Rolling Stones while in the recording studio, and also captured shots of artists such as Joni Mitchell and Iggy Pop at music festivals. He found that being a photographer opened doors to places where others were not allowed, and he was able to closely follow the stars and capture images that immortalised the 1960s culture. Following his early success as chief staff photographer for Rolling Stone he expanded into fashion photography and fine art. He became a television producer/director for KEMO-TV, an independent station in the San Francisco Bay Area. For a decade beginning in the mid-1990s, Altman taught web design and photoshop as adjunct professor for several institutions including San Francisco State University and the University of California, Berkeley.
mgt:
Robert Altman (1944 – September 24, 2021) was an American photographer renowned for his iconic images capturing the essence of the 1960s counterculture. His work documented the vibrancy of the era, including the Acid culture, Summer of Love, and the Hippie Movement, with a focus on themes of freedom, protest, and revolutionary ideologies. Altman’s photography, influenced by Ansel Adams, is celebrated for its raw, natural style and unique compositional perspective, which vividly portrayed the connection between performers and their audiences during events like Woodstock.

Altman’s photographs have been widely exhibited, including at Abbey Road Studios, The Beat Museum, and the Newseum, and are part of the permanent collections of institutions such as the Library of Congress, the Smithsonian Institution, and the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame. His work has been featured in numerous publications, and his book *The Sixties: Photographs* (2007) remains a definitive visual record of the decade.

In 2009, Altman collaborated with Macy's Herald Square in New York for the "Art Under Glass" series, showcasing 175 of his images. He was awarded an honorary Doctor of Arts degree from Digital Media Arts College in 2010. Altman passed away on September 24, 2021, from esophageal cancer, leaving behind a legacy as one of the foremost chroniclers of 1960s culture.

trgt: The Habbari (Lang-ar, Sindhi: حباري خاندان) were an Arab dynasty that ruled much of Greater Sindh, as a semi-independent emirate from 854 to 1024. Beginning with the rule of 'Umar bin Abdul Aziz al-Habbari in 854 CE, the region became semi-independent from the Abbasid Caliphate in 861, while continuing to nominally pledge allegiance to the Abbasid Caliph in Baghdad. The Habbari ascension marked the end of a period of direct rule of Sindh by the Umayyad and Abbasid Caliphates, which had begun in 711 CE. The Habbaris were based in the city of Mansura, and ruled central and southern Sindh south of Aror, near the modern-day metropolis of Sukkur. The Habbaris ruled Sindh until they were defeated by Sultan Mahmud Ghaznavi in 1026, who then went on to destroy the old Habbari capital of Mansura, and annex the region to the Ghaznavid Empire, thereby ending Arab rule of Sindh.
mgt:
The Habbari dynasty (854–1011) was an Arab Muslim dynasty that ruled the region of Greater Sindh, with its capital at Mansura. The dynasty was founded by 'Umar bin Abdul Aziz al-Habbari, a member of the Quraysh tribe, who became the de facto governor of Sindh in 841–842 and later established himself as an independent ruler in 861, while maintaining nominal allegiance to the Abbasid Caliphate. The Habbari Emirate controlled areas between the Arabian Sea, Aror, and Khuzdar, while other parts of Greater Sindh were ruled by separate Arab principalities.

Under Habbari rule, Mansura became a wealthy trade hub, attracting merchants and travelers. The dynasty adhered to Sunni Islam, though Ismaili missionary activity flourished in the region. The Habbari dynasty ended in 1011, succeeded by the Soomro and Ghaznavid dynasties. The dynasty played a significant role in the Islamization of Sindh, particularly through the decline of Buddhism and the spread of Islam.

trgt:
The 400th Missile Squadron is an inactive United States Air Force unit. It was last assigned to the 90th Operations Group at Francis E. Warren Air Force Base, Wyoming, where it was inactivated in 2005.

The squadron was first activated as the 10th Reconnaissance Squadron in 1942. Soon renamed the 400th Bombardment Squadron, it flew Consolidated B-24 Liberators in the Pacific during World War II, where it earned two Distinguished Unit Citations and a Philippine Republic Presidential Unit Citation for its actions in combat. After VJ Day, the squadron remained in the Philippines until January 1946, when it was inactivated.

The squadron was activated again in 1964 as the 400th Strategic Missile Squadron, an LGM-30B Minuteman I intercontinental ballistic missile squadron. In 1973 it modernized its Minutemen and in 1986 became the only operational squadron in the Air Force to equip with the LGM-118A Peacekeeper. The squadron was inactivated when the Peacekeeper was removed from the inventory in September 2005, during the implementation of the Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty.
mgt:
The 400th Missile Squadron was a unit of the United States Air Force that served in various roles from 1942 to 2005.
