Initially established as the 10th Reconnaissance Squadron during World War II, it was soon renamed the 400th Bombardment Squadron and operated B-24 Liberators in the Southwest Pacific Theater. The squadron conducted bombing missions in New Guinea, the Philippines, and other Pacific regions, earning three Distinguished Unit Citations and the Philippine Presidential Unit Citation for its actions. It was inactivated in 1946 after the war.

Reactivated in 1964 as an intercontinental ballistic missile squadron at Francis E. Warren Air Force Base, Wyoming, the 400th operated Minuteman I and III missiles before becoming the only squadron to deploy the LGM-118 Peacekeeper missile in 1986. The squadron received the General Samuel C. Phillips Award in 1999 for excellence. Following the retirement of the Peacekeeper system, the 400th Missile Squadron was inactivated on October 1, 2005.

trgt:
Angelino Dulcert (fl. 1339), probably the same person known as Angelino de Dalorto (fl. 1320s), and whose real name was probably Angelino de Dulceto or Dulceti or possibly Angelí Dolcet, was an Italian-Majorcan cartographer.

He is responsible for two notable 14th-century portolan charts, the "Dalorto" chart of 1325 and the "Dulcert" chart of 1339. The latter is the first portolan known to have been produced in Palma, and considered the founding piece of the Majorcan cartographic school. He is also believed to be the author of a third undated and unsigned chart held in London.
mgt: Angelino Dulcert (also known as Angelino Dulceti, Dalorto, or Dolcet) was a 14th-century cartographer, considered a key figure in the transition from the Genoese to the Majorcan school of cartography. Likely of Ligurian origin, he is believed to have emigrated to Majorca in the early 14th century. Dulcert is best known for three portolan charts: the 1325 Dalorto map, the 1339 Dulcert map, and a third, unsigned map attributed to him, dated around 1340. His work introduced inland illustrations and advanced geographic knowledge, influencing the development of European cartography.

trgt:
Brown Boy 2, also known as Good Intentions (Brown Boy 2 Deluxe Version), is a reissued studio album by Canadian rapper Nav. It was released on May 11, 2020, as the deluxe edition of his third studio album, Good Intentions, which was released three days prior, on May 8, 2020. The album also serves as the sequel to Nav's unofficial 2019 extended play, Brown Boy, that his manager Cash released on Audiomack to help Nav come out of retirement at the time. It features additional guest appearances from Quavo and Lil Duke. The original album features guest appearances from Young Thug, Future, Gunna, Travis Scott, Lil Uzi Vert, the late Pop Smoke, Don Toliver, and Lil Durk.

The deluxe edition contributed to Good Intentions debuting at number one on the Billboard 200. The relatively short span of time between the release dates of the re-issue and the standard edition of Good Intentions was a subject of controversy, with journalists viewing the back-to-back releases as being a form of chart manipulation.
mgt:
*Brown Boy 2* is a reissue of Canadian rapper and producer Nav's third studio album, *Good Intentions*. Released on May 11, 2020, just days after the standard edition of *Good Intentions*, the reissue features additional tracks described by Nav as "some snippets, some leaks and some new vibes." The project serves as a continuation of Nav's 2019 *Brown Boy* EP, which was initially released on SoundCloud and later included in the deluxe edition of his second studio album, *Bad Habits*. 

The title *Brown Boy 2* references Nav's nickname, "Brown Boy," a nod to his Indian heritage, as both of his parents are from Punjab, India. Nav has previously released songs titled "Brown Boy," including one on SoundCloud in 2014 and another as part of *Good Intentions*. The album features production from a variety of collaborators, including Tay Keith, Wheezy, and JetsonMade, and spans a runtime of 37 minutes and 40 seconds.

trgt:
Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place in Seneca County, New York, United States. The population was 6,681 at the 2010 census. The 2020 census population of Seneca Falls CDP was 6,809. The hamlet is in the Town of Seneca Falls, east of Geneva. It was an incorporated village from 1831 to 2011.

Finger Lakes Regional Airport (0G7) is south of the hamlet.

Seneca Falls was the site of the Seneca Falls Convention, a foundational event in the Women's Rights Movement. The town is believed by some to have been the inspiration for the fictional town of "Bedford Falls, N.Y." portrayed in filmmaker Frank Capra's classic 1940s film "It's a Wonderful Life".
mgt: Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place (CDP) in Seneca County, New York, located within the town of Seneca Falls. Originally incorporated as a village in 1831, it was dissolved on December 31, 2011, following a vote by residents. The area, historically inhabited by the Cayuga tribe, played a significant role in the Women's Rights Movement, hosting the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, the first women's rights convention in the United States. The village was also a hub for early industrial development, utilizing water power from the Seneca River. Today, Seneca Falls is home to the Women's Rights National Historical Park and the National Women's Hall of Fame.

trgt:
The Young Plan was a constitutional reform proposal carried out in 1946 attempting to introduce representative democracy in Colonial Hong Kong. Named after the then Governor, Mark Young, it was the first major reform proposal to give Hong Kong inhabitants a greater share of managing their own affairs by widening the base of Hong Kong's political system through the creation of a new Municipal Council. The proposed Council was to consist of an elected majority based on a fairly wide franchise, with powers and autonomy over all urban services, education, social welfare, town planning and other functions. It even allowed for indirect election of two Unofficial Members of the Legislative Council (LegCo) by the new Council.

Unofficial Members of the Legislative Council were opposed to the transfer of power to the new body and Young's successor, Governor Alexander Grantham who was opposed to the Young Plan, did not press this issue. Discussion dragged on but the continued opposition of Unofficials in LegCo in addition to the fear of the potential of penetration by Communist China finally killed the plan in 1952. This was the last move towards any form of major electorally-based government until in the 1980s during the Sino-British negotiations on Hong Kong's sovereignty.
mgt:
The Young Plan was a proposed constitutional reform for Hong Kong introduced by Governor Sir Mark Young in 1946, following the end of Japanese occupation during World War II. The plan aimed to establish a more representative government by creating a super municipal council with an elected majority, granting greater autonomy to local governance. The council would have included both Chinese and non-Chinese members, reflecting Hong Kong's diverse population, and would have overseen urban services, education, and social welfare. The proposal was part of broader British efforts to promote self-governance across its empire and to foster loyalty among Hong Kong's residents amid rising Chinese influence and calls for the colony's retrocession.

However, the plan faced significant opposition from colonial officials, British business elites, and some local leaders, who criticized its feasibility and potential political risks. Governor Alexander Grantham, who succeeded Young in 1947, was particularly resistant, delaying its implementation. By 1949, the plan was formally abandoned following a motion in the Legislative Council. Subsequent minor reforms, such as reintroducing elected seats in the Urban Council, were implemented instead. The failure of the Young Plan marked the end of major political reform efforts in Hong Kong until the 1980s, as Cold War tensions and other geopolitical factors overshadowed the issue.

trgt:
KGGF (690 MHz) is a commercial AM radio station in Coffeyville, Kansas. It airs a talk radio format and is owned by Sek Media, LLC. The studios and offices are on West 8th Street in Coffeyville.

KGGF has a daytime power of 10,000 watts, covering parts of Kansas, Oklahoma, Missouri and Arkansas. AM 690 is a Canadian and Mexican clear channel frequency. So at night, to reduce interference to other stations, KGGF reduces power to 5,000 watts. A directional antenna is used at all times. The transmitter is a Harris DX-10 installed in 1997. KGGF uses a two-tower array by day and a four-tower array at night, located off Clay Road.
mgt: KGGF (690 AM) is a talk radio station licensed to Coffeyville, Kansas, serving Southeast Kansas, Eastern Oklahoma, Southwest Missouri, and Northwest Arkansas. Established in 1930, it originally broadcast at 1010 kHz before moving to its current frequency of 690 kHz in 1941 under the North American Regional Broadcasting Agreement. The station operates at 10,000 watts during the day and 5,000 watts at night. Owned by SEK Media, LLC, KGGF features a mix of local news, farm reports, and nationally syndicated conservative talk shows, with hosts such as Sean Hannity and Dave Ramsey. Weekend programming includes specialty shows on gardening, technology, and old-time radio.

trgt: Exabyte Corporation was a manufacturer of magnetic tape data storage products headquartered in Boulder, Colorado, United States. Exabyte Corp. is now defunct, but the company's technology is sold by Tandberg Data under both brand names. Prior to the 2006 demise, Exabyte offered tape storage and automation solutions for servers, workstations, LANs and SANs. Exabyte is best known for introducing the Data8 (8mm) magnetic tape format in 1987. At the time of its demise, Exabyte manufactured VXA and LTO based products. The company controlled VXA technology but did not play a large role in the LTO community.
mgt: Exabyte Corporation was an American data storage company founded in 1985 in Boulder, Colorado, by Juan Rodriguez, Harry Hinz, Kelly Beavers, and former StorageTek engineers. The company pioneered the use of consumer videotape technology for data storage and introduced the Data8 magnetic tape format in 1987, which advanced computer backup technology. Exabyte went public on NASDAQ in 1989 under the symbol EXBT. In 2001, it merged with Ecrix, a company co-founded by two of Exabyte's original founders, gaining access to Ecrix's VXA Packet Technology. Exabyte was acquired by Tandberg Data in 2006.

trgt: Peaked Mountain (pronounced pea-kid) is a nature reserve located in Monson, Massachusetts. The property is approximately 2,000 acres (8.1 km2) and is owned by The Trustees of Reservations, a non-profit conservation organization. The principal landowners of its western slope, Leonard and Roslyn Harrington and Richard Elliott, have long permitted the public to hike through their properties to the summit. Concerned about the mountain's future, they sought the assistance of The Trustees of Reservations in developing a long-term strategy for protecting the mountain and securing public access after their lifetimes.
mgt: Peaked Mountain is a 1,227-foot (374 m) summit located in Monson, Massachusetts, and is the highest point on a north-south ridge extending into Connecticut. Managed by The Trustees of Reservations, the mountain is part of a diverse natural area featuring forests, rocky outcrops, wetlands, and ponds. Its summit offers panoramic views of the surrounding New England countryside, including Mount Monadnock, Mount Wachusett, and Shenipsit State Forest. Historically, the area was used for agriculture, charcoal production, and timber harvesting. Today, it is a popular destination for hiking, birdwatching, and other recreational activities.

trgt: Daniel James (1801–1876) was one of the three founder partners of Phelps, Dodge & Co., a New York trading organisation established in 1833/4, exporting cotton to England and importing manufactured goods in return such as tin, tin plate, iron and copper. James was born in America but was to live in Liverpool for 47 years running the British side of the business called Phelps, James & Co. The company was to dominate the export market of tinplate from the United Kingdom for three-quarters of a century at a time when Wales was the centre of world production.
mgt: Daniel James (April 17, 1801 – November 27, 1876) was an American-born British businessman and partner in the prominent mercantile firm Phelps, Dodge & Co., which dominated the export of tinplate from the UK and the import of metals and cotton to the United States. Born in Truxton, New York, he moved to Liverpool in 1831 to manage the firm's British operations, where he played a key role in its success despite economic challenges. A devout Christian, James was involved in philanthropy, including chairing relief efforts during the UK cotton famine. He became a naturalized British citizen in 1866.

trgt:
The surname Traill (also Trail, Traille, Traillie, Traily, etc.) Is derived from Norse to at least Norman via France (is not of French 'origin', 'origin' is the wrong word to use here, the first written records of it come from France via Normandy, its origin is Norse via Normandy at least), it does also seem to have some relation to words from the northern UK, pointing again to were it is derived from. It became "Traill" in Scotland (show any 'modern' non Scottish spread) and thence spread around the world.

The family is recorded in France from the 10th century, as Barons in Britain from the 11th century, as Lairds in Scotland from the 14th century and later in Orkney. In the 17th century they were prominent in Northern Ireland and also spread to various parts of the United States including the Cajun community. Other branches of the family settled in Argentina in the 19th century, and in the British Colonies.
mgt:
The Traill surname has French origins, with Goidfrid de Traillie arriving in England during the Norman Conquest and holding lands in Bedford and near the Scottish border. The family later expanded to Scotland, where members such as Walter Traill, Bishop of St Andrews, and Robert Traill of Greyfriars played notable roles. The Traills were associated with the Blebo estate in Fife, Scotland, which became a significant family seat. In the 16th century, branches of the family migrated to Orkney, Ireland, and beyond.

In Ireland, the Traills included prominent figures like Reverend Robert Traill, who served during the Great Famine, and Anthony Traill, Provost of Trinity College Dublin. The family also spread to the United States, settling in Maryland and other regions, and later to Argentina, where Johnny Traill became a renowned polo player. The Traill name has also appeared in literature, including Daisy Goodwin's *Silver River*, which explores the family's history and legacy.

trgt: A pressurized heavy-water reactor (PHWR) is a nuclear reactor that uses heavy water (deuterium oxide D2O) as its coolant and neutron moderator. PHWRs frequently use natural uranium as fuel, but sometimes also use very low enriched uranium. The heavy water coolant is kept under pressure to avoid boiling, allowing it to reach higher temperature (mostly) without forming steam bubbles, exactly as for pressurized water reactor. While heavy water is very expensive to isolate from ordinary water (often referred to as light water in contrast to heavy water), its low absorption of neutrons greatly increases the neutron economy of the reactor, avoiding the need for enriched fuel. The high cost of the heavy water is offset by the lowered cost of using natural uranium and/or alternative fuel cycles. As of the beginning of 2001, 31 PHWRs were in operation, having a total capacity of 16.5 GW(e), representing roughly 7.76% by number and 4.7% by generating capacity of all current operating reactors.
mgt:
A pressurized heavy-water reactor (PHWR) is a type of nuclear reactor that uses heavy water (deuterium oxide) as a neutron moderator and coolant, enabling the use of natural uranium as fuel. This design eliminates the need for expensive uranium enrichment facilities, making it more cost-effective in certain contexts. PHWRs, such as the CANDU reactor, are highly efficient due to their ability to moderate neutrons at lower temperatures, which increases the likelihood of fission reactions. They can also utilize a variety of fuels, including reprocessed uranium, spent nuclear fuel, and mixed oxide (MOX) fuel.

However, PHWRs have drawbacks, including the high cost of heavy water, increased spent fuel volume, and the production of tritium, a radioactive isotope. Additionally, their ability to produce weapons-grade plutonium poses a nuclear proliferation risk, as demonstrated by India's use of a heavy-water reactor to develop its first nuclear weapon. Despite these challenges, PHWRs remain a significant technology in nuclear energy production.

trgt: The Hồ dynasty (Vietnamese: Nhà Hồ, chữ Nôm: 茹胡;Sino-Vietnamese: Hồ triều, chữ Hán: 胡朝) was a short-lived Vietnamese dynasty consisting of the reigns of two monarchs, Hồ Quý Ly (胡季犛) in 1400–01 and his second son, Hồ Hán Thương (胡漢蒼), who reigned the kingdom of Đại Ngu from 1401 to 1406. The practice of bequeathing the throne to a designated son (not simply passing it on to the eldest) was similar to what had happened in the previous Trần dynasty and was meant to avoid sibling rivalry. Hồ Quý Ly's eldest son, Hồ Nguyên Trừng, played his part as the dynasty's military general. In 2011, UNESCO declared the Citadel of the Hồ Dynasty in Thanh Hóa Province a world heritage site.
mgt:
The Hồ dynasty (1400–1407), officially known as Đại Ngu, was a short-lived imperial dynasty in Vietnam founded by Hồ Quý Ly. It succeeded the Trần dynasty after Hồ Quý Ly's coup d'état and marked a period of significant political and social reform. Hồ Quý Ly implemented land reforms, introduced paper currency, promoted the Chữ Nôm script for the Vietnamese language, and restructured the education system to include mathematics and agriculture. He also renamed the country Đại Ngu, claiming descent from the ancient Chinese Emperor Shun.

Despite initial military successes, including territorial gains from Champa, the dynasty faced internal unrest and external threats. In 1407, the Ming dynasty invaded, capturing Hồ Quý Ly and his family, leading to the dynasty's collapse and the Fourth Era of Northern Domination.

trgt: Argentina was one of the seventeen nations that competed at the inaugural Summer Paralympic Games in 1960 held in Rome, Italy from September 19 to 24, 1968. Preparations for the Games began two years prior in 1958 to stage what was at the time called the 9th Annual International Stoke Mandeville Games. The team finished tenth in the medal table with a total of six medals, two gold, three silver and one bronze. The Argentinian team consisted of five athletes, one man and four women.
mgt: Argentina competed at the inaugural 1960 Summer Paralympics in Rome, Italy, with a delegation of five athletes, all participating in swimming events. The team achieved significant success, winning a total of six medals: two gold, three silver, and one bronze, ranking 10th in the medal table. Notable performances included Sznitowski, who won a gold and a silver medal, and Perazzo, who claimed a gold medal in the women's 50 metres crawl incomplete class 3. All athletes competed in events for individuals with spinal cord injuries requiring wheelchairs.

trgt: Publius Clodius Thrasea Paetus (died AD 66), Roman senator, who lived in the 1st century AD. Notable for his principled opposition to the emperor Nero and his interest in Stoicism, he was the husband of Arria, who was the daughter of A. Caecina Paetus and the elder Arria, father-in-law of Helvidius Priscus, and a friend and relative by marriage of the poet Persius. Thrasea was the most prominent member of the political faction known today as the Stoic Opposition.
mgt: Publius Clodius Thrasea Paetus (died 66 AD) was a Roman senator, Stoic philosopher, and prominent opponent of Emperor Nero. A member of a distinguished family from Patavium, he rose to political prominence as suffect consul in 56 AD and became known for his advocacy of senatorial freedom and opposition to imperial excesses. Thrasea's dissent from Nero's policies led to his withdrawal from public life and eventual condemnation. In 66 AD, he was forced to commit suicide, becoming a symbol of principled resistance to tyranny.

page_title: Eastwood After Hours: Live at Carnegie Hall
trgt:
Eastwood After Hours: Live at Carnegie Hall is a two-disc live album by American actor Clint Eastwood and various jazz musicians. Released on April 29, 1997 by Warner Bros. Records, it compiles material from Eastwood's film scores—including Play Misty for Me (1971), Honkytonk Man (1982), Bird (1988), Thelonious Monk: Straight, No Chaser (1988), and White Hunter Black Heart (1990)—performed by some of the most reputable practitioners of jazz. Issued five months after the concert, Eastwood After Hours coincided with celebrations for Eastwood's contributions to jazz, and was overseen by producer Bruce Ricker.

Well received upon release, Eastwood After Hours is recognized by music critics as a successful representation of Eastwood's cultural legacy. An accompanying foreword by Eastwood, rare archival photographs, film clips, and commentary documenting Eastwood's film career are featured on the two-disc set.
mgt:
*Eastwood After Hours: Live at Carnegie Hall* is a live jazz album by Clint Eastwood and The Carnegie Hall Jazz Band, released on April 29, 1997, by Warner Bros. Records. Recorded on October 17, 1996, at Carnegie Hall, the performance featured prominent jazz musicians, including Kenny Barron, Joshua Redman, and Roy Hargrove, performing music from soundtracks of Eastwood's films. The highlight was the "Eastwood: After Hours Suite," a medley featuring Eastwood on piano.

A celebrated actor and director, Eastwood's passion for jazz influenced his film career, from employing jazz composers to directing *Bird* (1988). The album, produced by Phil Ramone, Bruce Ricker, and Eastwood, received critical acclaim for its tribute to Eastwood's cultural legacy. Critics praised its spontaneity and musical diversity, with JazzTimes calling it a fitting celebration of Eastwood's musical integrity.

trgt:
The Great Lakes Depression (Lang-mn, Ikh Nuuruudyn Khotgor), also called the Great Lakes' Hollow, is a large semi-arid depression in Mongolia that covers parts of the Uvs, Khovd, Bayan-Ölgii, Zavkhan and Govi-Altai aimags. Bounded by the Altai in the West, Khangai in the East and Tannu-Ola Mountains in the North, it covers the area of over 100,000 km2 (39,000 sq mi) with elevations from 750 to 2,000 m (2,460–6,560 ft).

Small northern parts of the depression are part of Russia.

The depression is named so because it contains six major Mongolian lakes: saline Uvs Nuur, Khyargas Nuur and Dörgön Nuur; and freshwater Khar-Us Nuur, Khar Nuur and Airag Nuur, as well as a number of smaller ones. In addition, it includes 14,000 km2 (5,400 sq mi) of solonchaks and large sandy areas. Northern parts are dominated by arid steppes, and southern by semideserts or deserts. The major rivers are Khovd River, Zavkhan Gol, and Tesiin Gol.
mgt:
The Great Lakes Depression is a large freshwater basin located in Mongolia, covering an area of approximately 100,000 square kilometers (39,000 square miles). It is characterized by a system of interconnected shallow lakes with extensive reed belts, situated within a desert steppe environment. The region is ecologically significant as it supports important wetlands that serve as habitats for rare and migratory bird species, including the Eurasian spoonbill, black stork, osprey, white-tailed eagle, swan goose, and bar-headed goose. A small population of great white pelicans also remains in the area, nesting in regions with abundant fish and vegetation.

The Great Lakes Depression is notable for its unique fish biodiversity, with a high proportion of endemic or near-endemic species. These include members of the genera *Oreoleuciscus* (Altai osmans), *Thymallus* (graylings), and *Triplophysa* (stone loaches), which thrive in the region's aquatic ecosystems.

trgt:
The Hiller YH-32 Hornet (company designation HJ-1) was an American ultralight helicopter built by Hiller Aircraft in the early 1950s. It was a small and unique design because it was powered by two Hiller 8RJ2B ramjet engines mounted on the rotor blade tips which weigh 13 lb (5.9 kg) each and deliver an equivalent of 45 hp (34 kW) for a total of 90 hp (67 kW). Versions of the HJ-1 Hornet were built for the United States Army and the United States Navy in the early 1950s.

The Hiller Museum identifies the YH-32A, named the Sally Rand, as the first helicopter gunship.
mgt: The Hiller YH-32 Hornet was an experimental jet-powered helicopter developed in the 1950s by Hiller Aircraft. It featured a unique design with ramjet engines mounted on the rotor tips, eliminating the need for a tail rotor. While the design was mechanically simple and offered significant lifting capacity, it suffered from high fuel consumption, limited range, excessive noise, and poor autorotation capabilities. Evaluated by the United States Army and Navy, the YH-32 demonstrated potential as an armed helicopter but was ultimately deemed impractical due to its performance limitations. The program was discontinued, and no further production orders were placed.

trgt: AlTibbi (Arabic: الطبي) is a digital health platform in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA). Launched by both Jalil Labadi and his father, Dr. Abdel Aziz Labadi, in 2008 in Amman, Jordan. The platform aims to present reliable, up-to-date and simplified medical information to users in the region in Arabic, according to their proclaimed mission. Today, the website features thousands of medical articles, a medical glossary, a section that is dedicated to questions and answers, the latest news in medicine, telehealth services and consultations and a lot more. AlTibbi has also partnered with Mawdoo3, a leading online Arabic content provider, to provide high quality content to a growing audience in the region.
mgt: Altibbi is a leading Arabic-language digital health platform founded in 2008 by Jalil Labadi in Amman, Jordan. Building on a medical glossary created by his father, Dr. Abdel Aziz Labadi, Altibbi aims to provide reliable medical information and services to Arabic speakers across the MENA region. The platform connects users with over 12,000 accredited doctors, offering remote medical consultations and healthcare services. By 2017, it had over 50,000 registered users for phone consultations and attracted more than 10 million monthly visitors. Altibbi has received significant funding to expand its services and was awarded the Arab E-Content Award in 2013 for its high-quality Arabic medical content.

trgt:
A diatreme, sometimes known as a maar-diatreme volcano, is a volcanic pipe formed by a gaseous explosion. When magma rises up through a crack in Earth's crust and makes contact with a shallow body of groundwater, rapid expansion of heated water vapor and volcanic gases can cause a series of explosions. A relatively shallow crater (known as a maar) is left, and a rock-filled fracture (the actual diatreme) in the crust. Diatremes breach the surface and produce a steep, inverted cone shape.

The term diatreme has been applied more generally to any concave body of broken rock formed by explosive or hydrostatic forces, whether or not it is related to volcanism. The word comes from Ancient Greek δία- (dia-) 'through, across, over' and τρῆμα (trêma) 'hole, aperture'.
mgt:
A diatreme is a volcanic pipe formed by a gaseous explosion, often associated with maar-diatreme volcanoes, which are among the most common types of volcanoes on continents and islands. Diatremes form when igneous intrusions interact with groundwater, a specific geological setting that is not universally present. Notable examples include the Blackfoot and Cross diatremes in British Columbia, Canada.

Diatremes hold economic importance due to their potential association with kimberlite magmas, which originate in the Earth's upper mantle. Kimberlite diatremes can transport diamonds, formed at depths of 150–200 kilometers, to the surface as xenoliths, making them valuable for diamond mining. This connection to diamond deposits has made diatremes significant in the study of mineral resources.

trgt:
South Korea's pension scheme was introduced relatively recently, compared to other democratic nations. Half of the country's population aged 65 and over lives in relative poverty, or nearly four times the 13% average for member countries of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). This makes old age poverty an urgent social problem. Public social spending by general government (the central, state, and local governments, including social security funds) is half the OECD average, and is the lowest as a percentage of GDP among OECD member countries.

South Koreans aged 65 or older may receive three types of pension income: social welfare, a public pension, and a private pension.
mgt:
Pension policy in South Korea is shaped by a combination of public and private systems aimed at addressing the challenges of an aging population and growing social inequality. The National Pension Scheme, introduced in 1988, is the primary public pension system, providing income-related benefits to individuals aged 62 and older who meet contribution requirements. However, only a fraction of retirees receive pensions, contributing to high elderly poverty rates. The Basic Old-Age Pension, launched in 2008, offers additional support to low-income seniors.

Corporate pensions, introduced in 2005, supplement public pensions but face limited coverage. South Korea's rapidly aging population and low fertility rates pose significant challenges, prompting ongoing reforms to ensure financial sustainability and expand coverage.

trgt:
compress is a Unix shell compression program based on the LZW compression algorithm. Compared to more modern compression utilities such as gzip and bzip2, compress performs faster and with less memory usage, at the cost of a significantly lower compression ratio.

The uncompress utility will restore files to their original state after they have been compressed using the compress utility. If no files are specified, the standard input will be uncompressed to the standard output.

In the upcoming POSIX and Single Unix Specification revision, it is planned that DEFLATE algorithm used in gzip format be supported in those utilities.
mgt:
**Compress** is a Unix-based file compression software initially released in February 1985 by Spencer Thomas. It uses the LZW algorithm to reduce file sizes, with compressed files typically given the ".Z" extension. Files can be decompressed using the accompanying `uncompress` utility, which restores the original file attributes. 

The software gained popularity but later declined due to patent restrictions on the LZW algorithm, leading to the rise of alternatives like gzip and bzip2. Despite this, compress remains available on Unix, BSD, and IBM i systems. The LZW patent expired in 2003, making compress freely usable worldwide.

trgt:
The Tres Amigas SuperStation (lit. 'Three Friends SuperStation') is a planned project to unite North America’s two major power grids (the Eastern Interconnection and the Western Interconnection) and one of its eight minor grids (the Texas Interconnection), with the goals of enabling faster adoption of renewable energy and increasing the reliability of the U.S. grid. The project will use superconducting wires from Massachusetts-based American Superconductor Corp. for electrical distribution and to interconvert alternating current (AC) and direct current (DC) power.

Proposed by Tres Amigas, LLC in 2009, the project is in the process of finalizing funding. Permits and construction contracts are in place. CEO Phillip G. Harris is the former CEO of PJM Interconnection, an East Coast regional transmission organization (RTO).
mgt:
The Tres Amigas SuperStation is a proposed energy infrastructure project in the United States designed to connect the country's three major power grids: the Eastern Interconnection, Western Interconnection, and Texas Interconnection. Located near Clovis, New Mexico, the project aims to use superconductive high-voltage direct current (HVDC) transmission lines to enable controlled energy flow while isolating the grids' asynchronous frequencies. With a planned capacity of 30 GW, the SuperStation would facilitate renewable energy transmission and act as a hub for electricity trading.

Initially proposed in 2009, the project's estimated cost has fluctuated between $1 billion and $2 billion, with significant scaling back announced in 2017. Despite delays and challenges, the project remains under development, albeit with a reduced scope.

trgt:
WNSN (101.5 MHz) is a commercial FM radio station in South Bend, Indiana. It is owned by Mid-West Family Broadcasting and airs an adult contemporary radio format known as Sunny 101.5. WNSN is usually the market's top rated radio station according to the Nielsen ratings. Sunny 101.5 carries Delilah's syndicated love songs show in the evening from Premiere Networks. Most weekends it features 1980s hits. From mid-November to Dec. 26, WNSN switches to all Christmas music.

The studios and offices are on East Douglas Road in Mishawaka. The transmitter is off Ironwood Road, south of downtown South Bend. It is co-located with sister station 960 WSBT's tower.
mgt: WNSN (101.5 FM), branded as "Sunny 101.5," is a commercial adult contemporary radio station licensed to South Bend, Indiana, and serving the Michiana area. The station first signed on as WSBT-FM on August 1, 1962, initially simulcasting its AM counterpart before transitioning to beautiful music and later adopting its current adult contemporary format in 1984 under the call sign WNSN. Owned by Mid-West Family Broadcasting, WNSN operates alongside sister stations WSBT, WQLQ, and WZOC. The station has undergone several ownership changes, including its 2016 acquisition by Mid-West Family Broadcasting following the sale of its previous owner, Schurz Communications, to Gray Television.

trgt: Professor Kemal Deen is a Sri Lankan academic surgeon, and a consultant in GastroIntestinal Surgery. Currently, he is the chairman and senior professor of surgery at the University of Kelaniya Medical School, Sri Lanka. He is a founder professor of the Department of Surgery and previously, he held the position of the head of Department of Surgery from 1998 to 2003. His academic degrees are MBBS (Peradeniya); MD (Birmingham); MS (Colombo); FRCS (Glasgow). In 2014, he was elected as the president elect for The College of Surgeons of Sri Lanka.
mgt: Kemal Deen is a Sri Lankan professor of surgery at the University of Kelaniya, known for his contributions to medical education and research. Educated at Trinity College, Kandy, and the University of Peradeniya, he later trained at the University of Birmingham and the Mayo Clinic. Deen played a key role in introducing liver transplantation to Sri Lanka and has published extensively in peer-reviewed journals. He is a Fellow of the Royal College of Surgeons and holds visiting professorships at several international institutions, including the Tata Memorial Cancer Centre in India.

trgt:
The Treaty of Worms was a political alliance formed between Great Britain, Austria and the Kingdom of Sardinia, signed on 13 September 1743, during the War of the Austrian Succession.

It was an ambitious piece of foreign policy on the part of the British government which sought to split the Emperor Charles VII, prince-elector of Bavaria, from French influence, whilst simultaneously resolving the differences between the Emperor, Queen Maria Theresa of Hungary and King Charles Emmanuel III of Sardinia.
mgt: The Treaty of Worms, signed in 1743, was an agreement between Great Britain, Austria, and the Kingdom of Sardinia during the War of the Austrian Succession. Under its terms, Maria Theresa ceded territories, including parts of Piacenza and Pavia, to Sardinia and committed to maintaining troops in Italy. Britain provided financial subsidies and naval support, while Sardinia pledged to field 45,000 men. The treaty faced criticism, particularly for its impact on Genoa, and was condemned by figures such as William Pitt and later criticized by Thomas Carlyle.

trgt: Germania Fluggesellschaft mbH, trading as Germania (IPA-de), was a privately owned German airline with its headquarters in Berlin. It began by focusing on charter operations, then moved towards becoming a scheduled carrier, although some charter flights were still flown under the brand. The change in strategy led to growth over its last few years, and Germania operated to destinations in Europe, North Africa and the Middle East from several German bases. It carried 2.5 million passengers in 2009 and had around 850 employees as of summer 2014. It declared bankruptcy on 4 February 2019 and ceased operations on 5 February 2019.
mgt: Germania was a privately owned German airline founded in April 1978 as Special Air Transport (SAT) and rebranded as Germania in 1986. Headquartered in Berlin, it operated charter and scheduled flights, primarily serving leisure destinations and ethnic routes. The airline was known for its low-cost operations, pioneering fixed-fare pricing, and leasing aircraft to other carriers. Germania also launched the budget airline "gexx" in 2003, which was later absorbed by dba. Despite expansion efforts, including transitioning to an all-Airbus fleet, financial difficulties led to its bankruptcy and cessation of operations on February 5, 2019. Its subsidiaries included Germania Flug and Bulgarian Eagle.

trgt:
The Ministry of Health (Lang-tr) is the ministry of the Government of Turkey responsible for proposing and executing the government policy on health, planning and providing healthcare and protecting consumers. Likewise, it is responsible for proposing and executing the government policy on social cohesion and inclusion, family, protection of minors, youth and of care for dependent or disabled persons. The Ministry is headquartered in the Bakanlıklar in Ankara.

The Ministry of Health is headed by the Minister of Health, who is appointed by the President of Turkey at request of the Turkish Parliament. The current minister is Fahrettin Koca, serving since 10 July 2018.
mgt: The Ministry of Health (Turkey) (Turkish: Sağlık Bakanlığı) is a government agency responsible for public health and healthcare services in Turkey. Established on May 3, 1920, following the foundation of the Grand National Assembly, the ministry has played a central role in shaping the country's healthcare system. Early efforts focused on post-war recovery, legislation, and expanding healthcare access. Key milestones include the adoption of the National Health Plan in 1946, the socialization of health services in 1963, and the Health Transformation Program (2003–2013), which improved healthcare access and satisfaction. Headquartered in Ankara, the ministry is currently led by Fahrettin Koca.

trgt:
Super Smash Bros. (retroactively referred to as Super Smash Bros. 64 or Smash 64) is a 1999 crossover fighting video game developed by HAL Laboratory and published by Nintendo for the Nintendo 64. It was first released in Japan on January 21, 1999, in North America on April 26, 1999, and in Europe on November 19, 1999. The first installment in the Super Smash Bros. series, it is a crossover between several different Nintendo franchises, including Mario, The Legend of Zelda, Star Fox, Yoshi, Donkey Kong, Metroid, F-Zero, Mother, Kirby, and Pokémon. It presents a cast of characters and locations from these franchises and allows players to use each character's unique skills and the stage's hazards to inflict damage, recover health, and ultimately knock opponents off the stage.

Super Smash Bros. received mostly positive reviews upon its release. It was a commercial success, selling over five million copies worldwide by 2001, with 2.93 million sold in the United States and 1.97 million sold in Japan. It was given an Editors'Choice award from IGN for the "Best Fighting Game", and also became a Nintendo 64 Player's Choice title. The game spawned a series of sequels for each successive Nintendo console, starting with Super Smash Bros. Melee which was released for the GameCube in 2001.
mgt:
*Super Smash Bros.* is a crossover fighting game developed by HAL Laboratory and published by Nintendo for the Nintendo 64. Directed by Masahiro Sakurai, it was released in Japan on January 21, 1999, followed by North America and Europe later that year. The game features a unique fighting system where players aim to knock opponents off the stage rather than depleting a health bar. It includes 12 playable characters from popular Nintendo franchises, such as Mario, Link, and Pikachu, with eight available initially and four unlockable. Players can engage in single-player modes, including Classic Mode and minigames, or multiplayer battles for up to four players.

The game was originally conceived as *Dragon King: The Fighting Game* before Sakurai incorporated Nintendo characters without prior approval. Its development emphasized accessibility and originality, with simplified controls and dynamic stages. *Super Smash Bros.* received praise for its multiplayer mode, music, and innovative gameplay, though its single-player mode faced criticism. It was a commercial success, selling nearly 5 million copies worldwide by 2008, and became a Nintendo 64 Player's Choice title. The game launched the *Super Smash Bros.* series, which has since become one of Nintendo's most iconic franchises.

page_title: Monarchs, Rulers, Dynasties and Kingdoms of the World
trgt: Monarchs, Rulers, Dynasties and Kingdoms of the World: An Encyclopaedic Guide to More Than 13,000 Rulers and 1,000 Dynasties from 3000 BC to the 20th Century is a non-fiction work by R.F. Tapsell, published in 1983. It is a comprehensive record of kings, queens, sultans, and emperors all in a single volume. It includes many dynasties that are rarely described except in advanced studies of individual countries or regions. The relationship of each monarch to his or her predecessor is provided which enables the reader to construct a rudimentary family tree.
mgt: *Monarchs, Rulers, Dynasties and Kingdoms of the World* is a reference book that provides an extensive guide to the rulers and dynasties of various regions throughout history. It includes a bibliography, a 150-page "Guide to Dynasties and States" with brief descriptions, and tools to locate historical realms on modern maps. The book also explores royal family ties and offers historical context for countries and provinces. While praised for its comprehensiveness, it has faced minor criticism for the complexity of navigating its content to find specific information.

trgt:
Color correction is a process used in stage lighting, photography, television, cinematography, and other disciplines, which uses color gels, or filters, to alter the overall color of the light. Typically the light color is measured on a scale known as color temperature, as well as along a green–magenta axis orthogonal to the color temperature axis.

Without color correction gels, a scene may have a mix of various colors. Applying color correction gels in front of light sources can alter the color of the various light sources to match. Mixed lighting can produce an undesirable aesthetic when displayed on a television or in a theatre.

Conversely, gels may also be used to make a scene appear more natural by simulating the mix of color temperatures that occur naturally. This application is useful, especially where motivated lighting (lending the impression that it is diegetic) is the goal. Color gels may also be used to tint lights for artistic effect.
mgt:
Color correction is the process of adjusting and balancing colors in visual media to ensure they appear natural and realistic, as perceived by the human eye. It is widely used in photography, cinematography, television, stage lighting, and digital editing. The process involves correcting color issues, balancing whites and blacks, and ensuring overall color consistency. 

Key tools for color correction include color gels, such as color temperature blue (CTB) and color temperature orange (CTO), which adjust light sources to match desired color temperatures. Gels like minus green and plus green are used to correct the green cast of fluorescent lights. In digital systems, white balance can be adjusted electronically, though physical filters are often used in film cameras to optimize image quality.

In software like Photoshop, color correction tools allow users to edit, match, or change colors in images. The process is essential for achieving accurate and visually appealing results in various visual media disciplines.

trgt: SBI Cards & Payment Services Ltd., previously known as SBI Cards & Payment Services Private Limited, is a pure play credit card company and payment solutions provider in India. SBI Card was launched in October 1998 by State Bank of India, India's largest bank, and GE Capital. In December 2017, State Bank of India and The Carlyle Group acquired GE Capital's stake in the company. SBI Card is headquartered in Gurugram, Haryana/Delhi NCR and have branches in over 100 cities across India. The company is the only publicly listed pure-play credit card issuer in India.
mgt: SBI Card, officially known as SBI Cards & Payment Services Limited, is a non-banking financial company headquartered in Gurugram, India. Founded in October 1998, it specializes in credit card issuance and payment services across India. Initially a joint venture between the State Bank of India (SBI) and GE Capital, SBI Card became a publicly listed company after GE Capital exited the partnership in 2017, with SBI increasing its stake to 74% and The Carlyle Group acquiring 26%. In 2019, the company introduced a new brand identity to appeal to millennial consumers.

trgt:
Sthanu Ravi Varma (early Malayalam and Tamil: Ko Tanu Iravi), known as the Kulasekhara, was the Chera Perumal ruler of Kerala in southern India from 844/45 to 870/71 AD. He is the earliest Chera Perumal ruler known to scholars.

The Chera Perumal relations with the Chola dynasty were inaugurated during the reign of Sthanu Ravi. The famous Quilon Syrian Christian copper plates are dated in the fifth regnal year of king Sthanu Ravi. Two more inscriptions dated in the regnal years of Sthanu Ravi can be found at Irinjalakuda Kudalmanikyam Temple, and at Thiruvatruvay, Thiruvalla. Koyil Adhikarikal (the Royal Prince) during the time of Sthanu Ravi was his son-in-law (husband of his daughter) Vijayaraga. Sthanu Ravi had a son born to him in or about 870 AD. He was succeeded by Rama Rajasekhara (870/71–c. 883/84).

Sthanu Ravi probably abdicated the throne toward the end of his reign and became a Vaishnavite alvar saint known as Kulasekhara Alvar (seventh of the twelve mystic alvars). He is also identified with playwright Chera king Kulasekhara Varma.
mgt:
Sthanu Ravi Varma, also known by his regnal title Kulasekhara, was a ruler of the Chera Perumal kingdom in present-day Kerala, India, who reigned from 844/45 to 870/71 AD. His rule marked a period of significant cultural and political activity, with his authority centered around the capital Makotai (modern-day Kodungallur). While his kingship was largely ceremonial, local chieftains and Nambudiri-Brahmins wielded substantial political and religious power.

Sthanu Ravi is noted for his association with the Chola king Rajakesari Varma in military campaigns and for fostering advancements in astronomy. His court hosted the astronomer Sankara Narayana, who authored the *Laghu Bhaskariya Vyakha*, a commentary on Bhaskara I's works. The era also saw the issuance of the Quilon Syrian Christian copper plates, which recorded land grants to a Christian church in Kollam.

Sthanu Ravi's reign is referenced in literary works and inscriptions, and his contributions to culture, religion, and science underscore his significance in Kerala's medieval history.

trgt:
José Antonio Páez Herrera (IPA-es; 13 June 1790 – 6 May 1873) was a Venezuelan leader who fought against the Spanish Crown for Simón Bolívar during the Venezuelan War of Independence. He later led Venezuela's independence from Gran Colombia.

He dominated the country's politics for most of the next two decades once the country had achieved independence from Gran Colombia, serving either as president of Venezuela (1830–1835; 1839–1843; 1861–1863) or as the power behind puppet presidents. He is considered a prime example of a 19th-century South American caudillo, and imbued the country with a legacy of authoritarian rule that lasted, with few exceptions, until 1958. He lived in Buenos Aires and New York City during his years in exile and died in the latter in 1873.
mgt: José Antonio Páez (June 13, 1790 – May 6, 1873) was a Venezuelan military leader and politician who played a pivotal role in the country's independence from Spain and its early republican history. Known as "El Centauro de los Llanos" (The Centaur of the Plains), Páez was a key commander in the Venezuelan War of Independence, leading decisive victories such as the Battle of Las Queseras del Medio and the Battle of Carabobo, which secured Venezuela's liberation. He later became the first president of Venezuela after its separation from Gran Colombia in 1830, serving three non-consecutive terms (1830–1835, 1839–1843, and 1861–1863). A controversial figure, Páez was both celebrated for his leadership and criticized for his role as a military caudillo. He died in exile in New York City.

trgt: CNN Pipeline was an English language video news service providing both live and on-demand video to subscribers' computers via broadband Internet connections. It was part of the CNN group of news services. The service was subscription-based, and did not contain advertising like other CNN stations. The bandwidth, storage and streaming servers was provided by AOL, which is also owned by Time Warner. Each of the feeds broadcast in the 16:9 aspect ratio, however the resolution of the broadcast prohibited it from being considered a high definition channel, and in many cases not even SDTV.
mgt: CNN Pipeline was an internet streaming television service launched by CNN on December 5, 2005, offering live video feeds, archived content, and news programming through a subscription model. It featured four simultaneous video streams, known as "pipes," showcasing live news events, interviews, and special programming. Anchored by Veronica De La Cruz, Nicole Lapin, Melissa Long, and Richard Lui, the service operated from CNN's virtual studio. On June 27, 2007, CNN discontinued Pipeline, transitioning its content to a free, ad-supported format on CNN.com. In 2014, CNNgo was introduced as its successor, incorporating similar features.

trgt:
Moses Rischin (1925-2020) was an American historian, author, lecturer, editor, and emeritus professor of history at San Francisco State University. He coined the phrase new Mormon history in a 1969 article of the same name.

Rischin is considered an authority on American ethnic and immigration history and a pioneer in the field of American Jewish history. Historian Selma Berrol, however, has challenged the minimal treatment Rischin has given to the tensions between earlier German Jews and later Russian Jews in America.
mgt: Moses Rischin was an American historian and professor, known for his contributions to American Jewish history. Born and raised in Brooklyn, New York, he earned his Ph.D. from Harvard University in 1957 and became a professor at San Francisco State University in 1964. Rischin directed the Western Jewish History Center at the Judah L. Magnes Museum from its founding in 1967. He also served on the boards of prominent historical organizations and was honored with a collection of essays published in 1996.

trgt:
Ebirah, Horror of the Deep (ゴジラ・エビラ・モスラ 南海の大決闘, Gojira, Ebira, Mosura Nankai no Daikettō, lit. Godzilla, Ebirah, Mothra: Big Duel in the South Seas) is a 1966 Japanese kaiju film directed by Jun Fukuda and produced and distributed by Toho Co., Ltd. The film stars Akira Takarada, Kumi Mizuno, Akihiko Hirata and Eisei Amamoto, and features the fictional monster characters Godzilla, Mothra, and Ebirah. It is the seventh film in the Godzilla franchise, and features special effects by Sadamasa Arikawa, under the supervision of Eiji Tsuburaya. In the film, Godzilla and Ebirah are portrayed by Haruo Nakajima and Hiroshi Sekita, respectively.

During its development, Ebirah, Horror of the Deep was intended to feature King Kong, but the character was replaced by Godzilla. The film was released to theaters in Japan on December 17, 1966, and was released directly to television in the United States in 1968 under the title Godzilla versus the Sea Monster.
mgt:
*Ebirah, Horror of the Deep* (released in the United States as *Godzilla versus the Sea Monster*) is a 1966 Japanese kaiju film directed by Jun Fukuda and produced by Toho Co., Ltd. The film stars Akira Takarada, Kumi Mizuno, and Chotaro Togin, with special effects directed by Sadamasa Arikawa under the supervision of Eiji Tsuburaya. It is the seventh installment in the *Godzilla* franchise and follows a group of castaways who discover a terrorist organization, the Red Bamboo, enslaving natives on Letchi Island while using a giant lobster-like kaiju, Ebirah, to guard their operations. The group awakens Godzilla to battle Ebirah and ultimately thwart the Red Bamboo's plans.

Originally conceived as a King Kong film, the project was reworked to feature Godzilla after creative differences with Rankin/Bass. Released in Japan on December 17, 1966, the film was later distributed in the U.S. and gained cult status, including a feature on *Mystery Science Theater 3000*.

trgt:
Charles Van Dell Johnson (August 25, 1916 – December 12, 2008) was an American film, television, theatre and radio actor. He was a major star at Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer during and after World War II.

Johnson was described as the embodiment of the "boy-next-door wholesomeness" which made him a popular Hollywood star in the 1940s and 1950s, playing "the red-haired, freckle-faced soldier, sailor, or bomber pilot who used to live down the street" in MGM films during the war years, with such films as Thirty Seconds Over Tokyo, A Guy Named Joe, and The Human Comedy. He made occasional World War II films through the end of the 1960s, and he played a military officer in one of his final feature films in 1992. At the time of his death in 2008, he was one of the last surviving matinee idols of Hollywood's "golden age".
mgt:
Van Johnson (August 25, 1916 – December 12, 2008) was an American actor known for his boyish charm and all-American image, which made him a popular star during Hollywood's Golden Age. Born in Newport, Rhode Island, he began his career in theater before transitioning to film, where he gained recognition at Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer (MGM). Johnson's breakout role came in *A Guy Named Joe* (1943), and he became a leading man in musicals, comedies, and war dramas, including *Thirty Seconds Over Tokyo* (1944) and *The Caine Mutiny* (1954). 

Despite a serious car accident early in his career, Johnson's injuries exempted him from World War II service, allowing him to rise to stardom during a time when many actors were enlisted. He later appeared in television and stage productions, including *The Music Man* and *La Cage aux Folles*. Johnson died in 2008 at age 92, leaving behind a legacy as a beloved Hollywood icon.

trgt: Democratic centralism is a practice in which political decisions reached by voting processes are binding upon all members of the political party. It is mainly associated with Leninism, wherein the party's political vanguard of professional revolutionaries practised democratic centralism to elect leaders and officers, determine policy through free discussion, and decisively realise it through united action. Democratic centralism has also been practised by social democratic and democratic socialist parties as well. Scholars have disputed whether democratic centralism was implemented in practice in the Soviet Union and China, pointing to violent power struggles, backhanded political maneuvering, historical antagonisms and the politics of personal prestige in those regimes.
mgt: Democratic centralism is a Leninist organizational principle that combines internal democratic debate with strict unity in action. Originating from Vladimir Lenin's 1902 work *What Is to Be Done?*, it was designed to structure revolutionary parties, emphasizing "freedom of discussion, unity of action." The principle became central to the Bolshevik Party, contributing to its split with the Mensheviks in 1903. After the Bolsheviks consolidated power in the Soviet Union, democratic centralism evolved into a mechanism for decision-making and governance, often limiting dissent and centralizing authority. It influenced governance in socialist states like the Soviet Union, China, and Vietnam, shaping their political and legislative systems.

trgt:
Kanyaka is an Indian magazine in Malayalam primarily addressing women's issues. It is a fortnightly magazine published by the Mangalam Publications India Pvt. Ltd., Kottayam. Claramma Varghese is Chief Editor and Toshma Biju Varghese is the Managing Editor. Sajil Sreedhar is the Editor-In-Chief. The headquarters is in Mumbai.

The magazine contains features on current affairs, stories, poems, cartoons, interviews of celebrities and uncelebrated common personalities. The magazine is dedicated to the uplifting women in Indian society as well as in the societies of other countries.
mgt: *Kanyaka* is a leading Malayalam-language women's lifestyle magazine, known for its wide-ranging content, including cooking recipes, beauty and health tips, relationship advice, and a free family guide in every issue. Established over 33 years ago, it is the third-largest circulated women's fortnightly magazine in its segment. *Kanyaka* has been the official media partner for beauty pageants like Miss South India and Miss Asia, and has organized events such as the Kitchen Queen Cookery contest and a film award celebrating youth in Malayalam cinema.

trgt:
Jeff Danielson (born August 6, 1970) is a former Democratic Iowa State Senator. Having served the 30th Senate District between 2013 and 2019, he had also previously served the 10th Senate District up until redistricting, between 2005 and 2013.

Danielson served on following committees: Appropriations (Vice Chair), Economic Growth, State Government (Chair), Transportation, Veterans Affairs (Vice Chair), and the Administration and Regulation Appropriations Subcommittee (Vice Chair). Permanent statutory committees he serves on include: Public Retirement Systems and State Government Efficiency Review. Danielson additionally serves on the Iowa Capital Investment Board.

His prior public service experience includes experience as the Commissioner of the Iowa Department of Transportation and the Waterloo Planning, Programming, and Zoning Commission. He is a U.S. Navy veteran and serves the community of Cedar Falls as a professional firefighter.
mgt:
Jeff Danielson (born August 6, 1970) is an American politician and former firefighter who served as a Democratic member of the Iowa Senate from 2005 to 2019, representing District 10 and later District 30 following redistricting. During his tenure, he served as President Pro Tempore of the Iowa Senate from 2006 to 2012 and was a strong advocate for veterans' issues as Vice-Chair of the Veterans Affairs Committee. Danielson also explored legislation on online poker regulation in Iowa. 

A native of Waterloo, Iowa, he holds degrees from Hawkeye Community College and the University of Northern Iowa. In early 2019, Danielson resigned from the Senate, citing family and financial considerations, and later became a lobbyist for the American Wind Energy Association. A special election was held to fill his seat.

trgt: Paul Thomson (June 29, 1916 – May 31, 2008) was an American exotic fruit enthusiast, self-taught horticulturist and botanist, fruit farmer, and the co-founder of the California Rare Fruit Growers Association, a group of amateur horticulturists which now has more than 3,000 members in approximately 35 countries. Thomson is credited with helping to expand the farming of exotic fruits in California – everything from cherimoyas to longans to pitahayas. Thomson also wrote one of the few literary treatments of the genus Dudleya, and was an avid collector of the plants.
mgt: Paul H. Thomson (June 29, 1916 – May 31, 2008) was an American botanist, organic farmer, and co-founder of the California Rare Fruit Growers Association (CRFG). Known for his pioneering work with tropical and exotic fruits, Thomson cultivated over 90 fruit varieties in Southern California, including the first successful Mammee apple crop in the state. He also contributed to the study of the succulent genus *Dudleya*, publishing the *Dudleya and Hassenthaus Handbook* in 1993. Thomson's efforts helped popularize rare fruits and expand their availability in California markets.

trgt:
Password synchronization is a process, usually supported by software such as password managers, through which a user maintains a single password across multiple IT systems.

Provided that all the systems enforce mutually-compatible password standards (e.g. concerning minimum and maximum password length, supported characters, etc.), the user can choose a new password at any time and deploy the same password on his or her own login accounts across multiple, linked systems.

Where different systems have mutually incompatible standards regarding what can be stored in a password field, the user may be forced to choose more than one (but still fewer than the number of systems) passwords. This may happen, for example, where the maximum password length on one system is shorter than the minimum length in another, or where one system requires use of a punctuation mark but another forbids it.

Password synchronization is a function of certain identity management systems and it is considered easier to implement than enterprise single sign-on (SSO), as there is normally no client software deployment or need for active user enrollment.
mgt:
Password synchronization is a process that enables users to maintain the same password across multiple systems, simplifying access management and reducing the likelihood of forgotten passwords. Commonly used in enterprise networks, it helps minimize IT Help Desk calls and enhances security by discouraging users from writing down passwords. With fewer passwords to remember, users can be encouraged to create stronger, more secure passwords through training and automated policy enforcement.

However, password synchronization poses security risks. If the synchronized password is compromised—through guessing, disclosure, cryptanalysis, or social engineering—all systems sharing that password become vulnerable. This risk is similar to single sign-on and password vault solutions, where compromising the master password can expose all associated systems. 

Password synchronization can be triggered by a password change on any synchronized system or through a central interface, depending on the software used. Some systems may transfer password hashes between platforms if the hashing algorithm is compatible, but most require access to plaintext passwords.

trgt: ¡Que viva la lucha! is a 2007 film directed and produced by Gustavo Vazquez about Lucha libre in Tijuana, Mexico, considered to be a form of extreme lucha libre. The film follows Extreme Tiger, an up-and-coming professional luchador from Tijuana, on a journey that places him in a mask vs. hair match against Joe Líder. In addition, he follows other new talent, Angel Negro Jr. and Pancho Cachondo. In the process, the film also interviews other luchadores, promoters, commissioners, families, and fans who discuss the cultural significance of lucha libre as sport, ritual, and spectacle. The film includes music by Carne Cruda, Marziano, White Pine, and Nortec Collective. ¡Que viva la lucha! premiered at the 30th annual Mill Valley Film Festival on Saturday, Oct. 13, 2007, and has won two awards; at the Latino Film Festival and at the San Francisco International Film Festival.
mgt: *¡Que viva la lucha!* is a 2007 Mexican documentary film directed and produced by Gustavo Vazquez. The 54-minute film explores the vibrant culture of lucha libre in Tijuana, focusing on its cultural significance, the dual lives of its wrestlers, and the passionate fanbase. It follows the stories of luchadores such as Extreme Tiger, a técnico who dreams of international success, and rudos like Angel Negro Jr. and Pancho Cachondo, who embody the archetypal villains of the sport. The documentary delves into the symbolism of the wrestling mask, linking it to Mexican cultural and historical traditions, and highlights the diverse participants in lucha libre, including women, gay wrestlers, and referees. Through interviews and dynamic footage, the film captures the energy of the wrestling arena and the deep connection between the wrestlers and their fans, showcasing lucha libre as both a sport and a cultural phenomenon.

trgt:
Lucyan David Mech (/miːtʃ/; born January 18, 1937), also known as Dave Mech, is an American biologist specializing in the study of wolves. He is a senior research scientist for the U.S. Geological Survey and an adjunct professor at the University of Minnesota. He has researched wolves since 1958 in locations including northern Minnesota, Isle Royale, Alaska, Yellowstone National Park, Ellesmere Island, and Italy.

Mech is the founder of the International Wolf Center and is the vice-chair of its board of directors. The project to create the facility, which he started in 1985, was a natural outgrowth of his wolf research as well as his ambition to educate people about the nature of wolves that they may come to respect the creature through understanding.

He has published eleven books about wolves and other wildlife, including The Wolf: The Ecology and Behavior of an Endangered Species (1970, University of Minnesota Press) and Wolves: Behavior, Ecology, and Conservation which he co-edited with Luigi Boitani (2003, University of Chicago Press). Both books remain in print as of 2017. The 1997 book The Arctic Wolf: Ten Years with the Pack received an Honorable Mention by the National Outdoor Book Award (Nature and the Environment category). His latest book with Doug Smith and Dan MacNulty is Wolves on the Hunt: the Behavior of Wolves Hunting Wild Prey.
mgt:
L. David Mech (born January 18, 1937) is an American biologist and ecologist renowned for his pioneering research on wolf behavior and ecology. He earned a B.S. in conservation from Cornell University in 1958 and a Ph.D. in wildlife ecology from Purdue University in 1962. Mech began his career studying wolves on Isle Royale in Lake Superior, which led to his first book, *The Wolves of Isle Royale* (1966). He later conducted groundbreaking research on wolves in Minnesota's Superior National Forest and spent 25 summers observing wolves on Ellesmere Island, where he studied their behavior in a unique environment free from human interference.

Mech has authored or co-authored 11 books and over 380 scientific papers, including the influential works *The Wolf: The Ecology and Behavior of an Endangered Species* (1970) and *Wolves: Behavior, Ecology, and Conservation* (2003). He has also collaborated on National Geographic articles and films. A proponent of sustainable wolf management, Mech supports state-level decision-making on wolf conservation. His work has earned him recognition as a leading authority on wolves, and he continues to contribute to the field through research and publications.

trgt:
James McCulloch (4 June 1953 – 27 September 1979) was a Scottish musician best known for playing lead guitar and bass, as a member of Paul McCartney's band Wings from 1974 to 1977. McCulloch was a member of the Glasgow psychedelic band One in a Million (formerly known as the Jaygars), Thunderclap Newman, and Stone the Crows.

McCulloch also made appearances on many albums, including John Entwistle's Whistle Rymes in 1972, as lead guitarist playing alongside Peter Frampton on "Apron Strings" and "I Feel Better". McCulloch also played guitar on Roger Daltrey's album One of the Boys which was released in 1977. McCulloch was a friend of the Who and a member of the band Thunderclap Newman, which was created and produced by his mentor Pete Townshend. At age 11, he was also a protégé of the Shadows' Hank Marvin. His brother is drummer Jack McCulloch.
mgt:
Jimmy McCulloch (4 June 1953 – 27 September 1979) was a Scottish musician and songwriter, best known as the lead guitarist for Paul McCartney's band Wings from 1974 to 1977. A prodigious talent, McCulloch began playing guitar at age 11 and gained early recognition with the psychedelic rock band One in a Million and later with Thunderclap Newman, whose 1969 hit "Something in the Air" topped the UK charts. He also played with Stone the Crows, John Mayall's Bluesbreakers, and the reformed Small Faces, among others.

During his time with Wings, McCulloch contributed notable tracks such as "Medicine Jar" and "Wino Junko." He later formed the band White Line and collaborated with various artists. Known for his melodic, blues-infused guitar style, McCulloch tragically died of heart failure due to morphine and alcohol poisoning at age 26. His legacy includes contributions to rock and blues music across multiple bands and projects.

trgt:
The Oneida Community Mansion House is a historic house and museum that was once the home of the Oneida Community, a religiously-based socialist Utopian group led by John Humphrey Noyes. Noyes and his followers moved to the site in Oneida from Putney, Vermont in 1848. The Community lived in the Mansion House communally until 1880, when they dissolved into a joint-stock company.

The Mansion House has been continually occupied as a residence since its construction in 1862. In the 20th century the Mansion House became a National Historic Landmark. It is currently overseen by a non-profit organization chartered in 1987 by New York State Board of Regents. It also includes residential apartments as well as guest rooms for overnight lodging.
mgt: The Oneida Community Mansion House, located in Oneida, New York, is a historic site that served as the communal residence of the utopian Oneida Community, a 19th-century religious group founded by John Humphrey Noyes. Built in 1848 and expanded in subsequent decades, the Mansion House reflects the Community's values of shared living, complex marriage, and communal property. Its Italianate and Second Empire architectural styles include features like the Family Hall, a central gathering space, and the South Wing, which housed children born from the Community's eugenics experiment, known as "stirpiculture." After the Community disbanded in 1880, the property transitioned to private use and was later acquired by the non-profit Oneida Community Mansion House in 1987.

trgt: Godzilla vs. Kong (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack) is the soundtrack to the 2021 American monster film Godzilla vs. Kong. A sequel to Kong: Skull Island (2017) and Godzilla: King of the Monsters (2019), it is the fourth film in Legendary's MonsterVerse, the 36th film in the Godzilla franchise, the 12th film in the King Kong franchise, and the fourth Godzilla film to be completely produced by a Hollywood studio. Directed by Adam Wingard, the film features musical score written and composed by Tom Holkenborg (Junkie XL), the fourth film composer to work on the MonsterVerse franchise. He was announced as a part of the film in June 2020, before a planned meeting with Wingard, two years (when the film was under pre-production), expressing his interest on working on the score due to his personal love for the Godzilla film series during his teen years. The recording of the score took place for nearly two years, and was bundled into a soundtrack album, released by WaterTower Music in digital and physical formats on March 31, 2021, coinciding with the film's United States theatrical release.
mgt:
*Godzilla vs. Kong (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack)* is the film score for the 2021 monster film *Godzilla vs. Kong*, composed by Tom Holkenborg and released on March 31, 2021, by WaterTower Music. Holkenborg, a fan of Godzilla, began developing the score in 2018, aiming to create original themes that respected the legacy of the titular monsters while distinguishing them from previous works. The score features a hyper-modern style, with Godzilla's theme emphasizing power through lower brass and percussion, while Kong's theme takes an organic, human-like approach interwoven with electronic elements.

The soundtrack includes tracks such as "Pensacola, Florida (Godzilla Theme)" and "Skull Island (Kong Theme)," released ahead of the album. Additional music used in the film but not included in the soundtrack features songs by artists like Bobby Vinton, Elvis Presley, and Judas Priest. For the Japanese release, the band Man with a Mission contributed the track "Into the Deep."

Critical reception was mixed, with praise for the new themes but criticism for the lack of Akira Ifukube's iconic Godzilla motif and the overuse of electronic elements.

trgt:
WTRF-TV (channel 7) is a television station licensed to Wheeling, West Virginia, United States, serving the Wheeling, West Virginia–Steubenville, Ohio market as an affiliate of CBS, MyNetworkTV, and ABC. The station is owned by Nexstar Media Group and maintains studios on 16th Street in downtown Wheeling; its transmitter is located in Bridgeport, Ohio.

WTRF-TV was the first station to specifically serve Wheeling and Steubenville. It was originally an NBC affiliate before switching to CBS in January 1980. In the 2000s, it expanded to add two new subchannel services.
mgt: WTRF-TV is a television station licensed to Wheeling, West Virginia, serving the Wheeling–Steubenville market. It broadcasts on virtual and VHF digital channel 7 and is owned by Nexstar Media Group. Established on October 24, 1953, WTRF-TV originally affiliated with NBC before switching to CBS in 1980. The station also operates subchannels carrying MyNetworkTV, ABC, and Ion Mystery programming. Over its history, WTRF-TV has undergone multiple ownership changes and played a significant role in local broadcasting, including producing newscasts and regional programs. It is headquartered in Wheeling, West Virginia.

trgt: What Wild Ecstasy: The Rise and Fall of the Sexual Revolution is a 1997 book about the sexual revolution by John Heidenry. The book received mixed reviews. It was described as interesting and Heidenry was complimented for his discussions of figures such as Bob Guccione, Hugh Hefner, Larry Flynt, and Reuben Sturman. However, he was criticized for his research methods. He was accused of plagiarism because of the use he made of material by other writers, receiving criticism in particular from the journalist Philip Nobile. Critics also wrote that he focused disproportionately on pornography and gave insufficient attention to feminism and women's issues.
mgt: *What Wild Ecstasy: The Rise and Fall of the Sexual Revolution* is a 1997 book by John Heidenry that explores the history and impact of the sexual revolution, with a focus on the United States. The book examines key figures such as Alfred Kinsey, William Masters, Virginia E. Johnson, Hugh Hefner, and Larry Flynt, as well as topics like pornography, feminism, homosexuality, and swinging. It received mixed reviews, with praise for its engaging writing and balanced perspective but criticism for its reliance on secondary sources and oversimplified analysis. The book also faced allegations of plagiarism, which Heidenry and the publisher denied.

trgt: The Philippine Center is an agency of the Philippine Government in New York City and San Francisco in the United States. The New York City structure houses the Philippine Mission to the United Nations, the Philippine Consulate General, and the overseas offices of the Department of Trade and Industry and the Department of Tourism. The Philippine Center Management (PCMB) manages the building and its properties. It is committed to "nurture, promote, and propagate Philippine culture, encourage foreign tourists to visit the Philippines, expand foreign markets of Philippine products, and enhance the image of the Philippines."
mgt: The Philippine Center is a cultural and governmental hub established by the Philippine government to represent its presence abroad. The first center was inaugurated in New York City on May 10, 1973, under Presidential Decree No. 188 by President Ferdinand Marcos. Located at 556 Fifth Avenue, the building was purchased from the Knights of Columbus and underwent extensive renovations, becoming notable for its windowless façade. Additional centers were later established in San Francisco and Sydney. The New York center houses Philippine government offices, cultural exhibits, and an art collection featuring works by prominent Filipino artists.

trgt:
KNBN (channel 21) is a television station in Rapid City, South Dakota, United States, affiliated with NBC and MyNetworkTV. Owned by Jim Simpson's Rapid Broadcasting, the station maintains studios on South Plaza Drive in Rapid City, and its transmitter is located on Cowboy Hill west of downtown.

KNBN can also be seen on low-power digital translators KWBH-LD (channel 27) in Rapid City; K22AD-D (channel 22) in Gillette, Wyoming; and K35MW-D (channel 35) in Lead, South Dakota (also serving the Black Hills region).
mgt: KNBN (channel 21), branded as NewsCenter 1, is a television station in Rapid City, South Dakota, affiliated with NBC and MyNetworkTV. Owned by Rapid Broadcasting Company, it began broadcasting on May 14, 2000, after securing the NBC affiliation for the area. KNBN produces 16 hours of local newscasts weekly and operates several subchannels. The station transitioned to digital broadcasting in 2009. Its history includes the acquisition of a low-power station in 1996 and the launch of its full news service in 1997.

trgt:
Newfoundland French or Newfoundland Peninsular French (Lang-fr), refers to the French spoken on the Port au Port Peninsula (part of the so-called “French Shore”) of Newfoundland. The francophones of the region can trace their origins to Continental French fishermen who settled in the late 1800s and early 1900s, rather than the Québécois. Some Acadians of the Maritimes also settled in the area. For this reason, Newfoundland French is most closely related to the Norman and Breton French of nearby St-Pierre-et-Miquelon. Today, heavy contact with Acadian French—and especially widespread bilingualism with Newfoundland English—have taken their toll, and the community is in decline.

The degree to which lexical features of Newfoundland French constitute a distinct dialect is not presently known. It is uncertain how many speakers survive; the dialect could be moribund.
