trgt: The 1945 College Football All-America team is composed of college football players who were selected as All-Americans by various organizations and writers that chose College Football All-America Teams in 1945. The nine selectors recognized by the NCAA as "official" for the 1945 season are (1) Collier's Weekly, as selected by Grantland Rice, (2) the Associated Press, (3) the United Press, (4) the All-America Board, (5) the American Football Coaches Association (AFCA), (6) the Football Writers Association of America (FWAA), (7) the International News Service (INS), (8) Look magazine, (9) the Newspaper Enterprise Association (NEA) and (10) the Sporting News.
mgt: The 1945 College Football All-America Team is composed of standout college football players recognized as All-Americans for their exceptional performance during the 1945 season. The NCAA officially recognizes 10 All-American teams for determining consensus selections, highlighting the top players of the year. Additionally, during this era, African-American players were largely excluded from major college football programs and All-America teams. To address this, *The Pittsburgh Courier* selected its own All-America team, honoring players from historically black colleges and universities (HBCUs). These selections provided recognition for talented athletes who were otherwise overlooked by mainstream selectors.

trgt: Dardanella was a touring theatre company from the Dutch East Indies (modern-day Indonesia) established by Willy A. Piedro in 1926. Arising from a background of musical theatre, the troupe focused on realistic stories, both adaptations of foreign works and original stage plays about life in the Indies. Starring Dewi Dja' and Tan Tjeng Bok, the troupe performed original works by Piedro and Andjar Asmara. Popular both in the Indies and abroad, Dardanella dissolved during an international tour after 1936. Several of its members later went into film.
mgt: Dardanella was a prominent theatre company in the Dutch East Indies (modern-day Indonesia) during the 1920s and 1930s, known for its adoption of European theatrical styles, including spoken dialogue and original works, in contrast to the music-heavy bangsawan and komedi stambul traditions. Founded in 1926 by Willy A. Piedro, the troupe featured notable actors such as Devi Dja, Tan Tjeng Bok, and Andjar Asmara, who later became a key playwright. Dardanella's performances, often in Malay, were praised for their quality and cultural significance, contributing to the development of Indonesian theatre and film.

trgt:
Marcq-en-Barœul (IPA-fr; Lang-pcd; older Lang-nl) is a commune in the Nord department in the Hauts-de-France region in northern France.

It is a suburb of the city of Lille, and is immediately adjacent to it on the northeast. It is the sixth-largest component of the Métropole Européenne de Lille. Marcq-en-Barœul is known as the most affluent suburb city of Lille, it is part of the 'Triangle d'or' (Golden triangle) of Lille. It features expensive real estate, especially alongside the Grand Boulevard and Hippodrome des Flandres racecourse. The city is also home to one of the campuses of the elite international private collège and lycée active bilingue Jeannine-Manuel.
mgt: Marcq-en-Barœul is a commune in the Nord department in northern France, located within the Hauts-de-France region and part of the Métropole Européenne de Lille. Covering an area of 14.04 km², it had a population of 39,943 in 2022. The town is governed by Mayor Bernard Gérard (2020–2026) and is known for its economic contributions, including being the headquarters of Groupe Holder, which owns Ladurée and Paul. Marcq-en-Barœul features a racecourse, two golf courses, and a prominent rugby club, Olympique Marcquois Rugby, established in 1971. The club is affiliated with Stade Toulousain and supports over 600 players and 350 youth participants.

trgt:
Benjamin Franklin Grauer (June 2, 1908 – May 31, 1977) was a US radio and TV personality, following a career during the 1920s as a child actor in films and on Broadway. He began his career as a child in David Warfield's production of The Return of Peter Grimm. Among his early credits were roles in films directed by D.W. Griffith.

Grauer was born in Staten Island, New York. After graduating from Townsend Harris High School, he received his B.A. from the City College of New York in 1930. Grauer started in radio as an actor but soon became part of the broadcasting staff at the National Broadcasting Company. He was one of the four narrators, along with Burgess Meredith, of NBC's public affairs series The Big Story, which focused on courageous journalists.

In 1954, he married interior designer Melanie Kahane.
mgt:
Ben Grauer (June 2, 1908 – May 31, 1977) was an American broadcaster whose career spanned over 40 years in radio and television. He began working for NBC in 1930 and became a prominent commentator and announcer, known for his work with the NBC Symphony Orchestra under Arturo Toscanini, who regarded him as a favorite announcer. Grauer also co-created the Peabody Award-winning radio series *Toscanini, the Man Behind the Legend*. 

Grauer covered major historical events, including the Olympic Games, presidential inaugurations, and the New Year's Eve celebrations in Times Square, which he hosted 34 times between 1939 and 1972. On television, he provided commentary for NBC's first broadcast of the 1939 New York World's Fair and hosted various programs, including *The Ben Grauer Show*. He died in 1977, leaving a legacy as one of broadcasting's most enduring voices.

trgt: Sphinx (or Sphynx), was a French Sylphe-class brig launched at Genoa in 1813. She was handed over to naval suppliers at Genoa on 17 April 1814 when nearly completed as part-payment for debts. The next day the British occupied Genoa. Sphinx appears to have become the Royal Navy brig Regent, and then a Customs and Excise cruizer. Regent was sold in 1824, and then appeared as the Colombian government vessel Victoria, which is no longer traceable in online resources after 1828.
mgt: The French brig *Sphinx* was a Sylphe-class brig launched in Genoa in November 1813 during the Napoleonic Wars. Captured by the British in April 1814 during the fall of Genoa, she was renamed *Sphynx* and later *Regent* under British service. Initially used by the Royal Navy and later as a revenue cruiser, she was sold in 1824. By 1826, she was acquired by the Colombian government and renamed *Victoria*, but her ultimate fate remains unknown.

trgt:
Stone Brothers Racing (SBR) was an Australian motor racing team that competed in the International V8 Supercars Championship between 1998 and 2012. The team was formed in 1998 when Ross and Jim Stone bought Alan Jones's shares in Alan Jones Racing and renamed it Stone Brothers Racing.

The team won the Bathurst 1000 in its debut year with Jason Bright and Steven Richards. It won the 2003 and 2004 championships with Marcos Ambrose and the 2005 championship with Russell Ingall. SBR won the Teams Championship (combined points of both cars) in 2003, 2004 and 2005, the 2004 V8 Supercars Series being a 1–2 finish.

The team was based in Yatala on the Gold Coast. Their nominated test track was initially Lakeside Raceway and later Queensland Raceway.

At the end of 2012, the team was sold to Erebus Motorsport.
mgt:
Stone Brothers Racing (SBR) was an Australian motor racing team that competed in the Supercars Championship from 1998 to 2012. Founded by New Zealand-born brothers Ross and Jim Stone, the team emerged from Alan Jones Racing, which they co-owned with 1980 Formula One World Champion Alan Jones before acquiring full ownership. SBR achieved significant success, including three drivers' championships with Marcos Ambrose (2003, 2004) and Russell Ingall (2005), as well as 23 round wins and 27 pole positions.

The team was known for its strong driver lineup, including Ambrose, Ingall, James Courtney, and Shane van Gisbergen, and for fostering young talent. SBR also played a key role in endurance racing, with notable performances at the Bathurst 1000 and Sandown 500. In 2012, the team was sold to Erebus Motorsport, marking the end of its Supercars era.

trgt:
The Republic of Benin was a short-lived unrecognized secessionist state in West Africa that existed for one day in 1967. It was established on 19 September 1967 during the Nigerian Civil War as a puppet state of Biafra, following its occupation of Nigeria's Mid-Western Region, and named after its capital, Benin City, with Albert Nwazu Okonkwo as its head of government.

The new state was an attempt by Biafra to prevent non-Igbo residents of the neighbouring Mid-Western Region from siding with Nigeria following regional ethnic tensions early in the war. The Republic of Benin was officially declared even as the Nigerian federal forces were reconquering the region and ended the following day as they entered Benin City.: 369 The occupation of the Mid-Western Region turned residents against the secessionist cause, and was used by the Nigerian government as justification to escalate the war against Biafra.
mgt: The Republic of Benin was a short-lived puppet state established by Biafra during the Nigerian Civil War. Proclaimed on September 19, 1967, in the Mid-Western Region of Nigeria with its capital in Benin City, the state was led by Albert Nwazu Okonkwo, an American-educated doctor. The declaration was a last-ditch effort by Biafra to gain support from the region's non-Igbo population and to create a buffer against advancing Nigerian federal forces. However, the Republic of Benin failed to garner significant local support, as ethnic tensions and dissatisfaction with Biafran occupation persisted. The same day the republic was declared, Nigerian federal troops recaptured Benin City, effectively ending the state's existence. The failed occupation and proclamation of the Republic of Benin further eroded support for Biafra among non-Igbos and contributed to the escalation of the Nigerian Civil War.

trgt:
SMS Leopard was a torpedo cruiser (Torpedoschiff) of the Austro-Hungarian Navy. She and her sister ship, SMS Panther, were part of a program to build up Austria-Hungary's fleet of torpedo craft in the 1880s. Both ships, the only members of the Panther class, were built in Britain at the Armstrong shipyard in Elswick. Leopard was laid down in January 1885, launched in September 1885, and completed in March 1886. She was armed with a battery of two 12 cm (4.7 in) guns and ten 47 mm (1.9 in) guns, along with four 356 mm (14.0 in) torpedo tubes.

Leopard spent much of her career in the main Austro-Hungarian fleet. During this period, she visited Spain for the 1888 Barcelona Universal Exposition, and took part in an international naval demonstration off Crete in 1897 in an attempt to limit the Greco-Turkish War. From 1900, Leopard made two major overseas deployments, including a tour of the Pacific Ocean in 1900–1901, and a stint in the East Asia squadron in 1907–1909. Upon returning to Austria-Hungary, the ship was modernized in 1910 and then decommissioned in May 1914, shortly before the start of World War I in July. The ship was immediately reactivated and stationed in Pola as a guard ship, where she remained for the duration of the conflict. After Austria-Hungary's defeat, Leopard was ceded to Britain as a war prize and sold to ship breakers in Italy in 1920.
mgt:
SMS *Leopard* was a Panther-class cruiser of the Austro-Hungarian Navy, launched in 1885 and completed in 1886. Designed as a small, fast cruiser, she measured 73.19 meters in length, displaced 1,557 long tons, and was armed with a mix of guns and torpedo tubes. Powered by compound steam engines, she reached speeds of 18.7 knots. *Leopard* served in various roles during her career, including fleet maneuvers, international diplomacy, and training cruises.

In 1888, *Leopard* participated in the Barcelona Universal Exposition as part of the largest Austro-Hungarian naval squadron to operate outside the Adriatic. She later joined the International Squadron during the 1897–1898 Cretan uprising, a multinational intervention in the conflict between Greece and the Ottoman Empire. From 1900 to 1901 and again from 1907 to 1909, *Leopard* undertook extended deployments to East Asia and the Pacific, visiting ports in Australia, China, Japan, and Polynesia.

Relegated to reserve status for much of her later career, *Leopard* was rearmed and briefly served as a torpedo training ship before being reactivated for coastal defense during World War I. Stationed as a harbor guard ship in Pola, she remained there until the war's end. Transferred to Britain as a war prize in 1920, *Leopard* was sold for scrap in Italy, marking the end of her service.

trgt:
Filip Mangen Ingebrigtsen (born 20 April 1993) is a Norwegian middle distance runner who represents Sandnes Idrettslag. In 2016, Ingebrigtsen became European Champion at 1500 meters during the European Championships in Amsterdam, and took bronze over the same distance at the World Championships in 2017 in London. He previously held the Norwegian 1500m record with the time 3:30.01, set at a Diamond League meet in Monaco on 20 July 2018. At the 2019 London Diamond League Ingebrigtsen finished second to Samuel Tefera in the mile. With a time of 3:49.60 Ingebrigtsen ran a national record and new personal best.

Ingebrigtsen was born in Sandnes. His older brother Henrik Ingebrigtsen and younger brother Jakob Ingebrigtsen (born 2000) are also middle distance runners and European champions at 1500 meters. They are trained by their dad Gjert Ingebrigtsen.
mgt:
Filip Ingebrigtsen (born April 20, 1993) is a Norwegian middle-distance runner specializing in the 1500 meters and the mile. A member of the renowned Ingebrigtsen family of athletes, he has achieved significant success on the international stage, including winning the gold medal in the 1500 meters at the 2016 European Athletics Championships and a bronze medal in the same event at the 2017 World Championships. He holds personal bests of 3:30.01 in the 1500 meters and 3:49.60 in the mile, the latter being a Norwegian record.

Ingebrigtsen has also competed in multiple European and World Championships, as well as the Olympics, though his career has been marked by both triumphs and controversies, including disqualifications and injuries. Despite setbacks, he remains a prominent figure in middle-distance running, known for his resilience and competitive spirit.

trgt:
Washing Machine is the ninth studio album by the American experimental rock band Sonic Youth, released on September 26, 1995 by DGC Records. It was recorded at Easley Studios in Memphis, Tennessee and produced by the band and John Siket, who also engineered the band's previous two albums. The album features more open-ended pieces than its predecessors and contains some of the band's longest songs, including the 20-minute ballad "The Diamond Sea", which is the lengthiest track to feature on any of Sonic Youth's studio albums.

Released shortly after the band concluded their stint headlining the 1995 Lollapalooza music festival, Washing Machine reached No. 58 on the US Billboard 200 chart and No. 39 on the UK Albums Chart. Two songs from the album, "The Diamond Sea" and "Little Trouble Girl", were released as singles. The album received generally positive reviews from music critics, who praised the band for exploring new challenges as well as the guitar playing of band members Thurston Moore and Lee Ranaldo. It was ranked No. 18 in The Village Voice's 1995 Pazz & Jop critics' poll.
mgt:
*Washing Machine* is the tenth studio album by American experimental rock band Sonic Youth, released on September 26, 1995, by DGC Records. Recorded at Easley Studios in Memphis, Tennessee, the album marked a departure from the claustrophobic sound of its predecessor, *Experimental Jet Set, Trash and No Star* (1994), featuring a more open-ended and experimental approach. It includes some of the band's longest tracks, such as the 19-minute "The Diamond Sea," and showcases a three-guitar lineup with Kim Gordon primarily playing guitar instead of bass. The album's lyrics explore themes of femininity, relationships, and self-expression, with contributions from Gordon, Thurston Moore, and Lee Ranaldo.

The album received generally positive reviews, with critics praising its adventurous sound and intricate guitar interplay. It reached No. 58 on the US Billboard 200 and No. 39 on the UK Albums Chart. Two singles, "The Diamond Sea" and "Little Trouble Girl," were released, with the latter featuring Kim Deal of the Breeders. *Washing Machine* is considered one of Sonic Youth's most ambitious and mature works, blending noise rock with melodic and experimental elements.

trgt:
"Iced Out Audemars" is a song by American rapper Pop Smoke, featuring rapper Dafi Woo, from the deluxe version of the former's posthumous debut studio album, Shoot for the Stars, Aim for the Moon (2020). The song was written by Pop Smoke, who is credited under his legal name of Bashar Jackson, alongside John Stevens, Khadafi Julio, and its lead producer Rico Beats. A drill track, the lyrics see Pop Smoke and Dafi Woo celebrating over the finer things and expressing their love for Dior, among other designer jewelry.

A remix of the song that features Lil Wayne was released on October 15, 2020. For the lyrics of the remix, Lil Wayne replaces Dafi Woo and pays homage to Pop Smoke after he died in a home invasion on February 19, 2020. The remix of "Iced Out Audemars" received positive reviews from music critics, with a number of them praising Lil Wayne's verse.
mgt:
"Iced Out Audemars" is a song by American rapper Pop Smoke featuring Dafi Woo, released on July 20, 2020, as part of the deluxe edition of Pop Smoke's posthumous debut studio album *Shoot for the Stars, Aim for the Moon*. Produced by Rico Beats with additional production by L3gion, the drill track showcases Pop Smoke and Dafi Woo celebrating luxury and success. A remix featuring American rapper Lil Wayne was released on October 15, 2020, following a snippet that surfaced online earlier that month.

The remix features Lil Wayne replacing Dafi Woo, delivering a tribute to Pop Smoke, who was killed in a home invasion earlier in 2020. Critics praised Lil Wayne's performance, highlighting his energetic delivery, rapid-fire flow, and heartfelt homage. The song received positive reviews for its vibrant production and lyrical content, with outlets like *Billboard* and *Complex* commending its impact on New York's rap scene and Pop Smoke's legacy.

trgt:
Give It Up is the second studio album by American musician Bonnie Raitt. Released in 1972 by Warner Bros. Records, Give It Up is an amalgamation of various genres, including folk, blues, R&B, and soft rock. Seven of the ten tracks on the album are covers, and the tracks are either soft sentimental ballads or fast-paced folk rock pieces. Lyrically, Give It Up revolves around Raitt's femininity, relationships, and being comfortable with herself. Raitt recorded the album at Bearsville Studios with producer Michael Cuscuna.

Give It Up was Raitt's first album to reach the Billboard Top LPs & Tapes chart, where it peaked at number 138. The initial reception was positive, as critics liked the vocals and the musicianship throughout the album. Contemporary reviews are also positive, with some critics calling Give It Up a stand-out album within Raitt's discography. In 2012, Rolling Stone ranked Give It Up at number 495 on its list of the 500 Greatest Albums of All Time.
mgt:
*Give It Up* is the second studio album by American singer-songwriter Bonnie Raitt, released in September 1972 by Warner Bros. Records. Recorded at Bearsville Studios in New York and produced by Michael Cuscuna, the album blends folk, blues, R&B, and soft rock, showcasing a mix of sentimental ballads and energetic folk rock tracks. Featuring guest musicians such as John Hall, Freebo, and Paul Butterfield, *Give It Up* is noted for its polished sound compared to Raitt's debut album. The album includes three original songs by Raitt and covers by artists like Chris Smither and Jackson Browne, with lyrics exploring themes of femininity, relationships, and self-assurance.

*Give It Up* received critical acclaim for Raitt's confident vocal performance and musical versatility. It peaked at number 138 on the *Billboard* Top LPs & Tapes chart and was certified gold in 1985. Retrospective reviews have praised its influence, with *Rolling Stone* ranking it among the 500 Greatest Albums of All Time in 2012.

page_title: Rappresentatione di Anima, et di Corpo
trgt: Rappresentatione di anima et di corpo (Portrayal of the Soul and the Body) is a musical work by Emilio de' Cavalieri to a libretto by Agostino Manni (1548-1618). With it, Cavalieri regarded himself as the composer of the first opera or oratorio. Whether he was actually the first is subject to some academic debate, as is whether the work is better categorized as an opera or an oratorio. It was first performed in Rome in February 1600 in the Oratorio dei Filippini adjacent to the church of Santa Maria in Vallicella.
mgt: *Rappresentatione di Anima, et di Corpo* is a sacred opera by Emilio de' Cavalieri, with a libretto by Agostino Manni, first performed in February 1600 at the Oratorio dei Filippini in Rome. Often considered the earliest surviving opera, it is a fully staged work in three acts with a spoken prologue. Cavalieri claimed to be the true reviver of Greek-style acting with singing, a precursor to modern opera, and was recognized for pioneering "recitar cantando," a style of sung declamation that enabled dramatic monologues and dialogues.

trgt: Barley yellow mosaic virus is plant pathogenic virus that causes the yellow mosaic disease of barley. Its shape is categorized as being flexuous filamentous, with lengths of 275 and 550 nanometers. The virus has a limited host range, and barley appears to be the only known susceptible host. The virus is transmitted via Polymyxa graminis, which is a plasmodiophorid protist, through the resting spores that survive in the soil, and eventually zoospores. Eastern Asia is the most affected region, but the virus can be found worldwide. Current agricultural practices have been ineffective at eliminating the virus, but breeding resistance appears to be the only way to help reduce the disease.
mgt: Barley yellow mosaic virus (BaYMV) is a plant pathogenic virus belonging to the genus *Bymovirus* in the family *Potyviridae*. It primarily infects barley (*Hordeum vulgare*), causing systemic symptoms such as yellowing, mosaic flecking, stunting, and necrosis, which can lead to significant yield losses. The virus is transmitted through the soil-borne protist *Polymyxa graminis* and spreads via zoospores that invade barley roots. BaYMV thrives in regions with cold winters and wet autumns, particularly in Eastern Asia and Western Europe, where it has caused yield losses of up to 80%. Management relies on resistant barley cultivars, as no cure exists for infected plants.

trgt:
Yusef Abdul Lateef (born William Emanuel Huddleston; October 9, 1920 – December 23, 2013) was an American jazz multi-instrumentalist, composer, and prominent figure among the Ahmadiyya Community in America.

Although Lateef's main instruments were the tenor saxophone and flute, he also played oboe and bassoon, both rare in jazz, and non-western instruments such as the bamboo flute, shanai, shofar, xun, arghul and koto. He is known for having been an innovator in the blending of jazz with "Eastern" music. Peter Keepnews, in his New York Times obituary of Lateef, wrote that the musician "played world music before world music had a name".

Lateef's books included two novellas entitled A Night in the Garden of Love and Another Avenue, the short story collections Spheres and Rain Shapes, also his autobiography, The Gentle Giant, written in collaboration with Herb Boyd. Along with his record label YAL Records, Lateef owned Fana Music, a music publishing company. Lateef published his own work through Fana, which includes Yusef Lateef's Flute Book of the Blues and many of his own orchestral compositions.
mgt:
Yusef Lateef (born William Emanuel Huddleston; October 9, 1920 – December 23, 2013) was an American jazz multi-instrumentalist, composer, educator, and author. Known for his innovative blending of jazz with Eastern music and world influences, Lateef played a wide range of instruments, including the tenor saxophone, flute, oboe, and various non-Western instruments. He began his career in swing bands and rose to prominence in the 1950s and 1960s, recording influential albums such as *Eastern Sounds* and performing with Cannonball Adderley's Quintet. Lateef disliked the term "jazz," preferring to describe his music as "autophysiopsychic."

A devout Muslim, Lateef converted in the 1950s and incorporated spiritual and cultural themes into his work. He earned advanced degrees in music and education, taught at institutions such as the University of Massachusetts Amherst, and received numerous accolades, including a Grammy Award and the NEA Jazz Master Fellowship. Lateef continued to compose, perform, and teach until his death in 2013, leaving a lasting legacy in jazz and world music.

trgt:
A chase gun (or chaser), usually distinguished as bow chaser and stern chaser, was a cannon mounted in the bow (aiming forward) or stern (aiming backward) of a sailing ship. They were used to attempt to slow down an enemy ship either chasing (pursuing) or being chased, when the ship's broadside could not be brought to bear. Typically, the chasers were used to attempt to damage the rigging and thereby cause the target to lose performance.

Bow chasers could be regular guns brought up from the gundeck and aimed through specially cut-out ports on either side of the bowsprit, or dedicated weapons made with an unusually long bore and a relatively light ball, and mounted in the bow. Stern chasers could also be improvised, or left permanently in the cabins at the stern, covered up and used as part of the furniture.
mgt:
A chase gun was a naval cannon used during the Age of Sail, mounted at the bow or stern of a ship to fire directly ahead or behind during a pursuit. These guns were employed in prolonged chases, where ships fine-tuned their sails to exploit wind conditions. Chase guns, often made of brass for improved accuracy, were used to target an enemy ship's rigging or critical lines, with the aim of slowing or disabling the vessel. Despite their purpose, they were generally of limited accuracy, as demonstrated by an eighteenth-century British crew that required seventy-two shots to hit an enemy's sails.

By the late eighteenth century, the Royal Navy enhanced the effectiveness of chase guns through improved artillery training and modifications to gun carriages for greater range and elevation. From 1799, Royal Navy frigates were equipped with standardized bow and stern chasers for use in pursuit scenarios.

trgt: Intellectual property rights (IPRs) have been acknowledged and protected in China since the 1980s. China has acceded to the major international conventions on protection of rights to intellectual property. Domestically, protection of intellectual property law has also been established by government legislation, administrative regulations, and decrees in the areas of trademark, copyright, and patent. This has led to the creation of a comprehensive legal framework to protect both local and foreign intellectual property. Despite this, copyright violations are extremely common in the PRC. The American Chamber of Commerce in China surveyed over 500 of its members doing business in China regarding IPR for its 2016 China Business Climate Survey Report, and found that IPR enforcement is improving, but significant challenges still remain. The results show that the laws in place exceed their actual enforcement, with patent protection receiving the highest approval rate, while protection of trade secrets lags far behind. Many US companies have claimed that the Chinese government has stolen their intellectual property sometime in 2009–2019.
mgt:
Intellectual property (IP) in China is governed by a complex legal framework that has evolved significantly over time. China became a member of the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) in 1980 and has aligned its IP laws with international agreements such as the Berne Convention and the Agreement on Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS). The country has enacted national laws for patents, trademarks, and copyrights, alongside numerous regulations and measures to strengthen IP protection. Specialized intellectual property courts and tribunals have been established since 2014 to handle complex cases, reflecting China's growing focus on IP enforcement.

Despite these advancements, challenges remain, including local protectionism, counterfeiting, and inconsistent enforcement. High-profile cases, such as disputes involving Microsoft, Schneider, and Levi's, highlight both progress and ongoing issues in IP protection. China has faced international scrutiny, including placement on the U.S. "priority watch list" for IP violations. Nevertheless, foreign firms have increasingly succeeded in Chinese courts, and the government continues to enhance its IP enforcement mechanisms, including customs protections and specialized legal institutions.

trgt: Lewis Madison Terman (January 15, 1877 – December 21, 1956) was an American psychologist and author. He was noted as a pioneer in educational psychology in the early 20th century at the Stanford Graduate School of Education. He is best known for his revision of the Stanford–Binet Intelligence Scales and for initiating the longitudinal study of children with high IQs called the Genetic Studies of Genius. He was a prominent eugenicist and was a member of the Human Betterment Foundation. He also served as president of the American Psychological Association. A Review of General Psychology survey, published in 2002, ranked Terman as the 72nd most cited psychologist of the 20th century, in a tie with G. Stanley Hall.
mgt:
Lewis Madison Terman (January 15, 1877 – December 21, 1956) was an American psychologist and professor at Stanford University, best known for his work in intelligence testing and the study of giftedness. He revised the Binet-Simon intelligence test, creating the Stanford-Binet IQ test, which became a widely used measure of intelligence. Terman played a key role in the first large-scale administration of IQ tests during World War I and advocated for their use in education to classify students and improve efficiency.

Terman conducted a landmark longitudinal study, *Genetic Studies of Genius*, which followed gifted children throughout their lives, challenging stereotypes about giftedness. However, his involvement in the eugenics movement and support for controversial ideas about intelligence and heredity have drawn significant criticism.

trgt:
Mild androgen insensitivity syndrome (MAIS) is a condition that results in a mild impairment of the cell's ability to respond to androgens. The degree of impairment is sufficient to impair spermatogenesis and / or the development of secondary sexual characteristics at puberty in males, but does not affect genital differentiation or development. Female genital and sexual development is not significantly affected by the insensitivity to androgens; as such, MAIS is only diagnosed in males. The clinical phenotype associated with MAIS is a normal male habitus with mild spermatogenic defect and / or reduced secondary terminal hair.

MAIS is one of three types of androgen insensitivity syndrome, which is divided into three categories that are differentiated by the degree of genital masculinization: complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS) is indicated when the external genitalia is phenotypically female, mild androgen insensitivity syndrome (MAIS) is indicated when the external genitalia is phenotypically male, and partial androgen insensitivity syndrome (PAIS) is indicated when the external genitalia is partially, but not fully masculinized.

Androgen insensitivity syndrome is the largest single entity that leads to 46,XY undermasculinization.
mgt:
Mild androgen insensitivity syndrome (MAIS), also known as undervirilized male syndrome, is the least severe form of androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS), a condition caused by mutations in the androgen receptor (AR) gene that impair the body's response to androgens. Individuals with MAIS have a 46,XY karyotype and are phenotypically male with fully masculinized genitalia, but may experience impaired virilization or spermatogenesis. Common features include infertility, mild gynecomastia, decreased secondary terminal hair, and occasionally minor genital abnormalities such as hypospadias. MAIS is estimated to account for 40% of male infertility cases, with 2–3% of infertile men carrying AR gene mutations.

A variant of MAIS, spinal and bulbar muscular atrophy (SBMA), also known as Kennedy's disease, is a neurodegenerative disorder caused by a specific AR mutation. Diagnosis of MAIS typically involves genetic testing, as its mild presentation often goes unnoticed. Management focuses on symptomatic treatment, including testosterone supplementation and surgical correction of gynecomastia or hypospadias. Lifespan is generally unaffected, though regular monitoring for associated conditions like breast and prostate cancer may be necessary.

trgt:
Section 101 of the Constitution Act, 1867 (Lang-fr) is a provision of the Constitution of Canada giving the federal Parliament the power to create the Supreme Court of Canada and the federal courts. Although Parliament created the Supreme Court by an ordinary federal statute in 1875, the Court is partially entrenched by the amending formula set out in the Constitution Act, 1982. The composition of the Court can only be changed by a unanimous constitutional amendment, passed by the two houses of Parliament, and all of the provincial legislative assemblies.

The Constitution Act, 1867 is the constitutional statute which established Canada. Originally named the British North America Act, 1867, the Act continues to be the foundational statute for the Constitution of Canada, although it has been amended many times since 1867. It is now recognised as part of the supreme law of Canada.
mgt:
Section 101 of the *Constitution Act, 1867* grants the federal Parliament of Canada the authority to establish courts "for the better administration of the laws of Canada." This provision, found in Part VII of the Act, has been instrumental in shaping Canada's judicial system. It empowered Parliament to create the Supreme Court of Canada in 1875 as a general court of appeal, with jurisdiction over federal, provincial, civil, and criminal law. Initially, the Supreme Court's decisions could be appealed to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council in Britain, but this practice ended in 1949.

Section 101 also enabled the creation of federal courts, including the Exchequer Court (1875), which evolved into the Federal Court and Federal Court of Appeal in 1971, and specialized courts like the Tax Court of Canada. While federal courts can adjudicate constitutional issues, the provincial superior courts retain core constitutional jurisdiction under section 96 of the Act.

trgt:
In mathematics, the Tor functors are the derived functors of the tensor product of modules over a ring. Along with the Ext functor, Tor is one of the central concepts of homological algebra, in which ideas from algebraic topology are used to construct invariants of algebraic structures. The homology of groups, Lie algebras, and associative algebras can all be defined in terms of Tor. The name comes from a relation between the first Tor group Tor1 and the torsion subgroup of an abelian group.

In the special case of abelian groups, Tor was introduced by Eduard Čech (1935) and named by Samuel Eilenberg around 1950. It was first applied to the Künneth theorem and universal coefficient theorem in topology. For modules over any ring, Tor was defined by Henri Cartan and Eilenberg in their 1956 book Homological Algebra.
mgt:
In mathematics, the Tor functor is a homological algebra tool that measures the failure of flatness of modules over a ring. For a ring \( R \), the Tor groups, denoted \( \text{Tor}^R_i(A, B) \), are defined as the left derived functors of the tensor product \( A \otimes_R B \), where \( A \) and \( B \) are \( R \)-modules. These groups are computed using projective resolutions of \( A \) or \( B \), and their homology is taken at each degree \( i \). The construction is independent of the choice of projective resolution, as shown by Cartan and Eilenberg.

For commutative rings, the Tor groups are \( R \)-modules, while for non-commutative rings, they are generally abelian groups. The Tor functor is a bifunctor, meaning it is functorial in both module variables, and it has applications in algebraic topology and algebraic geometry.

trgt: Nicole Bilderback (born June 10, 1975) is a Korean-born American actress, known for her recurring guest roles on the television programs Dark Angel and Dawson's Creek, and the films Bring It On and Bad Girls From Valley High (aka A Fate Totally Worse than Death). She also was one of the Cordettes, Cordelia Chase's friends, on the Buffy the Vampire Slayer episode "The Wish" and the Unaired Buffy pilot. She also starred in a few episodes in season 6 of The Fresh Prince of Bel Air as Ashley's friend.
mgt: Nicole Bilderback (born June 10, 1975) is a South Korean-born American actress known for her roles in film and television. Adopted by American parents and raised in Oklahoma and Texas, she began studying dance and acting at a young age, eventually enrolling at the Dallas Young Actor's Studio. After graduating from Lake Highlands High School in 1993, she moved to Los Angeles to pursue acting, debuting in the 1995 film *Clueless*. Bilderback has since appeared in various projects, including the *Clueless* television series and the 2014 action film *Mercenaries*.

trgt: Aloysius Edward "Pie" Piechota (January 19, 1914 – June 13, 1996) was a professional baseball player whose career spanned 15 seasons, two of which were spent with the Major League Baseball (MLB) Boston Bees / Braves from 1940 to 1941. Piechota, a pitcher, compiled an earned run average (ERA) of 5.66, allowing 39 earned runs off of 68 hits, 6 home runs, and 42 walks while recording 18 strikeouts over 62 innings pitched. Piechota also played in 14 seasons of minor league baseball. He made his MLB debut at the age of 26 and was officially listed as standing 6 feet 0 inches (183 cm) and weighing 195 pounds (88 kg).
mgt:
Al Piechota (January 19, 1914 – June 13, 1996) was an American professional baseball pitcher who played in Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Boston Bees and Braves during the 1940 and 1941 seasons. Over his brief MLB career, he recorded a 2–5 win–loss record with a 5.66 earned run average. Piechota had a successful minor league career, including a standout 1939 season with the Kansas City Blues, where he won 16 games and helped secure an American Association pennant.

During World War II, Piechota served in the U.S. Navy and continued to play baseball for military teams. After retiring from baseball in 1951, he worked as a Chicago police officer for 25 years.

trgt:
Justin Meldal-Johnsen (born March 26, 1970) is an American musician, record producer, songwriter and musical director. He is best known for his work with artists such as Beck, Paramore, Nine Inch Nails, M83, Air, and St. Vincent.

Meldal-Johnsen served as the touring bassist, guitarist, and keyboardist for Nine Inch Nails between 2008 and 2009, and was Beck's bassist and musical director from 1996 until mid-2016. In 2021, he began work as a bassist, keyboardist and musical director for St. Vincent.

Other artists Meldal-Johnsen has worked with include Garbage, The Mars Volta, Frank Ocean, Dixie Chicks, Tori Amos, Dido, Charlotte Gainsbourg, Goldfrapp, Blood Orange, Pete Yorn, Turin Brakes, Marianne Faithfull and Ladytron. As a producer, Meldal-Johnsen has worked with M83, Paramore, Metric, Tegan and Sara, Deafheaven, Poppy, Jimmy Eat World, The Naked and Famous, School of Seven Bells, Neon Trees, Young the Giant, Ken Andrews, Division Day and more.
mgt:
Justin Meldal-Johnsen (born March 26, 1970) is an American musician, songwriter, and record producer known for his work in alternative and industrial rock. He began his career in Los Angeles, playing in local bands and working at recording studios before joining Beck's touring and recording band in 1996, contributing to albums such as *Odelay* and *Sea Change*. Meldal-Johnsen has also performed with artists like Nine Inch Nails, Air, and St. Vincent, and was a founding member of Ima Robot. 

In addition to his performance career, he is an accomplished producer, known for his work on M83's *Hurry Up, We're Dreaming* and Paramore's self-titled album, among others. His production of Wolf Alice's *Visions of a Life* earned the Mercury Prize in 2018. Meldal-Johnsen continues to produce and write music, working from his studio in Glendale, California.

trgt: Frederick William Twort FRS (22 October 1877 – 20 March 1950) was an English bacteriologist and was the original discoverer in 1915 of bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria). He studied medicine at St Thomas's Hospital, London, was superintendent of the Brown Institute for Animals (a pathology research centre), and was a professor of bacteriology at the University of London. He researched into Johne's disease, a chronic intestinal infection of cattle, and also discovered that vitamin K is needed by growing leprosy bacteria.
mgt: Frederick Twort (22 October 1877 – 20 March 1950) was an English bacteriologist known for his discovery of bacteriophages, viruses that infect and destroy bacteria. Educated at St Thomas's Hospital, he became superintendent of the Brown Animal Sanatory Institution, where he conducted groundbreaking research on bacterial mutation, growth factors, and diseases like leprosy and Johne's disease. His 1915 discovery of bacteriophages was initially overlooked but later recognized as a major contribution to microbiology. Twort was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1929.

trgt:
Twelve-step programs are international mutual aid programs supporting recovery from substance addictions, behavioral addictions and compulsions. Developed in the 1930s, the first twelve-step program, Alcoholics Anonymous (AA), aided its membership to overcome alcoholism. Since that time dozens of other organizations have been derived from AA's approach to address problems as varied as drug addiction, compulsive gambling, sex, and overeating. All twelve-step programs utilize a version of AA's suggested twelve steps first published in the 1939 book Alcoholics Anonymous: The Story of How More Than One Hundred Men Have Recovered from Alcoholism.

As summarized by the American Psychological Association (APA), the process involves the following:
mgt: A twelve-step program is a set of guiding principles for recovery from addiction, compulsion, or other behavioral problems, originally developed by Alcoholics Anonymous (AA) in 1935. The program has been widely adapted by over 200 mutual aid organizations, including Narcotics Anonymous, Gamblers Anonymous, and Overeaters Anonymous, to address various substance and behavioral issues. The twelve steps emphasize self-admission of the problem, spiritual growth, and mutual support, often facilitated by a sponsor. The program's effectiveness varies, with studies showing success in promoting abstinence for some participants. While praised for its community support, it has faced criticism for its spiritual focus and varied success rates.

trgt: Major General عبدالقیوم طوطاخیل (Abdul Qayum Tutakhail or Totakhil) was born in Sayed-Karam district of Paktia province Afghanistan. He was Assistant Minister of Defense for Health Affairs in Afghanistan. Prior to this General Tutakhail served as Surgeon General of the Afghan National Army and as a faculty lecturer of AFAMS teaching Combat Surgery, Tactics, Organization and Medical Ethics. Since the 1970s General Tutakhail has served in the Afghan Military in different capacities having a distinguished career. He is an ethnic Pashtun from the southern Paktia province.
mgt: Abdul Qayum Tutakhail (born March 28, 1940) is an Afghan neurosurgeon and retired major general in the Afghan National Army. Born in Paktia province, he earned his MD from Kabul Medical University and a PhD in neurosurgery from the Institute of Neurosurgery in Kiev. Tutakhail served as Surgeon General and specialized in combat trauma, performing over 3,000 surgeries and authoring numerous research papers. In 2010, he was reassigned as Surgeon General to address corruption and neglect in military healthcare, leading to significant improvements at Sardar Mohammad Dawood Khan hospital.

trgt:
The Togliattiazot Affair is a series of events connected to criminal proceedings against the owners and executives of the Russian firm Togliattiazot (Toaz) and their foreign partners. The case spanned 14 years, from 2005 to 2019, and resulted in the owners and executives receiving prison sentences of between to 8.5 and 9 years. The activity of the involved parties was described as "a threat to [Russia’s] economic activity" by the Chairman of the Investigative Committee of the Russian Federation, Alexander Bastrykin.

Togliattiazot is the largest ammonia producer in Russia, with an estimated global market share of 7.6%.
mgt: The Togliattiazot affair refers to a series of legal disputes, criminal investigations, and allegations of fraud and misappropriation involving Togliattiazot (Toaz), a major Russian chemical company, and its management. The controversy began with the privatization of Toaz and the Transammiak ammonia pipeline in the 1990s, leading to accusations of fraudulent transactions and violations of investment agreements. Key figures, including Vladimir and Sergei Makhlai, faced charges of large-scale fraud, tax evasion, and embezzlement. In 2019, several executives were convicted in absentia, receiving prison sentences and significant financial penalties. The case highlighted corporate governance and corruption issues in Russia.

trgt:
Bubble sort, sometimes referred to as sinking sort, is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the input list element by element, comparing the current element with the one after it, swapping their values if needed. These passes through the list are repeated until no swaps had to be performed during a pass, meaning that the list has become fully sorted. The algorithm, which is a comparison sort, is named for the way the larger elements "bubble" up to the top of the list.

This simple algorithm performs poorly in real world use and is used primarily as an educational tool. More efficient algorithms such as quicksort, timsort, or merge sort are used by the sorting libraries built into popular programming languages such as Python and Java.
mgt:
Bubble sort is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through a list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. This process is repeated until the list is sorted. While easy to understand and implement, bubble sort is highly inefficient for large datasets due to its worst-case and average time complexity of \(O(n^2)\), making it slower than many other sorting algorithms like insertion sort or quicksort. 

Despite its inefficiency, bubble sort is often used in educational contexts to introduce algorithmic concepts. It is a stable sorting algorithm and can perform well on nearly sorted data. However, it is rarely used in practice due to its poor performance. The algorithm has been criticized for its inefficiency, with some experts advocating against teaching it in computer science curricula.

trgt: Pridwen was, according to the 12th-century writer Geoffrey of Monmouth, King Arthur's shield; it was adorned with an image of the Virgin Mary. Geoffrey's description of it draws on earlier Welsh traditions found in Preiddeu Annwfn, Culhwch and Olwen, and the Historia Brittonum. The shield is also named and described by Wace, Layamon, Roger of Wendover and Robert of Gloucester among other medieval writers, and it directly inspired the description of Sir Gawain's shield in Sir Gawain and the Green Knight.
mgt: Pridwen is the legendary shield of King Arthur, first mentioned in Geoffrey of Monmouth's *Historia Regum Britanniae* in the 12th century. Depicted as bearing an image of the Virgin Mary, it symbolizes religious devotion and protection. The shield's name, derived from Welsh tradition, also appears in works like Wace's *Roman de Brut* and Layamon's *Brut*, with variations in its description, including being made of ivory. Pridwen influenced later medieval literature, inspiring similar motifs, such as in *Sir Gawain and the Green Knight*.

trgt: Shaarey Zedek Cemetery is a Conservative Jewish burial ground in the North End of Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada. Operated by the Shaarey Zedek Synagogue, it is the largest Jewish cemetery in the Canadian Prairies, with more than 8,000 graves as of 1996. In 2012, a Jewish interfaith burial ground was installed in a fenced-off section with a separate entrance to accommodate interment of Jews alongside their non-Jewish spouses. The cemetery features a war memorial honoring Winnipeg residents who fell in World War I and World War II.
mgt: Shaarey Zedek Cemetery (Hebrew: בית החיים של שערי צדק) is a Jewish cemetery located in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada. Established in 1894 by the Shaarey Zedek Synagogue, it is the largest Jewish cemetery in the Canadian Prairies, spanning 4 hectares (9.9 acres). The cemetery contains over 8,000 graves, including those of prominent rabbis and the Bronfman family. It features a war memorial honoring Jewish servicemen and an interfaith burial ground opened in 2012. Notable events include a 1918 "black wedding" during the Spanish flu pandemic and instances of vandalism in 1968 and 1993.

trgt: Eblana (Lang-el) is an ancient Irish settlement that appears in the Geographia of Claudius Ptolemaeus (Ptolemy), the Greek astronomer and cartographer, around the year 140 AD. It was traditionally believed by scholars to refer to the same site as the modern city of Dublin. The 19th-century writer Louis Agassiz used Eblana as a Latin equivalent for Dublin. However, more recent scholarship favours the north County Dublin seaside village of Loughshinny due to its proximity to Drumanagh, an important trading site with strong links to Roman Britain; it has even been described as a bridgehead of a possible Roman invasion. However, there is no definitive proof to tie Eblana with any location, so its exact identity is still a matter of speculation.
mgt: Eblana is a settlement mentioned in Ptolemy's 2nd-century work *Geographia*, which some early antiquarians, such as Sir John Ware and Walter Harris, speculated to be an early reference to Dublin. They suggested that Eblana might be a corruption of "Deblana," derived from the Gaelic "Dubh Linn" (Black Pool), the origin of Dublin's name. However, this theory is debated, as no evidence of a significant settlement at Dublin's site exists before the Norse fortifications in 841. Scholars like Eoin MacNeill argue that Eblana may have been farther north, possibly in County Louth. Other theories propose locations such as the Delvin River or the promontory fort of Drumanagh, leaving the true identity of Eblana uncertain and speculative.

trgt: General Motors (Thailand) Limited (GMT) was a holding company of sales and manufacturing subsidiaries of General Motors (GM) in Thailand. The company was registered in 1993 as a sales company and opened its manufacturing plant in 2000. At its height, GM Thailand exported vehicles to most regions in the world, including South America, Central America, Europe, Africa, the Middle East, Southeast Asia, Australia and Japan. In February 2020, GM announced that it would withdraw from the Thai market and the Rayong plant would be acquired by Great Wall Motors by the end of 2020. The company continued to support existing Chevrolet owners for ongoing aftersales, warranty and service.
mgt: General Motors Thailand was the Thai subsidiary of General Motors, operating from 1993 to 2020. Headquartered in Rayong and Bangkok, it produced automobiles and internal combustion engines, manufacturing over 1.36 million vehicles and 500,000 powertrains. The company established a major manufacturing plant in Rayong in 2000, producing models like the Chevrolet Zafira, Colorado, and Captiva, with significant exports to global markets. It also operated an engine plant producing Duramax diesel engines. After years of declining market share, GM restructured its operations in 2015, focusing on SUVs and pickups. In 2020, GM ceased production in Thailand, selling its Rayong plant to Great Wall Motors.

trgt:
Fadi Sawan (Arabic: فادي صوّان, born in 1960) is a Lebanese military investigative judge, who became known after being assigned with the immense task of investigating the 2020 Beirut Port explosion, one of the largest non-nuclear blasts in history, the 2,750 tonnes of ammonium nitrate that killed more than 200 people, wounded over 6,000 and destroyed much of Beirut, the capital of Lebanon, on August 4, 2020.

In a country where political influence over the judiciary is a problem, which is part of corruption in Lebanon, he was known for his honesty and integrity and for not receiving instructions from politicians, thus many doubted that he could accomplish his task. As a result, in February 2021, he was removed from the probe, which will further delay the investigation. This in turn sparked a new wave of demonstrations, as part of the 2019–2021 Lebanese Revolution, with the participation of the families of the victims. The move was widely condemned, as being planned by the Lebanese authorities, who were under of extensive pressure to provide answers for the crime, which have not yet seen the light six months after the explosion.
mgt:
Fadi Sawan (born 1960) is a Lebanese judge and Saint Joseph University alumnus with over 30 years of judicial experience. A Maronite Christian, he has served as an advocate general and investigative judge in Baabda and, since 2009, as an investigating judge at the military court. Sawan is recognized for his expertise in terrorism cases, having delivered numerous verdicts against groups such as ISIS, Al-Nusra, and other extremist organizations. He is also noted for issuing indictments against Islamist figures like Ahmed Al-Assir and others who clashed with the Lebanese Army.

In 2020, Sawan was appointed by the Supreme Judicial Council as the lead investigator into the Beirut Port explosion, which occurred on August 4, 2020. However, his investigation faced significant political opposition, including criticism from Hezbollah and former Prime Minister Saad Hariri. On February 18, 2021, Sawan was removed from the case following legal challenges by two ex-ministers, Ghazi Zeaiter and Ali Hassan Khalil, whom he had charged with negligence. His removal, widely criticized by human rights organizations, further delayed the investigation into the devastating blast.

trgt:
Christian College is a K-12 independent school located over six campuses in Geelong, Victoria, Australia.

Three campuses are located in suburban Geelong: the Senior Campus at Waurn Ponds (Year 10–12), the Middle School in Highton (Year 5–9), and the Junior School in Belmont (Prep – Year 4). The Bellarine Campus is located in Drysdale (Prep- Year 9), and the Surf Coast campus is located in the coastal town of Torquay, which opened in 2018. There are also two other specialist campuses: "Back Creek" in Scotsburn (near Ballarat) is a dairy farm attended by Year 9 students, including Back Creek Cafe located in Meredith. The specialised Arts centre, Villa Paloma, is located off campus in Waurn Ponds a short distance from the Senior School, where VCE Arts students have their classes. Christian College also offers preschool kindergarten programs at the Junior School campus in Belmont (Williams House Kindergarten), and at the Surf Coast campus in Jan Juc (Butterfield House Kindergarten).

Christian College students are from a number of different areas in Geelong, these areas include Torquay, Drysdale, Anglesea, Ocean Grove, Queenscliff and Winchelsea as well as a majority from Geelong itself. Notable people to have gone to Christian College Geelong include AFL players Gary Ablett, Jr., Nathan Ablett, Matt Maguire, Taylor Adams, Connor Idun, AFLW player Renee Garing, netballer Tegan Philip (nee Caldwell), and Olympian Jakara Anthony.
mgt:
Christian College, Geelong is an independent, co-educational school located in Geelong, Victoria, Australia. Established in 1980 in the suburb of Highton, the school has grown from an initial enrollment of 40 students to over 1,900 by 2007. The college operates multiple campuses, including the Middle School in the former St. Augustine's Orphanage, the Junior School in the former Glastonbury Orphanage, and the Senior Campus, which opened in 2000 on the former Highton campus of Geelong Grammar School. The Bellarine Campus, serving students on the Bellarine Peninsula, was established in 1996 on the site of the former Rural City of Bellarine council offices.

The school features a house system with four houses: Penman Panthers, Flynn Falcons, Taylor Tigers, and Burrows Bears, named after notable figures such as Eva Burrows and John Flynn. Christian College is also known for its extensive music program, offering instruction in various instruments and hosting bands, choirs, and orchestras that compete and perform internationally, including at the Sydney Opera House and the Pacific Basin Music Festival in Hawaii.

In 2015, the school faced controversy when three Year 12 students arrived at a "Celebration Day" event in an unauthorized helicopter, leading to the cancellation of the following year's event.

trgt:
Bryant is a brand of machine tools headquartered in Springfield, Vermont. Acquired by Fives in 2015, the Bryant product line specializes in the grinding field, building grinding machines with computer numerical control (CNC).

Bryant began as an independent machine tool builder. The Bryant Chucking Grinder Company was founded in Springfield, Vermont in 1909 and was an independent company for its first half century, during which time it was widely known in the machining industries by the colloquial name Bryant Grinder. In 1958, it was bought by Ex-Cell-O Corporation, another machine tool builder, and became a subsidiary. In 1986, Ex-Cell-O and its subsidiaries, including Bryant, were purchased by Textron. In 1988, Textron sold Bryant Grinder to the Goldman Group, a financial investment group. In 2002, Bryant was purchased by Vermont Machine Tool, where it was operated as a division. In 2015, Bryant was purchased by Fives, an international engineering group, expanding its expertise to the design and manufacture of high-precision/high-production inner-outer diameter grinders.
mgt:
Bryant Grinder, originally founded as the Bryant Chucking Grinder Company in 1909 by William LeRoy Bryant, is a historic American manufacturer of precision grinding machines. Based in Springfield, Vermont, the company played a significant role in the development of the machine tool industry, particularly in the automotive, aircraft, and bearing sectors. Bryant's innovations, including multispindle grinders and advancements in grinding technology, established its reputation for precision and accuracy. The company thrived during World War I and World War II, contributing to military production and expanding its facilities and workforce.

In the mid-20th century, Bryant became part of Ex-Cell-O Corporation and later developed groundbreaking CNC grinding machines, such as the Lectraline series. Ownership changes in the late 20th and early 21st centuries led to challenges, including a temporary closure in 2002. Vermont Machine Tool revived the brand, and in 2015, Fives acquired Bryant's technology and intellectual property, continuing its legacy in precision grinding under Fives Landis Corp.

trgt:
The Raccoon River is a 30.8-mile-long (49.6 km) tributary of the Des Moines River in central Iowa in the United States. As measured using the longest of its three forks, its length increases to 226 miles (364 km). Via the Des Moines River, it is part of the watershed of the Mississippi River. The river runs through an intensely cultivated area of croplands and livestock farming, receiving Tile drainage from slow-draining rich natural bottomland.

The Des Moines metropolitan area has been obtaining its drinking water from the Raccoon River just before it empties into the Des Moines River through water utilities since the 19th century. During the Great Flood of 1993, the Raccoon River flooded the water treatment facility of Des Moines, shutting off the city's supply of drinking water.
mgt:
The Raccoon River is a 30.8-mile-long (49.6 km) tributary of the Des Moines River in central Iowa, United States, with its length extending to 226 miles (364 km) when including its longest fork. Originating in an intensely cultivated agricultural region, the river flows eastward through Dallas and Polk counties, joining the Des Moines River near downtown Des Moines. It has historically provided drinking water to the Des Moines metropolitan area, though it has faced challenges such as flooding during the Great Flood of 1993, which disrupted the city's water supply.

The river is also impacted by agricultural pollution, with nitrate runoff from fertilizers causing periodic spikes in contamination levels, exceeding safe drinking water standards. First documented on an 1814 map by Lewis and Clark, the Raccoon River remains a vital waterway in Iowa's landscape and history.

trgt: Yamada Amy (山田 詠美, Yamada Eimi) born February 8, 1959, is a popular but controversial contemporary Japanese writer who is most famous for her stories that address issues of sexuality, racism, and interracial love and marriage. Her debut and subsequent popular success in the 1990s was a part of Japan's hip-hop and Black culture boom. While she is most known for her stories of complicated and messy romantic love, she also writes on the daily minutiae of life (slice-of-life), child-raising, and bullying.
mgt: Amy Yamada (山田詠美, born February 8, 1959) is a Japanese novelist, short-story writer, and essayist known for her exploration of themes such as race, identity, and relationships. She gained widespread recognition with her 1985 novel *Bedtime Eyes*, which won the Bungei Prize and drew on her experiences with African-American culture. Her works, including *Soul Music Lovers Only* and *Trash*, often address issues of separation, bullying, and childhood struggles. Yamada's writing has influenced contemporary Japanese authors and has been translated into English.

trgt: The White Obelisk is a large stone monolith found at the ancient Assyrian settlement of Nineveh, northern Iraq. Excavated by the British archaeologist Hormuzd Rassam in 1853, it is one of only two intact obelisks to survive from the Assyrian empire, the other being the Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III. Both are now preserved in the British Museum. The White Obelisk dates to the beginning of the Neo-Assyrian Empire and has been variously ascribed to the reigns of Ashurnasirpal I (c. 1040 BC), Tiglath-Pileser II (c. 950 BC) or Ashurnasirpal II (c. 870 BC).
mgt: The White Obelisk is a limestone monument from ancient Assyria, dating to approximately 1050–1031 BC. Discovered in Nineveh, Iraq, by archaeologist Hormuzd Rassam in 1853, it was transported to London and is now housed in the British Museum. Standing 284 cm tall, the obelisk features relief carvings and inscriptions depicting military campaigns, royal hunts, banquets, and religious rituals, including a libation to the goddess Ishtar. Believed to date to the reign of Ashurnasirpal I, it is one of the earliest examples of Assyrian art, showcasing intricate details of the period's cultural and political life.

trgt: The Lady in the Lake is a 1943 detective novel by Raymond Chandler featuring the Los Angeles private investigator Philip Marlowe. Notable for its removal of Marlowe from his usual Los Angeles environs for much of the book, the novel's complicated plot initially deals with the case of a missing woman in a small mountain town some 80 miles (130 km) from the city. The book was written shortly after the attack on Pearl Harbor and makes several references to America's recent involvement in World War II.
mgt: *The Lady in the Lake* is a 1943 detective novel by Raymond Chandler, featuring his iconic private investigator Philip Marlowe. Set in California, the story follows Marlowe as he investigates the disappearance of a wealthy businessman’s wife, uncovering a web of deception, multiple murders, and mistaken identities. The novel is notable for its intricate plot, moral ambiguity, and exploration of appearances versus reality. Chandler adapted elements from his earlier short stories to craft the narrative, which has been praised as one of his finest works. It was later adapted into film and radio productions.

trgt: Eskişehirspor Kulübü is a Turkish professional football club located in Eskişehir. Eskisehirspor has a total of six trophies, making them the sixth most trophies holders in Turkey. Founded in 1965, Eskişehirspor competed in the top division of Turkish football for 16 years until they were relegated. After being out of the Süper Lig for 12 years the club won the TFF First League playoffs and were promoted in May 2008. Since being relegated from the Süper Lig in 2016 they have competed in lower leagues.
mgt: Eskişehirspor Kulübü is a Turkish professional football club based in Eskişehir, founded on June 19, 1965, through the merger of three local teams. Known as "Red Lightnings" and "Star of Anatolia," the club has a rich history, including winning the Turkish Cup in 1969–70 and the President's Cup in 1970–71. Eskişehirspor has experienced fluctuating success, with periods in the Süper Lig and lower divisions. The team plays at the New Eskişehir Stadium, supported by passionate fans, including the renowned BandoESES group, known for creating a vibrant match atmosphere.

trgt: Anna Maria Rückerschöld (5 February 1725 – 25 May 1805), born Rücker, was a Swedish author who wrote several popular books on housekeeping and cooking in the late 18th and early 19th century. She was an advocate of women's right to a good education in household matters and propagated this view in public debate through an anonymous letter in 1770. Along with Cajsa Warg and other female cookbook authors, she was an influential figure in culinary matters in early modern Sweden.
mgt: Anna Maria Rückerschöld (1725–1805) was a Swedish writer and advocate for women's household education. Born into an upper-class family, she received no formal education, as was customary for women of her time. She married Jonas Jakobsson Dahl in 1750 and had four children, three of whom survived to adulthood. Rückerschöld is best known for her cookbooks and writings on efficient household management, including *En Liten Hushålls-Bok* (1785) and *Den Nya och Fullständiga Kok-Boken* (1796). She also wrote articles advocating for practical education for women, emphasizing household skills over social accomplishments. Her works, influenced by mercantilist ideas, promoted frugality and the use of local resources. Rückerschöld's contributions to household literature earned her recognition, including a silver medal from the Patriotiska Sällskapet for her cookbook aimed at poorer households. Her advocacy for women's education and practical skills has led some to describe her as a "cautious feminist."

trgt:
Barbil is a city and a Municipal Council in the Kendujhar district (also known as Iron City) of the state of Odisha, India. The region around Barbil has one of the largest deposit of iron ore and manganese ore in the world. It is a major source of revenue generation for both the Central and the State Governments.

According to sources, 45% to 48% ST and SCs are living here.It is a fifth scheduled area under the constitution of india.
mgt: Barbil is a city in the Keonjhar district of Odisha, India, known for its rich deposits of iron and manganese ore. Located at an elevation of 477 meters, it is surrounded by hills and has a population of approximately 300,000 as of 2017. The city is a hub for mining and industrial activities, including steel and iron ore pellet manufacturing. Barbil is well-connected by rail and road to major cities like Kolkata and Bhubaneswar. Odia is the official language, with several tribal languages also spoken.

trgt:
Glory Lefoshie Sedibe (16 May 1953 - 20 March 1994), popularly known as Comrade September, was a member of the African National Congress (ANC) and a senior Umkhonto we Sizwe (MK) operative who in August 1986 was abducted by an Apartheid death squad led by Eugene de Kock. After being severely tortured while detained in Piet Retief, Sedibe agreed to inform on his ANC comrades, becoming an askari or spy and murdered his own comrades in Vlakplaas with De Kock and others for the Apartheid state. He was a very prominent ANC activist in exile who went by the noms de guerre Comrade September, Lucky Seme and Wally Williams and was also nicknamed Dois M and Sebata.

Sedibe's name featured high during the Truth and Reconciliation Commission that was established by Nelson Mandela's democratic government in 1996 and he was an example of how inhumane the Apartheid regime was to black people "who were often faced with no real choice at all [but] to betray their comrades or be killed," as journalist Rebecca Davis puts it.
mgt:
Glory Lefoshie Sedibe (May 16, 1953 – March 20, 1994), also known by aliases such as Comrade September, was a South African anti-apartheid activist, intelligence operative, and later an askari (enemy agent). Born in Pilgrim's Rest, Mpumalanga, Sedibe joined the African National Congress (ANC) and its military wing, Umkhonto we Sizwe, in 1977, rising to become head of intelligence for the Transvaal region after receiving training in East Germany and the Soviet Union. 

In 1986, Sedibe was abducted by apartheid police in Swaziland with the assistance of local authorities. Following months of torture, he became an askari, collaborating with the apartheid regime to capture and kill his former ANC comrades. He later served as a key state witness in the 1988 treason trial of anti-apartheid activist Ebrahim Ebrahim and others. Sedibe died under mysterious circumstances in 1994, with conflicting accounts attributing his death to poisoning or heart failure. His legacy remains controversial due to his defection and actions as an askari.

trgt:
Primitive Love is the ninth studio album and second English-language record by the Miami Sound Machine, released in August 1985, by Epic Records.

A 2CD remastered version of the album was scheduled for March 31, 2017 by Vibe on Records, but it was postponed because Estefan is working on her new album. To date there is no set date for the re-release; however, the track listing was revealed to the public: it will include five songs that were previously unreleased on CD, sixteen remixes, two soundtrack songs, one demo and the Spanish version of "Words Get in the Way". The album sold over 6 million copies worldwide.
mgt: *Primitive Love* is the second English-language studio album by the American band Miami Sound Machine, released on August 13, 1985, by Epic Records. Produced by Emilio Estefan Jr., the album marked the band's breakthrough into mainstream success, reaching #23 on the *Billboard* 200 and ending 1986 at #10 on the *Billboard* Year-End Charts. It featured a blend of dance-pop rhythms and Gloria Estefan's distinctive vocals, which helped the band achieve significant crossover appeal. Four singles were released, including the hits "Conga" (#10), "Bad Boy" (#8), "Words Get in the Way" (#5), and "Falling in Love (Uh-Oh)" (#25).

trgt: Major General Hurdis Secundus Lalande Ravenshaw CMG (June 1869 – c. 6 June 1920) was a senior British Army officer in the First World War who served at the Royal Military College, Sandhurst and saw action on the North-West Frontier of India, in South Africa in the Second Boer War and in France and Greece in the First World War. In 1916 a German U-boat captured him, and he was a prisoner of war for the next two years. He died in 1920 in unusual circumstances after becoming lost in the South African bush near Port Elizabeth and succumbing to the elements.
mgt: Hurdis Ravenshaw (June 1869 – 6 June 1920) was a British Army major general who served in several key campaigns, including the Chitral expedition, Malakand, the Tirah Campaign, the Second Boer War, and the First World War. Commissioned in 1888, he held various commands, including the 83rd Brigade and the 27th Division, seeing action on the Western Front and in Salonica. Captured in 1916 after his ship was sunk, he spent two years as a prisoner of war. Post-war, he commanded British troops in South Africa, where he died under mysterious circumstances in 1920 near Port Elizabeth.

trgt:
The Mercedes-Benz W140 is a series of flagship vehicles manufactured by Mercedes-Benz from 1991 to 1998 in sedan/saloon and coupe body styles and two wheelbase lengths (SE and SEL). Mercedes-Benz unveiled the W140 S-Class at Geneva Motor Show in March 1991 with sales launch in April 1991 and North American launch on 6 August 1991.

All models were renamed in June 1993 as part of the corporate-wide nomenclature changes for 1994 model year on, becoming S regardless of wheelbase length or body style as well as fuel type. Diesel models carried a TURBODIESEL trunk/boot lid label. In 1996, the S-Class coupé was renamed again as CL-Class into its own model range.

The W140 series S-Class was superseded by the W220 S-Class sedan and C215 CL-Class coupé in 1998 after an eight-year production run.

Production of the W140 reached 432,732, with 406,710 sedans and 26,022 coupés.
mgt:
The Mercedes-Benz W140 is a full-size luxury car produced by Daimler-Benz from 1991 to 1998, with model years spanning 1992 to 1999. Available as a sedan in standard (W140) and long-wheelbase (V140) versions, as well as a coupé (C140), it succeeded the W126 and was later replaced by the W220 S-Class and C215 CL-Class. Designed by Olivier Boulay under the direction of Bruno Sacco, the W140 introduced advanced safety and comfort features, including double-pane windows and self-closing doors. It also marked the debut of Mercedes-Benz's first passenger V12 engine.

Despite its engineering excellence, the W140 faced criticism for its high cost and competition from Japanese luxury brands, leading to slower sales. Many enthusiasts regard it as the "last true S-Class" due to its robust design. The W140 gained notoriety as the car involved in the 1997 crash that killed Princess Diana.

trgt:
"Bizarre Love Triangle" is a song by the English rock band New Order, released as a single in 1986 from their fourth studio album, Brotherhood (1986), which reached the top five on the US Hot Dance Music/Club Play Singles chart, and No. 5 on the Australian ARIA Charts (No. 1 on the Victoria state chart) in March 1987.

It failed to make the top 40 in either the United Kingdom (only reaching No. 56) or the US Billboard Hot 100. In the United States, the song reached No. 8 on the Hot Dance Music/Maxi-Singles Sales chart, but failed to chart on the Hot 100 during its original 1986 release. However, a new mix included on The Best of New Order was released in 1994 and finally made a brief appearance on the Billboard Hot 100 in the No. 98 position in 1995.

In 2004, the song was ranked No. 204 in Rolling Stone's "The 500 Greatest Songs of All Time."
mgt:
"Bizarre Love Triangle" is a 1986 single by the English band New Order, featured on their fourth studio album, *Brotherhood*. Written by band members Gillian Gilbert, Peter Hook, Stephen Morris, and Bernard Sumner, the song is a hallmark of the new wave, synth-pop, and alternative dance genres. The single, released on November 5, 1986, gained acclaim for its innovative use of electronic instrumentation, including the Fairlight CMI music workstation, and its layered production. A remixed 12-inch version by Shep Pettibone appears on the compilation *Substance*, while other versions feature on subsequent compilations like *Best Of* and *Singles*.

The music video, directed by Robert Longo, incorporates imagery inspired by his "Men in the Cities" lithographs and features surreal scenes of people in business suits flying through the air. Critically lauded as a "synth-pop masterpiece," the song has been ranked among the greatest of all time by *Rolling Stone*, *Billboard*, and other publications, cementing its legacy in New Order's discography.

trgt:
Vahida Maglajlić (17 April 1907 – 1 April 1943) was a Yugoslav Partisan recognized as a People's Hero of Yugoslavia for her part in the struggle against the Axis powers during World War II. She was the only Bosnian Muslim woman to receive the order.

Born to a Banja Luka qadi, Maglajlić was denied a higher education by her father. She took up various social and humanitarian causes, including women's rights. After Yugoslavia was invaded by the Axis powers in April 1941, Bosnia became part of the Independent State of Croatia, a fascist puppet state. Maglajlić entered the Communist Party of Yugoslavia in May and joined the growing Partisan resistance movement. Her high social status allowed her to inconspicuously shelter and equip local Partisans for several months, but she was eventually discovered by the authorities and sent to prison. After months of torture, Maglajlić escaped and joined the Partisans. She was an influential political figure in Bosanska Krajina until her death in combat with German troops in Mala Krupska Rujiška.

After the war, her remains were exhumed from Mala Krupska Rujiška and reburied at the Partisan cemetery in Banja Luka. Her image has been displayed on the cover of leaflets distributed by the Palestine Liberation Organization, which has sought to promote her as an example for Palestinian women.
mgt:
Vahida Maglajlić (17 April 1907 – 1 April 1943) was a Bosnian Muslim resistance fighter and the only Bosnian Muslim woman to be named a People's Hero of Yugoslavia. Born into a prominent family in Banja Luka, she became one of the first Bosnian Muslim women involved in the labor and women's rights movements. During World War II, she joined the Yugoslav Partisans, organizing resistance against the fascist Ustaše regime in the Independent State of Croatia. Leveraging her family's social status, she sheltered communists, gathered supplies, and encouraged others to join the resistance.

After being imprisoned and tortured by the Ustaše, Maglajlić escaped and joined the Partisans in late 1941. She became a political leader, working to mobilize women and representing the communists in various regions. Maglajlić was killed in action during a German encirclement in 1943. Posthumously honored as a People's Hero in 1951, she remains a symbol of resistance and is buried in the Partisan cemetery in Banja Luka.
