Her legacy has been celebrated internationally, including by the Palestine Liberation Organization, which promoted her as a role model for women.

trgt:
Ryan Higa (born June 6, 1990), also known as nigahiga (/ˈniːɡəhiːɡə/ NEE-ɡə-HEE-ɡə), is an American Internet personality. Best known for his comedy videos on YouTube, Higa began making YouTube videos in 2006 and was one of the most popular creators on the platform in its early years. His main YouTube channel, nigahiga, was the most subscribed channel on YouTube for 677 consecutive days from 2009 to 2011, an achievement that has only been surpassed by PewDiePie and T-Series. Higa was the first person to reach the milestones of 2 million and 3 million subscribers on YouTube. Higa launched a podcast in 2018 called Off the Pill, which has featured YouTubers and celebrities such as KevJumba, Andrew Yang, and Jeremy Lin. In 2020, Higa started streaming on Twitch, where he reacts to his past videos and broadcasts video game content, most notably in Valorant.

As of November 2022, he has over 21 million subscribers on YouTube and 756,000 followers on Twitch. Higa has won a Shorty Award, has been named Forbes Top 30 Under 30, and has been nominated for three more Shorty Awards, six Streamy Awards, and five Teen Choice Awards.

Outside of his content on YouTube and Twitch, he has also published a memoir Ryan Higa's How to Write Good and appeared in feature films Tell Me How I Die (2016) and Finding'Ohana (2021).
mgt:
Ryan Higa (born June 6, 1990) is an American YouTuber, actor, and entrepreneur, best known for his comedic content on the YouTube channel *nigahiga*, which became one of the platform's most popular channels. Born in Hilo, Hawaii, Higa began creating videos in 2006 with friends while in high school, initially focusing on lip-syncing before expanding into comedy sketches and short films. His channel was the first to reach 3 million subscribers in 2010, and his content has amassed over 4.36 billion views. Higa also created a second channel, *HigaTV*, for vlogs and behind-the-scenes content.

Higa co-founded the parody K-pop group Boys Generally Asian (BgA), which achieved chart success with its comedic songs. He has also appeared in films, including *Ryan and Sean's Not So Excellent Adventure* (2008) and *Finding 'Ohana* (2021), and published a memoir, *Ryan Higa's How to Write Good* (2017). In addition to his entertainment career, Higa co-founded Ninja Melk, an energy drink company, in 2016. After stepping back from YouTube in 2020, he transitioned to Twitch, where he streams gaming content. Known for his creativity and humor, Higa remains a prominent figure in online entertainment.

trgt: The Whole Family: a Novel by Twelve Authors (1908) is a collaborative novel told in twelve chapters, each by a different author. This unusual project was conceived by novelist William Dean Howells and carried out under the direction of Harper's Bazaar editor Elizabeth Jordan, who (like Howells) would write one of the chapters herself. Howells's idea for the novel was to show how an engagement or marriage would affect and be affected by an entire family. The project became somewhat curious for the way the authors'contentious interrelationships mirrored the sometimes dysfunctional family they described in their chapters. Howells had hoped Mark Twain would be one of the authors, but Twain did not participate. Other than Howells himself, Henry James was probably the best-known author to contribute. The novel was serialized in Harper's Bazaar in 1907–08 and published as a book by Harper's in late 1908.
mgt:
*The Whole Family* is a collaborative novel published in 1908 by Harper & Brothers, featuring chapters written by twelve prominent authors, including William Dean Howells, Mary E. Wilkins Freeman, and Henry James. Conceived by Howells as a showcase of literary realism, the novel explores the dynamics of the Talbert family, a middle-class New England household, as they navigate the engagement of daughter Peggy Talbert. The project became notable for the creative tensions among its contributors, particularly Freeman's reinvention of a minor character, Aunt Elizabeth, as a vibrant and independent woman, which disrupted Howells's original vision.

Serialized in *Harper's Bazar* before its release, the novel received favorable contemporary reviews and decent sales, driven by its unique collaborative nature and rumors of behind-the-scenes conflicts. Critics have since highlighted its themes of family, marriage, and women's roles, as well as the artistic rivalries that shaped its creation.

trgt: Haruka Nishio (西尾 晴香, Nishio Haruka, born March 31, 1994), better known by her stage name Yūka Nishio (西尾 夕香, Nishio Yūka) is a Japanese musician, singer, voice actress and DJ from Kanagawa Prefecture who is affiliated with Hibiki. She was formerly active as a solo musician under the name Haruca, releasing the single "Eien no Kotae" in 2017. She is known for her roles as Nanami Hiromachi in BanG Dream!, where she also serves as the bassist of the band Morfonica, and as Rinku Aimoto, the protagonist of the multimedia franchise D4DJ.
mgt: Yūka Nishio (西尾 夕香, born March 31, 1994) is a Japanese musician, singer, voice actress, and DJ affiliated with the talent agency Hibiki. Initially performing under the stage name Haruca, she began her career as a singer in 2016 and later transitioned to voice acting. Nishio is best known for voicing Nanami Hiromachi in *BanG Dream!* and Rinku Aimoto in *D4DJ*. She is the bassist for the band Morfonica and co-hosts the YouTube series *Monica Radio*. Nishio's career also includes DJing and performing in live concerts for her music projects.

trgt:
Charles Boarman (December 24, 1795 – September 13, 1879) was a career officer in the United States Navy. He entered the naval service shortly before the War of 1812 and served until 1876, subsequently retiring as a rear admiral. He held a number of important posts, both in peace and wartime, in the Mediterranean, West Indies and Brazil Squadrons and as commandant of the Brooklyn Navy Yard. He was also assigned to special duty during the American Civil War and a member of the U.S. Naval Board at Washington, DC.

After attending naval school at the Washington Navy Yard, Boarman saw service as a young midshipmen aboard USS Jefferson during the War of 1812 and later took part in anti-piracy operations in the early 1820s. He commanded a number of warships between 1827 and 1850, most notably, USS Brandywine during the Mexican–American War. In 1876, Boarman was promoted to rear admiral on the retired list and died in Martinsburg, West Virginia, three years later.

He was among several of Catholic background, such as John Cassin, Patrick McDonough, and Philemon C. Wederstrandt, to become high-ranking naval officers in the early years of the U.S. Navy. He was also, at the time of his death, the longest serving naval officer on the Navy Register with 68 years service. The Boarman family home, the Boarman House, occupied by the family for over a century, is a state historical landmark in West Virginia.
mgt:
Charles Boarman (December 24, 1795 – September 13, 1879) was a United States Navy officer who served for over 68 years, making him one of the longest-serving officers in U.S. Navy history. Born in Bryantown, Maryland, into a prominent colonial Maryland family, Boarman joined the Navy in 1811 and served during the War of 1812 aboard the USS Jefferson. Over his extensive career, he commanded several ships, including the USS Weasel, USS Java, and USS Brandywine, and held significant posts such as Commandant of the Brooklyn Navy Yard. He participated in anti-piracy operations in the West Indies, served in the Mediterranean and Brazil Squadrons, and oversaw preparations for Commodore Matthew C. Perry's Japan expedition.

During the Civil War, despite his Southern roots, Boarman remained loyal to the Union and supported the creation of West Virginia. He retired as a rear admiral in 1876. Boarman and his wife, Mary Ann Abell, raised 13 children, and their family home in Martinsburg, West Virginia, known as the Boarman House, is a historic landmark. Boarman died in Martinsburg in 1879 and was buried in Saint Joseph's Cemetery. His legacy includes his contributions to naval service and his family's prominence in American history, with descendants who became pioneers, physicians, and musicians.

trgt:
Lieth Peter Von Stein (March 16, 1946 – July 25, 1988) was an American businessman whose murder launched a high-profile trial in North Carolina. The case became the subject of two books and two television films.

An executive at National Spinning, a textile factory in Washington, North Carolina, Von Stein was stabbed and bludgeoned to death in his home as the result of a plot devised by his stepson, Christopher Wayne Pritchard. Von Stein's wife, Bonnie (Pritchard's mother), was also attacked, but survived. Pritchard's friend James Bartlett Upchurch III was convicted of first-degree murder, while Pritchard and another co-conspirator, Gerald Neal Henderson, were convicted of aiding and abetting the murder. The trial revealed that Pritchard's motive was his desire to inherit his stepfather's fortune, derived from his family's dry cleaning chain, Camel City Dry Cleaners in Winston-Salem, North Carolina.
mgt:
The murder of Lieth Von Stein occurred on July 25, 1988, in Washington, North Carolina, when he and his wife, Bonnie, were attacked in their home. Lieth was killed, and Bonnie survived with serious injuries. The investigation revealed that Lieth's stepson, Chris Pritchard, had orchestrated the crime, enlisting his college friends Neal Henderson and James Upchurch to carry out the attack in hopes of inheriting part of Lieth's $2 million estate. All three were convicted, with Upchurch receiving a life sentence, Pritchard serving time before being paroled in 2007, and Henderson paroled in 2000.

The case gained national attention and was the subject of two books, *Cruel Doubt* and *Blood Games*, as well as television adaptations. These works controversially linked the crime to the perpetrators' interest in Dungeons & Dragons, a connection that critics have called tenuous.

trgt:
Port Dock railway station – named Port Adelaide until 1916 – was located in the commercial centre of Port Adelaide, South Australia at the corner of St Vincent Street and Lipson Street. It was the original terminus of the railway between Adelaide and Port Adelaide, which opened in 1856.

After closure in 1981, the passenger station site was redeveloped as the Port Adelaide Police Station and Magistrates' Court. The former goods yard, adjacent to Lipson Street, is now occupied by the National Railway Museum. Several proposals have been advanced to build a new station, including a budgeted project by the government of South Australia in 2019, but as of 2021 none has proceeded to funding.
mgt: Port Dock railway station was a historic railway station in Port Adelaide, South Australia, located at the intersection of St Vincent Street and Lipson Street. Opened in 1856 as Port Adelaide station, it served as the town's primary railway station until 1916, when it was renamed Port Dock following the opening of the Port Adelaide Commercial Road station. The station played a significant role in connecting goods and passengers to the port, with various lines extending to wharves and private sidings. Declining use led to its closure in 1981, and the site was later redeveloped to house the National Railway Museum. Plans for a new station near the original site were proposed but remain on hold.

page_title: First Congregational Church and Parsonage (Kittery, Maine)
trgt: The First Congregational Church and Parsonage is a historic church complex at 23 Pepperrell Road (Maine State Route 103) in the Kittery Point section of Kittery, Maine. Built in 1730 for a congregation first organized in 1653, the church is the oldest in Kittery, and one of the oldest in the state of Maine. It is accompanied by a parsonage house, built in 1729, and a small cemetery, established in 1733. The buildings were listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978; the cemetery was added to the listing in 1997.
mgt: The First Congregational Church and Parsonage in Kittery Point, Maine, is a historic church complex listed on the National Register of Historic Places. The church, built in 1729 after the original structure burned down, is a modest wood-frame building featuring Greek Revival and Colonial architectural elements, with significant renovations in 1840. The adjacent parsonage, constructed in 1729 and replaced in 1910, served as a community center after its replacement. The site also includes a cemetery established in 1733, which contains the graves of many prominent local figures and features a historic stone wall and iron gate.

trgt: USS Quartz (IX-150), a Trefoil-class concrete barge designated an unclassified miscellaneous vessel, was the only ship of the United States Navy to be named for quartz or silicon dioxide (SiO2) a hard, vitreous mineral occurring in many varieties and comprising 12% of the earth's crust. Her keel was laid down as MC hull 1330 by Barrett and Hilp, Belair Shipyards, San Francisco, California (T. B7.D1). She was launched on 4 December 1943, and accepted by the Navy and placed in service on 13 April 1944.
mgt: USS *Quartz* was a Trefoil-class concrete cargo barge built during World War II by Barrett and Hilp in San Francisco, California. Launched on December 4, 1943, and commissioned on April 13, 1944, the vessel served with the Service Force, Pacific Fleet, providing advanced base supply services across the Pacific, including at Majuro, Eniwetok, Ulithi, Leyte, and Guam. After the war, *Quartz* supported "Operation Crossroads," the atomic bomb testing at Bikini Atoll. Decommissioned in 1946, she was sold in 1947 and repurposed as a breakwater in Powell River, British Columbia.

trgt: Life is a 1999 American buddy comedy-drama film directed by Ted Demme. The film stars Eddie Murphy and Martin Lawrence. It is the second film featuring Murphy and Lawrence together, the first being Boomerang. The supporting cast includes Ned Beatty, R. Lee Ermey, Obba Babatundé, Bernie Mac, Anthony Anderson, Miguel A. Núñez Jr., Bokeem Woodbine, Guy Torry, Michael Taliferro and Barry Shabaka Henley. The film's format is a story being told by an elderly inmate about two of his friends, Ray (Murphy) and Claude (Lawrence), who are both wrongfully convicted of murder and sentenced to life in prison. It received an Oscar nomination for Best Makeup at the 72nd Academy Awards. Life failed to meet the studio's expectations at the box office, and received mixed reactions from critics. The film later found a strong cult following among Murphy and Lawrence’s fans, establishing Life as a cult classic.
mgt:
*Life* is a 1999 American comedy-drama film directed by Ted Demme and starring Eddie Murphy and Martin Lawrence. Written by Robert Ramsey and Matthew Stone, the film follows Ray Gibson and Claude Banks, two men wrongfully convicted of murder in 1932 Mississippi and sentenced to life imprisonment. Over several decades, the pair navigate hardships, form friendships, and attempt to escape, ultimately finding freedom in their old age. The film explores themes of resilience, friendship, and injustice, blending humor with poignant moments.

Produced by Imagine Entertainment and distributed by Universal Pictures, *Life* was released on April 16, 1999. Despite its $80 million budget, it grossed $73.3 million worldwide, making it a financial disappointment. Critical reception was mixed, with praise for the performances of Murphy and Lawrence. The film's soundtrack, featuring hip hop and R&B, was a commercial success, achieving platinum certification. Filming took place in California, though the story is set in Mississippi.

trgt: In Michigan folklore, the Michigan Dogman was allegedly witnessed in 1887 in Wexford County, Michigan. The creature is described as a seven-foot tall, blue-eyed, or amber-eyed bipedal canine-like animal with the torso of a man and a fearsome howl that sounds like a human scream. According to legends, the Michigan Dogman appears in a ten-year cycle that falls on years ending in 7. Sightings have been reported in several locations throughout Michigan, primarily in the northwestern quadrant of the Lower Peninsula. In 1987, the legend of the Michigan Dogman gained popularity when disc jockey Steve Cook at WTCM-FM recorded a song about the creature and its reported sightings.
mgt: The Michigan Dogman is a cryptid from Michigan folklore, first reported in 1887 in Wexford County, where two lumberjacks described seeing a creature with a man's body and a dog's head. Subsequent sightings have been reported across Michigan, including notable accounts in 1937, the 1950s, and 1967. The legend gained widespread attention in 1987 when disc jockey Steve Cook recorded "The Legend," a song inspired by North American myths, which led to numerous listener reports of similar creatures. The Dogman has since been featured in media, including the TV series *MonsterQuest* and the 2011 film *Dogman*. It remains a popular subject in cryptid lore.

trgt: The Katkari also called Kathodi, are an Indian tribe from Maharashtra. They have been categorised as a Scheduled tribe. They are bilingual, speaking the Katkari language, a dialect of the Marathi-Konkani languages, with each other; they speak Marathi with the Marathi speakers, who are a majority in the populace where they live. In Maharashtra the Katkari have been designated a Particularly vulnerable tribal group (PVTG), along with two other groups included in this sub-category: the Madia Gond and the Kolam. In the case of the Katkari this vulnerability derives from their history as a nomadic, forest-dwelling people listed by the British Raj under the Criminal Tribes Act of 1871, a stigma that continues to this day.
mgt: The Katkari people are a Scheduled Tribe primarily residing in the Indian states of Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, and Karnataka, with a population of over 300,000. Historically a forest-dwelling community in the Western Ghats, their name derives from their traditional livelihood of producing catechu (katha) from the khair tree. Over time, restrictions on forest-based activities and shifting cultivation forced many Katkari to migrate to agricultural villages, where they now live in scattered hamlets, often as landless laborers. Many work in brick kilns and charcoal units, facing social exclusion and land insecurity. Despite these challenges, the Katkari retain unique cultural practices, including their knowledge of forest resources and a diet that includes rodents, which contributes to their marginalization.

trgt:
Yemodish Bekele (born 1960) is a published author of more than seven books, including poetry, short stories, and a novella, and was the first Ethiopian woman to publish her own collection of short stories. A crime journalist, Yemedish was also the first woman to become the head editor for Polis Ena Ermijaw (‘Police and Future Journeys’) the newspaper of the Federal Police in Ethiopia.

An activist for women's rights, she works with and serves on board for organisations devoted to women's advancement. She is currently the president of the Ethiopian Women Writers’ Association, and serves as the chair of the board of the Network of Ethiopian Women's Associations.
mgt: Yemodish Bekele (born 1960) is an Ethiopian author, poet, and crime journalist, known for her contributions to literature and women's rights. Born in Addis Ababa, she began writing poetry at a young age, winning a national competition in 1979 that led to the publication of her poems in *Abyotawi Gitmoch* ("Revolutionary Poems"). In 1990, she published her first book of short stories, *Ye Bakene Gize* ("Wasted Time"). Yemodish worked for 26 years at *Polis Ena Ermijaw*, the Ethiopian Federal Police newspaper, becoming its first female head editor. She is currently the President of the Ethiopian Women Writers’ Association and advocates for women's rights and literature in Ethiopia.

trgt: Pneumodesmus newmani is a species of myriapod that lived in the Paleozoic. Its exact age is uncertain; it was originally interpreted as living 428 million years ago, in the Late Silurian; however, subsequent research dates it to around 414 million years old, in the Early Devonian (Lochkovian). It is one of the first myriapods, and among the oldest creatures to have lived on land. It was discovered in 2004, and is known from a single specimen from Stonehaven, Aberdeenshire, Scotland.
mgt: *Pneumodesmus* is an extinct genus of millipede-like myriapods from the Late Silurian to Early Devonian period, approximately 428 to 414 million years ago. It is represented by a single species, *Pneumodesmus newmani*, named after its discoverer, Mike Newman, who found the fossil in Cowie, Scotland. The fossil is significant as it contains spiracles, indicating it was among the earliest known oxygen-breathing terrestrial animals. It is also the oldest body fossil of a myriapod, predating the first known vertebrates on land by 50 million years.

trgt:
VENUE is a brand of live sound digital mixing consoles introduced by Digidesign in February 2005. The family now includes 5 different consoles and a number of ways they can be configured. They can all be connected to Pro Tools, the audio editing software also created by Avid/Digidesign, to provide recording and 'Virtual Soundcheck' facilities. One of the system's key marketing points is its use of the same AAX DSP/TDM plugins as Pro Tools, an industry standard digital audio workstation (DAW). This is designed to enable the sounds recorded by the artist in the studio to be easily recreated on stage, and to allow for greater flexibility in signal processing without heavy and mechanical-shock-sensitive racks of external processors. There is also a PC-based offline editor for creation and editing of show files, although there is no audio processing in the editor.

Digidesign was acquired by Avid Technology in 1995, but its products continued to be branded Digidesign until the brand was phased out in 2010, and VENUE systems are now branded Avid.
mgt:
The VENUE sound system is a series of professional live sound mixing consoles developed by Avid Technology, widely used in touring, festivals, and live events. Introduced with the D-Show console, the VENUE range has evolved to include various models, each offering unique features and capabilities. The D-Show, launched as the first and largest console, was followed by the Profile, a smaller but equally powerful console that became an industry standard. The SC48 introduced an all-in-one design, while the S3L-X offered a compact, modular system with Ethernet AVB architecture for flexible I/O sharing. 

In 2015, Avid released the flagship S6L system, featuring advanced processing power, a redesigned control surface, and support for over 300 processing channels and 200 plug-in slots. All VENUE systems are compatible with Pro Tools, enabling seamless recording and virtual soundchecks. VENUE consoles are known for their robust performance, modularity, and integration with Avid's software ecosystem, making them a staple in live sound production.

trgt:
The social season, or season, refers to the traditional annual period in the spring and summer when it is customary for members of the social elite of British society to hold balls, dinner parties and charity events. Until the First World War, it was also the appropriate time to be resident in the city (generally meaning London) rather than in the country in order to attend such events.

In modern times in the United Kingdom, "the Season" is known to encompass various prestigious events that take place during the spring and summer. According to The Sloaney magazine's online guide "Sloaney Season", it starts with Cheltenham Festival (March), and includes the Grand National (April), The Boat Race (April), Badminton Horse Trials (May), Chelsea Flower Show (May), Epsom Derby (June), Royal Ascot (June), Test matches at Lord's (July), Wimbledon (July), Henley Royal Regatta (July), Edinburgh International Festival (August) and others, ending with Goodwood Revival (September).
mgt:
The social season in the United Kingdom, particularly associated with London, originated in the 17th and 18th centuries and reached its peak in the 19th century. It was a period during which the British nobility and landed gentry gathered in the capital to engage in politics, social events, and matchmaking for their marriageable children. The season traditionally coincided with the parliamentary session, running from January to midsummer, and featured exclusive events hosted in aristocratic townhouses and venues like Almack's. Debutantes were formally introduced to society through presentations to the monarch at royal courts until the practice ended in 1958.

The season declined after World War I as aristocratic families abandoned their London homes, and events became more public. Today, the season includes events like Royal Ascot and the Henley Royal Regatta, often sponsored by corporations. According to Debrett's, the modern season runs from April to August, concluding with the Glorious Twelfth.

page_title: Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.
trgt:
Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection. (Danish: Eventyr, fortalte for Børn. Første Samling.) is a collection of nine fairy tales by Hans Christian Andersen. The tales were published in a series of three installments by C. A. Reitzel between May 1835 and April 1837, and represent Andersen's first venture into the fairy tale genre.

The nine tales of the three booklets were collected together and published in one volume and sold at seventy-two shillings. A title page, a table of contents, and a preface by Andersen were published in the volume.
mgt: *Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.* (*Eventyr, fortalte for Børn. Første Samling.*) is a three-part collection of literary fairy tales by Danish author Hans Christian Andersen, published between May 8, 1835, and April 7, 1837, by C. A. Reitzel in Denmark. The collection includes iconic tales such as "The Little Mermaid" and "The Emperor's New Clothes," which established Andersen's international reputation. While some stories were inspired by folktales, others were original creations. Initially met with critical disapproval for its informal style, the collection later became a cornerstone of Andersen's legacy.

page_title: Rachel Carson Center for Environment and Society
trgt:
The Rachel Carson Center for Environment and Society (RCC) is an international, interdisciplinary center for research and education in the environmental humanities located in Munich, Germany. It was founded in 2009 as a joint initiative of LMU Munich (Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München) and the Deutsches Museum, and is supported by the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research. The center is named after the American biologist, nature writer, and environmentalist, Rachel Carson.

The Rachel Carson Center's directors are Christof Mauch of LMU Munich and Helmuth Trischler of the Deutsches Museum.
mgt: The Rachel Carson Center for Environment and Society (RCC) is an interdisciplinary research center based in Munich, Germany, dedicated to exploring the interactions between humans and nature. It aims to strengthen the role of the humanities in environmental debates and supports research across six thematic clusters, including natural disasters, resource conservation, ecological imperialism, landscape transformation, environmental ethics, and knowledge societies. The RCC hosts Carson Fellows, organizes public events, and collaborates with the Deutsches Museum on exhibitions. It also publishes academic works and offers a doctoral program through LMU Munich.

trgt: "Jam" is a single by American singer-songwriter Michael Jackson. The song is the fourth single from his 1991 album Dangerous, where it is the opening track. It also appears as the second track on his 2009 This Is It compilation album. The single was re-released in 2006 as part of Jackson's Visionary: The Video Singles collection campaign, and it was remixed to the Cirque du Soleil's Immortal World Tour, releasing that remix on the soundtrack album. "Jam" is a new jack swing song whose bridge features a rap verse performed by Heavy D (of the group Heavy D & the Boyz), though no credit to him appears on the album. The music video of the song featured NBA basketball legend Michael Jordan. The song was also featured on the Chicago Bulls (Jordan's team at the time)'s 1992 NBA Championship video "Untouchabulls" and was also used in many promotional ads of the NBA in the said season. The single peaked at #26 on the Billboard Hot 100. The song re-entered the UK Singles Chart in 2006, reaching number 22.
mgt:
"Jam" is a song by American singer Michael Jackson from his 1991 album *Dangerous*. Released as the album's fourth single on July 13, 1992, the song blends new jack swing and industrial funk, featuring a rap verse by Heavy D. Written and produced by Jackson, Teddy Riley, Bruce Swedien, and René Moore, "Jam" received positive reviews from critics, who praised its energetic groove, horn samples, and Jackson's vocal performance. The song's lyrics explore themes of resilience and finding solace through music and dance.

The music video, directed by David Kellogg, features Jackson teaching basketball legend Michael Jordan how to dance, while Jordan teaches Jackson basketball. It includes cameos by Heavy D, Kris Kross, and Naughty by Nature. The video premiered on FOX in June 1992 and was later included in Jackson's video compilations.

"Jam" was performed as the opening number during Jackson's *Dangerous World Tour* and at the 1993 Super Bowl halftime show. It was also rehearsed for the *This Is It* concert series before Jackson's death.

trgt:
The Spurling test is a medical maneuver used to assess nerve root pain (also known as radicular pain). The examiner turns the patient's head to the affected side while extending and applying downward pressure to the top of the patient's head. A positive Spurling's sign is when the pain arising in the neck radiates in the direction of the corresponding dermatome ipsilaterally. It is a type of cervical compression test.

Patients with a positive Spurling's sign can present with a variety of symptoms, including pain, numbness and weakness. In addition to the clinical history, the neurological examination may show signs suggesting a cervical radiculopathy.
mgt: Spurling's test is a clinical maneuver used to assess cervical radiculopathy, a condition involving nerve root pain in the neck. It is typically performed on patients with radicular symptoms to aid in diagnosis and guide further imaging or treatment. While the test is relatively specific and sensitive for diagnosing cervical spondylosis or acute cervical radiculopathy, its sensitivity decreases in patients without classic radicular signs. Studies have shown varying sensitivity and specificity, with one reporting 95% sensitivity and 94% specificity for nerve root pathology. The test should be avoided in patients with acute cervical injuries, cervical instability, or other serious conditions such as infections or neoplasms.

trgt:
"Casa Tomada" (English: "House Taken Over") is a 1946 short story by Argentine writer Julio Cortázar. It was originally published in Los anales de Buenos Aires, a literary magazine edited by Jorge Luis Borges, and later included in his volume of stories Bestiario.

It tells the story of a brother and sister living together in their ancestral home which is being "taken over" by unknown entities. It starts in a realist manner and it slowly introduces a scene in which natural laws are distorted. The mystery that revolves around what those entities are is largely left up to interpretation, allowing the genre of the story to vary from fantasy to psychological fiction to magic realism, among others. Among the resources that are frequent in Cortázar's story, graphic signs (such as parenthesis) are used to reflect censorship. The writer based the house on one located in the city of Chivilcoy in the Province of Buenos Aires, which can still be found in the streets Suipacha and Necochea.
mgt:
"Casa Tomada" ("House Taken Over") is a short story by Argentine writer Julio Cortázar, first published in 1946. The story follows a brother and sister in their forties who live a quiet, reclusive life in their inherited family home. The siblings, the narrator and Irene, spend their days engaged in mundane routines—he collects French literature, while she knits obsessively. Their peaceful existence is disrupted when mysterious noises begin emanating from parts of the house, forcing them to retreat to smaller sections of the estate. As the unseen presence gradually takes over more of the house, the siblings are eventually driven out entirely, leaving behind their belongings and locking the door before discarding the key.

The story's ambiguous ending, with the source of the noises left unexplained, has been interpreted as an allegory for political and social upheaval, particularly as a critique of Peronism in Argentina. Cortázar himself faced political pressures during the time of the story's publication.

trgt:
Kemp Town branch line was a railway line running from Brighton to Kemptown in the UK that operated between 1869 and 1971. It ran from a junction off the Brighton to Lewes line between London Road and Moulsecoomb stations, to Kemp Town railway station. It opened in 1869 and was expensive to construct, requiring a tunnel and a large viaduct.

The passenger service declined after 1917 due to tramway competition, and ceased at the end of 1932, but goods trains continued to operate on the line until 1971.

For a time during the Second World War the tunnel on the branch was used for night storage of main-line passenger stock, as a precaution against bomb damage from enemy action.

Pre-grouping, the line was operated by the London, Brighton and South Coast Railway. Post-grouping it was operated by Southern Railway then Southern Region of British Railways.
mgt:
The Kemp Town branch line was a short railway line in Brighton, England, constructed by the London, Brighton and South Coast Railway (LBSCR) to connect the Brighton to Lewes line with the Kemp Town district. Authorized in 1864 and opened on August 2, 1869, the 1.5-mile line required significant engineering, including a tunnel and viaducts, due to the area's challenging terrain and high land costs. Initially built for passenger and goods services, the line saw the addition of intermediate stations, including Lewes Road and Hartington Road Halt, though the latter was short-lived.

Passenger services ceased on December 31, 1932, due to competition from trams, and the line continued as a goods route until its closure on June 26, 1971. Following closure, much of the line was redeveloped, with parts converted into William Clarke Park and other sections repurposed for industrial and commercial use.

trgt:
Ruslan Abubakar Said-Khusainovich Edelgeriev (Russian: Русла́н Абубакар Сайд-Хуса́йнович Эдельгери́ев; born 4 December 1974, Akhmat-Yurt, Chechen-Ingush ASSR, RSFSR, Soviet Union) is a Russian politician, currently serving as Advisor to the President of the Russian Federation on Climate Change since 22 June 2018.

Edelgeriev has also served as Chairman of the Government of the Chechen Republic from 24 May 2012 to 25 June 2018. Edelgeriev is a member of United Russia, serving as Secretary of the Chechen Regional Office from July 2012 to December 2019.
mgt: Ruslan Edelgeriev (born 4 December 1974) is a Russian politician and climate official. He has served as the Special Representative of the President of the Russian Federation on climate issues since 18 July 2018 and as Adviser to the President on climate change since 22 June 2018. Previously, Edelgeriev held various roles in Chechnya, including Minister of Agriculture (2008–2012) and Prime Minister of the Chechen Republic (2012). He began his career in law enforcement and graduated with a degree in jurisprudence from the Krasnodar Law Institute in 2002.

trgt:
Sanshiro Sugata Part II (Japanese: 續姿三四郎, Hepburn: Zoku Sugata Sanshirō, a.k.a. Judo Saga II) is a 1945 Japanese action drama film written and directed by Akira Kurosawa. It is based on the novel by Tsuneo Tomita, son of Tomita Tsunejirō, the earliest disciple of judo. It was filmed in early 1945 in Japan towards the end of World War II. Unlike the original Sugata Sanshiro, the sequel is in part considered a propaganda film.

It is believed by some to be the earliest known film sequel whose title is simply the original title followed by a number, predating the likes of French Connection II (1975) by decades, although possibly mistaken as the added kanji 續 (Zoku) is not a numeral but simply means "Continuation" or "Sequel" sort of in the style of Another Thin Man or The Invisible Man Returns.
mgt:
*Sanshiro Sugata Part II* is a 1945 Japanese martial arts film directed by Akira Kurosawa and a sequel to his debut film, *Sanshiro Sugata* (1943). Set in the 1880s, the story follows Sanshiro, a judo student, as he navigates moral dilemmas and rivalries between judo and karate practitioners. The film culminates in Sanshiro's victories against an American boxer and karate masters, symbolizing his personal and spiritual growth.

Released during World War II, the film has been noted for its propagandistic elements, emphasizing Japanese martial arts' superiority over Western boxing. Critics have highlighted its thematic shift from the original's spiritual focus to nationalistic undertones. Despite this, the film features visually striking sequences, such as a snow-covered battle, showcasing Kurosawa's early mastery of landscape and emotional expression. *Sanshiro Sugata Part II* was later included in a 2010 box set of Kurosawa's early works.

trgt:
Braeden Matthew Lemasters (born January 27, 1996) is an American actor, musician, and singer. He is best known for his role as Albert Tranelli in the TNT comedy-drama series Men of a Certain Age. He has made guest appearances in several television series such as, Criminal Minds, ER, House, Grey's Anatomy and Amazon Prime Video's The Romanoffs. He is also the lead guitarist and singer for the American alternative rock band Wallows.

In films, Lemasters has appeared in the thriller film The Stepfather (2009), the romantic comedy film Easy A (2010), the family comedy film A Christmas Story 2 (2012) and the horror film Totem (2017).
mgt:
Braeden Matthew Lemasters (born January 27, 1996) is an American actor, musician, and singer. He began his acting career at age nine, appearing in television series such as *Six Feet Under*, *House*, and *Men of a Certain Age*, for which he received critical acclaim and award nominations. Lemasters has also appeared in films, including *Easy A* and *R.L. Stine's Monsterville: Cabinet of Souls*. 

In addition to acting, Lemasters is the lead guitarist and vocalist for the alternative rock band Wallows, alongside Dylan Minnette and Cole Preston. The band signed with Atlantic Records in 2018 and released their debut album, *Nothing Happens*, in 2019.

trgt:
The instruction cycle (also known as the fetch–decode–execute cycle, or simply the fetch-execute cycle) is the cycle that the central processing unit (CPU) follows from boot-up until the computer has shut down in order to process instructions. It is composed of three main stages: the fetch stage, the decode stage, and the execute stage.

In simpler CPUs, the instruction cycle is executed sequentially, each instruction being processed before the next one is started. In most modern CPUs, the instruction cycles are instead executed concurrently, and often in parallel, through an instruction pipeline: the next instruction starts being processed before the previous instruction has finished, which is possible because the cycle is broken up into separate steps.
mgt: The instruction cycle is the process by which a computer's central processing unit (CPU) retrieves, decodes, and executes instructions from memory. It consists of several stages: fetch, decode, and execute. During the fetch stage, the CPU retrieves the instruction's memory address using the program counter (PC) and stores it in registers like the memory address register (MAR) and memory data register (MDR). In the decode stage, the control unit interprets the instruction and prepares the necessary components, such as the arithmetic logic unit (ALU) or floating point unit (FPU), for execution. The execute stage carries out the instruction, performing operations like arithmetic, logic, or memory access. The cycle begins at system startup and repeats continuously.

trgt:
Australia's National Reserve System (NRS) is a network of more than 10,000 Commonwealth plus state and territory protected areas which, in combination, on a national scale, protect more than 137 million hectares (340×10^6 acres), greater than 17% of the continent, of unique biodiversity and most significant ecological landscapes for future generations. The aim of the NRS is protect the diversity of all native landscapes, flora and fauna across Australia through strategic habitat protection. It consists of public, indigenous and privately protected areas of land and inland freshwaters.

As part of the National Reserve System Cooperative Program, the Interim Biogeographic Regionalisation for Australia framework was developed as a planning tool to assist in identifying areas of priority.
mgt:
The National Reserve System (NRS) is Australia's network of protected areas, established in 1992 to conserve the nation's biodiversity and fulfill commitments under the international Convention on Biological Diversity. Spanning over 137 million hectares, it includes public, private, and Indigenous lands, with private landowners entering into perpetual conservation covenants. Indigenous Protected Areas (IPAs) form a significant part of the system, protecting ecologically valuable landscapes.

The NRS was developed with cooperation between the Australian Government and states and territories, with substantial investments through programs like the National Heritage Trust. It has been praised by organizations such as WWF Australia and is considered a key tool for biodiversity conservation and addressing climate change impacts.

trgt:
Spindizzy is an isometric computer game released for several 8-bit home computer formats in 1986 by Electric Dreams Software. It combines action and puzzle game elements. Players must navigate a series of screens to explore a landscape suspended in a three-dimensional space. Development was headed by Paul Shirley, who drew inspiration from Ultimate Play the Game games that feature an isometric projection.

The game was successful in the United Kingdom and was well received by the video game press. Reviewers praised its visuals and design, but criticized its audio. Similarities were drawn to Marble Madness, which was released in arcades two years earlier. Spindizzy was followed by a 1990s sequel titled Spindizzy Worlds.
mgt: *Spindizzy* is an action-puzzle video game developed by Paul Shirley and released in 1986 by Electric Dreams Software. Played from an isometric perspective, the game tasks players with navigating a probe named GERALD through interconnected stages within a time limit, collecting power jewels to extend gameplay. Inspired by *Marble Madness* and isometric games by Ultimate Play the Game, *Spindizzy* was praised for its innovative design, challenging gameplay, and visuals, though its audio received mixed reviews. Initially released for the Commodore 64 and Amstrad CPC, it was later ported to other platforms. The game was commercially successful in the UK and inspired a sequel, *Spindizzy Worlds*, released in 1990.

trgt: Shaking tent ceremony is a ritual of some of the indigenous people in North America that is used to connect the people with the spirit realm and establish a connection and line of communication between the spirit world and the mortal world. These ceremonies require special tents or lodges to be made, and are performed under the direction of a shaman, or spiritual leader, who uses different practices, rituals, and materials to perform the ceremony. This ceremony is more commonly used by specific indigenous tribes long ago but is still practiced around the continent today.
mgt: The shaking tent ceremony is a traditional Indigenous ritual practiced by various tribes across Canada and the northern United States, including the Algonquin, Ojibwe, Cree, and Blackfeet. Conducted by a shaman, the ceremony involves summoning spirits into a cylindrical tent to seek guidance, knowledge, or healing. The tent shakes violently as spirits communicate with the shaman, who relays their messages to the tribe. Historically used for purposes such as curing illnesses or resolving mysteries, the ceremony remains a significant cultural practice, though its methods and interpretations vary among tribes. It emphasizes spiritual connection, community identity, and ancestral traditions.

trgt:
The karbovanets or karbovanet (Lang-uk, plural: карбованці, karbovantsi for 2–4, or карбованців, karbovantsiv for 5 or more), also known as kupon (Lang-uk, plural: купони, kupony) or coupon, has been a distinct unit of currency in Ukraine during three separate periods of the 20th century. It is also a predecessor currency of today's Ukrainian hryvnia.

The karbovanets was subdivided into one hundred kopiykas, but no denominations in kopiykas have ever been issued.

In the ISO 4217 standard, the official name is spelled as karbovanet, while English version of the National Bank of Ukraine's website refers to it as karbovanets.
mgt: The karbovanets was a historical currency of Ukraine, used during three distinct periods: the Ukrainian People's Republic (1917–1920), the Nazi occupation (1942–1945), and post-Soviet Ukraine (1992–1996). Initially introduced by the Central Rada in 1917, it was issued in various denominations and designs, including postage stamp currency and state credit notes. During World War II, the Nazi administration issued karbovanets banknotes pegged to the Reichsmark. In independent Ukraine, the karbovanets replaced the Soviet ruble in 1992 but suffered from hyperinflation. It was replaced by the hryvnia in 1996 at a rate of 100,000 karbovantsiv to 1 hryvnia.

trgt:
The Aeroprakt A-22 Foxbat is a Ukrainian two-seat, high-wing, tricycle landing gear ultralight aircraft that was designed by Yuri Yakovlev and is manufactured by Aeroprakt. In the United States the A-22 is referred to as the Valor, while in the UK and Australia it is called the Foxbat. It has also been marketed as the Vision. The Aeroprakt A-22 is supplied either as "ready-to-fly" factory built aircraft, or as a kit, consisting of 152 pieces. The kit can be built in about 500 man-hours.

The A-22 meets the definition of an FAI microlight.
mgt: The Aeroprakt A-22 Foxbat is a Ukrainian light aircraft designed and manufactured by Aeroprakt in Kyiv. Development began in 1990, with the first prototype flying on October 21, 1996, and production starting in 1999. The aircraft features a mostly metal structure with fabric-covered wings and control surfaces, and its large glazed cockpit provides excellent visibility. It uses a 3-axis control system with flaperons, enabling a low stall speed of 52 km/h (32 mph). The A-22 is powered by various engine options, including the Rotax 912 series, and offers dual control configurations.

trgt: Four Freedoms Plaza is a fictional structure appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics. It is depicted as being located in the Manhattan of the Marvel Universe; it served as the replacement headquarters for the Fantastic Four when their original dwelling, the Baxter Building, was destroyed by Kristoff Vernard, the adoptive son of Doctor Doom. It is located at 42nd Street and Madison Avenue in New York City. The title of the building comes from a Franklin D. Roosevelt speech urging the Congress of the United States to enter World War II. In it Roosevelt outlined the four freedoms the world would enjoy if it united together to defeat the Axis Power.
mgt: Four Freedoms Plaza is a fictional skyscraper and the headquarters of the Fantastic Four in Marvel Comics, first appearing in *Fantastic Four* #296 (November 1986). Designed by Reed Richards after the destruction of the original Baxter Building, the 100-story structure features advanced materials and technology, with the top 50 floors dedicated to the team and the lower floors housing former Baxter Building tenants. The building has been central to several storylines, including its partial destruction during the *Infinity War* and eventual takeover and destruction by the Masters of Evil. Following its loss, the Fantastic Four relocated to other headquarters. Four Freedoms Plaza has also appeared in alternate realities and other media.

trgt:
The Sanctuary was a stone and timber circle near the village of Avebury in the south-western English county of Wiltshire. Excavation has revealed the location of the 58 stone sockets and 62 post-holes. The ring was part of a tradition of stone circle construction that spread throughout much of Britain, Ireland, and Brittany during the Late Neolithic and Early Bronze Age, over a period between 3300 and 900 BCE. The purpose of such monuments is unknown, although archaeologists speculate that the stones represented supernatural entities for the circle's builders.

The Sanctuary was erected on Overton Hill, overlooking older Early Neolithic sites like West Kennet Long Barrow and East Kennet Long Barrow. It was connected to the Late Neolithic henge and stone circle at Avebury via the West Kennet Avenue of stones. It also lies close to the route of the prehistoric Ridgeway and near several Bronze Age barrows.

In the early 18th century, the site was recorded by the antiquarian William Stukeley although the stones were destroyed by local farmers in the 1720s. The Sanctuary underwent archaeological excavation by Maud and Ben Cunnington in 1930, after which the location of the prehistoric posts was marked out by concrete posts. Now a scheduled monument under the guardianship of English Heritage, it is classified as part of the "Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites" UNESCO World Heritage Site and is open without charge to visitors all year round.
mgt:
The Sanctuary is a Neolithic archaeological site located on Overton Hill in Wiltshire, England, approximately 1.5 miles southeast of Avebury. It is part of the Avebury section of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites," inscribed in 1986. The site originally consisted of concentric rings of timber posts and sarsen stones, with its final phase featuring 42 sarsen stones forming a boundary ring 40 meters in diameter. The Sanctuary is connected to the Avebury henge via the West Kennet Avenue, a 2.4-kilometer-long alignment of standing stones, suggesting a ritual or ceremonial link between the two sites.

Constructed in multiple phases beginning around 3000 BCE, the Sanctuary's purpose remains uncertain, though it may have served as a mortuary house, a high-status dwelling, or a ritual site. Excavations in 1930 revealed postholes, Neolithic pottery, animal bones, and the burial of a young girl, possibly a sacrificial offering. The site was largely destroyed by farmers in the 18th century, but antiquarian William Stukeley documented its layout and speculated on its symbolic significance. Today, the site is marked by concrete posts, and while visually modest, it holds significant archaeological and cultural importance as part of the Avebury complex.

trgt: NBC Nightside (also known as NBC News Nightside) is an American overnight news broadcasting program on NBC, that aired from 1991 to 1998. The program was produced in three half-hour segments. It usually aired live seven nights a week, and was fed to NBC stations beginning at 2:00 am ET Sunday through Friday (immediately following Later) and 2:30 am ET on Saturdays (after Friday Night Videos), and looped until the next morning. (NBC News at Sunrise followed Nightside on weekdays.)
mgt: **NBC Nightside** was an American overnight news program that aired on NBC from November 4, 1991, to September 20, 1998. Produced by NBC News Channel in Charlotte, North Carolina, it was the network's second attempt at a late-night news show, following *NBC News Overnight* (1982–1983). Unlike its competitors, *Nightside* aired seven days a week and featured anchors who later achieved national prominence, such as Antonio Mora and Campbell Brown. Despite its profitability and decent ratings, the program was canceled in 1998.

trgt:
Franco-Albertans (Lang-fr) are francophone residents of the Canadian province of Alberta. Franco-Albertans may also refer to residents of Alberta with French Canadian ancestry, although publications from the government of Alberta use the term Franco-Albertan to refer to its francophone residents. In the 2016 Canadian Census, there were 86,705 Albertans that stated their mother tongue was French. In the same census, there were 268,615 Albertans that claimed to be able to speak French.

Francophones were the first Europeans to visit the province, with French Canadian voyageurs employed in the fur trade exploring the region in the late 18th century. French Canadians settled into a number of communities in the Northwest Territories during the 19th century, including communities in present day Alberta. Several French toponyms exist in Alberta, exemplifying the Francophone presence in the region. In 1928, the Association canadienne-française de l'Alberta was formed to promote francophone rights, and to lobby the interests of Franco-Albertans to the province. Following the enactment of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms in 1982, Franco-Albertans pushed for further linguistic rights within the province, eventually resulting in the province establishing an independent public francophone school system in 1994.

Approximately 72 per cent of Franco-Albertans are situated within the province's two largest cities, Edmonton, and Calgary. The province is home to more than 100 francophone non-profit organizations. Radio-Canada, the country's French-language public broadcaster, serves as the main French-language media broadcaster in Alberta.
mgt:
Franco-Albertans are French-speaking residents of Alberta, Canada, and form the largest francophone community in Western Canada. As of the 2016 Canadian Census, 86,705 Albertans reported French as their mother tongue, while 268,615 were bilingual in English and French. The community is diverse, with only 25% of Franco-Albertans born in Alberta, while others have migrated from other Canadian provinces or abroad, particularly Africa and Europe. French is the sixth most commonly reported ethnic origin in Alberta.

The Franco-Albertan community has historical roots dating back to the late 18th century, when French Canadian fur traders and missionaries arrived in the region. French was once a prominent language in Alberta, but its status declined after the 19th century due to English-only policies. Despite this, Franco-Albertans have preserved their language and culture through institutions, education, and advocacy. The 1982 Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms strengthened their educational rights, leading to the establishment of francophone school boards in 1994.

Today, Franco-Albertans are represented by the Association canadienne-française de l’Alberta and supported by the provincial Francophone Secretariat. The community is centered in Greater Edmonton and the Calgary Region, with four officially bilingual municipalities. Cultural contributions include francophone media, arts, and festivals, while the University of Alberta’s Campus Saint-Jean offers French-language post-secondary education. The Franco-Albertan flag was officially recognized in 2017, symbolizing the community’s enduring presence and contributions to Alberta.

trgt:
Festac '77, also known as the Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture (the first was in Dakar, 1966), was a major international festival held in Lagos, Nigeria, from 15 January 1977 to 12 February 1977. The month-long event celebrated African culture and showcased to the world African music, fine art, literature, drama, dance and religion. About 16,000 participants, representing 56 African nations and countries of the African Diaspora, performed at the event. Artists who performed at the festival included Stevie Wonder from United States, Gilberto Gil from Brazil, Bembeya Jazz National from Guinea, Mighty Sparrow from Trinidad and Tobago, Les Ballets Africains, South African Miriam Makeba, and Franco Luambo Makiadi. At the time it was held, it was the largest pan-African gathering to ever take place.

The official emblem of the festival was a replica crafted by Erhabor Emokpae of the royal ivory mask of Benin. The hosting of the festival led to the establishment of the Nigerian National Council of Arts and Culture, Festac Village and the National Theatre, Iganmu, Lagos. Most of the events were held in four main venues: the National Theatre, National Stadium, Surulere, Lagos City Hall and Tafawa Balewa Square.
mgt:
FESTAC 77, the Second World Festival of Black and African Arts and Culture, was a major cultural and artistic event held in Lagos, Nigeria, from January 15 to February 12, 1977. Inspired by the ideas of Négritude and Pan-Africanism, the festival celebrated the rich heritage of Black and African cultures worldwide. It followed the First World Festival of Black Arts held in Dakar, Senegal, in 1966, and brought together over 17,000 participants from 59 countries, including artists, writers, musicians, and scholars of African descent.

The festival featured a wide array of events, including music and dance performances, art exhibitions, drama, film screenings, and a colloquium addressing themes of cultural revival and intellectual freedom. Highlights included the Durbar festival in Kaduna, a boat regatta in Lagos, and performances by renowned artists such as Miriam Makeba, Stevie Wonder, and Gilberto Gil. The National Theatre in Lagos, built for the occasion, served as a central venue.

FESTAC 77 left a lasting legacy, including the establishment of the Center for Black and African Arts and Civilization (CBAAC) in Nigeria to preserve its artifacts. The festival remains a landmark in the global celebration of Black and African cultural identity.

trgt:
Peter Blundell Jones (4 January 1949 – 19 August 2016) was a British architect and architectural historian. He trained as an architect at the Architectural Association School, and held academic positions at the University of Cambridge and London South Bank University. He was a professor of architecture at the University of Sheffield from 1994 until his death in 2016.

He was a prolific author on architectural history and theory. As well as being a regular contributor to the Architectural Review, he wrote or collaborated on monographs of the work of Erik Gunnar Asplund, Hans Scharoun, Erich Mendelsohn, Hugo Häring and Günter Behnisch.

Jones died on 19 August 2016 at the age of 67.
mgt: Peter Blundell Jones (1949–2016) was a British architect, academic, and architectural historian known for his contributions to architectural journalism and education. Born near Exeter, he studied at the Architectural Association (AA) and began his career designing buildings such as The Round House in Devon and Padley Mill in Derbyshire, showcasing a human-centered approach to modernism. He also wrote extensively for publications like *Architectural Review* and *Architectural Journal*. Blundell Jones held academic positions at institutions including Cambridge University, South London Polytechnic, and the University of Sheffield, where he became a professor in 1994. In 1992, he was named Architectural Journalist of the Year.

trgt:
Rui Pedro Gonçalves Pinto (born 20 October 1988) is a Portuguese activist, whistleblower, creator of the Football Leaks website, and responsible for Malta Files and Luanda Leaks revelations.

Pinto created the Football Leaks website in September 2015, with the intention of revealing the financial picture of association football, including the tax avoidance schemes used by top players and the controversial third-party ownership models used by clubs. He is being accused of having used illegal methods to gather the data he leaked, by accessing into private servers and exfiltrating hundreds of internal documents and communications. But these allegations have not yet been proven at trial.

Pinto was arrested in Budapest, Hungary, in January 2019 at the request of the Portuguese authorities on suspicion of attempted qualified extortion, violation of secrecy and illegally accessing information. He was released in August 2020.
mgt:
Rui Pedro Gonçalves Pinto (born October 20, 1988) is a Portuguese whistleblower and creator of the Football Leaks website, which exposed corruption, tax evasion, and financial misconduct in the football industry. Born in Vila Nova de Gaia, Portugal, Pinto studied history at the University of Porto and later gained international attention for leaking confidential documents that led to criminal investigations in multiple countries. His revelations implicated football stars, clubs, and organizations in tax evasion, financial fair play violations, and plans for a European Super League.

Pinto was arrested in Budapest in 2019 and extradited to Portugal, where he faced 147 criminal charges, including attempted extortion and illegal access to information. Despite his legal troubles, Pinto has been recognized as a whistleblower, receiving the European Whistleblower Award in 2019. His trial began in September 2020, with Pinto maintaining his role as a whistleblower.

trgt: The Utría National Natural Park (Lang-es) is a national park in the Chocó Department, Colombia. It contains diverse flora and fauna in a lush, mountainous rainforest environment with some of the highest rainfall in the world, at up to 10,000 millimetres (390 in) annually. The park also protects the coastal marine environment, and is known for visits by humpback whales, who give birth in the lagoon after which the park is named, and sea turtles who nest on the beaches. There is accommodation for visitors, and ecotourism services are provided by the local indigenous people and members of the coastal Afro-Colombian communities.
mgt: Utría National Natural Park (Parque Nacional Natural Utría) is a protected area located on the Pacific coast of Colombia in the Chocó Department. Established in October 1987, the park spans 54,300 hectares (210 square miles) and features diverse ecosystems, including mangroves, coral reefs, tropical forests, and the Utría Cove, a large seawater lagoon. It is home to a wide variety of flora and fauna, such as humpback whales, sea turtles, jaguars, and over 380 bird species. The park overlaps with the territories of the Emberá indigenous people and Afro-Colombian fishing communities, who contribute to its conservation. It is a popular ecotourism destination.

trgt: The giant skippers are butterflies in the disputed subfamily Megathyminae, which is part of the skipper family. Some authorities treat this as a distinct and separate subfamily, but more modern classifications tend to place them within the subfamily Hesperiinae. However, some works, such as the Integrated Taxonomic Information System, still treat is as a valid subfamily. The giant skippers include two tribes, Aegialini and Megathymini. There are three genera and about eighteen species in this subfamily. These butterflies typically live in the southwest United States and Mexico in desert areas.
mgt: Giant skippers are medium-sized butterflies belonging to the subfamily Megathyminae within the family Hesperiidae. Known for their thick bodies and brown coloration with yellow markings, they are larger than other Hesperiidae species. Males are territorial, with fast flight and long hairlike scales on their upperwings. Unlike most butterflies, adults rarely feed on flowers, though males drink from wet sand. Their eggs are laid on or near host plants, and the caterpillars create silk-lined tunnels in leaves or stems, where they feed and pupate. The subfamily includes genera such as *Megathymus*, *Stallingsia*, and *Agathymus*.

trgt:
Sir George Lisle (baptised 10 July 1615 – 28 August 1648) was a professional soldier from London who briefly served in the later stages of the Eighty and Thirty Years War, then fought for the Royalists during the Wars of the Three Kingdoms. Captured at Colchester in August 1648, he was condemned to death by a Parliamentarian court martial and executed by firing squad along with his colleague Charles Lucas.

Son of a successful publisher with connections to the powerful Villiers family, Lisle began his military career in Europe before returning to England. Quickly proving a brave and competent leader, he was promoted to command of a brigade in the Royalist field army until it was destroyed at Naseby in June 1645. He surrendered at Oxford in June 1646 and made terms with Parliament.

When the Second English Civil War began in 1648, he joined the Royalist uprising in Kent before retreating to Colchester. After capitulating in August, he and Lucas were executed and later enshrined as Royalist martyrs.
mgt:
Sir George Lisle (10 July 1615 – 28 August 1648) was an English Royalist officer during the Wars of the Three Kingdoms, known for his service in the English Civil Wars and his execution following the Siege of Colchester. Born in London, Lisle rose to prominence through his connections to the powerful Villiers family, serving in the Dutch States Army and later as a colonel in the Royalist army. He fought in numerous battles, including Edgehill, Newbury, Naseby, and the storming of Leicester, where his association with atrocities damaged his reputation.

After the Royalist defeat in the First Civil War, Lisle was knighted and appointed governor of Faringdon. During the Second Civil War, he joined the Royalist uprising in Kent and defended Colchester against Parliamentarian forces. Following the town's surrender, Lisle was executed by firing squad alongside Charles Lucas, becoming a Royalist martyr. He was later reburied in Colchester's St Giles Church, where his memorial remains, and his legacy was revived during the Victorian era.

trgt:
The 2009 FIA GT3 European Championship season was the fourth season of the FIA GT3 European Championship. The season began on 2 May at Silverstone and ended on 14 November 2009 at Zolder. The season featured six double-header rounds, with each race lasting for a duration of 60 minutes. Most of the events were support races to the 2009 FIA GT Championship season, the 2009 GT4 European Cup and the 2009 Lamborghini Super Trofeo.

Phoenix Racing pairing Christopher Haase and Christopher Mies claimed the overall title, with a fourth place in the final race of the season at Zolder. In the Teams Championship, Hexis Racing were champions ahead of Matech Racing and Team Rosberg.
mgt: The 2009 FIA GT3 European Championship was a sports car racing series featuring new entries from Audi with the R8 LMS and Alpina with the B6, while Ferrari introduced the 430 Scuderia GT3. The season saw structural changes, limiting teams to two cars each, with a maximum of three teams per manufacturer. Manufacturers like Ascari, Dodge, Jaguar, Lamborghini, and Morgan were ineligible for the Manufacturers Cup due to not meeting the minimum car supply requirement. Points were awarded to the top eight finishers, with partial points for cars completing at least 75% of the race distance. The Manufacturer Cups were open to manufacturers supplying at least two teams for the full season.

trgt:
George Richmond RA (28 March 1809 – 19 March 1896) was an English painter and portraitist. In his youth he was a member of The Ancients, a group of followers of William Blake. Later in life he established a career as a portrait painter, which included painting the portraits of the British gentry, nobility and royalty.

He was the son of Thomas Richmond, miniature-painter, and was the father of the painter William Blake Richmond as well as the grandfather of the naval historian, Admiral Sir Herbert Richmond.

A keen follower of cricket, Richmond was noted in one obituary as having been "an habitué of Lord's since 1816".
mgt: George Richmond (28 March 1809 – 19 March 1896) was a prominent English portrait painter and member of the Royal Academy. Known for his skill in capturing the character of his sitters, Richmond produced portraits of many notable figures of the Victorian era, including William Wilberforce, Charlotte Brontë, and Charles Darwin. Early in his career, he was influenced by William Blake and was part of the Blake-inspired group "The Ancients." Over time, he transitioned to more conventional portraiture, achieving widespread acclaim. Richmond also contributed to sculpture and art education, and his works are held in major collections, including the National Portrait Gallery.

trgt:
Coupon Mountain was an e-commerce website operated based in Monrovia, California that displayed syndicated online deals. Founded in 2001 by Harry Tsao and Talmadge O'Neill, Coupon Mountain began as one of the two original website properties for MeziMedia, which was acquired by ValueClick for up to $352 million. MeziMedia was renamed to ValueClick Brands in 2010. In November 2013, ValueClick sold several of its online properties to IAC which, in addition to Coupon Mountain, included price-comparison website PriceRunner and investment education site Investopedia for $80 million. Coupon Mountain, along with PriceRunner, were being actively shopped in 2016 after slumping sales attributed to a change in Google's search algorithm but only PriceRunner was sold in March 2016 to Swedish firm NS Intressenter for around $100 million.

At its peak in 2008, Coupon Mountain held 14% of the total market-share for coupon websites and has been offline since October 2014.
mgt:
Coupon Mountain was an online coupon and deals platform founded in 2001 by Harry Tsao and Talmadge O'Neill, headquartered in Monrovia, California. The website provided free coupon codes and printable coupons through affiliate marketing partnerships with major retailers such as Target, Walmart, Kohl's, and Nordstrom. Unlike daily deal sites like Groupon, Coupon Mountain aggregated promotions and discounts from various merchants, resembling a digital version of newspaper circulars. 

The platform offered services in the United States and internationally, with versions in the United Kingdom, Germany, and China. Users could access deals without registration and subscribe to email notifications, newsletters, and RSS feeds. Coupon Mountain featured 16 shopping categories, including clothing, electronics, travel, and home goods. Recognized as a leading source for online deals, it was highlighted by *The Wall Street Journal* and *The Washington Post* in 2008. The company ceased operations in October 2014.

trgt: Richardt Strauss (born 28 January 1986) is a former professional rugby union player who played as a hooker. Born in Pretoria, South Africa, he began his career with the Free State Cheetahs and played for the Cheetahs in Super Rugby before moving to Ireland to play for Leinster Rugby in 2009. After completing three years' residence in Ireland, Strauss qualified to play for the Ireland national team; he made 17 appearances between 2012 and 2016, including three at the 2015 Rugby World Cup. He is the brother of South Africa international Andries Strauss and the cousin of South Africa international Adriaan Strauss.
mgt: Conrad Richardt Strauss (born 29 January 1986) is a retired professional rugby union player who played as a hooker. Born in Pretoria, South Africa, he began his career with the Free State Cheetahs, winning the 2006 Currie Cup and later playing for the Central Cheetahs in Super Rugby. In 2009, he joined Leinster Rugby, where he became a fan favorite and contributed to their Heineken Cup victories, including the 2011 final. Strauss represented Ireland internationally, earning his first cap in 2012 and playing in the 2015 Rugby World Cup. He retired from professional rugby at the end of the 2017–18 season.

trgt: The Zhdanov Doctrine (also called Zhdanovism or Zhdanovshchina; Lang-ru) was a Soviet cultural doctrine developed by Central Committee secretary Andrei Zhdanov in 1946. It proposed that the world was divided into two camps: the "imperialistic", headed by the United States; and "democratic", headed by the Soviet Union. The main principle of the Zhdanov Doctrine was often summarized by the phrase "The only conflict that is possible in Soviet culture is the conflict between good and best". Zhdanovism soon became a Soviet cultural policy, meaning that Soviet artists, writers and intelligentsia in general had to conform to the party line in their creative works. Under this policy, artists who failed to comply with the government's wishes risked persecution. The policy remained in effect until the death of Joseph Stalin in 1953.
mgt: The Zhdanov Doctrine, or Zhdanovism, was a Soviet cultural policy introduced by Andrei Zhdanov in 1946, emphasizing strict ideological conformity in the arts and promoting socialist realism. It sought to purge "bourgeois" and "cosmopolitan" influences, instead prioritizing Russian patriotism and cultural independence. The doctrine targeted prominent figures, including writers Mikhail Zoshchenko and Anna Akhmatova, and later extended to composers such as Dmitri Shostakovich and Sergei Prokofiev during the "anti-formalism campaign" of 1948. Zhdanovism also influenced Soviet-controlled Eastern Europe, enforcing socialist realism and fostering pro-Soviet intellectual movements. The policy was part of a broader effort to consolidate Soviet power and cultural dominance but was rolled back after Joseph Stalin's death, with many condemned artists rehabilitated in 1958.

trgt: Klinga Church (Lang-no) is a parish church of the Church of Norway in Namsos municipality in Trøndelag county, Norway. It is located in the village of Klinga. It is the church for the Klinga parish which is part of the Namdal prosti (deanery) in the Diocese of Nidaros. The white, wooden church was built in a long church style in 1866 using plans drawn up by the architects Ernst Kulaas and Jacob Wilhelm Nordan. The church seats about 270 people.
mgt: Klinga Church (Norwegian: *Klinga kirke*) is a parish church of the Church of Norway located in Namsos, Trøndelag, Norway. It serves the Klinga parish within the Namdal prosti in the Diocese of Nidaros. The wooden long church, designed by Ernst Kulaas and Jacob Wilhelm Nordan, was completed in 1866 and has a seating capacity of 270. The church replaced an earlier stave church from the 14th century, originally located in Sævik. Klinga Church is a listed Norwegian Cultural Heritage Site.

trgt:
The Sun Fast 1/2 Ton, also called the Jeanneau 1/2 Ton, is a French sailboat that was designed by Tony Castro as an International Offshore Rule Half Ton class racer and first built in 1984.

The Sun Fast 1/2 Ton's prototype was named Sun Fast and the design was the first boat in the Sun Fast sailboat range.

The even though only ten boats were built, the design was developed into the Arcadia 30 and the Sun Way 28.
mgt: The Sun Fast 1/2 Ton is a French racing keelboat designed by Tony Castro and built by Jeanneau between 1984 and 1986, with only ten boats produced. Constructed primarily of fiberglass with optional carbon fiber or Kevlar reinforcement, it features a fractional sloop rig, a fin keel, and an internally mounted spade-type rudder. The boat displaces 7,275 lb and has a draft of 5.48 ft. Designed for racing, it offers minimal accommodations for four people and is supported by an active Half Ton Class racing community.

trgt: Swallow Music Awards (Lang-hy, Tsitsernak yerazhshtakan mrcanakabashkhutiun) is an annual national music awards ceremony first held in Yerevan, Armenia, on 25 March 2017. The awards are created and sponsored by RA Ministry of Culture to appreciate and spur the activities of individuals and creators of various genres of Armenian musical art, as well as to display the significant achievements in the field of music to the broader layers of society. The awards consist of four parts: achievements in classical genre, folk genre, mass pop genre, and special/other awards. During the ceremony artists are given the awards designed by Armenian painter and designer Arman Nur. Each year, the award ceremony airs live on Public Television company of Armenia.
mgt: The Swallow Music Awards (Armenian: Ծիծեռնակ երաժշտական մրցանակաբաշխություն) is an annual Armenian music awards ceremony established in 2017 and presented by the Ministry of Culture of Armenia. Held at the Armenian National Academic Theatre of Opera and Ballet in Yerevan, the awards recognize excellence and outstanding achievement in various music genres, including classical, folk, and pop. Unlike other Armenian music awards, the Swallow Music Awards require artists to submit applications, with nominees determined based on these submissions. The awards have been hosted by prominent Armenian figures and have honored notable artists and contributors to Armenian music. However, the application process has sparked criticism from some artists.

trgt:
Kentucky Route 8 is a 114.202-mile-long (183.790 km) east–west state highway divided into two distinct segments across northern Kentucky. The western terminus of the route is at KY 237 near Francisville. The eastern terminus is at U.S. Route 23 in South Portsmouth. The two distinct segments of this route were not meant to be connected together. KY 8 from its west end in Boone County to Augusta in Bracken County is designated as a part of an identified corridor for bikes named the Ramblin' River Tour by the American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials (AASHTO).

KY 8 is named the Mary Ingles Highway for part of its length. It is rumored that she was the first white woman in Kentucky. Captured by Indians in Virginia in 1755 and taken to Ohio, she later escaped a salt-making party at Big Bone Lick and made her way across the Kentucky wilderness back home to Virginia.
mgt:
Kentucky Route 8 (KY 8) is a 114.2-mile (183.8 km) state highway in Kentucky, maintained by the Kentucky Transportation Cabinet (KYTC). The route is divided into two segments: the western segment, spanning 78.8 miles (126.8 km), runs from KY 237 near Francisville to US 62 in Maysville, while the eastern segment, 35.4 miles (56.9 km) long, extends from KY 57 near Concord to US 23 in South Portsmouth. Both segments primarily run parallel to the Ohio River.

The western segment passes through cities such as Covington, Newport, and Augusta, and has been rerouted in some areas due to landslides, including a section near Fort Thomas. The eastern segment, formerly part of KY 10, features narrow and gravel roads near its western terminus. KY 8 serves as a scenic route along the Ohio River, with portions designated for recreational use, such as the Ramblin' River Tour.

trgt:
