"Ambush" is the premiere episode of the fourth season of the American medical drama ER. Written by executive producer Carol Flint and director Thomas Schlamme, it was first broadcast on NBC on September 25, 1997, as a live episode, filmed twice for the East and West Coast.

Shown through the perspective of a PBS documentary film crew, the emergency room is filmed as a day in the life of the doctors. The episode introduced British actress Alex Kingston as Elizabeth Corday to the cast. Inspiration for the episode came from production staffers having worked in documentaries, with Anthony Edwards and George Clooney suggesting to do it live.

In its initial airing, "Ambush" received the highest rating of the week. Seen by a total of 42.71 million viewers, it became the most watched season premiere episode for a drama series. Reception to the episode was mixed from critics. Praise was given to the live performances but the story and script writing was criticized being lackluster. "Ambush" received four nominations at the 50th Primetime Emmy Awards, winning one for Outstanding Technical Direction, Camerawork, Video Control for a Series.
mgt:
"Ambush" is the first episode of the fourth season of the American medical drama *ER*, originally airing on NBC on September 25, 1997. Directed by Thomas Schlamme and written by Carol Flint, the episode is notable for being broadcast live, with separate performances for the East and West Coasts. The storyline follows a PBS documentary crew filming the staff at County General Hospital as they treat various patients, including a dog bite victim and a good Samaritan injured during a gang initiation. The episode also introduces British surgeon Elizabeth Corday (Alex Kingston) and explores tensions among the staff, including Mark Greene's (Anthony Edwards) reluctance to discuss his assault from the previous season and John Carter's (Noah Wyle) struggles with his internship.

"Ambush" was a technical milestone, utilizing 11 cameras and incorporating live elements, with minimal gaffes. It was the highest-rated television episode of the week, watched by 42.71 million viewers, making it the most-watched season premiere of a drama series in U.S. history. The episode received mixed critical reviews but was praised for its technical achievements, winning an Emmy for Outstanding Technical Direction.

trgt:
The Historical Illuminatus Chronicles is a series of three novels by Robert Anton Wilson written after his highly successful The Illuminatus! Trilogy and his 1981 Masks of the Illuminati. His co-author from the first trilogy, Robert Shea, was not involved in this series, providing only a praising blurb.

It is composed of three books: The Earth Will Shake (1982) ISBN 1-56184-162-5, The Widow's Son (1985) ISBN 1-56184-163-3, and Nature's God (1991) ISBN 1-56184-164-1. A fourth book, The World Turned Upside Down, was promised at the end of Nature's God but was never written; Wilson also had stated he intended the Chronicles to be a pentalogy. His death in 2007 left the series as a trilogy, incomplete. There is an audiobook of the first novel read by Scott Crisp.
mgt:
*The Historical Illuminatus Chronicles* is a series of historical novels by Robert Anton Wilson. The trilogy follows the adventures of Sigismundo Celine, an ancestor of Hagbard Celine from *The Illuminatus! Trilogy*, as he navigates Europe and America during the Enlightenment. The series explores themes of secret societies, mysticism, and historical intrigue, with Sigismundo encountering Freemasons, imprisonment, and mysterious captors who claim he is a descendant of Jesus Christ. The novels blend historical events with fictional elements, offering a satirical and philosophical take on history and power.

The series gained additional notoriety in 1989 when a mentally ill man, Kenneth Lamar Noid, held hostages at a Domino's Pizza and requested a copy of the second novel, *The Widow's Son*, during the incident.

trgt: Colt Keliikoa Lyerla (born November 13, 1992) is a former American arena football wide receiver and convicted felon. He played tight end at the University of Oregon for the Ducks but left in October 2013 due to personal reasons. He signed with the Green Bay Packers after going undrafted during the 2014 NFL Draft. He was a member of the Arizona Rattlers and Portland Steel of the Arena Football League. He also played for AS Carcassonne in the Elite One Championship rugby league.
mgt: Colt Lyerla (born November 13, 1992) is a former American football tight end who played college football at the University of Oregon. Known for his athleticism, he was a standout high school player in Oregon and earned national attention before committing to Oregon, where he played from 2011 to 2013. Lyerla went undrafted in the 2014 NFL Draft but briefly signed with the Green Bay Packers and later played in the Arena Football League. His career was overshadowed by legal troubles, including arrests for drug possession and other offenses.

trgt: The Mourne Mountains (/mɔːrn/ MORN; Lang-ga), also called the Mournes or Mountains of Mourne, are a granite mountain range in County Down in the south-east of Northern Ireland. They include the highest mountains in Northern Ireland, the highest of which is Slieve Donard at 850 m (2,790 ft). The Mournes are designated an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty and it has been proposed to make the area Northern Ireland's first national park. The area is partly owned by the National Trust and sees many visitors every year. The Mourne Wall crosses fifteen of the summits and was built to enclose the catchment basin of the Silent Valley and Ben Crom reservoirs.
mgt: The Mourne Mountains (Irish: na Beanna Boirche) are a granite mountain range in County Down, Northern Ireland, known for their natural beauty and cultural significance. The range's highest peak is Slieve Donard, standing at 850 meters (2,790 feet). The Mournes are renowned for their hiking trails, rock climbing, and scenic views, attracting visitors for outdoor activities and Duke of Edinburgh's Award expeditions. Notable features include the Mourne Wall, a 31.4 km dry stone wall built to protect Belfast's water supply, and reservoirs like Silent Valley and Ben Crom. The mountains have inspired literature, music, and film, including C.S. Lewis's *The Chronicles of Narnia* and HBO's *Game of Thrones*.

trgt:
William Vernell Wood Sr. (December 23, 1936 – February 3, 2020) was an American professional football player and coach. He played as a safety with the Green Bay Packers in the National Football League (NFL). Wood was an eight-time Pro Bowler and a nine-time All-Pro. In 1989, Wood was elected to the Pro Football Hall of Fame.

Wood played college football for the USC Trojans, becoming the first African-American quarterback to play in what is now the Pac-12 Conference. Undrafted out of USC, he was granted a try-out with Green Bay. Wood changed his position to safety in his rookie year, and played for the Packers from 1960 to 1971, winning five NFL championships. He later coached in the NFL, World Football League (WFL), and Canadian Football League (CFL).
mgt:
Willie Wood (December 23, 1936 – February 3, 2020) was an American professional football player and coach, best known for his career as a safety with the Green Bay Packers in the National Football League (NFL) from 1960 to 1971. An undrafted free agent, Wood became a nine-time All-NFL honoree, eight-time Pro Bowler, and a key player in the Packers' five NFL championships, including victories in Super Bowls I and II. He led the NFL in interceptions and punt return yards in 1962 and holds the record for the most consecutive starts by a safety in NFL history. 

After retiring, Wood became the first African American head coach in modern professional football with the Philadelphia Bell in 1975 and later coached in the CFL. Inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 1989, Wood passed away in 2020 at age 83.

trgt:
Denshaw is a village in the civil parish of Saddleworth in the Metropolitan Borough of Oldham, in Greater Manchester, England. It lies by the source of the River Tame, high amongst the Pennines above the village of Delph, 4.6 miles (7.4 km) northeast of Oldham,3.3 miles (5.3 km) north-northwest of Uppermill and Shaw and Crompton. It has a population of around 500.

Historically a part of the West Riding of Yorkshire, Denshaw and its surroundings have provided archaeological evidence of Stone and Bronze Age activity in the area. The name Denshaw is of Old Norse derivation, and the oldest part of the village is an ancient hamlet.

Built up around the junction of five major roads, until the 20th century, Denshaw consisted mainly of smallholdings and a few public houses such as the Junction Inn, originally built as a coaching house for travellers. Denshaw is noted for its annual Whit Friday brass band contest.
mgt:
Denshaw is a small village in the civil parish of Saddleworth, within the Metropolitan Borough of Oldham, Greater Manchester, England. Historically part of the West Riding of Yorkshire, it is situated in the South Pennines, approximately 11 miles northeast of Manchester. The village, with a population of around 500, is known for its rural character, traditional stone buildings, and historical significance, including evidence of Stone Age and Bronze Age activity. The name "Denshaw" is derived from Old Norse, indicating a settlement during the Danelaw period.

Denshaw did not experience significant industrialization during the Industrial Revolution, though it was home to a calico printing factory and a reservoir supporting local mills. The village is notable for landmarks such as Christ Church, a Grade II listed building, and its war memorial. Denshaw hosts an annual Whit Friday brass band contest and has a vibrant community centered around its village hall and primary school. It is accessible via local bus services and nearby motorways.

trgt: Big Thunder Ski Jumping Centre was a twin ski jumping hill located in Thunder Bay in Northwestern Ontario, Canada. It constitutes part of Big Thunder National Training Centre. The first hills were built by Knute and Thor Hansen and opened in 1963. They were originally known as Lille Norway Ski Area, then Mt. Norway Ski Area, and Sundance Northwest Resort before taking the current name. The large and normal hills were built in 1974 and the venue was taken over by the provincial government in 1985. The hills hosted 29 FIS Ski Jumping World Cup and 50 Canadian Ski Jumping Championships tournaments between 1975 and 1995, climaxing with the FIS Nordic World Ski Championships 1995. Funding was then cut and the venue has since been closed and unmaintained.
mgt: The Big Thunder Ski Jumping Centre was a ski jumping facility located on Mount McRae in Thunder Bay, Ontario, Canada. Opened in 1963 as Lille Norway Ski Area, it underwent several ownership and name changes before becoming a national training center in the 1980s. The venue featured K-120 and K-90 hills, along with smaller training hills, and hosted numerous international events, including the 1995 FIS Nordic World Ski Championships, its most notable competition. During the championships, Tommy Ingebrigtsen set a hill record of 137 meters. Big Thunder was a regular site for FIS Nordic Ski Jumping World Cup events from 1980 to 1991. Despite its closure in 1996 due to provincial budget cuts, efforts have been made to reopen the facility for year-round training and events.

trgt:
Grange Barn is a historic timber-framed building in Coggeshall, Essex, England. Grange Barn was built by the Cistercians in the 13th century to serve Coggeshall Abbey. It underwent significant structural alteration in the 14th century. It is Grade I listed.

The barn is 36.57 metres long, 13.71 metres wide and 10.67 metres high. For comparison, England's largest medieval barn, Harmondsworth Great Barn, is 58.55 metres long, 11.3 metres wide, and 11.9 metres high. The barn has a nave and two aisles. There are two midstreys (gabled porches) which would have provided access for wagons: these are 18th century.
mgt: Grange Barn, located in Coggeshall, Essex, is one of the oldest surviving timber-framed buildings in Europe, dating back to the 13th century. Originally part of Coggeshall Abbey, the barn remained in agricultural use until 1960, despite the dissolution of the abbey in the 1530s. After falling into disrepair, it was restored between 1982 and 1985 following efforts by a local group and Braintree District Council, earning a Europa Nostra award. Now owned by the National Trust, the Grade I listed barn is open to the public, featuring historical displays and hosting special events.

trgt:
Harris Academy Riverside is a sixth-form entry coeducational secondary school and sixth form located in the Purfleet in the Thurrock unitary authority, Essex, England. Purfleet is bordered by the A13 road to the north and the River Thames to the south and is within the easternmost part of the M25 motorway but just outside the Greater London boundary. As its name suggests, the school is on the riverside, but separated from the water by a railway line.

The school was established in 2017 but moved into a new dedicated building in 2019.
mgt: Harris Academy Riverside is a coeducational free school and sixth form located in Purfleet, Essex, England. Established in 2017, the school is part of the Harris Federation and serves students aged 11 to 18, with a capacity for 1,140 pupils. Its £23 million campus, designed by LSI Architects, includes a four-story teaching block and a two-story sports block, contributing to the regeneration of Purfleet town centre. The academy follows the National Curriculum and aims to prepare students for the English Baccalaureate (EBACC). It is part of the Chafford Hundred Sixth Form Consortium, specializing in mathematics and sciences.

trgt:
Hoang Anh Gia Lai Group (Lang-vi). Group also Hoang Anh Gia Lai Joint Stock Company (Lang-vi), abbreviated as HAGL or HAG) is a diversified company headquartered in Pleiku, Vietnam.

From a small furniture producer the company diversified into other industries such as rubber, finances a football club (Hoang Anh Gia Lai Club) and was Vietnam's largest property developer in 2010 and the second largest in 2011 (behind Vingroup). It has also grown significantly outside of Vietnam, with investments of around $1bn in Laos, hundreds of millions in Myanmar, $100m in Cambodia and tens of millions of dollars in Thailand.
mgt: Hoang Anh Gia Lai Group (HAGL) is a Vietnamese joint-stock company founded in 1990 by Đoàn Nguyên Đức and headquartered in Pleiku, Gia Lai. Initially a furniture manufacturer, HAGL has diversified into industries such as real estate, rubber, sugar, energy, mining, and football. It operates rubber plantations across Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia, and has expanded into hydroelectric and sugar production. The company is also active in real estate, including major projects in Vietnam, Laos, and Myanmar. HAGL has faced controversy over land disputes in Cambodia and Laos, with allegations of environmental and social violations involving indigenous communities.

trgt: Multiple complex developmental disorder (MCDD) is a research category, proposed to involve several neurological and psychological symptoms where at least some symptoms are first noticed during early childhood and persist throughout life. It was originally suggested to be a subtype of autistic spectrum disorders (PDD) with co-morbid schizophrenia or another psychotic disorder; however, there is some controversy that not everyone with MCDD meets criteria for both PDD and psychosis. The term multiplex developmental disorder was coined by Donald J. Cohen in 1986.
mgt: Multiple complex developmental disorder (MCDD) is a proposed neurodevelopmental condition characterized by a combination of psychotic, affective, behavioral, autistic, and neurological symptoms. It is not officially recognized in the DSM-V or ICD-10, and its diagnostic criteria remain unclear and debated. MCDD may serve as a diagnosis for individuals whose symptoms do not align with other specific disorders, though it has been criticized as vague. The condition is believed to have genetic causes, with affected individuals often displaying unique symptom profiles and familial links to neuropsychological disorders.

trgt:
A debug port is a chip-level diagnostic interface (akin to a computer port) included in an integrated circuit to aid design, fabrication, development, bootstrapping, configuration, debugging, and post-sale in-system programming. In general terms, a debug port is not necessary for end-use function and is often hidden or disabled in finished products.

When constituted in a high availability or safety critical system, a debug port may continue to operate as a system monitor for integrity validation in the finished product. In low-cost or high-volume electronics, debug ports are sometimes left enabled to avoid costs of design changes, and can be used by modders and hackers to gain control of the device and/or access to extra functionality.

Due to their high complexity, debug ports are nearly universal in microprocessors (CPUs) and microcontrollers (MCUs). These are discussed below.
mgt:
A debug port is an interface on microprocessors and microcontrollers that facilitates testing, debugging, and programming during development and manufacturing. Microprocessors, with their immense complexity, often include debug ports to manage their billions of transistors. Microcontrollers, which have evolved into system-on-a-chip (SoC) devices, also rely on debug ports for initial configuration, such as setting up clock trees or loading the first software program. These ports are critical for accessing internal flash memory and EEPROM-based configuration fuses.

Since around 2010, many microcontrollers have integrated USB peripherals with Device Firmware Upgrade (DFU) capabilities in factory ROM, appealing to hobbyists by eliminating the need for specialized hardware tools like JTAG. While DFU serves as a software-based debug interface, it lacks the robustness of dedicated hardware debug ports, as it depends on functional software to operate effectively.

trgt: The Port of Gaza is a small port near the Rimal district of Gaza City. It is the home port of Palestinian fishing-boats and the base of the Palestinian Naval Police, a branch of the Palestinian National Security Forces. Under the Oslo II Accord, the activities of the Palestinian Naval Police are restricted to 6 nautical miles from the coast. Since 2007, the Port of Gaza has been under an Israeli-imposed naval blockade as part of a blockade of the Gaza Strip, and activities at the port have been restricted to small-scale fishing.
mgt: The Port of Gaza, located in the Gaza Strip and operated by the Palestinian National Authority, serves as a small port primarily used by Palestinian fishing boats. Historically, the area was home to the ancient port of Maiuma, with archaeological finds such as Roman columns and artifacts discovered in recent years. Since 2007, the port has been under an Israeli naval blockade, with limited access for ships. Efforts to expand and develop the port, including plans for a larger seaport since the 1993 Oslo I Accord, have been hindered by the ongoing Israeli–Palestinian conflict.

trgt: Iris variegata, commonly known as the Hungarian iris, is a plant species in the genus Iris, also in the subgenus Iris. It is a rhizomatous perennial from eastern Europe. It has dark green, ribbed leaves. The branched flowering stems can be as tall as the leaves, they can hold 2–3 flowers in summer. They are yellowish-white, with brown-purple veins on the drooping falls. It is very hardy and it is commonly cultivated as an ornamental plant in temperate regions. There are several cultivars.
mgt: *Iris variegata*, commonly known as the Hungarian iris, is a species of flowering plant in the family Iridaceae, native to central and southeastern Europe. It is characterized by its stout rhizomes, dark green ribbed leaves, and distinctive yellowish-white flowers with brown-purple veins, which bloom in early summer. The species is diploid with a chromosome count of 2n=24 and has played a significant role in the development of modern bearded iris hybrids. *Iris variegata* thrives in open, stony areas and is considered endangered in parts of its native range.

trgt: The Republic of Lakotah or Lakotah is a proposed independent republic in North America for the Lakota people. Proposed in 2007 by activist Russell Means, the suggested territory would be enclaved by the borders of the United States, covering thousands of square miles in North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, Wyoming, and Montana. The proposed borders are those of the 1851 Treaty of Fort Laramie between the United States government and the Lakota. These lands are now occupied by Indian reservations and non-Native settlements. None of the existing Lakota tribal governments support the proposed republic, and they were not consulted about the proposal.
mgt: The Republic of Lakotah is a proposed independent nation declared by a group of Lakota activists, led by Russell Means, in December 2007. The group, known as the Lakota Freedom Delegation, claimed to withdraw from treaties with the United States, citing violations of agreements such as the Fort Laramie Treaties and the adverse effects of U.S. policies on the Lakota people, including poverty, unemployment, and cultural erosion. The proposed nation would encompass parts of five U.S. states and operate as a confederation with libertarian principles. The movement has not been recognized by the U.S. government, tribal governments, or the international community.

trgt: Minority Report is an American science fiction crime drama television series that aired on Fox from September 21 to November 30, 2015. It was developed by Max Borenstein and is a sequel adaptation of the 2002 film of the same name based on the 1956 science fiction short story "The Minority Report" by Philip K. Dick. It was produced by Amblin Television, Paramount Television (whose film studio co-owns the film via the pre-2005 DreamWorks library), and 20th Century Fox Television (whose film studio co-produced the film). It is the first Steven Spielberg–directed film to be adapted for television. On October 9, 2015, Fox announced that the series order was cut from 13 episodes to 10. Fox cancelled the series on May 13, 2016.
mgt: *Minority Report* is an American science fiction crime drama television series that aired on Fox from September 21 to November 30, 2015. Developed by Max Borenstein, the series is a sequel to the 2002 film *Minority Report* and is based on Philip K. Dick's 1956 short story of the same name. Set in 2065, 11 years after the events of the film, the show follows Dash, a Precog with the ability to predict crimes, as he teams up with Detective Lara Vega to prevent future crimes while keeping his abilities hidden. Despite its connection to the acclaimed film, the series received mixed reviews and was canceled after one season, consisting of 10 episodes.

page_title: World Athletics Race Walking Team Championships
trgt:
The World Athletics Race Walking Team Championships is a racewalking event organised by the International Association of Athletics Federations. It has been held since 1961, and generally on a biennial basis. Women first entered the 1979 edition. It was formerly known as the Lugano Cup after the city that hosted the first event, then became the IAAF World Race Walking Cup until 2016 and then IAAF World Race Walking Team Championships until 2018. In 2004, a junior division was added, consisting of men and women aged under 20. Since 2008 it has been a constituent meeting of the World Athletics Challenge – Race Walking.

From 1975 to 1997 was awarded Lugano Trophy for combined team (20K + 50K). Since 1993 the medals have been awarded for the single events of the 20K and 50K teams, therefore in the 1993, 1995 and 1997 editions three team medals were assigned, from 1999 the combined was abolished and the team medals remained two until the present day.
mgt:
The World Athletics Race Walking Team Championships, formerly known as the IAAF World Race Walking Cup, is a biennial international racewalking competition organized by World Athletics. Established in 1961, the event features individual and team races across various distances, with senior and junior categories for men and women. The competition also includes the Lugano Trophy, a team ranking that combines results from the 20 km and 50 km events.

The championships have faced challenges, including relocations due to political and health crises, such as the 2016 event being moved from Russia due to the suspension of the All-Russia Athletic Federation and the postponement of the 2020 edition in Belarus due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Doping violations have been a recurring issue, with several athletes disqualified over the years, including prominent medalists. Despite these challenges, the event remains a key fixture in the racewalking calendar, with the most recent edition held in Oman in 2022.

trgt:
Brian C.J. Moore FMedSci, FRS (born 10 February 1946) is an Emeritus Professor of Auditory Perception in the University of Cambridge and an Emeritus Fellow of Wolfson College, Cambridge. His research focuses on psychoacoustics, audiology, and the development and assessment of hearing aids (signal processing and fitting methods).

Moore is a fellow of the Royal Society, the Academy of Medical Sciences, the Acoustical Society of America, the Audio Engineering Society, the British Society of Audiology, the Association for Psychological Science, and the Belgian Society of Audiology, and the British Society of Hearing Aid Audiologists. He has written or edited 21 books and over 750 scientific papers and book chapters.
mgt: Brian C.J. Moore FRS FMedSci (born February 10, 1946) is a British psychologist and auditory scientist known for his contributions to psychoacoustics, speech perception, and audiology. He is Emeritus Professor of Experimental Psychology at the University of Cambridge and an Emeritus Fellow of Wolfson College, Cambridge. Moore's research has advanced understanding of pitch and loudness perception, auditory scene analysis, and the effects of hearing loss and aging on speech perception. He has developed influential models of loudness perception, diagnostic hearing tests, and hearing aid technologies. Moore has authored several widely cited books, including *An Introduction to the Psychology of Hearing*, and his work has influenced international standards in audiology.

trgt:
Sapporo Television Broadcasting, Co., Ltd. (札幌テレビ放送株式会社, Sapporo Terebi Hōsō Kabushiki Gaisha) is a TV station of Nippon News Network (NNN) and Nippon Television Network System (NNS) in Hokkaidō, Japan. Headquartered in Sapporo, the capital city of Hokkaidō prefecture, the TV station was established on April 8, 1958. It is usually called "STV" for short, which is used as a name in a number of TV programs.

Since December 15, 1962, the company has worked as both television and radio station until July 12, 2005, when the radio section was split into the STV Radio Broadcasting, Co., Ltd. (STVラジオ株式会社, STV Rajio Kabushiki Gaisha) was established. Currently, the STV Radio is a radio station of National Radio Network (NRN) in Hokkaidō, Japan. The license of the radio broadcasting was succeeded to the STV Radio, and started radio broadcasting on October 1, 2005.
mgt:
Sapporo Television Broadcasting Co., Ltd. (STV) is a private television and radio broadcasting company based in Sapporo, Japan. Founded on April 8, 1958, it began television broadcasts on April 1, 1959, initially as a dual affiliate of NNN and FNN. STV later became a founding member of NNN in 1966 and transitioned to color broadcasts the same year. It also played a key role in broadcasting the 1972 Sapporo Winter Olympics. 

STV launched its radio operations in 1962, becoming the only Japanese broadcaster to start with television before radio. The company introduced digital broadcasting in 2006 and ended analog broadcasts in 2011. Known for its innovative approaches, such as adopting an automatic advertising broadcast system, STV has consistently ranked first in TV ratings since 1993, holding the longest record for all-day ratings in Japan.

trgt:
Miniclip SA is a mobile game publisher and former browser game website that launched in 2001. It was started by Robert Small and Tihan Presbie with a budget of £40,000. In 2008, the company was valued at over £275 million.

In 2009, Miniclip hosted over 400 applications on its website.

In 2015, Tencent acquired majority stakes of Miniclip. In December 2016, Miniclip crossed 1 billion downloads across its mobile games on iOS, Android, and Windows devices. In March 2022, Miniclip announced that it had reached 4 billion downloads, with 8 Ball Pool alone accounting for 1 billion.

In April 2022, Miniclip announced that it would begin prioritizing its mobile games. As a result, the browser game portal was shut down in July 2022 and the website lost all but its two most popular games, Agar.io and 8 Ball Pool.

In June 2022, Miniclip acquired SYBO, the publisher of Subway Surfers, in an undisclosed deal.
mgt:
Miniclip is a Swiss-based online gaming company founded on March 30, 2001, and headquartered in Neuchâtel, Switzerland. It operates as a subsidiary of Tencent, which acquired the company in 2015. Miniclip is known for developing and publishing a wide range of mobile games, including popular titles such as *8 Ball Pool*, *Golf Battle*, *Agar.io*, and *Mini Militia*. The platform supports multiple languages and offers games for iOS, Android, and other operating systems.

In addition to mobile gaming, Miniclip has developed games for Xbox on Windows 8, such as *Gravity Guy* and *Monster Island*, and released its first game for Xbox One, PC, and PlayStation 4, *MX Nitro*, in 2017. However, the company has faced criticism for security issues, including reports of malware associated with its downloads in the mid-2000s. Despite these challenges, Miniclip remains an active and influential player in the online gaming industry.

trgt: The Underground Railroad is a historical fiction novel by American author Colson Whitehead, published by Doubleday in 2016. The alternate history novel tells the story of Cora, a slave in the Antebellum South during the 19th century, who makes a bid for freedom from her Georgia plantation by following the Underground Railroad, which the novel depicts as a rail transport system with safe houses and secret routes. The book was a critical and commercial success, hitting the bestseller lists and winning several literary awards, including the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction, the National Book Award for Fiction, the Arthur C. Clarke Award, and the 2017 Andrew Carnegie Medal for Excellence. A TV miniseries adaptation, written and directed by Barry Jenkins, was released in May 2021.
mgt:
*The Underground Railroad* is a 2016 novel by American author Colson Whitehead. It reimagines the historical Underground Railroad as a literal subterranean train system used by enslaved people to escape to freedom. The story follows Cora, an enslaved woman in Georgia, as she flees northward, encountering various dangers and moral dilemmas along the way. The novel explores themes of slavery, freedom, and systemic racism, drawing parallels to historical and contemporary issues.

The book received widespread critical acclaim, winning the 2017 Pulitzer Prize for Fiction, the 2016 National Book Award for Fiction, and other honors. It was also selected for Oprah's Book Club. In 2021, a television adaptation directed by Barry Jenkins was released on Amazon Prime Video.

trgt:
Län (Swedish, IPA-sv), lääni (Finnish, IPA-fi) and len (Norwegian, IPA-no) refer to the administrative divisions used in Sweden and previously in Finland and Norway. The provinces of Finland were abolished on January 1, 2010. In Norway, the term was in use between 1308 and 1662.

They are also sometimes used in other countries, especially as a translation of the Russian word volost. During the period when Finland was a part of the Russian Empire (1809-1917), when Russian was made an official language alongside Swedish, it was synonymous with the word guberniya.
mgt: A *län* is an administrative division historically used in Sweden and Finland, often translated as "county" in English. The term, meaning "fief," reflects a shared administrative tradition between the two countries until 1809. In Sweden, *län* serve as extensions of the national government, lacking autonomy or legislative power, and differ from traditional provinces (*landskap*). Each *län* was overseen by a governor (*landshövding*), appointed by the government, and a locally elected assembly (*landsting*), which managed services like healthcare and transport until replaced by regions in 2020. Finland abolished *län* in 2010.

trgt:
Banjara Hills is an urban commercial centre and one of the most affluent neighbourhoods in Hyderabad, Telangana, India. This is an upmarket locality close to Jubilee Hills. This area was a hilly forest and was least inhabited in the past. Only few royal members of the Nizam's dynasty lived here, which was a hunting ground for them. Even with its history and status, this area now has completely been transformed to an urban commercial centre consisting of an array of high-end hotels, restaurants, night clubs and office buildings of global corporations. Banjara Hills is segregated by its road numbers, with each road having its own importance: the numbers start from 1 and end at 14.

Banjara Hills is considered the most expensive zip code in India according to Economic Times magazine and along with Jubilee Hills, is the most prestigious borough/city in the greater Hyderabad area to live in. Economic Times estimated that properties in Banjara Hills were worth "a whopping Rs 96,000 crore", an equivalent to US$20.7 billion (As of 8 September 2011).

The much neglected Banjara Lake is also located here.
mgt:
Banjara Hills is an upscale neighborhood in Hyderabad, Telangana, India, known for its luxurious lifestyle, commercial establishments, and historical significance. Originally developed in 1927 by Nawab Mehdi Nawaz Jung, a minister in the court of the last Nizam, the area was named after the Banjaras, its original inhabitants. The Nawab's residence, Banjara Bhavan, inspired by Antoni Gaudí's works, hosted notable figures like Jawaharlal Nehru and Rabindranath Tagore.

The area is renowned for its star hotels, including Taj Krishna, Taj Deccan, and Taj Banjara, as well as its diverse dining options and shopping malls like GVK One and City Center. Key landmarks include Jalagam Vengal Rao Park, KBR Park, and the 400-year-old Svayambhu Sri Lakshmi Narasimha Swamy Temple. Educational institutions like Muffakham Jah College of Engineering and Technology are also located here.

Banjara Hills is well-connected by TSRTC buses and nearby MMTS train stations, with improved infrastructure easing traffic congestion. Adjacent localities include Jubilee Hills, Somajiguda, and Panjagutta, making it a prominent hub in Hyderabad.

trgt: Rifle bedding is a process of providing a rigid and consistent foundation for a rifle’s operational components, by creating a stable and close-fitting bearing surface between the gun’s functional parts (i.e. the receiver housing the barrelled action) and its support part (i.e. the stock) that do not deform with pressure or shift under the shear stress of the recoil from firing. This is for the goal of accurizing the rifle and (to a lesser extent) prolonging the life of the stock. The bedding process is often an aftermarket improvement.
mgt: Rifle bedding is a process used to improve the accuracy and longevity of a rifle by creating a stable and consistent interface between the rifle's action and stock. Bedding reduces stress and flexing in the action, prevents movement within the stock, and helps maintain the rifle's zero. It also protects the stock from wear caused by repeated recoil forces. Common methods include glass bedding, pillar bedding, and the use of bedding blocks or chassis systems. Proper execution is critical, as improper bedding can damage or destroy the rifle.

trgt:
Deccani Architecture refers to the architectural styles developed during the Deccan sultanate period. The Deccan sultanates were five dynasties that ruled late medieval kingdoms, namely, Bijapur, Golkonda, Ahmadnagar, Bidar, and Berar in south-western India. The Deccan sultanates were located on the Deccan Plateau. Their architecture was a regional variant of Indo-Islamic architecture, heavily influenced by the styles of the Delhi Sultanate and later Mughal architecture, but sometimes also directly from Persia and Central Asia.

The rulers of five Deccan sultanates had a number of cultural contributions to their credit in the fields of art, music, literature and architecture. Deccan sultanates have constructed many grand and impregnable forts. Bidar and Golconda forts are classic example of military planning of Deccan sultanates. Apart from forts, they have constructed many tombs, mosques and madrasas. Gol Gumbaz (tomb of Mohammed Adil Shah), was the second largest dome in the world.

In 2014, UNESCO put a group of buildings on its "tentative list" to become a World Heritage Site under the name Monuments and Forts of the Deccan Sultanate (despite there being a number of different sultanates). These are:
mgt:
The architecture of the Deccan sultanates, which flourished in southern India between the 14th and 17th centuries, is renowned for its distinctive blend of Persian, Indian, and Islamic styles. This architectural legacy is evident in the monuments of five key sultanates: Ahmadnagar, Bidar, Bijapur, Golkonda, and Gulbarga.

Ahmadnagar is home to palaces like Farah Bakhsh Bagh and tombs of nobles and saints, such as Salabat Khan and Shah Sharif. Bidar features the grand Bidar Fort, the Mahmud Gawan Madrasa, and the Bahmani and Barid Shahi tombs. Bijapur is celebrated for the Gol Gumbaz, with the second-largest pre-modern dome, and the elegant Ibrahim Rouza. Other notable structures include the Jami Masjid and several palaces.

Golkonda is famed for its impregnable fort, the Qutb Shahi tombs, and Hyderabad's iconic Char Minar. Gulbarga, the Bahmani capital, boasts the Gulbarga Fort, the Great Mosque, and the Haft Gumbad tomb complex. Collectively, these monuments reflect the artistic and engineering achievements of the Deccan sultanates.

trgt:
Kirkthorpe hydro is a hydroelectric generating plant located on the River Calder at Kirkthorpe Weir, 4 miles (6.4 km) east of the City of Wakefield in West Yorkshire, England. The plant was opened in 2017 and expects to be generating electricity for 100 years. Kirkthorpe Weir is the highest industrial weir in Yorkshire and has prevented fish passing upstream to spawn; the new hydro project has a fish pass built into it.

At a nameplate capacity of 500 kW, along with another plant at Brotherton on the River Aire, the plant is jointly the largest hydroelectric generator in Yorkshire.
mgt: Kirkthorpe Hydro is a hydroelectric power station located on the River Calder in Kirkthorpe, Wakefield, England. Built on the grade II listed Kirkthorpe Weir, the facility was constructed by Barn Energy at a cost of £5.3 million and became operational in March 2017. It has a capacity of 500 kW, generating 2.3 million units of electricity annually, enough to power over 800 homes. The site includes a fish pass, eel pass, and a 1,200 kWh lithium-ion battery for energy storage. In 2019, Kirkthorpe Hydro was sold to JLEN as part of a £4.3 million deal.

trgt:
Ottery St Mary, known as "Ottery", is a town and civil parish in the East Devon district of Devon, England, on the River Otter, about 10 miles (16 km) east of Exeter on the B3174. At the 2001 census, the parish, which includes the villages of Metcombe, Fairmile, Alfington, Tipton St John, Wiggaton, and (until 2017) West Hill, had a population of 7,692. The population of the urban area alone at the 2011 census was 4,898.

There are two electoral wards in Ottery (Rural and Town). The total population of both wards, including the adjacent civil parish of Aylesbeare, at the 2011 census was 9,022.

The town as it now stands has several independent shops, mainly in Mill Street, Silver Street and Yonder Street. An area known as 'The Square', is the heart of Ottery St Mary. There are pubs, restaurants, and coffee and tea rooms. Ottery provides services, employment, and a wide range of shopping for local residents and visitors from nearby villages and towns.
mgt:
Ottery St Mary is a historic town and civil parish in East Devon, England, situated on the River Otter. With a population of 4,898 as of 2011, the town is known for its rich history, notable architecture, and unique traditions. Its name derives from the river and its association with St Mary, dating back to its mention in the Domesday Book of 1086. The parish church of St Mary, often compared to a "miniature Exeter Cathedral," features a painted ceiling, medieval misericords, and the Ottery St Mary Astronomical Clock, one of the oldest mechanical clocks in England.

The town is renowned for its annual Tar Barrels event, a centuries-old tradition where flaming barrels are carried through the streets, and Pixie Day, which celebrates a local legend. Ottery is also the birthplace of poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge. Other landmarks include The King's School, founded in 1335, and The Chanter's House, a historic residence tied to the Coleridge family. The town remains a vibrant cultural and historical hub in Devon.

trgt:
The economy of governments covers the systems for setting levels of taxation, government budgets, the money supply and interest rates as well as the labour market, national ownership, and many other areas of government interventions into the economy.

Most factors of economic policy can be divided into either fiscal policy, which deals with government actions regarding taxation and spending, or monetary policy, which deals with central banking actions regarding the money supply and interest rates.

Such policies are often influenced by international institutions like the International Monetary Fund or World Bank as well as political beliefs and the consequent policies of parties.
mgt: Economic policy refers to the strategies and actions implemented by governments and central banks to influence a nation's economy. It encompasses various types, including macroeconomic stabilization policies aimed at managing inflation, unemployment, and economic growth, as well as tools like interest rates, taxation, and government spending. Economic policy can be categorized into demand-side and supply-side approaches, as well as discretionary and rule-based frameworks, each with distinct advantages and challenges. Historically, economic policy has evolved from early taxation and trade practices to modern fiscal and monetary strategies. Recent trends emphasize evidence-based policymaking, incorporating experimental and econometric studies to guide decisions.

trgt: Age of War is the debut studio album by Pakistani progressive rock band Black Hour, released on 25 October 2011 in the United States by Yourlabel Records, and in Pakistan by Afterwind Records. It was reissued in 2020 as a remastered version by Rearts Records. The recording sessions occurred at the 11/8 Studios in Islamabad, Pakistan with production by Black Hour alongside Jonathan Jones. The album sold over 10,000 copies worldwide and helped the band gain success both locally and internationally.
mgt: *Age of War* is the debut studio album by the Pakistani heavy metal band Black Hour, released on October 25, 2011, through Yourlabel Records in the United States and Afterwind Records in Pakistan. Recorded between 2009 and 2011 at 11/8 Studios in Islamabad, the album features a blend of heavy metal and progressive rock. It gained critical acclaim and sold over 10,000 copies worldwide, establishing the band’s presence in both local and international music scenes. The album includes the single "Crucifix."

trgt:
Piero di Cosimo (2 January 1462 – 12 April 1522), also known as Piero di Lorenzo, was an Italian painter of the Renaissance.

He is most famous for the mythological and allegorical subjects he painted in the late Quattrocento; he is said to have abandoned these to return to religious subjects under the influence of Savonarola, the preacher who exercised a huge sway in Florence in the 1490s, and had a similar effect on Botticelli. The High Renaissance style of the new century had little influence on him, and he retained the straightforward realism of his figures, which combines with an often whimsical treatment of his subjects to create the distinctive mood of his works.

Vasari has many stories of his eccentricity, and the mythological subjects have an individual and quirky fascination. He trained under Cosimo Rosselli, whose daughter he married, and assisted him in his Sistine Chapel frescos.

He was also influenced by Early Netherlandish painting, and busy landscapes feature in many works, often forests seen close at hand. Several of his most striking secular works are in the long "landscape" format used for paintings inset into cassone wedding chests or spalliera headboards or panelling. He was apparently famous for designing the temporary decorations for Carnival and other festivities.
mgt:
Piero di Cosimo (2 January 1462 – 12 April 1522) was an Italian painter of the High Renaissance, known for his eccentric personality and innovative works that combined mythological, religious, and naturalistic themes. Born in Florence as the son of a goldsmith, he trained under Cosimo Rosselli, assisting him in the Sistine Chapel frescoes. Piero was influenced by Netherlandish naturalism, particularly the works of Hugo van der Goes, and incorporated detailed landscapes, flora, and fauna into his art. His mythological paintings, such as *Perseus Freeing Andromeda* and *The Death of Procris*, often featured hybrid creatures and themes inspired by Vitruvius' accounts of early human evolution.

Piero was also a skilled portraitist, with his depiction of Simonetta Vespucci being among his most celebrated works. Despite his artistic achievements, he was known for his reclusive and eccentric lifestyle, as described by Giorgio Vasari, including his fear of thunderstorms and peculiar eating habits. Later in life, under the influence of Girolamo Savonarola, he turned to religious art, producing works like *The Immaculate Conception with Saints*. Piero's students included Andrea del Sarto, and his work influenced many contemporaries. He died of the plague in Florence in 1522 and is featured in George Eliot's novel *Romola*.

trgt: Elizabeth Melise Jow, known professionally as Malese Jow and Melise, is an American actress. She is known for her roles as Geena Fabiano on the Nickelodeon sitcom Unfabulous, Lucy Stone on the Nickelodeon musical sitcom Big Time Rush and as Anna, a teenage vampire on The CW's fantasy drama The Vampire Diaries. She starred as Julia Yeung in the short-lived CW science fiction romantic drama Star-Crossed. Jow starred as Mareth Ravenlock in season two of The Shannara Chronicles. She voiced Dupli-Kate in the Amazon Prime Video animated series Invincible.
mgt: Malese Jow (born Elizabeth Melise Jow) is an American actress known for her roles in television and film. Born in Tulsa, Oklahoma, she began her career as a child, appearing on *Barney* and later gaining recognition as Geena Fabiano on Nickelodeon's *Unfabulous* (2004–2007). Jow has appeared in various TV shows, including *The Vampire Diaries*, *Big Time Rush*, and *The Flash*, where she portrayed Linda Park. She has also starred in films such as *Bratz: The Movie* and *Escape Plan: The Extractors*. Jow is of European, Cherokee, and Chinese descent.

trgt: Urbanus is a Flemish celebrity comic strip created by Willy Linthout and loosely based on Flemish comedian and singer Urbanus. The stories are written by Willy Linthout, Urbanus and Ann Smets, and drawn by Linthout. The first story was published in 1982 and is still in syndication as of this day, being the longest-running and most successful Flemish comic strip based on a celebrity. It is also the longest-running celebrity comic in the world made by the same writer/artist team. The series sells well in the Netherlands too, due to Urbanus' popularity there.
mgt: *Urbanus* is a Belgian comic strip created by Willy Linthout in collaboration with comedian Urbanus. First published on November 10, 1982, the series ran until October 26, 2022, spanning 201 stories. The comic portrays an absurd, black-humored, and often vulgar version of Urbanus as a mischievous child in the fictionalized village of Tollembeek. Known for its simple art style and use of Flemish dialect, the series gained popularity, with its first album selling 50,000 copies. It inspired animated adaptations, a feature film, and statues commemorating the characters in Tollembeek and Middelkerke.

trgt:
Pakistan's second Presidential Elections were held on 2 January 1965. The voting was to be indirect, as the President had to be elected by the 80,000 "basic democrats", who made up the Electoral College. These basic Democrats were basically democratically elected public representatives who served in the Divisional, District, Tehsil or Union councils.

There were two major parties contesting the elections: the Convention Muslim League and the Combined Opposition Parties. The Combined Opposition Parties consisted of five major opposition parties, and had a nine-point program, which included restoration of parliamentary democracy and introduction of direct elections and adult franchise. The Combined Opposition Parties had no single leadership and were therefore, unable to select a presidential candidate from amongst themselves. Hence, they chose Fatima Jinnah as their candidate who was seen as an undisputed leader due to her relationship with Muhammad Ali Jinnah. Meanwhile, the candidate for Convention Muslim League was the incumbent President Mohammed Ayub Khan.and other party Muhammad Irfan sameja
mgt:
The 1965 Pakistani presidential election was held on January 2, 1965, under the Basic Democracy System, where an electoral college of 80,000 Basic Democrats voted to elect the president. The election saw incumbent President Ayub Khan of the Convention Muslim League (CML) face Fatima Jinnah, sister of Pakistan's founder Muhammad Ali Jinnah, along with two independent candidates. Ayub Khan, who had come to power through a military coup in 1958 and later consolidated his rule with the 1962 Constitution, leveraged his economic reforms and political influence to secure victory. Despite losing the popular vote, Ayub won 62.43% of the electoral vote, aided by faithless electors and strong rural support.

Fatima Jinnah, though popular in urban centers like Karachi and Dhaka, faced significant challenges, including limited campaign resources, indirect elections, and opposition from some religious clerics. The election, marred by allegations of rigging, sparked protests but also marked a turning point for women's political participation in Pakistan, inspiring greater involvement in politics.

trgt: Jefferson Joseph DeBlanc (February 15, 1921 – November 22, 2007) was an American World War II Marine Corps fighter pilot and flying ace, credited with shooting down nine Japanese aircraft during two tours of duty in the Pacific at Guadalcanal and Okinawa. On January 31, 1943, despite mechanical problems with his aircraft, he pressed forward with a mission and shot down five Japanese aircraft in minutes before being shot down himself. DeBlanc parachuted to safety and was rescued by native islanders, traded for a sack of rice, and picked up by a Navy seaplane. He received the Medal of Honor for his actions that day. He served in the Marine Corps Reserve until 1972. In that time, he also taught school and completed graduate and doctoral degrees in math, physics and education.
mgt:
Jefferson Joseph DeBlanc (February 15, 1921 – November 22, 2007) was a United States Marine Corps fighter pilot and Medal of Honor recipient for his actions during World War II. Born in Lockport, Louisiana, he enlisted in the U.S. Navy Reserve in 1941 before joining the Marine Corps in 1942. DeBlanc became an "ace in a day" on January 31, 1943, during a mission over the Solomon Islands, where he shot down five Japanese aircraft despite critical fuel issues and was later rescued after bailing out near enemy territory.

After the war, DeBlanc pursued a career in education, earning multiple degrees and teaching math and physics. He retired as a colonel from the Marine Corps Reserve in 1972. DeBlanc passed away in 2007 and was the last surviving Medal of Honor recipient from Louisiana for World War II.

trgt:
Mam Tor is a 517 m (1,696 ft) hill near Castleton in the High Peak of Derbyshire, England. Its name means "mother hill", so called because frequent landslips on its eastern face have resulted in a multitude of "mini-hills" beneath it. These landslips, which are caused by unstable lower layers of shale, also give the hill its alternative name of Shivering Mountain.

In 1979, the continual battle to maintain the A625 road (Sheffield to Chapel en le Frith) on the crumbling eastern side of the hill was lost when the road officially closed as a through-route, with the Fox House to Castleton section of the road being re-designated as the A6187.

The hill is crowned by a late Bronze Age and early Iron Age univallate hill fort, and two Bronze Age bowl barrows. At the base of the Tor and nearby are four show caves: Blue John Cavern, Speedwell Cavern, Peak Cavern and Treak Cliff Cavern where lead, Blue John, fluorspar and other minerals were once mined. Mam Tor was declared to be one of the Seven Wonders of the Peak by Thomas Hobbes in his 1636 book De Mirabilibus Pecci.

Simon Jenkins rates the panorama from Kinder Scout to Stanage Edge as one of the top ten in England.
mgt:
Mam Tor is a 517-meter (1,696 ft) hill in Derbyshire, England, located in the Peak District. Known as the "Shivering Mountain" due to its active landslides, it lies on the boundary between the Dark Peak and White Peak regions, overlooking the Hope Valley and Edale. The hill is geologically significant, composed of Carboniferous-age shales and sandstones, with a rotational landslide on its southeastern side that has been active for approximately 4,000 years. This landslide has caused repeated damage to the old Mam Tor road, which was eventually closed in 1979.

Mam Tor is also of historical importance, featuring a late Bronze Age and early Iron Age hill fort at its summit, encircled by ramparts and containing evidence of occupation dating back to around 1200 BC. The site includes Bronze Age burial mounds and remains of timber hut platforms. Today, Mam Tor is a popular destination for walkers, forming part of the Great Ridge, which separates the Hope Valley from Edale. Its striking geological features and rich history make it a prominent landmark in the Peak District.

trgt:
NFL Blitz is a series of American football themed video games originally released by Midway featuring National Football League (NFL) teams. It began as a 1997 arcade game NFL Blitz that was ported to home consoles and spawned a series of sequels. Rather than being designed as a realistic interpretation of the sport of football, like Madden NFL or NFL 2K, the Blitz series was created as an over-the-top, exaggerated version of the sport, inspired by Midway's own NBA Jam basketball games.

In 2005, after losing the NFL license, Midway relaunched the Blitz series as Blitz: The League, depicting fictional players and teams in a fictional league with slightly more realistic (though still exaggerated) on-field play and a focus on the seedy behind-the-scenes lives of the players.

Following the dissolution of Midway, EA Sports acquired the rights to the Blitz name and relaunched the series on PlayStation Network and Xbox Live with a new entry in the series simply titled NFL Blitz. The game took the series back to the original style of Blitz, but removed the late hits due to input by the NFL.
mgt:
*NFL Blitz* is a series of arcade-style American football video games developed by Midway Games and later EA Sports. First released in 1997, the series is known for its fast-paced, over-the-top gameplay that deviates from traditional NFL rules, featuring smaller teams, exaggerated tackles, and a focus on aggressive, theatrical play. Early entries allowed for late hits, excessive celebrations, and wrestling-style moves, though these elements were toned down in later releases due to NFL restrictions. The series also introduced unique mechanics, such as 30-yard first downs and high-scoring, fast-paced matches.

After losing the NFL license in 2005, Midway released *Blitz: The League*, which featured fictional teams, mature themes, and a story mode, earning the franchise its first "Mature" ESRB rating. Following Midway's bankruptcy in 2009, EA Sports acquired the rights and released a reboot in 2012, returning to the arcade-style roots.

The series also included a spinoff, *Kurt Warner's Arena Football Unleashed* (2000), based on the Arena Football League. *NFL Blitz* received positive critical reception, particularly for its unique gameplay and departure from traditional football simulations.

trgt:
In polymer chemistry, condensation polymers are any kind of polymers whose process of polymerization involves a condensation reaction (i.e. a small molecule, such as water or methanol, is produced as a byproduct). Condensation polymers are formed by polycondensation, when the polymer is formed by condensation reactions between species of all degrees of polymerization, or by condensative chain polymerization, when the polymer is formed by sequential addition of monomers to an active site in a chain reaction. The main alternative forms of polymerization are chain polymerization and polyaddition, both of which give addition polymers.

Polycondensation A polymerization in which the growth of polymer chains proceeds by condensation reactions between molecules of all degrees of polymerization. Notes: 1. The growth steps are expressed by: Px+Py→Px+y+L {x}∈{1,2,.∞};{y}∈{1,2,.∞}

where Px and Py denote chains of degrees of polymerization x and y, respectively, and L a low-molar-mass by-product. 2. The earlier term 'polycondensation' was synonymous with 'condensation polymerization'. The current definitions of polycondensation and condensative chain polymerization were both embraced by the earlier term 'polycondensation'.

Condensation polymerization is a form of step-growth polymerization. Linear polymers are produced from bifunctional monomers, i.e. compounds with two reactive end-groups. Common condensation polymers include polyamides, polyacetals, and proteins.
mgt:
Condensation polymers are a class of polymers formed through a condensation reaction, where monomers combine and release small molecules such as water as byproducts. Two significant types of condensation polymers are polyamides and polyesters. Polyamides, such as nylons and proteins, are produced through the reaction of carboxylic acids with amines, with water as a byproduct. For example, nylon 66 is synthesized from diamines and dicarboxylic acids, releasing two water molecules per repeat unit. Polyesters, such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET), are formed through the reaction of carboxylic acids with alcohols.

Condensation polymers are notable for their environmental and safety characteristics. They tend to be more biodegradable than addition polymers due to the presence of hydrolyzable bonds, such as peptide or ester linkages, which can be broken down by catalysts or bacterial enzymes. This property makes them more environmentally friendly compared to many other types of synthetic polymers.

trgt: Hulu Selangor is a district in Selangor, Malaysia. It contains the towns of Serendah, Batang Kali and Kuala Kubu Bharu. Its principal town is Kuala Kubu Bharu. The district is located in the northeastern part of Selangor and borders the state of Perak to the north, Pahang to the east, Sabak Bernam district to the northwest, Kuala Selangor district to its southwest and Gombak district to the south. Selangor River sources from this area, hence giving the district's name.
mgt: The Hulu Selangor District (Malay: Daerah Hulu Selangor) is a district in the state of Selangor, Malaysia, with its administrative seat in Kuala Kubu Bharu. Covering an area of 1,756.30 km², it is known for its natural beauty, including forests, mountainous terrain, and the Titiwangsa mountain range. Granted municipal status in 2021, the district is governed by the Hulu Selangor Municipal Council. Its economy is driven by manufacturing, aerospace, and tourism, with ongoing developments like the Serendah High-Value Manufacturing Park and the proposed Serendah-Port Klang rail line.

trgt: The Ligier JS29 was a Formula One car designed by Michel Tétu and Michel Beaujon for the Ligier team for use in the 1987 season. It was originally developed for use with an Alfa Romeo turbo power plant but prior to the start of the season, Ligier lost the use of the engine. The car had to be re-designed around a Megatron Straight 4 turbo engine. Redesignated the JS29B, it scored a single point during the season when driver René Arnoux finished 6th in the Belgian Grand Prix. Later in the season, the car was further refined to a JS29C specification.
mgt: The Ligier JS29 was a Formula One car designed by Michel Tétu, Claude Galopin, and their team for the 1987 season. Initially intended to use a turbocharged Alfa Romeo engine, the project was abandoned after lead driver René Arnoux criticized the engine, prompting Fiat to withdraw support. Ligier secured Megatron turbo engines, redesigning the car as the JS29B and later the JS29C to address compatibility issues. Debuting at the San Marino Grand Prix, the car struggled with reliability and performance, scoring only one point that season. The JS29C marked Ligier's final turbo-powered car, succeeded by the naturally aspirated JS31 in 1988.

trgt: Tales of Zestiria the X (テイルズ オブ ゼスティリア ザ X, Teiruzu Obu Zesutiria Za Kurosu, pronounced "Tales of Zestiria the Cross") is a Japanese anime television series based on both Tales of Zestiria and Tales of Berseria video games developed by Bandai Namco Studios and tri-Crescendo, and published by Bandai Namco Entertainment. It is produced by Ufotable, directed by Haruo Sotozaki and written by Hikaru Kondo, featuring character designs by Akira Matsushima and music by Motoi Sakuraba and Go Shiina. The anime series is split into two halves: the first half premiered on July 3, 2016 and finished airing on September 25, 2016. The second half premiered on January 8, 2017 and finished airing on April 29, 2017.
mgt: *Tales of Zestiria the X* (テイルズ オブ ゼスティリア ザ X) is an anime television series adaptation of the video game *Tales of Zestiria*, produced by Ufotable. Directed by Haruo Sotozaki and written by Ufotable staff, the series aired in two parts from July 3, 2016, to April 29, 2017, totaling 26 episodes. The story is set in a world where humans and mystical beings called Seraphim once coexisted, but malevolence has turned creatures into monsters, threatening the balance. The protagonist, a young man raised among Seraphim, dreams of restoring harmony. The series features music by Motoi Sakuraba and Go Shiina, with themes by Flow, Fhána, and Minami Kuribayashi. It is licensed by Crunchyroll and Madman Entertainment.

trgt: Caserma Ederle (Camp Ederle) is a military complex in Vicenza, Italy, where the United States Army has troops stationed. It is under Italian military control and can be managed anytime by the Italian authorities. The Vicenza Military Community is composed of soldiers, family members, civilians and retirees with a small number of airmen and sailors also stationed there. The post serves as the headquarters of United States Army Africa and the 173rd Airborne Brigade. Caserma Ederle serves as the headquarters of U.S. Army Garrison Italy of the United States Army Installation Management Command, an umbrella for all U.S. military properties in Vicenza. The post is named after Major Carlo Ederle, an Italian hero of World War I and recipient of the French Croix de Guerre (French War Cross), among other military honors.
mgt:
Caserma Ederle is a United States Army base located in Vicenza, Italy, serving as a key installation for U.S. military operations in Europe. Established in 1955 as part of the U.S. Army Southern European Task Force (SETAF), the base has played a significant role in NATO operations and Cold War defense strategies, including the management of nuclear weapons stockpiles and regional tactical operations. Over the years, it has housed various infantry and support units, including elements of the 173rd Airborne Brigade Combat Team and the United States Army Africa headquarters.

The base provides extensive amenities for military personnel and their families, including housing, schools, and recreational facilities. Recent expansions include the construction of Caserma Del Din, a LEED-certified annex, to accommodate additional troops. Caserma Ederle remains a vital hub for U.S. military presence in southern Europe.

trgt:
The 1900 House is a historical reenactment reality television series made by Wall to Wall/Channel 4 in 1999. The program features a modern family attempting to live in the way of the late Victorians for three months in a modified house. It was first broadcast on Channel 4 in the United Kingdom and PBS in America (with American commentary).

The series was accompanied by the book 1900 House: Featuring Extracts from the Personal Diaries of Joyce and Paul Bowler and Their Family by Mark McCrum and Matthew Sturgis. It won a Peabody Award in 2000 for being "an often humorous, always perceptive, series about the realities of life in 1900 that reveals themes of perseverance, human adaptation and family dynamics."
mgt: *The 1900 House* is a historical reenactment reality television series that originally aired on Channel 4 in the United Kingdom and PBS in the United States from December 28, 1999, to July 3, 2000. The series follows the Bowler family as they live in a restored 1890s terraced house in Charlton, South-East London, modified to reflect the conditions of the year 1900. Modern amenities were removed, and period-appropriate features, such as gas lighting and a cast-iron oven, were installed. The family faced challenges adapting to the era's lifestyle, including hiring and dismissing a maid. The series, which featured historian Daru Rooke as a consultant, inspired several sequels and was later released on VHS and DVD.

trgt:
Alice – A Fight For Life is a documentary featuring 47-year-old Alice Jefferson, a British woman who developed malignant pleural mesothelioma thirty years after working for nine months at Cape Insulation's Acre Mill asbestos plant in Hebden Bridge, West Yorkshire. The film also explored the health issues surrounding the manufacture and use of asbestos products. Described by The Guardian newspaper as "a momentous film", the programme also explicitly linked asbestos with cancer, and attacked what it perceived as the government's complacency in limiting the manufacture and use of asbestos in Britain.

Alice died of mesothelioma in February 1982, a month after filming for the programme had ended. She left two children: Paul and Patsy aged, 15 and 5 respectively.
mgt: *Alice – A Fight For Life* is a 1982 British documentary film directed, narrated, and produced by investigative journalist John Willis for Yorkshire Television. The 94.5-minute film, broadcast on ITV, examines the devastating health effects of asbestos exposure, particularly focusing on the case of Alice Jefferson, a victim of asbestosis. The documentary builds on earlier investigations into asbestos-related occupational health hazards, including ITV's *World in Action: The Dust at Acre Mill* (1971) and BBC's *Horizon: The Killer Dust* (1975). Dissatisfied with the findings of the 1979 Simpson Committee report, which downplayed the risks of asbestos, Willis and his team began work on the film in 1980, highlighting industry negligence and the human cost of asbestos exposure.

trgt:
William Methwold (sometimes spelled Methold), (baptised 1590 in South Pickenham, near Swaffham, Norfolk; died 5 March 1653, Kensington), was an English merchant and colonial administrator in India. He is credited with identifying the site of Bombay as a strategic port.

A fictional character in the novel Midnight's Children by author Salman Rushdie bears the same name and is described as a direct descendant. (See List of Midnight's Children characters). He himself appears as a character in 1637: The Peacock Throne by Eric Flint and Griffin Barber.
mgt: William Methwold (1590 – March 5, 1653) was an English merchant, colonial administrator, and prominent figure in the East India Company. Known for his linguistic skills and negotiation abilities, he served as president of Surat and played a key role in the 1635 Goa Convention, which ended hostilities with the Portuguese in India. Methwold advocated for the acquisition of Bombay as a strategic trading center, a vision realized after his death. He later became deputy governor of the East India Company and facilitated its merger with the Assada Company in 1649.

trgt:
Polad Israyil oghlu Hashimov (Lang-az; 2 January 1975 – 14 July 2020) was an Azerbaijani military officer, National Hero of Azerbaijan and a major general who served as the Deputy Commander and the Chief of Staff of the 3rd Army Corps of the Azerbaijani Armed Forces.

Hashimov was a popular military figure who took part in the First Nagorno-Karabakh War and the Four-Day War. He was awarded numerous honours during his military career, including jubilee and other medals. He commanded the Azerbaijani forces during the border clashes with Armenia in July 2020 and was killed on the morning of 14 July while fighting on the front-line in the Tovuz District of Azerbaijan. His death sparked violent protests throughout the country calling for war with Armenia, a prelude to the 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh war, which began in September of the same year. He was awarded the title of National Hero of Azerbaijan posthumously in December 2020.

Hashimov is the first and only general of the Azerbaijani Armed Forces to be killed during battle. Before him, Ismat Gayibov, the first Prosecutor General of Azerbaijan, and Mahammad Asadov, a major general in the Azerbaijani Ministry of Internal Affairs, were killed in November 1991 when the Mil Mi-8 helicopter they were in was shot down by Armenian forces.
mgt:
Polad Israyil oglu Hashimov (January 2, 1975 – July 14, 2020) was an Azerbaijani major general and National Hero of Azerbaijan, known for his service in the Azerbaijani Armed Forces during key conflicts, including the First Nagorno-Karabakh War, the Four-Day War, and the Tovuz clashes. Born in Vandam, Gabala District, he pursued a military career, graduating from the Baku Higher Combined Arms Command School and later earning a master’s degree in military leadership. Rising through the ranks, he became a prominent military figure, respected for his leadership and dedication.

Hashimov played a significant role in the Four-Day War of 2016, where he led successful operations and was later promoted to major general. In July 2020, he commanded Azerbaijani forces during the Tovuz clashes on the Armenia-Azerbaijan border, where he was killed in action, becoming the first Azerbaijani general to die in combat. His death sparked widespread mourning and protests in Azerbaijan, highlighting his popularity and the public's frustration with the ongoing conflict.

Posthumously declared a National Hero of Azerbaijan, Hashimov's legacy is honored through books, songs, documentaries, and streets named after him. He remains a symbol of patriotism and sacrifice in Azerbaijani society.

trgt:
The Cunningham Cabin is a double-pen log cabin in Grand Teton National Park in the US state of Wyoming. It was built as a homestead in Jackson Hole and represents an adaptation of an Appalachian building form to the West. The cabin was built just south of Spread Creek by John Pierce Cunningham, who arrived in Jackson Hole in 1885 and subsisted as a trapper until he established the Bar Flying U Ranch in 1888. The Cunninghams left the valley for Idaho in 1928, when land was being acquired for the future Grand Teton National Park.

Cunningham and his wife grew about 100 acres (40 ha) of hay, later irrigating another 140 acres (57 ha) to provide feed for 100 cattle and eight horses. His brother, W. Pierce Cunningham, settled his family nearby. By 1924 the Cunningham ranch comprised 560 acres (230 ha). By 1926 Cunningham had moved out of cattle and was raising sheep on the land.

J. Pierce Cunningham was one of the original county commissioners chosen when Teton County was organized in 1923. He was also, at various times, justice of the peace, postmaster and game warden.

After 1895 the Cunninghams, who had built a more commodious house, used the cabin as a barn or a smithy. A small fortification was erected in 1895 during unrest involving the Bannack Indians. Traces of foundations survive.
mgt:
The Cunningham Cabin is a historic structure located near Moose, Wyoming, within Grand Teton National Park. Built in 1885, it is an example of a sod-roofed, double-pen or dog-trot style cabin, a design originating in Appalachia. Constructed without nails or metal fastenings, the cabin features saddle-V-notched logs and a central breezeway flanked by two rooms. The site spans 10 acres and includes remnants of other structures, such as a barn, bunkhouse, and a house site, as well as pits that may have served as wells or privies. The cabin was reconstructed in 1956 to preserve its historical integrity.

The cabin is also notable for its association with a violent incident in 1892, known as the "Gunfight at the Bar Flying U Ranch." Two men accused of horse theft were killed by a posse under unclear circumstances, with the men's guilt and the identity of the alleged U.S. Marshal leading the posse remaining unverified. The Cunningham Cabin was added to the National Register of Historic Places on October 2, 1973, as part of the Grand Teton National Park Multiple Property Submission. It remains a significant example of early frontier architecture and a site of historical intrigue.

trgt: Sir Frederick Wollaston Mann KCMG (2 May 1869 – 29 May 1958) was the chief justice of the Australian state of Victoria between 1 October 1935 and 31 January 1944. He was also Victoria’s lieutenant governor between 12 May 1936 and May 1945. Mann was nicknamed the “Little Gentleman” because of his height (he was 168 cm tall) and he was unfailingly courteous. He had a reputation of being a careful judge delivering decisions of precision and clarity. In 1935, he became the first Australian born person to become chief justice of Victoria.
mgt: Sir Frederick Mann KCMG (2 May 1869 – 29 May 1958) was an Australian jurist who served as Chief Justice of Victoria from 1935 to 1944 and Lieutenant Governor of Victoria from 1936 to 1945. Born in Mount Gambier, South Australia, he pursued a legal career after studying at the University of Melbourne. Mann served in the Boer War before becoming a barrister and later a justice of the Supreme Court of Victoria in 1919. Knighted in 1933, he was known for his judicial clarity and significant rulings, including his interpretation of legal terminology.

trgt:
Hopanoids are a diverse subclass of triterpenoids with the same hydrocarbon skeleton as the compound hopane. This group of pentacyclic molecules therefore refers to simple hopenes, hopanols and hopanes, but also to extensively functionalized derivatives such as bacteriohopanepolyols (BHPs) and hopanoids covalently attached to lipid A.

The first known hopanoid, hydroxyhopanone, was isolated by two chemists at The National Gallery, London working on the chemistry of dammar gum, a natural resin used as a varnish for paintings. While hopanoids are often assumed to be made only in bacteria, their name actually comes from the abundance of hopanoid compounds in the resin of plants from the genus Hopea. In turn, this genus is named after John Hope, the first Regius Keeper of the Royal Botanic Garden, Edinburgh.

Since their initial discovery in an angiosperm, hopanoids have been found in plasma membranes of bacteria, lichens, bryophytes, ferns, tropical trees and fungi. Hopanoids have stable polycyclic structures that are well-preserved in petroleum reservoirs, rocks and sediment, allowing the diagenetic products of these molecules to be interpreted as biomarkers for the presence of specific microbes and potentially for chemical or physical conditions at the time of deposition. Hopanoids have not been detected in archaea.
mgt:
Hopanoids are a class of pentacyclic triterpenoid lipids produced by various bacteria and some eukaryotes. Structurally similar to sterols in eukaryotes, hopanoids play critical roles in bacterial plasma membranes, enhancing rigidity, reducing permeability, and aiding adaptation to extreme environments. They are synthesized from squalene through a series of enzymatic reactions, including cyclization by squalene-hopene cyclase, and are often further functionalized by additional enzymes. Hopanoids are particularly abundant in nitrogen-fixing bacteria, where they contribute to stress tolerance and membrane stability, and in survival structures like akinetes in cyanobacteria.

Hopanoids are geologically significant due to their stability, persisting as molecular fossils (hopanes) over billions of years. They have been used as biomarkers to study ancient life and environmental conditions, though their association with specific processes, such as oxygenic photosynthesis, has been debated. Recent research suggests broader roles for hopanoids in microbial communities and environmental adaptation.

Beyond biology, hopanoids have applications in agriculture as biofertilizers and are being explored for their role in multidrug resistance mechanisms in bacteria. Their biosynthetic pathways and enzymatic mechanisms have also inspired advancements in chemical engineering.

trgt:
The Yaquina River (/jəˈkwɪnə/ yə-KWIN-ə) is a stream, 59 miles (95 km) long, on the Pacific coast of the U.S. state of Oregon. It drains an area of the Central Oregon Coast Range west of the Willamette Valley near Newport.

It rises in the mountains west of Corvallis along the county line between Benton and Lincoln counties. It flows south, then generally west, in a highly serpentine course, past Eddyville, Chitwood, Toledo, and Elk City, and enters the Pacific in Yaquina Bay, a broad estuary at Newport. When sea level was lower than today, the Yaquina River reached to Stonewall Bank, which is split by a rocky channel.

U.S. Route 20, the Corvallis–Newport highway, follows the river from Eddyville, at the confluence of the river with Little Elk Creek, to slightly downstream of Chitwood. Near the mouth of Yaquina Bay, the river passes under Yaquina Bay Bridge, which carries U.S. Route 101. Other bridges over the river include the Chitwood Covered Bridge at Chitwood.

The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's Pacific Marine Center and the Hatfield Marine Science Center are both along Yaquina Bay.
