Adjacent to the Hatfield Center is the Oregon Coast Aquarium. The Yaquina Bay Lighthouse, contained within the Yaquina Bay State Recreation Site, is on the coast near the mouth of the river.
mgt:
The Yaquina River is a 59-mile (95 km) river located in western Oregon, United States, flowing through Benton and Lincoln counties. Originating near Summit in the Central Oregon Coast Range, the river travels westward and empties into Yaquina Bay at Newport, where it meets the Pacific Ocean. The river's basin covers an area of 252 square miles (650 km²), and its average discharge is 248 cubic feet per second (7.0 m³/s).

The Yaquina River and its bay are popular for fishing, supporting a variety of species such as salmon, steelhead, cutthroat trout, sturgeon, and crabs. Newport hosts a large charter fishing fleet targeting halibut, salmon, and other ocean fish, while the river and bay are also fished from the banks and small boats, with boat-launching ramps available along the lower river.

The river has numerous tributaries, including the Little Yaquina River, Big Elk Creek, and Depot Slough, among others. These tributaries contribute to the river's ecological diversity and recreational opportunities. The Yaquina River and its surrounding areas are significant for both their natural resources and their role in supporting local fishing industries.

trgt: TransPeshawar or Peshawar BRT is a bus rapid transit system in Peshawar, capital of Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) province. TransPeshawar BRT system consists of two parts: the first encompasses an east–west corridor served by 32 stations on a dedicated lane for exclusive use by buses, while the second part consists of a network of feeder routes in which buses can enter and exit the system to travel on city streets. The system was inaugurated on August 13, 2020, and is the fourth BRT system in Pakistan.
mgt: TransPeshawar, also known as Zu Peshawar, is a bus rapid transit (BRT) system serving the Greater Peshawar Region in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan. Operational since August 13, 2020, it features a 27-kilometer east–west corridor with 32 stations, connecting Chamkani to Karkhano Market. The system uses a fleet of hybrid buses, offering environmentally friendly and efficient public transportation. Developed with assistance from the Asian Development Bank, the project faced delays, cost overruns, and corruption allegations but remains the most expensive BRT system in Pakistan. It aims to improve urban mobility and reduce traffic congestion in Peshawar.

trgt:
Taptu was a social media and technology company that built platforms, tools and applications that enabled content on touch screen mobile devices, including phones running iOS and Android. Taptu was a privately held company that was founded in Cambridge in 2007 and was funded by DFJ Esprit and Sofinnova. The company was based in Cambridge and Denver, Colorado.

In September 2012, Taptu was acquired by Mediafed Ltd. The Taptu service shut down March 31, 2015, a year before Mediafed went into administration.
mgt: Taptu was a UK-based social media company founded in 2007 and headquartered in Cambridge. It developed mobile-focused products, including a mobile search engine and a social news aggregator called My Taptu. The mobile search engine, optimized for phones and available via apps, was discontinued in early 2011. My Taptu, available on iPhone and Android, aimed to address information overload by aggregating personalized content streams, described as "content playlists." The app sought to differentiate itself in the social news space through its focus on personalization.

trgt:
Mirpur (Lang-phr; Lang-ur), officially known as New Mirpur City (Lang-ur), is the capital of Mirpur district located in Azad Kashmir, Pakistan. It is the second largest city of Azad Kashmir and 74th largest city in Pakistan.

A significant portion of the population from the district, the Mirpuri diaspora, migrated to the United Kingdom in the mid-to-late 1950s and in the early 1960s, mostly to West Yorkshire, East and West Midlands, Birmingham, Luton, Peterborough, Derby and East London. Mirpur is thus sometimes known as "Little England". Many British products are found, and many shops in the city accept the pound sterling.

The city itself has gone through a process of modernization, but most of the surrounding area relies on agriculture.
mgt:
Mirpur (Urdu: میرپور), also known as New Mirpur City, is a city in Azad Jammu and Kashmir, Pakistan. Located at an elevation of 458 meters, it serves as the headquarters of Mirpur District and is one of the largest cities in Azad Kashmir. Founded around 1640 by Miran Shah Ghazi, the city has a rich history, transitioning from Gakhar rule to Sikh and Dogra control before becoming part of Azad Kashmir during the Indo-Pakistani War of 1947–1948. 

Modern Mirpur, often called "Little England" due to its significant diaspora in the UK, is a well-planned city with modern infrastructure, educational institutions, and industrial development. It is also known for its cultural festivals, historical sites, and proximity to Mangla Lake.

trgt:
Old Street is a 1-mile (1.6 km) street in inner north-east Central London that runs west to east from Goswell Road in Clerkenwell, in the London Borough of Islington, via St Luke's and Old Street Roundabout, to the crossroads where it meets Shoreditch High Street (south), Kingsland Road (north) and Hackney Road (east) in Shoreditch in the London Borough of Hackney.

It has London Underground station Old Street on the Northern line which has other platforms for National Rail's Northern City Line.

Its west half is inside London's Congestion Charging Zone; a short part of its eastern half is the inner ring marking its limit.
mgt: Old Street is a historic thoroughfare in London, England, with origins dating back to at least the 13th century, and possibly earlier as part of a Roman or pre-Roman route connecting Silchester and Colchester. The street has undergone significant changes, including widening in the 19th century, but retains some 18th- and early 19th-century buildings. Located on the London Inner Ring Road, it forms part of the boundary of the congestion charge zone and is known for its vibrant nightlife, tech industry presence around "Silicon Roundabout," and notable graffiti by artists like Banksy. Old Street station, situated under the roundabout, serves as a key transport hub.

trgt:
Peter George Baines is an Australian geophysicist. He is an Honorary Senior Fellow at University of Melbourne and Honorary Secretary at Royal Society of Victoria.

Baines has published over 150 research articles and is the author of Topographic Effects in Stratified Flows, published in 1995 (paperback edition 1998). His major research areas include climate dynamics on the decadal time scale, volcano dynamics and Rossby wave hydraulics.

He is or has been the member of various societies such as the American Geophysical Union, Australian Meteorological and Oceanographic Society (AMOS - this new Society's first President, in 1988), Royal Society of Victoria and the Royal Meteorological Society. From 1960 to 1966, he was a member of the Royal Australian Naval Reserve, to the rank of Sub-lieutenant.
mgt:
Peter George Baines is an Australian researcher, academic, and author specializing in geophysical fluid dynamics, climate dynamics, oceanography, and volcano dynamics. He earned degrees in mathematics and physics from Melbourne University and a PhD in geophysical fluid dynamics from Cambridge University. Baines has held academic and research positions at institutions including the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, CSIRO Atmospheric Research, and the University of Melbourne, where he is an Honorary Professorial Fellow.

Baines' research has advanced understanding of stratified fluid flows, ocean tides, climate variability, and volcanic eruptions. He authored the influential book *Topographic Effects in Stratified Flows* and has received numerous accolades, including the AMOS Priestley Medal. He has also contributed to international meteorological organizations and served on editorial boards, making significant contributions to atmospheric and oceanic sciences.

trgt:
Mount Hope is a granite outcrop located to the north of Pyramid Hill in northern Victoria, Australia. The outcrop lies within the 126 hectare Mount Hope Nature Conservation Reserve which is managed by Parks Victoria.

The reserve has limited facilities, including picnic tables and an unsealed parking area at the foot of the outcrop which is accessed from the unsealed Old Leitchville Road. Activities include rock climbing and bird observing.

Communications facilities for Ambulance Victoria, Goulburn Murray Water and the Department of Sustainability and Environment are located near the mountain summit.
mgt: Mount Hope is a granite hill in Victoria, Australia, with an elevation of 202 meters (663 feet). It was first sighted and ascended by Scottish explorer Thomas Livingstone Mitchell in 1836, who discovered two new plant species on its summit and observed the surrounding landscape, including Pyramid Hill. The area later became the site of Mount Hope Station, established in 1845, which supported extensive sheep grazing. The mountain has historical significance, including its role in the Burke and Wills expedition of 1860. A quarry operated on Mount Hope until its closure in 1958.

trgt:
Cabot Tower is a tower in St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador, situated on Signal Hill. Construction of the tower began in 1898 to commemorate the 400th anniversary of John Cabot's discovery of Newfoundland, and Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee.

In 1901, Guglielmo Marconi received the first trans-Atlantic wireless message at a position near the tower, the letter "S" in Morse Code sent from Poldhu, Cornwall, United Kingdom. Cabot Tower is now the centre of the Signal Hill National Historic Site of Canada, with walking trails and an interpretation centre.
mgt: Cabot Tower is a Gothic Revival structure located on Signal Hill in St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada. Designed by architect William Howe Greene, it was constructed between 1898 and 1900 to commemorate Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee and the 400th anniversary of John Cabot's voyage to the New World. Built of red sandstone, the tower served as a flag signaling station until 1958 and later housed a Marconi station, which played a role in early wireless communication. Today, it is a prominent symbol of St. John's and a historic landmark.

trgt: Kim Hyo-yeon (born September 22, 1989), referred to as Hyoyeon or DJ Hyo, is a South Korean singer, dancer, DJ, and television personality. She debuted as a member of the girl group Girls'Generation in August 2007, which went on to become one of the best-selling artists in South Korea and one of the most popular K-pop groups worldwide. She has since participated in other SM Entertainment projects, including Girls' Generation-Oh!GG and Got the Beat. Since 2016, she has also released singles as a solo artist.
mgt: Kim Hyo-yeon (born September 22, 1989), known mononymously as Hyoyeon or by her DJ name Hyo, is a South Korean singer, dancer, DJ, and television personality. She debuted in 2007 as a member of the girl group Girls' Generation under SM Entertainment, achieving widespread fame. Hyoyeon has also pursued solo activities, releasing singles such as "Mystery," "Wannabe," and "Dessert," and transitioning into a DJ career in 2018. She is a member of Girls' Generation's sub-unit Oh!GG and the supergroup Got the Beat.

trgt:
Skimlinks is a content monetization platform for online publishers (including editorial sites, forums, bloggers, social networks, and app developers). It specializes in technology that automatically affiliates product links from publishers' commerce content.

The company was founded in London (2007) by Australian co-founders Alicia Navarro and Joe Stepniewski. It has over 62 employees, and has raised $24 million in funding as of August 2013. It has offices in London and New York City. On October 17, 2013, Skimlinks was announced as a member of Tech City UK's Future Fifty Programme, a government programme that supports fast-growing companies with the aim of potentially floating in the London Stock Exchange. In 2014, Skimlinks drove $625 million in sales for over 20,000 vendors.
mgt: Skimlinks is a London-based marketing and advertising company founded in 2007 by Alicia Navarro and Joe Stepniewski. Originally launched as Skimbit, a social shopping platform, the company pivoted to focus on affiliate marketing technology, helping publishers monetize their content through automated link affiliation. Skimlinks aggregates over 24,000 merchant programs and is used by major publishers like BuzzFeed, Condé Nast, and Time Inc. The company has raised $24 million in funding from investors such as Greycroft and Frog Capital and has acquired competitors like Atma Links and InvisibleHand. Skimlinks was acquired by Taboola on September 1, 2021, and continues to offer tools for content monetization, including its Skimlinks Editor Tool and Audiences platform.

trgt:
Saint Overboard is the title of a 1936 mystery novel by Leslie Charteris, one of a long series of novels featuring Charteris' creation Simon Templar, alias "The Saint". An edited version was previously published in November 1935 in The American Magazine as The Pirate Saint. Some paperback editions append the article The to the title (The Saint Overboard).

The book was first published in the United Kingdom by Hodder & Stoughton in 1936 (followed by an American edition by The Crime Club).
mgt: *Saint Overboard* is a 1936 mystery novel by Leslie Charteris, part of *The Saint* series. The story follows Simon Templar, known as The Saint, as he rescues private detective Loretta Page during a pleasure cruise along the French coast. Loretta enlists Templar's help to stop a group of modern-day pirates, led by Kurt Vogel, who use advanced technology to loot sunken treasure. The novel features recurring characters from the series and was adapted into a 1995 radio play, though plans for a film adaptation were never realized.

trgt: The Kilwa Sultanate (Lang-fa) was a sultanate, centered at Kilwa (an island off modern-day, Kilwa District in Lindi Region of Tanzania), whose authority, at its height, stretched over the entire length of the Swahili Coast. According to the legend, it was founded in the 10th century by Ali ibn al-Hassan Shirazi, a Persian prince of Shiraz. His family ruled the Sultanate until the year 1277. They were replaced by the Arab family of Abu Moaheb until 1505, when they were overthrown by a Portuguese invasion. By 1513, the sultanate was already fragmented into smaller states, many of which became protectorates of the Sultanate of Oman.
mgt: The Kilwa Sultanate (957–1513) was a medieval Islamic sultanate centered on Kilwa Kisiwani, an island off the Swahili coast of East Africa. Established by Ali ibn al-Hassan Shirazi, a Persian prince, it became a major trading hub, dominating commerce along the Swahili Coast and extending its influence to cities like Sofala, Zanzibar, and Mombasa. The sultanate thrived on trade in gold, ivory, and other goods with Arabia, Persia, and India, contributing to the development of Swahili culture and language. Kilwa's decline began in the late 15th century, exacerbated by internal strife and Portuguese intervention, which ultimately led to its fall in 1513.

trgt:
Wafa Mustafa (b. 1989 or 1990 (age 34–35)) is a Syrian journalist and activist who campaigns for the release of Syrian detainees. As an activist and former member of Families for Freedom, Mustafa has extensively lobbied the United Nations Security Council to call for the release of the names and locations of all those that Syrian authorities have in captivity. She calls for all detainees in Syria to be freed, whether they are held by the Assad regime or by opposition groups, though she also supports the public demonstrations that began in 2011 against the government of Bashar al-Assad.

Mustafa also campaigns for international recognition of Syrian refugees and against normalized international relations with the Assad regime.
mgt: Wafa Mustafa is a Syrian journalist, activist, and advocate for detainees and forcibly disappeared individuals, known for her efforts to raise awareness about human rights abuses in Syria. Born in Masyaf, she became politically active at a young age, participating in protests against the Assad regime during the Syrian Civil War. Her activism is deeply motivated by the disappearance of her father, Ali Mustafa, a human rights activist who was detained by Syrian authorities in 2013. After fleeing Syria with her family, Mustafa resettled in Berlin, where she graduated from Bard College Berlin in 2020. She has since worked with organizations like Families for Freedom and has addressed the UN Security Council on forced disappearances.

trgt:
Charles William Hargens, Jr. (1893−1997) was an American painter. He created over 3000 covers for magazines such as The Saturday Evening Post, Collier's, Country Gentleman, Farm Journal, Boys' Life, The Open Road for Boys, along with advertisements for companies such as Coca-Cola and covers for over 300 books, including the Zane Grey Western novels of the 1930s and 1940s.

He worked for over 80 years and died on January 30, 1997, at 103 years of age. He is buried at Glenside Cemetery in Montgomery County Pennsylvania.
mgt: Charles Hargens (1893–1997) was an American artist known for his detailed Western illustrations, magazine covers, book jackets, and advertisements. Born in Hot Springs, South Dakota, he showed artistic talent from a young age and studied at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, later earning a scholarship to study in Paris. Hargens worked for prominent publications like *The Saturday Evening Post* and *Collier's* and created advertisements for companies such as Coca-Cola. He eventually settled in Carversville, Pennsylvania, where his studio and many works are preserved at the Dakota Discovery Museum.

trgt:
In the theory of von Neumann algebras, a part of the mathematical field of functional analysis, Tomita–Takesaki theory is a method for constructing modular automorphisms of von Neumann algebras from the polar decomposition of a certain involution. It is essential for the theory of type III factors, and has led to a good structure theory for these previously intractable objects.

The theory was introduced by Minoru Tomita (1967), but his work was hard to follow and mostly unpublished, and little notice was taken of it until Masamichi Takesaki (1970) wrote an account of Tomita's theory.
mgt: Tomita–Takesaki theory is a fundamental framework in the theory of von Neumann algebras, providing deep insights into their structure and automorphisms. Central to the theory is the modular automorphism group, a 1-parameter group of automorphisms associated with a faithful state on a von Neumann algebra, derived from the polar decomposition of certain operators. The theory introduces key concepts such as the modular operator, modular conjugation, and KMS states, which connect quantum statistical mechanics and operator algebras. It also plays a crucial role in the classification of type III factors and the study of Hilbert algebras.

trgt:
Jerry Collins (4 November 1980 – 5 June 2015) was a Samoan – New Zealand rugby union player. He played for New Zealand, for whom he was capped 48 times, as well as for the Wellington Hurricanes in New Zealand, Toulon and Narbonne in the Rugby Pro D2, Ospreys in the UK, and Yamaha Júbilo in Japan. He played as a flanker and number eight, and was considered to be one of the hardest tacklers in rugby history, along with Brian Lima.

Collins and his partner, Alana Madill, were killed in a car accident in southern France in June 2015.
mgt:
Jerry Collins (November 4, 1980 – June 5, 2015) was a Samoan-born New Zealand rugby union player, renowned for his physicality and leadership. Playing as a flanker or number eight, he earned 48 caps for the All Blacks between 2001 and 2008, captaining the team in several matches, including during the 2007 Rugby World Cup. Collins also had a successful club career, representing the Wellington Hurricanes, Toulon, Ospreys, Yamaha Júbilo, and RC Narbonne.

Off the field, Collins was known for his humility and community involvement. He tragically died in a car accident in France in 2015, saving his infant daughter, who survived. Porirua Park Stadium was renamed in his honor.

trgt:
In ecology, rarefaction is a technique to assess species richness from the results of sampling. Rarefaction allows the calculation of species richness for a given number of individual samples, based on the construction of so-called rarefaction curves. This curve is a plot of the number of species as a function of the number of samples. Rarefaction curves generally grow rapidly at first, as the most common species are found, but the curves plateau as only the rarest species remain to be sampled.

The issue that occurs when sampling various species in a community is that the larger the number of individuals sampled, the more species that will be found. Rarefaction curves are created by randomly re-sampling the pool of N samples multiple times and then plotting the average number of species found in each sample (1,2,. N). "Thus rarefaction generates the expected number of species in a small collection of n individuals (or n samples) drawn at random from the large pool of N samples.".
mgt:
Rarefaction is an ecological technique used to estimate species richness and compare biodiversity across samples of different sizes. Developed in 1968 by Howard Sanders, it was initially applied to marine benthic ecosystems and has since been refined by researchers such as Stuart Hurlbert, Daniel Simberloff, and Ken Heck. Rarefaction generates curves that allow for comparisons of species richness by standardizing sample sizes, making it a valuable tool in community ecology, including studies of human microbiomes and the impacts of pollution.

The method assumes random distribution of individuals, sufficient sample size, and consistent sampling techniques. Rarefaction curves are used to smooth data for comparisons, avoiding biases from raw species counts. However, the technique has limitations, including its inability to account for species abundance, specific taxa, or beta diversity. It also assumes random spatial distribution, which may not reflect ecological realities. Despite these criticisms, rarefaction remains widely used for understanding biodiversity and taxonomic diversity in ecological studies.

trgt:
Pit-Fighter is a 1990 arcade fighting game by Atari Games which uses digitized live actors. It was Atari's first fighting game. The Japanese arcade release was published by Konami. Home versions were published by Tengen.

The graphical animations for the player character and opponents were created through a bluescreen process, where the various poses and moves were performed by real actors in front of a video camera. The game's on-screen character animation is replays of the actual footage, not a rotoscoped (redrawn) animation. Pit-Fighter is the second fighting game to use digitized sprites, after Home Data's Reikai Dōshi: Chinese Exorcist.
mgt: *Pit-Fighter* is a 1990 arcade fighting game developed by Atari Games and published by Atari in North America and Europe, and by Konami in Japan. It was later ported to various home platforms, including the Super NES, Sega Genesis, and Atari Lynx. The game features digitized sprites of real actors, a unique feature at the time, and allows up to three players to fight simultaneously. Players choose from three fighters, each with distinct abilities, and battle through 15 matches, including bonus "Grudge Matches" and a final fight against the Masked Warrior. *Pit-Fighter* was commercially successful, selling 6,500 arcade units and receiving mixed critical reception for its gameplay and graphics. A planned sequel was ultimately canceled, though elements were incorporated into Atari's later game, *Guardians of the 'Hood*.

page_title: Taking Chances World Tour: The Concert
trgt:
Taking Chances World Tour: The Concert is a home video/live album by Canadian singer Celine Dion. It was released by Columbia Records on 29 April 2010 in Australia and in early May 2010 in Europe and North America. The concert was issued as a DVD/CD combo in two editions: English-language Taking Chances World Tour: The Concert and French-language Tournée mondiale Taking Chances: le spectacle. Dion had embarked on the 2008–2009 Taking Chances World Tour for promotion of her 2007 studio album Taking Chances. It became one of the highest-grossing concert tours of all time. The performances in Boston and Montreal during the North American leg of Taking Chances World Tour were filmed for a video release and directed by Jean Lamoureux.

The release received positive reviews from music critics who praised Dion's stage presence, her powerful performances and chosen songs. It was also commercially successful. In Canada, Tournée mondiale Taking Chances: le spectacle was certified Diamond and Taking Chances World Tour: The Concert was awarded four-times Platinum. The DVD was also certified Platinum in the United States and the album received Gold certification in France. It topped the DVD charts in the United States and several European countries, and reached top ten around the world. In some countries, it entered the album charts peaking inside top ten in few of them, including number five in France.
mgt:
*Taking Chances World Tour: The Concert* is a live album and concert DVD/CD by Canadian singer Celine Dion, released on April 29, 2010. Recorded during her *Taking Chances World Tour* in 2008 at the TD Garden in Boston and the Bell Centre in Montreal, the release showcases performances of over 20 songs, including hits like "My Heart Will Go On" and "I Drove All Night," as well as covers of Queen's "We Will Rock You" and James Brown's "It's a Man's Man's Man's World." The album was released in both English and French editions, with some differences in setlists and content.

The release received positive reviews for Dion's dynamic performances and polished production. Commercially, it was a major success, debuting at the top of music video charts in Canada and the United States and achieving multiple certifications, including Platinum in the U.S. and Diamond in Canada. The French edition, *Tournée mondiale Taking Chances: le spectacle*, was also certified Gold in France and Belgium. The DVD/CD was nominated for a 2011 Juno Award for Music DVD of the Year. The release further solidified Dion's reputation as a global music icon, capturing the energy and scale of her record-breaking world tour, which spanned five continents and sold over three million tickets.

trgt: An entrée (/ˈɒ̃treɪ/, US also /ɒnˈtreɪ/; IPA-fr) in modern French table service and that of much of the English-speaking world (apart from the United States and parts of Canada) is a dish served before the main course of a meal. Outside North America, it is generally synonymous with the terms hors d'oeuvre, appetizer, or starter. It may be the first dish served, or it may follow a soup or other small dish or dishes. In the United States and parts of Canada, the term entrée refers to the main dish or the only dish of a meal.
mgt: The term "entrée" originated in 16th-century French culinary tradition, initially referring to the first stage of a meal, the "entrance to the table." Over time, its meaning evolved, and by the 17th century, it denoted a specific type of dish served as the second course, characterized by moist cooking methods and typically featuring meat or fish. Entrées were distinct from roasts and vegetable dishes, which were served in other stages of the meal. In modern French cuisine, the term "entrée" refers to the starter or appetizer course, preceding the main dish in a three-course meal.

trgt: Kachche Heere is a 1982 Bollywood Hindi action film directed by Narender Bedi, starring Feroz Khan, Reena Roy, Danny Denzongpa, Aruna Irani and Shakti Kapoor. The movie was produced by Netra Pal Singh and the music was composed by Rahul Dev Burman. The film was somewhat a sequel to the 1974 Blockbuster Khote Sikkay. The film had the same star cast and almost the exact story line. Among few changes were that Ranjeet was replaced by Shakti Kapoor. The film was released on 12 February 1982.
mgt: *Kachche Heere* is a 1982 Hindi-language film directed and written by Narender Bedi and produced by Netra Pal Singh. The film stars Feroz Khan, Reena Roy, Danny Denzongpa, Aruna Irani, and Shakti Kapoor in pivotal roles, with music composed by Rahul Dev Burman. Set in rural Madhya Pradesh, the story revolves around dacoits, feuds among the ruling class, and themes of redemption. It follows Feroz Khan's character, Ajnabi, as he uncovers his true identity and, alongside five reformed petty thieves, fights against the oppressive dacoit Lukka Singh to save a village.

trgt:
Trichomonas gallinae is a cosmopolitan parasite of birds including finches, pigeons, doves, turkeys, chickens, parrots, raptors (hawks, golden eagle, etc.). The condition in birds of prey is called frounce. It is believed to be an ancient pathogen causing frounce-like symptoms in theropod dinosaurs. The same condition in pigeons is commonly called canker.

The protozoa are small (5 to 20 μm size) oval or spherical flagellates with four free flagella found on the anterior aspect of the parasite. They also have an axostyle found on the posterior end as well as an undulating membrane on one side. An important diagnostic feature is the lack of a free posterior flagellum.
mgt: *Trichomonas gallinae* is a parasitic protozoan that infects birds, causing avian trichomoniasis, a disease primarily affecting the upper digestive and respiratory tracts. It is found worldwide, except in Antarctica, Greenland, and the northernmost regions of North America, Europe, and Asia. The parasite is transmitted through contaminated food, water, or direct contact, particularly during feeding, and is common in pigeons, doves, and birds of prey. Symptoms range from mild to fatal, with infected birds often displaying difficulty swallowing, weight loss, and necrotic lesions in the mouth and throat. Diagnosis is made through clinical signs and microscopic examination, and treatment typically involves nitroimidazole antibiotics.

trgt:
The Most Eminent Order of the Indian Empire is an order of chivalry founded by Queen Victoria on 1 January 1878. The Order includes members of three classes:

No appointments have been made since 1947, the year that British India gained independence as the Union of India and Dominion of Pakistan. With the death of the last surviving knight, the Maharaja Meghrajji III of Dhrangadhra, the order became dormant in 2010.

The motto of the Order is Imperatricis auspiciis, (Latin for "Under the auspices of the Empress"), a reference to Queen Victoria, the first Empress of India. The Order is the junior British order of chivalry associated with the British Indian Empire; the senior one is The Most Exalted Order of the Star of India.
mgt:
The Most Eminent Order of the Indian Empire was an order of chivalry established by Queen Victoria in 1878 to honor British and Indian officials who served in British India. Initially comprising a single class, the order expanded to three classes—Knight Grand Commander (GCIE), Knight Commander (KCIE), and Companion (CIE)—by 1887. It was intended as a less exclusive counterpart to the Order of the Star of India. Membership included British officials, Indian princely rulers, and other notable figures, with women generally excluded except for female rulers.

Appointments ceased after Indian independence in 1947, and the order became dormant in 2010. The British monarch remains its sovereign. Members were entitled to various privileges, including heraldic honors, and the order's insignia reflected respect for India's non-Christian traditions. Notable appointees included rulers of Bhutan and other dignitaries.

trgt: Utsayantha Mountain is a 3,209-foot-tall (978 m) mountain in the Catskill Mountains of New York. It is located east-southeast of Stamford in Delaware County. Variant names include Utsayanthe Mountain, Utsayanthia Mountain, Utsayantho Mountain, and Utsyantha Mountain. McGregor Mountain is located southeast of Utsayantha Mountain and Churchill Mountain is located southwest. Utsayantha Mountain is named after Utsayantha, the daughter of Chief Ubiwacha, chief of the Lenape Indians. In 1934, a 59-foot-3-inch-tall (18.06 m) steel fire lookout tower was built on the peak. The tower was ceased fire lookout operations at the end of the 1989 fire lookout season. In July 2005, restoration work that began in 2004 was completed. The tower is on the National Historic Lookout Register and is open to the public.
mgt:
Utsayantha Mountain is a 3,209-foot (978 m) peak located east-southeast of Stamford in Delaware County, New York, within the Catskill Mountains. Named after Utsayantha, the daughter of Lenape Chief Ubiwacha, the mountain is associated with a local legend involving tragedy and loss. A grave discovered on the mountain in 1862 is believed to be hers, though its identity remains unproven.

In 1934, a 59-foot (18 m) steel fire tower was constructed on the summit by the Civilian Conservation Corps. After its closure in 1989, the tower was restored in 2004–2005 and is now open to the public. It was added to the National Historic Lookout Register in 2015. The mountain also features a historic observation site.

trgt:
Govind Prasad Kukreti (25 August 1932 – 31 August 2016), popularly known as Dabral Baba, was an Indian yogi and a disciple of Vikrant Bhairav and Mahavatar Babaji. He was also known as Baba and Shri Dabral Baba.

Dabral Baba hailed from Ujjain in the state of Madhya Pradesh. He adopted his surname (Dabral) after his maternal uncle's who virtually brought him up at Dhar and later to Ujjain. He was also unusual among Indian yogis who was a householder - he got married, raised a family, worked in Vikram University in Ujjain. Dabral Baba lived with his family at his home (Bhairav Niwas) located in Rishinagar in Ujjain. Dabral Baba attainted substantial enlightenment and earned popularity among highest ranks of yogis in 20th and 21st century period.

In the later part of his life, he was visited by many great saints both physically and spiritually primarily Mahavatar Babaji who took him to his gupha as well. Dabral Baba unveiled Vikrant Bhairav temple in Bhairavgarh, Madhya Pradesh the idol of Vikrant Bhairav was deep buried in mud for years, Dabral Baba was guided to unveil this temple and he later attained enlightenment with the guidance of his guru.
mgt:
Dabral Baba (born Govind Prasad Kukreti; August 25, 1932 – August 31, 2016) was an Indian Hindu saint and spiritual leader known for his devotion to Vikrant Bhairav and his connection with Mahavatar Babaji, the legendary "deathless guru" described in *Autobiography of a Yogi*. Born in Timli village, Uttarakhand, he spent his early years in his ancestral region before moving to Madhya Pradesh, where he discovered the Vikrant Bhairav idol in 1960. This discovery marked a turning point in his life, earning him the title "Baba" and initiating his lifelong worship of Vikrant Bhairav.

Dabral Baba claimed to have been guided by Mahavatar Babaji, who he said appeared to him in various forms and provided spiritual direction. He also identified Babaji during the 2016 Kumbh Mela in Ujjain, where he was widely revered by other saints. Known for his selflessness, simplicity, and childlike demeanor, Dabral Baba attracted devotees from across India and abroad. He offered blessings and spiritual guidance to hundreds daily, never seeking fame or material gain. His teachings emphasized humility, service, and divine communion, leaving a lasting legacy of spiritual devotion and compassion.

trgt:
SMS Frankfurt was a light cruiser of the Wiesbaden class built by the German Kaiserliche Marine (Imperial Navy). She had one sister ship, SMS Wiesbaden; the ships were very similar to the previous Karlsruhe-class cruisers. The ship was laid down in 1913, launched in March 1915, and completed by August 1915. Armed with eight 15 cm SK L/45 guns, Frankfurt had a top speed of 27.5 knots (50.9 km/h; 31.6 mph) and displaced 6,601 t (6,497 long tons; 7,276 short tons) at full load.

Frankfurt saw extensive action with the High Seas Fleet during World War I. She served primarily in the North Sea, and participated in the Bombardment of Yarmouth and Lowestoft and the battles of Jutland and Second Heligoland. At Jutland, she was lightly damaged by a British cruiser and her crew suffered minor casualties. At the end of the war, she was interned with the bulk of the German fleet in Scapa Flow. When the fleet was scuttled in June 1919, Frankfurt was one of the few ships that were not successfully sunk. She was ceded to the US Navy as a war prize and ultimately expended as a bomb target in tests conducted by the US Navy and Army Air Force in July 1921.
mgt:
SMS *Frankfurt* was a German *Wiesbaden*-class light cruiser built for the Imperial German Navy during World War I. Laid down in 1913 and commissioned in August 1915, the ship was armed with eight 15 cm guns, four torpedo tubes, and could carry 120 mines. She served in several major naval engagements, including the Bombardment of Yarmouth and Lowestoft and the Battle of Jutland, where she acted as the flagship of II Scouting Group. *Frankfurt* also participated in Operation Albion and the Second Battle of Heligoland Bight in 1917. 

After Germany's defeat, *Frankfurt* was interned at Scapa Flow, where she was scuttled by her crew in June 1919. However, British forces salvaged the ship, and she was later transferred to the United States as a war prize. Commissioned into the U.S. Navy in 1920, *Frankfurt* was studied for her design features before being used as a target ship in bombing tests led by General Billy Mitchell. On July 18, 1921, she was sunk off Cape Henry, Virginia, during these tests, which demonstrated the effectiveness of aerial bombing against warships.

trgt:
The laminar flamelet model is one of the methods of modelling turbulent combustions apart from SCRS, eddy flamelet model and others. Combustion is a very important thermochemical process with significant material and aerodynamic implications and thus CFD modeling of combustion has become indispensable. The laminar flamelet model is basically for non pre-mixed fuel (the system in which the fuel and oxygen is supplied from two different pipes).

The concept of ensemble of laminar flamelets was first introduced by Forman A. Williams in 1975, while the theoretical foundation was developed by Norbert Peters in the early 80s.
mgt: The laminar flamelet model is a combustion model used to describe turbulent flames as an ensemble of thin, laminar, one-dimensional flamelet structures. It simplifies the complex interactions in turbulent non-premixed combustion by assuming that the local flame front behaves like a steady, laminar flame. The model relies on parameters such as mixture fraction and scalar dissipation to calculate species mass fractions and temperature fields. While it effectively captures the coupling between chemical reactions and molecular transport, it has limitations, such as neglecting transient and curvature effects. Variants like the transient laminar flamelet model address these shortcomings.

trgt:
The Twelve Apostles is a collection of limestone stacks off the shore of Port Campbell National Park, by the Great Ocean Road in Victoria, Australia.

Their proximity to one another has made the site a popular tourist attraction. Seven of the original eight stacks remain standing at the Twelve Apostles viewpoint, after one collapsed in July 2005. Though the view from the promontory by the Twelve Apostles never included twelve stacks, additional stacks—not considered part of the Apostles group—are located to the west within the national park.
mgt: The Twelve Apostles are a group of limestone stacks off the shore of Port Campbell National Park, near Port Campbell in Victoria, Australia. Formed by erosion of the Port Campbell Limestone during the Mid-Late Miocene, the stacks were created through the gradual collapse of cliffs into caves, arches, and eventually isolated rock formations up to 50 meters (160 feet) high. Originally known as the Pinnacles and the Sow and Pigs, the site was officially named the Twelve Apostles, despite only ever having eight stacks. Seven remain visible today due to ongoing erosion.

trgt:
Valmiera (pronunciation; Lang-de; Lang-pl see other names) is the largest city of the historical Vidzeme region, Latvia, with a total area of 19.35 square kilometres (7 square miles). As of 2002, Valmiera had a population of 27,323, and in 2020 – 24 879. It is a state city the seat of Valmiera Municipality.

Valmiera lies at the crossroads of several important roads, 107 kilometres (66 miles) to the north-east from Riga, the capital of Latvia, and 50 km (31 mi) south of the border with Estonia. Valmiera lies on both banks of the Gauja River.
mgt: Valmiera is a historic city in northern Latvia, located on the banks of the Gauja River. First mentioned in 1323, it has roots dating back over 9,000 years, with significant development during the medieval period as a member of the Hanseatic League. The city endured destruction during the Livonian War, Great Northern War, and World War II but emerged as an industrial and cultural hub. Today, Valmiera is known for its fiberglass production, educational institutions, and sports achievements, including its connection to Olympic athletes. It is part of the UNESCO Global Network of Learning Cities and hosts extensive sports infrastructure.

trgt: John M. Simpson (born 1948) is an American consumer rights advocate and former journalist. Since 2005, he has worked for Consumer Watchdog, a nonpartisan nonprofit public interest group, as the lead researcher on Inside Google, the group's effort to educate the public about Google's dominance over the internet and the need for greater online privacy and as director of the organization's Stem Cell Oversight and Accountability Project to ensure that California’s stem cell program, created by Proposition 71, served the interest of voters.
mgt: John M. Simpson (born January 7, 1948) is an American journalist and consumer rights advocate. He worked for over 30 years as a reporter and editor for Gannett Company, including as deputy editor and founding managing editor of *USA Today*'s international edition. After leaving journalism, Simpson joined Consumer Watchdog in 2005, where he led initiatives such as the Stem Cell Oversight and Accountability Project and Inside Google, advocating for public benefits from stem cell research and greater online privacy. He resides in Redondo Beach, California.

trgt: Fauna in Armenia is diverse given the country's relatively small geographic size, owing to the varied habitats created by the area's mountainous terrain. Armenia is an important area for migratory animals, about 350 different bird species were recorded in the country. Many of the world's domesticated animals originated in the area Armenia is located in, and the mouflon, the ancestor of domesticated sheep, is present there. Research suggests that about a quarter of the animal species in Armenia are internationally endangered. The mouflon are suffering a great population decline due to poaching and habitat loss, and the Sevan trout, which made up thirty percent of the fish in Lake Sevan, have virtually disappeared.
mgt: The fauna of Armenia includes a variety of species, with the Persian leopard being one of the most notable. Southern and southwestern Armenia serve as a critical habitat for the Persian leopard, which is connected to the larger population in Iran. The total population in Armenia is estimated to be between 10 and 20 individuals, though exact numbers are uncertain. Historically, the region was also home to the Asiatic lion, which inhabited areas around Yerevan and the Araks River before being hunted to extinction in the Trans-Caucasus during the 10th century. Conservation efforts are vital to protect Armenia's remaining wildlife.

trgt:
Melukote in Pandavapura taluk of Mandya district, Karnataka, in southern India, is one of the sacred places in Karnataka.It is built on rocky hills, known as Yadugiri, Yaadavagiri and Yadushailadeepa, overlooking the Cauvery valley. Melukote is about 51 km (32 miles) from Mysore and 133 km (83 mi) from Bangalore.

Melukote is the location of the Cheluvanarayana Swamy Temple, with a collection of crowns and jewels which are brought to the temple for the annual celebration. On the top of the hill is the temple of Yoganarasimha. Many more shrines and ponds are located in the town. Melukote is home to the Academy of Sanskrit Research, which has collected thousands of Vedic and Sanskrit manuscripts.

Early in the 12th century, the famous Srivaishnava saint Sri Ramanujacharya, who hailed from Tamil Nadu, stayed at Melukote for about 12 years. It has thus become a prominent centre of the Srivaishnava sect.
mgt:
Melukote, also known as Yadavagiri, is a historic town and pilgrimage center in the Mandya district of Karnataka, India. Situated 51 km (32 miles) from Mysore, it is renowned for its religious, cultural, and architectural significance. The town is home to the Cheluva-Narayana Swamy Temple, a prominent Vaishnavite shrine associated with the 12th-century saint Ramanujacharya, who resided here for 14 years, establishing it as a center of Srivaishnavism. The temple houses the Vairamudi crown, central to the annual Vairamudi Brahmotsava festival, which attracts thousands of devotees.

Melukote also features the Yoga-Narasimha Swamy Temple atop a hill, the Kalyani pond, and several other shrines. The town is known for its contributions to Sanskrit scholarship, with institutions like the Academy of Sanskrit Research and the Sri Veda Vedantha Bodhini Sanskrit College. Additionally, Melukote hosts the Melkote Temple Wildlife Sanctuary, preserving local flora and fauna, including wolves and blackbucks.

trgt: The Palm Court, also known at other times as the Franco-Italian Dining Room, the Grand Ballroom and the Continental Room, is a ballroom at the Hotel Alexandria in downtown Los Angeles, California. In its heyday from 1911 to 1922, it was the scene of speeches by U.S. Presidents William Howard Taft and Woodrow Wilson and Gen. John J. Pershing. It is also the room where Paul Whiteman, later known as the "Jazz King", got his start as a bandleader in 1919, where Rudolph Valentino danced with movie starlets, and where Hollywood held its most significant balls during the early days of the motion picture business. Known for its history and its stained-glass Tiffany skylight, noted Los Angeles columnist Jack Smith called it "surely the most beautiful room in Los Angeles". The Palm Court was designated as a City of Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument (HCM#80) in 1971.
mgt:
The Palm Court, located within the historic Alexandria Hotel in Los Angeles, is a renowned ballroom celebrated for its architectural beauty and cultural significance. Designed by John Parkinson as part of a 1911 expansion, the Palm Court quickly became the city's premier venue for high-profile events, hosting receptions for U.S. presidents, Hollywood stars, and prominent figures like Rudolph Valentino and Charlie Chaplin. The Alexandria Hotel, built in 1906, was a hub of social and business activity during its heyday, with its "million-dollar carpet" symbolizing its prestige.

Following the hotel's decline after the opening of the Biltmore in 1923, the Palm Court was repurposed for boxing events in the mid-20th century. Restored in the 1980s, it was praised as one of Los Angeles' most beautiful rooms. Designated a Historic-Cultural Monument in 1971, the Palm Court remains a symbol of the city's rich history and architectural heritage.

trgt:
A sponge city is a new urban construction model for flood management, strengthening ecological infrastructure and drainage systems, proposed by Chinese researchers in early 2000 and accepted by the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) and the State Council as urbanism policy in 2014. It can alleviate urban flooding, water resources shortage, and the urban heat island effect and improve the ecological environment and biodiversity by absorbing and capturing rain water and utilizing it to reduce floods. Rain water harvested can be repurposed for irrigation and for home use. It is a form of a sustainable drainage system on an urban scale and beyond. Sponge city policies are a set of nature based solutions that use natural landscapes to catch, store and clean water; the concept has been inspired by ancient wisdom of adaptation to climate challenges, particularly in the monsoon world.

“Sponge cities are part of a worldwide movement that goes by various names: green infrastructure in Europe, low-impact development in the United States, water-sensitive urban design in Australia, natural infrastructure in Peru, nature-based solutions in Canada. In contrast to industrial management, in which people confine water with levees, channels, and asphalt and rush it off the land as quickly as possible, these newer approaches seek to restore water’s natural tendency to linger in places like wetlands and floodplains.”
mgt:
A sponge city is an urban water management concept that aims to mitigate flooding, enhance water quality, and improve water reuse by mimicking natural hydrological processes. Originating in China, the initiative was adopted in response to the limitations of traditional grey infrastructure and the increasing frequency of urban flooding, notably after the 2012 Beijing flood. The approach emphasizes natural retention, infiltration, and purification of rainwater through green infrastructure such as wetlands, rain gardens, green roofs, and permeable surfaces. It also integrates ecological restoration and low-impact development to create sustainable urban environments.

China launched its sponge city initiative in 2015, selecting 16 pilot cities, followed by 14 more in 2016, with the goal of harvesting and reusing 70% of rainwater in 80% of urban areas by 2030. Despite challenges such as funding gaps, mismanagement, and resistance to shifting from conventional urban planning, the model has gained international attention, influencing cities in climate-vulnerable regions like Dhaka, Kenya, Berlin, and Los Angeles. The sponge city philosophy contrasts with traditional centralized water management by prioritizing distributed, nature-based solutions to urban water issues.

trgt:
The 1957 Alberta liquor plebiscite was a province-wide plebiscite conducted in Alberta, Canada The plebiscite asked voters if they were in favour of adding extra ALCB outlets in their district. It was held on October 30, 1957. At the time, the Alberta Liquor Control Board operated only a few stores from which liquor could be legally purchased.

A vote among egg producers was also held on the question of an egg marketing board.

An additional vote was held regarding mixed drinking in the major cities, also on October 30, 1957.

The 1957 votes were not held in conjunction with an Alberta election.
mgt: The 1957 Alberta liquor plebiscite was a province-wide vote held to address demands for loosening liquor regulations that had been in place since the Prohibition era. The plebiscite included two questions: one on expanding liquor store locations and another, in select urban areas, on allowing "mixed drinking" in beer parlours, which had been prohibited since 1928. The liquor store expansion was approved, with urban areas supporting it and rural areas opposing it, while the mixed drinking measure passed by a wide margin. The plebiscite also coincided with a vote among egg producers on establishing a compulsory marketing board, which later led to controversy.

trgt: The motive power depot (MPD) or locomotive depot, or traction maintenance depot (TMD), is the place where locomotives are usually housed, repaired and maintained when not being used. They were originally known as "running sheds", "engine sheds" or, for short, just sheds. Facilities are provided for refuelling and replenishing water, lubricating oil and grease and, for steam engines, disposal of the ash. There are often workshops for day to day repairs and maintenance, although locomotive building and major overhauls are usually carried out in the locomotive works. (Note: In American English, the term depot is used to refer to passenger stations or goods (freight) facilities and not to vehicle maintenance facilities.)
mgt:
A motive power depot, commonly known as an engine shed, is a facility used for the maintenance, repair, and storage of locomotives. Historically, these depots were essential during the steam era, providing services such as coaling, watering, ash removal, boiler washouts, and basic repairs. Engine sheds were often located in towns, cities, and industrial sites, with some specializing in passenger or freight locomotives. Designs varied, including straight, dead-end, and roundhouse layouts, often featuring turntables for locomotive turning.

With the transition to diesel and electric traction, many steam-era depots were replaced or modernized to accommodate new technologies. Today, some depots are operated by train manufacturers under contracts with operators, while smaller stabling and fueling points serve specific needs.

trgt: In mathematics and computer programming, exponentiating by squaring is a general method for fast computation of large positive integer powers of a number, or more generally of an element of a semigroup, like a polynomial or a square matrix. Some variants are commonly referred to as square-and-multiply algorithms or binary exponentiation. These can be of quite general use, for example in modular arithmetic or powering of matrices. For semigroups for which additive notation is commonly used, like elliptic curves used in cryptography, this method is also referred to as double-and-add.
mgt: Exponentiation by squaring is an efficient algorithm for computing large integer powers of a number. It is based on the observation that powers can be calculated recursively by squaring and multiplying, reducing the number of operations to a logarithmic scale compared to the linear approach. The method can be implemented recursively or iteratively, with variations that optimize memory usage or computational efficiency. It is widely used in applications such as modular arithmetic, cryptography, and matrix exponentiation, where fast computation of powers is essential. Advanced techniques, like the 2k-ary method and Montgomery's ladder, further enhance its efficiency and security.

trgt: Jatavarman Sundara I, also known as Sadayavarman Sundara Pandyan, was a emperor of the Pandyan dynasty who ruled regions of Tamilakkam (present day South India) between 1250–1268 CE. He is remembered for his patronage of the arts and tamil architecture, along with refurbishment and decoration of many Kovils (temple) in the Tamil continent. He oversaw a massive economic growth of the Pandyan empire. On the eve of his death in 1268 CE, the second Pandyan empire's power and territorial extent had risen to its zenith.
mgt: Jatavarman Sundara Pandyan I (reigned 1251–1268 CE) was a prominent emperor of the Pandyan dynasty in South India. Known for his military conquests, he defeated the Cheras, Cholas, Hoysalas, and Kadavas, expanding the Pandyan Empire to include parts of Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, and Sri Lanka. He also led successful campaigns in the north, annexing territories like Kanchipuram and Nellore. Sundara Pandyan was a patron of temple architecture, funding significant renovations and gold-plating temple roofs, including those at Chidambaram and Srirangam. He was succeeded by his son, Maravarman Kulasekara Pandyan I.

trgt: Antony Thekkek, also known as Thampy Antony, is an Indian-American film actor, writer, activist and producer. As a writer he has published books in Malayalam and English. He is the author of the books Koonampara County, Life of Ouso, Lady Biker and Vasco da Gama. As an actor, he is active primarily in Malayalam cinema. He is also the elder brother of Malayalam actor Babu Antony. He is the first Malayalam actor to own a Tesla car. The actor has won the Basheer Amma Malayalam Award for his book Vasco da Gama.
mgt: Antony Thekkek, also known as Thampy Antony, is an Indian-American actor, producer, and author, originally from Ponkunnam, Kerala, India. An architect by profession, he immigrated to the United States and has lived in the San Francisco Bay Area for over three decades. Antony made his acting debut in the film *Arabia* and has since appeared in numerous American and Indian productions, primarily in Malayalam cinema. He won the Best Actor Award at the Honolulu International Film Festival in 2005 for *Beyond the Soul*. He also produces films under the banner of Kayal Films.

trgt: Hostile vehicle mitigation (HVM) is a generic term that covers a suite of anti-terrorist protective measures that are often employed around buildings or publicly accessible spaces/venues of particular significance. The design of these various vehicle security barriers and landscape treatments came about as security authorities across the globe sought to mitigate the effects of vehicle borne improvised explosive devices (VBIED). The sorts of places that warrant consideration as potential terrorist targets in need of HVM include: government buildings, airports, large railway stations, sports venues, concentrations of entertainment and crowded night time economy, etc.
mgt: Hostile vehicle mitigation (HVM) refers to security measures designed to prevent or reduce the impact of vehicle-based attacks, such as vehicle ramming or vehicle-borne improvised explosive devices (VBIEDs). Common HVM solutions include crash-tested barriers, bollards, reinforced street furniture, and architectural features integrated into urban landscapes. These systems are tested to withstand specific vehicle impacts and are often implemented with protective standoff distances to mitigate blast effects. HVM is used to safeguard buildings, crowded spaces, and high-value targets, such as museums and jewelry stores, while maintaining pedestrian accessibility and urban functionality.

trgt:
Steven Williams (born January 7, 1949) is an American actor in films and television. He is known for his roles as Captain Adam Fuller on 21 Jump Street, Lt. Jefferson Burnett on The Equalizer, Det. August Brooks on L.A. Heat, X on The X-Files, Russell "Linc" Lincoln in Linc's, and Rufus Turner in Supernatural.

Throughout his career, Williams has appeared in numerous films, including The Blues Brothers (1980), Twilight Zone: The Movie (1983), Jason Goes to Hell: The Final Friday (1993), 22 Jump Street (2014), It (2017), and Birds of Prey (2020).

He has been nominated for a Screen Actors Guild Award and one NAACP Image Award.
mgt: Steven Williams (born January 7, 1949) is an American actor known for his extensive work in film and television since 1975. He gained prominence for his role as Captain Adam Fuller on the Fox series *21 Jump Street* (1987–1991) and as X on the science fiction series *The X-Files*, earning a Screen Actors Guild nomination in 1997. Williams has also appeared in *The Chi*, *Supernatural*, and *Locke & Key*, among other series. His film credits include *The Blues Brothers* (1980) and *Jason Goes to Hell: The Final Friday* (1993). Married to talent agent Ann Geddes, he has two daughters.

trgt:
HIV is recognized as a health concern in Pakistan with the number of cases growing. Moderately high drug use and lack of acceptance that non-marital sex is common in the society have allowed the HIV epidemic to take hold in Pakistan, mainly among injecting drug users (IDU), male, female and transvestite sex workers (MSW, FSW and TSW) as well as the repatriated migrant workers. HIV infection can lead to AIDS that may become a major health issue.

The National AIDS Programme's latest figures show that by the mid-2000s, the number of HIV cases had increased to approximately 0,102 million. This number was estimated little over 4,000, as the HIV cases reported since 1986. The UN and government estimated the number of HIV/AIDS cases around 97,000 ranging from a lowest estimate of 46,000 to a highest estimate of 210,000.

More realistic estimates that are based on actual surveillance figures, however, suggest that this number may be closer to 40,000–45,000. The overall prevalence of HIV infection in adults aged 15 to 49 is 0.1%. (0.05% if one accepts the lower estimates). Officials say that the majority of cases go unreported due to social taboos about sex and victims’ fears of discrimination. The other reason for all the cases not being reported is that HIV is a disease either not systematically tested for or reported in the routine surveillance system.
mgt:
HIV/AIDS in Pakistan is a growing public health concern, with the epidemic evolving from a low-prevalence phase to a concentrated epidemic since the early 2000s. The disease is primarily concentrated among high-risk groups, including people who inject drugs (27.2% prevalence), transgender sex workers (5.2%), and male sex workers (1.6%), while prevalence among female sex workers remains low (0.6%). Geographic trends show the epidemic expanding from major urban centers to smaller cities and peripheral areas. Contributing factors include unsafe therapeutic injection practices, with Pakistan having one of the highest rates of injection use globally, and cultural stigmas that hinder HIV testing and surveillance.

Pakistan's response began in 1987 with the establishment of the National AIDS Control Program, which has since implemented prevention, surveillance, and treatment initiatives. Efforts were bolstered in 2004 with international funding, enabling targeted interventions for high-risk populations and the provision of free HIV treatment. However, challenges persist, including low service coverage, stigma, and limited integration of HIV programs into broader health systems. Future strategies focus on improving monitoring, expanding access to care, and addressing systemic inefficiencies. Despite these efforts, the epidemic remains concentrated in high-risk groups, with limited data on its spread in the general population due to cultural barriers and inadequate surveillance.

trgt:
The 2017 Ole Miss Rebels football team represented the University of Mississippi in the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season. The Rebels played their home games at Vaught–Hemingway Stadium in Oxford, Mississippi and competed in the Western Division of the Southeastern Conference (SEC). They were led by then-interim head coach Matt Luke. They finished the season 6–6, 3–5 in SEC play to finish in sixth place in the Western Division.

On February 22, 2017, the University of Mississippi self-imposed a one-year ban from post-season play on the Ole Miss football team for the 2017 season, due to alleged NCAA violations. On July 20, 2017, head coach Hugh Freeze resigned in the wake of allegations about repeated calls to a female escort service from his school-issued cell phone. He was given the ultimatum, either resign or be fired.
mgt:
The 2017 Ole Miss Rebels football team represented the University of Mississippi in the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season as a member of the Southeastern Conference (SEC). Led by first-year head coach Matt Luke, the Rebels played their home games at Vaught–Hemingway Stadium in Oxford, Mississippi, and finished the season with a 6–6 record (3–5 in SEC play). The team faced significant challenges, including an NCAA investigation that resulted in 21 charges, including 15 Level I violations, and self-imposed penalties such as a postseason ban, scholarship reductions, and recruiting restrictions.

The season also saw major coaching staff changes, including the hiring of Wesley McGriff as defensive coordinator and Jacob Peeler as wide receivers coach. The Rebels' schedule featured seven home games and five away games, including matchups against SEC opponents and their first-ever game against a Pac-12 team, the California Golden Bears.

trgt: FC Emmen is a Dutch football club based in Emmen, Drenthe. They play in the Eredivisie, the top tier of Dutch football, after winning the 2021–22 Eerste Divisie title. Founded in 1925, the club entered the professional Eerste Divisie in 1985. In 2018, Emmen were promoted to the Eredivisie for the first time, but in 2021 they were relegated to the Eerste Divisie again. 2022 they were promoted again and play the 2022-2023 season in the Eredivisie again. Home games are played at De Oude Meerdijk.
mgt: Football Club Emmen, commonly known as FC Emmen, is a Dutch professional football club based in Emmen, Drenthe. Founded on 21 August 1925, the club initially operated as an amateur team before joining the professional ranks in 1985. FC Emmen plays its home matches at De Oude Meerdijk, which has a capacity of 8,600. The club achieved its first promotion to the Eredivisie, the top tier of Dutch football, in 2018 and remained there for three seasons before being relegated in 2021. They returned to the Eredivisie in 2022 after winning the Eerste Divisie.

trgt:
The AMA Motocross Championship (commercially known as Lucas Oil Pro Motocross) is an American motorcycle racing series. The motocross race series was founded and sanctioned by the American Motorcyclist Association (AMA) in 1972. The series is the major outdoor motocross series in the United States and is sanctioned by AMA Pro Racing and managed by MX Sports Pro Racing.

With respect to the MXGP holding the discipline's worldwide title, and the advent of the Supercross World Championship resulting in the AMA Supercross Championship losing its world title status, the two series will form the SuperMotocross World Championship from 2023.
mgt: The AMA Motocross Championship is an American motorcycle racing series sanctioned by the American Motorcyclist Association (AMA) that began in 1972. Initially featuring 500 cc and 250 cc classes, a 125 cc class was added in 1974. Over time, the series transitioned from two-stroke to four-stroke engine dominance, with class structures evolving to reflect this shift. The championship includes two 30-minute motos per round, with winners determined by combined points. The AMA also oversees Supercross, a stadium-based offshoot of motocross introduced in the 1970s, which features shorter races and separate championships for 250 cc and 450 cc classes.

trgt:
The Bab Ballads is a collection of light verses by W. S. Gilbert (1836–1911), illustrated with his own comic drawings. The book takes its title from Gilbert's childhood nickname. He later began to sign his illustrations "Bab". Gilbert wrote the "ballads" collected in the book before he became famous for his comic opera librettos with Arthur Sullivan. In writing these verses Gilbert developed his "topsy-turvy" style in which the humour is derived by setting up a ridiculous premise and working out its logical consequences, however absurd. The ballads also reveal Gilbert's cynical and satirical approach to humour.

They became famous on their own, as well as being a source for plot elements, characters and songs that Gilbert recycled in the Gilbert and Sullivan operas. They were read aloud at private dinner-parties, at public banquets and even in the House of Lords. The ballads have been much published, and some have been recorded or otherwise adapted.
mgt:
The *Bab Ballads* is a collection of humorous poems written and illustrated by W. S. Gilbert, best known as the librettist of the Gilbert and Sullivan operas. The poems, which first appeared in the satirical magazine *Fun* between 1865 and 1871, were later compiled into several collected editions, beginning with *The "Bab" Ballads – Much Sound and Little Sense* in 1869. The title derives from Gilbert's childhood nickname, "Bab." Known for their wit, absurdity, and satirical commentary, the *Bab Ballads* were accompanied by Gilbert's own whimsical illustrations.

The poems gained popularity and influenced Gilbert's later work in comic opera. Subsequent editions, including *Songs of a Savoyard* (1890) and *The Bab Ballads, with which are included Songs of a Savoyard* (1898), integrated lyrics from his operas. The *Bab Ballads* have remained in print for decades, with modern editions expanding the canon and adaptations for recordings, music, and stage performances.

trgt: Uncle Jam Wants You is the eleventh studio album by American funk rock band Funkadelic. It was originally released by Warner Bros. Records on September 21, 1979, and was later reissued on CD by Charly Groove Records and Priority Records. It was produced by George Clinton under the alias Dr. Funkenstein. It is the first Funkadelic album since America Eats Its Young in 1972 not to sport a cover illustrated by Funkadelic artist Pedro Bell, though Bell did provide artwork for the album’s back cover and interior. Uncle Jam Wants You was the second Funkadelic album to be certified gold. The album peaked at #18 on the US Billboard 200 and #2 on the US Billboard Top R&B/Hip-Hop Albums charts.
mgt: *Uncle Jam Wants You* is the eleventh studio album by the American funk band Funkadelic, released on September 21, 1979, by Warner Bros. Records. Produced by George Clinton, the album serves as a more militant sequel to *One Nation Under a Groove* (1978), aiming to "rescue dance music from the blahs" and provoke a cultural shift into Funkadelia. Its cover art, featuring Clinton in a Huey Newton-inspired pose, reflects the album's martial themes. The record includes Funkadelic's last major hit, "(Not Just) Knee Deep," which topped the Billboard Soul singles chart and became highly influential in West Coast hip-hop, inspiring artists like De La Soul and Dr. Dre.

trgt: Solder paste is used in the manufacture of printed circuit boards to connect surface mount components to pads on the board. It is also possible to solder through-hole pin in paste components by printing solder paste in and over the holes. The sticky paste temporarily holds components in place; the board is then heated, melting the paste and forming a mechanical bond as well as an electrical connection. The paste is applied to the board by jet printing, stencil printing or syringe; then the components are put in place by a pick-and-place machine or by hand.
mgt: Solder paste is a material used in the assembly of printed circuit boards (PCBs) to create solder joints between components and the board. It consists of powdered solder suspended in flux, which provides tackiness to hold components in place during the reflow soldering process. Solder paste is applied using stencil printing, pin transfer, or jet printing, and its properties, such as viscosity and flux type, are critical for successful application. Classified by particle size and flux type, solder pastes are predominantly lead-free due to environmental regulations. Proper storage and evaluation are essential to minimize defects during PCB manufacturing.

trgt:
Typhoon Hagupit, known in the Philippines as Severe Tropical Storm Dindo, was a Category 1 typhoon that heavily impacted Eastern China and South Korea in August 2020. It was the fourth named storm and the second typhoon of the annual typhoon season. The JMA began monitoring a tropical depression that developed in the Philippine Sea on July 30, with PAGASA assigning the name “Dindo” to the storm, later that day it strengthened into a tropical storm, according to the JMA. PAGASA issued its final advisory on Dindo early on August 3, as it moved out of their area of responsibility. Hagupit intensified into a typhoon on August 3, before making landfall in Wenzhou, China at 19:30 UTC that day at peak intensity. Hagupit subsequently weakened over China, before degenerating into an extratropical low on August 5. Hagupit's remnants persisted for another several days, as the storm moved eastward, before dissipating south of Alaska on August 14.

Hagupit caused over a foot of rainfall in portions of Eastern China and South Korea. Hagupit caused 17 fatalities due to bringing heavy rains in the Korean Peninsula, and triggered a landslide which killed 6 people. Damage totals was reported to be at US$411 million.
mgt:
Typhoon Hagupit, known in the Philippines as Tropical Storm Dindo, was a Category 1 typhoon that impacted East Asia in early August 2020 during the 2020 Pacific typhoon season. Forming on July 30, Hagupit intensified in the Philippine Sea before making landfall in Zhejiang, China, on August 3 with winds of 85 mph (140 km/h) and a pressure of 975 hPa. The storm caused significant damage in China, including torrential rainfall, flooding, and power outages affecting over 830,000 customers. Economic losses in Wenzhou alone reached $411 million, and at least one fatality was reported in the country.

Hagupit also brought heavy rainfall to Taiwan, the Ryukyu Islands, and the Korean Peninsula, exacerbating severe flooding in South Korea, where 15 people died and 11 were reported missing. The storm transitioned into an extratropical cyclone on August 6 and dissipated south of Alaska on August 14. In total, Hagupit caused 17 fatalities, 11 missing persons, and widespread damage across its path.

trgt:
Sir Edward Aloysius McTiernan, KBE (16 February 1892 – 9 January 1990), was an Australian lawyer, politician, and judge. He served on the High Court of Australia from 1930 to 1976, the longest-serving judge in the court's history.

McTiernan was born in Glen Innes, New South Wales. He graduated from the University of Sydney in 1915, and was called to the bar the following year. McTiernan was elected to the New South Wales Legislative Assembly in 1920, representing the Labor Party, and was soon after appointed Attorney-General of New South Wales. He served as attorney-general under John Storey, James Dooley, and Jack Lang, but left state politics in 1927. McTiernan was elected to the House of Representatives in 1929, but served for little over a year before Prime Minister James Scullin nominated him to the High Court. He was 38 at the time; only H. V. Evatt (another Scullin nominee) was appointed at a younger age. On the court, McTiernan was considered a moderate, and was known for the consistency of his decisions. He generally favoured the position of the federal government, upholding the constitutionality of contentious legislation from both sides of politics. McTiernan retired reluctantly at the age of 84, after just under 46 years on the High Court bench. He lived to the age of 97, and was the last surviving MP from the 1920s.
mgt:
Sir Edward Aloysius McTiernan KBE (16 February 1892 – 9 January 1990) was an Australian jurist, politician, and the longest-serving justice in the history of the High Court of Australia, holding office from 1930 to 1976. Born in Glen Innes, New South Wales, to Irish Catholic immigrants, McTiernan studied law at the University of Sydney, graduating with first-class honors. He began his career as a barrister and later entered politics, serving as Attorney-General of New South Wales and as a member of both the New South Wales Legislative Assembly and the Australian House of Representatives.

Appointed to the High Court at the age of 38 by Prime Minister James Scullin, McTiernan participated in landmark cases, including *Bank of New South Wales v Commonwealth* and *Australian Communist Party v The Commonwealth*. He was knighted in 1951 and served under five Chief Justices. His tenure ended in 1976 after a record 46 years, following a hip injury.

McTiernan also conducted a secret wartime inquiry into aircraft safety in 1943. He married Kathleen Lloyd in 1948 but had no children. McTiernan died in 1990 at the age of 97 and was buried in Rookwood Cemetery. His legacy includes his significant contributions to Australian law and his unparalleled judicial service.

trgt:
The Doughnut is the nickname given to the headquarters of the Government Communications Headquarters (GCHQ), a British cryptography and intelligence agency. It is located on a 71 hectares (176 acres) site in Benhall, in the suburbs of Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, in South West England. The Doughnut houses 5,500 employees; GCHQ is the largest single employer in Gloucestershire. Built to modernise and consolidate GCHQ's multiple buildings in Cheltenham, the Doughnut was completed in 2003, with GCHQ staff moving in the same year, and fully moved into the building in 2004. It is the largest building constructed for secret intelligence operations outside the United States.

The Doughnut was too small for the number of staff at its completion, and a second building in a secret and undisclosed location in the 'Gloucestershire area' now also accommodates staff from GCHQ. The Doughnut is surrounded by car and bicycle parking in concentric rings, and is well protected by security fencing, guards, and CCTV systems.

The construction of the building was financed by a private finance initiative, and construction costs were greatly increased after difficulties in transferring computer infrastructure to the building. The building is modern in design, and built primarily from steel, aluminium, and stone. GCHQ management aspired for the building to be as well known internationally as the Pentagon.
mgt:
The Doughnut is the headquarters of the UK Government Communications Headquarters (GCHQ), located in Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, England. Completed in 2003 and officially opened in 2004 by Queen Elizabeth II, the building consolidates GCHQ's operations into a single site, replacing over 50 buildings. Designed by architect Chris Johnson of Gensler in a structural expressionist style, the circular structure spans 200 meters in diameter and features a central open-air courtyard, large enough to hold the Royal Albert Hall. It houses advanced facilities, including a 24/7 operations center, supercomputers, and extensive archives.

Constructed at a cost of £337 million under a private finance initiative, the project faced significant cost overruns due to the complexity of transferring GCHQ's computer systems. The Doughnut is designed for security and efficiency, with features to withstand potential threats and facilitate collaboration among staff. It includes amenities such as restaurants, a gym, and a prayer room.

The building has hosted visits from notable figures, including Prime Ministers and members of the royal family. It has also been a site for charitable initiatives, such as forming a giant poppy for the Royal British Legion and lighting displays supporting LGBTQ and other causes. Despite its size, the Doughnut has faced challenges accommodating GCHQ's growing workforce.

trgt: "Winter" is a song by rock band U2. The track was originally planned to be included on the band's 2009 album No Line on the Horizon, but it was cut at the end of the recording sessions as it did not fit the album's theme. The song does appear on the accompanying film Linear. "Winter" was written for the 2009 war film Brothers at the request of director Jim Sheridan, and it plays over the closing credits. Several different versions of the song have been recorded, including an uptempo rock arrangement on Linear and a slower acoustic version in Brothers.
