Recognized for its innovation, the project has inspired similar initiatives and serves as a model for open-source collaboration in Jewish cultural preservation and practice.

trgt:
The Children's Museum of the Arts (“CMA”) is located at 103 Charlton Street, Manhattan, New York, United States in the South Village district. Founded by Kathleen Schneider in 1988, CMA opened its new, 10,000-square-foot space in October 2011.

At CMA, kids ages 0–15 work with teaching artists to create art, including drawings, sculpture, sound art, textiles, and stop-motion animation. As part of its mission to promote art for all children, CMA features programs and partnerships designed for children with learning disabilities, children in foster care, and children in homeless families.

The museum maintains a collection of over 2,000 pieces of international children's art dating back to the 1930s from over 50 different countries.
mgt: The Children's Museum of the Arts (CMA) is a New York City-based museum dedicated to engaging children and their families in the arts through exhibitions and creative programs. Notable exhibitions have included “Make Art (in) Public,” featuring works by Keith Haring and Christo and Jeanne-Claude, as well as Ian Berry's denim installation "Secret Garden" in 2017. CMA also offers art programs for diverse groups, including children with autism, families in transitional housing, and low-income teenage parents. These programs are conducted both at the museum and through outreach initiatives, emphasizing accessibility and creative expression for underserved communities.

trgt: The Navy Midshipmen men's lacrosse team represents the United States Naval Academy in National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) Division I men's lacrosse. Navy currently competes as a member of the Patriot League and play their home games at Navy–Marine Corps Memorial Stadium in Annapolis, Maryland. During the 20th century, the Midshipmen secured 17 national championships, including 2 United States Intercollegiate Lacrosse Association titles and 15 Wingate Memorial Trophy awards. During the 1960s, a period of dominance for the Midshipmen, they won eight consecutive titles. The program's main rivals include Army, Maryland, and Johns Hopkins.
mgt: The Navy Midshipmen men's lacrosse team represents the United States Naval Academy in NCAA Division I lacrosse as a member of the Patriot League. Established in 1908, the program has a storied history, including 17 pre-NCAA national championships and eight NCAA Final Four appearances. The team experienced a "Decade of Dominance" in the 1960s, winning eight consecutive national titles under coach Willis Bilderback. Navy joined the Patriot League in 2004, winning five conference tournament championships. The team plays at Navy–Marine Corps Memorial Stadium in Annapolis, Maryland, and is currently coached by Joe Amplo, who began in 2020.

trgt: William W. Arnold (commonly "W. W."; August 28, 1843 – March 30, 1923) was a physician better known across the country for his avocation as an ornithologist. Born in Connersville, Indiana, he initially practiced medicine near Rushville, Indiana. In 1886, he visited Colorado and decided he would like to move there and he settled in Colorado Springs in October of that year. He operated a practice in downtown Colorado Springs and compounded medicine for his patients. He is best known as the founder and operator of a bird hospital, where he cared for ill and injured birds. He took care of domestic birds and had a large influx of wild birds after bad weather, like hail storms. He wrote articles about nature and birds, and he gave talks about them to school children in Colorado and Indiana. He worked as a physician and caregiver for the bird hospital until shortly before his death at age 79.
mgt: William W. Arnold (August 28, 1843 – March 30, 1923) was an American physician and ornithologist known for his contributions to bird protection and wildlife advocacy. Born in Connersville, Indiana, he practiced medicine in Colorado Springs from 1886 until his death, becoming a respected member of the medical community. In his later years, Arnold became an active ornithologist, joining the American Ornithologists' Union and the Cooper Ornithological Club. He and his wife, Mary Evelina Shaw, operated a bird hospital beginning around 1913, where they treated injured wild and domestic birds, often releasing them back into the wild. Arnold also wrote articles on bird care and conservation, including the story "Stubby, The Bird Evangelist," and served as an associate editor for *Colorado Sky*. A member of the Colorado Audubon Society, he frequently advocated for bird protection and educated children about wildlife. Arnold died of influenza in 1923 and is buried in Colorado Springs.

trgt:
The Fairchild 9440 MICROFLAME, also known as the F9440 and μFLAME, was a 16-bit microprocessor introduced by Fairchild Semiconductor in 1977. The 9440 implemented the Data General Nova 2's instruction set in a single-chip 40-pin DIP. The name "MICROFLAME" was part of a wider branding exercise called "FIRE", which was a development software system.

An updated version, the 9445, was announced in 1978 but did not reach market until late 1981. By this time the 16-bit designs were being surpassed by 32-bit designs and hybrids like the Motorola 68000, and Fairchild began turning their attention to their own 32-bit Fairchild Clipper design. The underlying core of the 9445 was also used to implement the 9450, which used new microcode to implement the MIL-STD-1750A instruction set.

The 9440 and 9445 were subject to constant lawsuits from Data General (DG) that dragged on both companies. DG finally settled all ongoing litigation in September 1986 by paying Fairchild over $52 million. Fairchild was purchased and sold several times during this period, ending up at National Semiconductor in 1987. National ended production of the 9445, leaving several users in the lurch. A final version, the IDC9445, was introduced to fill this need.
mgt:
The Fairchild 9440 was a single-chip implementation of the Data General (DG) Nova minicomputer's instruction set, developed by Fairchild Semiconductor in the late 1970s. It was based on Fairchild's proprietary 3-micron bipolar Isoplanar Integrated Injection Logic (I3L) process, which allowed it to run faster and require simpler power supply requirements compared to DG's microNOVA mN601. The 9440 executed the Nova 2 instruction set in microcode, offering comparable performance to the Nova 1200, and included features like an integrated clock generator and direct memory access (DMA) support. However, it lacked certain advanced features of the mN601, such as hardware multiply and divide, which could be added via optional components.

Fairchild later introduced the 9445, an improved version using a 2-micron process. It implemented the Nova 3 instruction set, added hardware multiply and divide, expanded memory addressing, and significantly increased performance. Despite these advancements, production delays and legal disputes with Data General over licensing and competition hindered the 9440 and 9445's success. The 9445 eventually became obsolete as the minicomputer market declined. Fairchild's Nova-based processor line ended after its acquisition by National Semiconductor in 1987, though the design briefly continued as the ICD9445 under Strobe Data.

trgt:
A homewrecker (sometimes styled as home wrecker or home-wrecker) is a person, object or activity that causes or comes close to causing the breakup of a marriage (or similar partnership). The homewrecker is said to have taken one of the spouses away from the marriage, thus "wrecking" the marital home.

Most commonly, the label "homewrecker" is applied to a person having an affair with someone else's spouse or domestic partner; it can also refer to other forces that are destructive to a marital relationship and tied only to one party to that relationship.
mgt: The term "homewrecker" is commonly used to describe a person who disrupts a pre-existing relationship, often through an affair, with the intent of replacing one partner. It can also refer to other factors that damage relationships, such as addictions, domestic violence, or excessive devotion to careers or hobbies. The label is sometimes disputed, particularly in cases where relationships were already troubled. The term has been used in arts, including titles of songs and films, and even for cocktails. Scientific studies have also explored its metaphorical use in describing certain behavioral phenomena.

trgt:
The mandolin is a modern member of the lute family, dating back to Italy in the 18th century. The instrument was played across Europe but then disappeared after the Napoleonic Wars. Credit for creating the modern bowlback version of the instrument goes to the Vinaccia family of Naples. The deep bowled mandolin, especially the Neapolitan form, became common in the 19th century, following the appearance of an international hit, the Spanish Students. They toured Europe and America, and their performances created a stir that helped the mandolin to become widely popular.

Although the modern instruments date to the 18th century, ancestral instruments of similar construction and range, the mandore and gittern, were used across Europe (including Spain, Italy, England, France, Germany and Poland) centuries earlier. These instruments developed from short-handled lutes that entered Christian Europe from Muslim Sicily and Spain. Muslims picked these instruments in Central Asia, calling them barbat and oud. Residents of Asia were playing them as far back as the 2nd century A.D.
mgt:
The history of the mandolin traces its origins to ancient stringed instruments, evolving over millennia into the modern mandolin. Early precursors include the musical bow, which led to the development of harps, lyres, and lutes. The first lute-like instruments appeared in Mesopotamia around 3100 BC, with further advancements in Bactria, Gandhara, and the Islamic world, where the barbat evolved into the oud. The oud was introduced to Europe via Andalusia and Sicily, influencing the development of European lutes.

By the 16th century, the mandore, a small lute, emerged in Europe, eventually evolving into the mandola and later the mandolino in Italy. The Neapolitan mandolin, developed by Pasquale Vinaccia in the 19th century, introduced steel strings and modernized features, becoming the standard mandolin design. The instrument experienced a "Golden Age" of popularity in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with widespread use in orchestras and social settings. Today, the mandolin remains integral to various musical genres, including classical, bluegrass, and jazz.

trgt:
The Simla Conference of 1945 was a meeting between the Viceroy of India Lord Wavell and the major political leaders of British India at the Viceregal Lodge in Simla. When it was clear that British intended to leave India, they desperately needed an agreement on what should happen when they leave.

Talks, however, stalled on the issue of the selection of Muslim representatives. Seeking to assert itself and its claim to be the sole representative of Indian Muslims, the All-India Muslim League refused to back any plan in which the Indian National Congress, the dominant party in the talks, appointed Muslim representatives. This scuttled the conference, and perhaps the last viable opportunity for a united, independent India. When the Indian National Congress and the All India Muslim League reconvened under the Cabinet Mission the next year, the Indian National Congress was far less sympathetic to the Muslim League's requests despite Jinnah's approval of the British plan.

On 14 June 1945 Lord Wavell announced a plan for a new Executive Council in which all members except the Viceroy and the Commander in Chief would be Indians. This executive council was to be a temporary measure until a new permanent constitution could be agreed upon and come into force. All portfolios except Defense would be held by Indian members.
mgt:
The Simla Conference, held from June 25 to July 14, 1945, was a high-level political meeting convened by the Viceroy of India, Lord Wavell, in Simla, British India. The conference aimed to address the communal deadlock between the Indian National Congress and the All-India Muslim League and to discuss the Wavell Plan, which proposed the reconstitution of the Viceroy's Executive Council with equal representation for high-caste Hindus and Muslims, alongside other Indian communities. The plan sought to advance Indian self-governance while maintaining British control over defense and trade.

Despite initial optimism, the conference failed due to disagreements between Congress and the Muslim League, particularly over the League's demand to be recognized as the sole representative of Indian Muslims. The failure of the Simla Conference underscored the deepening communal divide and set the stage for subsequent political developments, including the Cabinet Mission of 1946. The conference is considered a pivotal moment in the lead-up to Indian independence and the eventual partition of India.

trgt: I Even Met Happy Gypsies is a 1967 Yugoslav film by Serbian director Aleksandar Petrović. Its original Serbian title is Skupljači perja, which means The Feather Gatherers. The film is centered on Romani people's life in a village in northern Vojvodina, but it also deals with subtler themes such as love, ethnic and social relationships. Beside Bekim Fehmiu, Olivera Vučo, Bata Živojinović and Mija Aleksić, film features a cast of Romani actors speaking the Romani language. I Even Met Happy Gypsies is considered one of the best films of the Black Wave in Yugoslav cinema.
mgt: *I Even Met Happy Gypsies* is a 1967 Yugoslav film directed by Aleksandar Petrović. The story follows Beli Bora, a Romani man, and Tisa, a young woman, as they navigate love, conflict, and exile from their community. The film explores themes of Romani life and culture, with a cast led by Bekim Fehmiu, Olivera Vučo, and Gordana Jovanović. It was a critical success, winning the Special Grand Prize of the Jury and the FIPRESCI Prize at the 1967 Cannes Film Festival. It was also nominated for an Academy Award and a Golden Globe for Best Foreign Language Film.

trgt: The Canajoharie Creek (/ˌkænədʒəˈhɛəri/) is a river that flows into the Mohawk River in the Village of Canajoharie in the U.S. State of New York. The name "Canajoharie" is a Mohawk language term meaning "the pot that washes itself", referring to the "Canajoharie Boiling Pot", a 20-foot (6.1 m) wide and 10-foot (3.0 m) deep pothole in the Canajoharie Creek, just south of the village of Canajoharie. Bowmans Creek is one main tributary that enters the creek east of the Hamlet of Sprout Brook. The other main tributary is Brimstone Creek which enters the creek north-northwest of the Village of Ames.
mgt: Canajoharie Creek is a 34-mile-long (55 km) tributary of the Mohawk River located in Central New York, flowing through Otsego and Montgomery counties. Originating north of Cherry Valley, the creek features notable waterfalls, including Judds Falls and Canajoharie Falls, and passes through the Canajoharie Gorge before emptying into the Mohawk River in the village of Canajoharie. The creek's name, derived from the Mohawk language, means "the pot that washes itself," referencing a distinctive pothole formation known as the "boiling pot." Historically, the creek was significant to early European settlers and the Mohawk people, and it remains a site for fishing and geological interest.

page_title: Victory Day over Nazism in World War II
trgt: Victory Day over Nazism in World War II (Lang-uk) or Victory Day (Lang-uk) is a national holiday and a day off in Ukraine. It was first celebrated on 9 May 2015 and follows the Day of Remembrance and Reconciliation on May 8 (which pays tribute to the victims of World War II and was also first celebrated in 2015). The holiday replaced the Soviet Union/Russian Federation "Victory Day", which was celebrated in the post-Soviet Union states, including Ukraine, until 2014 inclusive.
mgt: Victory Day over Nazism in World War II is a Ukrainian national holiday observed annually on May 9. Established in 2015 as part of Ukraine's decommunization efforts, it replaced the Soviet-era Victory Day and emphasizes remembrance over celebration, akin to the United Kingdom's Remembrance Day. The holiday prohibits the use of Soviet and Nazi symbols and avoids the term "Great Patriotic War," favoring "World War II." Its official symbol is the red poppy, and its motto is "1939–1945. We remember. We prevail."

trgt:
Carfilzomib, sold under the brand name Kyprolis, is an anti-cancer medication acting as a selective proteasome inhibitor. Chemically, it is a tetrapeptide epoxyketone and an analog of epoxomicin. It was developed by Onyx Pharmaceuticals.

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved it on 20 July 2012, for use in people with multiple myeloma who have received at least two prior therapies, including treatment with bortezomib and an immunomodulatory therapy (such as lenalidomide) and have demonstrated disease progression on or within 60 days of completion of the last therapy. Initial approval was based on response rate. Data demonstrating an Overall Survival (OS) benefit was later demonstrated in the ENDEAVOR trial and approved by the FDA.

The abbreviation CFZ is common for referring to carfilzomib, but abbreviating drug names is not best practice in medicine.
mgt:
Carfilzomib, sold under the brand name Kyprolis, is a proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma. Administered intravenously, it works by irreversibly inhibiting the chymotrypsin-like activity of the 20S proteasome, leading to the accumulation of polyubiquitinated proteins, which induces cell cycle arrest and apoptosis. Carfilzomib was developed from the natural product epoxomicin and advanced through clinical trials by Proteolix, later acquired by Onyx Pharmaceuticals. It received FDA approval in 2012 based on Phase II trials demonstrating efficacy in heavily pretreated patients.

Clinical trials, including the ASPIRE trial, have shown improved progression-free survival and overall response rates when combined with lenalidomide and dexamethasone. Common side effects include hematologic toxicity, gastrointestinal disturbances, and cardiovascular events. Carfilzomib is considered a significant advancement in multiple myeloma therapy, particularly for patients resistant to prior treatments.

trgt: Francisco de Holanda (originally Francisco d'Olanda; 6 September 1517 – 19 June 1585) was a Portuguese court painter and sculptor for King John III of Portugal, and later for Sebastian of Portugal. He wrote what is regarded as the first treatise on portrait painting in Europe, Do tirar polo natural (1549). He is considered to be one of the most important figures of the Portuguese Renaissance, also being an essayist, architect and historian. He represented the intelligible reality of the Holy Trinity through a "hypothetical" syntax of geometrical figures. He insisted on the contrast between the ideal plane, the incorporeal form and the "imperfect copy in the terrestrial zone". His visual language demonstrated a mixture of Neoplatonism, Christian Kabbalah and finally Lullism. In education, Francisco de Holanda emphasized mathematics and geometry, subsequently anticipating Clavius's reforms of the late 16th century. Sylvie Deswarte said that "Francisco de Holanda gives a privileged place to cosmography and astrology in the education of the painter. On par with geometry, mathematics and perspective, he recommended them. in order to reach the heavens in the hope of one day arriving to the Empyrean and realising celestial works."
mgt:
Francisco de Holanda (6 September 1517 – 19 June 1585) was a Portuguese Renaissance architect, sculptor, painter, essayist, and historian. Born in Lisbon, he began his career as a royal illuminator, following in the footsteps of his father, António de Holanda. Between 1538 and 1547, he studied in Italy, where he interacted with prominent figures of the Italian Renaissance, including Vittoria Colonna and Michelangelo, who greatly influenced his embrace of Classicism. Upon returning to Portugal, he worked under the patronage of King John III, King Sebastian, and the Cardinal-Archbishop of Évora.

Francisco is best known for his treatise *On Ancient Painting* (*Da Pintura Antiga*, 1548), which includes dialogues with Michelangelo and reflects his passion for Renaissance aesthetics, emphasizing originality, symmetry, and the connection between nature and classical antiquity. He also created a series of drawings on Italian antiquities and designed the façade of the Church of Our Lady of Grace in Évora. Additionally, he authored the first essay on urbanism in the Iberian Peninsula, *On the construction lacking to the city of Lisbon* (*Da fábrica que falece à cidade de Lisboa*), and other notable works on art and history.

trgt:
Loop (ループ, Rūpu) is a horror novel by Japanese writer Koji Suzuki, and the third in his series of Ring.

The story revolves around a simulated reality, exactly the same as our own, known as the Loop: created to simulate the emergence and evolution of life. It is in this alternate universe that the events of the previous novels, Ring and Spiral took place.

Loop is the only book in the series to have not been the basis of any Ring films.
mgt: *Loop* (ループ, *Rūpu*) is a 1998 horror and science fiction novel by Japanese author Koji Suzuki, and the third installment in the *Ring* series. The story follows Kaoru Futami, a medical student who uncovers the origins of a deadly disease, Metastatic Human Cancer (MHC), linked to a virtual reality experiment called the LOOP project. As Kaoru delves deeper, he discovers shocking truths about his own identity and the connection between the LOOP simulation and the real world. The novel was translated into English in 2006.

trgt: Casey Cizikas (born February 27, 1991) is a Canadian professional ice hockey centre for the New York Islanders of the National Hockey League (NHL). He spent four seasons with the Mississauga St. Michaels Majors of the Ontario Hockey League (OHL), where he had two 20-goal seasons. Cizikas was selected by the Islanders in the fourth round, 92nd overall, of the 2009 NHL Entry Draft. He began playing for their minor league affiliate, the American Hockey League (AHL)'s Bridgeport Sound Tigers, two years after the draft, during the 2011–12 season; he was recalled to the Islanders' NHL roster mid-season.
mgt: Casey Cizikas (born February 27, 1991) is a Canadian professional ice hockey player who serves as a centre for the New York Islanders of the National Hockey League (NHL). Selected 92nd overall by the Islanders in the 2009 NHL Entry Draft, Cizikas made his NHL debut in 2012 and has since become known as one of the league's top fourth-line players. He has twice won the Islanders' Bob Nystrom Award for leadership and hustle. Internationally, Cizikas has represented Canada, winning gold at the 2008 Ivan Hlinka Memorial Tournament and silver at the 2011 World Junior Ice Hockey Championships.

trgt: Ecological sanitation, commonly abbreviated as ecosan (also spelled eco-san or EcoSan), is an approach to sanitation provision which aims to safely reuse excreta in agriculture. It is an approach, rather than a technology or a device which is characterized by a desire to "close the loop", mainly for the nutrients and organic matter between sanitation and agriculture in a safe manner. One of the aims is to minimise the use of non-renewable resources. When properly designed and operated, ecosan systems provide a hygienically safe system to convert human excreta into nutrients to be returned to the soil, and water to be returned to the land. Ecosan is also called resource-oriented sanitation.
mgt:
Ecological sanitation (ecosan) is an approach to sanitation that emphasizes the safe recycling of nutrients from human excreta and wastewater back into agriculture, minimizing the use of non-renewable resources. First introduced in 1995, ecosan systems aim to reduce health risks, prevent water pollution, and promote resource recovery, particularly as fertilizers. While often associated with urine-diverting dry toilets (UDDTs), ecosan encompasses a variety of technologies and practices tailored to local conditions.

Ecosan has historical roots in traditional agricultural societies and has been promoted for its potential to address issues like phosphorus scarcity and malnutrition. Despite its benefits, challenges include user acceptance, cost, and scalability. The concept has influenced modern sustainable sanitation initiatives globally.

trgt:
Tauherenikau is a rural locality and a statistical area in the South Wairarapa District and Wellington Region of New Zealand's North Island. The locality is on SH 1 about 5 km east of Featherston and 7 km southwest of Greytown by road, and the statistical area covers the Tauherenikau River valley and plain north of SH 53. The statistical area surrounds but does not include Greytown.

The name, originally Tauwharenikau, means "the house made of nikau palm fronds".

Tauherenikau Racecourse opened in 1874.

Tauherenikau School operated from 1878 to 1936. It closed as the roll had dwindled to five students. The peak roll was 46 in 1917.

Fernside Homestead is a large wooden house built in 1924 on the west side of the river near Tauherenikau.
mgt: Tauwharenīkau, also spelled Tauherenikau, is a locality in the South Wairarapa District of New Zealand's Wellington Region. Covering an area of 337.71 km², it had an estimated population of 1,500 as of June 2022, with a population density of 4.4 people per km². The area has experienced steady population growth, increasing by 39.2% between 2006 and 2018. The 2018 New Zealand census recorded a population of 1,353, with a median age of 47.5 years, higher than the national median. The community is predominantly European/Pākehā, with smaller proportions of Māori, Pacific peoples, and Asians. Tauwharenīkau has a higher proportion of residents with no religion compared to the national average, and its median income exceeds the national figure.

trgt:
Ceiling Unlimited (later known as America — Ceiling Unlimited) (1942–1944) is a CBS radio series created by Orson Welles and sponsored by the Lockheed-Vega Corporation. The program was conceived to glorify the aviation industry and dramatize its role in World War II.

"Welles wrote, produced, and narrated this show, and his work was considered a prime contribution to the war effort," wrote the Museum of Broadcasting.

At the end of Welles's 13-episode contract (November 9, 1942 – February 1, 1943), Ceiling Unlimited was hosted by a variety of personalities including Charles Boyer, Joe E. Brown, Ronald Colman, Marlene Dietrich, Cary Grant, Alan Ladd, William Powell, Basil Rathbone, Edward G. Robinson and writer James Hilton.

Ceiling Unlimited began as a 15-minute drama series broadcast Mondays at 7:15 p.m. ET. The program changed format for its second season, becoming a half-hour variety show hosted by Joseph Cotten. Retitled America — Ceiling Unlimited, the program featured vocalists Nan Wynn and Constance Moore, and music by Wilbur Hatch. The show aired Sundays at 2 p.m. ET beginning August 8, 1943, and ending April 30, 1944.
mgt:
*Ceiling Unlimited* was an American radio anthology series that aired on CBS from November 9, 1942, to April 30, 1944. Created and initially hosted by Orson Welles, the program was sponsored by the Lockheed-Vega Corporation and aimed to boost morale during World War II through patriotic stories of aviation. The first season, directed and narrated by Welles, featured 15-minute episodes blending news, entertainment, and education, with contributions from writers like Arthur Miller and music by Bernard Herrmann. Welles left after 13 episodes, and subsequent episodes were hosted by various celebrities, including Ronald Colman and James Hilton.

The second season, retitled *America — Ceiling Unlimited*, adopted a musical and variety format, hosted by Joseph Cotten. The series also featured original works by John Steinbeck, including "With Your Wings," a story about a Black pilot's homecoming. While most episodes are lost, some recordings are preserved in archives. The program is remembered for its wartime propaganda efforts and its connection to prominent figures in American literature and entertainment.

trgt:
Greeny is a 1959 Gibson Les Paul Standard, named after its original owner Peter Green. He used it during his time in John Mayall & the Bluesbreakers and Fleetwood Mac, before selling it to Gary Moore in the early 1970s. Moore used the guitar throughout his career, both as a solo artist and in bands, such as Thin Lizzy. Due to financial troubles, he was forced to sell it in 2006, after which it passed through several private collectors and guitar dealers. In 2014, Greeny was acquired by Metallica guitarist Kirk Hammett, who has since used it both in the studio and during live performances.

The guitar is known for its unique out-of-phase tone, which is the result of a reversed neck pickup. This sound has become highly sought-after amongst guitar players, so much so that flipping the neck pickup has been affectionately referred to as "the Peter Green mod". The Gibson Custom Shop has produced two limited runs of Greeny replicas in 2010 and 2022, repectively. Gibson also released the Gary Moore Les Paul Standard in 2013, which included many of the same features as Greeny.
mgt:
Greeny is a 1959 Gibson Les Paul Standard electric guitar, renowned for its unique tone and storied history. Originally owned by Peter Green of Fleetwood Mac, the guitar was used to record iconic tracks such as "Albatross," "Black Magic Woman," and "The Green Manalishi." Green later sold the guitar to Gary Moore, who used it extensively throughout his career, including on Thin Lizzy's *Black Rose: A Rock Legend* and his signature song, "Parisienne Walkways." Moore made modifications to the guitar, including replacing the tuners and knobs, and released the tribute album *Blues for Greeny* in 1995, using the instrument throughout.

After passing through private collectors, Greeny was purchased in 2014 by Metallica guitarist Kirk Hammett, who has since used it in studio recordings and live performances, including a 2020 Peter Green tribute concert. The guitar is celebrated for its "out-of-phase" tone, resulting from a reversed neck pickup, and has inspired numerous reproductions by Gibson and other manufacturers. Greeny remains one of the most famous and influential guitars in rock history, symbolizing the legacy of its legendary owners.

trgt:
Occupy San Francisco was a collaboration that began with a demonstration event located at Justin Herman Plaza in the Embarcadero and in front of the Federal Reserve building on Market Street in the Financial District in San Francisco, California. It is based on the Occupy Wall Street movement that began in New York City on September 17, 2011 and is one of several "Occupy" protest sites in the San Francisco Bay Area; other sites include Occupy Oakland and Occupy San Jose.

As of June 2012, Occupy San Francisco had continued to engage in organized meetings, events and actions.
mgt: Occupy San Francisco was a protest that took place from September 17 to December 11, 2011, as part of the broader Occupy movement against economic inequality and corporate influence in government. The protest began with a small encampment in front of a Bank of America building and later moved to sites such as the Federal Reserve Building and Justin Herman Plaza. Protesters engaged in nonviolent demonstrations, marches, and civil disobedience, with several encampments dismantled by police. The movement also supported initiatives like the "99-mile March for Education" and proposed creating a credit union to aid underserved communities.

trgt:
Oleg Vladimirovich Losev (Lang-ru, sometimes spelled Lossev or Lossew in English) (10 May 1903 – 22 January 1942) was a Russian scientist and inventor who made significant discoveries in the field of semiconductor junctions and the light emitting diode (LED).

Although he was never able to complete a formal education and never held a research position, Losev conducted some of the earliest research into semiconductors, publishing 43 papers and receiving 16 "author's certificates" (the Soviet version of patents) for his discoveries. He observed light emission from carborundum point-contact junctions, constructing a light-emitting diode (LED), did the first research on them, proposed the first correct theory of how they worked, and used them in practical applications such as electroluminescence. He explored negative resistance in semiconductor junctions, and was first to use them practically for amplification, building the first solid-state amplifiers, electronic oscillators, and superheterodyne radio receivers, 25 years before the invention of the transistor. However his achievements were overlooked, and languished unknown for half a century before being recognized in the late 20th and early 21st century.
mgt:
Oleg Vladimirovich Losev (May 10, 1903 – January 22, 1942) was a Russian physicist and inventor, recognized as a pioneer in semiconductor physics and the inventor of the light-emitting diode (LED). Despite lacking formal higher education, Losev conducted groundbreaking research on point-contact crystal detectors, leading to his discovery in 1924 that silicon carbide junctions emitted light when a current was applied. He published extensively on the phenomenon, theorizing its quantum mechanical basis and envisioning applications in telecommunications. His work, however, was largely overlooked during his lifetime.

Losev also explored negative resistance in diodes, using zincite crystals to develop solid-state amplifiers, oscillators, and radio receivers decades before the invention of the transistor. His contributions to solid-state electronics were underappreciated, overshadowed by the dominance of vacuum tube technology.

Losev worked at several Soviet institutions, including the Nizhny Novgorod Radio Laboratory and the Ioffe Physical-Technical Institute. He died of starvation during the Siege of Leningrad in 1942. Modern researchers credit him as a visionary in semiconductor technology.

trgt:
The Apostolic Faith Church, formerly the Apostolic Faith Mission, is a Holiness Pentecostal denomination of Christianity, with nationwide reach and headquartered in Portland, Oregon, United States. The Apostolic Faith Mission of Portland was founded in 1907 by Florence L. Crawford, who was affiliated with William J. Seymour and the Azusa Street Revival of Los Angeles, California. By 1908 Crawford had independently founded what would become the Apostolic Faith Church. Since July 2000, the Superintendent General of the Apostolic Faith Church has been Darrel Lee.

The Apostolic Faith Church has a presence in the United States, Canada and across Africa, Asia, Europe, Central and South America, and Australia. In 1997, the AFC had 2,013 members in 115 churches served by 188 clergy in the United States. There are ten churches in Canada, several hundred in Africa (six-hundred in Nigeria alone), twenty throughout the Philippines and Korea, nine in Europe (Romania and Norway), and over sixty in the West Indies. Church-affiliated groups also regularly assemble in other parts of the world and recently include India. In some locations, member churches carry the name Trinity Apostolic Faith Church in order to differentiate themselves from non-affiliated churches in the same area.
mgt:
The Apostolic Faith Church is a Holiness Pentecostal Christian denomination founded in 1907 by Florence L. Crawford in Portland, Oregon. It traces its origins to the Azusa Street Revival, a pivotal event in the Pentecostal movement, where Crawford was a key participant. After breaking with Azusa leader William J. Seymour in 1911, Crawford established an independent mission in Portland, which became the headquarters of the Apostolic Faith Church. The denomination gained influence when Clara Lum, editor of *The Apostolic Faith* newspaper, controversially transferred the publication to Crawford's mission in 1908. The paper, later renamed *The Higher Way*, continues to be published.

The church adheres to Trinitarian and Wesleyan doctrines, emphasizing a born-again experience, sanctification, and the baptism of the Holy Spirit. It promotes outward holiness, modesty, and abstinence from alcohol and tobacco. Services feature music, testimonies, Bible-based messages, and altar calls. The church operates in 37 countries with over 715 congregations and provides free literature in multiple languages. Ministers are chosen by elders rather than formal training. During the COVID-19 pandemic, the church received federal loans to retain jobs. Its headquarters remain in Portland, Oregon.

trgt: Infection and Immunity is a peer-reviewed medical journal published by the American Society for Microbiology. It focuses on interactions between bacterial, fungal, or parasitic pathogens and their hosts. Areas covered include molecular pathogenesis, cellular microbiology, bacterial infection, host responses and inflammation, fungal and parasitic infections, microbial immunity and vaccines, and molecular genomics. The journal publishes primary research articles, editorials, commentaries, minireviews, and a spotlight report highlighting articles of particular interest selected by the editors. Articles are freely accessible after 6 months (delayed open access). Through its "Global Outreach Program," free online access is available to qualified microbiologists in eligible developing countries.
mgt: *Infection and Immunity* is a peer-reviewed scientific journal covering research on infectious diseases and immunology. Established in 1970, it was created to publish original research previously included in the *Journal of Bacteriology*. The journal is published monthly by the American Society for Microbiology and has a delayed open-access policy. Its current editor-in-chief is Andreas J. Bäumler. According to the 2017 Journal Citation Reports, *Infection and Immunity* has an impact factor of 3.256, ranking it 30th among infectious disease journals and 75th in immunology. The journal is abstracted and indexed in several major scientific databases.

page_title: Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee v. FEC
trgt: Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee v. FEC, 518 U.S. 604 (1996), was a case heard by the Supreme Court of the United States in which the Colorado Republican Party challenged the Federal Election Commission (FEC) as to whether the "Party Expenditure Provision" of the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 (FECA) violated the First Amendment right to free speech. This provision put a limit on the amount of money a national party could spend on a congressional candidate's campaign. The FEC argued that the Committee violated this provision when purchasing a radio advertisement that attacked the likely candidate of the Colorado Democratic Party. The court held that since the expenditures by the Committee were made independently from a specific candidate, they did not violate the campaign contribution limitations established by the FECA, and were protected under the First Amendment.
mgt:
*Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee v. FEC*, 518 U.S. 604 (1996), was a landmark United States Supreme Court case addressing the constitutionality of limits on political party expenditures under the Federal Election Campaign Act (FECA). The case arose when the Federal Election Commission (FEC) alleged that the Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee violated FECA's "Party Expenditure Provision" by funding radio advertisements critical of a Democratic candidate. The Committee argued that the provision infringed on its First Amendment rights.

In a 7–2 plurality decision, the Court ruled that independent expenditures by political parties, made without coordination with a candidate, are protected under the First Amendment. The Court found that the advertisement in question qualified as an independent expenditure and did not violate FECA. The case set a significant precedent for campaign finance law and was revisited in 2001, further clarifying the distinction between independent and coordinated expenditures.

trgt: Vibal Publishing House, Inc. (also known as Vibal Group, Inc., Vibal, or VPHI) is a major publishing house in the Philippines based in Quezon City. Vibal was founded in 1953 by Hilarion P. Vibal and by his wife, Esther Asuncion-Vibal. Vibal is a textbook, reference materials, and multimedia products publisher and is the biggest in the country. In 2011, in partnership with Procter & Gamble, Vibal introduces digital classrooms and low-cost digital education tablets, for the digitization of education in the country.
mgt: Vibal Publishing House, founded in 1953 by Hilarion P. Vibal and Esther Asuncion-Vibal, is a leading Philippine publishing company headquartered in Quezon City. Specializing in educational materials, it produces textbooks, e-books, and other learning resources. Over the decades, Vibal has expanded into digital education, introducing multimedia materials, digital classrooms, and low-cost tablets to support Philippine schools. It has received multiple National Book Development Board Quality Seal Awards and continues to innovate in education through partnerships with organizations like Microsoft and initiatives like the Center for Digital Education.

trgt:
Justin Kiriakis is a fictional character on the NBC daytime soap opera Days of Our Lives. Created by head writer Leah Laiman, he is portrayed by actor Wally Kurth from February 3, 1987, to February 5, 1991. From 2007 to 2008, Justin was mentioned but not seen on screen. Kurth reprised the role again August 10, 2009, remaining in the role for nearly five years before departing on April 3, 2014. However, he once again returned in February 2015. Justin is the nephew of Greek tycoon Victor Kiriakis. Justin married Adrienne Johnson Kiriakis, and together they have three sons while Justin has one by a past romance.

Kurth's performance has been met with critical acclaim, having garnered him a Daytime Emmy Award nomination for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series in 2020.
mgt:
Justin Kiriakis is a fictional character from the NBC soap opera *Days of Our Lives*, portrayed by Wally Kurth. The character first appeared on February 3, 1987, and has been a recurring presence on the show since, with Kurth reprising the role intermittently. Justin is introduced as the nephew of Victor Kiriakis and becomes a central figure in the series, particularly through his tumultuous relationship with Adrienne Johnson, whom he marries, divorces, and remarries. The couple faces numerous challenges, including infidelity, family conflicts, and struggles with parenthood.

Outside of his personal life, Justin transitions from running a construction company to becoming a lawyer, representing high-profile cases and forming alliances with characters like EJ DiMera. Kurth's portrayal of Justin has been well-received, and he has alternated between regular and recurring status on the show, with his most recent return occurring in 2019.

trgt:
Joseph Hamilton (August 28, 1938 – August 3, 2021) was an American professional wrestler, promoter, and trainer. He was best known as one half of the tag team The Assassins where he was called "Assassin #1". When the Assassins ended, He wrestled as "The Assassin" and "The Flame" both while wearing a mask.

Hamilton teamed up with Tom Renesto in late 1961 to form the masked heel (bad guy) team known as "The Assassins" and, for over a decade, he and Renesto wrestled all over the world with great success. Once Renesto retired, Hamilton kept the Assassins team alive by partnering with wrestlers such as Randy Colley and "Hercules" Hernandez under the trademark gold and black masks of the Assassins.

In the late 1980s, Hamilton founded Deep South Wrestling (DSW), where he also wrestled until a back injury forced him to retire in 1988. After Deep South Wrestling closed, he worked extensively for World Championship Wrestling both on the booking team and later on as a trainer at WCW's Power Plant training facility. In 2005, he reopened Deep South Wrestling, this time as an affiliate of World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE), to train wrestlers the WWE had signed to developmental contracts. In 2007, WWE abruptly ended their contract with DSW and the promotion suspended operations later in the year.
mgt:
Jody Hamilton (born Joseph Nicholas Zwaduk III; August 28, 1938 – August 3, 2021) was an American professional wrestler, promoter, and trainer, best known for his work as "The Assassin" and later "The Flame." Debuting in 1955, Hamilton achieved fame as part of the masked tag team "The Assassins" alongside Tom Renesto, becoming one of the most notorious and successful tag teams in wrestling history. The duo held multiple championships, including a record 12 NWA Georgia Tag Team Championships, and headlined events worldwide. Hamilton also found success as a singles competitor, holding titles such as the NWA Central States Heavyweight Championship.

After retiring from in-ring competition in the 1980s due to a back injury, Hamilton became a prominent trainer, running World Championship Wrestling's Power Plant, where he trained stars like Bill Goldberg and Kevin Nash. He later partnered with WWE to establish Deep South Wrestling as a developmental territory. Hamilton also worked with Rampage Pro Wrestling and was involved in a legal dispute with WWE, which was settled in 2010. 

Hamilton's son, Nick Patrick, followed in his footsteps as a wrestling referee. Hamilton passed away on August 3, 2021, at the age of 82, leaving behind a legacy as a wrestler, trainer, and influential figure in professional wrestling.

trgt: Hatton Castle stands on the lower part of Hatton Hill, the most easterly of the Sidlaw Hills, to the south of Newtyle in Angus, Scotland. The castle overlooks the wooded Den of Newtyle, and its views extend across Strathmore and include Ben Lawers and Schiehallion as well as the Angus and Glenshee hills. The 16th-century castle was originally built in a typical Scottish "Z plan" tower house design, as a fortified country house or château. There was an earlier castle called Balcraig Castle which stood less than half a mile from the present building, also on Hatton Hill.
mgt: Hatton Castle is a 16th-century castle located near Newtyle in Angus, Scotland. Built in 1575 by Laurence, 4th Lord Oliphant, it features a rare scale and platt staircase and served as the residence of the Masters of Oliphant. The castle was de-roofed in the early 18th century after the Jacobite rising but was faithfully restored in the 20th century, incorporating traditional materials and modern amenities. Now a family home, it hosts musical events and gatherings, including performances by Scottish traditional musicians. The surrounding gardens feature dry-stone walls, an orchard, and remnants of the historic Dundee and Newtyle Railway.

trgt:
Dynamic texture (sometimes referred to as temporal texture) is the texture with motion which can be found in videos of sea-waves, fire, smoke, wavy trees, etc. Dynamic texture has a spatially repetitive pattern with time-varying visual pattern. Modeling and analyzing dynamic texture is a topic of images processing and pattern recognition in computer vision.

Extracting features that describe the dynamic texture can be utilized for tasks of images sequences classification, segmentation, recognition and retrieval. Comparing with texture found within static images, analyzing dynamic texture is a challenging problem. It is important that the extracted features from dynamic texture combine motion and appearance description, and also be invariance to some transformation such as rotation, translation and illumination.
mgt: Dynamic texture refers to the visual patterns in video sequences that exhibit temporal and spatial variations, such as sea waves, smoke, or moving clouds. It is a key concept in computer vision and video analysis, with methods for its recognition categorized into various analytical approaches. Dynamic textures have diverse applications, including segmenting natural scene sequences, aiding navigation by distinguishing between elements like streets and grass, detecting abnormal crowd activities through motion analysis, classifying videos based on scene types, and retrieving videos containing specific dynamic patterns. These features make dynamic texture analysis valuable in fields such as surveillance, navigation systems, and multimedia content management.

trgt: Mộng-Lan is a Vietnamese-born American writer, visual artist, musician, dancer, and educator. Former Stegner Fellow at Stanford University, Fulbright Scholar, she has published seven books of poetry & artwork, three chapbooks, has won numerous prizes such as the Juniper Prize and the Pushcart Prize. Poems have been included in international and national anthologies such as Best American Poetry Anthology and several Norton anthologies. Her books include: Song of the Cicadas (Juniper Prize Winner); Why is the Edge Always Windy?; Tango, Tangoing: poems & art; One Thousand Minds Brimming, 2016; and Dusk Aflame: poems & art, 2018. Her latest music album releases include Arrabal de Tango: Tango por Siempre, voice & guitar, 2020; Perfumas de Amor, de Argentina y Viet Nam, (Tango por Siempre) (voice and guitar), 2018; New Orleans of My Heart, jazz piano, 2019; Dreaming Orchid: Poetry & Jazz Piano, 2016. www.monglan.com
mgt:
Mong-Lan is a Vietnamese-American poet, artist, musician, dancer, and educator, known for her multidisciplinary contributions to the arts. Born in Saigon, South Vietnam, she fled the country during the fall of Saigon and later became a Stegner Fellow at Stanford University and a Fulbright Scholar. She has published eight books and chapbooks of poetry and art, earning accolades such as the Pushcart Prize and the Juniper Prize. Her work has been featured in prominent anthologies, including *Best American Poetry* and *The Pushcart Book of Poetry*.

As a visual artist, Mong-Lan's paintings and photographs have been exhibited in major institutions like the Dallas Museum of Art and internationally in cities such as Tokyo and Buenos Aires. A skilled musician and tango dancer, she has released ten albums blending jazz, poetry, and tango. Mong-Lan has also taught at various universities and continues to perform and present her work globally.

page_title: Bitter Tears: Ballads of the American Indian
trgt:
Bitter Tears: Ballads of the American Indian is a 1964 concept album, the twentieth album released by singer Johnny Cash on Columbia Records. It is one of several Americana records by Cash. This one focuses on the history of Native Americans in the United States and their problems. Cash believed that his ancestry included Cherokee, which partly inspired his work on this recording. The songs in this album address the harsh and unfair treatment of the indigenous peoples of North America by Europeans in the United States. Two deal with 20th-century issues affecting the Seneca and Pima peoples. It was considered controversial and rejected by some radio stations and fans.

In 2014 a tribute album, Look Again to the Wind: Johnny Cash's Bitter Tears Revisited, was released with contributions by Gillian Welch, Dave Rawlings, Emmylou Harris, Bill Miller, and others. This was also the name of a documentary film about the suppression of Cash's Native American-themed album in the 1960s. This aired on PBS in February and November 2016.
mgt:
*Bitter Tears: Ballads of the American Indian* is a 1964 concept album by American country singer Johnny Cash, addressing the struggles and injustices faced by Native Americans. Inspired by Native American activism and his belief in having Cherokee ancestry (later disproven), Cash collaborated with songwriter Peter La Farge, who contributed five of the album's eight tracks. The album includes "The Ballad of Ira Hayes," a poignant story of a Pima Marine who raised the flag at Iwo Jima but faced poverty and alcoholism upon returning home. Other songs, such as "As Long as the Grass Shall Grow," highlight the loss of Seneca land due to the Kinzua Dam, while "Custer" critiques the glorification of General George Custer.

Despite initial resistance from radio stations and fans, Cash's advocacy helped the album reach No. 2 on the Billboard Country Albums chart, with "The Ballad of Ira Hayes" peaking at No. 3. The album was later reissued and inspired a 2014 tribute album and a 2016 PBS documentary, *We're Still Here: Johnny Cash's Bitter Tears Revisited.*

trgt:
The Chalfont Viaduct (also known as the Misbourne Viaduct) is the first of two five-arch brick railway viaducts on the Chiltern Main Line in south-east England. It is located between Gerrards Cross and Denham Golf Club stations. The M25 motorway passes beneath it between junctions 16 and 17 at Gerrards Cross near Chalfont St Peter, from where the bridge gets its name. The bridge is known as Chalfont No. 1 Viaduct; the longer Chalfont No. 2 Viaduct is a short distance to the west and spans the A413.

The bridge is noted as a local landmark because for many years it bore a graffiti slogan, "give peas a chance" painted in large white letters on the south-facing parapet.
mgt:
The Chalfont Viaduct, also known as the Chalfont No. 1 Viaduct or Misbourne Viaduct, is a brick arch railway bridge in Gerrards Cross, England. Built between 1902 and 1906 by the Great Western Railway, it was designed by James Charles Inglis and Robert Cherry Sikes to carry the Chiltern Main Line across the River Misbourne. The viaduct is 104 meters long, 12.5 meters high, and features five semi-elliptical arches. In the 1980s, the M25 motorway was constructed to pass through its arches, with minimal alterations to the structure.

The viaduct gained local fame for its graffiti, including the slogan "give peas a chance," which became a beloved landmark. Subsequent modifications to the graffiti sparked public debate and media attention.

trgt: Michael Korovkin (born December 28, 1948 in Russia) is a socio-cultural anthropologist, novelist and poet. He began as a neuro-physiologist in Russia and after emigrating in 1976 he went on to study anthropology in Canada, England and the Netherlands. His academic work focuses on medical anthropology and European and Mediterranean studies. His literary work covers a diverse range of socio-cultural milieus and psychological perspectives, extending cultured and incisive insights into the human condition. He has lived in Italy since 1989.
mgt: Michael Korovkin is a Russian-born anthropologist, writer, and poet known for his work in political and economic anthropology, medical anthropology, and literary contributions. Educated in biology and medicine in Russia, he later studied cultural anthropology in Canada and Europe. Korovkin has taught at universities in Canada and Italy and served as Academic Director at the European School of Economics. He is the author of several novels, including *Orfani di Madre-Russia* and *Memorie di una maîtresse moscovita*, as well as poetry collections like *Fields of Vision*.

trgt:
An acoustic torpedo is a torpedo that aims itself by listening for characteristic sounds of its target or by searching for it using sonar (acoustic homing). Acoustic torpedoes are usually designed for medium-range use, and often fired from a submarine.

The first passive acoustic torpedoes were developed nearly simultaneously by the United States Navy and the Germans during World War II. The Germans developed the G7e/T4 Falke, which was first deployed by the submarines U-603, U-758 and U-221 in March 1943. Few of these torpedoes were actually used and quickly phased out of service in favor of the T4's successor, the G7es T5 Zaunkönig torpedo in August 1943. The T5 first saw widespread use in September 1943 against North Atlantic escort vessels and merchant ships in convoys.

On the Allied side, the US Navy developed the Mark 24 mine, which was actually an aircraft launched, anti-submarine passive acoustic homing torpedo. The first production Mk. 24s were delivered to the U.S. Navy in March 1943, and it scored its first verified combat kills in May 1943. About 204 torpedoes were launched against submarine targets, with 37 Axis submarines being sunk and a further 18 damaged.

Since its introduction, the acoustic torpedo has proven to be an effective weapon against surface ships as well as serving as an anti-submarine weapon. Today, acoustic torpedoes are mostly used against submarines.
mgt:
An acoustic torpedo is a self-guided underwater weapon that uses sonar technology to detect and track targets, such as submarines or ships. Equipped with acoustic transducers, these torpedoes can operate in passive mode, detecting noise from the target's machinery or propellers, or in active mode, emitting sonar pulses to locate the target. Once launched, they function as "fire-and-forget" weapons, capable of homing in on targets even during evasive maneuvers or in the presence of countermeasures.

First deployed during World War II, acoustic torpedoes had a significant impact on naval warfare, particularly in the Battle of the Atlantic. German U-boats used the T5 torpedo to target Allied ships, but the Allies countered with innovations like the Foxer noisemaker and tactical maneuvers such as "Step-Aside." These countermeasures significantly reduced the effectiveness of German torpedoes.

Postwar developments included advanced decoy systems like the AN/SLQ-25 Nixie, used by modern navies to defend against acoustic torpedoes. Captured German technology, such as the T5 torpedo, also contributed to advancements in Allied and Soviet naval weaponry. Acoustic torpedoes remain a critical component of naval defense and offense, evolving with advancements in sonar and guidance systems.

trgt:
La Grosse Décharge Est is a tributary of the north shore of La Grosse Décharge Ouest, flowing entirely in the town of Baie-Saint-Paul, in the Charlevoix Regional County Municipality, in the administrative region of Capitale-Nationale, in the province of Quebec, in Canada.

This valley is mainly served by secondary forest roads which connect to the Chemin du Séminaire which runs along the south side of La Grosse Décharge Ouest. Forestry is the main economic activity in this valley; recreational tourism, second.

The surface of La Grosse Décharge Est is generally frozen from the beginning of December until the beginning of April; however, safe circulation on the ice is generally done from mid-December to the end of March. The water level of the river varies with the seasons and the precipitation; the spring flood generally occurs in April.
mgt:
La Grosse Décharge Est is a small river located in Baie-Saint-Paul, within the Charlevoix Regional County Municipality in the Capitale-Nationale region of Quebec, Canada. Originating from a small lake at an elevation of 673 meters (2,208 feet), the river flows over a course of 6.5 kilometers (4.0 miles) with a vertical drop of 249 meters (817 feet) before joining La Grosse Décharge Ouest. Its waters eventually flow into the Rivière des Mares, a tributary of the Gouffre River, and ultimately into the Saint Lawrence River.

The surrounding geography features mountainous terrain, with peaks reaching up to 971 meters (3,186 feet). The name "La Grosse Décharge Est" was formalized on March 25, 1997, and appears on historical maps, including the 1955 "Domaine forestier du Séminaire de Québec."

trgt:
Kihikihi, a small town located in the Waikato region of the North Island of New Zealand, serves as a satellite community of Te Awamutu, five kilometres to the north, and lies 35 kilometres south of Hamilton. The 2018 New Zealand census recorded a population of 2,808 people The main reason for the large increase since 2013 is the construction of a large number of new dwellings.

The town's outer rim has merged with the expanding rim of Te Awamutu, rendering the boundary between the two towns difficult to perceive.

Kihikihi is a Māori-language word meaning "cicada"; the name imitates the sound made by the insect. A large statue of a cicada stands at the northern entrance to the town.

Kihikihi's multi-purpose sports domain hosts national and international equestrian events such as the FEI Eventing World Cup.

The town is also home to the historic Kihikihi Polo Club, founded in 1892 by the Kay family.
mgt:
Kihikihi is a small town in the Waikato region of New Zealand, located within the Waipa District and part of the Te Awamutu Ward. Covering an area of 10.09 km², it had an estimated population of 3,620 as of June 2024. The town has a rich history, including its role as a center of Māori agricultural development in the 1850s and its significance during the New Zealand Wars in 1863. Notable figures associated with Kihikihi include Rewi Maniapoto, a prominent Māori leader, and John Rochford, an early railway surveyor.

The town features 20 archaeological sites, nine of which are listed by Heritage New Zealand, and a management plan has been developed to preserve its historic areas. Kihikihi School, a dual-medium primary school offering both English and Te Reo Māori instruction, has served the community since 1873. The town is connected to nearby Te Awamutu and Hamilton via State Highway 3 and a cycleway.

trgt:
The International Pool Tour was a professional sports tour created in 2005 by Kevin Trudeau and hosted by Rebecca Grant. It aimed to elevate pool to the level of other modern sports. Closely modeled on the PGA Tour, the IPT offered the largest prize funds in pool history in its first year. The tour attracted the top pool players in the world. It differed from the many nine-ball tournaments, as all IPT events were eight-ball matches. The company was based in Hinsdale, Illinois.

Many pool enthusiasts were initially skeptical, but the first event was successful, and at the time was the biggest tournament in billiards history. However, by the end of 2006, the tour was in serious financial trouble, and was forced to stop staging major tournaments.
mgt: The International Pool Tour (IPT) was a professional pool tour founded by Kevin Trudeau, aiming to elevate the sport's profile through high-stakes tournaments and a professional dress code reminiscent of pool's earlier eras. The IPT sought to promote the sport by live-streaming matches featuring top players and implementing a formal dress code requiring suits and leather shoes for male competitors. Despite initial success, the tour faced financial difficulties, including the cancellation of events and failure to secure funding. In 2006, an acquisition deal with billionaire Stanley Ho's company fell through, leading to the IPT's demise. While the tour deviated from some traditional pool governing body rules, it was supported by organizations like the Women's Professional Billiards Association and the U.S. Professional Pool Players Association.

trgt: The Wenceslas Bible (Lang-de) or the Bible of Wenceslaus IV (Lang-cs) is a multi-volume illuminated biblical manuscript written in the German language. The manuscript was commissioned by the King Wenceslaus IV of Bohemia (that time also the King of the Romans) and made in Prague in the 1390s. The Wenceslas Bible is unique and very precious not only because of its text, which is one of the earliest German translations of the Bible, but also because of its splendid illuminations. This oldest German deluxe Bible manuscript remained uncompleted.
mgt: The Wenceslas Bible is a richly illuminated German translation of the Bible, created in the 1390s in Prague, Bohemia, under the patronage of King Wenceslaus IV and his wife, Sophia. Commissioned by Martin Rotlev, a wealthy Prague burgher, it represents one of the earliest German Bible translations from the Latin Vulgate. Despite Emperor Charles IV's earlier ban on vernacular Bible translations, Wenceslaus supported this monumental project, reflecting ties to the Bohemian Reformation. The incomplete manuscript contains 654 miniatures and was intended to be one of the most elaborate medieval manuscripts.

trgt:
In the mathematical discipline of general topology, Stone–Čech compactification (or Čech–Stone compactification) is a technique for constructing a universal map from a topological space X to a compact Hausdorff space βX. The Stone–Čech compactification βX of a topological space X is the largest, most general compact Hausdorff space "generated" by X, in the sense that any continuous map from X to a compact Hausdorff space factors through βX (in a unique way). If X is a Tychonoff space then the map from X to its image in βX is a homeomorphism, so X can be thought of as a (dense) subspace of βX; every other compact Hausdorff space that densely contains X is a quotient of βX. For general topological spaces X, the map from X to βX need not be injective.

A form of the axiom of choice is required to prove that every topological space has a Stone–Čech compactification. Even for quite simple spaces X, an accessible concrete description of βX often remains elusive. In particular, proofs that βX \ X is nonempty do not give an explicit description of any particular point in βX \ X.

The Stone–Čech compactification occurs implicitly in a paper by Andrey Nikolayevich Tychonoff (1930) and was given explicitly by Marshall Stone (1937) and Eduard Čech (1937).
mgt:
The **Stone–Čech compactification** is a construction in topology that associates a compact Hausdorff space, denoted βX, to a given topological space X. It is characterized by a universal property: any continuous map from X to a compact Hausdorff space uniquely extends to a continuous map from βX. This makes βX the largest and most "complete" compactification of X, with the image of X being dense in βX. The construction is particularly significant for Tychonoff spaces, where it preserves the topological properties of X.

The concept was developed independently by Andrey Tikhonov, Eduard Čech, and Marshall Stone in the 1930s. Tikhonov introduced completely regular spaces and proved the existence of compactifications, while Čech and Stone provided further constructions and formalized the notation βX.

The Stone–Čech compactification can be constructed using various methods, including products of compact spaces, ultrafilters, and C*-algebras. It has applications in set-theoretic topology, functional analysis, and algebra, such as characterizing bounded sequences and extending operations on semigroups. A notable example is βN, the compactification of the natural numbers, which has been extensively studied for its complex structure and connections to set theory.

trgt: Formula One Grand Prix (known as World Circuit in the United States) is a racing simulator released in 1991 by MicroProse for the Amiga, Atari ST and PC created by game designer Geoff Crammond. It is often referred to as Grand Prix 1, MicroProse Grand Prix, or just F1GP. Although the game itself was not affiliated officially with the FIA or any Formula One drivers, team liveries and driver helmets were accurate to represent the 1991 season, but the names were fictional. The game is a simulation of Formula One racing at the time and was noted for its 3D graphics, remarkably high framerate (25 fps) and attention to detail, in particular the player's ability to edit the teams and drivers and set up their car to their own personal specifications. The game was ranked the 27th best game of all time by Amiga Power. Grand Prix's success spawned three sequels, called Grand Prix 2, Grand Prix 3 and Grand Prix 4.
mgt:
*Formula One Grand Prix* (F1GP) is a racing simulation video game developed and published by MicroProse, designed by Geoff Crammond, and released in 1991 for the Amiga and Atari ST, with a DOS version following in 1992. The game is notable for its realistic physics, accurate track modeling, and innovative features, such as driving assists and customizable car settings, which set a benchmark for racing simulators. Players could compete in a full Formula One season across 16 tracks, with detailed car handling and features like tyre wear and functional rearview mirrors enhancing the realism.

F1GP had a significant impact on the racing simulation genre, building on the foundation of *Indianapolis 500: The Simulation*. It also fostered an active online community, with competitions based on save-game submissions and extensive modding capabilities. Despite technical limitations, such as slow-motion effects due to CPU load, the game remains a classic. Modern-day enthusiasts continue to develop tools and host competitions, preserving its legacy in the racing simulation genre.

page_title: Dancing with the Stars (American TV series) season 14
trgt:
Season fourteen of Dancing with the Stars premiered on March 19, 2012, on the ABC network.

Starting the week of April 16, and for the next two weeks after that, the bottom two couples competed in a dance duel. Both couples performed at the same time, at which point the judges decided who would be eliminated that week.

Green Bay Packers wide receiver Donald Driver and Peta Murgatroyd won the competition over British classical singer Katherine Jenkins and Mark Ballas, who finished second, and Cuban-American Telenovela star William Levy and Cheryl Burke, who finished third.

This season introduced "Ballroom Battles" where Cheryl Burke, Mark Ballas, Louis van Amstel, Lacey Schwimmer, Karina Smirnoff, and Tony Dovolani trained a pair of amateur dancers to compete against other amateur couples. The teams of Burke, Dovolani, and van Amstel competed in the final stage by performing a jive. Burke's team were named the champions by the judges with Dovolani's and van Amstel's teams taking second and third places respectively.
mgt:
The fourteenth season of *Dancing with the Stars*, an American reality television series, premiered on ABC on March 19, 2012, and concluded on May 22, 2012. Hosted by Tom Bergeron and Brooke Burke Charvet, with judges Carrie Ann Inaba, Len Goodman, and Bruno Tonioli, the season featured twelve celebrity and professional dancer pairs competing for the Mirrorball Trophy. The Harold Wheeler orchestra and singers provided live music throughout the season.

NFL player Donald Driver and his professional partner Peta Murgatroyd were crowned the winners, marking Murgatroyd's first win on the show. The season introduced two new professional dancers, Henry Byalikov and Emma Slater, to the dance troupe, joining returning members Sharna Burgess, Kiki Nyemchek, Oksana Dmytrenko, and Sasha Farber.

The season included themed weeks such as Rock Week, Motown Week, and Trio Week, where couples performed various dance styles. Weekly scores and performances were judged on a 30-point scale, with the highest and lowest scores highlighted throughout the competition.

trgt: The 2007 World Indoor Lacrosse Championship was the second World Indoor Lacrosse Championship, an international box lacrosse tournament organized by the Federation of International Lacrosse every four years. It took place between May 14 and 20 at the Halifax Metro Centre in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada. Canada was the defending champion and again beat the Iroquois Nationals in the final, this time 15–14 in overtime. Eight nations took part in this event, the six nations from the 2003 WILC and two newcomers - England and Ireland. There was an estimated 850,000 television viewers of the tournament in Canada and the games were streamed live in Europe.
mgt: The 2007 World Indoor Lacrosse Championship was the second edition of the international box lacrosse tournament, held from May 14 to 20 at the Halifax Metro Centre in Canada. Eight teams competed, with Canada claiming their second consecutive title after defeating the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois Nationals) 15–14 in a thrilling overtime final, widely regarded as one of the greatest games in box lacrosse history. The United States secured third place, while England finished fourth. The tournament featured 20 games, with teams competing in round-robin pool play before advancing to the knockout stages. The Czech Republic and Ireland finished winless but showed determination throughout the competition.

trgt:
Terrace House: Closing Door (Japanese: テラスハウス クロージング・ドア, Hepburn: Terasu Hausu Kurōjingu Doa) is a 2015 Japanese reality show theatrical film that acts as a continuation and ending to the Fuji Television show Terrace House: Boys × Girls Next Door. It was released by Toho on February 14, 2015. Tetsuya Sugaya and Seina Shimabukuro continue as residents from the show, with four new members joining them. One of whom is writing a book on Terrace House and briefly interviews previous members. Studio commentators You, Reina Triendl, Yoshimi Tokui, Azusa Babazono, Ryota Yamasato and Hiroomi Tosaka also return.

Closing Door is the last installment in the Terrace House franchise before Netflix became a co-producer later in the year with Terrace House: Boys & Girls in the City.
mgt: *Terrace House: Closing Door* (テラスハウス クロージング・ドア) is a 2015 Japanese film directed by Masato Maeda, serving as a continuation and conclusion to the reality television series *Terrace House: Boys × Girls Next Door*. The film follows the lives and relationships of new and returning house members as they navigate romance, personal growth, and career aspirations within the shared living space. Released on February 14, 2015, the film was produced by Fuji Television and East Entertainment and distributed by Toho. It debuted at number one at the Japanese box office, grossing ¥1.3 billion in total. The film's soundtrack, released by Universal Music, reached number 34 on the Oricon Albums Chart. *Terrace House: Closing Door* marked the end of the original *Terrace House* series.

trgt: Pinecone Burke Provincial Park is a provincial park in British Columbia, Canada. It extends from the southwest corner of Garibaldi Provincial Park, west of Pitt Lake and Pitt River to include Burke Mountain in the City of Coquitlam. Most of the park is unserviced wilderness with very rough trails such as the Fools Gold Trail in the Boise Valley. There is canoe access to Widgeon Slough and from there hiking trails that lead to Widgeon Lake, and a network of old logging roads, hiking and mountain biking trails on Burke Mountain. The parks boundaries were established in 1995 by the provincial government and covers 38,000 hectares. Parts of the parks boundaries are located in the Katzie First Nation, Kwikwetlem First Nation, and Stó:lō First Nations territories. The parks diverse ecosystems supports several different species on land and in its inlet. There are 6 species that have been labeled as sensitive or vulnerable that live in the park. Before the parks protected status the area was logged and mined. It also was used for recreational purposes such as hiking and rock climbing. Visitors can use the park for hiking, camping, back country skiing and snowshoeing.
mgt:
Pinecone Burke Provincial Park is a 380 km² protected area located in Metro Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. Established in 1995, the park spans from Burke Mountain in Coquitlam to the western shore of Pitt Lake in Maple Ridge, with its northern boundary connecting to Garibaldi Provincial Park. Known for its diverse ecosystems, the park features old-growth forests, alpine lakes, wetlands, and a variety of wildlife, including black bears, grizzly bears, mountain goats, and numerous bird and fish species. It also supports sensitive species like the blue heron and tailed frog.

The park has historical significance, with remnants of early logging and mining activities, and is situated on the traditional territories of the Katzie, Kwikwetlem, and Stó:lō First Nations. Conservation efforts by groups like Friends of Burke Mountain led to its protection, and a management plan is being developed in collaboration with BC Parks, the Katzie First Nation, and the City of Coquitlam.

Visitors enjoy hiking, camping, canoeing, and winter activities like snowshoeing, with access to trails, peaks, and lakes. The park is part of the Lower Mainland Nature Legacy, emphasizing ecological preservation and sustainable recreation.

trgt:
A mercy rule, slaughter rule, knockout rule, or skunk rule ends a two-competitor sports competition earlier than the scheduled endpoint if one competitor has a very large and presumably insurmountable scoring lead over the other. It is called the mercy rule because it spares further humiliation for the loser. It is common in youth sports in North America, where running up the score is considered unsporting. It is especially common in baseball and softball in which there is no game clock and a dominant team could in theory continue an inning endlessly.

The rules vary widely, depending on the level of competition, but nearly all youth sports leagues and high school sports associations and many college sports associations in the United States have mercy rules for sports including baseball, softball, American football and association football.

However, mercy rules usually do not take effect until a prescribed point in the game (like the second half of an association football game). Thus, one team, particularly if it is decidedly better than a weaker opponent, can still "run up the score" before the rule takes effect. For instance, in American football, one team could be ahead by 70 points with three minutes left in the first half; in baseball, the better team could have a 20-run lead in the second inning, but the game would still continue.
mgt:
The "mercy rule" is a regulation in sports designed to end or modify a game when one team has an insurmountable lead, preventing further humiliation for the losing team and reducing unnecessary playtime. It is commonly applied in youth, high school, and amateur sports, with variations across sports and governing bodies. In American football, many states implement a "continuous clock" once a team leads by a certain margin, while in baseball and softball, games may end early if a team leads by a specified number of runs after a set number of innings. Similarly, high school basketball often uses a running clock when a lead reaches a certain threshold.

In college football, the NCAA allows coaches to agree to shorten game periods, though a running clock is not permitted. Other sports, such as soccer, boxing, and wrestling, have their own mercy rules, including ending games or matches when a specific point differential is reached. International sports like cricket and curling have no formal mercy rules but include mechanisms to expedite play. The mercy rule aims to balance sportsmanship with competitive integrity, ensuring games remain fair and enjoyable for all participants.

trgt: Archbishop Boghos Lévon Zékiyan (Lang-hy; born October 21, 1943 in Constantinople) is an Armenologist, philosopher, Professor of Armenian Language and Literature at Ca' Foscari University of Venice, Pontifical Oriental Institute of Rome and Istanbul University, a member of the Academy of Venice, Foreign member of Armenian National Academy of Sciences, Corresponding member of the Istituto Veneto di Scienze, Lettere ed Arti (1992). He is the Armenian Catholic Archeparch of Istanbul.
