Zekiyan is the Founding President of the Associazione "Padus-Araxes", the director of Summer Intensive Course of the Armenian Language and Culture at the University of Venice (since 1986), and former editor of Hye Endanik (1974–82) and Bazmavep (1980-1985) periodicals.
mgt: Levon Zekiyan (born 1943) is an Armenian Catholic archbishop, scholar, and theologian. He graduated from the Mekhitarist Seminary of San Lazzaro degli Armeni and the Pontifical Gregorian University, earning degrees in philosophy and theology, and was ordained a priest in 1967. He completed his PhD at the State University of Istanbul in 1973, focusing on St. Augustine's theory of knowledge. Zekiyan has held prominent academic and administrative roles, including Principal of Moorat-Raphael College and Secretary of the Armenian Academy of the Mekhitarist Fathers. In 2014, Pope Francis appointed him apostolic administrator of the Armenian Catholic Archieparchy of Istanbul, elevating him to the rank of archbishop.

trgt:
Pa amb tomàquet (IPA-ca) ("Bread with tomato"), is a traditional food of Catalan, Aragonese and Balearic cuisine. Pa amb tomàquet is considered a staple of Catalan cuisine and identity. While considered a signature toast dish in the Catalan Countries, it is common in bars throughout the rest of Spain, where it is also known as pan tumaca.

It consists of bread, which may or may not be toasted, with tomato rubbed over and seasoned with olive oil and salt.

It is considered one of the typical examples that define the Mediterranean diet, extended as a traditional recipe throughout the Catalan Countries.

It is popularly consumed on its own as a snack or a tapa with any meal, from breakfast to dinner.
mgt: Pa amb tomàquet is a traditional Catalan dish consisting of bread, tomato, olive oil, and sometimes garlic, often served as a tapa or snack. Originating in Catalonia, the Balearic Islands, and Aragon, it is considered a cornerstone of Catalan cuisine and identity. The dish is typically prepared by rubbing garlic and tomato onto bread, followed by salt and olive oil, though some restaurants serve a pre-made tomato mixture. It is commonly accompanied by cured meats, cheeses, omelettes, anchovies, or grilled vegetables like escalivada. The dish's origins are debated, with some attributing it to the 18th or 19th century during abundant tomato harvests. It shares similarities with other Mediterranean dishes, such as Italian bruschetta and Maltese ħobż biz-Zejt.

trgt:
Kazimieras Jaunius (1848–1908) was a Lithuanian Catholic priest and linguist. While Jaunius published very little, his major achievements include a well regarded Lithuanian grammar, systematization and classification of the Lithuanian dialects, and descriptions of Lithuanian accentuation. Though most of his conclusions on etymology and comparative linguistics were proven to be incorrect, his works remain valuable for vast observational data.

Jaunius studied at the Kaunas Priest Seminary and Saint Petersburg Roman Catholic Theological Academy. He was ordained a priest in 1875 and earned his Master of Theology in 1879. He taught several subjects, including moral theology and homiletics, at the Kaunas Priest Seminary from 1880 to 1892. His class notes on the Lithuanian language became a well regarded Lithuanian grammar book first published in 1897. After disagreements with Bishop Mečislovas Leonardas Paliulionis, Jaunius became a dean in Kazan in 1893. However, he experienced severe mental health issues and returned to Lithuania to recuperate in 1895. He obtained a teaching position at the Saint Petersburg Roman Catholic Theological Academy in 1898. He was of poor health and developed graphophobia (fear of writing). In 1902, Kazimieras Būga was hired as Jaunius'personal secretary to help collect and publish Jaunius' work. Jaunius retired from teaching in 1906 and died in 1908. Būga published two of his major works already after Jaunius' death.
mgt:
Kazimieras Jaunius (May 19, 1848 – March 9, 1908) was a Lithuanian Catholic priest, linguist, and philologist known for his pioneering work in Lithuanian grammar and dialectology. Born to a peasant family in the Kovno Governorate of the Russian Empire, he studied at the Kaunas Priest Seminary and the Saint Petersburg Roman Catholic Theological Academy, where he developed a deep knowledge of multiple languages, including Lithuanian, Latin, Ancient Greek, and Sanskrit. Jaunius became a prominent teacher at the Kaunas Priest Seminary, inspiring students to join the Lithuanian National Revival, and later served as a professor at the Theological Academy in Saint Petersburg.

Jaunius made significant contributions to Lithuanian linguistics, including his influential Lithuanian grammar, which provided insights into dialects and linguistic features. He also classified Lithuanian dialects, a system later refined by Antanas Salys. Despite his expertise, Jaunius published little, and much of his work was posthumously edited and published by his student Kazimieras Būga. Plagued by health issues and poverty in his later years, Jaunius died in Saint Petersburg. He was honored with an honorary doctorate, a memorial museum, and a monument in Lithuania, and his work remains foundational in the study of the Lithuanian language.

trgt:
The Big Red Machine is a nickname for the Cincinnati Reds baseball team that dominated the National League from 1970 to 1979 and is widely recognized as being among the best in baseball history. The team won six National League West Division titles, four National League pennants, and two World Series titles. Between 1970 and 1979 the team averaged over 95 wins a season, with a total record of 953 wins and 657 losses.

The core of that Reds team had the best record in the Major Leagues in 1981, but did not make the postseason because of Bowie Kuhn's split-season playoff format due to the player's strike.
mgt: The Big Red Machine was the nickname for the Cincinnati Reds baseball teams of the 1970s, renowned for their dominance in Major League Baseball. Managed by Hall of Famer Sparky Anderson, the team featured legendary players such as Pete Rose, Johnny Bench, Joe Morgan, and Tony Pérez, collectively known as the "Great Eight." The Reds won four National League pennants and two consecutive World Series titles in 1975 and 1976, becoming the only National League team since the 1921–22 New York Giants to achieve back-to-back championships. Known for their fierce rivalries, particularly with the Los Angeles Dodgers, the Big Red Machine remains one of baseball's most celebrated dynasties.

trgt:
A publicist is a person whose job is to generate and manage publicity for a company, a brand, or public figure – especially a celebrity – or for a work such as a book, film, or album. Publicists are public relations specialists who have the role to maintain and represent the images of individuals, rather than representing an entire corporation or business. Publicists are also hired by public figures who want to maintain or protect their image. Publicists brand their clients by getting magazine, TV, newspaper, and website coverage. Most top-level publicists work in private practice, handling multiple clients.

The term publicist was coined by the legal scholar Francis Lieber to describe the public-like role of internationalists during the late nineteenth century. Publicists are sometimes called flacks which traces back to Gene Flack, who was a well-known movie publicist in the 1930s.
mgt:
A publicist is a professional responsible for managing the public image and media coverage of individuals, brands, or organizations. Their primary role is to generate positive press coverage, often by crafting press releases, networking with journalists and media professionals, and organizing press tours. Publicists also handle crisis management, ensuring their clients' reputations are protected during controversies. They play a crucial role in shaping public perception through traditional media, social media, and blogs.

Publicists often work in industries such as entertainment, sports, and business, with Hollywood publicists managing relationships between celebrities and media outlets. They help stars maintain visibility and navigate the challenges of modern entertainment news. Publicists typically have backgrounds in communications, journalism, or public relations, and their salaries vary widely, with celebrity publicists earning more based on their clientele. The profession requires strong writing, networking, and crisis management skills, as well as adaptability to the evolving media landscape.

trgt:
Parmalat S.p.A. is a dairy and food corporation which is a subsidiary of French multinational company Lactalis. It was founded by Calisto Tanzi in 1961.

Having become the leading global company in the production of long-life milk using ultra-high-temperature processing, the company collapsed in 2003 with a €14bn ($20bn; £13bn) hole in its accounts in what remains Europe's biggest bankruptcy. Since 2011, it has been a subsidiary of French group Lactalis (with fuller control since 2019). Today, Parmalat is a company with a global presence, having operations in Europe, North America, South America, Australia, China, and South Africa.

Still specializing in UHT milk and milk derivatives (varieties of yogurt, cheese, butter, ice cream, etc.), the group also sells fruit juices distributed under the brand names Lactis, Santal, Malù, and Kyr. Its worldwide operations include almost 140 production centres and some 16,000 employees, with 5,000 Italian dairy farms supplying it. Its shares were listed on the Borsa Italiana.
mgt:
Parmalat S.p.A. is an Italian multinational food company specializing in milk, dairy products, fruit juices, and tea. Founded in 1961 by Calisto Tanzi in Parma, Italy, the company grew into a global corporation by the 1990s, employing over 30,000 people in 30 countries. Parmalat became a household name through its sponsorship of Formula 1 teams in the 1980s and 1990s. However, in 2003, it was embroiled in one of Europe’s largest financial scandals, with €14.3 billion in hidden debt uncovered. Founder Calisto Tanzi was convicted of fraud, and the company underwent bankruptcy and restructuring under government-appointed administrator Enrico Bondi. 

In 2011, French dairy giant Lactalis acquired Parmalat, eventually delisting it in 2019 and relocating strategic operations to France. Despite its troubled history, Parmalat remains a significant player in the global dairy industry, with 2015 revenues of €6.42 billion and a workforce of over 27,000 employees.

trgt:
Protein–protein interaction prediction is a field combining bioinformatics and structural biology in an attempt to identify and catalog physical interactions between pairs or groups of proteins. Understanding protein–protein interactions is important for the investigation of intracellular signaling pathways, modelling of protein complex structures and for gaining insights into various biochemical processes.

Experimentally, physical interactions between pairs of proteins can be inferred from a variety of techniques, including yeast two-hybrid systems, protein-fragment complementation assays (PCA), affinity purification/mass spectrometry, protein microarrays, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and Microscale Thermophoresis (MST). Efforts to experimentally determine the interactome of numerous species are ongoing. Experimentally determined interactions usually provide the basis for computational methods to predict interactions, e.g. using homologous protein sequences across species. However, there are also methods that predict interactions de novo, without prior knowledge of existing interactions.
mgt:
Protein–protein interaction (PPI) prediction is a computational field focused on identifying and modeling interactions between proteins, which are essential for understanding biological processes. Various methods are employed to predict PPIs, including phylogenetic profiling, which identifies co-evolved proteins based on similar phylogenetic trees, and the Rosetta Stone method, which detects interactions through gene fusion events. Other approaches include conserved gene neighborhood analysis, classification methods using machine learning, and inference from homologous structures. 

Advanced techniques, such as Bayesian network modeling and domain-pair exclusion analysis, integrate diverse data sources to improve prediction accuracy. Additionally, structural pattern identification and association methods analyze sequence motifs and protein interfaces. PPI prediction is closely related to protein docking, which models physical interactions between proteins with known structures. These computational methods complement experimental studies, providing insights into protein functions and interactions within complex biological systems.

trgt:
The Hoosiers are an English pop rock band formed in Indianapolis, Indiana, United States. The band consists of members Irwin Sparkes (lead vocals, lead guitar) and Alan Sharland (drums, percussion, vocals).

Their first single "Worried About Ray" reached number 5 on the UK Singles Chart in July 2007. In October 2007, they released their debut album The Trick to Life, which reached number one in the album charts. The band released their second top ten album The Illusion of Safety on 16 August 2010 and was preceded by the lead single "Choices", released on 1 August 2010, and which reached the UK top 20.

In July 2015, bassist Martin Skarendahl left the band in what was described by the remaining members as "one of the most amicable decisions in music history".
mgt: The Hoosiers are an English pop rock band formed in 2003 by Irwin Sparkes and Alan Sharland, who were inspired to name the band after their time in Indiana, where residents are colloquially called Hoosiers. The band gained prominence with their debut album *The Trick to Life* (2007), which included the hit single "Goodbye Mr A." Known for their blend of pop rock and indie pop, they later released albums such as *The Illusion of Safety* (2010), *The News from Nowhere* (2014), and *The Secret Service* (2015). Over the years, the band underwent lineup changes, with founding bassist Martin Skarendahl and keyboardist Sam Swallow departing. In 2021, they released the charity single "Route 66," which topped the Official Singles Sales Chart. The band remains active, continuing to perform and release music.

trgt:
The Czech National Bank, (Lang-cs, ČNB) is the central bank and financial market supervisor in the Czech Republic, headquartered in Prague. It is and a member of the European System of Central Banks. It was established on 1 January 1993 from the division of the State Bank of Czechoslovakia as part of the process of dissolution of Czechoslovakia, together with the National Bank of Slovakia.

In accordance with its primary objective, the CNB sets monetary policy, issues banknotes and coins and manages the circulation of the Czech koruna, the payment system and settlement between banks. It also performs supervision of the banking sector, the capital market, the insurance industry, pension funds, credit unions and electronic money institutions, as well as foreign exchange supervision.
mgt:
The Czech National Bank (CNB) is the central bank of the Czech Republic, headquartered in Prague. Established on January 1, 1993, following the dissolution of Czechoslovakia, it succeeded the State Bank of Czechoslovakia. The CNB is fully state-owned and operates independently, with its primary objective being price stability, achieved through inflation targeting. It also supports sustainable economic growth and regulates the Czech koruna (CZK), the national currency. 

The CNB oversees financial markets, including banking, insurance, and pension funds, and employs various monetary policy tools, such as open market operations, interest rate adjustments, and foreign exchange interventions. While the Czech Republic joined the European Union in 2004, the CNB has not adopted the euro, reflecting public preference for retaining the koruna. The bank is governed by a seven-member Bank Board, led by Governor Aleš Michl.

trgt: Spider-Man: India is a superhero comic book series originally published in India by Gotham Entertainment Group in 2004, retelling the story of Marvel Comics'Spider-Man in an Indian setting. It ran for four issues, which were later also published in the United States in 2005 and collected into a trade paperback (ISBN 0-7851-1640-0). The series was created by Sharad Devarajan, Suresh Seetharaman, and Jeevan J. Kang with Marvel Comics. The Indian version was conceptualized by Indian director Satyajit Ray, who had once met Stan Lee in New York to discuss creating an Indian version of the superhero. However, this was not done during Ray's lifetime.
mgt: *Spider-Man: India* is a four-issue limited comic book series published by Marvel Comics from November 2004 to February 2005. Written by Sharad Devarajan, Jeevan Kang, and Suresh Seetharaman, with art by Jeevan Kang, the series reimagines Spider-Man in an Indian setting. The story follows Pavitr Prabhakar, a village boy who moves to Mumbai and gains spider-like powers from a mystical yogi. Struggling with his responsibilities, Pavitr battles the demon-possessed crime lord Nalin Oberoi and other villains, ultimately embracing his role as a hero. Pavitr later appears in Marvel's *Spider-Verse* and *Web Warriors* storylines, teaming up with alternate-reality Spider-heroes.

trgt:
WUFO (1080 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station licensed to Amherst, New York, and serving the Buffalo metropolitan area. It is owned by the Visions Multi Media Group headed by Sheila. L. Brown and it broadcasts a hybrid Urban AC and Classic Hip Hop radio format. Its studios are on Broadway Avenue in Buffalo.

WUFO transmits 1,000 watts non-directional. WUFO is a daytimer station because 1080 AM is a clear-channel frequency reserved for Class A stations WTIC Hartford and KRLD Dallas, so WUFO must sign off at sunset to avoid interference. The transmitter is on Genesee Street in Cheektowaga. Programming is heard around the clock on 35-watt FM translator W243DX at 96.5 MHz. It uses the FM translator frequency in its moniker, "Power 96.5."
mgt: WUFO (1080 AM), branded as "Power 96.5," is a radio station based in Amherst, New York, serving the Buffalo metropolitan area. Established in 1948, it operates on a frequency of 1080 kHz and simulcasts on FM translator W243DX at 96.5 MHz. Owned by Sheila L. Brown's Vision Multi Media Group, WUFO is notable for being the first radio station in Western New York programmed for the African American community, a format it adopted in 1962 under the ownership of Dynamic Broadcasting. Over the years, it has featured prominent African American radio personalities and transitioned through various formats, including gospel music. In 2017, WUFO rebranded as "Power 96.5," adopting an Urban AC and Classic Hip Hop format, further solidifying its cultural significance in the region.

trgt: Terry Mutchler is an American attorney and writer. She was formerly a journalist and public servant in Pennsylvania. As a journalist, she won several awards for her reporting and was Associated Press' Bureau Chief in New Jersey and Alaska. She later became an attorney, and was appointed the first executive director of the newly established Pennsylvania Office of Open Records. Her memoir, Under This Beautiful Dome: A Senator, A Journalist, and the Politics of Gay Love in America, was a finalist for the Lambda Literary Award for Lesbian Memoir or Biography.
mgt: Terry Mutchler is an American attorney, journalist, and advocate for government transparency. She began her career as a journalist, reporting for *The Morning Call* and the Associated Press, where she became the first woman appointed as a Statehouse Correspondent in 1993. After earning a law degree, she served as a clerk for the Illinois Supreme Court and worked in the Clinton administration. In 2008, Mutchler became the first director of Pennsylvania's Office of Open Records, significantly improving public access to government information. She is also the author of a memoir about her secret relationship with Illinois Senator Penny Severns.

trgt:
Mathew Street is a street in Liverpool, England, notable as the location of the new Cavern Club, the Beatles having played in the original club on numerous occasions in their early career.

Mathew Street is visited by thousands of tourists a year, who visit the Cavern Club and many surrounding attractions including a statue of John Lennon, a Beatles store, the Liverpool Beatles Museum and several pubs formerly frequented by the band. A wall in Mathew Street is adorned by a sculpture by Arthur Dooley entitled "Four Lads Who Shook the World".
mgt: Mathew Street is a historic street in Liverpool, England, located in the city centre's Cavern Quarter. Originally named Mathew Pluckington Street, it was once the hub of Liverpool's wholesale fruit and vegetable market. The street is best known for its association with music, particularly the iconic Cavern Club and Eric's, which hosted influential punk and post-punk bands. A popular nightlife destination today, Mathew Street also features a statue of Carl Jung, commemorating his dream of Liverpool. In 2006, it gained attention when a burglary was reported via its webcam from Dallas, Texas.

trgt: Zheng Zhi (Chinese: 郑智; pinyin: Zhèng Zhì; born 20 August 1980) is a Chinese professional footballer and manager who played most of his career for Chinese Super League club Guangzhou F.C., becoming their captain and serving also as their caretaker manager in two stints. After starting his career as a defender, Zheng was later moved into a central midfield role by then head coach Zhu Guanghu at Shenzhen Jianlibao and experienced immediate success there by winning the 2004 league title with the club. A move to Shandong Luneng Taishan saw a prolific goal scoring period in his career and he soon became the captain of the Chinese national team, which then led to moves to Charlton Athletic and Celtic. He moved back to China in 2010 and joined Guangzhou Evergrande, making over 300 appearances so far as captain while helping the club win all major trophies a Chinese club could compete for, including Chinese Super League for a record 8 times and AFC Champions League twice.
mgt:
Zheng Zhi (born 20 August 1980) is a Chinese professional footballer and manager, currently serving as the head coach of Guangzhou F.C. and playing as a midfielder. Renowned for his versatility and leadership, Zheng enjoyed a distinguished playing career spanning over two decades, representing clubs in China, England, and Scotland. He began his career with Liaoning F.C. before achieving domestic success with Shenzhen Jianlibao and Shandong Luneng Taishan. Zheng later played for Charlton Athletic in the Premier League and Celtic in the Scottish Premier League, before joining Guangzhou Evergrande in 2010, where he captained the team to multiple Chinese Super League titles and the 2013 AFC Champions League victory. 

Internationally, Zheng earned 108 caps for the Chinese national team between 2002 and 2019, serving as captain and competing in major tournaments, including the 2008 Olympics. He was named Asian Footballer of the Year in 2013. Transitioning to management, Zheng has held various coaching roles at Guangzhou since 2019.

trgt:
Wawne /ˈwɔːn/, also spelled Waghen, is a small village and civil parish in the East Riding of Yorkshire, England. The village is situated approximately 5.5 miles (8.9 km) north of Hull city centre and 4 miles (6.4 km) south-east of Beverley on the east bank of the River Hull.

The civil parish consists of the village of Wawne and the hamlet of Meaux. According to the 2011 UK census, Wawne parish had a population of 975, an increase on the 2001 UK census figure of 878.
mgt: Wawne is a village and civil parish in the East Riding of Yorkshire, England, located within the Yorkshire and the Humber region. It had a population of 975 according to the 2011 census. The village, historically mentioned in the Domesday Book as "Wagene," has roots dating back to the Middle Ages and features the Grade I listed St Peter's Church. Wawne was historically significant for its ferry across the River Hull, which operated until 1946. The village has seen modern development, including new housing and community enhancements, while maintaining its historic connections to nearby Sutton on Hull.

trgt:
The Pacific geoduck ("gooey-duck"; /ˈɡuːiˌdʌk/; Panopea generosa) is a species of very large saltwater clam in the family Hiatellidae. The common name is derived from the Lushootseed (Nisqually) word gwídəq.

The geoduck is native to the coastal waters of the eastern North Pacific Ocean from Alaska to Baja California. The shell of the clam ranges from 15 centimetres (6 in) to over 20 centimetres (8 in) in length, but the extremely long siphons make the clam itself much longer than this: the "neck" or siphons alone can be 1 metre (3 ft 3 in) in length. The geoduck is the largest burrowing clam in the world. It is also one of the longest-living animals of any type, with a typical lifespan of 140 years; the oldest has been recorded at 179 years old. The precise longevity of geoducks can be determined from annual rings deposited in the shell which can be assigned to calendar years of formation through crossdating. These annual rings also serve as an archive of past marine variability.
mgt:
The geoduck (*Panopea generosa*) is a species of large, burrowing saltwater clam native to the west coast of North America, particularly Washington and British Columbia. Known for its long siphon and distinctive appearance, the geoduck is the largest burrowing clam in the world, with some specimens weighing over 7 kilograms (15 pounds) and living up to 140 years. Its name originates from the Lushootseed word *gwídəq*, meaning "dig deep," and it is also referred to as the elephant-trunk clam.

Highly valued in East Asian cuisine, geoduck is considered a delicacy and is often served in hot pots, sashimi, or soups. Its high market value has created a multimillion-dollar industry in the United States and Canada, though it is closely regulated to prevent overharvesting and poaching. Geoduck farming has sparked environmental debates, but studies suggest minimal long-term ecological impact. The species has also gained cultural recognition, serving as the mascot of Evergreen State College and achieving internet notoriety for its phallic appearance.

trgt:
Celanese Corporation, formerly known as Hoechst Celanese, is an American technology and specialty materials company headquartered in Irving, Texas. A Fortune 500 corporation, the company is the world’s leading producer of acetic acid, producing about 1.95 million tonnes per year, representing approximately 25% of global production. Celanese is also the world's largest producer of vinyl acetate monomer (VAM).

Celanese operates 25 production plants and six research centers in 11 countries, mainly in North America, Europe, and Asia. The company owns and operates the world's three largest acetic acid plants: one in the Clear Lake area of Pasadena, Texas, one on Jurong Island in Singapore, and a third in Nanjing, China.
mgt: Celanese Corporation is a global chemical and advanced materials company headquartered in Irving, Texas. Founded in 1918 in New York City by Swiss chemist Camille Dreyfus, the company initially produced cellulose acetate fabrics and yarns. Over the years, Celanese expanded its operations, becoming a leading producer of acetyl intermediates, advanced engineered materials, and consumer specialties, including food preservatives and cellulose acetate for cigarette filters. The company is also known for its TCX Technology, a process for producing ethanol from natural gas. Publicly traded on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol "CE," Celanese reported $8.54 billion in revenue and employed over 8,500 people as of 2021.

trgt:
Hamish Angus McLachlan (born 28 August 1975) is an Australian sports broadcaster and host with Seven Sport.

Since he joined the Seven Network in January 2008, he has been a host/presenter/reporter at multiple events, including being a part of every Australian Open tennis tournament until 2018 and hosting AFL Game Day since its beginning through to this day. McLachlan was the host for the network's Olympics and Commonwealth Games, a position previously held by the likes of Sandy Roberts and Matt White.
mgt: Hamish McLachlan (born 28 August 1975) is an Australian sports broadcaster and television presenter for the Seven Network. Beginning his career in sports management, he transitioned to media in 2008, hosting *AFL Game Day* and co-commentating Australian Football League matches. McLachlan has hosted major sporting events, including the Olympics, Commonwealth Games, and Australian Open, and has written for the *Herald Sun*. Originally from Adelaide, he holds a commerce degree from the University of Adelaide and is the younger brother of AFL CEO Gillon McLachlan.

trgt:
Barasat Junction railway station is the primary rail hub in the city of Barasat It is one of the major suburban city railway stations in the district of North 24 Parganas. Generally, the people commuting daily use this station for travelling to Kolkata's Central Business District or other towns, with the busiest times at the station being the morning and evening peak periods. This station provides the facility of separate rest rooms for both male & female passengers. Barasat Junction Railway station consists of 6 permanent platforms (Platform no: 1–6).

Recently, the government-owned Indian Railways corporation approved a proposal for an underground metro railway station connecting Barasat Junction to Noapara metro station. The construction of the project has started in October 2011.

An EMU carshed is situated in the vicinity of Barasat Junction railway station.
mgt:
Barasat Junction railway station is a major railway station in Barasat, North 24 Parganas, West Bengal, India. Operated by Eastern Railway, it serves as a key junction on the Kolkata Suburban Railway network, connecting the Sealdah–Bangaon line and the Barasat–Hasnabad and Ranaghat–Bangaon branch lines. Opened in 1906 and electrified in 1972, the station has five platforms and six tracks but lacks parking facilities. 

The station features a reservation counter, smart card ticket booths, and a railway car shed for maintenance. Historically, it was part of the Barasat Basirhat Railway, a narrow-gauge line established in 1914 and later converted to broad gauge. Barasat is also part of the proposed Noapara–Barasat metro extension, which aims to improve connectivity but has faced delays due to land encroachment and contractor withdrawal.

trgt: William Howard Ashton (born 19 August 1943), known professionally as Billy J. Kramer, is an English pop singer. With The Dakotas, Kramer was managed by Brian Epstein during the 1960s and scored hits with several Lennon–McCartney compositions never recorded by the Beatles, among them the UK number one "Bad to Me" (1963). Kramer and the Dakotas had a further UK chart-topper in 1964 with "Little Children" and achieved U.S. success as part of the British Invasion. Since the end of the beat boom, Kramer has continued to record and perform. His autobiography, Do You Want to Know a Secret, was published in 2016.
mgt: Billy J. Kramer (born William Howard Ashton, August 19, 1943) is an English pop singer known for his role in the 1960s Merseybeat scene. Managed by Brian Epstein and produced by George Martin, Kramer achieved fame with hits written by Lennon and McCartney, including "Do You Want to Know a Secret," "Bad to Me," and "Little Children," the latter becoming his biggest success. Backed by the Dakotas, he enjoyed chart-topping success in the UK and the US. After his peak, Kramer pursued a solo career, later moving to the United States. He continues to perform and record, releasing new music and an autobiography in recent years.

trgt:
The Borscht Corporation is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit that creates short films and videos in and about the city of Miami, Florida. In addition to hosting a quasi-annual film festival that screens their work, Borscht Corp. provides financial and technical support to its commissioned filmmakers.

Since receiving a challenge grant from the John S. and James L. Knight Foundation in 2010, Borscht Corp. has spearheaded productions by untested moviemakers and provided a creative platform for underrepresented (often Latin American) identities in film.
mgt: Borscht Corporation is a Miami-based film collective and production company known for its innovative and experimental approach to filmmaking. Their work has been showcased at prestigious venues and festivals worldwide, including the Museum of Modern Art, Sundance, SXSW, and Venice Film Festival. Borscht Corp. has received numerous accolades, including grants from the Knight Foundation and Creative Capital, and their films have won awards at Sundance, AFI Fest, and SXSW. They also host the Borscht Film Festival, celebrated as one of the "coolest" and "weirdest" film festivals globally.

page_title: Corbett and Courtney Before the Kinetograph
trgt:
Corbett and Courtney Before the Kinetograph (also known as Edison Kinetoscopic Record of Boxers and The Corbett-Courtney Fight) is an 1894 American short black-and-white silent film produced by William K.L. Dickson and starring James J. Corbett. It was only the second boxing match to be filmed following The Leonard-Cushing Fight which had been filmed by Dickson on June 14, 1894.

The films are listed as "1st Round," "2nd Round," "3rd Round," "4th Round," "5th Round," and "6th Round". Only one partial round of the original six rounds remains intact.
mgt: *Corbett and Courtney Before the Kinetograph* is an 1894 American silent film produced by the Edison Manufacturing Company and directed by William K.L. Dickson. It features a staged six-round boxing match between heavyweight champion James J. Corbett and underdog Peter Courtney, filmed at the Black Maria studio in West Orange, New Jersey, the first movie studio in the United States. The film was created for viewing on a Kinetoscope and is one of the earliest sports films. Now in the public domain, it is preserved by the Library of Congress.

trgt:
A Fazenda 4 was the fourth season of the Brazilian reality television series A Fazenda which premiered Tuesday, July 19, 2011 at 11:15 p.m. on RecordTV.

This season was confirmed on late December 2010, before the finale of the third season. Britto Junior and Chris Couto reprise their hosting stints for the show. Tina Roma makes her debut as the show's new special correspondent.

For the first time ever, four women reached the finals, making this the first reality show in Brazilian television history with public voting to do so. No Limite 1 (2000) and Aprendiz 6 − Universitário (2009) also managed to create all female final fours but there was no public vote involved.

On October 12, 2011, personal trainer Joana Machado won the competition with 48% of the public vote over TV host Monique Evans (44%) and DJ Raquel Pacheco (8%).
mgt:
*A Fazenda 4* was the fourth season of the Brazilian reality television series *A Fazenda*, which aired in 2011. The season lasted 87 days and featured 16 contestants, including 14 new celebrities and three returning participants from previous seasons. Joana emerged as the winner, with Monique as the runner-up. The grand prize remained R$2,000,000, and the season introduced a twist where contestants were divided into three teams, competing for an additional R$500,000 prize.

The season was notable for its controversies, including the ejection of boxer Duda Yankovich for violent behavior and a public outcry over comments made by actor Thiago Gagliasso regarding animal mistreatment. It was the first season to be broadcast in high-definition and returned to its original winter-spring schedule. The show received significant media attention and maintained strong ratings throughout its run.

trgt:
The Tihvinskaya water system was one of the waterways connecting the Volga River with the Baltic Sea, and specifically the Mologa River with Syas River. In terms of the current administrative division of Russia, the waterway belongs to Vologda and Leningrad Oblasts.

It was first conceived by Peter the Great, but construction started only in 1802. The Tihvinskaya water system was built for the passage of middle-sized ships. This Tikhvin system functioned until the middle of the 20th century, when shipping along the Svir River and the construction of railways had increased competition which led to its closure.
mgt: The Tikhvinskaya water system was a historic waterway in Russia, connecting the Volga River to St. Petersburg via a network of rivers, lakes, and canals. Spanning 902 kilometers, it included the Tikhvin Canal and several locks and sluices, facilitating navigation through the region. Proposed multiple times in the 18th century, its construction was approved by Alexander I in 1802 following a severe drought that disrupted other waterways. Today, the system is in a state of disrepair, with destroyed gates, reduced water levels, and pollution, while architectural monuments along its route have fallen into neglect.

trgt: Interstate 285 (I-285) in the US state of North Carolina is the designation for a freeway connecting the cities of Lexington and Winston-Salem. The route was approved in February 2018 and was first signed in November 2018. An auxiliary route of I-85, it branches off of its parent route and runs northeast, bypassing central Lexington, before turning due north and heading toward Winston-Salem, terminating at an interchange with I-40. I-285 is cosigned with US Highway 52 (US 52) for its entire route, and as of November 2018, still uses US 52 milemarkers and exit numbers. Other routes cosigned along parts of the route include I-85 Business (I-85 Bus), US 29, US 70, and North Carolina Highway 8 (NC 8). I-285 has been approved for an extension along the US 52 freeway through Winston-Salem to meet the future Winston-Salem Northern Beltway.
mgt:
Interstate 285 (I-285) is a 23.49-mile (37.80 km) auxiliary Interstate Highway in North Carolina, connecting Lexington and Winston-Salem. Designated in 2018, it serves as a bypass of Lexington and is cosigned with US 52, US 29, and US 70 for much of its route. The highway begins at I-85 in Lexington and ends at I-40 in Winston-Salem, passing through Davidson and Forsyth counties. 

Originally approved in 2005, the route underwent significant upgrades to meet Interstate standards, with construction completed in 2017. Future plans include extending I-285 northward to connect with the Winston-Salem Northern Beltway (I-74/I-274) and potentially widening the highway to six lanes. The extension aims to enhance freight transportation and support economic development in the region, including the Innovation Quarter in Winston-Salem.

trgt:
"Hold on Me" is the third single released by Grinspoon from their fourth studio album Thrills, Kills & Sunday Pills. It was released on 21 February 2005 on the Universal Records label. The initial single release included a lapel pin badge under shrink wrap, with 'Hold On Me - Grinspoon EP' themed artwork. It debuted on the ARIA Singles Chart at No. 44.

Four minutes and fifty-two seconds into the last track on the single, "Get the Fuck Out of Here", the original version of "Hold on Me" can be heard. It was entitled "Showpony" and contains different lyrics as well as slightly different arrangement. According to the band, the album's US record producer, Howard Benson, told them they couldn't call a song "Show Pony"'because he'd never heard the term, so the title of the song and the musical arrangement was changed. To promote the release of the single Grinspoon embarked on a national tour with New Zealand band, Shihad, called 'The Show Pony Express Tour'.
mgt:
"Hold on Me" is a song by Australian rock band Grinspoon, released on 21 February 2005 as the second single from their fourth studio album, *Thrills, Kills & Sunday Pills* (2004). Written by band members Pat Davern and Phil Jamieson and produced by Howard Benson, the post-grunge track runs for 2 minutes and 48 seconds. The song received notable recognition, ranking as the 35th most heard song by an Australian artist in 2005 according to the *Australian Music Guide*. 

Critics described "Hold on Me" as a catchy and accessible track, with *FasterLouder* magazine praising its hooks and danceable quality, while noting its "cheesiness" as part of its charm. The accompanying music video, presented mostly in black-and-white, mimics a 1960s television performance and features an introduction by Jamieson explaining the song's origins. "Hold on Me" followed the single "Better Off Alone" and preceded "Bleed You Dry" in Grinspoon's discography.

trgt:
General elections were due to be held for the Legislative Council in Nyasaland on 28 April 1964, and would have been the first in the country under universal suffrage. However, there were no opposition candidates to either the Malawi Congress Party in the general roll seats (the Nyasaland Asian Convention had dissolved itself and declared its support for the MCP), or the Nyasaland Constitutional Party in the special roll seats, resulting in all 53 candidates winning without votes being cast.

MCP leader Hastings Banda remained as Prime Minister, leading the country to independence on 6 July that year.
mgt: The 1964 Nyasaland general election was held following the announcement of the elections on 17 November 1963. The electoral system featured two voter rolls: a general roll for Africans, Asians, and mixed-race individuals, and a special roll for Europeans, though the latter faced significant controversy and was ultimately reduced to three seats. Voter registration saw over 1.87 million people register for the general roll, while the Malawi Congress Party (MCP), led by Hastings Banda, dominated the campaign. Banda selected 50 candidates for the MCP, and opposition to the party was minimal, with the Mbadwa party withdrawing from the race.

trgt:
In literature, polyphony (Lang-ru) is a feature of narrative, which includes a diversity of simultaneous points of view and voices. Caryl Emerson describes it as "a decentered authorial stance that grants validity to all voices." The concept was introduced by Mikhail Bakhtin, using a metaphor based on the musical term polyphony.

Bakhtin's primary example of polyphony was Fyodor Dostoevsky's prose. According to Bakhtin, the chief characteristic of Dostoevsky's novels is "a plurality of independent and unmerged voices and consciousnesses, a genuine polyphony of fully valid voices". His major characters are, "by the very nature of his creative design, not only objects of authorial discourse but also subjects of their own directly signifying discourse."

Polyphony in literature is the consequence of a dialogic sense of truth in combination with the special authorial position that makes possible the realization of that sense on the page. The dialogic sense of truth, as it manifests in Dostoevsky, is a radically different way of understanding the world to that of the monologic. Dostoevsky's novels, according to Bakhtin, cannot be understood from within the monological tradition of western thought, a way of thinking about'truth' that has dominated religion, science, philosophy and literature for many centuries.
mgt:
Polyphony in literature, a concept developed by Russian philosopher and literary theorist Mikhail Bakhtin, refers to a narrative style in which multiple, independent voices or consciousnesses interact on equal terms within a text. Unlike monologic works, where the author's ideology dominates and characters' ideas are subordinated to a unified worldview, polyphonic works allow characters to express their own "voice-ideas"—a fusion of personality and perspective—without being finalized or objectified by the author. This approach fosters open-ended dialogue and the coexistence of diverse, unmerged perspectives.

Bakhtin identified Fyodor Dostoevsky as a master of the polyphonic novel, where ideas are not abstract propositions but are embodied in the unique orientations of characters. In such works, the author relinquishes monologic control, engaging as an equal participant in the dialogue rather than imposing predetermined conclusions. This creates a dynamic, eventful narrative where truths emerge through the interaction of autonomous voices.

In contrast, monologic literature is characterized by the author's ultimate authority, where ideas are either affirmed or repudiated within a fixed ideological framework. Polyphony, however, celebrates the unfinalizable nature of human consciousness, emphasizing the creative potential of dialogue and interaction.

trgt:
The Silver Jubilee Independence Cup was a One Day International cricket tournament held in Dhaka, Bangladesh during January 1998. The tournament was held as a celebration of 25 years of Bangladesh's independence and all the games were held at Bangabandhu National Stadium, Dhaka, Bangladesh.India, Pakistan and the hosts Bangladesh were the participating teams in the tournament.

India were the winners of the tournament after beating Pakistan in the third final of the best of three finals. India successfully chased Pakistan's total of 314/5 in a match that, at the time, was a world record for the highest successful run chase in One Day International cricket. Hrishikesh Kanitkar hit a four (Video)when 3 runs were required off the last two balls to help India chase down the record target and lift the Silver Jubilee Independence Cup.

Saurav Ganguly was awarded player of the match for his century in the third final, while Sachin Tendulkar was named the player of the series.
mgt:
The 1997–98 Silver Jubilee Independence Cup was a One Day International (ODI) cricket tournament held in Bangladesh from January 10 to 18, 1998, to commemorate the country's 25th year of independence. The tournament featured three teams—India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh—competing in a round-robin format, followed by a best-of-three finals. India emerged as the champions, defeating Pakistan in the final series to claim their first title in the competition.

The tournament saw standout performances, with Pakistan's Saeed Anwar scoring the most runs (315) and Saqlain Mushtaq taking the most wickets (13). India's Sachin Tendulkar was named Player of the Series for his contributions. The squads underwent notable changes, including Pakistan's decision to drop key players like Wasim Akram and Moin Khan, while Bangladesh made several adjustments to their lineup. The event highlighted competitive cricket and marked a significant milestone in Bangladesh's sporting history.

trgt: The 152 mm howitzer-gun M1937 (ML-20) (Lang-ru), is a Soviet heavy gun-howitzer. The gun was developed by the design bureau of the plant no 172, headed by F. F. Petrov, as a deep upgrade of the 152-mm gun M1910/34, in turn based on the 152-mm siege gun M1910, a pre-World War I design by Schneider. It was in production from 1937 to 1946. The ML-20 saw action in World War II, mainly as a corps / army level artillery piece of the Soviet Army. Captured guns were employed by Wehrmacht and the Finnish Army. Post World War II, the ML-20 saw combat in numerous conflicts during the mid to late twentieth century.
mgt: The 152 mm howitzer-gun M1937 (ML-20) was a Soviet artillery piece developed in the late 1930s by the design bureau of Plant No. 172, led by F. F. Petrov. Combining the characteristics of a howitzer and a gun, it was versatile in both direct and indirect fire roles. Produced from 1937 to 1947, with 6,884 units built, it was widely used during World War II in conflicts such as the Winter War and the Eastern Front. Known for its reliability, range, and powerful 152 mm shells, the ML-20 was also mounted on self-propelled guns like the SU-152 and ISU-152. It remained in service post-war and was exported globally.

trgt:
Kāfiristān, or Kāfirstān (Pashto: کاپیرستان) (Lang-prs), is a historical region that covered present-day Nuristan Province in Afghanistan and Chitral District of Pakistan. This historic region lies on, and mainly comprises, the basins of the rivers Alingar, Pech (Kamah), Landai Sin river and Kunar, and the intervening mountain ranges. It is bounded by the main range of the Hindu Kush on the north, Pakistan's Chitral District to the east, the Kunar Valley in the south and the Alishang River in the west.

Kafiristan took its name from the enduring kafir (non-Muslim) Nuristani inhabitants who once followed a distinct form of ancient Hinduism mixed with locally developed accretions; they were thus known to the surrounding predominantly Sunni Muslim population as Kafirs, meaning "disbelievers" or "infidels". They are closely related to the Kalash people, a fiercely independent people with a distinctive culture, language and religion.

The area extending from modern Nooristan to Kashmir was known as "Peristan", a vast area containing a host of "Kafir" cultures and Indo-European languages that became Islamized over a long period of time, which eventually led them to become Muslim on the orders of Emir Abdul Rahman Khan who conquered the territory in 1895–96. The region was earlier surrounded by Buddhist states that temporarily brought literacy and state rule to the mountains; the decline of Buddhism heavily isolated the region. It was surrounded by Muslim states in the 16th century.
mgt:
Kafiristan, meaning "land of the infidels" in Persian, is a historical region located in present-day northeastern Afghanistan and northwestern Pakistan. The name derives from the Arabic term *kāfir*, meaning "non-believer," and historically referred to the region's inhabitants, who practiced a form of polytheism before their forced conversion to Islam in 1895–96 under Emir Abdur Rahman Khan of Afghanistan. Following their conversion, the region was renamed Nuristan, meaning "land of the enlightened."

Kafiristan was historically part of the ancient Kapisa kingdom, which flourished under Buddhist rule until the decline of Buddhism in the region. The area became isolated and retained its distinct culture and religion until the late 19th century. European explorers, including Bento de Góis, Alexander Gardner, and George Scott Robertson, documented the region and its people before their conversion.

The region was known for its steep valleys, intricate wood carvings, and unique cultural practices. After its conquest, many artifacts were destroyed or repurposed, with some preserved in museums. A small group of Kafirs fled to Chitral, where the Kalasha people, descendants of the "Black Kafirs," continue to practice their traditional beliefs. Today, the region is part of Afghanistan's Nuristan Province and Pakistan's Chitral District, with the Kalasha culture surviving in isolated valleys.

trgt:
Sadik Ahmad Turkistani is an ethnic Uyghur born and raised in Taif, Saudi Arabia and an opponent of the Taliban. Held by the Taliban in Kandahar prison in Afghanistan, he was briefly freed when they were overthrown in late 2001.

One of the Kandahar Five, he was taken into custody by the Americans and shipped to Guantanamo Bay detention camp, Cuba in early 2002, where he was treated as an enemy combatant. Finally he was cleared for release in late 2005. He was repatriated to Saudi Arabia on June 24, 2006.
mgt: Sadik Ahmad Turkistani is a Turkmenistani national who was detained at Guantanamo Bay from 2001 to 2006 without charge. Prior to his capture by U.S. forces, he was imprisoned and tortured by the Taliban in Kandahar for over four years, accused of opposing the Taliban and al-Qaeda. After being freed by the Northern Alliance, he was handed over to U.S. forces and transported to Guantanamo, where he was later determined not to have been an enemy combatant. Turkistani was repatriated to Saudi Arabia in 2006 and later resettled in Taif, planning to start a business.

trgt: "Heartache Tonight" is a song written by Don Henley, Glenn Frey, Bob Seger and J. D. Souther, recorded by the Eagles and features Glenn Frey on lead vocals. The track was included on their album The Long Run and released as a single in 1979. It reached No. 1 on the U.S. Billboard Hot 100 in November of that year and was certified Platinum by the Recording Industry Association of America representing one million copies sold. It was the Eagles' final chart-topping song on the Hot 100.
mgt: "Heartache Tonight" is a song by the Eagles, released on September 18, 1979, as the lead single from their album *The Long Run*. Written by Don Henley, Glenn Frey, Bob Seger, and J.D. Souther, the song originated from a jam session and features a lively, blues rock style. It won the 1979 Grammy Award for Best Rock Performance by a Duo or Group with Vocal. The song was later covered by artists such as Conway Twitty, whose 1983 version reached No. 6 on the Billboard Hot Country Singles chart.

trgt: Kennicott Grove is an area of prairie and wooded lands that includes the home of John Kennicott (1802–1863) and his family, including his son Robert Kennicott (1835–1866). John Kennicott was an agriculturalist and a doctor. Robert Kennicott was a naturalist and an explorer, who founded the Chicago Academy of Sciences. The grove is 123 acres (0.50 km2) in size. It is located near the intersection of Milwaukee Ave. and Lake Ave. in Glenview, Illinois, in the United States. Kennicott Grove was declared a National Historic Landmark in 1976. The site is maintained by the Glenview Park District as The Grove National Historic Landmark or also The Grove, and contains a nature interpretive center, historic buildings (some original and some recreated), and nature trails.
mgt:
Kennicott Grove, located in Glenview, Illinois, is a U.S. National Historic Landmark and a site on the National Register of Historic Places. Established in 1836 by Dr. John A. Kennicott, the property was originally a 250-acre homestead known as "The Grove." Kennicott, a physician and agricultural pioneer, co-founded the Illinois State Agricultural Society and established the region's first commercial nursery. His son, Robert Kennicott, became a renowned naturalist and co-founder of the Chicago Academy of Sciences.

The site features the original 1856 house, restored in 1983, and additional educational facilities, including a nature center and a replica Potawatomi loghouse. Efforts by the Grove Heritage Association have preserved and expanded the property, which now spans 123 acres and attracts approximately 200,000 visitors annually.

trgt: The Michigan Northern Railway (reporting mark MIGN) was a railroad that provided service to the northern part of the Lower Peninsula of Michigan from 1976 to 1986. At the beginning of service on April 1, 1976, the MIGN operated the former Grand Rapids and Indiana Railroad from Comstock Park to Mackinaw City and the Walton Junction Branch to Traverse City. In 1982, the MIGN assumed operation of the former Chesapeake and Ohio Railway from Grawn to Williamsburg and from Charlevoix to Petoskey, and the former Ann Arbor Railroad from Alma to Frankfort.
mgt: The Michigan Northern Railway (MIGN) was a short-line railroad that operated in Michigan from 1976 to 1986. Established to take over the former Pennsylvania Railroad's Grand Rapids and Indiana Railroad between Comstock Park and Mackinaw City, it provided freight service, including connections to the Upper Peninsula via the SS *Chief Wawatam* ferry. The Michigan Department of Transportation subsidized its operations and later expanded its network. However, financial difficulties, declining traffic, and the loss of state subsidies led to its decline. By 1986, the MIGN ceased operations, and its remaining lines were abandoned or transferred to other operators.

trgt: William Archer (1820–1874) was an Australian architect, naturalist, grazier, politician and member of the prominent Archer family. He was the second son of Thomas Archer, a prominent pastoralist and politician himself. A keen interest in architecture led to him going to London to study architecture when he finished school, where he studied under William Rogers and Robert Stephenson. During his life he built many colonial buildings across Tasmania, served as a member of both the Tasmanian House of Assembly and Tasmanian Legislative Council and made significant contributions to botany, with several native Tasmanian plants named after him. Despite this he died penniless at his brothers house Fairfield on 15 October 1874.
mgt: William Archer (1820–1874) was an Australian architect, botanist, and politician from Tasmania. Born into the prominent Archer family, he studied architecture and surveying in London before returning to Tasmania, where he managed the family estate and later pursued a career in architecture. Archer designed notable buildings, including The Hutchins School, additions to the Woolmers and Brickendon estates, and the calendar house Mona Vale. He also contributed to botany, studying in England, illustrating orchids, and sending Tasmanian plant specimens to Kew Gardens. A member of the Tasmanian parliament, he served in both the Legislative Council and House of Representatives. Several plant species and a genus are named in his honor.

trgt: Henry Hastings, 3rd Earl of Huntingdon, KG, KB (c. 1535 – 14 December 1595) was an English Puritan nobleman. Educated alongside the future Edward VI, he was briefly imprisoned by Mary I, and later considered by some as a potential successor to Elizabeth I. He hotly opposed the scheme to marry Mary, Queen of Scots, to the Duke of Norfolk, and was entrusted by Elizabeth to see that the Scottish queen did not escape at the time of the threatened uprising in 1569. He served as President of the Council of the North from 1572 until his death in 1595.
mgt: Henry Hastings, 3rd Earl of Huntingdon (1535/1536 – 14 December 1595), was an English nobleman, administrator, and military commander. Educated in the principles of Humanism, he was a supporter of Protestantism and a patron of Puritan figures. Hastings played a significant role in Tudor politics, serving as President of the Council of the North from 1572 and assisting in the trial of Mary, Queen of Scots. Despite his Yorkist ancestry and claim to the throne, his political ambitions were limited by Queen Elizabeth I's distrust. He died childless in 1595 and was buried at St Helen's Church, Ashby-de-la-Zouch.

trgt:
Norma Aleandro (born 2 May 1936) is an Argentine actress. She is considered one of the most celebrated and prolific Argentine actresses of all time and is recognized as a cultural icon in her home country.

Aleandro starred in the 1985 film The Official Story, a role that earned her the Cannes Award for best actress. She has performed in other successful films like The Truce (1974), Cousins (1989), Autumn Sun (1996), The Lighthouse (1998), Son of the Bride (2001) and Cama Adentro (2005). For her performance as Florencia Sánchez Morales in the 1987 film Gaby: A True Story, she received a Golden Globe nomination and an Academy Award nomination for Best Supporting Actress.

Aleandro wrote the 1970 film The Inheritors and performed in various plays such as August: Osage County. Aleandro also appeared in the Argentine adaptation of BeTipul, the critically successful En terapia.
mgt:
Norma Aleandro (born May 2, 1936) is an acclaimed Argentine actress, screenwriter, and playwright. Born in Buenos Aires to a family of actors, she began her career in 1952 and became one of Argentina's most celebrated performers. Exiled during the military dictatorship of the late 1970s due to her progressive views, she lived in Uruguay and Spain before returning to Argentina in 1983.

Aleandro gained international recognition for her role in the Academy Award-winning film *The Official Story* (1985), earning the Cannes Award for Best Actress. She also appeared in notable Argentine films such as *Son of the Bride*, *Sol de Otoño*, and *El Faro*, as well as Hollywood productions like *Gaby: A True Story* (1987), for which she received an Academy Award nomination.

A prominent stage actress, Aleandro won the María Guerrero Award in 1996 and was named an Illustrious Citizen of Buenos Aires the same year.

trgt:
I've Sound, or simply called I've (アイブ, Aibu), is a Japanese techno/trance music production group based in Sapporo, Hokkaidō, Japan. Led by Kazuya Takase, it features the talents of seasoned "sound creators" and many different vocalists, known as utahime (歌姫) to their fans.

They have created the theme songs for many Japanese adult games, most famously the opening "Tori no Uta" to the visual novel Air by Key. More recently, they have been creating the theme songs to anime series. Among anime fans, I've Sound is well known for providing the theme songs to such series as the Please! franchise (Please Teacher! and Please Twins!), Kanon, Air, Kannazuki no Miko, Black Lagoon, the Shakugan no Shana series, Starship Operators, Higurashi no Naku Koro ni, the Hayate the Combat Butler series, Nanatsuiro Drops, A Certain Magical Index, Rideback, Sky Girls, High School DxD, Jormungand, Tokyo Ravens, To Love-Ru series, Waiting in the Summer, Nagi-Asu: A Lull in the Sea, Aokana: Four Rhythm Across the Blue and Girly Air Force.
mgt:
I've Sound is a Japanese music production group founded in 1998, known for its work in creating theme songs for visual novels, anime, and other media. Initially producing music for adult games and karaoke, the group gained recognition with their contributions to the popular visual novel *Kanon* in 1999, which featured their arranged theme songs. This success led to further collaborations with Visual Art's and the release of their first compilation album, *Regret*, in 1999.

The group expanded its reach into anime in 2002 with the series *Please Teacher!* and saw several of its vocalists, including Kotoko, Mami Kawada, and Mell, achieve major debuts. Over the years, I've Sound has held concerts, released numerous albums, and introduced new vocalists and sound creators. Despite lineup changes and retirements, the group has continued to produce music, with notable releases and contributions to anime and visual novels through the 2010s and beyond, maintaining its influence in the Japanese music industry.

trgt: Courtney Andrew Walsh OJ (born 30 October 1962) is a former Jamaican cricketer who represented the West Indies from 1984 to 2001, captaining the West Indies in 22 Test matches. He is a fast bowler and considered one of the all time greats, best known for a remarkable opening bowling partnership along with fellow West Indian Curtly Ambrose for several years. Walsh played 132 Tests and 205 ODIs for the West Indies and took 519 and 227 wickets respectively. He shared 421 Test wickets with Ambrose in 49 matches. He held the record of most Test wickets from 2000, after he broke the record of Kapil Dev. This record was later broken in 2004 by Shane Warne. He was the first bowler to reach 500 wickets in Test cricket. His autobiography is entitled "Heart of the Lion". Walsh was named one of the Wisden Cricketers of the Year in 1987. In October 2010, he was inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame. He was appointed as the Specialist Bowling Coach of Bangladesh Cricket Team in August 2016.
mgt:
Courtney Andrew Walsh (born October 30, 1962) is a former Jamaican cricketer and one of the greatest fast bowlers in the history of cricket. Representing the West Indies from 1984 to 2001, Walsh played 132 Test matches and 205 One Day Internationals (ODIs), taking 519 and 227 wickets, respectively. He was the first bowler in Test cricket history to surpass 500 wickets and held the record for the most Test wickets until 2004. Known for his remarkable durability, accuracy, and sportsmanship, Walsh formed a legendary bowling partnership with Curtly Ambrose, sharing 421 wickets in 49 Tests.

Walsh also had a distinguished domestic career, playing for Jamaica and Gloucestershire County Cricket Club. He was named one of Wisden's Cricketers of the Year in 1987 and was inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame in 2010. Post-retirement, Walsh has served as a coach, including as head coach of the West Indies women's team. Renowned for his dedication and humility, Walsh remains a celebrated figure in cricket history.

trgt:
Anthony Seibold (/siːboʊld/) (born 3 October 1974) is an Australian rugby union coach, and former rugby league coach and player. He is currently defence coach with the England national rugby union team, and coach for the Manly Warringah Sea Eagles NRL team.

Seibold played rugby league for the Brisbane Broncos, Saint-Esteve, Canberra Raiders, London Broncos and Hull Kingston Rovers.

After retiring, he moved into coaching, and was head coach of the South Sydney Rabbitohs in the 2018 NRL season, and of the Brisbane Broncos from 2019 to 2020. He moved to rugby union and joined England in September 2021.
mgt: Anthony Seibold (born 3 October 1974) is an Australian rugby league coach and former professional player. He is currently the head coach of the Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles in the National Rugby League (NRL) and has also worked as a defence coach for the England national rugby union team. As a player, Seibold had stints with the Canberra Raiders, London Broncos, Hull Kingston Rovers, and other clubs. His coaching career includes roles with the South Sydney Rabbitohs, where he was named Dally M Coach of the Year in 2018, and the Brisbane Broncos, as well as assistant coaching positions.

trgt:
Te Mapoutahi or simply Mapoutahi is a peninsula on the coast of Otago, New Zealand, between the townships of Waitati and Pūrākaunui, within the limits of Dunedin City. It lies some 20 kilometres (12 mi) north of Dunedin's city centre.

Known also, confusingly, as "Goat Island", the peninsula's narrow isthmus and rugged coastline made it the perfect defensive location for a pā, a fortified settlement. The headland was, indeed, occupied by a Kāti Māmoe pā during the century before the arrival of Europeans in the early 1800s, and was the site of a major massacre in around 1750 when the warrior Taoka attacked the fortifications and killed those within.

The peninsula is now a scenic and historic reserve and is the site of a popular walking track.

The adjacent beach, Mapoutahi Cove, is used by naturists for nude walks and sunbathing. New Zealand has no official nude beaches, as public nudity is legal on any beach where it is "known to occur".
mgt:
Mapoutahi was the site of a historic massacre in southern New Zealand, involving a conflict between Kāti Māmoe chiefs Taoka and Te Wera. The dispute began when Taoka, based near present-day Timaru, visited Te Wera at his pā (fortified village) at Huriawa, near the Waikouaiti River. During a visit to another relative, Kapo, an argument escalated, leading to Te Wera killing Taoka's son. In retaliation, Taoka raised a war party and besieged Huriawa for a year without success. 

Taoka then turned his attention to Te Wera's ally, Pakihaukea, at Mapoutahi Pā. Exploiting a lapse in security, Taoka's forces attacked at night, breaching the pā's defenses and massacring approximately 250 inhabitants. The aftermath of the slaughter was so devastating that the nearby settlement of Pūrākaunui was named in reference to the sight of bodies piled in a heap outside the pā. This event remains a significant episode in the history of intertribal warfare in the region.

trgt:
Sergio Llull Melià (IPA-es; born November 15, 1987) is a Spanish professional basketball player and the team captain for Real Madrid of the Spanish Liga ACB and the EuroLeague. He is a 6 ft 3 in (1.91 m) tall point guard and shooting guard.

Llull is a two−time All-EuroLeague Team selection, and was the EuroLeague MVP of the 2016–17 season. As a regular member of the senior Spain national team, he has won a FIBA World Cup title, three EuroBasket gold medals, as well as a bronze. He also won two Summer Olympics medals, a silver in 2012, and a bronze in 2016.
mgt:
Sergio Llull (born November 15, 1987) is a Spanish professional basketball player for Real Madrid of the Liga ACB and the EuroLeague. A versatile guard, he has played for Real Madrid since 2007, winning numerous titles, including two EuroLeague championships (2015, 2018) and seven Liga ACB titles. Llull was named EuroLeague MVP in 2017 and has earned multiple individual accolades throughout his career.

Selected in the 2009 NBA draft, his rights are currently held by the New York Knicks. Llull has also been a key player for the Spanish national team, winning gold at the 2019 FIBA World Cup and three EuroBasket championships.

trgt:
Hambleden Lock is a lock with a long weir situated on the River Thames in England, about 2 miles downstream of Henley Bridge. The lock is on the Berkshire bank between Aston and Remenham. Built by the Thames Navigation Commission in 1773, the lock is named after the village of Hambleden, a mile (1.5 km) to the north.

The great weir is impressive and there are walkways over it from the lock to the small village of Mill End on the Buckinghamshire bank. Here is situated the picturesque Hambleden Mill, and the site of a Roman villa is nearby.
mgt: Hambleden Lock is a lock with a weir on the River Thames in Berkshire, England, maintained by the Environment Agency. First built in 1773 and rebuilt in 1994, it operates hydraulically and features a fall of 1.44 meters. The lock is accessible from the village of Aston and via walkways across the weirs. Historically, the site dates back to the Domesday Book, with references to a weir and flash lock as early as 1338. The reach above the lock includes notable landmarks such as Greenlands, Temple Island, and the Henley Royal Regatta course.

trgt: Gina Bachauer (Greek: Τζίνα Μπαχάουερ; May 21, 1910, Athens – August 22, 1976, Athens), was a Greek classical pianist who toured extensively in the United States and Europe. Interested in piano at a young age, Bachauer graduated from the Athens Conservatory and studied under Alfred Cortot and Sergei Rachmaninoff. She is best known for playing Romantic piano concertos. She played hundreds of concerts for the Allied troops in the Middle East during World War II while she lived in Egypt. She spent a lot of time touring the United States and Europe, giving over 100 concerts each year. Bachauer also recorded extensively, both as a soloist and with orchestras. She received an honorary doctorate from the University of Utah. During her career she was called the "queen of pianists". The Gina Bachauer International Piano Foundation was named in honor of her contributions to the musical world. In her personal life, Bachauer married music conductor Alec Sherman, who became her manager. She died at the age of 66 at the Athens Festival.
mgt:
Gina Bachauer (1913–1976) was a renowned Greek pianist, celebrated as one of the greatest pianists of the 20th century. Born in Athens, she showed early talent for the piano, giving her first recital as a child and graduating from the Athens Conservatory in 1929. She studied under Alfred Cortot and Sergei Rachmaninoff, the latter of whom she followed around the world for lessons. Despite interruptions to her career due to financial difficulties and World War II, she achieved international acclaim, performing over 100 concerts annually across Europe and the Americas. Known as the "queen of pianists," she was particularly admired for her interpretations of Romantic piano concertos.

Bachauer recorded extensively and supported young pianists through mentorship and competitions. She was a close collaborator with the Utah Symphony Orchestra and held an honorary doctorate from the University of Utah. The Gina Bachauer International Piano Competition, established in 1976, continues her legacy. She died of a heart attack in 1976, moments before a scheduled performance in Athens.

trgt:
Save Our Waterways Now (SOWN) is a community-based organisation rehabilitating Enoggera catchment in Brisbane, the largest city in Queensland, Australia. The principal waterway in the Enoggera catchment is Enoggera Creek with two main tributaries, Fish Creek and Ithaca Creek.

SOWN was incorporated on 18 February 1998 as Save Our Waterways - Now Inc., organisation number IA19057, an association under the jurisdiction of the Office of Fair Trading, Queensland. SOWN was formed as a response to habitat loss and environmental degradation. Enoggera Creek and its tributaries had been degraded by past agricultural land-uses and by the dumping of refuse and garden waste from the homes and developments in the catchment.

SOWN operates a volunteer-run plant nursery which propagates more than 30,000 locally native species every year and provides them free to SOWN members.
mgt:
Save Our Waterways Now (SOWN) is an Australian environmental organization focused on habitat restoration and waterway management in the Enoggera catchment area of Brisbane, Queensland. Established in 1994 by Brisbane City Council Alderman Brian Hallinan as a project of Men of the Trees, SOWN brought together environmental activists and local residents to restore native vegetation and improve waterways. The organization received funding from the National Heritage Trust, enabling it to expand its efforts.

By the late 1990s, SOWN had become a significant environmental group, with numerous restoration sites and hundreds of active volunteers contributing thousands of hours to planting native trees and grasses. In 2007, SOWN merged with the Brisbane Rainforest Action and Information Network (BRAIN) and launched its flagship project, Ithaca Intact, in 2008 to restore Ithaca Creek.

trgt:
The handscroll is a long, narrow, horizontal scroll format in East Asia used for calligraphy or paintings. A handscroll usually measures up to several meters in length and around 25–40 cm in height. Handscrolls are generally viewed starting from the right end. This kind of scroll is intended to be read or viewed flat on a table, in sections. The format thus allows for the depiction of a continuous narrative or journey.

The traditional alternative format in East Asian painting is the vertical hanging scroll, which is rarely as long.
mgt: A handscroll (Chinese: 手捲) is a traditional East Asian art format used for paintings and texts, designed to be viewed horizontally by unrolling it section by section. Originating in ancient China, handscrolls were initially used for text documents on bamboo or wooden slips during the Spring and Autumn period (770–481 BCE) and later adapted for paper and silk by the Eastern Han period (25–220 CE). They became a standard format for paintings by the Three Kingdoms period (220–280). Handscrolls were introduced to Japan through Buddhism, where they evolved into emakimono, often depicting narrative themes.

trgt: Sage Kotsenburg (born July 27, 1993) is an American snowboarder. He won the first-ever Olympic gold medal in men's snowboard slopestyle at the 2014 Winter Olympic Games in Sochi, Russia, and became the first gold medalist at these Olympics. Kotsenburg won a silver medal in snowboard slopestyle at the 2012 Winter X Games XVI in Aspen, Colorado, behind Mark McMorris. Kotsenburg won a bronze medal in Snowboard Big Air at the 2011 Winter X Games XV in Aspen, Colorado, behind Torstein Horgmo and Sebastien Toutant.
mgt: Sage Cullen Kotsenburg (born July 27, 1993) is an American professional snowboarder and Olympic gold medalist. He won the inaugural gold medal in slopestyle snowboarding at the 2014 Winter Olympics in Sochi, becoming the first gold medalist of those Games and the first U.S. medalist. Known for his creativity, Kotsenburg performed a trick he had never attempted before, featuring his signature grab, the "Holy Crail." Born in Coeur d'Alene, Idaho, and raised in Park City, Utah, he began snowboarding at age five and turned professional in 2010.

trgt: Ibex Peak, is a 2,039-metre (6,690-foot) granitic horn located in the North Cascades of southwestern British Columbia, Canada. It is situated 13 km (8 mi) west-northwest of Coquihalla Summit, and 1 km (1 mi) northwest of Steinbok Peak. Its nearest higher peak is Alpaca Peak, 6.2 km (4 mi) to the east. Precipitation runoff from the peak drains into tributaries of the Anderson River. The mountain was named for the ibex, as part of the ungulate theme for several other nearby peaks that were submitted by Philip Kubik of Vancouver. The mountain's name was officially adopted on February 5, 1976, by the Geographical Names Board of Canada.
mgt:
Ibex Peak is a 2,039-meter (6,690-foot) mountain in the North Cascades of British Columbia, Canada. It is part of the Yale Division Yale Land District and is located near its parent peak, Guanaco Peak. The peak was first ascended in 1962 by Asger Bentzen, Hamish Mutch, Glenn Woodsworth, and Bob Woodsworth, with the easiest route being a scramble via the northwest slope. 

Geologically, Ibex Peak and the North Cascades were formed through volcanic activity, tectonic uplift, and glaciation over millions of years, resulting in rugged terrain and diverse ecosystems. The area experiences a marine west coast climate, with heavy precipitation and favorable climbing conditions from July to September.

trgt:
Derek Christopher Shepherd, M.D., F.A.C.S., also referred to as "McDreamy", is a fictional surgeon from the ABC medical drama Grey's Anatomy, portrayed by actor Patrick Dempsey. He made his first appearance in the pilot episode, "A Hard Day's Night", which was broadcast on March 27, 2005.

Derek was married to Addison Montgomery (Kate Walsh) before their divorce in 2007. Before his death in 2015, Derek was happily married to his longtime partner and wife Meredith Grey (Ellen Pompeo). The couple had three children together. Shepherd was formerly the Chief of Surgery at Seattle Grace Mercy West (now Grey Sloan Memorial Hospital) but abruptly resigned from his position as Chief in season 7 following the shooting.

Dempsey was nominated for the 2006 and 2007 Golden Globe for Best Performance by an Actor in a Television Series Drama for the role, and the 2006 SAG Award for Outstanding Performance by an Actor in a Drama Series.
mgt:
Derek Shepherd, also known as "McDreamy," is a fictional character from the medical drama *Grey's Anatomy*, portrayed by Patrick Dempsey. Created by Shonda Rhimes, Derek first appeared in the series premiere in 2005 and remained a central character until his death in season 11, with later appearances in dream sequences. A renowned neurosurgeon and former Chief of Surgery at Seattle Grace Hospital, Derek was known for his exceptional skill, compassionate bedside manner, and willingness to take on challenging cases. His complex personal life, including his relationships with Meredith Grey, Addison Montgomery, and his family, was a major focus of the series.

Derek's romance with Meredith Grey became one of television's most iconic love stories, marked by challenges such as infidelity, professional conflicts, and family struggles. The character received widespread acclaim for Dempsey's performance, earning him the nickname "McDreamy" and cementing his status as a fan favorite. Derek's death remains one of the show's most emotional moments.

trgt:
Mark Maryboy (born December 10, 1955) is a retired American politician for San Juan County, Utah, and a former Navajo Nation Council Delegate for the Utah Navajo Section of the Navajo Tribe.
