He is the brother of Kenneth Maryboy who currently serves as the Navejo Nation Council Delegate, in the positions he once stood. Mark is of the Navajo Indian Tribe.

Mark Maryboy was also the San Juan County, Utah Commissioner for District Three. He made history when he was elected in 1986 as the first Native American county commissioner in Utah's history.

Rebecca M. Benally along with the former Navajo Nation Council Delegate Mark Maryboy and protesters from the Aneth Chapter Blocked the main ExxonMobil corporate office for three days in Aneth, Utah.
mgt: Mark Maryboy (born December 10, 1955) is a Navajo politician, activist, and advocate for Indigenous rights. He was the first Native American elected as a San Juan County Commissioner in Utah, serving four terms starting in 1986, and later represented the Aneth area as a delegate to the Navajo Nation Council from 1991 to 2007. Maryboy is known for his efforts to address inequities faced by Navajo communities, including advocating for improved infrastructure, water rights, and fair distribution of oil royalties. He played a key role in the Aneth Oil Protest and co-founded the Utah Navajo Health Systems in 1999. Maryboy has also served on national advisory committees and represented Indigenous interests at international forums.

trgt:
Fredric Sheldon Brandt (June 26, 1949 – April 5, 2015) was an American physician, researcher, lecturer, author, and radio host specializing in cosmetic dermatology. Among the first to use botulinum toxin ("botox") and fillers, Brandt was noted for his role in the FDA approval of numerous fillers and botulinum toxins for cosmetic use in the United States.

Brandt, who hosted a radio show called Ask Dr. Brandt on SiriusXM radio and whose celebrity patients included Madonna, was called the "Baron of Botox" by W magazine and "King of Collagen" for using more botox and collagen than any other dermatologist in the world.
mgt: Fredric Sheldon Brandt (June 26, 1949 – April 5, 2015) was an American physician, author, and pioneer in cosmetic dermatology. Known for his innovative skin care techniques, he operated dermatology practices in Miami and New York City and conducted clinical research on fillers, botulinum toxins, lasers, and cosmetic ingredients at his Dermatology Research Institute in Miami. Brandt authored two books on skin aging and maintaining a youthful appearance. Born in Newark, New Jersey, he earned his medical degree from Hahnemann Medical College and completed residencies in internal medicine and dermatology. Brandt died by suicide in Coconut Grove, Florida, at the age of 65.

trgt: Jose Vasquez Aguilar (March 23, 1900 - January 31, 1980) is the first Filipino recipient of the Ramon Magsaysay Award (Asian equivalent of Nobel Prize) and also the first awardee of the said award for Government Service (Ramon Magsaysay Award for Government Service). He is also a recipient in the government service category of the said award which he shared with Chintaman Deshmukh in 1959. He is also recognized as the Father of the Community School Movement in the Philippines, pioneering reforms to ensure that community life is integrated in the education system. His community school model banks on the partnership between teachers, parents, and community with the goal of ensuring a practical education using the vernacular as a medium of instruction.
mgt: Jose Vasquez Aguilar (March 23, 1900 – January 31, 1980) was a Filipino educator, writer, and government official known for his contributions to the Philippine educational system and community development. He pioneered the Community School model, emphasizing collaboration between teachers, parents, and communities, and conducted groundbreaking research advocating for the use of vernacular languages in early education. Aguilar served as a division superintendent in various provinces and later became dean of the College of Education at the University of the Philippines. In 1959, he co-directed the Philippine Center for Language Studies. For his work, he received the Ramon Magsaysay Award for Government Service in 1959. Aguilar also authored the novel *The Great Faith*, reflecting on the Japanese Occupation of the Philippines.

trgt: John Pierrakos (February 8, 1921 – February 1, 2001) was an American physician and psychiatrist. A student of Wilhelm Reich, he developed bioenergetic analysis, a form of mind-body psychotherapy, with his then-colleague Alexander Lowen (December 23, 1910 – October 28, 2008). Pierrakos was the founder and director of the Institute of Core Energetics (1973-2001), co-founder of the Bioenergetics Institute in New York, NY with Lowen (1955-1970), and co-founder of The Pathwork Center, Phoenicia, NY, in association with his wife, Eva Pierrakos.
mgt: John Pierrakos (February 8, 1921 – February 1, 2001) was a Greek-American psychiatrist and co-founder of bioenergetic analysis, a body-oriented psychotherapy. A student of Wilhelm Reich, he collaborated with Alexander Lowen to establish the Bioenergetics Institute in 1955, introducing concepts like grounding and energy flow in the body. In 1969, Pierrakos founded The Pathwork Center with his wife, Eva Pierrakos, integrating spiritual and psychological approaches. He later established the Institute of Core Energetics in 1973, emphasizing holistic healing through physical, emotional, and spiritual integration.

trgt:
Coryphodon (from Greek κορῦφὴ, "point", and ὀδοὺς, "tooth", meaning peaked tooth, referring to "the development of the angles of the ridges into points [on the molars].") is an extinct genus of pantodonts of the family Coryphodontidae.

Coryphodon was a pantodont, a member of the world's first group of large browsing mammals. It migrated across what is now northern North America, replacing Barylambda, an earlier pantodont. It is regarded as the ancestor of the genus Hypercoryphodon of Late Eocene Mongolia.

Coryphodon is known from many specimens in North America and considerably fewer in Europe, Mongolia, and China. It is a small to medium-sized coryphodontid who differs from other members of the family in dental characteristics.
mgt: **Coryphodon** is an extinct genus of large, slow-moving mammals that lived during the Late Paleocene to Middle Eocene, approximately 57 to 46 million years ago. Belonging to the order Pantodonta, it was one of the largest mammals of its time, with a body length of 2.5 meters (8.2 feet) and a weight ranging from 340 to 700 kilograms (750 to 1,500 pounds). Despite its size, Coryphodon had a small brain relative to its body. It likely had a semi-aquatic lifestyle, inhabiting swamps and marshes, and its diet varied seasonally, including plants, fungi, and leaf litter. Fossils have been found as far north as Ellesmere Island, revealing adaptations to Arctic conditions.

trgt: The Swiss Social Archives (Lang-de, Lang-fr) in Zurich is a historical archive, an academic library, a collection of documentation and a research facility specialising in social issues and social movements. The Swiss Confederation recognizes the archives as the country's leading research facility for social issues and social movements. The Social Archives run their own research endowment fund, the Ellen Rifkin Hill Foundation. The SSA play an important role in communicating scholarship to the broader community. They have convened several exhibitions, published essay collections on Swiss social history and conduct lecture series, presentations and information sessions. The archives work with Swiss secondary and tertiary educational institutions, archives and libraries and with similar institutions abroad. The SSA are a founding member of the International Association of Labour History Institutions (IALHI).
mgt: The Swiss Social Archives (SSA) is a memory institution and research facility located in Zurich, Switzerland. Established in 1906 by social reformer Paul Pflüger, it was originally known as the Centre for Switzerland's Social Literature and aimed to document social issues, inspired by the Musée social in Paris. Over the years, it became a hub for intellectuals and political refugees, including Lenin and Trotsky, as well as antifascist and Cold War-era Eastern European refugees. Renamed in the 1940s, the archives were officially recognized as a research facility by the Swiss Confederation in 1974. Today, the SSA is designated as a Swiss cultural property of national significance and is managed by an independent association, employing historians, archivists, and librarians under the leadership of Christian Koller.

trgt:
Nutrisystem is a commercial provider of weight loss products and services headquartered in Fort Washington, Pennsylvania. Nutrisystem's initial product in 1972 was a liquid protein diet, but the company changed its offering after Slim-Fast came to prominence in that market. The company originally offered weight loss counseling and products in brick and mortar centers, but in 1999, Nutrisystem moved to a direct-to-consumer business model, selling its products and programs on the Internet and by telephone.

The company launched its mobile platform in 2010. WebDiet, Inc., a Silicon Valley startup, accused Nutrisystem of stealing their technology, but the case was dismissed in May, 2014.

In 2021, Stephen Mikulak was named President of Nutrisystem.

In 2015, the firm acquired the South Beach Diet brand. In December 2018, Tivity Health announced that it would acquire the Nutrisystem brand. On October 19, 2020, Kainos Capital acquired the brand from Tivity Health.
mgt:
Nutrisystem is an American company specializing in weight loss and weight management products and services. Founded in 1972 by Harold Katz, the company is headquartered in Fort Washington, Pennsylvania, and operates in the United States and Canada. Nutrisystem offers pre-packaged meals, nutrition plans, and counseling through telephone, internet, and mobile platforms. It is a subsidiary of Kainos Capital, with Stephen Mikulak serving as its president.

A 2015 systematic review found that Nutrisystem showed promise for short-term weight loss compared to control groups, though no studies on its long-term efficacy were available. The program's cost, averaging $274.99 for a four-week basic plan as of 2019, has been criticized as potentially prohibitive. Additionally, experts note that the diet's structured nature may complicate social eating. Despite these criticisms, Nutrisystem remains a prominent option for individuals seeking structured weight management solutions.

trgt:
Beautiful Creatures is the debut studio album by American hard rock band Beautiful Creatures. It was produced by Sean Beavan at Bay 7 Studios in Valley Village, Los Angeles, California, and was released on August 16, 2001, through Warner Bros. Records. The album peaked at number 29 on the Billboard Top Heatseekers, while the single "Wasted" peaked at number 37 on the Mainstream Rock Tracks.

Following the release of the album, Beautiful Creatures went on to promote the album, performing at Ozzfest 2001, from June 8—August 12, 2001, the Rolling Rock Tour as well performing in Japan. The album received mixed reviews.
mgt: *Beautiful Creatures* is the self-titled debut studio album by the American hard rock band Beautiful Creatures, released on August 16, 2001, through Warner Bros. Records. The album was recorded between 2000 and 2001 at Bay 7 Studios in Los Angeles, California, with producer Sean Beavan. Formed in 1999 by guitarist DJ Ashba and vocalist Joe LeSte, the band gained attention after opening for Kiss during their reunion tour. The album received mixed reviews, with critics praising its musicianship and comparisons to Guns N' Roses, while noting a lack of originality. The lead single, "Wasted," was released in 2001.

trgt:
Alnus glutinosa, the common alder, black alder, European alder, European black alder, or just alder, is a species of tree in the family Betulaceae, native to most of Europe, southwest Asia and northern Africa. It thrives in wet locations where its association with the bacterium Frankia alni enables it to grow in poor quality soils. It is a medium-sized, short-lived tree growing to a height of up to 30 metres (98 feet). It has short-stalked rounded leaves and separate male and female flowers in the form of catkins. The small, rounded fruits are cone-like and the seeds are dispersed by wind and water.

The common alder provides food and shelter for wildlife, with a number of insects, lichens and fungi being completely dependent on the tree. It is a pioneer species, colonising vacant land and forming mixed forests as other trees appear in its wake. Eventually common alder dies out of woodlands because the seedlings need more light than is available on the forest floor. Its more usual habitat is forest edges, swamps and riverside corridors. The timber has been used in underwater foundations and for manufacture of paper and fibreboard, for smoking foods, for joinery, turnery and carving. Products of the tree have been used in ethnobotany, providing folk remedies for various ailments, and research has shown that extracts of the seeds are active against pathogenic bacteria.
mgt:
*Alnus glutinosa*, commonly known as the black alder or common alder, is a deciduous tree in the birch family (Betulaceae) native to much of Europe, parts of Asia, and North Africa. It thrives in moist soils near rivers, lakes, and wetlands but can also grow in drier locations. The tree typically reaches heights of 20–30 meters (66–98 feet) and is characterized by its sticky young leaves and shoots, rounded serrated leaves, and distinctive catkins. It is monoecious, with wind-pollinated male and female flowers, and its seeds are adapted for water dispersal.

The species plays a significant ecological role due to its symbiotic relationship with the nitrogen-fixing bacterium *Frankia alni*, which enhances soil fertility. It is also a pioneer species in ecological succession and supports diverse wildlife, including insects, birds, and fungi. However, it is susceptible to diseases such as phytophthora disease of alder.

*Alnus glutinosa* has been widely introduced outside its native range, where it can become invasive. Its wood is valued for its durability underwater, making it historically significant for construction, including the foundations of Venice. The tree is also used for erosion control, dyeing, tanning, and traditional medicine. Despite its utility, its pollen is a common allergen in Europe. The species is classified as "Least Concern" by the IUCN.

trgt:
Guylian (/ˈɡiːliən/, IPA-fr) is a Belgian chocolate brand and manufacturer best known for its seashell shaped pralines. The company was founded in 1958 in Sint-Niklaas, Belgium by Guy Foubert and is now owned by the South Korean company Lotte Confectionery.

Foubert and his wife formulated the original chocolate recipe and "Guylian" was formed as a portmanteau from the first names of Guy and Liliane Foubert. While Guylian specialise in roasted hazelnut pralines they produce a wide variety of products including chocolate covered fruit pieces and chocolate truffles.

The company headquarters remain in Sint-Niklaas along with their production plant but now Guylian exports internationally to approximately 120 countries. In 2003, the first Guylian Belgian Chocolate café was opened in Antwerp, Belgium; there are now 9 franchises in different locations around the world.

Guylian donates to and works with Project Seahorse to help with marine wildlife conservation and raise awareness for the charity. They have also worked with the Cocoa Horizons Foundations on Project Cocoa to sustainably source cocoa and have aimed to reduce any negative environmental impacts from their production. For this work, they have received Corporate Responsibility Awards along with multiple other awards for innovation and their chocolates.
mgt:
Guylian is a Belgian chocolate brand founded in 1958 by Guy Foubert in Sint-Niklaas, Belgium. The company is renowned for its signature praline-filled chocolates shaped like seashells and seahorses, a design created by Foubert's wife, Liliane. The name "Guylian" is a blend of their names, symbolizing their shared passion for chocolate-making. Now a subsidiary of South Korea's Lotte Confectionery, Guylian continues to produce its chocolates in Belgium, using Foubert's original recipe and sustainably sourced cocoa.

Guylian has expanded globally, with cafés in locations such as Sydney, Dubai, and Seoul, and exports accounting for 60% of its sales. The company has received numerous awards, including Belgium's highest export honor and multiple Superior Taste Awards. It also holds a Guinness World Record for the largest chocolate Easter egg sculpture.

Committed to sustainability, Guylian collaborates with the Cocoa Horizons Foundation and removed palm oil from its products in 2018. The company is a major sponsor of Project Seahorse, supporting marine conservation and raising awareness through initiatives like the Seahorses of the World Photo Competition. Guylian's dedication to quality, innovation, and environmental responsibility has solidified its reputation as a leading premium chocolate brand.

trgt: The 1910 Deperdussin monoplane, is a general term for a variety of Deperdussin aircraft models built between 1910 and 1911. Initially released in August 1910, it was the first aircraft to be built in significant quantities by Aéroplanes Deperdussin. Many variations of the aircraft were produced, differing in size, engine type and horsepower and tail configuration. These were loosely labelled as the "Deperdussin monoplane", but were sometimes more specifically classified either by function or by seating-type. A number of Deperdussin monoplanes were flown successfully in air races and gained several speed and endurance records during 1911–1912. Several have survived, including an airworthy example in the Shuttleworth Collection in England.
mgt: The Deperdussin 1910 monoplane was the first successful aircraft designed by Aéroplanes Deperdussin, a company founded in 1909 by Armand Deperdussin with Louis Béchereau as technical director. First flown in October 1910, it was a mid-wing monoplane with a narrow fuselage, wing warping for control, and a distinctive undercarriage. Powered initially by an Anzani engine, later variants used Gnome rotary engines. The aircraft achieved commercial success, with numerous variants produced, including racing and military models. The Type B, introduced in 1911, featured significant design improvements and saw service with the French military. Deperdussin monoplanes were also used in flying schools and exported internationally.

trgt:
Malcolm Rice Patterson (June 7, 1861 – March 8, 1935) was an American politician and jurist. He served in the U.S. House of Representatives from 1901 to 1906, and as the 30th governor of Tennessee from 1907 to 1911. He later served as a circuit court judge in Memphis (1923–1934), and wrote a weekly column for the Memphis Commercial Appeal (1921–1933).

Patterson was one of Tennessee's most controversial governors. While praised for quelling the Night Riders of Reelfoot Lake uprising in 1908, he was accused of issuing pardons to political allies, most notably his advisor Duncan Cooper, who had been convicted of murdering his political foe Edward W. Carmack. Patterson's veto of a popular prohibition bill in 1909 and his attempts to control the state Democratic primaries in 1910 created a division in the party that allowed Ben W. Hooper to become the first Republican governor elected in the state in nearly 30 years.
mgt:
Malcolm R. Patterson (June 7, 1861 – March 8, 1935) was an American attorney, politician, and judge who served as the 30th governor of Tennessee from 1907 to 1911 and as a U.S. Representative from Tennessee's 10th district from 1901 to 1906. A member of the Democratic Party, Patterson's governorship was marked by significant reforms, including the creation of a State Highway Commission, food and drug regulations, and the establishment of four state colleges. However, his tenure was marred by controversy, including a high-profile feud with Senator Edward W. Carmack, whose murder in 1908 led to accusations against Patterson and widespread criticism of his use of pardons.

After leaving office, Patterson became an advocate for Prohibition, joined the Anti-Saloon League, and later served as a judge in Shelby County. He unsuccessfully ran for U.S. Senate in 1915 and governor in 1932. Patterson died in 1935 and is buried in Memphis, Tennessee.

trgt:
Sainte-Geneviève-de-Batiscan is a parish municipality in the Mauricie region of the province of Quebec in Canada. This municipality is part of the Les Chenaux Regional County Municipality in Batiscanie and in the administrative region of Mauricie. It is located along the Batiscan River.

The town of Sainte-Geneviève-de-Batiscan was the seat of the Lordship of Batiscan from 1665 and Champlain County from 1855 to 1981. During the first centuries of its history, the economy of the area has been focused on forestry, agriculture, fishing and river transport. Sainte-Geneviève was also a prominent center of trade for the Batiscanie.
mgt: Sainte-Geneviève-de-Batiscan is a parish municipality in the Mauricie region of Quebec, Canada, located within the Les Chenaux Regional County Municipality. Constituted on July 1, 1855, it is situated along the Batiscan River, with its main village on the northeast shore and a smaller village on the southwest. The municipality has a population of 1,028 as of the 2021 Census, with a population density of 10.5/km². Named after Saint Genevieve, the patron saint of Paris, and the Algonquin chief Batiscan, the area has historical roots dating back to its Catholic parish's establishment in 1833 and its municipal incorporation in 1841.

trgt:
Dejarte de Amar (English: Stop Loving You) is the second studio album recorded by Mexican pop rock band Camila, It was released by Sony Music Latin on February 9, 2010 (see 2010 in music) Produced by band leader Mario Domm and mostly written by Domm and Mónica Velez, the album became a success in the United States and Mexico. The album was certified Disco Platino in the United States by the RIAA and 4× Platinum in Mexico by AMPROFON. It was recorded in Mexico at Mamita Studio with theme about love, loneliness and lies. The album spawned three singles: "Mientes", "Aléjate de Mi", and "Bésame", all of which reached number-one on the Billboard Latin Pop Airplay chart. The album received generally positive reviews for its arrangements and compositions.

The album reached number-one on the Billboard Top Latin Albums and Latin Pop Albums in the United States and number-one on the Mexican Albums Chart. It received a Latin Grammy award for Best Pop Vocal Album by a Duo or Group and a nomination for Album of the Year. The lead single, "Mientes", received two Latin Grammy awards for Song of the Year and Record of the Year. The album is currently nominated a Lo Nuestro award for "Latin Pop Album of the Year". Mientes was featured in the telenovela Corazón Salvaje (2009-2010) starring Eduardo Yáñez and Aracely Arámbula. Bésame was also featured in the telenovela Triunfo del Amor (2010-2011).
mgt:
*Dejarte de Amar* is the second studio album by Mexican pop rock band Camila, released on February 9, 2010, through Sony Music Latin. Produced by the band's lead vocalist Mario Domm, the album features a blend of Latin pop, pop rock, and alternative rock. It follows their successful debut album, *Todo Cambió* (2006), and includes six singles, such as the chart-topping hits "Mientes," "Aléjate de Mí," and "Bésame." The album's lead single, "Mientes," won two Latin Grammy Awards for Record of the Year and Song of the Year.

Critically acclaimed, *Dejarte de Amar* was praised for its evolved sound, with comparisons to Coldplay for its layered arrangements and emotional depth. It won the Latin Grammy for Best Pop Album by a Duo or Group and received multiple nominations, including Album of the Year. Commercially, the album debuted at number one on the Billboard Top Latin Albums and Latin Pop Albums charts, achieving Platinum certification in the United States and Diamond certification in Mexico for sales exceeding 450,000 copies. It also charted internationally, reaching the top ten in Mexico and Argentina.

The album solidified Camila's status as a leading Latin pop act, with Domm emphasizing the band's commitment to creating music that resonates deeply with listeners. *Dejarte de Amar* remains a milestone in the band's career and Latin pop music.

trgt:
In New York State, Regents Examinations are statewide standardized examinations in core high school subjects. Students are required to pass these exams to earn a Regents Diploma. To graduate, students are required to have earned appropriate credits in a number of specific subjects by passing year-long or half-year courses, after which they must pass at least five Regents examinations in some of the subject areas. For higher-achieving students, a Regents with Advanced designation and an Honors designation are also offered. Students with disabilities or enrolled in an English as a Second Language program are able to earn a local diploma.

The Regents Examinations are developed and administered by the New York State Education Department (NYSED) under the authority of the Board of Regents of the University of the State of New York. Regents exams are prepared by a conference of selected New York teachers of each test's specific discipline who assemble a test map that highlights the skills and knowledge required from the specific discipline's learning standards. The conferences meet and design the tests three years before the tests' issuance, which includes time for field testing and evaluating testing questions.
mgt:
The New York Regents Examinations are a series of standardized tests administered by the New York State Education Department (NYSED) to assess high school students' proficiency in core subjects. First introduced in 1866, the exams were expanded in 1878 to serve as high school end-of-course assessments. Over time, the exams evolved to reflect changes in educational priorities, transitioning from a wide array of subject-specific tests to more comprehensive exams. Vocational exams were discontinued by the 1970s, and foreign language exams were largely eliminated by 2011 due to budget cuts.

Regents exams are typically three hours long and are offered in January, June, and August, with exceptions like the Earth Science lab component. Students must pass at least five exams with a score of 65 or higher to earn a Regents Diploma, with additional requirements for Advanced Designation. Special provisions exist for students with disabilities and English language learners.

The exams have undergone significant changes, including alignment with Common Core Standards and adjustments due to the COVID-19 pandemic. While public schools are required to administer Regents exams, some private and alternative schools opt for different assessment methods.

trgt: Fear (commonly stylized as fear) is the third studio album by American alternative rock band Toad the Wet Sprocket. The album is their second album for Columbia Records, and was released on August 27, 1991. It became the first commercially successful album for the band, selling over a million copies and was certified platinum three years after release, on September 1, 1994. The album reached #49 on Billboard's Top 200 Albums in September 1992. Two of the album's singles charted in the US top 40, "All I Want" and "Walk on the Ocean" which peaked at #15 and #18 on the Billboard Hot 100, respectively.
mgt: *Fear* is the third studio album by American alternative rock band Toad the Wet Sprocket, released on August 27, 1991, by Columbia Records. Recorded in January 1991 at Granny's House in Reno, Nevada, the album was produced by Gavin MacKillop, who also worked on the band's subsequent releases. *Fear* features a blend of alternative and pop rock and includes notable singles such as "All I Want," "Walk on the Ocean," and "Hold Her Down." Two tracks from the recording sessions, "Good Intentions" and "All in All," were excluded from the album but later appeared on the 1995 rarities collection *In Light Syrup*.

trgt:
Faces of Ground Zero: A Photographic Tribute to America's Heroes was a traveling photo exhibition about the September 11 attacks. It was shown at several major cities in the United States, aiming to educate the public about the impact of modern urban terrorism. Faces of Ground Zero was one of the most widely seen exhibits about 9/11 and its aftermath.

The exhibit consisted of life-size photographs (9 ft × 4 ft framed images) of emergency workers, survivors, and relatives of victims of the attacks; some 272 people in all. (Some of the portraits included two or three subjects.) The aim was to capture the sense of loss, pain, and bravery of the time.

The touring exhibit was open to the public, free of charge. Made by photographer Joe McNally, in the Moby C Studio, a few blocks from the "Ground Zero" World Trade Center site in the immediate aftermath of 9/11, with "Moby C", the world's largest one-of-a-kind instant camera.

A hardcover companion photobook, Faces of Ground Zero: Portraits of the Heroes of September 11, 2001 (New York: Little, Brown & Co., 2002) was published to commemorate the project with a foreword by Rudy Giuliani and an original essay by McNally. A large percentage of the proceeds went to 9/11 charities.
mgt:
*Faces of Ground Zero* is a photographic exhibition and collection of portraits capturing the individuals impacted by the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks. Created by photographer Joe McNally, the exhibit features life-sized Polaroid images of first responders, survivors, and others connected to the events of 9/11. The exhibition debuted in 2002 and toured several major cities, including New York, Boston, London, Chicago, San Francisco, and Los Angeles. It returned to New York for significant anniversaries of the attacks, including the first, fifth, and tenth anniversaries, with notable displays at Rockefeller Center, the New York City Fire Museum, and the Time Warner Center. The tenth-anniversary exhibition, titled *Faces of Ground Zero: 10 Years Later*, included more than 50 images from the original collection alongside new portraits.  

The portraits from the exhibit were also published in photobooks, preserving the stories and faces of those affected by the tragedy. The project has been widely recognized for its emotional impact and its role in commemorating the resilience and humanity of those connected to 9/11.

trgt:
Mothers against decapentaplegic homolog 7 or SMAD7 is a protein that in humans is encoded by the SMAD7 gene.

SMAD7 is a protein that, as its name describes, is a homolog of the Drosophila gene: "Mothers against decapentaplegic". It belongs to the SMAD family of proteins, which belong to the TGFβ superfamily of ligands. Like many other TGFβ family members, SMAD7 is involved in cell signalling. It is a TGFβ type 1 receptor antagonist. It blocks TGFβ1 and activin associating with the receptor, blocking access to SMAD2. It is an inhibitory SMAD (I-SMAD) and is enhanced by SMURF2.

Smad7 enhances muscle differentiation.
mgt: Mothers against decapentaplegic homolog 7 (SMAD7) is a protein encoded by the *SMAD7* gene, located on chromosome 18 in humans. It is a key regulator of the transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β) signaling pathway, acting as an inhibitor by preventing the formation of Smad2/Smad4 complexes and blocking receptor activation. SMAD7 also mediates cross-talk between TGF-β and other signaling pathways, such as those involving epidermal growth factor (EGF) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α). Mutations in the *SMAD7* gene are associated with colorectal cancer susceptibility, and its role in other cancers, including pancreatic and breast cancer, remains under investigation.

trgt: In bioinformatics, a spaced seed is a pattern of relevant and irrelevant positions in a biosequence and a method of approximate string matching that allows for substitutions. They are a straightforward modification to the earliest heuristic-based alignment efforts that allow for minor differences between the sequences of interest. Spaced seeds have been used in homology search., alignment, assembly, and metagenomics. They are usually represented as a sequence of zeroes and ones, where a one indicates relevance and a zero indicates irrelevance at the given position. Some visual representations use pound signs for relevant and dashes or asterisks for irrelevant positions.
mgt: A spaced seed is a computational model used in bioinformatics to improve the sensitivity and efficiency of sequence alignment, homology search, and sequence assembly. Unlike non-spaced models, which require exact matches across consecutive bases, spaced seeds allow for mismatches at specific positions, enabling the identification of more potential matches. This approach, first formalized in the early 2000s with tools like PatternHunter, has been shown to enhance the speed and accuracy of genome analysis, particularly in large-scale applications such as metagenomics and de novo assembly. Spaced seeds are also used in algorithms like SHRiMP2 and can incorporate multiple seed patterns for greater sensitivity.

trgt:
Jumping Flash! 2: Big Trouble in Little Muu (Japanese: ジャンピングフラッシュ! 2 アロハ男爵大弱りの巻, Hepburn: Janpingu Furasshu! Tsū: Aroha Danshaku Ō Yowari no Maki) is a first-person shooter platform video game developed by Exact and MuuMuu and published by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation in 1996. It is the direct sequel to Jumping Flash!, which was released the previous year. It was later re-released for the PlayStation 3 and PlayStation Portable via the PlayStation Network in 2009.

The game revolves around the robotic rabbit Robbit as he searches for lost MuuMuus, which are scattered throughout different levels in the game by Captain Kabuki. Robbit must explore each expansive new world to recapture all the lost MuuMuus. As with its predecessor, the game is presented in a first-person perspective. Much of the interface was transferred from the first Jumping Flash! to the second in order to save time during development. New features include a much longer draw distance, updated enemy AI and a meter displaying whether or not Robbit can triple-jump.

Similar to its predecessor, Jumping Flash! 2 was well received by critics, who praised the game for its updated interface, advanced graphics and gameplay. However, also like its predecessor, it was criticised for its short length, lack of challenging gameplay and close similarities to the first game. Despite this, Jumping Flash! 2 gained enough popularity to spawn a final sequel, Robbit Mon Dieu.
mgt:
*Jumping Flash! 2* is a first-person platform game developed by Exact and MuuMuu and published by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation. Released in 1996, it is the sequel to *Jumping Flash!* and retains much of its predecessor's gameplay, featuring a first-person perspective, triple-jump mechanics, and projectile-based combat. Players control Robbit, a robotic rabbit, as he rescues the inhabitants of Little Muu from the alien invader Captain Kabuki, who teams up with Robbit's former foe, Baron Aloha. The game introduces new features, including rescuing MuuMuus, a revamped interface, power-ups, and collectible performance medals.

The game was praised for its improved graphics, larger levels, and expanded features, though critics noted its short length and low difficulty. It was lauded for its innovative gameplay and visual design, with *Electronic Gaming Monthly* ranking it among the "100 Best Games of All Time" in 1997. The soundtrack, composed by Takeo Miratsu, was released in Japan alongside the game. *Jumping Flash! 2* was re-released on the PlayStation Network in 2009, maintaining its original gameplay and controls. Despite being considered more of an expansion than a groundbreaking sequel, it remains a notable entry in the early 3D platforming genre.

trgt:
Svalikhita-jivani is an autobiography written in 1896 by Kedarnatha Datta (also known as Bhaktivinoda Thakur), a prominent thinker of Bengali Renaissance and a leading philosopher, savant and spiritual reformer of Gaudiya Vaishnavism who effected its resurgence in India in late 19th and early 20th century and was hailed by contemporary scholars as the most influential Gaudiya Vaisnava leader of his time.

Written in Bengali on the request of Bhaktivinoda's son Lalita Prasad, Svalikhita-jivani gives a detailed autobiographical account that spanned most of his life from his birth in 1838 until retirement in 1894. The book was published by Lalita Prasad in 1916, after Bhaktivinoda's death.
mgt: *Svalikhita-jivani* is the autobiography of Bhaktivinoda Thakur, written in 1896 at the request of his son, Lalita Prasad. Covering the first 56 years of his life, the work candidly recounts Bhaktivinoda's struggles with finances, health, doubts, and introspection, which ultimately led him to embrace the teachings of Caitanya Mahaprabhu. Despite his status as a prominent Gaudiya Vaisnava leader, Bhaktivinoda presented himself with humility, avoiding claims of spiritual powers or saintliness. The autobiography offers an honest and modest portrayal of his life and spiritual journey. It was posthumously published by Lalita Prasad in 1916, following Bhaktivinoda's death.

trgt:
The 1944 explosion in Aarhus or the 4th of July Disaster (Lang-da) was an explosion in the city of Aarhus, Denmark when a barge loaded with ammunition exploded in the harbor, killing 39 people and injuring another 250.

The explosion occurred in conjunction with the occupation of Denmark during the Second World War. Aarhus had become an increasingly important transport hub for German supplies and troops to occupied Norway, by virtue of a large port in the Kattegat and a railway connection to Germany. Supplies arrived by rail from Germany and was loaded from rail cars to barges in the harbor by Danish dock workers, often by hand. Officials from the Aarhus municipal government had prior to 4 July approached German authorities with safety concerns since accidents with ammunition had previously occurred in Norway; specifically the 1943 Filipstad explosion and the 1944 explosion in Bergen. Discussions were underway about the possibility to move transhipment to a less populated area but by July 1944 no decision had yet been made.
mgt:
The 1944 explosion in Aarhus occurred on 4 July 1944 at the Port of Aarhus, Denmark, when a barge carrying 150 tonnes of ammunition detonated. The blast sent a kilometer-high column of water into the air, caused widespread destruction, and was heard up to 20 kilometers away. The explosion killed 39 people, including 33 Danish dock workers, and injured 250 others, 50 seriously. The blast also caused significant damage to nearby buildings, cranes, and storehouses, and scattered debris, including grenades and projectiles, across large sections of the city. A German ship carrying additional ammunition caught fire but was successfully towed away and extinguished.

German authorities attributed the explosion to communist sabotage, a claim contested by Danish labor unions, though the investigation was never reopened. After the war, a memorial service was held at Aarhus Cathedral, and a monument was erected in Vestre Cemetery in December 1945. Donations from across Denmark supported the victims' families, and 12 unidentified victims were buried in a mass grave.

page_title: Swedish Society for Anthropology and Geography
trgt:
The Swedish Society for Anthropology and Geography (SSAG; Lang-sv) is a scientific learned society founded in December 1877. It was established after a rearrangement of various sections of the Anthropological Society, which was formed in 1873 by Hjalmar Stolpe, Hans Hildebrand, Oscar Montelius, and Gustaf Retzius.

The society functions as a link between science and the public, especially in the subjects of anthropology and geography. It awards research fellowships, organizes excursions and lectures, and hands out awards including the Vega Medal and Retzius Medal. In 1880, the society published the first edition of the Swedish yearbook Ymer, and it has published the international journal Geografiska Annaler since 1919, a publication that is divided between physical geography and human geography.

In 2018, it established Kritisk Etnografi, an academic journal of ethnography.
mgt:
The Swedish Society for Anthropology and Geography (SSAG) is a scholarly organization dedicated to the fields of anthropology and geography. Established in 1878, the society is known for its prestigious awards, including the Vega Medal, created in 1881 to honor Adolf Erik Nordenskiöld's discovery of the Northeast Passage. The medal is awarded approximately every three years to an outstanding physical geographer. The society also awards the SSAG Medal, previously named the Anders Retzius Medal, which was renamed in 2015 due to Retzius's controversial racial studies. These awards are presented annually by the King of Sweden on April 24.

The society also publishes *Kritisk Etnografi* (Critical Ethnography), a biannual, peer-reviewed, open-access journal launched in 2018, focusing on anthropology and ethnography in collaboration with Uppsala University's DiVA archive.

trgt:
Louis-Henri Murger, also known as Henri Murger and Henry Murger (27 March 1822 – 28 January 1861), was a French novelist and poet.

He is chiefly distinguished as the author of the 1851 book Scènes de la vie de bohème (Scenes of Bohemian Life), which is based on his own experiences as a desperately poor writer living in a Parisian garret (the top floor of buildings, where artists often lived) and as a member of a loose club of friends who called themselves "the water drinkers" (because they were too poor to afford wine). In his writing he combines instinct with pathos, humour, and sadness. The book is the basis for the 1896 opera La bohème by Puccini, Leoncavallo's opera of the same name, and, at greater removes, the zarzuela Bohemios (Amadeu Vives), the 1930 operetta Das Veilchen vom Montmartre (Kálmán), and the 1996 Broadway musical Rent. He wrote lyrics as well as novels and stories, the chief being La Chanson de Musette, "a tear," says Gautier, "which has become a pearl of poetry".
mgt:
Henri Murger (28 January 1822 – 28 January 1861) was a French novelist and poet, best known for his work *Scènes de la vie de bohème* (Scenes of Bohemian Life), which inspired Giacomo Puccini's opera *La Bohème*. Born in Paris to a Savoyard immigrant family, Murger had a modest upbringing and limited education. He worked various menial jobs before becoming secretary to a Russian nobleman, Count Tolstoi, which allowed him to pursue his literary ambitions. Initially writing poetry and essays, he later turned to fiction under the encouragement of writer Champfleury, achieving success with *Scènes de la vie de bohème* in 1847. 

Despite his literary achievements, Murger struggled with financial difficulties and ill health throughout his life. He received the Légion d'honneur in 1859 but died nearly penniless in a Paris hospital in 1861 at the age of 38. His funeral, funded by the French government, was a significant public event. Murger occasionally altered the spelling of his name early in his career, settling on "Henry Murger" for his published works.

trgt: The Maas–Hoffman model is a mathematical tool to characterize the relation between crop production and soil salinity. It describes the crop response by a broken line of which the first part is horizontal and the second is sloping downward. The breakpoint (Pb) or threshold is also called tolerance because up to that point the yield is unaffected by the salinity, so the salt is tolerated, while at greater salinity values the crops are affected negatively and the yield goes down.
mgt: The Maas–Hoffman model is a mathematical model used to analyze crop tolerance to soil salinity. It represents the relationship between crop yield and salinity through two linear equations, with key parameters including maximum yield, salinity threshold, and the slope of the descending line. The model's parameters are determined using regression analysis to optimize data fit. An inverted version of the model is applied to growth factors, such as watertable depth, that negatively impact crop production at low values.

trgt:
Arenys de Mar (IPA-ca, arenys being Catalan for "sands (of a seasonal creek)", and de mar for "by the sea" as opposed to Arenys de Munt, "sands up hill") is one of the main municipalities of the comarca of Maresme, Barcelona Province, Catalonia, Spain. It is situated on the coast between Caldes d'Estrac and Canet de Mar, about 40 kilometres (25 miles) northeast of Barcelona. As of 2010, it had a population of 14,688.

Arenys' harbour was for many years the only existing port on the north coast of Barcelona. The beaches, framed by hills which run right down to the sea, give a preview of the neighbouring Costa Brava.
mgt: Arenys de Mar is a municipality in the comarca of Maresme, located in the province of Barcelona, Catalonia, Spain. Situated along the Mediterranean coast, it is known for its large beaches, the Baroque parish church of Santa Maria, and the Arenys de Mar Museum, which includes the Marés Museum of Lace-making and the Mollfulleda Museum of Mineralogy. The town's economy is centered on fishing and related industries, with its harbor being the most important in the Maresme region. Arenys de Mar also features a vibrant commercial center, traditional festivals, and educational institutions. It is well-connected by road, rail, and bus to Barcelona and nearby regions.

trgt: Nodes of Ranvier is a metalcore band from Sioux Falls, South Dakota, US, that was signed to Victory Records. The band is described as "one of Sioux Falls' most successful and popular acts ever." They have played with bands such as Bleeding Through, Every Time I Die, Bury Your Dead, Caliban, Comeback Kid, Norma Jean, Misery Signals, Extol and more across the US. The band's name refers to the myelin sheath gaps which are also called Nodes of Ranvier.
mgt: Nodes of Ranvier is an American metalcore and Christian metal band from Sioux Falls, South Dakota, formed in 2000. The band released three albums under Facedown Records before signing with Victory Records in 2007. Despite their initial success, they disbanded later that year, with guitarist Jon Parker as the only remaining original member. In 2017, the band reunited to perform at Facedown Fest, celebrating the label's 20th anniversary. Over its history, the band has featured 18 different members.

trgt:
Rod Diridon Sr. (born February 8, 1939) is an American politician known for his leadership in mass transportation. He served as chair of the California High-Speed Rail Authority from 2001-2003. He currently chairs the advisory board of the United States High Speed Rail Association, and he chairs the National Heritage Area Project for Santa Clara County, California. The New York Times calls Diridon "a tireless advocate of public transport for the Bay Area."

Diridon has also been active in Silicon Valley in leadership on environmental issues, the arts and culture, higher education, community organizations, and philanthropy.
mgt: Rod Diridon Sr. (born February 8, 1939) is an American transportation and environmental leader, retired public official, and non-profit advocate, often referred to as the "father of modern transit service" in Silicon Valley. He served on the Santa Clara County Board of Supervisors for 20 years, chaired numerous regional transportation projects, and played a key role in developing light rail and commuter rail systems in the San Francisco Bay Area. Diridon was the founding executive director of the Mineta Transportation Institute and has been involved in various philanthropic, environmental, and educational initiatives. San Jose's Diridon Station is named in his honor.

trgt: Gorky Central Park of Culture and Leisure (Russian: Центральный парк культуры и отдыха имени Горького, romanized: Tsentralny park kultury i otdykha imeni Gorkogo, IPA: [tsɨnˈtraljnɨj ˈpark kʊljˈturɨ i ˈoddɨxə ˈimɪnɪ ˈɡorkova]) is a central urban park in Rostov-on-Don, Rostov oblast, Russia, named after Maxim Gorky. It is the eldest park in Rostov-on-Don. The park was established in 1813 on the skirts of the city. Now it is located in center of Rostov-on-Don between Pushkinskaya street (Gorky Park North), Bolshaya Sadovaya street (Gorky Park South), Budyonovskiy avenue (Gorky Park West) and Semashko lane (Gorky Park East). Since 1998 the park is protected as an object of cultural heritage of regional value.
mgt:
Gorky Park, also known as Gorodskoy Garden, is a historic urban park located in Rostov-on-Don, Russia. Established in 1813 on the site of a former town dump, the park spans 12.2 hectares (30 acres) and features landscaped gardens, walking paths, fountains, sculptures, and recreational facilities. Initially formed by combining several smaller gardens, the park was developed with contributions from private organizations and underwent significant improvements over the years, including the addition of exotic plants, a stone grotto, and a reservoir with swans.

Notable landmarks include the first Municipal Theatre, a rotunda, and the park's iconic entrance designed by architect Nikolai Doroshenko. Today, it remains a popular cultural and recreational destination.

trgt:
The Yalta International Economic Forum (shortened as YIEF) is an annual business event held in Yalta on the Crimea Peninsula since 2015. It is considered the fourth largest Russian economic forum, after the Petersburg International Economic Forum, the Eastern Economic Forum and the Sochi Investment Forum. The forum is sponsored by the local Government of the Republic of Crimea and the Yalta International Economic Forum Fund, all Russian sources.

The YIEF sees itself as an international venue for discussing socio-economic development issues, for Russia and the world economy as a whole.

According to the organizers, the forum is attended by Russian government officials, international experts, Russian business people and economists, as well as representatives of business organisations and other thinkers. One theme of the forums discussions, is the focus on diversification from Energy and boosting competitiveness of the Russian economy.
mgt: The Yalta International Economic Forum (YIEF) is an annual economic conference held in Yalta, Crimea, established in 2015 by the Yalta International Economic Forum Foundation with support from the Republic of Crimea's government and the Russian Presidential Administration. The forum aims to foster international economic cooperation and dialogue. The inaugural event in 2015 was deemed a success, leading to its establishment as an annual gathering. By 2017, the forum attracted 2,200 participants from 46 countries, and in 2018, over 3,000 participants from more than 60 countries were expected to attend. Topics discussed at the forum have included emerging economic trends, such as cryptocurrencies.

page_title: Tight Connection to My Heart (Has Anybody Seen My Love)
trgt: "Tight Connection to My Heart (Has Anybody Seen My Love)" is a song by Bob Dylan that was released on his 1985 album Empire Burlesque. As a single, it was a Top 40 Hit in New Zealand and Belgium. An earlier version of the song, entitled "Someone's Got a Hold of My Heart", was recorded for Dylan's 1983 LP Infidels, but was not included on that album; it later appeared on The Bootleg Series Volumes 1–3 (Rare & Unreleased) 1961–1991.
mgt: "Tight Connection to My Heart (Has Anybody Seen My Love)" is a song by American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan, released in 1985 as the opening track on his album *Empire Burlesque* and as a single. Originally developed as "Someone's Got a Hold of My Heart" during sessions for his 1983 album *Infidels*, the song underwent significant lyrical changes, incorporating references to classic Hollywood films. The single achieved modest chart success and received critical praise for its engaging melody and production. A music video directed by Paul Schrader was filmed in Tokyo. The song has been covered and featured in various performances, including the musical *Girl from the North Country*.

trgt: Boon Island is a barren, rocky island in the Gulf of Maine 6 mi (9.7 km) off the coast of York, Maine. The island, which is approximately 300 ft (91 m) by 700 ft (210 m) at low tide, is the site of Boon Island Light, at 137 ft (42 m) high, it is the tallest lighthouse in New England. Numerous vessels have been wrecked on its rocky shoreline. John Winthrop, the English Puritan lawyer and one of the leading figures in founding the Massachusetts Bay Colony mentions passing Boon Island in the 1600s.
mgt: Boon Island is a small, rocky islet off the coast of Maine, known for its treacherous conditions, historic shipwrecks, and the Boon Island Light, the tallest lighthouse in New England. The island's name is believed to originate from the survival of castaways from the wreck of the *Increase* in 1682. It gained notoriety for the 1710 wreck of the *Nottingham Galley*, where survivors resorted to cannibalism. The island also saw the 1944 wreck of the *Empire Knight*, which released mercury into nearby waters. The lighthouse, automated in 1978, is now privately owned and maintained.

page_title: 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship Division I
trgt: The 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship Division I was an international inline hockey tournament run by the International Ice Hockey Federation. The Division I tournament ran alongside the 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship tournament and took place between 25 June and 1 July 2017 in Bratislava, Slovakia at the Ondrej Nepela Arena Rink 2 and Ondrej Nepela Arena. The tournament was won by Slovenia who upon winning gained promotion to the 2019 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship. While New Zealand and Brazil were relegated to the Qualifications after losing their placement round games along with Hungary who lost the relegation game against Argentina.
mgt: The 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship Division I was an international inline hockey tournament held in Bratislava, Slovakia, from June 25 to July 1, 2017. Eight teams competed, with Slovenia winning their second Division I title after defeating Latvia in the gold medal game. Australia secured third place by defeating Great Britain. The tournament featured 23 games, with a total of 227 goals scored. Jure Sotlar was the tournament's top scorer. New Zealand, Brazil, and Hungary were relegated following their performances. The event served as a qualifier for the 2019 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship, with Slovenia earning promotion.

Tariq Fancy (born 1978) is a Canadian entrepreneur and former Chief Investment Officer (CIO) of sustainable investing at BlackRock. He is best known as the founder of The Rumie Initiative, a nonprofit organization focused on providing free digital education to underserved communities. Fancy has gained significant attention for his outspoken criticism of environmental, social, and governance (ESG) investing practices in the financial industry. 

In a widely discussed op-ed and subsequent articles, Fancy accused the financial services industry of "greenwashing" by promoting sustainable investing as a public relations strategy rather than a meaningful solution to climate change. He argued that ESG investing lacks evidence of social impact and serves as a distraction from addressing critical environmental issues. Fancy expanded on these claims in a three-part series published on Medium, where he detailed his experiences and interactions within the sustainable investment sector during his tenure at BlackRock. His critiques have sparked debate about the effectiveness and integrity of ESG practices in the financial world.

trgt: George Washington's reception at Trenton was a celebration hosted by the Ladies of Trenton social club on April 21, 1789, in Trenton, New Jersey, as George Washington, then president-elect, journeyed from his home at Mount Vernon to his first inauguration in the then capital of the United States, New York City. A ceremonial triumphal arch was erected on the bridge over the Assunpink Creek to commemorate his two victories here, the Battle of Trenton on December 26, 1776 and the Battle of the Assunpink Creek on January 2, 1777.
mgt: George Washington's reception at Trenton took place on April 21, 1789, during his journey to New York City for his inauguration as the first president of the United States. The event featured a grand celebration, including a triumphal arch commemorating his Revolutionary War victories at Trenton, adorned with laurel and flowers, and a banner reading, "The Defender of the Mothers Will Also Protect Their Daughters." Local women and children, dressed in white, sang a sonata and scattered flowers in his path. The reception has been commemorated through re-enactments, historical markers, and numerous artistic depictions.

trgt: Jemima Anne Morrell (7 March 1832 – 13 October 1909) was an English traveller and illustrator. Morrell was born into a middle class family in Selby, Yorkshire and was a member of the Junior United Alpine Club, a club with majority women members that organised annual holiday trips. Morrell was one of the tourists who in 1863 partook in the first ever guided tour of Switzerland, led and conducted by Thomas Cook, making her one of the first modern international tourists. Her account of the journey was published in 1963 under the title Miss Jemima's Swiss Journal: The First Conducted Tour of Switzerland.
mgt: Jemima Anne Morrell (7 March 1832 – 13 October 1909) was an English traveler, artist, and diarist, best known for her journal documenting the first guided tour of Switzerland led by Thomas Cook in 1863. A member of the Junior United Alpine Club, Morrell's detailed and often humorous account of the three-week journey offers a unique perspective on Victorian travel and the emergence of modern tourism. Her journal, rediscovered during World War II and published in 1963 as *Miss Jemima's Swiss Journal*, has become a significant historical document. Morrell later married John Broadley Greenwood and spent her later years in Yorkshire.

trgt: Messier 83 or M83, also known as the Southern Pinwheel Galaxy and NGC 5236, is a barred spiral galaxy approximately 15 million light-years away in the constellation borders of Hydra and Centaurus. Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille discovered M83 on 23 February 1752 at the Cape of Good Hope. Charles Messier added it to his catalogue of nebulous objects (now known as the Messier Catalogue) in March 1781. It is one of the closest and brightest barred spiral galaxies in the sky, and is visible with binoculars. Its nickname of the Southern Pinwheel derives from its resemblance to the Pinwheel Galaxy (M101).
mgt: Messier 83 (M83), also known as the Southern Pinwheel Galaxy, is a barred spiral galaxy located in the constellation Hydra, approximately 14.7 million light-years from Earth. Classified as SAB(s)c, it features loosely wound spiral arms and a weak central bar. M83 is notable for its high star formation rate, particularly along the leading edges of its spiral arms, and the discovery of new stars in its outer regions, as reported by NASA in 2008. It has hosted six observed supernovae and is a central member of the Centaurus A/M83 Group, a nearby galaxy group.

trgt: Mafdet (also Mefdet, Maftet) was a goddess in the ancient Egyptian religion. She was often depicted wearing a skin of a cheetah, and protected against the bite of snakes and scorpions. She is part of the ancient Egyptian deities during the First Dynasty of Egypt. She was prominent during the reign of pharaoh Den whose image appears on stone vessel fragments from his tomb and is mentioned in a dedicatory entry in the Palermo Stone. Mafdet was the deification of legal justice, or possibly of capital punishment. She was associated with the protection of the king's chambers and other sacred places, and with protection against venomous animals, which were seen as transgressors against Maat. In the Pyramid Texts of the Old Kingdom of Egypt, she is mentioned as protecting the sun god Ra from venomous snakes.
mgt: Mafdet was an ancient Egyptian goddess associated with protection, justice, and execution. She was believed to defend the sun god Ra during his nightly voyage, earning the epithet "Piercer of Darkness," and played a role in reassembling Osiris after his dismemberment. Mafdet was often depicted as a feline or mongoose, a woman with the head of such an animal, or an animal with a woman's head, with the feline commonly interpreted as a cheetah or serval. In art, she was shown running up the side of an executioner's staff, symbolizing her role in delivering justice by ripping out the hearts of wrongdoers and presenting them to the pharaoh. During the New Kingdom, she was associated with the judgment hall in Duat, where she executed the enemies of the pharaoh.

trgt: The medieval University of Dublin (Lang-la) was an early but generally unsuccessful attempt to establish a university in Dublin, the capital city of the Lordship of Ireland. Founded in 1320, it maintained an intermittent existence for the next two centuries, but it never flourished, and disappeared for good at the Reformation in Ireland (1534–41). It was located in Saint Patrick's Cathedral, Dublin. It had no connection with the present University of Dublin, better known as Trinity College Dublin, which was founded in 1592.
mgt: The Medieval University of Dublin was an early university established in 1320 by Alexander de Bicknor, Archbishop of Dublin, under a papal brief granted by Pope Clement V in 1311. Closely associated with St. Patrick's Cathedral, it had faculties of Theology and Law and the authority to confer degrees. Despite its promising foundation, the university struggled due to political instability and a lack of funding, leading to its decline. It ceased to exist entirely during the Irish Reformation in the mid-16th century.

trgt:
Hopatcong State Park is a state park in the Landing section of Roxbury Township, New Jersey. Operated and maintained by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry, the park consists of two parcels of land: one that encompasses Lake Hopatcong and some of its southwestern shore, and another that encompasses Lake Musconetcong about one mile to the west-southwest.

The park contains remnants of Morris Canal, which operated from the 1830s to the 1920s and was largely fed by the lake. The park is also home to the Lake Hopatcong Historical Museum, housed in the former home of the lock tender and his family.
mgt: Hopatcong State Park is a 163-acre public recreation area located in Roxbury Township, New Jersey. Established in 1922 and operated by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry, the park offers a variety of activities, including swimming, fishing, boating, and winter ice fishing. It features playgrounds, sports facilities, picnic areas, and a boat ramp. The park is home to the Lake Hopatcong Historical Museum, housed in a former lock tender's house from the Morris Canal era, which showcases the area's history of transportation and entertainment. The park also displays technology from the Morris Canal, including a historic Scotch Turbine.

trgt: Whiteboard animation is the process of which an author physically draws and records an illustrated story using a whiteboard, or whiteboard-like surface, and marker pens. The animations frequently are aided with narration by script. The authors commonly use time-lapsed drawing and stop motion animation to liven hand-drawn illustrations, with YouTube used as a common platform. It is also used in television and internet advertisements to communicate with consumers in a personal way. The earliest videos made using Whiteboard Animation were published in 2009 on YouTube, used mostly for experimental purposes until developing into a storytelling device, focusing mostly on narratives and educational explanations.
mgt: Whiteboard animation is a style of video presentation that involves creating drawn images on a whiteboard, recorded in sequence to produce an animated effect, often resembling time-lapse or stop-motion. Also known as "video scribing" or "animated doodling," this technique has evolved with software tools, leading to diverse variations in quality and style. Commonly used by businesses and educators, whiteboard animations are employed for training, product demonstrations, and online learning. They have appeared in TV commercials, on platforms like YouTube, and in educational contexts, such as language instruction and academic communication. Notably, the Royal Society of Arts popularized the format with its RSA Animate series.

trgt: Buttlejorrk is a parish of the County of Bourke located to the west of Sunbury, in Victoria, Australia and a neighbourhood within the locality of Diggers Rest. It was named in 1839 by the surveyor William Darke. A township developed and was initially known as Aitken's Gap. By the 1890s it had commonly become known as Buttlejorrk. A Post Office opened on 5 September 1856 as The Gap, was renamed Buttlejork (sic) in 1875 and closed in 1919. One of the early settlers in the area was George Millett. George and his wife Suzanne established the Bald Hill Hotel, the first staging stop out of Melbourne on the road to the goldfields at Bendigo. The hotel was licensed in 1853 until 1907. The Aitken's Gap Gaol, now located in the forecourt of the Sunbury Police Station stood beside the hotel
mgt:
Buttlejorrk is a parish in Victoria, Australia, located within the County of Bourke. Historically significant as a key staging post during the Victorian gold rush, the area around Aitken's Gap was a bustling hub for travelers and gold escorts in the mid-19th century. The settlement featured hotels, stores, and a police station, with the latter playing a vital role in securing gold transport. However, the opening of the Bendigo railway in the late 1850s led to the town's decline.

Notable landmarks include the Aitken's Gap Gaol, built in 1857, which was later restored and relocated to Sunbury in the 1990s as part of heritage preservation efforts. The area also retains remnants of its past, such as an avenue of Monterey Pines and archaeological evidence of early structures, reflecting its historical and cultural significance.

trgt:
The American Federation of Aviculture, Inc. (AFA) is a national organization dedicated to aviculture, whose purpose is to educate the public and assist members regarding best practices for keeping and living with exotic birds. Local affiliate bird clubs throughout the United States, including Puerto Rico, along with national and international specialty organizations; comprise the Federation. The American Federation of Aviculture is registered as a non-profit 501(c)3 educational organization, with a business office located in Austin, Texas.

AFA members work to promote responsible breeding and care of birds. The volunteer organization provides assistance and resources to all people involved with companion birds including breeders, owners, researchers, vets and rescue organizations. AFA members actively support research projects designed to make life better for parrots in their native habitats.
mgt: The American Federation of Aviculture (AFA) is a nonprofit educational organization founded in 1974 to promote the advancement of aviculture, including the care, breeding, and conservation of exotic birds. Headquartered in Austin, Texas, the AFA was established in response to government actions to euthanize exotic parrots during an outbreak of Exotic Newcastle Disease, advocating for bird owners and clubs. The organization supports aviculture through educational programs, publications like the *AFA Watchbird*, and a certification program on avian care and conservation. It also hosts annual conventions featuring expert speakers and provides grants for avian research and conservation projects. Additionally, the AFA monitors legislation affecting bird ownership and breeding, working to protect the interests of bird enthusiasts and professionals worldwide.

page_title: Just a Love Song... Christian Bautista Live!
trgt:
Just a Love Song. Christian Bautista Live! is the first live album by Filipino singer Christian Bautista, released on January 24, 2007 in the Philippines by Warner Music Philippines. The album is composed of nineteen cover versions of songs from the 1970s up to the 1990s. It was recorded overnight at Teatrino, Greenhills, backed by basic rhythm section, a grand piano and a string section, while being watched by an audience composing of specially invited fans. It was released both in the Philippines and Indonesia on CD and DVD formats. To date, the album has been certified platinum by the Philippine Association of the Record Industry.

A "Special Christmas Edition" of the album was released in October 2007, with a new track list. Five additional Christmas songs were included plus the standard edition of the album.
mgt:
*Just a Love Song... Christian Bautista Live!* is a live album by Filipino singer Christian Bautista, released on January 24, 2007, under Warner Music. Recorded in 2006 at Teatrino, Greenhills, the album features pop revival tracks and was produced by Jim Baluyut and Neil C. Gregorio. Initially hesitant to create a revival album, Bautista collaborated with his team to finalize the tracklist, offering a fresh take for his fans. 

Despite being Bautista's weakest-selling album, it achieved platinum status in the Philippines and received a gold record award on *ASAP '07* for selling over 15,000 units. Critics gave the album favorable reviews, with Resty Odon of *Titik Pilipino* praising its romantic appeal while noting some vocal limitations. The album includes singles such as "Got to Believe in Magic" and "Trying to Get the Feeling Again."

trgt: Demetrios John "Mitch" Skandalakis is an American lawyer and former Republican Party politician from Georgia who rose quickly to national prominence in the early-1990s. He upset Martin Luther King III to become chairman of the Fulton County Board of Commissioners and in 1998, was the unsuccessful Republican nominee for lieutenant governor of Georgia. Afterward, he became subjected to a wide-ranging federal corruption investigation of Fulton County government and admitted lying to an FBI agent. He was disbarred, but was later reinstated, as an attorney.
mgt: Mitch Skandalakis is an American attorney, former politician, and businessman. A Republican, he served in the Georgia House of Representatives from 1992 to 1993 and gained national attention after defeating Martin Luther King III in a 1993 special election for chairman of the Fulton County Board of Commissioners. Known for his anti-tax stance and controversial policies, he unsuccessfully ran for lieutenant governor of Georgia in 1998, a campaign marked by negative advertising. In 2003, he pled guilty to lying to the FBI during a corruption investigation and served six months in prison. Disbarred in 2005, he later worked as an executive at Waffle House before being reinstated as an attorney in 2013.

trgt:
The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) is the central bank of South Africa. It was established in 1921 after Parliament passed an act, the "Currency and Bank Act of 10 August 1920", as a direct result of the abnormal monetary and financial conditions which World War I had brought. The SARB was only the fourth central bank established outside the United Kingdom and Europe, the others being the United States, Japan and Java. The earliest suggestions for the establishment of the Central Bank in South Africa date back to 1879. A select committee, of ten members of Parliament, was established on 31 March 1920 to examine the benefits to the national interest of the establishing of the central bank.

Following on the recommendations of the committee, the South African Reserve Bank opened for business on 30 June 1921, making it the oldest central bank in Africa. The first banknotes were issued to the public by the Bank on 19 April 1922. Set of ZAR notes 2012 to present R 104 000 000 000.00 and Set of ZAR notes 2018 to present 400 000 000 print.

Unlike the Bank of England, which provided the model for establishing the SARB, the SARB is privately owned.
mgt:
The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) is the central bank of South Africa, headquartered in Pretoria. Established on June 30, 1921, it is one of the few central banks globally that is privately owned, with 2 million issued shares held by 696 shareholders as of 2018. Shareholders are limited to owning a maximum of 10,000 shares each and receive a capped annual dividend of 10 South African cents per share, with the remaining profits directed to the South African government. The government has announced plans to nationalize the Reserve Bank.

The SARB is governed by a board of directors, including a Governor, currently Lesetja Kganyago, three Deputy Governors, and eleven other directors. The Governor and Deputy Governors are appointed by the President of South Africa in consultation with the Minister of Finance, while other directors are appointed by the President or elected by shareholders.

The SARB oversees the issuance of the South African rand (ZAR) and manages reserves of approximately 38.92 billion USD. It is responsible for maintaining monetary stability and implementing monetary policy in South Africa. The bank's official website is resbank.co.za.

trgt:
"Ghost Town" is a song by American rock band Cheap Trick, released in 1988 as the third single from their tenth studio album Lap of Luxury. It was written by Diane Warren and guitarist Rick Nielsen, and produced by Richie Zito. The song reached number 33 on the Billboard Hot 100.

"Ghost Town" dated back to 1981 when Nielsen recorded a demo of the song during sessions for the band's One on One album. The song remained undeveloped until the sessions for Lap of Luxury when Warren added some contributions to the finished song.
mgt: "Ghost Town" is a song by American rock band Cheap Trick, released in October 1988 as the fourth single from their album *Lap of Luxury*. Written by Diane Warren and Rick Nielsen, and produced by Richie Zito, the song is a rock and power pop ballad. Its music video, directed by Nick Morris, received heavy rotation on MTV. Critics praised the song, with *Billboard* highlighting Robin Zander's vocals and *Cash Box* calling it a "fine bit of writing." *Spin* noted its vocal arrangements and guitar solo, while AllMusic described it as a "grandly romantic power ballad."

trgt:
Nnamdi Moweta (born March 27, 1958), is a radio personality, music producer, music supervisor, music promoter and consultant. He is the host of Radio Afrodicia, a radio show on KPFK, a listener-sponsored radio station based in Los Angeles, California. With an audience of approximately 50,000 listeners weekly, Moweta is the only African-born radio host in Los Angeles, California. In 2011, Moweta was featured on Reuters Africa Journal segment about African DJs in the United States.

Moweta is recognized by UCLA's ethnomusicology program as an expert in the field of African and African-influenced music—he has served as a panelist for UCLA's annual ethnomusicology conferences which attempt to educate the public about global arts and cultures.
mgt: Nnamdi Moweta (born March 27, 1958) is a Nigerian-American radio host, music promoter, producer, and boxing promoter, best known as the host of *Radio Afrodicia*, a long-running program on KPFK that highlights African and African-influenced music. Originally from Jos Plateau, Nigeria, Moweta moved to the United States in 1978 to study filmmaking and later established himself in Los Angeles as a prominent figure in promoting African culture and music. He has managed and produced for notable artists, including Chief Stephen Osita Osadebe, and co-founded Odogwu Entertainment to connect African musicians to global markets. Moweta has also worked as a music supervisor for award-winning films and as a boxing promoter and commentator.

trgt:
Nestor Labastilla Principe, also called "Ka Wadi" (March 15, 1945 - May 7, 1973) was a Filipino activist, writer, and martial arts instructor best known for his student activism at the Lyceum of the Philippines, his community mobilization work among the poor communities of Manila's port ara, and for his martyrdom on May 7, 1973, when he was killed and beheaded by elements of the Philippine Constabulary during Ferdinand Marcos' Martial Law regime.

On November 30, 2018, Principe's name was engraved on the Wall of Remembrance of the Bantayog ng mga Bayani, which honors the martyrs and heroes that fought against the Martial Law regime of Ferdinand Marcos.
mgt: Nestor Principe (March 15, 1945 – May 7, 1973) was a Filipino martial artist, activist, and journalist honored as a martyr for his opposition to the Martial Law regime of Ferdinand Marcos. Born in Tagbilaran, Bohol, he grew up in Caloocan, Metro Manila, where he mastered Sikaran, a Filipino martial art, and introduced it to Malaysia as a bodyguard and instructor. Principe later traveled globally before returning to the Philippines, inspired by the First Quarter Storm, to study journalism and engage in activism. He was killed by the Philippine Constabulary in 1973, and his name was posthumously inscribed on the Bantayog ng mga Bayani Wall of Remembrance in 2018.

trgt:
Margaret JoBeth Williams (born December 6, 1948) is an American actress and television director. Her directorial debut with the 1994 short film On Hope earned her an Academy Award nomination for Best Live Action Short Film. In 2009 she began serving as president of the Screen Actors Guild Foundation; she is President Emeritus of the foundation.

Williams rose to prominence appearing in such films as Stir Crazy (1980), Poltergeist (1982), The Big Chill (1983), The Day After (1983), Teachers (1984), and Poltergeist II: The Other Side (1986). A three-time Emmy Award nominee, she was nominated for Outstanding Lead Actress in a Miniseries or a Movie for her work in the TV movie Adam (1983) and the TV miniseries Baby M (1988). Her third nomination was for her guest role in the sitcom Frasier (1994). She also starred in the TV series The Client (1995–96) and had recurring roles in the TV series Dexter (2007) and Private Practice (2009–11).
mgt:
JoBeth Williams (born Margaret JoBeth Williams; December 6, 1948) is an American actress and television director known for her work in film, television, and theater. She gained prominence for her roles in films such as *Poltergeist* (1982) and its sequel, *Poltergeist II: The Other Side* (1986), *The Big Chill* (1983), and *American Dreamer* (1984), for which she won the Kansas City Film Critics Circle Award for Best Actress. Williams also earned an Academy Award nomination for her directorial debut, the 1994 short film *On Hope*. 

On television, she received critical acclaim and Emmy nominations for her performances in *Adam* (1983) and *Baby M* (1988). She has appeared in numerous TV series, including *Frasier*, *Private Practice*, and *Dexter*. Williams is married to director John Pasquin, with whom she has two sons.
