In 2019 the National POW/MIA Flag Act was signed into law, requiring the POW/MIA flag to be flown on certain federal properties, including the U.S. Capitol Building, on all days the U.S. flag is flown.
mgt: The POW/MIA flag is a symbol of the United States' commitment to resolving the fates of American prisoners of war (POWs) and those missing in action (MIA). Designed in 1971 by Newt Heisley for the National League of Families, the flag features a silhouette of a POW, a guard tower, and barbed wire, with the words "POW/MIA" and "You Are Not Forgotten." Officially recognized by Congress in 1990, it is flown on federal properties and military sites to honor and remember POW/MIAs from all U.S. conflicts.

trgt:
Dutchman is a play written by African-American playwright Amiri Baraka, then known as LeRoi Jones. Dutchman was first presented at the Cherry Lane Theatre in Greenwich Village, New York City, in March 1964. The play won an Obie Award; it shared this distinction with Adrienne Kennedy's Funnyhouse of a Negro. Baraka's stage play was made into a film in 1967, starring Shirley Knight and Al Freeman Jr. Dutchman was the last play produced by Baraka under his birth name, LeRoi Jones. At the time, he was in the process of divorcing his Jewish wife, Hettie Jones, and embracing Black Nationalism. Dutchman may be described as a political allegory depicting black and white relations during the time Baraka wrote it.

The play was revived in 2007 at the Cherry Lane Theatre starring Dulé Hill, and in 2013 was restaged by Rashid Johnson at the Russian and Turkish Baths in the East Village.
mgt:
*Dutchman* is a one-act play written by Amiri Baraka, first performed in March 1964 at the Cherry Lane Theatre in New York City. The play explores themes of race, identity, and power dynamics through an intense and symbolic interaction between two characters: Lula, a white woman, and Clay, a young Black man, aboard a New York City subway. Their flirtatious conversation escalates into a confrontation, with Lula manipulating and mocking Clay before ultimately murdering him. The play ends with Lula targeting another Black man, suggesting a cyclical pattern of violence and oppression.

The title *Dutchman* alludes to the transatlantic slave trade and the myth of the Flying Dutchman, symbolizing the inescapable cycles of racial history. The subway car serves as a metaphor for this unending journey. The play is a powerful critique of racism and assimilation during the Civil Rights Movement, blending realism with allegory.

trgt: Marguerite is a musical with a book by Alain Boublil, Claude-Michel Schönberg and Jonathan Kent, lyrics by Alain Boublil and Herbert Kretzmer, and music by Michel Legrand, with original French lyrics by Boublil. Based on the romantic novel La Dame aux Camélias by Alexandre Dumas, fils, the musical updates the story to 1940s German-occupied Paris. It tells the tale of a 40-year-old ex-singer and her affair with a young musician who is mixed up with the French Resistance, whilst she is the mistress of a Nazi officer.
mgt: *Marguerite* is a musical with music by Michel Legrand, lyrics by Alain Boublil and Herbert Kretzmer, and a book by Boublil, Claude-Michel Schönberg, and Jonathan Kent. Inspired by Alexandre Dumas' *La Dame aux Camélias*, it is set in Nazi-occupied Paris and follows Marguerite, a socialite entangled in a love affair with a young musician while being the mistress of a German officer. The musical premiered in London's West End in 2008 and has since undergone significant revisions, with productions in Japan, the Czech Republic, and a London revival in 2012.

trgt:
Zeitenspiegel Reportagen is a co-operative of writers and photographers based in Weinstadt, near Stuttgart, Germany. The agency works for all the major German magazines such as Stern, Focus, GEO, and Die Zeit, as well as for international newspapers and magazines.

The agency’s signature style is reportage.

There is that split second in which a gesture, a disarming look, the play of colours in an iris coalesce into a moment that creates a full-length story.

In 2008, Zeitenspiegel members received awards, including the Ernst Schneider Award of the German Chamber of Industry and Commerce and a first place in the European Parliament Prize for Journalism. In addition to classic photo reportages, the Zeitenspiegel group also takes on short assignments. Zeitenspiegel Reportagen is active in the field of advertising (Daimler, Pfizer) and corporate publishing through their subsidiary agentur.zs.
mgt:
Zeitenspiegel is a German journalism agency founded in 1976 by Uli Reinhardt and headquartered in Weinstadt, Germany. Specializing in print and news publishing, the agency reported a revenue of €1.7 million in 2008. Zeitenspiegel operates the Zeitenspiegel-Reportageschule Günter Dahl, a journalism academy that offers one-year courses to train aspiring feature journalists. The academy, taught by Zeitenspiegel members and renowned professionals, has been recognized as one of the top journalism schools in Germany.

Since 1998, Zeitenspiegel has awarded the Hansel Mieth Prize, honoring its honorary member Johanna "Hansel" Mieth, for outstanding reporting on socially relevant topics. The prize is given to a reporter-photographer team, while the Gabriel Grüner Grant supports emerging journalists in researching promising stories. Both awards reflect Zeitenspiegel's commitment to high-quality, socially conscious journalism.

trgt:
Cumberlandia monodonta (common name spectaclecase) is a freshwater mussel endemic to the United States. Currently, C. monodonta is listed as an endangered species by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the International Union for Conservation of Nature.

Cumberlandia monodonta was first reported in a newspaper titled The Disseminator (New Harmony, Ind.) by Thomas Say in 1829 under the name Unio monodonta. The spectaclecase was then reclassified into the genus Cumberlandia by A. E. Ortmann in 1912.

Cumberlandia monodonta has experienced a drastic decline in population due to human activity, resulting in its listing as "Endangered" by the Endangered Species Act and the IUCN. In particular, the construction of dams has caused significant problems that disrupt C. monodonta's habitat and food supply. The Endangered Species Act classifies C. monodonta as a highly threatened species with a low potential for recovery.
mgt:
The spectacle case pearly mussel (*Cumberlandia monodonta*) is a freshwater bivalve mollusk in the family Margaritiferidae, endemic to the Mississippi, Ohio, and Missouri River basins. Known for its oblong shell, which can grow over 9 inches, the species is endangered due to habitat loss, pollution, and other environmental stressors. Its populations have declined by 55% and are now highly fragmented, with only 20 streams currently supporting the species. 

The mussel has a complex life cycle, including a parasitic larval stage that relies on specific fish hosts. Conservation efforts include habitat restoration, genetic research, and laboratory propagation, with some success in in-vitro growth. Despite these efforts, recovery potential remains low, and the species faces ongoing threats from climate change, invasive species, and inadequate regulatory protections. It was officially listed as endangered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in 2012.

trgt: Wild Jack is a 1974 young adult science fiction novel written by the English prize-winning author John Christopher. Set in twenty-third century England, the plot concerns the young aristocrat Clive Anderson, who lives in decadent isolation within one of many walled-in hierarchal city-states, whose walls keep out the dangerous wilderness of the Outlands, and the barbaric savages known to hide there. When Clive is falsely accused of questioning the status quo, he is sent off to a brutal prison island. Faced with hunger and cruelty, he begins to truly question the good of his world.
mgt: *Wild Jack* is a 1974 science fiction novel by British author John Christopher. Set in a post-apocalyptic 23rd century, the story follows Clive Anderson, a privileged boy from the walled city-state of London, as he is exiled to a harsh prison island for questioning societal norms. Escaping with two fellow inmates, Clive ventures into the Outlands, a wilderness inhabited by so-called "savages," where he encounters the titular Wild Jack, a Robin Hood-like figure leading a band of outlaws. Through his journey, Clive grapples with themes of freedom, privilege, and societal inequality, ultimately questioning the values of his former life.

trgt: True Liberty is an American Christian hardcore band, and they primarily play hardcore punk and punk rock. They come from Richmond, Virginia. The band started making music in 2005, and their members are lead vocalist and lead guitarist, Mike Liberty, bassist, Tom Liberty, and drummer and background vocalist, Aaron Liberty. Their first studio album, This Is War, was released in 2007 by Raging Storm Records. They independently released, Brotherhood, that came out in 2008. The studio album, City of the One, was released by Wounded Records, in 2010. Their subsequent studio album, Marked for Life, was released by Thumper Punk Records, in 2012. They released a live album, Give Me True Liberty or Give Me Death, with Thumper Punk Records, in 2014.
mgt: True Liberty is a Christian hardcore band from Richmond, Virginia, formed in 2005. The band consists of lead vocalist and guitarist Mike Liberty, bassist Tom Liberty, and drummer and background vocalist Aaron Liberty. Known for their blend of Christian hardcore, hardcore punk, and punk rock, True Liberty has released several albums through various labels, including Raging Storm Records, Wounded Records, and Thumper Punk Records. Their discography includes *This Is War* (2007), *Brotherhood* (2008), *City of the One* (2010), and *Marked for Life* (2012). They also released a live album, *Give Me True Liberty or Give Me Death*, in 2014. The band remains active, continuing to contribute to the Christian punk and hardcore music scene.

trgt:
Volinia Township is a civil township of Cass County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 1,112 at the 2010 census. When the township was organized in 1833, it was at first named Volhynia, after the province in Poland. It is believed to have been given to honor General Tadeusz Kościuszko, the Polish patriot who helped the United States during the American Revolutionary War. The name was altered first to "Volenia" and then by 1901 or earlier to "Volinia".

The township is the site of Newton Woods, a U.S. National Natural Landmark.
mgt: Volinia Township is a civil township located in Cass County, Michigan, United States. Covering a total area of 35.1 square miles (91.0 km²), the township consists predominantly of land, with 2.07% of its area being water. As of the 2010 census, the population was 1,112, with a population density of 32 people per square mile. The township has no incorporated municipalities, and its postal services are provided by nearby communities. The area is characterized by a predominantly White population, with a median household income of $38,900 and a family-oriented demographic structure.

trgt:
Titus Lucretius Carus (/ˈtaɪtəs luːˈkriːʃəs/ TY-təs loo-KREE-shəs, IPA-la; c. 99 – c. 55 BC) was a Roman poet and philosopher. His only known work is the philosophical poem De rerum natura, a didactic work about the tenets and philosophy of Epicureanism, and which usually is translated into English as On the Nature of Things and somewhat less often as On the nature of the universe. Lucretius has been credited with originating the concept of the three-age system that was formalised in 1836 by C. J. Thomsen.

Very little is known about Lucretius's life; the only certainty is that he was either a friend or client of Gaius Memmius, to whom the poem was addressed and dedicated.

De rerum natura was a considerable influence on the Augustan poets, particularly Virgil (in his Aeneid and Georgics, and to a lesser extent on the Eclogues) and Horace. The work was almost lost during the Middle Ages, but was rediscovered in 1417 in a monastery in Germany by Poggio Bracciolini and it played an important role both in the development of atomism (Lucretius was an important influence on Pierre Gassendi) and the efforts of various figures of the Enlightenment era to construct a new Christian humanism. Lucretius's scientific poem On the Nature of Things (c. 60 BC) has a remarkable description of Brownian motion of dust particles in verses 113–140 from Book II. He uses this as a proof of the existence of atoms.
mgt:
Titus Lucretius Carus (c. 99 BC – c. 55 BC) was a Roman poet and philosopher, best known for his didactic epic *De rerum natura* (*On the Nature of Things*), which introduced Epicurean philosophy to Roman audiences. Written in six books of poetic hexameters, the work explores atomism, the nature of the mind and soul, the development of the universe, and the evolution of human society, rejecting divine intervention in favor of naturalistic explanations. Lucretius also theorized about the progression of human technology, proposing a sequence of tools and materials—stone, copper, bronze, and iron—that prefigured the modern three-age system.

Little is known about Lucretius's life. Ancient sources suggest he was born in the 90s BC and died in the 50s BC, possibly by suicide, though such claims are often dismissed as speculative or biased. His work was praised by contemporaries like Cicero and Virgil for its intellectual depth and poetic brilliance.

Lucretius's natural philosophy anticipated elements of evolutionary theory, emphasizing nature's experimentation and the survival of well-adapted organisms. However, he rejected the idea of species evolving from one another and denied the spherical Earth theory. Despite his influence waning in antiquity, *De rerum natura* was rediscovered in the Renaissance, profoundly shaping modern scientific and philosophical thought.

page_title: University of St. Thomas School of Law
trgt: The University of St. Thomas School of Law is one of the professional graduate schools of University of St. Thomas in Minnesota, United States. It is one of three law schools in Minneapolis–Saint Paul. It currently enrolls 434 students. St. Thomas Law is accredited by the American Bar Association. It is also a member of The Association of American Law Schools. The University of St. Thomas School of Law is the second highest ranked law school in Minnesota amongst the state's three law schools. Founded in 1999, the School of Law graduated its first class in 2004.
mgt: The University of St. Thomas School of Law, located in Minneapolis, Minnesota, is a private law school established in 1999, with its first graduating class in 2004. Accredited by the American Bar Association in 2006 and a member of the Association of American Law Schools since 2012, the school emphasizes practical training, social justice, and mentorship. Its Mentor Externship Program, which pairs students with legal professionals, has received national recognition. The school offers various degree programs, including J.D., combined degrees, and LL.M. programs. It is also home to notable centers and publications focused on ethics, law, and public policy.

trgt:
Audubon Aquarium of the Americas is an aquarium in New Orleans, Louisiana, United States.

It is run by the Audubon Nature Institute, which also supervises Audubon Zoo, Audubon Louisiana Nature Center, Freeport-McMoRan Audubon Species Survival Center, Audubon Center for Research of Endangered Species (ACRES), Coastal Wildlife Network, Audubon Wilderness Park, and Audubon Park. It is located along the banks of the Mississippi River by the edge of the historic French Quarter off Canal Street, at the upper end of Woldenberg Park. It opened on September 1, 1990.
mgt: The Audubon Aquarium of the Americas, located in New Orleans, Louisiana, is a prominent aquarium specializing in aquatic life from North and South America. Opened on September 1, 1990, it houses 10,000 animals representing 530 species and features exhibits such as the Gulf of Mexico tank and Caribbean displays. The aquarium suffered significant losses during Hurricane Katrina in 2005 but reopened on May 26, 2006, with revamped exhibits. It is a member of the Association of Zoos and Aquariums (AZA) and is operated by the Audubon Nature Institute.

trgt:
Waist beads are a piece of jewelry worn around the waist or hip area. Originating in Africa, waist beads are traditionally worn by women as a symbol of waist size, beauty, sexuality, femininity, fertility, well-being, and maturity.

Commonly made of glass, metal, crystals, gemstones, charms, wooden beads, or plastic beads, waist beads are typically strung on a cotton thread, twine, thread, wire, clear cord, or elastic cord. The colors and materials used may have symbolic, cultural, or personal significance to the wearer.
mgt: Waist beads are traditional accessories originating in ancient Egypt, where they were known as girdles. Worn around the waist, they are now prevalent across various African cultures, including Ghanaian, Yoruba, Igbo, and Hausa communities, each attributing unique cultural and symbolic meanings to them. Made from materials like glass, shells, stones, and metals, waist beads symbolize femininity, maturity, fertility, and spirituality. They are also used for adornment, posture, and weight tracking. Today, waist beads serve both cultural and personal purposes, often reflecting heritage and individual beliefs.

trgt:
Daniel Farrell Reeves (June 30, 1912 – April 15, 1971) was an American sports entrepreneur, best known as the owner of the National Football League's Rams franchise from 1941 to his death in 1971.

Reeves is remembered for his move of the Rams from Cleveland to Los Angeles in 1946, where it became the first American major league sports franchise on the Pacific Coast. He was also the first NFL owner to sign an African-American player in the post World War II era, inking deals with halfback Kenny Washington and end Woody Strode in 1946, as well as being the first to employ a full-time scouting staff.

Reeves was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 1967.
mgt:
Daniel Farrell Reeves (June 30, 1912 – April 15, 1971) was an American businessman and sports executive, best known as the owner of the Cleveland/Los Angeles Rams of the National Football League (NFL) from 1941 until his death in 1971. Under his ownership, the Rams won two NFL championships (1945 and 1951) and became one of the league's most successful franchises. Reeves was instrumental in relocating the team from Cleveland to Los Angeles in 1946, making the Rams the first NFL team on the West Coast. 

A pioneer in professional football, Reeves introduced innovations such as full-time scouting staffs, early television broadcasting strategies, and the integration of the NFL by signing Kenny Washington in 1946. He was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 1967. Reeves also owned the Los Angeles Blades hockey team and contributed to sports in Los Angeles. He died of cancer in 1971.

trgt:
Purcari, part of the Purcari Wineries LLC (pronunciation: [purˈkarj]), is a popular wine brand in Central and Eastern Europe. The Winery is located in Purcari Wine Region, Ștefan Vodă District. Through the years, Purcari Winery has won over 250 medals at international contests like Decanter World Wine Awards, International Wine & Spirits Competition, International Wine Challenge and many other competitions.

As an exporter, Purcari Winery has entered in over 30 markets in Europe, North America, and East Asia, in countries like the US, Canada, Great Britain, Norway, Czech Republic, China, Poland, Germany, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Ukraine, Romania, Turkey, Japan, Luxembourg, and in the Baltic Countries.

The Government of Republic of Moldova awarded Purcari Winery with the Grand Prix for achievements in wine making.
mgt:
Purcari is a historic winery located in the Ștefan Vodă district of Moldova, renowned for its premium wines and rich heritage. Established in 1827 by decree of Emperor Nicholas I of Russia, it became the first specialized winery in Bessarabia. Purcari gained international acclaim in 1878 when its Negru de Purcari wine won a gold medal at the Paris World Expo, impressing French experts who mistook it for Bordeaux. The winery's wines were once served to European royalty, including Emperor Nicholas II and Queen Victoria.

After a period of decline during the Soviet era, Purcari was revitalized in 2003, restoring its historic cellars and production techniques. Today, it produces a wide range of wines, including its iconic vintage collections, and exports to over 25 countries.

trgt:
In Hindustani music (North Indian classical music), a gharānā is a system of social organisation in the Indian subcontinent, linking musicians or dancers by lineage or apprenticeship, and more importantly by adherence to a particular musical style.

The word gharana comes from the Hindi word 'ghar' which is derived from the Sanskrit word Griha, which means 'house'. It typically refers to the place where the musical ideology originated; for example, some of the gharanas well known for singing khyals are: Dilli(Delhi), Agra, Gwalior, Indore, Atrauli-Jaipur, Kirana and Patiala. Four famous kathak gharanas are: Lucknow, Atrauli-Jaipur, Benares and Raigarh.
mgt: A *gharana* is a system of lineage and stylistic tradition in Indian classical music and dance, rooted in the guru–shishya (teacher–student) tradition. It encompasses various forms, including khyal, dhrupad, thumri, tabla, and Kathak dance, each with distinct regional styles and techniques. The khyal gharanas, influenced by the decline of the Mughal Empire, developed in princely states like Gwalior, Lucknow, and Patiala, emphasizing unique approaches to improvisation and rhythm. Similarly, thumri and Kathak gharanas evolved with distinct characteristics, such as the Benares, Lucknow, and Jaipur-Atrauli styles, reflecting the rich diversity and adaptability of Indian classical traditions.

trgt:
An institute is an organisational body created for a certain purpose. They are often research organisations (research institutes) created to do research on specific topics, or can also be a professional body.

In some countries, institutes can be part of a university or other institutions of higher education, either as a group of departments or an autonomous educational institution without a traditional university status such as a "university institute" (see Institute of Technology). In some countries, such as South Korea and India, private schools are sometimes referred to as institutes, and in Spain, secondary schools are referred to as institutes.

Historically, in some countries institutes were educational units imparting vocational training and often incorporating libraries, also known as mechanics' institutes.

The word "institute" comes from a Latin word institutum meaning "facility" or "habit"; from instituere meaning "build", "create", "raise" or "educate".
mgt: In the United Kingdom and the Isle of Man, the term "institute" is a protected designation, reserved for organizations conducting research at the highest level or professional bodies of the highest standing. The use of "institute" in a company or business name requires approval from the Secretary of State, in accordance with regulations such as the Business Names Act. Unauthorized use of the term without approval constitutes a criminal offence.

trgt: Hugh Anthony Allison (born 1986), better known by his stage name Mr. Muthafuckin'eXquire (often censored as Mr. MFN eXquire or simply eXquire), is an American rapper. Born and raised in Brooklyn, he debuted with the release of his first mixtape, The Big Fat Kill, in 2008. Allison would follow-up with what is considered to be his breakout project, the mixtape Lost in Translation, released by Mishka NYC in 2011. He signed his first major-label record deal with Universal Republic Records, in 2012, which he subsequently left. In 2017, with the release of Allison's fourth EP Brainiac, he launched Chocolate Rabbit, his own record label imprint. His self-titled debut studio album was independently released in 2019.
mgt: Mr. Muthafuckin' eXquire, born Hugh Anthony Allison in 1986, is an American rapper from Brooklyn, New York. Emerging in 2008 with his debut mixtape *The Big Fat Kill*, he gained widespread recognition in 2011 with the release of his song "Huzzah" and its remix, "The Last Huzzah," which featured prominent rappers and was named one of the best songs of the year by *Rolling Stone*. Known for his eclectic style and bold lyricism, eXquire released several acclaimed mixtapes and EPs, including *Lost in Translation* and *Power & Passion*. After a brief stint with Universal Republic Records, he returned to independent releases, culminating in his self-titled debut studio album in 2019.

trgt: Yvette Paula Fielding (born 23 September 1968) is an English television presenter, producer and actress. She became the youngest presenter on Blue Peter aged 18, and one of her episodes was subsequently voted the "Favourite Blue Peter moment" ever. With her husband Karl Beattie, she presented the Most Haunted series on the Living channel, via their own production company, followed by Ghosthunting With., establishing Fielding as 'first lady of the paranormal'. She has appeared in a wide range of other programmes, from The Wright Stuff to Through the Keyhole and I'm A Celebrity. Get Me Out of Here!.
mgt: Yvette Paula Fielding (born 23 September 1968) is an English television presenter, producer, actress, and author, best known as the youngest-ever presenter of *Blue Peter* and as the host of the paranormal investigation series *Most Haunted*. Beginning her career in 1983 with the BBC series *Seaview*, she gained prominence on *Blue Peter* from 1987 to 1992, earning widespread recognition. Fielding later transitioned to adult television, hosting various shows before co-founding Antix Productions and creating *Most Haunted* in 2002. She has also authored books, including *The Ghost Hunter Chronicles* series, and lives with her family in Cheshire.

trgt: South Burnie is a locality and suburb of Burnie in the local government area of City of Burnie, in the North West region of Tasmania. It is located about 2.5 kilometres (1.6 mi) south-east of the town of Burnie. The Bass Highway passes from south-east to north-west along the shore of Bass Strait, which forms part of the northern boundary. The Emu River forms part of the eastern boundary. The 2016 census determined a population of 331 for the state suburb of South Burnie.
mgt: South Burnie is a suburb of Burnie, located in North West Tasmania, Australia, approximately 2.5 km southeast of the Burnie city center. As of the 2016 census, it had a population of 331. Part of the City of Burnie local government area, South Burnie shares its history with Burnie, which was named after William Burnie, a director of the Van Diemen's Land Company. The suburb is connected by the C112 route (Massy-Greene Drive), which links to the Bass Highway and provides access to surrounding areas.

trgt: Seventeen: A Tale of Youth and Summer Time and the Baxter Family Especially William is a humorous novel by Booth Tarkington that gently satirizes first love, in the person of a callow 17-year-old, William Sylvanus Baxter. Seventeen takes place in a small city in the Midwestern United States shortly before World War I. It was published as sketches in the Metropolitan Magazine in 1915 and 1916, and collected in a single volume by Harper and Brothers in 1916, when it was the bestselling novel in the United States.
mgt: *Seventeen* is a 1916 novel by American author Booth Tarkington. Set in a middle-class neighborhood, the story follows 17-year-old William Baxter as he becomes infatuated with Lola Pratt, an out-of-town visitor known for her flirtatious demeanor and baby talk. William's comedic attempts to win her affection, including wearing his father's dress suit and writing love poems, lead to humorous conflicts with his family and friends. Praised for its humor and insight into adolescence, *Seventeen* was described by *The New York Times* as a "delicious lampoon" and remains a notable work of its era.

trgt:
King Arthur's Disasters is a British animated series which first aired on CITV. Co-created by Paul Parkes and Will Ashurst, the series follows and depicts attempts by King Arthur, assisted by the wizard Merlin, to woo the beautiful self-obsessed Princess Guinevere. Due to the popularity of the show, it was picked up for a second series which began transmission on CITV from 6 November 2005. Both were executive-produced by Genevieve Dexter at cake entertainment

King Arthur's Disasters was the highest-rated new CITV show during spring 2005. It regularly achieved an audience share of over 20% of children and it regularly won its time slot against CBBC. In 2006 the show was nominated for a children's BAFTA for Best Animation; however, it lost to The Amazing Adrenalini Brothers. It airs on Pop in the UK.
mgt:
*King Arthur's Disasters* is a British animated television series that aired on CITV from April 11, 2005, to February 1, 2006. The show, starring Rik Mayall, Matt Lucas, Morwenna Banks, and Phil Cornwell, ran for two seasons with a total of 26 episodes. It was executive produced by Genevieve Dexter and featured 30-minute episodes.

The series follows King Arthur's comedic and perilous quests to win the affection of Princess Guinevere, who demands various objects or creatures in exchange for her hand in marriage. Accompanied by Merlin and occasionally others, Arthur faces numerous challenges, only for his efforts to end in failure or Guinevere's dissatisfaction. The show also features rebellious knights Sir Lancelot and Sir Martyn, who oppose Arthur. The characters are inspired by modern interpretations of Arthurian legends, blending medieval themes with contemporary humor.

trgt:
Grimethorpe Hall is a manor house in Grimethorpe, South Yorkshire, England. Built circa 1670 for Robert Seaton (also spelled Seton), it is thought to be in the style of York architect Robert Trollope. Around 1800 the hall passed to John Farrar Crookes of Tunbridge Wells. It was last used as a house in the 1960s and afterwards was purchased by the National Coal Board. The National Coal Board applied to demolish it in 1981 but, after a campaign by the Ancient Monuments Society, this was unsuccessful. The structure received statutory protection as a grade II* listed building in 1985.

Proposals by later owners to convert the hall into a nursing home, restaurant or social housing came to naught and the hall fell into the ownership of the Crown Estate in 2016 after the company owning it was dissolved. Concerns have since been raised over the structure and a local campaign has been launched in an attempt to restore it.
mgt:
Grimethorpe Hall is a Grade II* listed building in Grimethorpe, South Yorkshire, England, built around 1670 for Robert Seaton. The hall, likely inspired by the designs of York architect Robert Trollope, features a square plan with red brick and sandstone elevations, a Welsh slate roof, and Georgian-era modifications, including sash windows and a straight pediment. The interior includes Doric columns and decorative wooden doors.

The hall underwent alterations in the 18th, 19th, and 20th centuries but has been unoccupied since the 1960s. It was purchased by the National Coal Board, which unsuccessfully sought to demolish it in 1981. Since then, the hall has faced neglect, legal complications, and failed redevelopment plans, leaving it in a state of disrepair. Historic England and local residents have raised concerns about its condition, and campaigns to save the building are ongoing. The hall has been considered as a potential headquarters for the Grimethorpe Colliery Band.

trgt:
In evolutionary genetics, mutational meltdown is a sub class of extinction vortex in which the environment and genetic predisposition mutually reinforce each other. Mutational meltdown (not to be confused with the concept of an error catastrophe) is the accumulation of harmful mutations in a small population, which leads to loss of fitness and decline of the population size, which may lead to further accumulation of deleterious mutations due to fixation by genetic drift.

A population experiencing mutational meltdown is trapped in a downward spiral and will go extinct if the phenomenon lasts for some time. Usually, the deleterious mutations would simply be selected away, but during a mutational meltdown, the number of individuals thus suffering an early death is too large relative to the overall population size so that mortality exceeds the birth rate.
mgt:
Mutational meltdown is a process in population genetics where the accumulation of deleterious mutations in a small population leads to a decline in population size and growth rate, ultimately resulting in extinction. This occurs as harmful mutations become fixed in the population, reducing fitness and increasing mortality rates. The process is particularly rapid in small populations due to genetic drift and the shorter time required for mutations to become fixed.

The effects of mutational meltdown differ between asexual and sexual populations. Asexual populations are more vulnerable due to the lack of genetic recombination, which allows mutations to accumulate more quickly. In contrast, sexual populations benefit from genetic diversity and recombination, which can slow or prevent mutational meltdown. However, under certain conditions, such as low birth rates or population bottlenecks, even large sexually reproducing populations can experience mutational meltdown.

page_title: Athletics at the 2004 Summer Olympics – Men's hammer throw
trgt: The men's hammer throw competition at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens was held at the Olympic Stadium on 20–22 August. There were 35 competitors from 24 nations. The event was marred by doping-related disqualifications, with the original first- and third-place athletes being removed. After those disqualifications, the event was won by Koji Murofushi of Japan, the nation's first medal in the event. No silver medal was awarded. Eşref Apak of Turkey received bronze, Turkey's first medal in the event as well.
mgt: The men's hammer throw event at the 2004 Summer Olympics took place from August 20 to 22 at the Olympic Stadium in Athens, with 35 athletes from 24 nations competing. Koji Murofushi of Japan was ultimately awarded the gold medal after the original winner, Adrián Annus of Hungary, was disqualified for tampering with a doping sample. Ivan Tsikhan of Belarus, initially the silver medalist, was later disqualified in 2012 for doping, leaving Eşref Apak of Turkey with the bronze medal. No new records were set during the competition.

trgt: Brazil is a predominantly Christian country with Islam being a minority religion, first brought by African slaves and then by Lebanese and Syrian immigrants. Due to secular nature of the Brazil's constitution, Muslims are free to proselytize and build places of worship in the country. However, Islam isn't independently included in charts and graphics representing religions in Brazil due to its very small size, being grouped in "other religions", which generally represent about 1% of the country's population. The number of Muslims in Brazil, according to the 2010 census, was 35,207 out of a population of approximately 191 million people. This correspond to 0.018% of the Brazilian population.
mgt: Islam in Brazil has a history dating back to the transatlantic slave trade, when African Muslims were brought to the country as slaves. Brazil received more enslaved Muslims than any other region in the Americas, and their influence culminated in the Malê Revolt of 1835, the largest urban slave uprising in the Americas. Following this period, Islam in Brazil was shaped by immigration from the Middle East and Southeast Asia, particularly in the 20th century. Today, Brazil's Muslim population, primarily Sunni, is concentrated in São Paulo and Paraná, with over 150 mosques nationwide. Recent decades have seen a rise in conversions to Islam and growing cultural visibility.

page_title: Saint Vincent Martyr School (Madison, New Jersey)
trgt:
Saint Vincent Martyr School (SVMS), the oldest Catholic elementary school (established in 1848) in New Jersey, is the coeducational parish school for Saint Vincent Martyr parish in Madison borough, Morris County, New Jersey, United States. SVMS educates approximately 400 students in grades PK-3 through Eighth Grade. The school is named for Vincent of Saragossa. The school nickname is the Eagles.

The school and parish are in the Southeastern Morris Deanery of the Diocese of Paterson of the Province of Newark of the Latin Rite of the Catholic Church.

SVMS was one of three elementary schools in New Jersey, and the only non-public school, recognized by the national Blue Ribbon Schools Program, awarded by the United States Department of Education, for the 2005-06 school year. It is fully accredited by the Middle States Association of Colleges and Schools.
mgt:
Saint Vincent Martyr School (SVMS) is a Roman Catholic elementary school located in Madison, New Jersey, serving students from pre-kindergarten through eighth grade. Established in 1848, it is one of the oldest schools in the area and has an enrollment of approximately 400 students. The school is accredited by the Middle States Association of Colleges and Schools and has been recognized as a Blue Ribbon School.

SVMS offers a variety of extracurricular activities, including a robust sports program with teams in basketball, baseball, softball, track and field, cross country, and cheerleading. The school also provides scouting opportunities through Girl Scouts and Cub Scouts. The sports program includes travel teams for older students and intramural leagues for younger grades, fostering athletic development and community engagement.

trgt:
Unconscious (or intuitive) communication is the subtle, unintentional, unconscious cues that provide information to another individual. It can be verbal (speech patterns, physical activity while speaking, or the tone of voice of an individual) or it can be nonverbal (facial expressions and body language). Some psychologists instead use the term honest signals because such cues are involuntary behaviors that often convey emotion whereas body language can be controlled. Many decisions are based on unconscious communication, which is interpreted and created in the right hemisphere of the brain. The right hemisphere is dominant in perceiving and expressing body language, facial expressions, verbal cues, and other indications that have to do with emotion but it does not exclusively deal with the unconscious.

Little is known about the unconscious mind or about how decisions are made based on unconscious communications except that they are always unintentional. There are two types of unconscious communications: intrapersonal and interpersonal.

Research has shown that human conscious attention can attend to 5–9 items simultaneously. All other information is processed by the unconscious mind. For example, the unconscious mind sometimes picks up on and relates nonverbal cues about an individual based on how they have arranged their settings such as their home or place of work.
mgt:
Unconscious communication refers to the exchange of information and emotions that occurs without conscious awareness, encompassing both intrapersonal and interpersonal interactions. Rooted in the concept of the unconscious mind, popularized by psychoanalyst Sigmund Freud, unconscious communication involves thoughts, feelings, and instincts that influence behavior without entering conscious awareness. Freud theorized that unconscious emotions are often projected onto others, shaping interactions and perceptions.

Intrapersonal unconscious communication occurs within an individual and includes internal processes such as dreams, past experiences, and hypnosis, which can unconsciously influence decisions and behaviors. This form of communication also relates to internal monologues and self-perception, with some researchers, like Joseph Jordania, suggesting it evolved to avoid discomfort with silence.

Interpersonal unconscious communication involves unintentional cues exchanged between individuals, such as facial expressions, body language, tone of voice, and speech patterns. These nonverbal signals are often interpreted instinctively by others, influencing social interactions. Studies have shown that people subconsciously mimic emotional facial expressions, highlighting the automatic nature of this form of communication.

Unconscious communication plays a significant role in human interaction, shaping both internal thought processes and external social dynamics through subtle, often unnoticed mechanisms.

trgt: Hirebenakal or Hirébeṇakal or Hirébeṇakallu (ಹಿರೇಬೆಣಕಲ್ಲು in Kannada) is a megalithic site in the state of Karnataka, India. It is among the few megalithic sites in India that can be dated to the 800 BCE to 200 BCE period. The site is located in the Koppal district, some 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) west of the town of Gangavati and some 35 kilometres (22 mi) from Hospet city. It contains roughly 400 megalithic funerary monuments, that have been dated to the transition period between Neolithic period and the Iron Age. Known locally (in the Kannada language) as eḷu guḍḍagaḷu (or 'the seven hills'), their specific name is moryar guḍḍa (or 'the hill of the moryas"). Hirebenakal is reported to be the largest necropolis among the 2000 odd megalithic sites found in South India, most of them in the state of Karnataka. Since 1955, it has been under the management of the Dharwad circle of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI). On May 19, 2021, it was proposed that Hirebenakal be made a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
mgt:
Hire Benakal (Hirebenakallu) is a prehistoric archaeological site located in the Koppal district of Karnataka, India. Dating back over 2,000 years, the site is renowned for its megalithic monuments, including dolmens, cists, and funerary structures, which are believed to have been constructed between 800 BCE and 200 BCE during the Iron Age. Spread across a rocky terrain of seven hills near the Tungabhadra River, the site features approximately 400 megalithic monuments, many of which are arranged in circular layouts and include anthropomorphic structures, menhirs, and stone enclosures. Notable discoveries include cave paintings, pottery, and iron implements from the Neolithic and megalithic periods.

First documented in 1835 by Philip Meadows Taylor, the site has since been studied by archaeologists such as Mortimer Wheeler and Adiga Sundara. Despite its historical significance, Hire Benakal remains relatively unknown and poorly maintained, with limited preservation efforts by the Archaeological Survey of India. Local villagers attribute spiritual significance to the site, believing it to be visited by a deity during annual festivals.

trgt: '(born 1973) is a German political scientist, lawyer and politician of the Christian Democratic Union of Germany (CDU). A resident of Belgium, he is an official of the Secretariat of the European Parliament. As a political scientist, he is known as a student of Eckhard Jesse in the tradition of German extremism research, and has published works on the political tradition of the former Party of Democratic Socialism. He was one of the leading figures within the Junge Union during the early 2000s and is currently chairman of the Brussels branch of the CDU, the party's only branch outside Germany. He ran unsuccessfully as a CDU candidate for the Bundestag in the 2005 German federal election.
mgt: Tim Peters is a German political scientist, lawyer, and European Parliament official. Born in Hamburg, he studied law at TU Dresden, Paris Nanterre University, and Humboldt University of Berlin, earning qualifications in both German and French law. He later pursued political science, completing a PhD on the anti-fascist tradition of the Party of Democratic Socialism under Eckhard Jesse. Peters has worked in various legal and advisory roles, including for General Electric, the German Ministry of Finance, and the Confederation of German Industry in Brussels. Politically active from a young age, he chaired the Junge Union in Berlin, ran as a CDU candidate in the 2005 federal election, and leads the CDU in Brussels.

trgt:
Richard Roswell Lyman (November 23, 1870 – December 31, 1963) was an American engineer and religious leader who was an apostle in The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church) from 1918 to 1943.

Lyman is often noted as the most recent LDS Church apostle to have been excommunicated. In 1943, J. Reuben Clark, the first counselor in the LDS church's First Presidency and the church's de facto leader due to church president Heber J. Grant's poor health, became aware that Lyman had for a number of years been in an intimate relationship with a woman he had earlier been assigned to counsel. The Quorum of the Twelve Apostles convened a disciplinary council to hear the case and Lyman's explanation, after which he was immediately excommunicated. Lyman was rebaptized in 1954 at age 83, and his full priesthood blessings were restored posthumously in 1970.
mgt:
Richard Roswell Lyman (November 23, 1870 – December 31, 1963) was an American civil engineer, educator, and leader in the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church). He served as a member of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles from 1918 until his excommunication in 1943 for unlawful cohabitation. Born in Fillmore, Utah Territory, Lyman was closely connected to early LDS Church leaders and pursued an extensive education, earning degrees from the University of Michigan, the University of Chicago, and Cornell University. He married Amy Brown, who later became the eighth general president of the Relief Society.

Lyman's excommunication stemmed from a long-term relationship with Anna Jacobsen Hegsted, which he considered a plural marriage despite the church's ban on the practice. He was rebaptized into the church in 1954 but was not reinstated as an apostle. Lyman died in Salt Lake City in 1963.

trgt:
In mathematics, the dimension of a vector space V is the cardinality (i.e., the number of vectors) of a basis of V over its base field. It is sometimes called Hamel dimension (after Georg Hamel) or algebraic dimension to distinguish it from other types of dimension.

For every vector space there exists a basis, and all bases of a vector space have equal cardinality; as a result, the dimension of a vector space is uniquely defined. We say is finite-dimensional if the dimension of is finite, and infinite-dimensional if its dimension is infinite.

The dimension of the vector space over the field can be written as or as read "dimension of over". When can be inferred from context, is typically written.
mgt:
In mathematics, the dimension of a vector space is the number of vectors in a basis of the space, which is a set of linearly independent vectors that span the entire space. The dimension depends on the base field over which the vector space is defined. For example, the complex numbers form a vector space of dimension 2 over the real numbers but have dimension 1 over the complex numbers. Finite-dimensional vector spaces with the same dimension are isomorphic, meaning they share the same structure.

The concept of dimension extends to other mathematical structures, such as modules, abelian groups, and commutative rings, with related notions like rank and Krull dimension. Generalizations also include infinite-dimensional spaces, trace class operators, and applications in representation theory, where dimension connects to characters and twisted dimensions.

trgt:
Alice's Restaurant is a 1969 American comedy film directed by Arthur Penn. It is an adaptation of the 1967 folk song "Alice's Restaurant Massacree", originally written and sung by Arlo Guthrie. The film stars Guthrie as himself, with Pat Quinn as Alice Brock and James Broderick as Ray Brock. Penn, who resided in the story's setting of Stockbridge, Massachusetts, co-wrote the screenplay in 1967 with Venable Herndon after hearing the song, shortly after directing Bonnie & Clyde.

Alice's Restaurant was released on August 19, 1969, a few days after Guthrie appeared at the Woodstock Festival. A soundtrack album for the film was also released by United Artists Records. The soundtrack includes a studio version of the title song, which was originally divided into two parts (one for each album side); a 1998 CD reissue on the Rykodisc label presents this version of the song in full, and adds several bonus tracks to the original LP.
mgt:
*Alice's Restaurant* is a 1969 American comedy-drama film directed by Arthur Penn and based on Arlo Guthrie's 1967 song "Alice's Restaurant Massacree." Starring Guthrie as himself, the film follows his experiences as a young musician navigating the counterculture of the 1960s, including his interactions with friends Alice and Ray Brock, a littering arrest that leads to a surreal draft board experience, and the challenges of communal living. While the film incorporates real-life events, many elements were fictionalized, including subplots and characters.

The film explores themes of countercultural idealism, generational conflict, and the Vietnam War draft, with its final scene symbolizing the fading of the 1960s counterculture dream. Featuring cameos by the real Alice Brock and Stockbridge police chief William Obanhein, the film received mixed reviews but was praised for its social commentary. It grossed $6.3 million and was the 23rd highest-grossing film of 1969.

trgt:
A President's Scholar is a recipient of the academic scholarship awarded by the Government of Singapore annually, to pursue undergraduate education at a university, usually abroad. The scholarship is considered to be the most prestigious public undergraduate scholarship in Singapore awarded to students of Singaporean nationality.

All recipients have legal obligation to serve a bond—in the form of a public service career for a certain period of time, usually ranging from 4 to 6 years, after completing his or her studies in the university.

Shortlisted candidates are interviewed by a selection committee chaired by the Chairman of the Public Service Commission (PSC) of Singapore.

The President's Scholarship by itself does not award any money or lead to any particular career in public service. As such, it is generally paired with another scholarship, which could be either of the following:
mgt:
The President's Scholarship is Singapore's most prestigious undergraduate scholarship, awarded to outstanding students who demonstrate exceptional academic performance, leadership qualities, and a commitment to serving the nation. It traces its origins to the Queen's Scholarship, established in 1885 by Cecil Clementi Smith, then Governor of the Straits Settlements. The Queen's Scholarship was replaced by the Singapore State Scholarship in 1959 following Singapore's attainment of self-governance. In 1964, the Yang di-Pertuan Negara Scholarship succeeded the State Scholarship, and it was renamed the President's Scholarship in 1966 after Singapore's independence.

Recipients of the President's Scholarship often go on to hold prominent positions in Singapore's civil service. Schools with the highest number of President's Scholars among their alumni have been identified through data collected by *The Straits Times*, reflecting the scholarship's role in shaping the nation's leadership.

trgt: Hyundai Chungun High School (Korean: 현대청운고등학교; HCU), is a private boarding high school located in Dong-gu, Ulsan, South Korea. It is one of the six independent private high schools (자율형 사립고) in South Korea that require an application of nationwide scale and admissions test to the applicant students. Since students who want to get in HCU usually get straight A when they are at middle school and take a test to get in HCU, they usually get rank 1 or rank 2 for their grades of CSAT(대학수학능력시험)College Scholastic Ability Test statistically. For example, there was a research for those who took CSAT in 2016(2017학년도 대학수학능력시험), and HCU placed the 4th in nationwide for the average CSAT grade for Korean, Mathematics, and English. The percentage of rank 1 and rank 2 is 4%, 7% respectively. The aggregate percentile for rank 2 is 11%. Academic grading in South Korea 72.6% of students at HCU in 2016 got rank 1 or rank 2 for their CSAT grade.
mgt:
Hyundai Chungun High School (현대청운고등학교) is a private boarding school located in Ulsan, South Korea. Established in 1981 by Chung Ju-yung, the founder of Hyundai Group, the school is known for its specialized education programs aimed at fostering gifted students. It offers an "Elite Leadership Education" program, including Advanced Placement (AP) courses, and has been an official AP test center since 2007. The curriculum emphasizes foreign languages, algebra, natural sciences, and essay writing, supported by the Academic Adviser Policy.

In 2008, the school launched the Hyundai Chungun Global Leader Program (HCGLP), an international class designed to prepare students for admission to top foreign universities. The program includes English presentation courses and the Global Leader Scholarship (GLS), a three-week study program at Portland State University in the United States. Additionally, the school promotes Korean traditional music and arts through classes and clubs for Samul Nori, Daegeum, and Gayageum. Hyundai Chungun High School enrolls approximately 530 students and employs 61 faculty members.

trgt:
JD Eicher (born July 27, 1986) is an American singer, songwriter, producer, and novelist from the Youngstown, Ohio area.

Both billed as JD Eicher & the Goodnights and independently as JD Eicher, Eicher has released nine albums—one of which also featured a Deluxe Edition—as well as a live EP and vinyl collection of his most popular songs.

Eicher also created an EP of his music to accompany the release of Nicholas Sparks' 20th novel called Two by Two. The musician and the author toured the country to promote this project.

Touring mostly in the United States, Eicher has a strong following in many regions of the nation. Eicher also occasionally tours in Europe and has fans in many cities abroad. Additionally, a number of Eicher's songs receive radio airplay and have been used in businesses, television ad campaigns, and television shows.
mgt:
JD Eicher is an American singer, songwriter, producer, and novelist from Youngstown, Ohio, known for his alt-pop and indie music. He began his music career in 2006, forming the band JD Eicher & the Goodnights while attending Westminster College, where he earned a degree in music and business. The band released a trilogy of albums with interconnected themes before Eicher transitioned to performing under his own name. His music is characterized by introspective lyrics and melodic arrangements, earning critical acclaim and airplay on alternative radio stations.

Eicher has collaborated with author Nicholas Sparks, creating a soundtrack EP for Sparks' novel *Two by Two* and touring together. He has released several albums, including *The Middle Distance*, *Court Street*, and *Majesto Sessions*, the latter accompanied by his debut novel, *The Lights Along Majesto*. Eicher also organizes charity events and regularly performs at festivals and music cruises.

trgt: The Deceased Correa (in Spanish La Difunta Correa) is a semi-pagan legendary figure in folk-religion, for which a number of people in Argentina and Chile, especially among the popular classes, feel a great devotion. It has spread, in a limited way, to neighbouring countries such as Uruguay. Every year since its inception in 1840, miracles are said to have occurred at the shrine of La Difunta Correa, and thousands of people have visited there to pay their respects. The shrine is situated in the small town of Vallecito, 1160 km from Buenos Aires and 63 km from the city of San Juan.
mgt: Difunta Correa is a figure of folk veneration in Argentina, regarded as an unofficial popular saint in Folk Catholicism. According to legend, Deolinda Correa died in the desert of San Juan Province in the 1840s while searching for her sick husband, leaving behind a baby who miraculously survived by nursing from her lifeless body. Her grave in Vallecito became a sanctuary, attracting devout followers who believe in her miraculous intercession. Her shrine, featuring votive chapels and offerings such as water bottles, is a major pilgrimage site, especially during Easter and All Souls' Day, drawing thousands of visitors annually.

trgt: Clepsicosma is a genus of moths in the family Crambidae. As at 2022, this genus contains only one described species, Clepsicosma iridia, which is endemic to New Zealand. The species inhabits native forest in the North Island as well as the northern and western parts of the South Island down to Westland. The larval host of this species is assumed to be species of Cutty grass, possibly including Gahnia setifolia and Gahnia xanthocarpa, although the life history of this species is unknown. The adults of C. iridia are on the wing from December until May. They are nocturnal, and are attracted to light. During the day the adults rest on the underside of leaves, including those Cutty grass species that may possibly be their larval hosts.
mgt: *Clepsicosma* is a genus of moths in the family Crambidae, containing the single described species *Clepsicosma iridia*. Both the genus and species were first described by Edward Meyrick in 1888, based on a female specimen collected in the Waitākere Ranges, Auckland, New Zealand. The species is endemic to New Zealand, where it is commonly found in native forests across the North Island and parts of the South Island. Adult moths are nocturnal, active from late December to May, and are attracted to light. They rest on the underside of leaves during the day. The larval host plant is believed to be species of cutty grass, particularly *Gahnia setifolia* and *Gahnia xanthocarpa*, though this has not been confirmed through rearing in captivity.

trgt:
Adriaan van Dis (Bergen aan Zee, 16 December 1946) is a Dutch author. He debuted in 1983 with the novella Nathan Sid. In 1995 his book Indische Duinen (My Father's War), which in its narrative is a follow up to his debut novella, was also awarded several prestigious literary awards.

He is also known as the host of his own award-winning television talkshow named Hier is. Adriaan van Dis, that lasted from 1983 to 1992 and several successful award-winning television documentaries.

With the publication of his Indies inspired compilation book De Indie boeken (The Indies books) in 2012, van Dis establishes himself as one of the most significant second generation authors of Dutch Indies literature.
mgt:
Adriaan van Dis (born December 16, 1946) is a Dutch author and television presenter, known for his novels, novellas, and literary contributions. Born in Bergen aan Zee, Netherlands, to a family deeply affected by the Second World War and the Indonesian revolution, van Dis grew up feeling like an outsider due to his mixed heritage and complex family history. He studied Dutch in Amsterdam, where he developed an interest in Afrikaans and South African literature, earning a doctorate in 1979.

Van Dis debuted with the novella *Nathan Sid* in 1983, which won the Gouden Ezelsoor prize. He gained further recognition as the host of literary talk shows and documentary series, including *Van Dis in Afrika* and a series on Dutch-Indonesian roots.

trgt:
Howard John Ehmke (April 24, 1894 – March 17, 1959) was an American baseball pitcher. He played professional baseball for 16 years from 1914 to 1930, including 15 seasons in Major League Baseball for the Buffalo Blues (1915), Detroit Tigers (1916–1917, 1919–1922), Boston Red Sox (1923–1926), and Philadelphia Athletics (1926–1930).

Ehmke compiled a career win–loss record of 166–166 with a 3.75 earned run average (ERA). His greatest success was with the Red Sox, including a no-hitter and his only 20-win season in 1923. Ehmke still holds the American League record for fewest hits allowed (one) in two consecutive starts. Ehmke also ranks sixteenth all-time in hitting batters. He hit 137 batters in his career and led the American League in the category seven times, including a career-high 23 in 1922. Ehmke is best known for being the surprise starter who won Game 1 of the 1929 World Series for the Athletics at the age of 35.

After retiring from baseball, he started his own company that began making tarpaulins to cover baseball diamonds during rain.
mgt:
Howard Ehmke (April 24, 1894 – March 17, 1959) was an American professional baseball pitcher who played 15 seasons in Major League Baseball (MLB) from 1915 to 1930. He pitched for the Buffalo Blues, Detroit Tigers, Boston Red Sox, and Philadelphia Athletics, compiling a career win–loss record of 166–166 with a 3.75 earned run average (ERA). Ehmke is best known for his performance in Game 1 of the 1929 World Series, where he struck out a then-record 13 batters to lead the Athletics to victory over the Chicago Cubs. He also pitched a no-hitter for the Red Sox in 1923 and was a key contributor to the Athletics' 1929 World Series championship.

Beyond baseball, Ehmke was an entrepreneur credited with inventing the tarpaulin to protect sports fields from rain. He founded the Howard Ehmke Company, which manufactured tarps, tents, and other products, and later contributed to defense work during World War II. Ehmke died in Philadelphia in 1959 at the age of 64.

trgt:
To the east and north of the Maasai Mara National Reserve, some of the former Maasai cattle-grazing areas have been converted into wildlife conservancies for tourism.

The conservancies include Enonkishu, Lemek, Mara Naboisho, Mara North, Nashulai, Olare Motorogi, Olarro North and Olarro South, Olchorro Oirowua, Ol Kinyei, Olderkesi, Oloisukut, Pardamat and Siana.

The conservancies came about when Maasai land owners with neighbouring land came together to agree that safari camp operators could use their land for tourism purposes, in return for either a percentage of profits or leasing fee.

While this varies between conservancies, in some instances the Maasai retain livestock grazing rights in. Due to this, it is fairly common to see cattle alongside wildlife in certain areas.
mgt: The Maasai Mara conservancies are privately managed wildlife conservation areas surrounding the Maasai Mara National Reserve in Kenya. These conservancies aim to balance wildlife conservation with sustainable tourism and the needs of the local Maasai communities. Tourism in the conservancies is highly regulated, with strict limits on tourist accommodations and safari vehicles, and activities such as night drives, off-road driving, and walking safaris are permitted, unlike in the national reserve. However, tensions have arisen between Maasai pastoralists and tourism operators due to issues like cattle grazing in restricted areas and allegations of unfair profit distribution and exclusion of local communities from decision-making processes, leading to growing distrust in conservancy management.

trgt:
Codex Boturini, also known as the Tira de la Peregrinación de los Mexica (Tale of the Mexica Migration), is an Aztec codex, which depicts the migration of the Azteca, later Mexica, people from Aztlán. Its date of manufacture is unknown, but likely to have occurred before or just after the Conquest of the Aztec Empire. At least two other Aztec codices have been influenced by the content and style of the Boturini Codex. This Codex has become an insignia of Mexica history and pilgrimage and is carved into a stone wall at the entrance of the National Museum of Anthropology and History in Mexico City.

The codex is currently located in the National Museum of Anthropology in Mexico City.
mgt: The *Codex Boturini*, also known as the *Tira de la Peregrinación de los Mexica*, is an early 16th-century Aztec manuscript that chronicles the migration of the Mexica people from Aztlán to the Basin of Mexico, culminating just before the founding of Tenochtitlan. Created on amate paper and folded accordion-style, the codex is notable for its unfinished state, with red and black ink used to depict date glyphs, footprints, and key events. It is attributed to a single tlacuilo (scribe) familiar with Aztec writing systems. Acquired by Lorenzo Boturini Benaduci in the 18th century, the codex is now housed in Mexico's National Museum of Anthropology. It shares similarities with other Aztec codices, such as the Aubin and Mexicanus Codices.

trgt:
The Lucknow Mail is an Indian Railways train running between Lucknow and New Delhi on a daily basis. It is numbered 12229 when starting from Lucknow and terminating at New Delhi, 12230 between New Delhi and Lucknow. It has received an ISO 9000 certification after Bhopal Express. Lucknow Mail is the first LHB train of Indian Railways running with 24 coaches (22 coaches+2 EOG) on permanent basis. Lucknow Mail also has the maximum number of AC coaches for a non fully-AC train (11 AC Coaches) in India.

Lucknow Mail along with Shram Shakti Express, Prayagraj Express, Shiv Ganga Express enjoys highest priority all over the route
mgt: The *Lucknow Mail* is a prominent mail train operated by Northern Railways, connecting Lucknow Junction (LJN) and New Delhi (NDLS). Covering a distance of 492 kilometers, it operates daily with an average journey time of approximately 8 hours and 40 minutes from New Delhi to Lucknow and 9 hours in the reverse direction. The train offers various classes, including Sleeper, General, First AC, Second AC, and Third AC, with both seating and sleeping arrangements. Originally numbered 29 Up and 30 Down, it was later renumbered under rationalization schemes and elevated to the superfast category in 2005–06. It is powered by WAP-7, WAP-4, or WAP-5 locomotives.

trgt: Cycling in Prague is a popular leisure activity and a not so common mode of transport within the capital city of the Czech Republic. However, levels of utility cyclists have been rising recently. In 2018, 2% of people commuted by bike in Prague (4% in summer, less than 2% in winter). As of 2017, there were 178 km (111 mi) of protected cycle paths and routes in the city. Also, there are 48 km (30 mi) of bike lanes and 24 km (15 mi) of specially marked bus lanes that can be used by cyclists.
mgt: Cycling in Prague has evolved significantly over time, transitioning from a city with minimal cycling infrastructure to one with growing support for cyclists. The first bicycle lanes appeared in 1956, but modern cycling paths, such as the one in Stromovka park, were only introduced in 1986. Following the Velvet Revolution, car traffic surged, but leisure cycling gained popularity, and plans for extensive cycling routes were initiated, though many were not completed. Prague now features bike-sharing systems, with several operators active as of 2022. Bicycles are also accommodated on public transport, including the metro, ferries, and select buses.

page_title: Sir John Wedderburn, 5th Baronet of Blackness
trgt: Sir John Wedderburn, 5th Baronet of Blackness, was a Perthshire gentleman who joined the 1745 rebellion of Charles Edward Stuart. He was captured at the Battle of Culloden, taken to London, and convicted of treason. He was hanged, his estates were forfeit to the Crown, and his family was attainted. The Blackness in his title is Blackness House in Dundee rather than Blackness in Lothian just west of Edinburgh. His son John Wedderburn of Ballendean fled after his father's death to Jamaica, where he re-established the family's fortunes via slave sugar, and eventually regained his father's title.
mgt: Sir John Wedderburn, 5th Baronet of Blackness (died 28 November 1746), was a Scottish landowner and Jacobite who participated in the Jacobite rising of 1745, known as the "Forty Five." A supporter of Charles Edward Stuart, he served as a colonel in the Jacobite army and was captured at the Battle of Culloden. Wedderburn was tried for treason in London, found guilty, and executed at Kennington Common. Born into a family with Jacobite ties, he faced financial hardship before the rebellion. He married Jean Fullerton in 1724 and had several children, including two sons, John and James.

trgt:
Henrique "Rico" S. Malvar (born 1957) is a distinguished Brazilian engineer and a signal processing researcher at Microsoft Research's largest laboratory in Redmond, Washington, United States. He was the managing director of the lab following the departure of long-time Managing Director Dan Ling in 2007, when he oversaw about 350 researchers. Currently, he is a Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft Research, heading a team that develops new user experiences with new devices and new input/output interaction modes, with special attention to inclusiveness and empowering people with disabilities.

Rico is also an Affiliate Professor at the Electrical and Computer Engineering Department at the University of Washington.
mgt: Henrique "Rico" S. Malvar (born 1957) is a Brazilian electrical engineer, computer scientist, and researcher known for his contributions to signal processing and data compression. He developed the lapped transform, Malvar wavelets, and algorithms for audio and image codecs, including Windows Media Audio and JPEG XR. Malvar earned his Ph.D. from MIT and was a professor at the University of Brasília, where he founded the Digital Signal Processing Research Group. He joined Microsoft Research in 1997, where he has held leadership roles, including Chief Scientist and Distinguished Engineer. A member of the National Academy of Engineering, he holds over 120 patents.

trgt:
Ports North, the trading name of the Far North Queensland Ports Corporation Limited, is a Queensland Government statutory corporation that is responsible for the Cairns Marlin Marina and the Cairns Cityport project and the ports in Cairns, Cape Flattery, Karumba, Mourilyan, Skardon River, Quintell Beach, Thursday Island, Burketown and Cooktown, in Queensland, Australia. Since 2017, the shareholding Ministers are the Honourable Jackie Trad, Deputy Premier, Treasurer and Minister for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Partnerships, and the Honourable Mark Bailey, Minister for Transport and Main Roads.

Formerly known as the Cairns Port Authority, the corporation was responsible for the operation of the Cairns International Airport until the sale of the airport in December 2008 to a private consortium. The former authority was responsible for the Cairns Seaport and in 2009 became responsible for the regional ports of Mourilyan, Cooktown, Cape Flattery, Quintell Beach, Thursday Island, Skardon River, Karumba and Burketown.
mgt: Ports North, officially known as the Far North Queensland Ports Corporation Limited, is a statutory corporation responsible for managing the ports in Far North Queensland, Australia. Headquartered in Cairns, Queensland, it oversees key facilities including the Cairns Seaport, Cairns Marlin Marina, and the Cityport urban renewal project. The Cairns Seaport, located on Trinity Inlet, is a vital hub for cargo and passenger vessels, contributing significantly to the region's tourism economy as one of Australia's busiest cruise ship destinations. It handles over 1.16 million tonnes of cargo annually, supporting exports like sugar and molasses and imports such as petroleum and fertiliser. The Cairns Marlin Marina, near the entrance to Trinity Inlet, features 264 berths and serves private yachts, catamarans, and commercial tourism operators offering Great Barrier Reef tours. The Cityport project focuses on urban redevelopment near the seaport, enhancing the southern end of the Cairns central business district.

trgt:
Radio Dabanga (Arabic: راديو دبنقا) is a shortwave radio station, TV and online news magazine, that serves Sudan with daily reports on the latest political, economic and social information, including health issues and social programs such as Lost and Found. The shortwave radio has been broadcasting since 1 December 2008. Current broadcasts last for a total of one hour each day.

While Radio Dabanga only broadcast on shortwave radio for the first few years, the station now has several channels. The editors have innovated and experimented in the sphere of new media, often at the request of their listeners. These include Satellite TV, Facebook, WhatsApp, and SoundCloud. Depending on the medium, Radio Dabanga produces news in Sudanese Arabic and/or English.

Shortwave radio remains the mainstay of the radio station; one quarter of all Sudanese people are illiterate, a number that is relatively higher in Sudan’s remote areas and camps for displaced people. Many people here have no lifeline to the outside world other than telephone and radio, so it is Radio Dabanga’s mission to provide this.
mgt:
Radio Dabanga is an independent Sudanese radio station that broadcasts news and programming in Sudanese Arabic via shortwave radio, satellite TV, and online platforms. Established on December 1, 2008, and operated by the Dutch NGO Free Press Unlimited, the station is based in exile in Amsterdam. Initially focused on conflict areas like Darfur and the Blue Nile, Radio Dabanga has since expanded its coverage to include a wide range of issues across Sudan, providing reliable information to millions of listeners and viewers.

The station broadcasts twice daily, with additional programs addressing topics such as justice, culture, and reuniting families separated by conflict. It also operates a satellite TV channel and maintains a strong online presence, including social media and WhatsApp. Despite facing censorship, jamming, and government opposition, Radio Dabanga remains a vital source of independent news in Sudan. Its audience includes millions of radio listeners and satellite TV viewers, with significant engagement from Sudanese citizens contributing news and tips.

trgt:
Joint Assistant for Development and Execution (JADE) is a U.S. military system used for planning the deployment of military forces in crisis situations.

The U.S. military developed this automated planning software system in order to expedite the creation of the detailed planning needed to deploy military forces for a military operation. JADE uses Artificial Intelligence (AI) technology combining user input, a knowledge base of stored plans, and suggestions by the system to provide the ability to develop large-scale and complex plans in minimal time.
mgt: The Joint Assistant for Deployment and Execution (JADE) is a U.S. military planning system developed by BBN Technologies under DARPA's ARPA-Rome Planning Initiative. Initially a prototype in 1999, JADE was later integrated into the Global Command and Control System (GCCS) and the Adaptive Course of Action (ACOA) environment. Utilizing artificial intelligence, JADE supports rapid crisis action planning by generating Time Phased Force Deployment Data (TPFDD) for organizing combat-ready forces. It combines case-based and generative planning methods, enabling efficient plan adaptation and collaboration across command centers.

trgt: Thomas P. Coates (1803 – October 11, 1895) was a 19th-century American musician who achieved initial prominence in Pennsylvania for his performances on the cornet and French horn. The director of Pomp's Cornet Band in Easton, Pennsylvania, he was commissioned as the first conductor of the regimental band of the 47th Pennsylvania Volunteer Infantry Regiment during the early months of the American Civil War. Post-war, he became a prolific and popular composer of band music, and was subsequently nicknamed "the Father of Band Music in America."
mgt: Thomas P. Coates (1803 – October 11, 1895) was an American band conductor, composer, and musician, widely regarded as the "Father of Band Music in America." Born in Easton, Pennsylvania, he began his musical career in a circus band and later performed with prominent ensembles, including Pomp's Cornet Band and Patrick Gilmore's band. During the American Civil War, he led the regimental band of the 47th Pennsylvania Infantry. Coates composed numerous works, including the "Twelfth Funeral March," played at Ulysses S. Grant's funeral. He died in Easton and is commemorated with a monument.

trgt: Ralfi Pagán (born Rafael Pagán; 1946–1978) was a Bronx, New York-based Latin soul and salsa singer of Puerto Rican and Cuban parentage who was active from the mid-1960s until his death in 1978. He specialized in soul ballads sung both in Spanish and English and released five albums during the 1970s. He is well known for the duet with Sylvia Robinson entitled Soul Je T'aime and for his solo reworking of Make It With You previously recorded by David Gates and Bread. Ralfi toured with legendary music producer and promoter Eddie Torres for several years throughout the Southwest United States. Prior to leaving on tour Ralfi visited Eddie’s home to share he was leaving to Colombia. Eddie told Ralfi he had a bad feeling about the trip. Eddie advised Ralfi not to go. Ralfi went to Colombia against Torres’ advise. In 1978, he was murdered while on a promotional tour in Colombia, South America. His murder remains unsolved.
mgt:
Ralfi Pagán (born Rafael Pagán; September 11, 1946 – November 1978) was an American Latin soul, salsa, and disco singer, known for his falsetto tenor and romantic ballads. Born in the Bronx, New York, he began his career in the mid-1960s, releasing his first single under the name Ray Paige. Pagán gained popularity among young Hispanic Americans with his self-titled debut album in 1969 and his 1971 Latin cover of "Make It With You," which became a national hit and earned him a spot on *Soul Train*, making him one of the first Latino artists to appear on the show.

Pagán released several albums, blending Latin soul, salsa, and disco, and became a favorite among Chicano audiences in Los Angeles. His career was cut short in 1978 when he was murdered while on tour in Colombia. Despite his untimely death, Pagán's music has experienced a resurgence, with much of his catalog reissued since the 1990s.

trgt: Angela Jackson is a collegiate women's basketball coach. She coached the Chicago State University women's basketball team, the Cougars, for 15 years. Jackson led them to win the 2010-2011 Great West Conference Championships and to the Women's Basketball Invitational Final Four, for which she was named Coach of the Year. Among the coaches of Chicago State athletic teams, she has the strongest career record, 123 wins. She coached several 1,000 point scorers, including six players who went on to professional contracts.
mgt: Angela Jackson is an American basketball coach and former player from Detroit, Michigan. She played college basketball at Odessa College and Old Dominion University, graduating in 1991 with a degree in Health and Physical Education. Jackson began her coaching career as an assistant at Wayne State University and later served as head coach at Saginaw Valley State and Chicago State University. During her tenure at Chicago State (2003–2018), her team won a conference championship and reached the WBI Final Four in 2010–2011.
