trgt: Terranova is a German band based between Berlin and Paris made up of current members Fetisch and &me. Terranova are signed to Kompakt records. Terranova was formed in 1996 by Fetisch, Marco Meister and Kaos. They previously went under the moniker Turntable Terranova on the Compost label and sometimes as Edition Terranova. Their music is a fusion of punk rock, electronic, hip hop and House. They currently reside between Paris and Berlin and perform DJ sets worldwide. They have been constant collaborators with artists in the past, Cath Coffey, Nicolette Krebitz, Tricky, Ari Up, Mike Ladd, Khan, Snax, Billie Ray Martin e.a.
mgt: Terranova is a German electronic music group formed in Berlin, known for their eclectic style blending electronic, hip-hop, and rock influences. The group was founded by Fetisch and Shapemod in the mid-1990s, with their debut release, *Fiasko*, in 1996. They gained recognition with their 1998 DJ mix album *DJ-Kicks* and their debut LP *Close the Door* (1999), featuring collaborations with artists like Cath Coffey and Tricky. Over the years, Terranova released several albums, including *Hitchhiking Nonstop with no Particular Destination* (2002) and *Hotel Amour* (2012), while collaborating with labels like !K7 Records, Ministry of Sound, and Kompakt.

trgt:
Contour crafting is a building printing technology being researched by Behrokh Khoshnevis of the University of Southern California's Information Sciences Institute (in the Viterbi School of Engineering) that uses a computer-controlled crane or gantry to build edifices rapidly and efficiently with substantially less manual labor. It was originally conceived as a method to construct molds for industrial parts. Khoshnevis decided to adapt the technology for rapid home construction as a way to rebuild after natural disasters, like the devastating earthquakes that have plagued his native Iran.

Using a quick-setting, concrete-like material, contour crafting forms the house's walls layer by layer until topped off by floors and ceilings set in place by the crane. The notional concept calls for the insertion of structural components, plumbing, wiring, utilities, and even consumer devices like audiovisual systems as the layers are built.
mgt: Contour crafting is an innovative 3D printing technology developed by Behrokh Khoshnevis for automated construction, capable of building structures such as homes in a single day with minimal material waste and environmental impact. First gaining attention in the early 2000s, the technology has been supported by organizations like Caterpillar Inc. and NASA, which explored its potential for constructing bases on the Moon and Mars using primarily local materials. In 2009, the ACASA project was launched to commercialize contour crafting, with Khoshnevis serving as CTO. By 2017, the Contour Crafting Corporation, led by Khoshnevis, partnered with Doka Ventures to advance the technology, with plans to deliver the first printers soon after. The method has been highlighted for its efficiency, sustainability, and potential to revolutionize construction both on Earth and in extraterrestrial environments.

trgt:
The Terror of Tiny Town is a 1938 American musical Western film produced by Jed Buell, directed by Sam Newfield and starring Billy Curtis. The film was shot at a sound studio in Hollywood and partly at Placeritos Ranch in Placerita Canyon, California. The inspiration came when Buell overheard an employee jokingly say “If this economic dive keeps going, we'll be using midgets as actors”.

Using a conventional Western story with an all-diminutive cast, the filmmakers were able to showcase gags such as cowboys entering the local saloon by walking under the swinging doors, climbing into cupboards to retrieve items and dwarf cowboys galloping around on Shetland ponies while roping calves. It is considered to be one of the worst films ever made.
mgt:
*The Terror of Tiny Town* is a 1938 American Western film directed by Sam Newfield and produced by Jed Buell. Notable for its all-little-person cast, the film features a traditional Western plot involving cattle rustling, feuds, and romance, with a comedic and unconventional twist. Starring Billy Curtis, the movie was promoted as the first of its kind and gained attention for its novelty. 

Initially released independently and later distributed by Columbia Pictures, the film received positive reviews upon its debut, praised for its humor and unique premise. Over time, it has become a cult classic, often regarded as a "so bad it's good" film. Despite plans for sequels, none were produced. The film entered the public domain in 1966 and has since been released on various home media formats.

trgt: Fairhope Plantation is a historic Carpenter Gothic plantation house and historic district, located one mile east of Uniontown, Alabama, US. The 2+1⁄2-story wood-framed main house was built in the Gothic Revival style in the late 1850s. The plantation historic district includes six other contributing buildings, in addition to the main house. It was added to the Alabama Register of Landmarks and Heritage on December 19, 1991, and subsequently to the National Register of Historic Places on May 29, 1992, due to its architectural and historical significance.
mgt: Fairhope Plantation is a historic Gothic Revival plantation house near Uniontown, Alabama, built between 1857 and 1861 for Joseph Selden and his wife, Elizabeth Collier Minge. Designed by local builder Theophilus G. Fowler, the house features intricate Gothic Revival details, including bargeboards, hood moldings, and a cast-iron veranda. The plantation was established on land gifted by Elizabeth's family and remained in the Selden family for over a century. It was added to the Alabama Register of Landmarks and Heritage in 1991 and the National Register of Historic Places in 1992.

trgt:
PRNP (prion protein) is the human gene encoding for the major prion protein PrP (prion protein, Pr for prion, and P for protein), also known as CD230 (cluster of differentiation 230). Expression of the protein is most predominant in the nervous system but occurs in many other tissues throughout the body.

The protein can exist in multiple isoforms: the normal PrPC form, and the protease-resistant form designated PrPRes such as the disease-causing PrPSc(scrapie) and an isoform located in mitochondria. The misfolded version PrPSc is associated with a variety of cognitive disorders and neurodegenerative diseases such as in animals: ovine scrapie, bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE, mad cow disease), feline spongiform encephalopathy, transmissible mink encephalopathy (TME), exotic ungulate encephalopathy, chronic wasting disease (CWD) which affects cervids; and in humans: Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (CJD), fatal familial insomnia (FFI), Gerstmann–Sträussler–Scheinker syndrome (GSS), kuru, and variant Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (vCJD). Similarities exist between kuru, thought to be due to human ingestion of diseased individuals, and vCJD, thought to be due to human ingestion of BSE-tainted cattle products.
mgt:
The major prion protein (PrP) is a glycoprotein encoded by the *PRNP* gene, located on chromosome 20 in humans. It is highly conserved across mammals and exists in two main isoforms: the normal cellular form (PrP<sup>C</sup>) and the misfolded, disease-associated form (PrP<sup>Sc</sup>). PrP<sup>C</sup> is primarily found on the surface of neurons and other cells, where it is anchored to the membrane via a glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) anchor. While its precise function remains unclear, PrP<sup>C</sup> is thought to play roles in copper ion transport, cell signaling, synaptic function, and memory formation.

The misfolded PrP<sup>Sc</sup> isoform is associated with transmissible spongiform encephalopathies (TSEs), a group of fatal neurodegenerative diseases, including Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease and kuru. PrP<sup>Sc</sup> aggregates in neural tissue, leading to neurodegeneration. Polymorphisms in *PRNP* influence susceptibility to prion diseases and other conditions, such as Alzheimer's disease, where PrP interacts with amyloid-beta oligomers. Research continues to explore PrP's physiological roles, its involvement in disease, and its potential as a therapeutic target.

trgt:
Edith Gertrude Clements (1874–1971), also known as Edith S. Clements and Edith Schwartz Clements, was an American botanist and pioneer of botanical ecology who was the first woman to be awarded a Ph.D. by the University of Nebraska. She was married to botanist Frederic Clements, with whom she collaborated throughout her professional life. Together they founded the Alpine Laboratory, a research station at Pikes Peak, Colorado. Clements was also a botanical artist who illustrated her own books as well as joint publications with Frederic.

Both Clementses were involved with the study of phytogeography, especially those factors determining the ecology of vegetation in particular regions, and they would be praised as "the most illustrious husband-wife team since the Curies." It is impossible to entirely disentangle the work of each Clementses as they worked together during their noteworthy years.
mgt:
Edith Gertrude Clements (née Schwartz; 1874–1971) was an American botanist, ecological pioneer, and botanical illustrator. She was the first woman to earn a Ph.D. in botany from the University of Nebraska, where she specialized in phytogeography. Alongside her husband, Frederic Clements, she conducted groundbreaking research in plant ecology, supported by the Carnegie Institution. The couple developed the Alpine Laboratory at Pikes Peak, Colorado, training many ecologists and publishing extensively on plant succession and adaptation. Edith illustrated numerous scientific works, including *Rocky Mountain Flowers* (1914), and translated their research into foreign languages.

During the Dust Bowl, the Clementses promoted conservation efforts across the Great Plains. Edith's memoir, *Adventures in Ecology* (1960), detailed her multifaceted contributions to their expeditions. Her legacy includes archival collections at the University of Wyoming and the University of Nebraska. She was nominated for the Nebraska Hall of Fame in 2012.

trgt:
The Burrumbeet Cup is a thoroughbred horse race, held under handicap conditions over a distance of 1800 metres at the Burrumbeet racecourse, Burrumbeet, Victoria, Australia on New Year's Day every year.

The 2020 Petrogas Regional Burrumbeet Cup will be held on 1 January 2020 and will be the 130th running of the race.

Prize money for the 2020 Burrumbeet Cup is $25,000 and Petrogas Regional is race sponsor. Petrogas Regional became the Burrumbeet Cup sponsor from 2015 onwards after the decision by Scott Petroleum to end their Burrumbeet Cup sponsorship following the running of the 2014 Burrumbeet Cup.

The Burrumbeet Park & Windermere Racing Club (BP&WRC) administer racing at Burrumbeet under the jurisdiction of Racing Victoria and all 14 committee members are volunteers. President of the BP & WRC is Rod McKinnon and club secretary is Paul Brumby.
mgt:
The Burrumbeet Cup is an annual thoroughbred horse race held at the Burrumbeet racecourse in Western Victoria, Australia. Inaugurated in 1891, the race is run over a distance of 1,800 metres on turf under handicap conditions and is open to horses meeting a Benchmark 78 qualification. Sponsored by Petrogas Regional, the event has a prize purse of $15,000 as of 2016. Traditionally held on New Year's Day, the Burrumbeet Cup is a popular local event, attracting large crowds and supporting charitable causes.

Notable recent winners include Asset Hound in 2015, trained by Glen and Barry James, and Heisman in 2014, trained by Darren Weir. The race has also featured significant community involvement, with funds raised for causes such as the National Jockeys Trust and cystic fibrosis. The event remains a highlight of the Burrumbeet Park & Windermere Jockey Club's calendar.

trgt:
Cuneo railway station, or Cuneo Altipiano railway station (Lang-it or Stazione di Cuneo Altipiano), is the main station serving the city and comune of Cuneo, in the Piedmont region, northwestern Italy. Opened in 1937, it is the junction of the Fossano–Cuneo, Savigliano–Cuneo, Cuneo–Ventimiglia and Cuneo–Mondovì railways.

The station is currently managed by Rete Ferroviaria Italiana (RFI). However, the commercial area of the passenger building is managed by Centostazioni. Train services are operated by Trenitalia. Each of these companies is a subsidiary of Ferrovie dello Stato (FS), Italy's state-owned rail company.

The other station in the city, Cuneo Gesso, forms part of the Cuneo–Mondovì railway, and is not far from the hamlet of Borgo San Giuseppe (formerly Borgo Gesso).
mgt: Cuneo railway station (Italian: Stazione di Cuneo) is a railway station located in Piazzale della Libertà in Cuneo, Piedmont, Italy. Opened on November 7, 1937, it replaced the old Cuneo Gesso–Boves–Borgo San Dalmazzo line and serves as a key hub on the Fossano–Cuneo, Savigliano–Cuneo, Cuneo–Ventimiglia, and Cuneo–Mondovì lines. Operated by Rete Ferroviaria Italiana and Centostazioni, the station handles approximately 3 million passenger movements annually, with regional services connecting to Turin, Savona, Ventimiglia, and Nice. The station features five passenger tracks, a locomotive depot, and modern public information systems, including loudspeakers and monitors. It is classified as a "Gold" station and offers connections to urban and suburban buses.

trgt: The Tianfei Palace, officially the Mazu Cultural Palace and also known as the Tianhou Palace, is a restored temple of the Chinese sea-goddess Mazu, the deified form of the medieval Fujianese shamaness Lin Moniang, located in Fangta Park in Songjiang, Shanghai, in eastern China. Officially classified as a museum, the Tianfei Palace conducts Mazuist rites twice a year, on the traditional anniversaries of Lin Moniang's birth and death. It is also used as the site for an annual commemoration of Songjiang's city god Li Daiwen.
mgt: Tianfei Palace, also known as the Mazu Cultural Palace or Tianhou Temple, is a historic temple in Songjiang, Shanghai, dedicated to Mazu, the Chinese sea goddess. Originally built in downtown Shanghai in 1883, it was relocated to Songjiang's Fangta Park in 1978 as part of a cultural restoration project. The temple, restored and reopened in 2002, features Qing-era architectural elements and carvings. While unregistered with religious authorities, it hosts biannual Mazuist ceremonies and an annual commemoration for Ming official Li Daiwen.

trgt:
Danielson Provincial Park is located at the northern end of Lake Diefenbaker in the southern part of the Canadian province of Saskatchewan. The park surrounds the Gardiner Dam, which was built in 1967 and is among the largest embankment dams in Canada and the world. The South Saskatchewan River flows north beyond the park, towards Outlook and Saskatoon. Saskatchewan Highways 44, 45, and 219 provide access to the park. SaskPower's Coteau Creek Hydroelectric Station is located near the park.

The park opened to the public in 1971 and was named for former MLA Gustaf Herman Danielson. It is one if three provincial parks on Lake Diefenbaker, with the other two being Douglas Provincial Park and Saskatchewan Landing Provincial Park.

Danielson Provincial Park's Visitor Centre is located between Lake Diefenbaker and Coteau Bay, and features numerous interactive displays about the Gardiner Dam and the river systems of southern Saskatchewan in addition to a café and beach.
mgt:
Danielson Provincial Park is a provincial park located in Saskatchewan, Canada, near the town of Outlook. Established in 1971, the park offers a range of recreational activities and amenities, making it a popular destination for outdoor enthusiasts. It features three main campgrounds—Shady Lane, Bayside, and Elmview—as well as an overflow site, with facilities such as electric hookups, potable water, washrooms, showers, and sani-dumps. Visitors can enjoy a sandy beach for swimming, a boat launch, a picnic area, and a fish cleaning station. 

The park is also home to two main trails: the Great Trail, which is part of the Trans Canada Trail, and the Prairie View Nature Trail, offering opportunities for hiking and exploring the natural surroundings. Governed by Saskatchewan Parks, Danielson Provincial Park provides a variety of attractions for camping, swimming, and hiking enthusiasts.

trgt: El Camino High School is one of two high schools of the Oceanside Unified School District, located at 400 Rancho Del Oro Drive in Oceanside, California. Declared a California Distinguished School in the early 2000s, it was originally named "Oceanside High School East", and officially became El Camino High School in 1976. El Camino's Truax Theatre was built in the early 1980s and houses a large performance venue, an adjacent classroom with stage and large music and drama rooms. It also recently built a new Science and Technology Building and is undergoing constant construction to clean up the campus. El Camino maintains a close athletic rivalry with Oceanside High School. The ECHS mascot is the "Wildcat" and the school colors are brown and gold.
mgt:
El Camino High School is a public high school located in Oceanside, California, established in 1975. Part of the Oceanside Unified School District, the school serves nearly 3,000 students with a faculty of 144 and a student-to-teacher ratio of 26:1 as of the 2018–19 academic year. Its mascot is the Wildcat, and its colors are brown and gold. The school competes in the CIF San Diego Section's Avocado West League and maintains a rivalry with Oceanside High School.

The school has garnered media attention, notably in 1986 when a request to ban books on sorcery and satanism was rejected, and in 2008 for a controversial anti-drunk driving program involving a staged fake car accident to raise awareness among students.

trgt:
Raymond "La La" Lalonde (born October 22, 1940) is a former Republican member of the Louisiana House of Representatives from Carencro in Lafayette Parish. He formerly resided in the town of Sunset in St. Landry Parish. He served in the Louisiana House from 1980 to 1996.

In 2000, Lalonde then ran for Lafayette City-Parish Council. After that defeat, he switched affiliation to the Republican Party. He was defeated again in the November 17, 2007, general election for his former legislative seat by the Democrat Bobby G. Badon, a small businessman and a former member of the Carencro City Council. Badon polled 3,914 (52 percent) to Lalonde's 3,662 (48 percent) in a low-turnout general election. Badon said that crossover Republican voters helped to secure his Democratic victory and that he is committed to ending corrupt practices in Louisiana state government: "I have not sold my vote to any special interest group. When I get to Baton Rouge, I don't owe anyone any favors," he told the Lafayette Daily Advertiser. Incumbent Clara Baudoin was term-limited and hence ineligible to seek a fourth term. In the October 20 jungle primary Lalonde had led Badon, 4,352 (30 percent) to 4,028 (27 percent). Two other primary candidates, both Democrats, held a combined and critical 43 percent of the vote.
mgt:
Raymond "La La" Lalonde (born October 22, 1940) is an American politician, educator, and former member of the Louisiana House of Representatives, where he represented District 39 from 1980 to 1996. A native of Arnaudville, Louisiana, Lalonde served in the U.S. Air Force before earning degrees in mathematics, physics, and industrial technology. He worked in the aerospace industry and later became an educator, teaching and serving as director at T. H. Harris Technical College until his retirement in 2003.

During his legislative career, Lalonde was a prominent advocate for legalized gambling in Louisiana, sponsoring key legislation to establish the state lottery, riverboat casinos, and a land-based casino in New Orleans. He also supported cockfighting, citing its cultural significance in Cajun heritage, and introduced a bill to allow Cajuns to qualify for minority set-aside contracts. Lalonde's political career was marked by controversy, including allegations of campaign finance improprieties tied to gambling interests. After leaving office in 1996, he made unsuccessful bids to return to politics, including a 2007 campaign for his former House seat and a 2012 race for the Louisiana Republican State Central Committee.

trgt: Sir John Dinham (1359–1428) was a knight from Devonshire, England. His principal seats were at Hartland in North Devon, Kingskerswell and Nutwell in South Devon, Buckland Dinham in Somerset and Cardinham in Cornwall. He killed one of the murderers of his father in Exeter Cathedral, for which he was pardoned by the king. He later broke into Hartland Abbey and assaulted the Abbot over a long-standing disagreement, and also performed other acts of violence. He married three times; his heir was John Dinham (1406–1458). His monument survives in Kingskerswell parish church.
mgt: John Dinham (1359–1428) was an English landowner and member of the prominent Dinham family, which originated from Brittany and settled in Devon. He inherited extensive estates, including Hartland, Nutwell, and Kingskerswell, following the murder of his father in 1383. Known for his violent temperament, Dinham avenged his father's death by killing one of the murderers in Exeter Cathedral, for which he received a royal pardon but was ordered to perform penance. He was involved in numerous disputes, including conflicts with local abbots. Dinham married three times and was succeeded by his son, Sir John Dinham.

trgt:
"Like a Surgeon" is a song recorded by American recording artist Ciara. Written by The-Dream and produced by Tricky Stewart, the song is included on her third studio album, Fantasy Ride. It was planned to be released as the third single from the album in North America, but its release was cancelled. It still manage to chart on the lower half of US R&B charts.

The song's promotion began on MTV. During a commercial for the album, Ciara sang part of the song in a cappella.
mgt: "Like a Surgeon" is a song by American singer Ciara from her third studio album, *Fantasy Ride* (2009). Written by Christopher Stewart and Terius Nash, and produced by Tricky Stewart and The-Dream, the R&B track features metaphorical lyrics with a medical theme. Although planned as a single in North America, its release was canceled due to the album's poor sales. The song received mixed reviews from critics, with some praising its bold production and Ciara's delivery, while others criticized its metaphors and lack of a strong hook.

trgt:
Petaluma and Santa Rosa Railroad was a 600 volt DC electric interurban railway in Sonoma County, California, United States. It operated between the cities of Petaluma, Sebastopol, Forestville, and Santa Rosa. Company-owned steamboats provided service between Petaluma and San Francisco.

Portions of the original right-of-way have been acquired by Sonoma County government for the West County Trail and Joe Rodota Trail, managed by the Sonoma County Regional Parks Department. A portion of the original right-of-way can be found along the waterfront in Petaluma. Efforts have been made by volunteers to re-activate this line to become a trolley line once again.
mgt: The Petaluma and Santa Rosa Railroad (P&SR) was an interurban electric railroad that operated in Sonoma County, California, from 1904 to 1932. Formed through the consolidation of several horse car lines in 1903, it connected Petaluma, Santa Rosa, Sebastopol, and Forestville, with ferry service to San Francisco. The railroad faced significant challenges, including the "Battle of Sebastopol Road," a conflict with a competing steam railroad over track crossings. Despite early expansion and success, including freight and passenger services, the P&SR declined due to competition and economic shifts. It was acquired by the Northwestern Pacific Railroad in 1932, with operations ceasing by the mid-20th century.

trgt: Andy González (born on January 1, 1951 in Manhattan New York) was a jazz double bassist of Puerto Rican descent recognized as was one of the innovators of Latin Jazz."González was a versatile player, as well as an arranger, composer, music historian and producer of other musicians’ records. He embraced African, Cuban and Puerto Rican styles, various strains of jazz and other influences, often merging them into something fresh."Raised in the Bronx, he played violin in grammar school and later picked up the bass after taking lessons with jazz bassist Steve Swallow from 5th to 8th grade, thereafter he attended the High School of Music & Art. "Andy González came to the public's attention playing for future NEA Jazz Master Ray Barretto's band, while he was still a student at Music & Art High School. Although it was a salsa group in the Cuban conjunto trumpet tradition, Barretto treated the group like a jazz combo, featuring all the players as soloists."
mgt:
Andy González (January 1, 1951 – April 9, 2020) was an American bassist and prominent figure in Afro-Cuban jazz, salsa, and Latin music. Born in New York City, he was a prolific musician known for his innovative contributions to the tumbao, the rhythmic foundation of Cuban and Puerto Rican music. González co-founded several influential bands, including Conjunto Libre, Grupo Folklórico y Experímental Nuevayorquíno, and The Fort Apache Band, often collaborating with his brother, Jerry González. These groups blended salsa, jazz, and traditional Afro-Caribbean music, producing acclaimed albums such as *Concepts in Unity* (1975) and *Lo Dice Todo* (1976).

Throughout his career, González worked with legendary artists like Tito Puente, Eddie Palmieri, Ray Barretto, and Dizzy Gillespie, and participated in hundreds of recording sessions. In 2016, he released his first solo album, *Entre Colegas*, which was nominated for a Grammy Award. González died in the Bronx in 2020 from pneumonia and complications of diabetes.

trgt: The Vidarbha movement includes political activities organised by various individuals, organizations and political parties, for creation of a separate state of Vidarbha, within the republic of India, with Nagpur as the capital. The proposed state corresponds to the eastern 11 districts of the state of Maharashtra. It makes up for 31% of area and 21% of population of the present state of Maharashtra. The area is covered by thick tropical forests and is surplus in electricity, minerals, rice and cotton.
mgt: The Vidarbha movement is a long-standing demand for the creation of a separate state of Vidarbha, comprising the eastern region of Maharashtra, India. The demand dates back over a century, with the Central Provinces legislature passing a resolution for a separate "Mahavidarbha" state in 1938. Despite recommendations by the State Reorganization Commission in 1956 for a separate Vidarbha state, it was merged with Maharashtra in 1960. The movement has persisted due to perceived regional neglect, economic disparities, and political marginalization, with support from various political groups and organizations.

trgt:
Ariyalur (IPA-ta) is a town and district headquarters of Ariyalur district in the South Indian state of Tamil Nadu and is rich in limestone, surrounded with seven cement factories and two sugar factories. The town is located at a distance of 310 km (190 mi) from the state capital Chennai.

Ariyalur was a part of the erstwhile Trichirapally District until India's independence in 1947 and Tiruchirappalli district until 1995, Perambalur district until 2007 and subsequently a part of the newly formed Ariyalur district. The town is a part of the fertile Cauvery Delta and the major profession in the town is agriculture.

Ariyalur is administered by a municipality established in 1994. As of 2011, the municipality covered an area of 7.62 km2 (2.94 sq mi) and had a population of 28,902. Ariyalur comes under the Ariyalur assembly constituency which elects a member to the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly once every five years and it is a part of the Chidambaram constituency which elects its Member of Parliament (MP) once in five years. Roadways are the major mode of transportation to the town and it also has rail connectivity which acts as a main station after Tiruchirappalli junction. The nearest seaport is Karaikal port, located 95 km (59 mi) away, while the nearest airport is the Tiruchirappalli International Airport, located 76 km (47 mi) away from the town.
mgt:
Ariyalur is a town and the administrative headquarters of Ariyalur district in the state of Tamil Nadu, India. Located in the Chola Nadu region, it is historically significant as part of the Chola dynasty, with Gangai Konda Cholapuram serving as the capital during the late Chola period. The town later came under the rule of the Vijayanagar Nayaks, Marathas, and eventually the British. Ariyalur is known for its rich limestone resources, which support a thriving cement industry, with major companies like UltraTech Cement and Dalmia Cement operating in the area. Agriculture, particularly sugarcane, cashew, and rice cultivation, also plays a significant role in the local economy.

Ariyalur has a hot semi-arid climate and is connected by National Highway NH136 and a railway station on the Chennai–Tiruchirappalli line. The municipality, established in 1943, oversees local governance and infrastructure, including water supply, waste management, and healthcare. As of the 2011 census, the town had a population of 28,902, with a literacy rate of 76.04%. Ariyalur is part of the Chidambaram Lok Sabha constituency and the Ariyalur State Assembly constituency. The town is also home to historical landmarks, including temples built during the Vijayanagar period, and serves as a hub for transportation and commerce in the region.

trgt:
The 2020–21 Nepal Tri-Nation Series was a Twenty20 international cricket tournament that took place in April 2021 in Nepal. The participating teams were Nepal, Malaysia and the Netherlands. The matches were all played at the Tribhuvan University International Cricket Ground in Kirtipur. The tournament was played in a double round-robin format, followed by a final between the top two sides.

The penultimate round-robin match, between Malaysia and the Netherlands, ended in a tie. It was a rain-curtailed game with no Super Over taking place due to the lost time. This result meant that the Netherlands and Nepal advanced to the final. Nepal defeated the Netherlands in the final by 142 runs to win the series.
mgt: The 2020–21 Nepal Tri-Nation Series was a Twenty20 International (T20I) cricket tournament held in Nepal from 17 to 24 April 2021. The series featured the national teams of Nepal, the Netherlands, and Malaysia. Nepal emerged as the tournament champions, with Kushal Bhurtel named Player of the Series for his outstanding performances. The Netherlands fielded a squad missing several key players due to county and domestic commitments, while Malaysia included experienced players such as captain Ahmad Faiz and vice-captain Virandeep Singh. Nepal's squad saw the absence of senior players Basant Regmi and Sharad Vesawkar, and Paras Khadka was ruled out due to injury just before the tournament, replaced by Sundeep Jora.

trgt:
In an international context Denmark is viewed as a somewhat peculiar country when it comes to internet access. The former state owned telephone company TDC owns the entire last mile infrastructure in terms of copper telephone lines and the vast majority of the coaxial cable infrastructure as well. Even though the Danish telecommunications infrastructure is very heavily dominated by one company, Danish internet customers still enjoy fair prices and a wide availability of different next generation access internet connections in comparison with most other EU countries. Furthermore, TDCs de facto monopoly on last mile infrastructure has come under attack. In the last decade regional power companies have formed national business alliances aimed at implementing FTTH for private and business end users.

In 2012, Denmark was ranked third by OECD in terms of wired broadband subscriptions per 100 inhabitants (see the bar chart below). The same year 99,9 % of all households and companies were able to connect to the internet via a broadband connection of at least 2 Mbit/s. As of 2015, 1.3 million Danish households are connected to the internet via TDC's coax and fiber, all of whom will soon have the opportunity to receive one gigabit per second connection.

In 2012, Denmark performed poorly in terms of next generation access in rural areas compared to several other countries in the EU and the US.
mgt:
The Internet in Denmark is characterized by widespread access, high speeds, and a competitive market. Denmark was an early adopter of internet technology, with the establishment of the Nordic university network NORDUnet in 1988 and the Danish Internet Exchange Point (DIX) in 1994. Broadband access began in 1996 with cable internet, followed by the introduction of ADSL in 1999. By 2012, xDSL connections were available to 98% of households, with TDC dominating the market.

Denmark has also developed extensive mobile broadband infrastructure, with near-universal 3G coverage and 4G coverage reaching 65% by 2012. Fiber-to-the-premises (FTTP) has expanded significantly, driven by investments from local power companies, with 43% of households having access by 2013. However, the rollout in Copenhagen has been limited due to TDC's acquisition of DONG Energy's fiber network.

DOCSIS 3 technology contributes significantly to next-generation access, covering 62% of households, though it is less prevalent in rural areas. WiMAX networks have also been deployed, primarily in underserved regions, though their presence has diminished in recent years.

Denmark has minimal internet censorship, with isolated cases of site blocking for copyright infringement. The competitive market includes major providers like TDC, Stofa, and FIBIA, alongside smaller players. Denmark's internet infrastructure reflects a blend of advanced technology and regulatory efforts to ensure widespread access.

trgt:
Cavaliere Modesto Marini (born in Lake Garda, Italy) is an Italian chef, restaurateur and entrepreneur. He is the Founder of The Marini's Group which includes Marini's on 57 (Malaysia's highest rooftop bar, lounge and contemporary Italian restaurant), Marble 8 (Kuala Lumpur's premier steakhouse specializing in producing its own dry-aged steak); and M Marini Caffè, Malaysia's first dedicated champagne and caviar cafe.

Cavaliere Modesto Marini was awarded the prestigious Ordine Della Stella D’Italia (Knight of the Order of the Star of Italy) award in October 2016, which carries the title “Cavaliere” and is one of the highest civilian honors of the Italian government.

It recognizes Italians living abroad or foreigners who have been instrumental in the preservation and promotion of national prestige abroad, who have made important contributions to relations between their country and Italy. It is awarded in many areas, including philanthropic activities, scientific and technological research, commercial enterprises and the promotion of Italian food and wine.
mgt:
Cav. Modesto Marini is an Italian chef, restaurateur, and entrepreneur, known for his contemporary Italian cuisine and as the founder of The Marini's Group. Born near Lake Garda, Italy, he began his culinary career as a teenager, training professionally and working at prestigious establishments like Cecconi's in London. After moving to Singapore and later Malaysia in 1995, he established the successful Modesto's chain of Italian restaurants, which expanded across Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia.

In 2012, Marini launched Marini's on 57, Malaysia's highest rooftop bar, restaurant, and lounge, located in Kuala Lumpur. He later opened M Marini Caffè, a champagne and caviar café, and Marble 8, a premium steakhouse. His establishments have hosted numerous celebrities and events, including Formula 1 parties and the Laureus World Sports Awards after-party. Marini has also collaborated on music projects and received several prestigious awards, including the Hospitality Asia Platinum Awards' Icon of the Year and the Seven Stars Global Luxury Award.

trgt:
Daniël Termont (born 19 May 1953) is a Belgian politician, member of the Socialist Party and former mayor of Ghent, Belgium. He was the son of shopkeepers who lived in Mariakerke. His mother's family was strongly socialistically engaged. In 1971, Daniel obtained a degree in accounting at the Stedelijk Instituut voor Handel en Secretariaat. He did his military service as from April 1972 until April 1973 and worked later on for ten years as a federal charge of LTA (Mutuality of Young Workers), the youth organization of the Socialist Unions at that time. Until 1980 he was also an information officer at the East Flemish Division (Bond Moyson) in the area Ghent-Eeklo. He has been elected to the city council since 1976. Later on, from 1983 until 1988, he was also director of De Ceder holiday centre in Deinze. He was the first director of the centre after a renovation which followed after many years of decline of the institution. Subsequently, from 1988 to 1995, he was secretary-treasurer of Bond Moyson, the year he took his first full-time political office as a councilor in Ghent. From 1995 until 2006, he was alderman of the harbour (Port of Ghent). Between 2001 and 2006 he was also alderman of festivities.

After the municipal elections of 2006 he became mayor of Ghent. He was succeeded on January 1 2019 by Mathias De Clercq of Open VLD.
mgt:
Daniël Termont (born 19 May 1953) is a Belgian politician who served as the mayor of Ghent from 2007 to 2018, succeeding Frank Beke. A member of the Socialist Party (SP.a), Termont began his political career at the age of 14, becoming active in the socialist movement and later holding leadership roles in local socialist organizations. He was first elected as a city councillor in Ghent in 1976 and served continuously in that role. In 1995, he became an alderman, overseeing the port, economic development, and utilities, and was re-elected in subsequent municipal elections. In 2007, he became mayor after leading the SP.a-Spirit cartel to victory in the 2006 elections.

In addition to his role as mayor, Termont held numerous mandates in the energy and infrastructure sectors, including vice president of Fluxys and FARYS, and president of Publigas and the Vlaamse Energieholding. He was also vice president and board member of the Port of Ghent and a supporter and board member of the football club AA Gent. Internationally, he served as vice-chairman of Eurocities in 2014. Termont gained recognition for his leadership, finishing second in the 2014 World Mayor competition. Throughout his career, he received several decorations for his contributions to public service.

trgt:
KWJC (91.9 FM) is a classical music station operated by the University of Missouri-Kansas City. The station covers much of the Kansas City Metropolitan Area.

Between 1974 and 2006, KWJC was a conventional college radio station, operated by William Jewell College in suburban Liberty, Missouri, with a student staff. After that time, KWJC aired the national K-LOVE and Air1 formats from the Educational Media Foundation. When EMF bought a Kansas City translator (K300CH 107.9) and relocated Air1 there, KWJC went temporarily silent on May 31, 2019. On July 1, 2020, UMKC assumed control of KWJC and the station returned to the air with a classical music format.
mgt: KWJC (91.9 FM) is a non-commercial radio station licensed to Liberty, Missouri, serving the Kansas City metropolitan area with a classical music format. Owned by the University of Missouri-Kansas City (UMKC) and branded as "91.9 Classical KC," the station originally launched in 1974 as KWPB under the ownership of William Jewell College. Initially an educational station with student-led programming, it underwent several format changes over the decades, including alternative and adult contemporary music. In 2019, UMKC acquired KWJC for $2 million, relaunching it in 2020 as a full-time classical music station, restoring the format to Kansas City's FM airwaves.

trgt:
The 500-series format is a standardized format for a modular signal processor system consisting of a combination of 500-series modules installed within a 500-series chassis (or rack), with the chassis providing power and audio connections for the individual modules. Originally invented by Automated Processes, Inc. co-founder Saul Walker, today numerous companies manufacture 500-series format products.

The modular nature of the 500-series format allows individual modules to be combined to create a customized signal processing chain, with individual modules added or changed as budget allows. Many 500-series chassis also offer portability, allowing a recording engineer to bring their desired signal processing chain to any recording session.
mgt:
The 500-series format is a modular audio equipment standard widely used in professional recording studios. It originated in 1967 when Lou Lindauer and Saul Walker designed a modular mixing console for Apostolic Recording Studio in New York City. Walker's design became the foundation of the 500-series format, and the two co-founded Automated Processes, Inc. (API) in 1969. API developed iconic 500-series modules, including preamplifiers, compressors, and equalizers. 

The format gained popularity in the 1970s when engineers began repurposing modules from API consoles. API later introduced the Lunchbox, a portable chassis for 500-series modules. By 2020, the format had expanded significantly, with contributions from numerous manufacturers.

page_title: New South Wales State Heritage Register
trgt:
The New South Wales State Heritage Register, also known as NSW State Heritage Register, is a heritage list of places in the state of New South Wales, Australia, that are protected by New South Wales legislation, generally covered by the Heritage Act 1977 and its 2010 amendments. The register is administered by the Heritage Council of NSW via Heritage NSW, a division of the Government of New South Wales Department of Planning and Environment.

The register was created in 1999 and includes items protected by heritage schedules that relate to the State, and to regional and to local environmental plans. As a result, the register contains over 20,000 statutory-listed items in either public or private ownership of historical, cultural, and architectural value. Of those items listed, approximately 1,785 items are listed as significant items for the whole of New South Wales; with the remaining items of local or regional heritage value. The items include buildings, objects, monuments, Aboriginal places, gardens, bridges, landscapes, archaeological sites, shipwrecks, relics, bridges, streets, industrial structures and conservation precincts.

Typically, an item will first attract local listing, then regional or State listing. If the item is of significance to the nation, the State will advocate for listing on the Australian National Heritage List or the Commonwealth Heritage List. Finally, if the item is of global significance, the Australian Government will advocate for the item to be listed on the UNESCO World Heritage List.
mgt:
The New South Wales State Heritage Register is an official list of heritage sites in New South Wales, Australia, encompassing both natural and cultural heritage. Established on 2 April 1999, the register is maintained by Heritage NSW and the Heritage Council of New South Wales, a statutory body appointed by the state government. The Heritage Council, which includes representatives from the community, government, conservation professionals, and organizations like the National Trust of Australia, advises the Minister for Environment and Heritage and recommends places and objects for inclusion on the register.

The register operates under the provisions of the Heritage Act 1977 and its 2010 amendments, which require government agencies to compile and manage a register of heritage assets under their care. Aboriginal heritage is also protected under the National Parks and Wildlife Act 1974. Items nominated for the State Heritage Register are assessed against specific criteria to determine their significance, with eligibility requiring that they meet at least one of these criteria. The register aims to ensure the preservation and protection of sites and objects deemed significant to the people of New South Wales. Elizabeth Farm was the first item inscribed on the register.

trgt:
Zach Gage is an indie video game developer based in New York City and best known for his iOS games, including SpellTower.

Gage learned to code throughout his youth and studied art at Skidmore College and Parsons School of Design, where he created installation and interactive works. With the 2008 opening of Apple's App Store, Gage created multiple apps and games, including the word games SpellTower (2011), TypeShift (2017), and collaborative work on Vlambeer's 2013 Ridiculous Fishing and Bennett Foddy's 2017 Getting Over It iOS port. Gage also created multiple games based on playing card and board games, including Really Bad Chess (2016).
mgt: Zach Gage (born 1985) is an American video game developer and artist known for creating innovative and experimental games, including the popular word game *SpellTower*. Raised in Westchester, New York, Gage developed an early interest in game design and programming, which he pursued alongside his studies in art at Skidmore College and later at Parsons School of Design, where he earned a Master of Fine Arts degree. His work spans conceptual art projects, such as the controversial *Lose/Lose*, and successful mobile games like *Ridiculous Fishing*, *Sage Solitaire*, and *Really Bad Chess*. Gage is recognized for blending creativity and technology, and he remains based in New York.

trgt:
The 2017 Big 12 Conference football season was the 22nd season of Big 12 Conference football, taking place during the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season. The season began with non-conference play on Saturday, September 2, 2017, when all 10 of the league's teams played their first non-conference opponents. Big 12 Conference play began on Saturday, September 23, 2017.

The 2017 season was the sixth for the Big 12 since the 2010–13 Big 12 Conference realignment brought the Big 12 membership to its current form. The conference had ten members: Baylor, Iowa State, Kansas, Kansas State, Oklahoma, Oklahoma State, TCU, Texas, Texas Tech and West Virginia. The Big 12 is a Power Five conference under the College Football Playoff format, along with the ACC, the Big Ten, the Pac-12 and the SEC.

As a ten-team league, the Big 12 will played a nine-game, round-robin conference schedule. Each member will played three non-conference games–one of was required to be against another Power Five conference foe. The regular season was followed by a conference championship game played between the regular season champion and regular season runner-up. The 2017 Big 12 Championship Game was held Saturday, December 2, 2017, at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.
mgt:
The 2017 Big 12 Conference football season was the 22nd season of Big 12 football, part of the NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision. The season began on September 2, 2017, and concluded with the Big 12 Championship Game on December 2, 2017. The conference consisted of 10 teams, with games broadcast on ABC, FOX, ESPN, FS1, FSN, and LHN. Oklahoma was selected as the preseason favorite in the media poll and went on to win the regular season championship. The Sooners were led by quarterback Baker Mayfield, who was later selected as the first overall pick in the 2018 NFL Draft by the Cleveland Browns.

The season featured weekly matchups, culminating in the Big 12 Championship Game, and included non-conference games against Power Five opponents. Big 12 teams also participated in several bowl games, with rankings based on the College Football Playoff (CFP) rankings. Individual players received weekly and postseason honors, including Consensus All-American selections. The season also highlighted home game attendance figures and academic achievements. The 2017 Big 12 season showcased competitive play and significant individual performances, solidifying the conference's reputation in college football.

trgt: The Prussian Quartets are a set of three string quartets, nos. 21–23 (K. 575, 589, 590), nicknamed 'Prussian', composed by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart between 1789–90 and dedicated to the King of Prussia, Friedrich Wilhelm II. They were posthumously published in December 1791 by Artaria as Op. 18, only a few weeks after Mozart's death. Although not considered on a par with the earlier, more famous'Haydn Quartets'(K. 387, 421, 428, 458, 464, 465), these pieces are noted for their beauty, equilibrium amongst the instruments and the exceptional prominence and cantabile nature of the cello part (the instrument played by the king himself). These are also the only pieces in this genre that Mozart composed'to order', i.e. as a commission from a royal patron.
mgt: The *Prussian Quartets* are a set of three string quartets (K. 575, K. 589, and K. 590) composed by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart in 1789–1790. They are notable for their prominent cello parts, earning them the nickname "Concertante Quartets." Mozart composed these works while also writing a sonata for Princess Friederike, daughter of King Friedrich Wilhelm II of Prussia, who was an amateur cellist. Although Mozart initially intended to dedicate the quartets to the King, he withheld the dedication after not receiving the promised fees. The title "Prussian Quartets" originates from Mozart's private thematic catalogue. The quartets were well-received, with their publication announced enthusiastically in the *Wiener Zeitung*.

trgt:
The Prosecution Ministry (Spanish: Ministerio Fiscal) is a constitutional body (Part VI § 124) integrated into the Judiciary of Spain, but with full autonomy. It is entrusted with defending the rule of law, the rights of the citizens, and public interest, as well as watching over the independence of the courts of justice.

The requirements to enter into a career of prosecution are a public exam that can only be taken by those with a law degree who meet the required capacity requirements (EOMF § 42). The exam for admission to the judicial and prosecution careers are connected, so that anyone who passes the tests must proceed to the entry position of their chosen career. Those who choose prosecution must complete a training course at the Center for Legal Studies, after which they become a prosecutor by swearing the corresponding oath and taking possession of their jurisdiction (EOMF § 45).
mgt:
The Prosecution Ministry of Spain (Ministerio Fiscal) is a constitutional body responsible for promoting the enforcement of justice, defending the rule of law, protecting citizens' rights and freedoms, and ensuring the independence of the judiciary. Established in 1835, it operates under the principles of unity, hierarchical subordination, rule of law, and impartiality, as outlined in the Spanish Constitution and the Organic Regulation of the Prosecution Ministry (EOMF). The Attorney General serves as its highest authority, overseeing its operations across the country.

Historically, the Prosecution Ministry evolved from a state-level prosecution organization in the 19th century to a more autonomous institution following Spain's transition to democracy and the 1978 Constitution. It regained its independence and expanded its role to include safeguarding both state and citizen interests. Headquartered in Madrid, the Ministry is a key component of Spain's judicial system, functioning independently from the General Council of the Judiciary.

trgt:
The 1991 State of Origin series saw the tenth time the annual three-match State of Origin series between the New South Wales and Queensland representative rugby league teams was played entirely under 'state of origin' selection rules. It was notable as Wally Lewis'farewell from Origin football and featured his half-time stoush with Mark Geyer in Game II which match culminated in Michael O'Connor's sensational match-winning sideline conversion in teeming rain.

For the first time since State of Origin became a three-game series in 1982, Queensland named the same starting XIII for each game of the series. The Maroons only made two changes during the series, with Origin veterans Dale Shearer coming onto the bench for Game 2 in Sydney to replace Steve Renouf, and Bob Lindner, the player of the 1990 Kangaroo tour, named on the bench for Game 3 in Brisbane (replacing Gavin Allen) after recovering from a broken leg. This saw Queensland only use 19 players for the series. In contrast, NSW only had seven players there for the entire series (five of whom started each game) and used a total of 24 players.
mgt:
The 1991 State of Origin series was the 10th annual best-of-three rugby league series between Queensland and New South Wales, with Queensland claiming their seventh title by winning the series 2–1. The series was marked by intense competition, dramatic moments, and the retirement of Queensland captain Wally Lewis from State of Origin football. Queensland won the opening game 6–4 in a nail-biting contest, with Mal Meninga scoring the decisive try. New South Wales leveled the series in Game II with a 14–12 victory in a rain-soaked and violent match, remembered for a brawl involving Mark Geyer and Wally Lewis. The decider in Brisbane saw Queensland triumph 14–12, with Dale Shearer scoring the match-tying try and Meninga converting from the sideline to secure the win.

The series also featured notable performances, including Paul Hauff's debut for Queensland and Lewis's emotional farewell, announced during Game III after learning of his daughter's diagnosis of deafness. The 1991 series remains one of the most memorable in State of Origin history for its drama, physicality, and iconic moments.

trgt: Phoebe Forrester is a fictional character from the CBS Daytime soap opera The Bold and the Beautiful. The first actress to play the character on a contract basis was Addison Hoover, who joined the show in April 2005 and left the following year. From July 11, 2006 to December 9 2008, Phoebe was played by MacKenzie Mauzy, until the character was killed off. Phoebe and her twin sister, Steffy (Jacqueline MacInnes Wood), were born in September 1999 to Ridge Forrester (Ronn Moss) and Taylor Hayes (Hunter Tylo). As a result of constant SORASing, Phoebe was seventeen by 2006. The character has been described as an "ill-fated beauty".
mgt: Phoebe Forrester is a fictional character from the American soap opera *The Bold and the Beautiful*, portrayed by MacKenzie Mauzy from 2006 to 2008, following a series of child actors. Introduced in 1999 as the daughter of Ridge and Taylor Forrester, Phoebe was part of a prominent family storyline. The character experienced significant aging over the years and was involved in dramatic plots, including a controversial romance with her uncle Rick Forrester, a love triangle with singer Constantine Parros, and her tragic death in a car accident in 2008. Phoebe's storylines often explored themes of family loyalty, romance, and personal growth.

trgt: Sri Venkatesa Perumal Temple (Melathiruppathi) – Mondipalayam is counted among the noted Vishnu temples of Kongunadu. The image of the lord is flat on the four sides and conical in the center. The form was first discovered by one Kondamanaicker around 300 years ago. The devotee once sighted his cow shedding her milk on a sandhill. Induced by curiosity, he dug it, when manifested the image with Conch (Sanguchakkram) and Disc (Chakkram) as well as Saligram and Chikandi (weapon). Inspired by the divine revolution, he ordered a temple to be built up for Sri Venketachalapathi enshrining the form. Devotees who have taken vows to make offering to the lord of the seven hills can fulfill them in Mondipalayam, in the event of their inability to go to Tirupati.
mgt:
Sri Venkatesa Perumal Temple, also known as Melathiruppathi, is a 16th-century Hindu temple located in Mondipalayam, Tirupur district, Tamil Nadu, India. Dedicated to Sri Venkatesa Perumal (Lord Balaji), the temple is renowned for its Kongunadu-style architecture and its serene spiritual ambiance. The main deity, a Swayambu (self-manifested) idol, is enshrined in the sanctum sanctorum, offering divine darshan to devotees.

The temple hosts several significant rituals and festivals, including the Thirukalyana Utsavam, Satyanarayana Puja, and the grand Purattasi festival, which attracts devotees from across India. The annual temple chariot festival and other vibrant celebrations further enhance its cultural and religious significance. Managed by hereditary trustees and the Tamil Nadu HR and CE department, the temple remains a prominent center of devotion and community activities in the region.

trgt:
Slaughter Studios is a 2002 comedy/horror film. It was directed by Brian Katkin, with the screenplay by John Huckert and Dan Acre, from a story by Huckert, Acre, and Damian Akhavi. The film, 86 minutes long and rated R, was released through New Concorde.

It was the last film shot at Roger Corman's studios in Venice Beach, California, and it only had a 12-day shooting schedule. The studio was being torn down during the production.

The film began life as a remake of Slumber Party Massacre, and writers John Huckert and Dan Acre were hired to pen the script. But after visiting the soon-to-be-demolished studio they came up with this idea instead. Producer Damien Akhavi helped them write the story. Footage from the original Slumber Party Massacre can still be seen during a murder sequence as an in-joke for the production crew.

Though principal photography took place in February 2001, the final sequences were not filmed until July 2002. The long break was necessitated by the fact that the scenes were to be shot in Malibu, but the rainfall was unusually heavy, so the completion of the film was postponed. Director Katkin shot another film during the break in this movie's production.
mgt:
*Slaughter Studios* is a 2002 American horror-comedy film directed by Brian Katkin and written by Dan Acre, John Huckert, and Damian Akhavi. The film follows a group of film students who break into an abandoned movie studio, infamous for a tragic on-set death decades earlier, to shoot a low-budget horror film before the building is demolished. As the students race against time to complete their project, they are stalked by a mysterious killer, leading to a series of gruesome murders.

The film blends slasher tropes with dark humor, featuring an ensemble cast that includes Nicolas Read, Amy Shelton-White, Tara Killian, and Darren Keefe. The story culminates in a twist revealing the killer's identity and a supernatural connection to the studio's haunted past. An alternate ending, which was ultimately unused, would have framed the events as part of a "film within a film."

*Slaughter Studios* was distributed by New Concorde and released on December 31, 2002. It runs for 86 minutes and is noted for its self-referential humor and homage to classic horror films. The movie explores themes of ambition, exploitation, and the blurred lines between reality and fiction in the filmmaking process.

trgt:
YourTV (formerly TVCogeco and CogecoTV) is the brand of community channels owned by Cogeco. YourTV broadcasts into the Canadian provinces of Ontario and Quebec. Some channels broadcast in both the English and French languages, often on separate channels, in which case the French-language station is branded NousTV.

Programming the channels is produced with the assistance of volunteers. This programming was created in response to CRTC regulations which previously required that cable companies produce local content (this is no longer required but strongly encouraged)..
mgt: YourTV is a Canadian community channel owned by Cogeco Connexion, operating in Ontario and Quebec. Formerly known as TVCogeco and CogecoTV, it provides local programming, including community access shows, public service announcements, and an electronic bulletin board service. In Ontario, YourTV broadcasts Ontario Hockey League games, while in Quebec, it airs Quebec Major Junior Hockey League games in partnership with Vidéotron. Available exclusively to Cogeco Cable subscribers, YourTV also offers programming through Cogeco's "Free On Demand" service for digital cable users.

trgt:
The Kuban is a river in Russia that flows through the Western Caucasus and drains into the Sea of Azov. The Kuban runs mostly through Krasnodar Krai for 660 kilometres (410 mi), but also in the Karachay–Cherkess Republic, Stavropol Krai and the Republic of Adygea.

The Kuban flows 870 kilometres (540 mi) north and west from its source near Mount Elbrus in the Caucasus Mountains, eventually reaching Temryuk Bay in the Sea of Azov. It is navigable up to Krasnodar. Major cities on the river are Karachayevsk, Cherkessk, Nevinnomyssk, Armavir, Novokubansk, Kropotkin, Ust-Labinsk, Krasnodar and Temryuk. Despite its name, Slavyansk-na-Kubani lies not on the Kuban River, but on its distributary the Protoka.
mgt: The Kuban River is a major waterway in Eastern Europe, flowing through Russia from its source in the Caucasus Mountains to its mouth in the Sea of Azov. Spanning 870 kilometers (540 miles), it has a basin area of 57,900 square kilometers (22,400 square miles). The river originates on Mount Elbrus and forms a diverse landscape, including gorges, floodplains, and a vast delta. Its delta, designated as a Ramsar Wetland, supports rich biodiversity, including endemic fish species and migratory birds. Historically significant, the Kuban River was central to the settlement of the Kuban Cossacks and remains vital for agriculture, fisheries, and navigation, particularly in the Krasnodar region.

trgt: The Apostles'Fast, also called the Fast of the Holy Apostles, the Fast of Peter and Paul, or sometimes St. Peter's Fast, is a fast observed by Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, Eastern Catholic, and Reformed Orthodox Christians. In the Byzantine tradition, the Fast begins on the second Monday after Pentecost (the day after All Saints' Sunday), whereas in the Coptic and old Syriac traditions, the Fast begins on the first Monday after Pentecost. It continues until the Feast of Saints Peter and Paul on June 29. Traditionally, its duration varies from eight to forty-two days because of the moveable nature of Pascha. However, in Eastern Orthodox Churches that follow the Revised Julian calendar, the fast can be as long as 29 days, or may not occur at all in some years.
mgt:
The Apostles' Fast is a period of fasting and prayer observed in various Christian traditions, particularly in the Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, and Eastern Catholic Churches. It commemorates the preparation of the Apostles for their missionary work following the Ascension of Jesus Christ and the Feast of Pentecost. Rooted in Sacred Tradition and scriptural references, the fast is seen as a time of thanksgiving for the Apostles' endurance and dedication.

The fast begins on the Monday after the Sunday of All Saints and ends on June 29, the Feast of Saints Peter and Paul. Its duration varies depending on the date of Pascha (Easter). The fasting rules are less strict than those of Great Lent, with variations across traditions. Oriental Churches, such as the Coptic, Syriac, and Maronite Churches, observe modified versions of the fast, with differing durations and practices.

trgt: The Mizoram Peace Accord, 1986 was an official agreement between the Government of India and the Mizo National Front (MNF) to end insurgency and violence in Mizoram, India, that started in 1966. The Mizo National Front was an organisation of Mizo secessionists led by Laldenga to fight for independence from India. The movement was basically due to lack of support from the government during the great famine (called Mautam) in Mizoram in the late 1950s. Political insurgency and social unrest ensued in the next decades. After a number of negotiations, the document titled Mizoram Accord, 1986: Memorandum of Settlement was finally signed on 30 June 1986. It was signed by Laldenga for MNF, R.D. Pradhan, Home Secretary, Government of India, and Lalkhama, Chief Secretary, Government of Mizoram. It is remarked as the most and only successful peace agreement in India after its independence from British Empire in 1947.
mgt:
The Mizoram Peace Accord, signed on June 30, 1986, in New Delhi, India, marked the end of two decades of insurgency led by the Mizo National Front (MNF) against the Indian government. The conflict stemmed from grievances over the integration of the Mizo people into Assam, neglect during the 1959 famine, and demands for independence or a unified "Greater Mizoram." The MNF, under Laldenga, declared independence in 1966, leading to widespread violence and military intervention.

Negotiations for peace, facilitated by church leaders and social organizations, began in the 1970s and culminated in the accord. The agreement required the MNF to renounce violence and adhere to Indian laws. In return, Mizoram was granted statehood on February 20, 1987, with Laldenga becoming its first Chief Minister. The accord is celebrated annually as "Remna Ni" (Peace Day) and is considered a model for resolving insurgencies in India.

trgt: Alfred Lubbock (31 October 1845 – 17 July 1916) was an English insurance underwriter and banker. He is best known as an amateur cricketer who played first-class cricket for a variety of sides including Kent County Cricket Club and the Marylebone Cricket Club between 1863 and 1875. He was considered to be one of the best batsman of his era, comparable to WG Grace, and also played association football, playing for Old Etonians in the 1875 FA Cup Final.
mgt: Alfred Lubbock (31 October 1845 – 17 July 1916) was an English cricketer and footballer. A right-handed batsman, he played 28 first-class cricket matches between 1863 and 1875, primarily for Kent County Cricket Club and the Marylebone Cricket Club (MCC). Known for his batting skill, he scored two centuries and was considered one of the best batsmen of his era. Lubbock also played football, appearing for Old Etonians in the 1875 FA Cup Final replay. He later worked as an underwriter and banker, authoring *Memories of Eton and Etonians* in 1899.

trgt:
Apple juice is a fruit juice made by the maceration and pressing of an apple. The resulting expelled juice may be further treated by enzymatic and centrifugal clarification to remove the starch and pectin, which holds fine particulate in suspension, and then pasteurized for packaging in glass, metal, or aseptic processing system containers, or further treated by dehydration processes to a concentrate.

Due to the complex and costly equipment required to extract and clarify juice from apples in large volume, apple juice is normally produced commercially. In the United States, unfiltered fresh apple juice is made by smaller operations in areas of high apple production, in the form of unclarified apple cider. Apple juice is one of the most common fruit juices globally, with world production led by China, Poland, the United States, and Germany.
mgt:
Apple juice is a beverage made by pressing and juicing apples, commonly harvested between September and mid-November in the Northern Hemisphere and February to mid-April in the Southern Hemisphere. Typically made from cultivars like McIntosh apples, the juice is filtered to remove solid particles, distinguishing it from apple cider. It is often pasteurized to reduce the risk of foodborne illnesses caused by pathogens such as *E. coli* and *Salmonella*. Unpasteurized apple juice has been linked to outbreaks of illness in North America.

Nutritionally, apple juice is 88% water and 11% carbohydrates, with negligible protein or fat. It provides 46 calories per 100 ml. Fresh apple juice requires refrigeration, while sealed bottles can be stored in cool, dark places. In some regions, "apple cider" refers to unfiltered juice, while in others, it denotes an alcoholic beverage.

trgt: The Capture of Santiago was a military engagement that took place between 11 and 28 November 1585 during the newly declared Anglo-Spanish War. (Santiago is the largest island of the Cape Verde archipelago.) An English expedition led by Francis Drake captured the port town of Cidade Velha in the Cape Verde islands that had recently belonged to the Crown of Portugal. He sacked it and then marched inland before doing the same at São Domingos and Praia. Afterwards Drake left and continued his expedition to successfully raid the Spanish possessions in the Americas.
mgt: The **Capture of Santiago** occurred from November 11 to 28, 1585, during the Anglo-Spanish War, when an English fleet led by Sir Francis Drake seized the island of Santiago in Cape Verde, then under Spanish control. The English forces, numbering around 1,000 men, captured the town of Cidade Velha, looted its resources, and destroyed its defenses. Subsequent raids targeted nearby settlements, including São Domingos and Praia, which were also plundered and burned. The campaign marked the beginning of Drake's expedition against Spanish holdings in the Americas, culminating in significant raids in the Caribbean.

trgt: In psychologist Hans Eysenck's P–E–N (psychoticism, extraversion and neuroticism) model of personality, psychoticism is a trait which is typified by aggressiveness and interpersonal hostility. In 2010, a paper titled "The nature of the relationship between personality traits and political attitudes" claimed to find a strong positive correlation between conservatism and psychoticism. This error was repeated in subsequent papers by the same authors; however, around 2015, the authors acknowledged the correlation is actually negative rather than positive, and began issuing corrections.
mgt: The 2010 conservatism-psychoticism correlation error refers to a mistake in a study by Brad Verhulst, Peter K. Hatemi, and Nicholas G. Martin, which incorrectly reported positive correlations between psychoticism and conservative attitudes. The error, later repeated in subsequent publications, reversed the actual negative correlations. While the mistake did not affect the study's primary thesis, it caused confusion in later research and public discourse. The error was eventually corrected after scrutiny by researchers, sparking debates over data transparency and the interpretation of psychoticism in political psychology.

trgt:
Michael Hugh Meacher (4 November 1939 – 21 October 2015) was a British politician who served as a government minister under Harold Wilson, James Callaghan and Tony Blair. A member of the Labour Party, he was Member of Parliament (MP) for Oldham West and Royton, previously Oldham West, from 1970 until his death in 2015. He was also a 9/11 conspiracy theorist.

Before entering politics, Meacher was a lecturer in social administration at the University of Essex and the University of York.
mgt: Michael Hugh Meacher (4 November 1939 – 21 October 2015) was a British Labour Party politician who served as Member of Parliament (MP) for Oldham West and later Oldham West and Royton from 1970 until his death in 2015. A prominent figure on the Labour left, he held several junior ministerial roles under Harold Wilson, James Callaghan, and Tony Blair, including Minister of State for the Environment (1997–2003). Meacher was also a long-serving member of the Shadow Cabinet, a vocal critic of New Labour policies, and an advocate for environmental and social justice issues.

trgt:
Dissymmetry of lift: 2–20 (also known as asymmetry of lift: 342 or asymmetric lift) in rotorcraft aerodynamics refers to an unequal amount of lift on opposite sides of the rotor disc. It is a phenomenon that affects single-rotor helicopters and autogyros in forward flight.

A rotor blade that is moving in the same direction as the aircraft is called the advancing blade and the blade moving in the opposite direction is called the retreating blade. When viewed from above, most American helicopter rotors turn counter-clockwise; French and Russian helicopters turn clockwise.: 141

Balancing lift across the rotor disc is important to a helicopter's stability. The amount of lift generated by an airfoil is proportional to the square of its airspeed (velocity). In a hover, the rotor blades have equal airspeeds and therefore equal lift. However, in forward flight the advancing blade has a higher airspeed than the retreating blade, creating uneven lift across the rotor disc.: 1–104
mgt:
Dissymmetry of lift is an aerodynamic phenomenon in helicopters that occurs during forward flight due to differences in lift generated by the advancing and retreating rotor blades. As the helicopter moves forward, the advancing blade experiences a higher relative airspeed and generates more lift, while the retreating blade, with a lower relative airspeed, generates less lift. This imbalance can cause the rotor disc to tilt and affects the helicopter's stability.

To counter dissymmetry of lift, helicopters employ mechanisms such as blade flapping and cyclic feathering. Blade flapping adjusts the angle of attack of the rotor blades, with the advancing blade flapping upward to reduce lift and the retreating blade flapping downward to increase lift. Cyclic feathering changes the angle of incidence of the blades as they rotate. These measures help maintain stability but impose limits on the helicopter's maximum forward speed.

Helicopters with coaxial rotors cancel dissymmetry of lift, while tandem rotor helicopters use automatic systems to mitigate its effects.

trgt: Kris Fisher is a fictional character from the British Channel 4 soap opera Hollyoaks, played by Gerard McCarthy. The character first appeared on-screen in September 2006 and left in August 2010 after McCarthy quit the show. He is noted for his storylines involving the PEP drug, a bisexual love triangle, his outlandish personality and being the only permanent cross dressing character in a British soap opera. Following his departure, it was announced that McCarthy would return in November 2010 for a short stint.
mgt: Kris Fisher is a fictional character from the British soap opera *Hollyoaks*, portrayed by Gerard McCarthy. Introduced in 2006 as a flamboyant and non-conformist student, Kris became a popular character, known for his bold personality, unique style, and complex storylines. These included a groundbreaking HIV awareness plot, a bisexual love triangle, and family conflicts. Kris was also involved in dramatic events such as a carbon monoxide poisoning incident and his brother Malachy's HIV diagnosis. The character departed in 2010 but remains a memorable figure in the series.

trgt: Canal Park is a baseball stadium located in Akron, Ohio, United States, that is the home of the Akron RubberDucks of the Eastern League. The team is a double-A minor-league affiliate of the Cleveland Guardians. Opened in 1997, the stadium was designed by Populous, then known as HOK Sport, the same architectural firm that designed the Guardians' Progressive Field, which opened three years earlier. The stadium takes its name from its location adjacent to the Ohio and Erie Canal, which runs behind the left-field wall.
mgt: Canal Park is a baseball stadium located in Akron, Ohio, serving as the home of the Akron RubberDucks, the city's Eastern League team, since its opening in 1997. Owned by the City of Akron and designed by Populous, the stadium has a seating capacity of 7,630 and features Kentucky Bluegrass playing surfaces. Notable amenities include luxury boxes, a team shop, a restaurant, and a picnic berm. Canal Park also hosts special events, such as high school and college baseball games, the Akron Marathon, and the 2016 Eastern League All-Star Game.

trgt: The Cambridge Literary Review (CLR) is a literary magazine published on an occasional basis. It is edited by Lydia Wilson, Rosie Šnajdr, Jocelyn Betts and Paige Smeaton and is run from Trinity Hall college at the University of Cambridge in England. It was founded in 2009 by Boris Jardine and Lydia Wilson with assistance from the University's 800th anniversary fund. It publishes poetry, short fiction and criticism, and although its commitment to experimental and often difficult works is influenced by the'Cambridge School' of poetry it has included contributions by writers from around the world and in many languages. It has received notice in The Times Literary Supplement.
mgt: The *Cambridge Literary Review* (CLR) is a literary journal that publishes poetry, fiction, essays, and translations, with contributions from prominent and emerging writers. Founded in Cambridge, the journal has featured works by poets such as J.H. Prynne, Rae Armantrout, and Susan Howe, as well as essays by figures like Raymond Geuss and J.H. Prynne. Each issue often explores specific themes, such as translation or children's literature, and includes reprints of rare or historical texts. The journal has been edited by various contributors, including Lydia Wilson and Rosie Šnajdr, and has received critical attention, including reviews in the *Times Literary Supplement*.

trgt: Larry Keel (born November 25, 1968 in Manassas, Virginia) is a bluegrass musician "reared in" Southwest Virginia who's been performing "seriously" since 1976. He was a founding member of Magraw Gap in 1990 with Danny Knicely and Will Lee. He performs with his wife, bassist Jenny Keel, in The Larry Keel Experience – and with other musicians in other groups. He has released over a dozen albums, the latest being Experienced in 2016, which he recorded with his group, Larry Keel Experience. He is married to his bass player, Jenny Keel.
mgt: Larry Keel (born November 25, 1968) is an American bluegrass musician, guitarist, singer, and bandleader known for his innovative approach to traditional bluegrass music. Born in Manassas, Virginia, he began playing guitar at a young age and gained recognition through bluegrass competitions, including winning the guitar competition at the Telluride Bluegrass Festival twice. Keel has led several bands, including Magraw Gap, the Larry Keel Experience, and Larry Keel & Natural Bridge, and has collaborated with prominent artists such as Keller Williams and Tony Rice. He continues to tour extensively and record acclaimed albums.

trgt:
Al-Azhar Park (Lang-ar) is a public park located in Cairo, Egypt.

Among several honors, this park is listed as one of the world's sixty great public spaces by the Project for Public Spaces. The park was created by the Historic Cities Support Programme of the Aga Khan Trust for Culture, an entity of the Aga Khan Development Network. The park was developed at a cost in excess of USD $30 million, its funding a gift to Cairo from Aga Khan IV, a descendant of the Fatimid Caliphs of Cairo.

The park project, an urbanism initiative, included:
mgt: Al-Azhar Park is a 30-hectare public park in Medieval Cairo, Egypt, opened in 2005. Initiated by Aga Khan IV and developed through the Historic Cities Support Programme, the park transformed Al-Darassa Hill, a centuries-old landfill, into a lush green space.
