The project involved extensive excavation, soil rehabilitation, and the integration of traditional Islamic landscape elements, such as fountains, waterways, and gardens. The restoration of the historic Ayyubid Wall and revitalization of the adjacent Darb al-Ahmar neighborhood were key components, addressing both cultural preservation and socio-economic development. The park also features an Urban Plaza and a forthcoming Museum of Historic Cairo.

trgt: San José Castle -also known as Castillo de San José in Spanish- was opened to the public on 25 May 1846 on the «Buena Vista Hill» on the southeast of Guatemala City by general Rafael Carrera government; it was also known as «Carrera Castle». San José Fort was located where later Guatemalan administrations built a new city Hall, a National Theater and part of the Bolivar Avenue. The fort had a shooting range for the soldiers, ammunition warehouses, horses, diners and a small pond that was called "Soldier's Pond".
mgt: San José Castle, also known as Castillo de San José or Santa Barbara Fort, is a 19th-century military fort located in Guatemala City, Guatemala. Constructed between 1843 and 1846 under the direction of architect José María Cervantes, it was commissioned by President Rafael Carrera to protect the city following political unrest and invasions. Strategically positioned on Buena Vista Hill, the fort served as a key military stronghold, ammunition depot, and prison. It played a significant role during the 1920 revolution against President Manuel Estrada Cabrera, ultimately falling to revolutionary forces on April 14, 1920.

trgt: Poilão is a small uninhabited island in the Bijagós Archipelago of Guinea-Bissau. It contains the country's southernmost point, at 10°51'53"N,15°43'36"W. It is part of the João Vieira and Poilão Marine National Park. The island is heavily forested. The nearest islands are Meio, 7 nmi (13 km) to the north, Cavalos, 9 nmi (17 km) to the north and João Vieira, 11 nmi (20 km) to the north-northeast. Its area is 43 ha. Between ca. 7000 and 29,000 green sea turtle (Chelonia mydas) nests are laid per year at the globally important site of Poilão Island. There is a lighthouse on the island, with focal height 27 m (89 ft).
mgt: Poilão is a small, uninhabited island in the Bijagós Archipelago of Guinea-Bissau, located in the Atlantic Ocean. Covering 43 hectares, it is part of the João Vieira and Poilão Marine National Park, established in 2000, and is considered a sacred site by the local Bijagós people. Traditionally owned by the community of Roxa, the island is used for cultural ceremonies, with restricted access for men and prohibitions on young women. Poilão is a critical nesting site for green sea turtles, hosting 5% of the global population and making it the largest nesting site in Africa. The island is monitored by park rangers, with local communities involved in its management.

trgt:
Peter Friedrich Röding (17 June 1767 – 8 June 1846) was a German malacologist who lived in Hamburg. Very little is known about this naturalist.

Many of Röding's descriptions (often simply a German rendition of the Latin binomial name) are of species which were first named by earlier authors such as Johann Hieronymus Chemnitz, Friedrich Wilhelm Martini and Martin Lister. Röding's references to pre-existing descriptions and figures make these names also valid, since they are unequivocally recognizable, and were (after Röding) subsequently adopted by many later authors.
mgt: Peter Friedrich Röding (17 June 1767 – 8 June 1846) was a German malacologist known for his contributions to taxonomy through the 1798 publication *Museum Boltenianum*, a catalogue of mollusc specimens from the collection of Joachim Friedrich Bolten. Initially overlooked as a taxonomic work, the catalogue was later recognized by William Healey Dall for introducing valid taxa, leading to Röding being credited with naming numerous mollusc genera. The work, published anonymously, was officially attributed to Röding by the International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature in 1956.

trgt:
The Complete Beat is a 5-CD box set by British ska/new wave band The Beat (known as The English Beat in the US) released on 10 July 2012 only in the US and Canada. The box set contains The Beat's three studio albums remastered and two CDs of bonus content: the first contains extended and dub versions, and the second contains all the tracks from their three Peel Sessions and several tracks live from the Boston Opera House.

The studio albums are the US release versions, as "Tears of a Clown", "Ranking Full Stop", "Too Nice to Talk To" and "Hit It" weren't included on the original UK releases of their respective albums. Also, both Wha'ppen and Special Beat Service CDs include bonus tracks.

On the same day of release, Shout! Factory also released the single-CD compilation album Keep The Beat: The Very Best of The English Beat and a live album, Live at the US Festival, '82 &'83.
mgt:
*The Complete Beat* is a box set by the British ska and new wave band The English Beat, released on July 10, 2012, by Shout! Factory. Spanning recordings from 1979 to 1982, the collection includes the band’s three studio albums, extended mixes, dub versions, live recordings, and a 24-page booklet by Alex Ogg detailing the band’s history. While no previously unreleased songs are included, many rarities make their CD debut in this set.

The box set received critical acclaim, earning a score of 90/100 on Metacritic based on eight reviews. Critics praised its comprehensive nature and the quality of the live recordings, which showcase the band’s energetic performances and political themes. AllMusic and Rolling Stone both rated it 4.5/5, with reviewers highlighting the visceral force of the recordings and the band’s enduring impact. Paste magazine also lauded the live tracks, calling them the most compelling aspect of the collection.

trgt:
Sir Robert James Buckland KBE KC (born 22 September 1968) is a British politician who served as Secretary of State for Wales from July to October 2022. He previously served as Secretary of State for Justice and Lord Chancellor from 2019 to 2021. A member of the Conservative Party, he has been Member of Parliament (MP) for South Swindon since 2010.

Buckland was Solicitor General for England and Wales from 2014 to 2019 and Minister of State for Prisons from May to July 2019. He was appointed Secretary of State for Justice and Lord Chancellor by Boris Johnson in July 2019, serving until the cabinet reshuffle in September 2021. In July 2022 he was appointed Secretary of State for Wales, a position he held until October of the same year.
mgt:
Sir Robert James Buckland KBE KC MP (born 22 September 1968) is a British Conservative Party politician and barrister who has served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for South Swindon since 2010. He has held several ministerial roles, including Secretary of State for Justice and Lord Chancellor (2019–2021) under Boris Johnson, and Secretary of State for Wales (July–October 2022) under Johnson and Liz Truss. Buckland also served as Solicitor General for England and Wales (2014–2019) and Minister of State for Prisons (2019). 

Before entering politics, Buckland practiced as a barrister specializing in criminal law and was appointed Queen's Counsel in 2014. Known for his advocacy on autism and special educational needs, he has received several awards for his work. Buckland was knighted in 2022 and returned to the backbenches later that year.

trgt:
Stephen Lake (born December 1963) is an Anglican clergyman and author who has been Bishop of Salisbury since April 2022; he was previously Dean of Gloucester from June 2011.

After a curacy at Sherborne Abbey he was priest in charge at St Aldhelm, Branksome then Rural Dean of Poole. He was a Canon Residentiary and Sub-Dean at St Albans Cathedral from 2001 until June 2011, when he became Dean of Gloucester. He was confirmed as Bishop of Salisbury in April 2022.
mgt: Stephen Lake (born December 1963) is a British Anglican bishop and former Dean of Gloucester, currently serving as the Bishop of Salisbury. Born in Poole, Dorset, he was ordained in 1988 and held various roles, including vicar, Rural Dean of Poole, and Sub-Dean of St Albans Cathedral. As Dean of Gloucester (2011–2022), he led significant projects like Project Pilgrim and chaired city regeneration initiatives. Consecrated as bishop in April 2022, Lake is also a long-time Scout Association member and Tottenham Hotspur fan.

trgt: Gomer Hill is a 2,106-foot-tall (642 m) mountain in the Tug Hill region of New York. It is located west-northwest of Turin in Lewis County. In 1940, a 67-foot-6-inch-tall (20.57 m) steel fire lookout tower was built on the mountain. The tower ceased fire lookout operations at the end of the 1988 season, and officially closed the next year. The tower site is open to the public, but the tower is closed and used as an antenna support structure.
mgt: Gomer Hill is a 2,106-foot (642 m) peak located west-northwest of Turin in Lewis County, New York, United States. In 1940, the Civilian Conservation Corps constructed a 67.5-foot (20.57 m) steel fire lookout tower on the hill, which was staffed from 1941 until the end of the 1988 fire season. The tower is now closed and serves as an antenna support structure, while its cabin was relocated to the Lowville Demonstration Forest, where it is part of a forest fire control exhibit.

trgt:
A stage box is an interface device used in sound reinforcement and recording studios to connect equipment to a mixing console. It provides a central location to connect microphones, instruments, and speakers to a multicore cable (snake), which allows the sound desk to be further from the stage and simplifies setup.

Stage boxes typically consist of a rugged metal enclosure, with XLR connectors on the front whose signals are routed through a snake. In the traditional sense, a stage box is effectively a simple termination box at the end of an analog multicore cable. However, many modern stage boxes convert between analog and digital, using a single twisted pair cable instead of an analog multicore.
mgt: A stage box is an audio device used in live sound and recording setups to consolidate multiple audio connections into a single cable, simplifying the connection between microphones, instruments, and a mixing console. Typically housing 16–32 XLR inputs and 4–8 outputs, stage boxes can be analog or digital. Digital stage boxes convert analog signals to digital, enabling long cable runs with reduced noise and compact cabling, often using Cat 5e, Cat 6, or fiber optic cables. They may include features like microphone preamps and DI interfaces. Smaller versions, called drop boxes, handle fewer connections and are used for localized setups. Stage boxes are essential for efficient audio management in live and studio environments.

trgt: The Eurasian otter (Lutra lutra), also known as the European otter, Eurasian river otter, common otter, and Old World otter, is a semiaquatic mammal native to Eurasia. The most widely distributed member of the otter subfamily (Lutrinae) of the weasel family (Mustelidae), it is found in the waterways and coasts of Europe, many parts of Asia, and parts of northern Africa. The Eurasian otter has a diet mainly of fish, and is strongly territorial. It is endangered in some parts of its range, but is recovering in others.
mgt: The Eurasian otter (*Lutra lutra*) is a semi-aquatic mammal native to Europe, Asia, and parts of Africa, and is the most widely distributed otter species. It is characterized by its brown fur, cream underside, and adaptations for aquatic life, such as dense bones to reduce buoyancy. Primarily feeding on fish, it also consumes amphibians, crustaceans, and birds. Eurasian otters are solitary, territorial, and breed year-round, with females raising pups alone. Once threatened by pollution, habitat loss, and hunting, populations have rebounded in parts of Europe due to conservation efforts and legal protections.

trgt:
A mobile (UK: /ˈmoʊbaɪl/,US: /ˈmoʊbiːl/) is a type of kinetic sculpture constructed to take advantage of the principle of equilibrium. It consists of a number of rods, from which weighted objects or further rods hang. The objects hanging from the rods balance each other, so that the rods remain more or less horizontal. Each rod hangs from only one string, which gives it the freedom to rotate about the string. An ensemble of these balanced parts hang freely in space, by design without coming into contact with each other.

Mobiles are popular in the nursery, where they hang over cribs to give infants entertainment and visual stimulation. Mobiles have inspired many composers, including Morton Feldman and Earle Brown who were inspired by Alexander Calder's mobiles to create mobile-like indeterminate pieces. John Cage wrote the music for the short film Works of Calder that focused on Calder's mobiles. Frank Zappa stated that his compositions employ a principle of balance similar to Calder mobiles.
mgt:
A mobile is a type of kinetic sculpture characterized by its ability to move, typically through air currents or direct interaction. The term "mobile" was first coined by Marcel Duchamp in 1931 to describe the early mechanized works of Alexander Calder, though it later became more specifically associated with Calder's free-moving, hanging sculptures. Calder's mobiles consist of brightly colored, abstract shapes made from sheet metal, connected by wire in a balanced arrangement. These elements can move independently or as a whole, creating dynamic, ever-changing compositions. Calder is credited with inventing this art form and produced numerous mobiles from the 1930s until his death in 1976, solidifying the term's association with his work.

While Calder's mobiles are the most iconic, similar concepts were explored by other artists, including Man Ray in the 1920s, Armando Reverón in the 1930s, and Bruno Munari, who created his "Useless Machines" in 1933 using playful colors and cardboard.

trgt:
Tulane University Law School is the law school of Tulane University. It is located on Tulane's Uptown campus in New Orleans, Louisiana. Established in 1847, it is the 12th oldest law school in the United States.

In addition to the usual common law and federal subjects, Tulane offers electives in the civil law, giving students the opportunity to pursue comparative education of the world's two major legal systems (Louisiana is the only state to have a civil law system, rather than common law). Students are permitted to survey a broad range of subject areas or to concentrate in one or more.

Tulane Law School's environmental law and sports law programs are considered among the strongest nationwide, and its maritime law program is among the most well-regarded in the world. For more than 20 years, the school has hosted the Tulane Corporate Law Institute, a preeminent mergers and acquisitions (M&A) and corporate law forum.
mgt: Tulane University Law School, established in 1847, is a private law school located in New Orleans, Louisiana, and is part of Tulane University. It is one of the oldest law schools in the United States and is ranked 55th nationally by *U.S. News & World Report* (2023). The school is housed in John Giffen Weinmann Hall on Tulane's Uptown campus, which features a law library, classrooms, and faculty offices. Tulane Law offers a Juris Doctor (J.D.) program, six optional concentrations, and graduate degrees, including Master of Laws (LL.M.) and Doctor of Juridical Science (S.J.D.) programs. It is renowned for its programs in maritime, environmental, and international law, as well as its study-abroad opportunities. The school also offers joint degree programs, including JD/MBA and JD/MHA. Tulane Law emphasizes experiential learning through clinical programs and externships and was the first U.S. law school to require pro bono service for graduation.

trgt:
"Everything Starts with an 'E'" is a song by E-Zee Possee featuring vocals from MC Kinky. It is considered by many to be the anthem of the acid house movement of the late 1980s, with the "E" in the title widely understood to refer to the drug Ecstasy (MDMA).

The song began life as an instrumental interpretation of Jeremy Healy and Simon Rogers' idea of house music after a trip to Ibiza which upon being presented to Boy George had vocals added to it by MC Kinky, Boy George and Eve Gallagher. This vocal version charted #15 on the UK Singles Chart, and although a music video was created, it was rarely broadcast due to its controversial lyrical content.
mgt: "Everything Starts with an 'E'" is a 1989 acid house single by E-Zee Possee featuring MC Kinky, written and produced by Jeremy Healy and Simon Rogers. Initially released on July 31, 1989, the track gained popularity in the UK rave scene despite being banned by BBC Radio 1 for its controversial lyrics. After a re-release on March 5, 1990, it peaked at #15 on the UK Singles Chart and became an anthem of the late 1980s acid house movement. The song features backing vocals by Boy George and Eve Gallagher and was included on E-Zee Possee's album *The Bone Dance*. Its success solidified its status as a flagship release for Boy George's More Protein label.

trgt:
Harmony, also known as Node 2, is the "utility hub" of the International Space Station. It connects the laboratory modules of the United States, Europe and Japan, as well as providing electrical power and electronic data. Sleeping cabins for four of the crew are housed here.

Harmony was successfully launched into space aboard Space Shuttle flight STS-120 on 23 October 2007. After temporarily being attached to the port side of the Unity module, it was moved to its permanent location on the forward end of the Destiny module on 14 November 2007. Harmony added 70 m3 (2,500 cu ft) to the station's living volume, an increase of almost 20%, from 420 m3 (15,000 cu ft) to 490 m3 (17,000 cu ft). Its successful installation meant that from NASA's perspective, the station was considered to be "U.S. Core Complete".
mgt:
Harmony, also known as Node 2, is a module of the International Space Station (ISS) that serves as a central hub for connecting various science laboratories and visiting spacecraft. Built by Thales Alenia Space in Turin, Italy, under a NASA-European Space Agency (ESA) agreement, Harmony was delivered to NASA in 2003 and launched aboard Space Shuttle Discovery on October 23, 2007, during mission STS-120. It was installed on the ISS through a series of spacewalks and robotic operations.

Harmony expanded the station's living and working space, enabling the addition of the European Columbus and Japanese Kibō laboratories. It features six Common Berthing Mechanisms (CBMs) for docking and is equipped with International Standard Payload Racks for crew quarters and storage. Harmony also supports visiting vehicles like SpaceX's Dragon and Northrop Grumman's Cygnus. It plays a key role in ISS operations and international collaboration in space exploration.

trgt: Dysbindin, short for dystrobrevin-binding protein 1, is a protein constituent of the dystrophin-associated protein complex (DPC) of skeletal muscle cells. It is also a part of BLOC-1, or biogenesis of lysosome-related organelles complex 1. Dysbindin was discovered by the research group of Derek Blake via yeast two-hybrid screening for binding partners of α-dystrobrevin. In addition, dysbindin is found in neural tissue of the brain, particularly in axon bundles and especially in certain axon terminals, notably mossy fiber synaptic terminals in the cerebellum and hippocampus. In humans, dysbindin is encoded by the DTNBP1 gene.
mgt: Dysbindin, encoded by the DTNBP1 gene located on chromosome 6 in humans, is a protein involved in various cellular processes, including synaptic function, dopamine regulation, and organelle organization. It has been implicated in schizophrenia through family-association studies, though the genetic link varies across populations due to genetic locus and disease allele heterogeneity. Dysbindin mutations are also associated with Hermansky–Pudlak syndrome type 7. In model organisms like Drosophila, dysbindin is essential for neural plasticity. The protein interacts with SNAPAP, MUTED, and PLDN, contributing to its diverse biological roles.

trgt:
In July 2022, the Islamist militant group al-Shabaab launched an invasion from Somalia into Ethiopia's Somali Region. Following attacks on the Somali side of the border, the rebel militants initially attacked Ethiopia's Afder Zone on 21 July and occupied the town of Hulhul before being driven back by Somali Region paramilitary forces. On 25 July, the militants launched a second incursion at Ferfer which was also defeated. Further cross-border attacks continued in the following days, while Ethiopia launched counter-attacks in response. Clashes between the Somali rebels and security forces inside Ethiopia extended into early August, and at least one small al-Shabaab contingent succeeded in evading the Ethiopian force and reached its main target, the Bale Mountains.

The invasion was the largest attack by al-Shabaab in Ethiopian territory to date.
mgt: The 2022 al-Shabaab invasion of Ethiopia was a military incursion by the Somali militant group al-Shabaab into Ethiopia's Somali Region, beginning on July 20, 2022. The invasion, part of the broader Somali Civil War and Ethiopian-Somali conflict, marked one of al-Shabaab's most significant cross-border operations. The group aimed to exploit Ethiopia's internal instability and establish a foothold in the Bale Mountains. Ethiopian forces, including the Somali Region's paramilitary Liyu police and the Ethiopian National Defense Force (ENDF), repelled the invasion, inflicting heavy casualties on al-Shabaab and destroying most of its forces. Despite this, a small contingent of militants reached the Bale Mountains. The invasion highlighted al-Shabaab's growing ambitions and Ethiopia's ongoing security challenges amidst its internal conflicts.

trgt:
Kampong Siam is an ethnic Siamese enclave (where the population are mostly a minority consisting of Thai expats and recent migrants as well as local-born Siamese descendants with ancestral roots from neighbouring areas such as Perlis, Kedah, Hulu Perak district of Perak as well as Kelantan) within the city of George Town in Penang, Malaysia. Located within the Pulau Tikus suburb, the neighbourhood lies 2.6 km (1.6 mi) west of the city centre. The 2,681 m2 (0.662-acre) neighbourhood is situated near the corner between Burmah Road and Burmah Lane, immediately adjacent to Wat Chaiyamangkalaram.

The neighbourhood is still inhabited by ethnic Siamese, who had moved into the area as early as the 19th century. However, the enclave has also, in recent years, been under threat from rapid urbanisation and redevelopment.
mgt:
Kampung Siam is a historic Siamese ethnic enclave located in the Pulau Tikus area of George Town, Penang, Malaysia. Established in the early 19th century, it became home to a significant Siamese community, with a census in 1828 recording 1,117 residents. In 1845, the British authorities granted the land to the Siamese as a goodwill gesture to Siam, and the community's trustees built the iconic Wat Chaiyamangkalaram temple adjacent to the village.

In recent years, Kampung Siam has faced challenges due to land disputes. In 2014, the land was sold to a developer without the knowledge of the trust holders, leading to eviction notices and a subsequent lawsuit by residents. The lawsuit was dismissed in 2015, raising concerns about the potential redevelopment and loss of the historic enclave.

trgt: Rhizomatic learning is a variety of pedagogical practices informed by the work of Gilles Deleuze and Félix Guattari. Explored initially as an application of post-structural thought to education, it has more recently been identified as methodology for net-enabled education. In contrast to goal-directed and hierarchical theories of learning, it posits that learning is most effective when it allows participants to react to evolving circumstances, preserving lines of flight that allow a fluid and continually evolving redefinition of the task at hand. In such a structure, "the community is the curriculum", subverting traditional notions of instructional design where objectives pre-exist student involvement.
mgt: Rhizomatic learning is an educational concept inspired by the rhizome, a plant structure used by philosophers Gilles Deleuze and Félix Guattari as a metaphor for non-hierarchical, interconnected knowledge systems. It contrasts with traditional, linear models of learning, emphasizing organic, student-driven exploration. The concept has roots in John Dewey's ideas about conversational, interest-driven learning and has been further developed by feminist and critical pedagogy scholars. Rhizomatic learning has gained renewed attention in the context of online education, particularly in relation to MOOCs. However, it has faced criticism, with some questioning its practicality and its critique of formal education systems.

trgt:
Theresa Park (born 1967) is a New York-based literary agent. She discovered and represents several internationally best-selling authors, including Nicholas Sparks, Debbie Macomber, Emily Giffin, Janice Y.K. Lee, Taylor Jenkins Reid, Cecelia Ahern, and Deborah Harkness, among others.

She grew up principally in Germany, Lebanon, and Austria, but attended UC Santa Cruz and Harvard Law School in the United States after graduating from the American International School in Vienna, Austria. She is fluent in German and can speak French.
mgt: Theresa Park is an American literary agent, producer, and president of Per Capita Productions. After graduating from Harvard Law School, she worked as a corporate attorney before transitioning to literary representation, founding Park Literary and Media in 2005. In 2019, she co-founded Park & Fine Literary and Media. Park's production company, Per Capita Productions, has produced projects such as *After Yang*, *The Expatriates*, and *ROAR*. Her work spans literature, film, and television, collaborating with prominent creators and production companies.

trgt:
Joshua Nyamweya Oigara (born 1975) is Kenyan businessman, accountant and corporate executive who is the immediate former Group chief executive officer of the Kenya Commercial Bank Group. He served in that position from 1 January 2013. until 25 May 2022.

Before that, he was the chief financial officer and Member of the board of directors of both KCB Group and KCB Bank Kenya, between January 2012 and January 2013. At age 37, his appointment in November 2012 to replace the outgoing CEO Martin Oduor-Otieno made him the youngest CEO of a publicly traded bank at the Nairobi Securities Exchange.
mgt: Joshua Oigara (born 1975) is a Kenyan businessman, accountant, and corporate executive. He served as the Group Chief Executive Officer and Managing Director of Kenya Commercial Bank (KCB) Group from 2012 to 2022, overseeing operations across multiple East African countries. Born in Kisii, Kenya, he earned a Bachelor of Commerce degree from the University of Nairobi and is a certified public accountant. Oigara has also completed postgraduate management courses at institutions such as INSEAD and the London Business School. In 2015, he publicly declared his net worth at KSh220 million (approximately US$2.2 million at the time).

trgt:
Wilpena Pound – also known by its Adnyamathanha name of Ikara, meaning "meeting place" – is a natural amphitheatre of mountains located 429 kilometres (267 mi) north of Adelaide, South Australia, Australia in the heart of the Ikara-Flinders Ranges National Park. It is accessible via a sealed road which continues on to the northern Flinders Ranges town of Blinman and to the south, Hawker.

Attempts at farming the Pound failed during the early 20th century. Following this, the tourism potential was recognised in 1945.
mgt: Wilpena Pound, also known as Ikara, is a natural amphitheater of mountains located in the Flinders Ranges of South Australia. It is a synclinal basin with significant geological and cultural importance, featuring peaks such as St Mary Peak, the highest in the Flinders Ranges. Traditionally known as Ikara, meaning "meeting place," it holds deep significance for the Adnyamathanha people. Now part of the Flinders Ranges National Park, Wilpena Pound is a popular tourist destination, offering bushwalking, rock climbing, and scenic flights, alongside rich Aboriginal heritage.

trgt: Harry Porter Deuel (December 11, 1836 – November 23, 1914) was a prominent railroad man in Omaha, Nebraska. Over the course of his career Deuel was employed throughout the transportation sector in Omaha, working for the foremost Missouri River shipping company in Omaha during its early years. Later he worked for the Union Pacific and the Chicago, Burlington and Quincy railroads, among others. He is the namesake of Deuel County, Nebraska. When he resigned from his last railroad job to become the Douglas County Registrar of Deeds, Deuel was the oldest railroad man in continuous service in Omaha, and was said to be one of the best known passenger men in the Western United States.
mgt: Harry P. Deuel (born in Clarkson, New York) was a prominent railroad agent, public official, and community leader in Omaha, Nebraska. After moving to Omaha in 1859, he worked with his uncle in the steamboat business before transitioning to the railroad industry, serving as an agent for the Kansas City, St. Joseph, & Council Bluffs Railroad, the Burlington and Missouri River Railroad, and later the Union Pacific Railroad. He became superintendent of Omaha's Burlington Station in 1896 and served as registrar of deeds in 1901. Deuel was also active in Omaha's social and fraternal circles, holding leadership roles in Masonic organizations and other civic groups for over 50 years.

trgt:
George Anthony Sifakis is a former United States federal government official and government relations professional. Sifakis is currently CEO of IdeagenGlobal.com, which convenes global leaders to develop partnerships and solutions to the world's most troubling issues.

Following his role on the Presidential Transition, assisting with the confirmation process of Wilbur Ross as Secretary of Commerce, Sifakis was appointed by President Trump on January 23, 2017, to be Special Assistant to the President and Deputy Director of the Office of Public Liaison, responsible for engaging the business community, including CEOs of the Fortune 500, non-profit and public sector leaders.

On March 6, 2017, Sifakis was appointed by President Trump as Assistant to the President and Director of the Office of Public Liaison, and one of approximately 20 senior White House staff directly reporting to the White House Chief of Staff, Reince Priebus. On August 18, 2017, multiple Trump administration sources acknowledged that Sifakis would be departing the White House in September.
mgt:
George Sifakis is an American political advisor who served as the Director of the Office of Public Liaison under President Donald Trump from March 6, 2017, to September 25, 2017. Prior to this role, he was a member of the George W. Bush administration and played a key role in the Trump transition team, focusing on congressional relations. Sifakis initially joined the Trump administration as Special Assistant to the President and Deputy Director of Public Liaison before being promoted to Director. He left the position in September 2017, with Johnny DeStefano succeeding him in an interim capacity.

Sifakis holds a Bachelor of Arts from Rhode Island College, a Master of Science in Political Science from Suffolk University, and a paralegal certification from Northeastern University. He also attended the Harvard Kennedy School Executive Education program and the Southern New England School of Law. A Greek Orthodox Christian, Sifakis resides in Alexandria, Virginia, with his wife, Adriana, and their three children.

trgt:
The Secret of Roan Inish is a 1994 independent fantasy-adventure film written and directed by John Sayles. It is based on the 1957 novel Secret of the Ron Mor Skerry, by Rosalie K. Fry.

It is centered on the Irish and Orcadian folklores of selkies—seals that can shed their skins to become human. The story, set on the west coast of Ulster in the north-west of Ireland, is about Fiona, a young girl who is sent to live with her grandparents and her cousin Eamon near the island of Roan Inish, where the selkies are rumored to reside. It is a family legend that her younger brother was swept away in his infancy and raised by a selkie. Part of the film takes place in Donegal Town.
mgt:
*The Secret of Roan Inish* is a 1994 independent fantasy drama film written and directed by John Sayles, based on Rosalie K. Fry's novel *Secret of the Ron Mor Skerry*. Set in 1946 Ireland, the story follows Fiona, a young girl sent to live with her grandparents in a coastal village, where she uncovers family lore about selkies—mythical seal-people—and the mysterious disappearance of her baby brother. The film explores themes of folklore, family, and the connection between humans and nature.

Shot primarily in County Donegal, Ireland, the film features cinematography by Haskell Wexler and a traditional Irish score by Mason Daring. Praised for its atmospheric visuals and storytelling, it holds a 96% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes. A digitally restored version was released in 2020 by the UCLA Film and Television Archive.

trgt: Robin Tyler (born Arlene Chernick, April 8, 1942) is the first lesbian or gay comic to come out on national television, a feminist and pioneer in the grassroots struggle for LGBTQ civil rights and marriage equality in the U.S., and a producer. She emceed and produced the main stage at three marches on Washington for LGBTQ rights, including the historic first National March On Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights in 1979. Tyler coined the phrase "We are everywhere" as a rallying cry for the LGBTQ community. This became the powerful signature chant of the 1979 march, appearing on banners and posters. The chant "We are everywhere" has continued as a popular rallying cry for LGBTQ equality.
mgt: Robin Tyler (born Arlene Chernick, April 8, 1942) is a Canadian-American comedian, producer, and LGBTQ+ activist. She was the first openly lesbian comedian to appear on U.S. national television and a pioneer in LGBTQ+ rights advocacy. Tyler produced and emceed the first National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights in 1979, as well as subsequent marches, including the 1987 event where she staged "The Wedding," a mass protest for marriage equality. She and her late wife, Diane Olson, successfully sued California for marriage equality, becoming the first lesbian couple married in Los Angeles in 2008. Tyler also organized women's music and comedy festivals and co-founded the StopDrLaura.com campaign against homophobia.

trgt:
Brodie Smith is a former American Ultimate Disc League player, and current professional disc golf player and YouTube personality best known for his frisbee trick shot videos.

Brodie rose to prominence as a premier ultimate player at the University of Florida, where he won two USA Ultimate College Championships, in 2006 and 2010. He also won two National Club Championships, first in 2012 with Austin's Doublewide, and again in 2014 with Denver's Johnny Bravo. He has played professionally with the AUDL's Indianapolis AlleyCats, Chicago Wildfire, Dallas Roughnecks, and Jacksonville Cannons.

Brodie achieved internet fame in 2011 when his Frisbee Trick Shots video became popular on YouTube. As of July 2018, his channel has over 2 million subscribers and 300 million total video views. Clips of Brodie's trick shots have been featured on ESPN's SportsCenter "Top Plays" segment numerous times and other major media outlets, including the Huffington Post. He has a sponsored line of merchandise produced by DiscStore.com

He appeared in the 28th installment of The Amazing Race alongside Ultimate teammate and friend Kurt Gibson, finishing 5th.

As of 2020 he is a professional disc golf player on the DGPT (Disc Golf Pro Tour). He is currently signed to Discraft.
mgt:
Brodie Smith (born in Jacksonville, Florida) is an American professional Ultimate Disc League player, disc golfer, and trick shot performer. He began playing ultimate frisbee at Nease High School in Ponte Vedra, Florida, and later joined the University of Florida's ultimate team in 2006. During his college career, the team won two National College Championships, in 2006 and 2010. 

Smith started his professional ultimate career in 2011 with the Indianapolis Alleycats of the American Ultimate Disc League (AUDL) and later played for teams including the Chicago Wildfire, Dallas Roughnecks, and Jacksonville Cannons. His career was interrupted by injuries, including a torn ACL and meniscus, which sidelined him for two seasons.

Smith gained widespread recognition for his frisbee trick shot videos, starting with his 2011 YouTube video "Frisbee Trick Shots," which garnered millions of views. His creative and challenging trick shots have been featured on ESPN SportsCenter's Top Plays and have helped promote awareness of ultimate frisbee as a sport. Smith has performed trick shots worldwide, collaborated with YouTube group Dude Perfect, and appeared on a Japanese game show. He continues to share his content on social media platforms, inspiring fans globally.

trgt: Down High School is a controlled co-educational grammar school located in Downpatrick, County Down, Northern Ireland. The school has students from the ages of eleven to eighteen in the senior school — of which there are approximately 970 pupils. On 21 July 2014, John O'Dowd, the education minister, approved a development proposal to close the preparatory department of Down High School and it closed on 30 June 2014. There are roughly 250 pupils engaged in A-level study in the sixth form. In July 2015, some £20 million was granted by the education minister to begin the new build project in 2017.
mgt: Down High School is a grammar school in Downpatrick, County Down, Northern Ireland, established in 1933. The school serves approximately 1,080 students aged 11 to 18 and is situated on a rural campus. Its motto, *Floreat Dunum, Absque Labore Nihil* ("May Down flourish, nothing is achieved without effort"), reflects its commitment to academic and personal growth. The school’s crest, inspired by the badge of 12th-century Norman soldier John de Courcy, symbolizes its historical ties to the region. Notable features of the campus include a gatehouse and stone walls, remnants of the former county gaol, and five student houses fostering community spirit.

trgt: The Offer in Compromise (OIC) program, in the United States, is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) program under 26 U.S.C. § 7122, which allows qualified individuals with an unpaid tax debt to negotiate a settled amount that is less than the total owed to clear the debt. A taxpayer uses the checklist in the Form 656, OIC package to determine if the taxpayer is eligible for the program. The objective of the OIC program is to accept a compromise when acceptance is in the best interests of both the taxpayer and the government, and promotes voluntary compliance with all future payment and filing requirements.
mgt: An offer in compromise (OIC) is a program administered by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) in the United States that allows taxpayers to settle their federal tax liabilities for less than the full amount owed. To qualify, taxpayers must meet one of three conditions: doubt as to liability, doubt as to collectibility, or effective tax administration. The program is often used by individuals facing financial hardship or extenuating circumstances. OICs can address various tax liabilities, including income and business taxes, but only for assessed taxes. The program includes specific payment requirements, protections against levies during processing, and warnings about fraudulent promoters targeting taxpayers.

trgt:
The Aleutian tern (Onychoprion aleuticus) is a migratory bird living in the subarctic region of the globe most of the year. It is frequently associated with the Arctic tern, which it closely resembles. While both species have a black cap, the Aleutian tern may be distinguished by its white forehead (although juvenile Arctic terns also have white foreheads). During breeding season, the Arctic terns have bright red bills, feet, and legs while those of the Aleutian terns are black.

Onychoprion aleuticus has not been thoroughly studied yet. If its winter migratory range was completely unknown until the late 1980s; it is now known that many Aleutian terns spend the winter near the Equator in the western Pacific. The Aleutian tern breeds in wide-ranging coastal colonies only in Alaska and eastern Siberia. Although Alaskan and Siberian populations are not well monitored, both are thought to be in significant decline.
mgt:
The Aleutian tern (*Onychoprion aleuticus*) is a medium-sized seabird in the family Laridae, known for its migratory behavior between subarctic breeding grounds in Alaska and Russia and tropical wintering areas in Southeast Asia and the South Pacific. It is characterized by its distinctive plumage, including a white forehead, black cap, and gray underparts during the breeding season, and its graceful flight. The species primarily feeds on small fish, crustaceans, and insects, foraging by surface-dipping in shallow waters.

Breeding in loose colonies, often alongside Arctic terns, Aleutian terns are less aggressive in defending their nests. They face significant population declines, particularly in Alaska, due to habitat disturbance, predation, and ecological pressures in their wintering areas. Listed as "Vulnerable" by the IUCN, conservation efforts focus on population monitoring and understanding the causes of decline, with research initiatives such as geolocator deployment aiding in these efforts.

trgt:
Kate Walsh (née Shimmin; born in 1992) is an Australian netball player in the Suncorp Super Netball league, playing for the Sunshine Coast Lightning. In August 2019, Jess Thirlby the new England Roses Coach announced Shimmin would join the full time England Roses programme.

Shimmin grew up in South Australia and was a part of the South Australian Institute of Sport Volleyball program before being offered a SASI Netball scholarship which she chose over volleyball. When she finished High School she moved to Canberra where she developed her skills at the Australian Institute of Sport.
mgt: Kate Walsh (née Shimmin, born 13 February 1992) is a retired Australian-born netball player who represented both Australia and England internationally. Primarily playing as a goal defence (GD) and goal keeper (GK), she had a successful domestic career, including stints with the Adelaide Thunderbirds, Queensland Firebirds, and Sunshine Coast Lightning in the Suncorp Super Netball league. Walsh was part of the Thunderbirds' 2013 championship-winning team and retired from elite netball in 2022. Internationally, she played for the Australian Fast5 team (2012–2017) and later represented England starting in 2019.

trgt:
In various science/engineering applications, such as independent component analysis, image analysis, genetic analysis, speech recognition, manifold learning, and time delay estimation it is useful to estimate the differential entropy of a system or process, given some observations.

The simplest and most common approach uses histogram-based estimation, but other approaches have been developed and used, each with its own benefits and drawbacks. The main factor in choosing a method is often a trade-off between the bias and the variance of the estimate, although the nature of the (suspected) distribution of the data may also be a factor.
mgt: Entropy estimation refers to the process of approximating the entropy of a probability distribution, which quantifies the uncertainty or randomness in a dataset. Various methods are used for entropy estimation, each with its strengths and limitations. The histogram estimator approximates entropy using histograms of observations but can produce biased results. Sample-spacing methods estimate entropy based on the spacing between ordered data points, though they struggle with multidimensional data. Nearest-neighbor approaches use distances between data points to estimate entropy, while Bayesian estimators, such as the NSB estimator, incorporate prior distributions for improved accuracy in under-sampled scenarios.

trgt:
The white collar--2 (wc-2) gene in Neurospora crassa encodes the protein White Collar-2 (WC-2). WC-2 is a GATA transcription factor necessary for blue light photoreception and for regulating circadian rhythms in Neurospora. In both contexts, WC-2 binds to its non-redundant counterpart White Collar-1 (WC-1) through PAS domains to form the White Collar Complex (WCC), an active transcription factor.

The WCC has two major and distinct roles in the cell. In the light, the WCC acts as a photoreceptor to mediate acute regulation of light-induced genes involved in various physiological processes such as carotenoid (type of pigment) biosynthesis and conidiation. In a separate and distinct role in the dark, WCC acts as the positive element in the autoregulatory transcription-translation negative feedback loop that controls circadian rhythmic behaviors in Neurospora. In this context, WCC regulates expression of the Frequency (FRQ) gene, a light-induced clock protein.
mgt: White Collar-2 (WC-2) is a nuclear protein in *Neurospora crassa* that plays a critical role in the organism's circadian clock and light response mechanisms. Discovered in the 20th century, WC-2 is part of the White Collar Complex (WCC), a heterodimer formed with WC-1, which regulates the expression of light-induced and circadian genes, including the *frq* gene. WC-2 contains a GATA-family zinc finger DNA-binding motif and a PAS domain, enabling it to bind to promoter elements and interact with WC-1. While WC-1 acts as the photoreceptor, WC-2 is essential for transcriptional regulation and maintaining rhythmicity in both light and dark conditions. Mutations in WC-2 disrupt circadian rhythms and light-driven gene expression. Current research focuses on understanding the molecular interactions of WC-2 within the WCC and its broader role in circadian regulation in *Neurospora crassa*.

trgt:
Rajkumar Singhajit Singh, (born 1 May 1935) is a leading exponent, choreographer and a guru of Indian classical dance form of Manipuri, including the Pung cholom and Raslila. He was awarded with the Sangeet Natak Akademi Award in 1984 and the Padma Shri in 1986 for his contribution to the Manipuri dance. In the year 2011, Sangeet Natak Akademi, India's The National Academy for Music, Dance and Drama, awarded him with its highest award, the Sangeet Natak Akademi Fellowship for his contribution to Indian Dance. In 2014 he was also conferred with the Tagore Award.

Guru Singhajit Singh and his wife Charu Sija Mathur, who is also a Sangeet Natak Akademi Award recipient, have established, Manipuri Nrityashram, a Manipuri dance school, in New Delhi.
mgt: Rajkumar Singhajit Singh (born May 1, 1931) is an acclaimed Indian classical dancer, choreographer, and scholar specializing in Manipuri dance. Born into a family of dancers and musicians in Manipur, he trained under renowned gurus, mastering various aspects of Manipuri dance and martial arts. Singhajit Singh joined Triveni Kala Sangam in New Delhi in 1954 as the head of the Manipuri Dance Section and later founded the Triveni Ballet in 1962, serving as its director and principal dancer. He has performed and choreographed extensively, touring across Europe, the Americas, Asia, and Africa. His recent choreography, *Ashta Nayika*, has garnered critical acclaim. Singhajit Singh is a recipient of prestigious honors, including the Sangeet Natak Akademi Award and the Tagore Award.

trgt:
Burlington Street is a partially at grade and elevated roadway in Hamilton, Ontario, stretching along the south shore of Hamilton Harbour in Lake Ontario. Burlington Street has four different statuses along the route. In its western terminus, it is a collector route ending at Bay Street North. Upon the intersection at Wellington Street, the road becomes an arterial route with four lanes. The Street ends at Parkdale Avenue. On October 14, 2015, city council approved the renaming of the upper portion of Burlington Street from 165 meters east of Ottawa St to the QEW to Nikola Tesla Boulevard.

Nikola Tesla Boulevard is the official exit #90 from QEW. The signage along the QEW for Burlington Street was eventually replaced between October 22-23, 2016 to reflect the name change. After this change, the westbound lanes of Burlington street begin from the first exit on Nikola Tesla Boulevard, heading westbound.
mgt:
Burlington Street is a major roadway in Hamilton, Ontario, Canada, spanning 8.5 kilometers (5.3 miles) from Bay Street in the west to east of Parkdale Avenue. Originally named "Industrial Road," it was renamed for its proximity to Hamilton Harbour, historically known as Burlington Bay. The street played a pivotal role during Hamilton's industrial peak in the mid-20th century, hosting major manufacturing companies such as Stelco, Dofasco, Procter & Gamble, and International Harvester. It was also central to the city's early electrification, powered by the Decew Falls Power Generating Station in 1898.

Today, Burlington Street remains a hub of industrial and port activity, with the Hamilton Oshawa Port Authority managing significant cargo traffic. The area is home to key industries, including steel manufacturing, oilseed processing, and brewing. The street's upper level has been renamed Nikola Tesla Boulevard, reflecting its historical connection to electrical innovation.

trgt:
Pennsylvania Route 934 (PA 934) is a 10.9-mile-long (17.5 km) state route located in Lebanon County, Pennsylvania. The southern terminus is at a junction with U.S. Route 322 (US 322)/PA 241 in the South Annville Township hamlet of Fontana. Its northern terminus is at Interstate 81 (I-81) at the southern edge of the Fort Indiantown Gap Military Reservation. The route passes through rural areas along with the community of Annville in between. Along its routing, PA 934 intersects US 422 in Annville and US 22 in the East Hanover Township hamlet of Harper Tavern.

PA 934 was first designated in 1928 to run from US 22 (now US 422) in Annville north to Bellegrove. By 1946, the southern terminus was extended to US 322/PA 241 while the northern terminus was extended to US 22 and PA 343. The northern terminus was extended to PA 72 in Lickdale in 1963 and realigned to PA 72 and PA 443 within the Edward Martin Military Reservation in the late 1960s. In the 1970s, the north end of PA 934 was moved to its current location at I-81.
mgt:
Pennsylvania Route 934 (PA 934) is a 10.9-mile (17.5 km) state highway located in Lebanon County, Pennsylvania. The route begins at an intersection with U.S. Route 322 (US 322) and PA 241 in South Annville Township and runs north to its terminus at an interchange with Interstate 81 (I-81) in Fort Indiantown Gap. PA 934 is primarily a two-lane undivided road, passing through agricultural areas, residential communities, and notable landmarks such as Lebanon Valley College in Annville. The route also crosses several waterways, including the Quittapahilla Creek and Swatara Creek, and transitions to a divided highway near its northern terminus.

Designated in 1928, PA 934 originally ran from Annville to Bellegrove, with subsequent extensions south to US 322 and north to I-81 over the decades. The route was realigned in the 1960s to serve the Pennsylvania National Guard's Edward Martin Military Reservation. By the 1970s, its northern terminus was shortened to the I-81 interchange. PA 934 remains an important connector in Lebanon County, linking rural areas with major highways.

trgt:
Ticagrelor, sold under the brand name Brilinta among others, is a medication used for the prevention of stroke, heart attack and other events in people with acute coronary syndrome, meaning problems with blood supply in the coronary arteries. It acts as a platelet aggregation inhibitor by antagonising the P2Y12 receptor. The drug is produced by AstraZeneca.

The most common side effects include dyspnea (difficulty breathing), bleeding and raised uric acid level in the blood.

It was approved for medical use in the European Union in December 2010, and in the United States in July 2011. In 2020, it was the 247th most commonly prescribed medication in the United States, with more than 1 million prescriptions.
mgt: Ticagrelor, sold under the brand names Brilinta and Brilique, is an oral antiplatelet medication used to reduce the risk of stroke, myocardial infarction, and other atherothrombotic events. It is commonly prescribed alongside low-dose aspirin as part of dual antiplatelet therapy. Unlike other antiplatelet drugs, ticagrelor is a reversible allosteric antagonist of the P2Y12 receptor and does not require hepatic activation, offering faster onset and offset of action. Common side effects include an increased risk of bleeding and shortness of breath. Clinical trials, such as the PLATO trial, have demonstrated its superiority over clopidogrel in reducing cardiovascular events, though its use may require caution in certain populations, including East Asian individuals.

trgt: The Liberators'civil war (43–42 BC) was started by the Second Triumvirate to avenge Julius Caesar's assassination. The war was fought by the forces of Mark Antony and Octavian (the Second Triumvirate members) against the forces of Caesar's assassins, led by Marcus Junius Brutus and Gaius Cassius Longinus, also called the Liberatores. The latter were defeated by the Triumvirs at the Battle of Philippi in October 42 BC, and committed suicide. Brutus would also commit suicide after the second part of the battle.
mgt: The Liberators' civil war (43–42 BC) was a conflict during the Crisis of the Roman Republic between the forces of the Second Triumvirate—Mark Antony, Octavian, and Lepidus—and the Liberatores, led by Brutus and Cassius, who had assassinated Julius Caesar. The war culminated in the two Battles of Philippi in Macedonia, where the triumvirs decisively defeated the Liberators. Both Cassius and Brutus committed suicide, marking the end of the Republican resistance. The victory solidified the triumvirs' control over Rome, though tensions with the Parthian Empire persisted.

trgt:
Hotel Kazakhstan (Lang-kk, Lang-ru) is the third-tallest building in the city of Almaty, Kazakhstan. It is 102 m (335 ft) tall, with 26 floors. It is situated in the southern center of Almaty. It is located on Dostyq Avenue, a major road in the east part of the city which runs all the way from Medeo to Panfilov Park, in the north part of town.

The total area is 44887.5 sq. meters.

The building was erected in 1977. It is constructed to stand an earthquake that measures 9.0 on the Richter scale. It is a famous landmark all over Almaty, and serves as a symbol of the city. It is also the eighth-tallest building in all Kazakhstan.

In October 2018, architectural lighting was installed on the hotel building.

The "Friendship" public garden adjoins the hotel territory, where the Camels panel, a monument to Shamshi Kaldayakov and a fountain are located. Convenient access to the embankment of the Malaya Almatinka River has been arranged.
mgt:
Hotel Kazakhstan (Kazakh: Qazaqstan Qonaq Üiı) is a prominent 26-story hotel located in Almaty, Kazakhstan. Completed in 1977, the building stands at 102 meters (335 feet) tall and is designed to withstand seismic activity of up to 9 points. Its distinctive architecture features an ellipse-shaped structure, a curvilinear canopy at the entrance, and a golden crown atop the building. Constructed with a monolithic reinforced concrete core, the hotel is both a functional and aesthetic landmark.

The hotel lobby houses the 34-meter tapestry "The Rainbow of Kazakhstan," created by a group of artists and awarded the State Prize of the Kazakh SSR in 1980. The building's interiors and facades are adorned with materials such as marble, granite, and shell rock. Recognized for its cultural significance, the hotel was designated a historical and cultural monument of Almaty on April 4, 1979. It remains an iconic symbol of the city and a notable example of Soviet-era architecture.

trgt: When Hope Calls is an American drama television series. The series is a spin-off of Hallmark Channel's When Calls the Heart, which is based on the Canadian West book series by Janette Oke. It premiered on August 30, 2019 as the inaugural original programming on Hallmark Movies Now, the Hallmark Channel's digital streaming service, with new episodes being released through October 25, 2019. The series was subsequently aired on Hallmark Channel in early 2020. The series was renewed for a second season and premiered on Great American Family on December 18, 2021.
mgt: *When Hope Calls* is an American family drama and Western television series created by Alfonso H. Moreno. A spin-off of *When Calls the Heart*, the series follows orphaned sisters Lillian and Grace, who reunite as adults in 1916 to open an orphanage in the fictional Canadian town of Brookfield. The show premiered on August 30, 2019, as Hallmark Movies Now's first original streaming series and later moved to GAC Family for its second season in 2021. Filmed in Powassan, Ontario, it stars Morgan Kohan, Jocelyn Hudon, and Lori Loughlin, among others.

trgt: Soyuz TMA-11 was a human spaceflight mission using a Soyuz-TMA spacecraft to transport personnel to and from the International Space Station (ISS). The mission began at 13:22 UTC on October 10, 2007, when the spacecraft was launched from the Baikonur Cosmodrome by a Soyuz FG launch vehicle. It brought to the station two members of the ISS Expedition 16 crew, as well as Sheikh Muszaphar, the first Malaysian in space. TMA-11 remained at the station as an escape craft, and returned safely to Earth on April 19, 2008, after it had been replaced by Soyuz TMA-12. Although the vehicle landed safely, it suffered a partial separation failure which caused a ballistic re-entry that in turn caused it to land 475 km from the intended landing point.
mgt:
Soyuz TMA-11 was a 2007–2008 crewed spaceflight to the International Space Station (ISS) operated by Roscosmos. Launched on October 10, 2007, aboard a Soyuz-FG rocket from the Baikonur Cosmodrome, the spacecraft carried Yuri Malenchenko, Peggy Whitson, and Sheikh Muszaphar Shukor, the first Malaysian astronaut. It docked with the ISS on October 12, 2007, and remained there for 189 days. The mission concluded with a landing in Kazakhstan on April 19, 2008, after a ballistic reentry caused by a separation malfunction, which resulted in a steeper descent and minor injuries to South Korean astronaut Yi So-yeon.

The mission was notable for Sheikh Muszaphar's participation under a Russian-Malaysian agreement and for being the first Soyuz flight with a female-majority crew. The reentry anomaly was later attributed to a pyro-bolt failure.

trgt:
"Kashmiri Song" or "Pale Hands I Loved" is a 1902 song by Amy Woodforde-Finden based on a poem by Laurence Hope, pseudonym of Violet Nicolson.

The poem first appeared in Hope's first collection of poems, The Garden of Kama (1901), also known as India's Love Lyrics.

The following year, when Amy Woodforde-Finden set to music Four Indian Love Lyrics, "Kashmiri Song" emerged as the most popular, quickly becoming a drawing room standard and remaining popular until the Second World War.
mgt: "Kashmiri Song" is a popular song inspired by the poetry of Laurence Hope, with lyrics referencing the Shalimar Gardens, possibly in Kashmir or Lahore. The song gained prominence through numerous recordings in the inter-war years by artists such as Peter Dawson, Frank Titterton, John McCormack, and Richard Tauber. It has also had a notable presence in popular culture, being mentioned or quoted in various contexts. The song's origins and interpretations continue to be a subject of interest.

page_title: Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963
trgt:
The Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963 is an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom. At the time of its passage, the Act was intended to extend the protection of workplace health, safety and welfare under the Factories Act 1961 to other employees in Great Britain. Though as of 2008 some of it remains in force, it has largely been superseded by the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 and regulations made under it.

Breach of the residual provisions is still a crime punishable on summary conviction in the magistrates' court by a fine of up to £400 or, on indictment in the Crown Court, imprisonment for up to two years and an unlimited fine.

In the event of damage arising from a breach of the Act, there may be civil liability for breach of statutory duty. Though no such liability is stipulated by the Act itself, none is excluded and the facts could be such as to give rise to a cause of action in that tort. A breach not actionable in itself may be evidential towards a claim for common law negligence. In particular, a criminal conviction may be given in evidence.
mgt:
The *Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963* was an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom aimed at improving the health, safety, and welfare of employees working in office, shop, and certain railway premises. Introduced by John Hare, the Minister of Labour, the Act received royal assent on 31 July 1963 and came into effect on 18 February 1964. It extended workplace protections to approximately 8 million additional employees, building on recommendations from the 1949 Gowers Report, which had previously influenced other safety legislation such as the Factories Act 1961.

The Act outlined general provisions for safe and healthy working conditions, including requirements for machinery safeguarding, restrictions on hazardous tasks for young workers, and limits on manual lifting. However, many of its provisions were later repealed and replaced by subsequent regulations, such as the Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations 1992, the Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations 1992, and the Health and Safety (Young Persons) Regulations 1997. While largely superseded, the Act retains limited applicability to certain premises not covered by modern workplace definitions. It marked a significant step in the evolution of workplace safety legislation in the United Kingdom.

trgt:
A cigarette filter, also known as a filter tip, is a component of a cigarette, along with cigarette paper, capsules and adhesives. Filters were introduced in the early 1950s.

Filters may be made from plastic cellulose acetate fiber, paper or activated charcoal (either as a cavity filter or embedded into the plastic cellulose acetate fibers). Macroporous phenol-formaldehyde resins and asbestos have also been used. The plastic cellulose acetate filter and paper modify the particulate smoke phase by particle retention (filtration), and finely divided carbon modifies the gaseous phase (adsorption).

Filters are intended to reduce the harm caused by smoking by reducing harmful elements inhaled by smokers. They have been shown to reduce the risk of lung cancer. While laboratory tests show a reduction of "tar" and nicotine smoke, filters are ineffective at removing gases of low molecular weight, such as carbon monoxide. Most of these measured reductions occur only when the cigarette is smoked on a smoking machine; when smoked by a human, the compounds are delivered into the lungs regardless of whether or not a filter is used.

Most factory-made cigarettes are equipped with a filter; those who roll their own can buy them from a tobacconist.
mgt:
A cigarette filter is a component of a cigarette designed to reduce the inhalation of harmful substances during smoking. First patented in 1925 by Hungarian inventor Boris Aivaz, filters became widely adopted in the 1950s following health concerns linking smoking to lung diseases. By the 1960s, filtered cigarettes dominated the market, with filters primarily made from cellulose acetate, a plastic derived from cotton or wood pulp. Filters may also include additives such as menthol, sweeteners, or activated charcoal to modify flavor or reduce tar and nicotine levels.

Despite initial claims of reduced health risks, later studies found little difference in lung cancer risk between filtered and non-filtered cigarettes. Filters have also contributed significantly to environmental pollution, with cigarette butts being the most littered waste item globally. The cellulose acetate in filters degrades slowly, releasing toxins into ecosystems. Efforts to address this issue include developing biodegradable filters, imposing penalties for littering, and exploring innovative recycling methods, such as converting filter waste into energy storage materials.

The tobacco industry has also marketed "light" cigarettes with reduced tar and nicotine yields, though these have not significantly lowered health risks. Filters remain a controversial component of cigarettes due to their environmental and health impacts.

trgt:
The Sportpalast speech (Lang-de) or Total War speech was a speech delivered by German Propaganda Minister Joseph Goebbels at the Berlin Sportpalast to a large, carefully selected audience on 18 February 1943, as the tide of World War II was turning against Nazi Germany and its Axis allies. The speech is particularly notable as Goebbels almost mentions the Holocaust, when he begins saying "Ausrotten" (using the German word for extermination), but quickly changes it to Ausschaltung (i.e. exclusion). This was the same word Heinrich Himmler used on 18 December 1941, when he recorded the outcome of his discussion with Adolf Hitler on the Final Solution, wherein he wrote "als Partisanen auszurotten" ("exterminate them as partisans").

It is considered the most famous of Joseph Goebbels's speeches. The speech was the first public admission by the Nazi leadership that Germany faced serious dangers. Goebbels called for a total war (German: totalen Krieg) to secure victory over the Allies, and exhorted the German people to continue the war even though it would be long and difficult because—as he asserted—both Germany's survival and the survival of a non-Bolshevist Europe were at stake.
mgt:
The Sportpalast speech, delivered by Joseph Goebbels on February 18, 1943, in Berlin, was a pivotal moment in Nazi Germany's propaganda during World War II. Addressing a carefully selected and fervent audience at the Berlin Sportpalast, Goebbels called for "total war" to counter the growing Allied advances following the Axis defeats at Stalingrad, North Africa, and in the Pacific. The speech marked the Nazi leadership's first public acknowledgment of Germany's dire military situation and launched a campaign for full national mobilization, including austerity measures and the closure of non-essential establishments.

Goebbels framed the war as a struggle against "International Jewry" and Bolshevism, using inflammatory rhetoric to justify radical policies. The speech is historically significant for its role in prolonging the war and for its propagandistic fervor, as Goebbels sought to rally the German population and gain greater control over the war economy. While millions listened via radio, the speech's recorded and written versions reveal differences, including Goebbels' near-mention of the "extermination" of Jews. Despite its immediate impact, the speech also inspired late-war jokes reflecting growing disillusionment among Germans.

trgt:
Stuyvesant Street is one of the oldest streets in the New York City borough of Manhattan. It runs diagonally from 9th Street at Third Avenue to 10th Street near Second Avenue, all within the East Village, Manhattan, neighborhood. The majority of the street is included in the St. Mark's Historic District.

Although the street runs diagonally in relation to the Manhattan street grid, geographically it is one of the few true east–west streets in Manhattan, since most of the grid runs southeast–northwest at a 28.9 degree offset. It is a one-way street, running eastbound.
mgt: Stuyvesant Street is a historic street in Manhattan, New York City, originally part of Petrus Stuyvesant's 17th-century farm. It ran from Bowery Road (now Fourth Avenue) to the Stuyvesant manor house, which burned down in 1778. The street, known for its quaint charm, is part of the St. Mark's Historic District and features landmarks like the Hamilton Fish House. Stuyvesant Street is notable for being one of the few streets to deviate from Manhattan's 1811 grid plan. Portions of the street have been repurposed, including Abe Lebewohl Park, named after a local deli owner.

trgt: Basugaon (IPA:ˈbɑːsʊˌgɑ̃ʊ) is a Town in Chirang District of Assam, India. The name of this town is derived from its high bamboo plantation and bamboo market basically "basu" or "bash" means bamboo and "gaon" means village that is Basugaon, 'The village of bamboo'. It is an industrial town and a Municipal Board area. It is a neighbourhood of Bongaigaon UA, and it is situated approximately 180 km from Guwahati City in the western part of the Lower Assam Region.
mgt: Basugaon is a town in the Chirang district of Assam, India, located at an elevation of 47 meters. Governed by the Basugaon Municipal Board, it had a population of 27,350 as of 2019. The town is well-connected by roadways, with National Highways 31, 31C, and 37 linking it to major cities in Assam and neighboring states. Basugaon also falls under the Northeast Frontier Railway zone, with Basugaon Station serving as a key rail link. The town has several schools, though secondary education options are limited.

trgt:
Irving Trust was an American Commercial bank headquartered in New York City that operated between 1851 and 1988 when it was acquired by Bank of New York. From 1965 the bank was the principal subsidiary of the Irving Bank Corporation.

Between 1913 and 1931, its headquarters was in the Woolworth Building; after 1931, until it was acquired by Bank of New York, its headquarters was located at One Wall Street, at what is now known as the BNY Mellon Building.
mgt: Irving Trust was a prominent American commercial bank founded in 1851 in New York City. Named after author Washington Irving, the bank gained recognition for its widely accepted banknotes featuring his portrait. Over the years, it underwent several mergers and name changes, becoming the Irving Trust Company in 1929. Known for its international reach and corporate banking services, it was a sponsor of the 1980 Winter Olympics. In 1988, Irving Trust was acquired by the Bank of New York after a hostile takeover.

trgt: Ranpak Holdings Corp. manufactures machines and paper products used in protective paper-based packing for shipping goods and merchandise for e-commerce and industry, along with automation solutions. The company is based in Concord, Ohio, and has production facilities and offices in Reno, Nevada; Raleigh, North Carolina; Kansas City, Missouri; the Netherlands; Czech Republic; Shanghai and Singapore. The paper packaging material can be used for multiple applications like wrapping, cushioning, void fill and cold chain purposes, and is sometimes known as dunnage. Ranpak counts customers globally like Amazon, IKEA and Walmart.
mgt: Ranpak is a public company specializing in recyclable packaging and packaging machinery, founded in 1972 by Raymond Q. Armington and George R. Johnson in Painesville, Ohio. The company developed innovative technology to convert kraft paper into cushioning material for shipping. Headquartered in Concord, Ohio, Ranpak serves markets across the Americas, Europe, Asia, and Australasia. It has undergone multiple ownership changes, with Omar Asali leading as CEO since 2019. Recognized for its innovation, Ranpak has been lauded for its sustainable packaging solutions, including being named a top innovator by *Fast Company* in 2021.

trgt:
Thrilling is an online marketplace for vintage clothing, apparel and home goods. The company partners with independent vintage and secondhand clothing stores that use the platform to list, sell, and ship inventory. Thrilling was founded by Shilla Kim-Parker and Brad Mallow in 2018, In 2021, the company received $8.47 million in series A funding.

Thrilling has partnered with Banana Republic, Academy Award nominated costume designer Ruth E. Carter, and offers a studio service that uses the company's network of stores to find authentic period clothing for film and television productions. In 2020, Thrilling partnered with stylist Zerina Akers of "Black Owned Everything" to curate an online collection of vintage items from black-owned stores.

As of January 2022, Thrilling hosted listings from more than 900 vintage stores in approximately 200 cities and planned to use series A funding to improve technology such as partner store analytics, partially automating the item listing process, and adding personalization for customers shopping on the site.

In May 2022, Thrilling introduced a home goods section that allows thrift shop owners to list and sell vintage home decor items.
mgt:
Thrilling is a private vintage clothing company founded in 2018 by Shilla Kim-Parker and Brad Mallow, headquartered in Los Angeles, California. The company operates an online platform that connects brick-and-mortar thrift and vintage stores with customers, distinguishing itself from competitors like Etsy, Depop, Poshmark, and Tradesy, which primarily rely on peer-to-peer sales. Thrilling handles sales, shipping, and returns for a 10% commission, offering technology to help store owners digitize large inventories, including tools for size and price detection. During the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic, the company waived commissions to support shop owners.

Thrilling has raised significant funding since its inception, including $100,000 in seed funding in 2018, $2.1 million in 2019, and $8.5 million in a 2021 Series A round led by Prelude Ventures. Other investors include Elemental Excelerator, Defy, Urban US, Phoenix Rising, Closed Loop, and Congruent Ventures. Despite its innovative business model, Thrilling's social media presence and customer review system remain less developed compared to its competitors. The company continues to focus on supporting small businesses in the vintage and thrift industry.

trgt:
Cipriano di Michele Piccolpasso (1524 – 21 November 1579) was a member of an Italian patrician family of Bologna that had been settled since the mid-fifteenth century in Castel Durante, which was an important center for the manufacture of maiolica. Today he is remembered for writing Li tre libri dell'arte del vasajo ("The three books of the potter's art"), which are a storehouse of information on the techniques of maiolica from the choice of clays and their refinement, the shaping of the body, the composition of the glazes, to the preparation of the colors. The work"is now widely accepted as the first comprehensive account of the manufacture of any kind of pottery ever produced in Europe".

His brother operated a maiolica workshop, but it is not clear how much hands-on potting experience Cipriano himself had. He mentions that he had never used lustreware pigments, which might imply that he had used other types. His account of workshop techniques is in places unclear and hard to follow, either because he did not understand the process himself, or had difficulty forming a written description. The date of the book is uncertain, but most writers follow Bernard Rackham who suggested it (or at least the manuscript in the Victoria and Albert Museum) was written between around 1556 and 1559.
mgt:
Cipriano Piccolpasso (1524–1579) was an Italian Renaissance polymath, known for his contributions as a surveyor, civil and military engineer, draughtsman, poet, and writer. He was a member of the literary Accademia degli Eccentrici in Perugia and co-founded the Accademia del Disegno in 1573, one of the earliest academies for Italian artists. Piccolpasso is best known for his treatise *Li tre libri dell'arte del vasaio* (The Three Books of the Potter’s Art), a detailed manual on the production of majolica pottery, written at the request of Cardinal François de Tournon, possibly to aid French faience production. 

He also authored *Le piante ed I ritratti delle Città e Terre dell'Umbria*, an illustrated topography of Umbria commissioned by Pope Pius IV, who later knighted him. A manuscript of *Li tre libri*, featuring Piccolpasso's drawings of pottery techniques and decorative motifs, is housed in the Victoria and Albert Museum. Acquired in 1861, it was first published in 1857 and has since been reissued multiple times. Piccolpasso was buried in the church of San Francesco in Castel Durante, leaving a legacy as a key figure in the documentation of Renaissance art and craftsmanship.

trgt:
The Finse Tunnel (Lang-no) is a 10,300-metre-long (6.4 mi; 11,300 yd) railway tunnel west of the village of Finse in Eidfjord Municipality in Vestland county, Norway. The tunnel is part of the Bergen Line between the cities of Oslo and Bergen. It is the longest tunnel on the line, and the third longest railway tunnel in the kingdom. Inside the tunnel, at 1,237 metres (4,058 ft) elevation, is the highest point on the Norwegian railway network.

The tunnel was constructed to increase the regularity of the line past the Finse peak which was often blocked by snow. Planning started during the 1980s to remove this section that was regularly snowed down, and at the same time reduce the length and increase the speed of the railway. Construction started in 1990, and it was opened by King Harald V on 16 May 1993. Following the construction of the tunnel, 32 kilometres (20 mi) of railway around this area was also rebuilt to achieve higher train speeds, a shorter overall distance, and to maximize the natural protection from the weather. This was built in five stages, opening between 1995 and 1998.
mgt:
The Finse Tunnel is a 10.3-kilometer (6.4-mile) railway tunnel on the Bergen Line in Norway, located near Finse. Opened on May 16, 1993, it was constructed to address the severe winter weather conditions that frequently disrupted rail operations in the area. The tunnel, owned by the Norwegian National Rail Administration and operated by Norges Statsbaner and other freight companies, is electrified and allows trains to travel at speeds of up to 170 kilometers per hour (110 mph). It is situated at an elevation of 1,237 meters (4,058 feet), making it one of the highest railway tunnels in Europe.

The project was initiated in the 1980s to improve reliability and reduce operational costs associated with snow clearance. Construction began in 1990 and was completed in 1992, with the tunnel officially opening the following year at a total cost of 480 million kr. The tunnel includes a passing loop and safety features, though concerns about fire safety and evacuation have been raised due to its remote location. The Finse Tunnel significantly improved travel times and operational efficiency on the Bergen Line.

trgt:
