The Jackson Plan or Raffles Town Plan, an urban plan of 1822 titled "Plan of the Town of Singapore", is a proposed scheme for Singapore drawn up to maintain some order in the urban development of the fledgling but thriving colony founded just three years earlier. It was named after Lieutenant Philip Jackson, the colony's engineer and land surveyor tasked to oversee its physical development in accordance with the vision of Stamford Raffles for Singapore, hence it is also commonly called Raffles Town Plan. Raffles gave his instructions in November 1822, the plan was then drawn up in late 1822 or early 1823 and published in 1828. It is the earliest extant plan for the town of Singapore, but not an actual street map of Singapore as it existed in 1822 or 1827 since the plan is an idealised scheme of how Singapore may be organised that was not fully realised. Nevertheless, it served as a guide for the development of Singapore in its early days, and the effect of the general layout of the plan is still observable to this day.

The plan is currently on display in the Singapore History Gallery at the National Museum of Singapore.
mgt:
The Jackson Plan, also known as the Raffles Town Plan, was a city plan for Singapore drawn up in 1822–1823 by Lieutenant Philip Jackson under the instructions of Sir Stamford Raffles. It was created to address the haphazard development of the colony under William Farquhar's administration and to organize the town into functional and ethnic subdivisions. The plan divided Singapore into distinct zones, including a European Town, a Chinese Kampong (now Chinatown), an Indian area (Chuliah Kampong), and Kampong Glam for Malays, Arabs, and Bugis. While the ethnic divisions were not strictly enforced, they influenced the city's development and remain evident in modern Singapore.

The plan also designated areas for government and commercial use, with the latter evolving into today's financial hub at Raffles Place. Raffles personally oversaw land reclamation and infrastructure improvements, such as leveling hills and embanking the Singapore River. Additionally, Raffles proposed architectural guidelines, including fire-resistant masonry buildings with uniform facades and sheltered walkways, which influenced the region's distinctive "five-foot ways." Although not fully implemented at the time, the Jackson Plan laid the foundation for Singapore's urban development and the present-day Downtown Core.

page_title: The Things That I Used to Do
trgt:
"The Things That I Used to Do" is a blues standard written by Guitar Slim. He recorded it in New Orleans, where the young Ray Charles arranged and produced the session. Specialty Records released the song as a single in 1953 and it became a bestseller the following year. Specialty founder Art Rupe believed that the appeal would be limited to the Southern U.S. rural audience. However, urban rhythm and blues radio stations in the North began airing the song and built it into a national hit. As a result, Guitar Slim became in great demand as a performer and played at venues such as the Apollo Theater in New York City.

The single was one of the biggest hits in Specialty's history and stayed on the Billboard's Rhythm and Blues Records charts for 42 weeks. The song remained at number one for six weeks and was the best-selling R&B record of the year, selling more than a million copies.
mgt:
"The Things That I Used to Do" is a 1953 blues song by Guitar Slim, written by Eddie Jones and produced by Ray Charles. Recorded at J&M Studio in New Orleans, the song features a gospel-influenced arrangement and lyrics of regret, with Slim's distinctive guitar work and a rising and falling melody. It became a major commercial success and a standard in the blues genre, influencing the development of soul music and the "electric sound" of rock. The song's use of distorted guitar tones predated similar techniques by artists like Jimi Hendrix, who cited Guitar Slim as an influence.

Recognized as one of the "500 Songs That Shaped Rock and Roll" by the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, the track has been covered by artists such as James Brown, Tina Turner, and Pee Wee Crayton. James Brown's 1964 version reached number 99 on the Billboard Hot 100. The song remains a classic of New Orleans blues and a landmark in music history.

trgt:
Donald Clarke Sweeney (born August 17, 1966) is a Canadian former ice hockey defenceman who played over 1,100 games in the National Hockey League (NHL), mostly with the Boston Bruins. He ranks among the top ten in many Bruins team statistics, including fourth overall in total games played. After retiring from hockey following the 2003–04 season, he worked briefly as a broadcaster before rejoining the Bruins as a team executive in 2006.

He is the current general manager of the Bruins, a position he has held since May 2015 after replacing Peter Chiarelli.
mgt: Don Sweeney (born August 17, 1966) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey player and current executive. A defenseman, he played 16 seasons in the NHL, primarily with the Boston Bruins, appearing in 1,115 games and recording 273 points. Sweeney also played briefly for the Dallas Stars before retiring in 2004. He was named general manager of the Bruins in 2015, overseeing key trades and guiding the team to the 2019 Stanley Cup Finals. Sweeney was awarded the NHL General Manager of the Year Award in 2019. He is married to former figure skater Christine Hough.

trgt: Boane District is a district of Maputo Province in southern Mozambique. The principal town is Boane. The district is located in the center of the province, and borders with Moamba District in the north, the city of Matola in the northeast, Matutuíne District in the south, and with Namaacha District in the west. In the east, it is bounded by the Indian Ocean. The area of the district is 815 square kilometres (315 sq mi). It has a population of 98,964 as of 2007.
mgt: Boane District is a district in Maputo Province, Mozambique, with its capital in the town of Boane. Covering an area of 815 square kilometers, it had a population of 98,964 as of the 2007 census. The district is characterized by a subtropical humid climate and is traversed by the Umbeluzi, Tembe, and Matola rivers. Historically administered by the Portuguese since 1895, Boane is divided into two administrative postos. Its economy is primarily agricultural, and it is connected by national roads and the Goba railway.

trgt: The Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic (Lang-ro, Moldovan Cyrillic: Република Аутономэ Советикэ Cочиалистэ Молдовеняскэ; Lang-uk), shortened to Moldavian ASSR, was an autonomous republic of the Ukrainian SSR between 12 October 1924 and 2 August 1940, encompassing the modern territory of Transnistria (today de jure in Moldova, but de facto functioning as an independent state; see Transnistria conflict) as well as much of the present-day Podilsk Raion of Ukraine. It was an artificial political creation inspired by the Bolshevik nationalities policy in the context of the loss of larger Bessarabia to Romania in April 1918. In such a manner, the Bolshevik leadership tried to radicalize pro-Soviet feelings in Bessarabia with a goal to return it in the presence of favorable conditions and creation of geopolitical "place d'armes" (bridgehead) to execute a breakthrough in the Balkan direction by projecting influence upon Romanian Bessarabia, which was eventually occupied and annexed in 1940 after the signing of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact.
mgt:
The Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic (MASSR) was an autonomous republic within the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic, established on October 12, 1924, and dissolved on August 2, 1940. It was created as part of Soviet efforts to promote Moldavian identity and assert territorial claims over Bessarabia, then part of Romania. The MASSR's capital was initially Balta, later moved to Tiraspol in 1929. Although the republic was intended to represent the Moldavian population, Moldavians constituted less than one-third of its population, with significant Ukrainian and Russian communities.

The MASSR served as a political and propaganda tool to promote Soviet influence in Bessarabia and Romania. Policies included the promotion of a distinct Moldavian identity, the use of Cyrillic script for the Moldavian language, and industrialization efforts. The republic faced challenges such as famines, anti-Soviet uprisings, and forced collectivization. It was dissolved in 1940 when the Moldavian Soviet Socialist Republic was established, incorporating parts of Bessarabia and the MASSR.

trgt:
End of the World (Lang-fr) is a 1931 French science fiction film directed by Abel Gance based on the novel Omega: The Last Days of the World by Camille Flammarion. The film stars Victor Francen as Martial Novalic, Colette Darfeuil as Genevieve de Murcie, Abel Gance as Jean Novalic, and Jeanne Brindau as Madame Novalic. The plot concerns a comet hurtling toward Earth on a collision course and the different reactions people have to the impending disaster. Scientist Martial Novalic who discovers the comet, seeks a solution to the problem and becomes a fugitive after skeptical authorities blame him for starting a mass panic.

End of the World was director Abel Gance's first sound film. The original film was to be over three hours long, but the backing production took the film from Gance, and cut it to be 105 minutes. It was again cut on its release in the United States under the title of Paris after Dark. Neither abridged version of the film was well received by audiences or critics.
mgt:
*End of the World* (French: *La Fin du monde*) is a 1931 French science fiction film directed by Abel Gance. The film follows the story of a scientist, Martial Novalic, who discovers that Lexell's Comet is on a collision course with Earth, leading to global chaos, societal collapse, and a desperate attempt to unite humanity under a single world government. The film explores themes of apocalypse, human greed, and redemption, culminating in the comet narrowly missing Earth after widespread destruction.

Gance had envisioned the project since 1913 and began production in 1929, originally intending for the film to run over three hours. However, the producers took control and heavily edited it down to 105 minutes, significantly altering Gance's vision. Upon its release in France in January 1931, the film was met with critical and commercial failure, with Gance himself calling it "an abortive work." A heavily re-edited version was later released in the United States in 1934 under the title *Paris After Dark*.

trgt:
Akai Katana (赤い刀, Red Katana) is a bullet hell shooter video game developed by Cave that was released on August 20, 2010 in arcades. A video game console port, Akai Katana Shin (赤い刀 真), was released on the Xbox 360 on May 26, 2011 in Japan. Rising Star Games released the game in North America and Europe on May 15, 2012.

It is the fourth horizontal shoot 'em up game from the company, the prior three being Progear, Deathsmiles, and Deathsmiles II. The game's soundtrack was composed by Ryu Umemoto. City Connection is scheduled to re-release Akai Katana Shin for Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One on December 15, 2022.
mgt: *Akai Katana* is a bullet hell shoot 'em up video game developed by Cave, first released in arcades in Japan on August 20, 2010. Set in a parallel world resembling Japan's Taishō period, the game follows a group of rebels wielding powerful Blood Swords and advanced fighter planes as they rise against a tyrannical empire. The game was later ported to the Xbox 360 in 2011 and globally released for modern platforms, including the Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One, in December 2022. *Akai Katana* received critical acclaim for its challenging gameplay, 2D graphics, and Ryu Umemoto's hard rock soundtrack, earning praise as a standout entry in the bullet hell genre.

trgt: Whitland Abbey (Lang-cy or simply Y Tŷ Gwyn ar Daf; Latin, Albalanda) was a country house and Cistercian abbey in the parish of Llangan, in what was the hundred of Narbeth, Carmarthenshire, Wales. The town which grew up nearby is now named Whitland after it. It was widely known as Ty Gwyn ar Daf, meaning White House on the Taf, in reference to the country house originally built here before it became a monastic settlement which was known under that name. It is most associated with being the place where Hywel Dda drew up his laws around 940. It functioned as a Cistercian monastery between the 12th and 16th centuries.
mgt: Whitland Abbey, located in Carmarthenshire, Wales, was a Cistercian monastery founded on September 16, 1140, by monks from Clairvaux Abbey. Initially established at Little Trefgarn near Haverfordwest, it moved to Whitland around 1155. The abbey became a significant cultural and religious center in southwest Wales, known for its support of Welsh independence and its association with Rhys ap Gruffudd, Prince of Deheubarth. It founded several daughter houses, including Strata Florida Abbey and Strata Marcella Abbey. The abbey flourished until its dissolution in the 16th century, after which the site was repurposed, eventually becoming part of the Yelverton family estate.

trgt:
The Bali Botanic Garden (Lang-id) is the largest botanic garden in Indonesia and is located in the mountainous region of Bedugul, Tabanan Regency, central Bali, around 90 minutes drive north of Denpasar. The Garden was established on 15 July 1959 and is situated around 1300 metres above sea level overlooking Bratan Lake and the Ulun Danu Temple on the slopes of Tapak Hill. The Garden is a centre for botanical research, conservation, education and recreation. It is operated by the Indonesian Institute of Sciences (LIPI).

The Garden has an area of 157.5 hectares (389 acres) and daytime temperatures range from 17 - 25 °C and 10 - 15 °C at night. The humidity averages around 70-90%.

The Garden contains more than 21,000 living specimens belonging to 2,400 species, representing various species from mountainous areas of eastern Indonesia: Bali, Nusa Tenggara, Sulawesi, Maluku and Papua. In addition its herbarium contains 10,000 preserved plant specimens ranging from algae to flowering plants.

Apart from plant collections that include orchids, ferns, cacti and carnivorous plants, there is also a traditional Balinese style guesthouse that functions as a guest house for visitors. One of the world's largest displays of begonias is also on display in the conservatory building.
mgt:
The Bali Botanic Garden, located in Bedugul, Bali, Indonesia, is a 157.5-hectare botanical garden established on July 15, 1959, by Indonesia's first president, Sukarno. Originally named the Eka Karya Botanic Garden, it was the first Indonesian botanical garden created after the country’s independence. Initially focused on conifer cultivation and recreation, the garden expanded its role to include ex-situ conservation of plants from eastern Indonesia's mountainous regions. After a period of closure due to political instability, it reopened in 1975 and has since grown in size and scope.

The garden features diverse plant collections, including over 2,300 species such as orchids, begonias, bamboo, cacti, ferns, and traditional medicinal plants. It also houses a large begonia collection, a rose garden, and a rhododendron garden. Visitors can explore open recreational spaces, remnant mountain rainforest, and attractions like a giant ficus tree and views of Lake Bratan. The garden is home to various wildlife, including 79 bird species, treeshrews, and occasional macaques. Additionally, three Hindu temples are accessible within the garden. Open daily, the Bali Botanic Garden attracts over 500,000 visitors annually, offering a blend of scientific, cultural, and recreational experiences.

trgt:
"If Love Was a Crime" is a song recorded by Bulgarian recording artist Poli Genova. Written by Borislav Milanov, Sebastian Arman, Joacim Bo Persson, Johnny K. Palmer and Genova, the track represented Bulgaria in the Eurovision Song Contest 2016. As a result, it came in fourth place in the final, with it becoming the highest placing in the contest for Bulgaria. An accompanying music video for "If Love Was a Crime" was released on 21 March 2016, followed by the launching of the single on 30 March 2016 through the Bulgarian National Television (BNT) and Universal. Upon its appearance in the contest, the recording charted at number 200 on the UK Singles Chart and peaked within the top eighty in Austria, Belgium, Scotland and Sweden.

Despite its success, the song was removed from digital music platforms after its initial distribution deal with Universal expired. A campaign by the Pop Activism website saw a new deal struck between the rights holders BNT and distributor Universal in April 2018 with the song returning to digital music platforms.
mgt:
"If Love Was a Crime" is a dance-pop song by Bulgarian singer Poli Genova, released on 21 March 2016. Written by Borislav Milanov, Sebastian Arman, Joacim Persson, Johnny K. Palmer, and Genova, the song was Bulgaria's entry for the Eurovision Song Contest 2016. Internally selected by the Bulgarian National Television (BNT), the song debuted alongside its official music video on the Eurovision website. Genova performed it during the second semi-final on 12 May 2016 at the Globe Arena in Stockholm, Sweden, where it qualified for the final. On 14 May 2016, the song placed 4th in the final with 307 points, marking Bulgaria's best Eurovision result at the time and its first qualification for the final since 2007.

The song achieved significant popularity, with over four million streams on Spotify and nearly 15 million views of its official video. It has also been covered in Greek by singer Demy under the title "Isovia Mazi" ("Life Imprisonment Together").

trgt:
Homaro "Omar" Cantu Jr. (September 23, 1976 – April 14, 2015) was an American chef and inventor known for his use of molecular gastronomy. As a child, Cantu was fascinated with science and engineering. While working in a fast food restaurant, he discovered the similarities between science and cooking and decided to become a chef. In 1999, he was hired by his idol, Chicago chef Charlie Trotter. In 2003, Cantu became the first chef of Moto, which he later purchased.

Through Moto, Cantu explored his unusual ideas about cooking including edible menus, carbonated fruit, and food cooked with a laser. Initially seen as a novelty only, Moto eventually earned critical praise and, in 2012, a Michelin star. Cantu's second restaurant, iNG, and his coffee house, Berrista, focused on the use of "miracle berries" to make sour food taste sweet. He was working on opening a brewery called Crooked Fork at the time of his suicide in 2015.

In addition to being a chef, Cantu was a media personality, appearing regularly on TV shows, and an inventor. In 2010, he produced and co-hosted a show called Future Food. Through his media appearances, he advocated for an end to world hunger and thought his edible paper creation and the miracle berry could play a significant role in that goal. Cantu volunteered his time and money to a variety of charities and patented several food gadgets.
mgt:
Homaro Cantu (September 23, 1976 – April 14, 2015) was an American chef, inventor, entrepreneur, and advocate known for his pioneering work in molecular gastronomy and his innovative approach to food. Born in Tacoma, Washington, Cantu overcame a challenging childhood, including homelessness, to attend the Western Culinary Institute. He gained prominence as a sous chef under Charlie Trotter before opening his acclaimed Chicago restaurant, Moto, in 2004. At Moto, Cantu became renowned for his experimental dishes, such as edible menus, carbonated fruit, and laser-cooked food, earning the restaurant a Michelin star in 2012. He also opened iNG and Berrista, focusing on "flavor-tripping" with miracle berries.

Cantu was a prolific inventor, holding over 100 patents for culinary innovations, including edible paper and a polymer cooking box. He appeared on numerous television shows, including *Iron Chef America*, and co-hosted *Future Food*. A passionate advocate for combating world hunger, he envisioned using his inventions to provide sustainable food solutions. Cantu also founded the Trotter Project, a nonprofit promoting culinary education.

Despite his success, Cantu faced personal and professional challenges, including a lawsuit shortly before his death. On April 14, 2015, he died by suicide in Chicago. Remembered as a visionary and a "mad scientist" of cuisine, Cantu left a lasting legacy in the culinary world, blending science, creativity, and social impact. His life and work were chronicled in the 2016 documentary *Insatiable: The Homaro Cantu Story*.

trgt:
Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan (Lang-hy), is a hotel at the central Kentron District of Yerevan, Armenia. It was opened in 1958 as Armenia Hotel, a state-owned enterprise during the Soviet period. After the collapse of the Soviet Union, the hotel was privatized in 1998 and following a major renovation; it was reopened as the Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan in 1999.

The hotel is located on 1 Amiryan Street, overlooking the Republic Square. The National Gallery and the government building are located in front of the hotel.

As of 2016, with 259 guestrooms, Armenia Marriott is the 2nd-largest hotel in Armenia. As of 2020, the hotel has 144 rooms in the inventory with the Ararat wing being temporarily closed.
mgt:
The Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan is a luxury hotel located in Yerevan, Armenia, overlooking Republic Square. Originally opened in 1958 as the Hotel Armenia, it was the largest hotel in the Armenian SSR and was operated by the Soviet Intourist agency. Designed by architects Mark Grigorian and Eduard Sarapyan, the building was constructed with pink Armenian tufa and basalt stone. Following Armenia's economic crisis in the 1990s, the hotel was acquired by AK Development in 1998 and underwent a $40 million renovation before reopening in 1999 under the Marriott Hotels & Resorts brand.

The hotel features 144 rooms, 23 suites, four restaurants, 10 conference halls, a fitness center, and an outdoor pool.

trgt: Charles J. Givens (February 5, 1941 – July 12, 1998) was a bestselling author of three books, Wealth Without Risk and Financial Self Defense and More Wealth Without Risk. Givens founded the Charles J. Givens Organization that grew to over 600,000 members. He frequently appeared on nationally syndicated daytime television shows to promote his "financial strategies" and hosted a weekly radio program with self named "Christian Financial Planner" James L. Paris. At his peak Givens extravagant lifestyle was profiled on the popular television program Lifestyles of the Rich and Famous. His organization collapsed after a number of lawsuits, and regulatory investigations of investments sold by Paris, central Florida radio personality Jack Dicks, and former vacuum cleaner salesman Charles C Smith Jr.
mgt:
Charles J. Givens (born in Decatur, Illinois – July 12, 1998) was an American entrepreneur, author, and financial advisor who rose to prominence in the late 1980s and 1990s. Despite a challenging upbringing, Givens built a multimillion-dollar empire and authored best-selling books such as *Wealth Without Risk* and *Financial Self-Defense*. At his peak, he led the Charles J. Givens Organization, which provided financial education to over 600,000 members and generated $104 million in revenue.

However, Givens' career was marred by controversy, including numerous lawsuits and fraud claims. He was ordered to refund $14.1 million in a California fraud case and settled other legal disputes. Givens died of prostate cancer on July 12, 1998.

trgt: The Leader of Alderney is the civil leader of Alderney. Alderney is a dependency of the Bailiwick of Guernsey. Its leader has traditionally been appointed by the British Crown and has been known by various titles including Lord of Alderney, Governor of Alderney, and the current President of the States of Alderney. The President of the States of Alderney is directly elected every four years and there is no constitutional limit to the number of terms served. The current president, William Tate has held the post since 2019.
mgt: The Leader of Alderney is the highest civil authority in Alderney, currently held by the President of the States of Alderney, who is elected for a four-year term. The President also serves as chairman of the States of Alderney, with a deciding vote in the event of a tie. Historically, Alderney's leadership evolved from feudal lords and governors to the democratically elected President. Significant changes occurred after World War II, when Alderney became part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, and the role of the President was established in 1949.

trgt:
"Adrift" is the second episode of the second season of Lost and the 27th episode overall. The episode was directed by Stephen Williams and written by Steven Maeda and Leonard Dick. It first aired on September 28, 2005, on the American Broadcasting Company. In the episode, flashbacks depict Michael Dawson's struggles for the custody of his son Walt Lloyd. The realtime events show Michael and Sawyer, who have just had their raft destroyed, becoming hostile towards each other as they drift back to shore, while Kate Austen and John Locke enter the mysterious hatch and encounter Desmond Hume.

The storyline picks up the raft storyline which was left hanging after season one's finale, "Exodus", and also revisits the events seen in the previous episode, "Man of Science, Man of Faith". Originally envisioned as a Sawyer episode, the episode was rewritten to focus on Michael. The sea scenes were shot on the Hawaiian shoreline. "Adrift" had 23.17 million American viewers and stands as the second largest audience on the series' run, but received negative reviews, which focused criticism on the flashbacks, the raft scenes, and the lack of plot advancement.
mgt:
"Adrift" is the second episode of the second season of the American television series *Lost*, which originally aired on September 28, 2005. Directed by Stephen Williams and written by Steven Maeda and Leonard Dick, the episode focuses on Michael Dawson's (Harold Perrineau) flashbacks, which explore his legal battle with his ex-girlfriend over custody of their son, Walt. On the island, Michael and Sawyer (Josh Holloway) struggle to survive after their raft is destroyed by the Others, while John Locke (Terry O'Quinn) and Kate Austen (Evangeline Lilly) encounter Desmond Hume (Henry Ian Cusick) in the mysterious hatch.

The episode was originally intended to center on Sawyer but was rewritten to focus on Michael. Filming the aquatic scenes presented challenges, requiring three nights to complete. The episode also features an "easter egg" with a DHARMA Initiative logo on a shark. "Adrift" was watched by 23.17 million viewers, making it one of the most-watched episodes of the series. However, it received mostly negative reviews, with critics citing its lack of plot advancement and unengaging flashbacks, though some praised the performances and hatch storyline. It is often ranked among the weaker episodes of *Lost*.

trgt: The Fairmont Century Plaza is a landmark 19-story luxury hotel in Los Angeles. Located in Century City, the hotel forms a sweeping crescent design fronting the Avenue of the Stars, adjacent to the twin Century Plaza Towers and the 2000 Avenue of the Stars complex. At the time of its opening in 1966, the Century Plaza Hotel was the highest building in Century City, with views extending all the way to the Pacific Ocean. It was also the first hotel to have color televisions in all of its rooms. The hotel closed for renovations in 2016, and reopened on September 27, 2021. It is now operated by Fairmont Hotels and Resorts.
mgt: The Fairmont Century Plaza, originally opened in 1966 as the Century Plaza Hotel, is a luxury hotel located at 2025 Avenue of the Stars in Los Angeles, California. Designed by architect Minoru Yamasaki, the 19-story, arc-shaped building was the centerpiece of the Century City development, conceived as "a city within a city." Over the decades, the hotel has hosted numerous high-profile events, including presidential visits, state dinners, and award ceremonies. After a $2.5 billion renovation, the hotel reopened in 2021 with 400 guest rooms and 63 condominiums, alongside two new residential towers. It remains a prominent cultural and historical landmark in Los Angeles.

trgt:
Meir Tobianski (Lang-he, also Tubianski; 20 May 1904 – 30 June 1948) was an officer in the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) who was executed as a traitor on circumstantial evidence on the orders of Isser Be'eri, the first director of the IDF's intelligence branch. A year after the execution, Tobianski was exonerated of all charges.

Tobianski was born in Lithuania and served as a major in the British army during the Second World War, then a captain in the Haganah, and was later sworn into the IDF on 28 June 1948, during the 1948 Arab-Israeli War. He was also the former commander of Camp Schneller, a military base in Jerusalem. In June 1948 Tobianski had been transferred to command of Jerusalem airstrips. He was an employee of the British-run Jerusalem Electric Corporation. Suspected of passing information on targets for Jordanian artillery, he was taken into custody and sentenced to death by firing squad in a drumhead court-martial.
mgt:
Meir Tobianski (20 May 1904 – 30 June 1948) was a Lithuanian-born Israeli soldier and engineer who was wrongfully executed during the 1948 Arab-Israeli War after being accused of espionage. Born in Kovno (modern Kaunas, Lithuania), Tobianski served in the Lithuanian Army and later immigrated to Mandatory Palestine, where he joined the Haganah and worked as an engineer. During World War II, he served as a major in the British Army's Royal Engineers. 

In June 1948, Tobianski was subjected to a drumhead court-martial and executed by firing squad after being accused of passing information to the enemy. He was denied legal representation and the right to appeal. A posthumous inquiry later exonerated him, revealing the trial and execution were unjust. Tobianski was reburied with full military honors in 1949, and his case became a symbol of miscarriage of justice. His story has been commemorated in literature, poetry, and film.

trgt: The Henschel Hs 126 was a German two-seat reconnaissance and observation aircraft of World War II that was derived from the Henschel Hs 122. The pilot was seated in a protected cockpit under the parasol wing and the gunner in an open rear cockpit. The prototype aircraft frame was that of a Hs 122A fitted with a Junkers engine. The Hs 126 was well received for its good short takeoff and low-speed characteristics which were needed at the time. It was put into service for a few years, but was soon superseded by the general-purpose, STOL Fieseler Fi 156 Storch and the medium-range Focke-Wulf Fw 189 "flying eye".
mgt: The Henschel Hs 126 was a German two-seat reconnaissance and observation aircraft used primarily by the Luftwaffe during World War II. Developed in the late 1930s, it entered service in 1938 after operational evaluation during the Spanish Civil War. The Hs 126 proved effective in early campaigns, including the invasion of Poland and the Battle of France, but suffered losses when intercepted by Allied fighters. It remained the Luftwaffe's main short-range reconnaissance aircraft until 1942, when it was replaced by the Focke-Wulf Fw 189. The aircraft also saw use in North Africa and Greece and was later adapted for roles such as glider towing and night ground attacks.

page_title: The University City Home Rental Trust
trgt: The University City Home Rental Trust (UCHRT) was an experiment in maintaining racial integration of a neighborhood. It took place in University City, Missouri from 1968 to 1990. One of its leading investors was Leo Drey. His wife, Kay Drey, was its chief spokesperson. The UCHRT bought houses in a part of the city into which African Americans were moving, as whites were moving out. The houses were rented to white families with school-age children. The goal was to keep the neighborhood and the local public school integrated. In 1979, the St. Louis Post-Dispatch said, "The project--which experts say may be the only one of its kind in the United States--appears to have promoted integration successfully."
mgt: The University City Home Rental Trust (UCHRT) was a housing initiative established in 1968 in University City, Missouri, to promote racial integration and counteract white flight during a period of demographic change. Spearheaded by Rev. William G. Lorenz and supported by community leaders like Kay Drey, the trust aimed to attract white families to the racially mixed area near Pershing Elementary School by purchasing and renting renovated homes. Despite initial financial challenges and controversy, the trust grew to own 35 houses at its peak and played a significant role in maintaining integration in the neighborhood. By the mid-1980s, the trust began winding down, selling its properties and declaring its mission a success.

trgt:
Wilson's snipe (Gallinago delicata) is a small, stocky shorebird. The genus name gallinago is New Latin for a woodcock or snipe from Latin gallina, "hen" and the suffix -ago, "resembling". The specific delicata is Latin for "dainty".

This species was considered to be a subspecies of the common snipe (G. gallinago) until 2003 when it was given its own species status, though not all authorities recognized this immediately. Wilson's snipe differs from the latter species in having a narrower white trailing edge to the wings, and eight pairs of tail feathers instead of the typical seven of the common snipe. Its common name commemorates the American ornithologist Alexander Wilson.
mgt: The Wilson's snipe (*Gallinago delicata*) is a small, well-camouflaged wading bird in the family Scolopacidae, found across North America and parts of Russia. Measuring 23–28 cm (9.1–11.0 in) in length, it has a long, straight bill, mottled brown plumage, and pointed wings. It breeds in wetlands, tundra, and meadows in northern regions and migrates to the southern United States, the Caribbean, and South America, though some populations are year-round residents on the U.S. Pacific coast. Known for its unique "winnowing" courtship display, the species forages in soft mud for insects, earthworms, and plant material. Despite past population declines, it is currently listed as Least Concern by the IUCN.

trgt: Tbilisoba (Georgian: თბილისობა) is an annual October festival which celebrates the diversity and history of Tbilisi, the capital of Georgia. It was first held on October 28, 1979, and has since become an established tradition. The festival features open-air concerts of traditional music and dancing and various cultural events, centered on Old Tbilisi, the historical part of the city. Beyond celebrating the city's past and present, people from all over Georgia represent their region at the fair of the harvest and Rtveli. Awarding honorary citizenship of Tbilisi by the city government also occurs in the framework of Tbilisoba.
mgt: Tbilisoba is an annual festival held in Tbilisi, Georgia, celebrating the city’s history, culture, and traditions. Established in 1979 during the Soviet era under Eduard Shevardnadze, the festival was intended to honor the capital and promote new "socialist traditions." Over time, it became a celebration of Tbilisi's 1,500-year history, fostering a deeper connection to Georgian national heritage. The festival was suspended during the civil unrest of the early 1990s but was revived in 1995. Since then, it has been held annually, typically in late October, featuring various cultural and historical events.

trgt:
An ultrasonic motor is a type of piezoelectric motor powered by the ultrasonic vibration of a component, the stator, placed against another component, the rotor or slider depending on the scheme of operation (rotation or linear translation). Ultrasonic motors differ from other piezoelectric motors in several ways, though both typically use some form of piezoelectric material, most often lead zirconate titanate and occasionally lithium niobate or other single-crystal materials. The most obvious difference is the use of resonance to amplify the vibration of the stator in contact with the rotor in ultrasonic motors. Ultrasonic motors also offer arbitrarily large rotation or sliding distances, while piezoelectric actuators are limited by the static strain that may be induced in the piezoelectric element.

One common application of ultrasonic motors is in camera lenses where they are used to move lens elements as part of the auto-focus system. Ultrasonic motors replace the noisier and often slower micro-motor in this application.
mgt:
An ultrasonic motor is a type of electric motor that uses ultrasonic vibrations in a stator to generate motion in a rotor, typically through dry friction. These motors operate by modulating frictional forces at the stator-rotor interface, enabling motion beyond a single vibration cycle. Two primary vibration methods are used: traveling-wave and standing-wave. Early designs in the 1970s utilized standing-wave vibration, while later advancements favored traveling-wave vibration for improved efficiency and reduced wear. High-torque hybrid transducer motors combine axial and torsional vibrations for enhanced performance.

Ultrasonic motors are capable of operating at high frequencies, with vibration displacements ranging from micrometers to nanometers, depending on the frequency. Most motors are contact-based, though non-contact versions using working fluids like air have been developed.

The technology has been widely adopted in consumer electronics, particularly in autofocus systems for photographic lenses, with Canon pioneering its use in the late 1980s.

trgt:
Clare Rita Mary Maguire (born 15 September 1988) is an English singer-songwriter. She was signed to Universal Music Group, Polydor Records in 2009. She was rated in fifth place in the BBC Sound of 2011 list of the top 15 most promising new artists. Maguire was also singled out as one of MTV's Brand New: For 2011 Acts. Her voice has been compared to Stevie Nicks and Annie Lennox. Maguire's debut album, Light After Dark, was released on 25 February 2011 peaking at number seven in the Official UK Top 40, and achieving the BPI Silver Sales Award in the UK.

In 2016, she announced the forthcoming release of her second album Stranger Things Have Happened on 27 May with the song "Elizabeth Taylor". The album was released on Virgin Records.
mgt:
Clare Maguire (born 15 September 1988) is an English singer and songwriter from Solihull, West Midlands. Known for her deep, powerful voice, her music spans genres such as pop, soul, jazz, blues, and electropop. Maguire gained early attention by posting demos on MySpace, leading to a record deal with Polydor Records in 2009. Her debut album, *Light After Dark* (2011), achieved commercial success, reaching number 7 on the UK Albums Chart and earning BPI Silver certification. 

Following a period of personal challenges, Maguire returned with a series of EPs and her second studio album, *Stranger Things Have Happened* (2016), which showcased a blues-influenced sound. She has collaborated with artists like Mike Skinner and High Contrast and continues to release music, including the 2021 vinyl reissue of *Light After Dark*.

trgt: The E3 Tug Project is a joint R&D project between Smit International, Damen Shipyards Gorinchem and Alewijnse Marine Technology. The goal of the project is to create an energy efficient tugboat engine to be integrated into future tugboat designs. The purpose of which is to alleviate unnecessary ship pollution by studying the operational patterns of harbor tug use. The Port of Rotterdam is spearheading the project with a goal that by the 2020 it will have reduced its air pollution by ten percent.
mgt: The E3 Tug Project is an initiative focused on developing tugboats that are efficient in operations, environmentally friendly, and economically viable. Spearheaded by Smit International and Damen Shipyards Gorinchem, the project is divided into three stages. The first stage involves studying the existing Smit Elbe tug to gather data. The second stage focuses on designing a new propulsion system to enhance efficiency and reduce emissions. The final stage envisions a future tugboat design incorporating advanced technologies, such as LNG power, that are not yet widely feasible.

trgt:
George Frederick Blanda (September 17, 1927 – September 27, 2010) was an American football placekicker and quarterback who played professionally in the American Football League (AFL) and National Football League (NFL). Blanda played 26 seasons of professional football, the most in the sport's history, and had scored more points than anyone in history at the time of his retirement.

Blanda retired from pro football in August 1976 as the oldest player to ever play at the age of 48. One of only two players to play in four different decades (the other being John Carney), he holds the record for most extra points made (943) and attempted (959). During his career, he played under head coaches Bear Bryant, George Halas, Clem Crowe, Lou Rymkus, Wally Lemm, Pop Ivy, Sammy Baugh, Hugh Taylor, John Rauch, and John Madden.
mgt:
George Blanda (September 17, 1927 – September 27, 2010) was an American professional football player who had a record-setting 26-season career as a quarterback and placekicker in the National Football League (NFL) and American Football League (AFL). Known for his longevity and versatility, Blanda played for the Chicago Bears, Baltimore Colts, Houston Oilers, and Oakland Raiders from 1949 to 1975. He won three AFL championships, was named AFL MVP in 1961, and was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 1981.

Blanda set numerous records, including most career points scored (2,002) at the time of his retirement, and he remains the oldest player to have played in an NFL game at age 48. He was also the first player drafted in fantasy football history. Blanda passed away in 2010 at the age of 83, leaving a lasting legacy in professional football.

trgt: Hadia Tajik (born 18 July 1983) is a Pakistani-Norwegian jurist, journalist and politician from the Labour Party. She served as Minister of Labour and Social Inclusion from 2021 to 2022. She previously served as Minister of Culture from 2012 to 2013. She was 29 years of age at the time and became the youngest minister to serve in the Norwegian government. She is the first Cabinet member that is a Muslim. Tajik has served as a Member of Parliament representing Rogaland since 2017, and Oslo from 2009 to 2017. She was also the party's deputy leader from 2015 until 2022.
mgt: Hadia Tajik (born 18 July 1983) is a Norwegian politician, lawyer, and journalist who has served as a Member of Parliament for the Labour Party since 2009. She was the youngest minister in Norwegian history and the first Muslim to serve in the cabinet, holding the position of Minister of Culture from 2012 to 2013. She later served as Minister of Labour and Social Inclusion from 2021 to 2022. Tajik has also been the deputy leader of the Labour Party (2015–2022). Born to Pakistani immigrants in Rogaland, she holds degrees in journalism, human rights, and law.

trgt:
A zograscope is an optical device for magnifying flat pictures that also has the property of enhancing the sense of the depth shown in the picture. It consists of a large magnifying lens through which the picture is viewed. Devices containing only the lens are sometimes referred to as graphoscopes. Other models have the lens mounted on a stand in front of an angled mirror. This allows someone to sit at a table and to look through the lens at the picture flat on the table. Pictures viewed in this way need to be left-right reversed; this is obvious in the case of writing. A print made for this purpose, typically with extensive graphical projection perspective, is called a vue d'optique or "perspective view".

Zograscopes were popular during the later half of the 18th century as parlour entertainments. Most existing ones from that time are fine furniture, with turned stands, mouldings, brass fittings, and fine finishes.

According to Michael Quinion, the origin of the term is lost, but it is also known as a diagonal mirror, as an optical pillar machine, or as an optical diagonal machine.

In Japan, the zograscope became known as 和蘭眼鏡 (Oranda megane, 'Dutch glasses') or 覗き眼鏡 (nozoki megane 'peeping glasses'), and the pictures were known as 眼鏡絵 (megane-e, 'optique picture') 繰絵 (karakuri-e 'tricky picture').
mgt:
A zograscope is an optical device, first developed in the early 18th century, that enhances the depth perception and realism of images, creating an immersive viewing experience. Often described as an early form of "virtual reality," the zograscope magnifies images using a biconvex or plano-convex lens, allowing viewers to perceive objects in greater depth. This effect is achieved by minimizing depth cues that indicate flatness, such as blocking the edges of the image and collimating light to prevent accommodation. Additionally, binocular stereopsis, chromostereopsis, and color contrast further enhance the perception of depth.

Zograscopes were particularly popular in France, where they allowed presbyopic individuals to view images clearly without glasses, though their enduring appeal was largely due to the realistic appearance of the depicted scenes. Early prints often used hand-tinted colors to exploit depth cues, with saturated colors in the foreground and pale washes in the background.

Modern variations of the zograscope can be constructed using simple materials, such as a fresnel lens and a frame, to enhance the depth of images displayed on computer monitors. The device remains a fascinating example of early optical technology and its ability to create lifelike visual experiences.

page_title: Center of gravity of an aircraft
trgt: The center of gravity (CG) of an aircraft is the point over which the aircraft would balance. Its position is calculated after supporting the aircraft on at least two sets of weighing scales or load cells and noting the weight shown on each set of scales or load cells. The center of gravity affects the stability of the aircraft. To ensure the aircraft is safe to fly, the center of gravity must fall within specified limits established by the aircraft manufacturer.
mgt: The center of gravity (CG) of an aircraft is a critical factor in ensuring safe and stable flight. It is calculated along the longitudinal axis, with some helicopters also requiring lateral CG calculations. Proper weight distribution, including fixed and removable items, is essential to keep the CG within manufacturer-prescribed limits. An out-of-range CG can lead to severe control issues, affecting stability, maneuverability, and safety. Aircraft operators must account for fuel burn, passenger distribution, and cargo placement to maintain balance throughout the flight. Helicopters are particularly sensitive to CG shifts.

trgt:
The Holliday Transfer Facility (TDCJ Identification Code: NF, also referred to as the Holliday Transfer Unit), is a Texas Department of Criminal Justice transfer facility for men located in Huntsville, Texas. Holliday is along Interstate 45 and.5 miles (0.80 km) north of Texas State Highway 30. The unit, on a 1,412-acre (571 ha) plot of land, is co-located with the Wynne Unit. 

Holliday, one of the largest transfer facilities in Texas, is across the street from the Texas Prison Museum. Holliday is one of two prisons in the TDCJ that, as of 2003, is named after an African-American.
mgt: The C. A. Holliday Transfer Facility is a Texas Department of Criminal Justice (TDCJ) prison in Huntsville, Texas, named after C. A. Holliday, an African-American community activist and pastor. Opened in January 1994, the $30 million facility has a capacity of approximately 2,000 beds and was the first TDCJ prison to implement a smoking ban, preceding a system-wide ban in 1995. The facility, described as an industrial-style complex with sheet metal siding and guard towers, serves as a transfer unit for inmates transitioning between jail and long-term prison facilities.

trgt:
RPT Realty, formerly known as RAMCO Properties Trust and Ramco-Gershenson Properties Trust, is a publicly traded real estate investment trust that owns and operates open-air shopping centers. As of August 4, 2021, the company owned 50 shopping centers comprising 12.6 million square feet.

Notable properties owned by the company include The Shops on Lane Avenue in Upper Arlington, Ohio; Woodbury Lakes in Woodbury, Minnesota; Bedford Marketplace in Bedford, Massachusetts; Lakehills Plaza in Austin, Texas; River City Marketplace in Jacksonville, Florida; Mission Bay Plaza in Boca Raton, Florida; The Crossroads in Royal Palm Beach, Florida; Coral Creek Shops in Coconut Creek, Florida.
mgt: RPT Realty is a publicly traded real estate investment trust (REIT) specializing in open-air shopping centers. Founded in 1955 by Aaron and William Gershenson as A & W Management Company, the company transitioned to public ownership in 1996 through a reverse merger with RPS Realty Trust, adopting the name Ramco-Gershenson Properties Trust. It was later rebranded as RPT Realty in 2018. Headquartered in New York City, the company is a component of the S&P 600 and reported $234 million in revenue and $91 million in net income in 2019. RPT Realty manages assets totaling $1.918 billion with 104 employees.

trgt:
Ajantrik (known internationally as The Unmechanical, The Mechanical Man or The Pathetic Fallacy) is a 1958 Indian Bengali film written and directed by revered parallel filmmaker Ritwik Ghatak. The film is adapted from a Bengali short story of the same name written by Subodh Ghosh.

A comedy-drama film, Ajantrik is one of the earliest Indian films to portray an inanimate object, in this case an automobile, as a character in the story. It achieves this through the use of sounds recorded post-production to emphasize the car's bodily functions and movements.

The film was considered for a special entry in the Venice Film Festival in 1959.
mgt:
*Ajantrik* is a 1958 Indian Bengali-language film directed by Ritwik Ghatak, based on a short story by Subodh Ghosh. The film follows Bimal, a lonely taxi driver in a small industrial town, and his deep attachment to his dilapidated 1920 Chevrolet taxi, Jagaddal. Through episodic storytelling, it explores themes of human connection, industrialization, and modernity. 

The film received international attention, including at the Venice Film Festival, where it captivated audiences despite the lack of subtitles. Critics have praised its portrayal of the conflict between nature and mechanized civilization, with poet Alokeranjan Dasgupta describing it as a unique contribution to modernist cinema.

trgt:
Health Canada (HC; Lang-fr) is the department of the Government of Canada responsible for national health policy. The department itself is also responsible for numerous federal health-related agencies, including the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) and the Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC), among others. These organizations help to ensure compliance with federal law in a variety of healthcare, agricultural, and pharmaceutical activities. This responsibility also involves extensive collaboration with various other federal- and provincial-level organizations in order to ensure the safety of food, health, and pharmaceutical products—including the regulation of health research and pharmaceutical manufacturing/testing facilities.

The department is responsible to Parliament through the minister of health—presently Jean-Yves Duclos—as part of the federal health portfolio. The minister is assisted by the associate minister of health, and minister of mental health and addictions—presently Carolyn Bennett. The deputy minister of health, the senior most civil servant within the department, is responsible for the day-to-day leadership and operations of the department and reports directly to the minister.

Originally created as the "Department of Health" in 1919—in the wake of the Spanish flu crisis—what is known as Health Canada today was formed in 1993 from the former Health and Welfare Canada department (established in 1944), which split into two separate units; the other department being Human Resources and Labour Canada.
mgt:
Health Canada (French: Santé Canada) is the federal department responsible for overseeing national health policy in Canada. With a workforce of over 11,000 employees as of 2020 and an annual budget of $3.9 billion for 2021–22, the department is led by the Minister of Health, currently Jean-Yves Duclos, and the Associate Minister of Health and Minister of Mental Health and Addictions, Carolyn Bennett. Its operations are supported by the Health Portfolio, which includes agencies such as the Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) and the Canadian Institutes of Health Research.

Health Canada enforces regulations on consumer products, drugs, medical devices, and natural health products through its Compliance and Enforcement Directorate. It also manages a special access program for medications not commercially available in Canada. The department has faced criticism for its regulation of natural health products and delays in drug approval processes, with calls for stricter oversight and faster reviews.

Internationally, Health Canada collaborates on public health initiatives, including the acquisition of botulism antitoxins to address bioterrorism threats. During the COVID-19 pandemic, it played a key role in vaccine approval and policy. Despite its efforts, the department has been scrutinized for transparency and efficiency in drug safety and approval processes.

trgt: Marcus Furius Camillus (c. 26 BC – after AD 18) was a Roman senator and a close friend of the emperor Tiberius. Despite being without previous military experience, he enjoyed several successes against the Numidian rebel Tacfarinas while serving as governor of Africa, and was even praised in public by the Emperor and awarded triumphal honours. The historian Tacitus, in his Annales (published AD 109), joked that Camillus subsequently lived invisibly enough to survive this great honour (an allusion to the endless series of executions of prominent senators on spurious treason charges under Tiberius).
mgt: Marcus Furius Camillus was a Roman senator and consul in 8 AD, belonging to the ancient gens Furia, which had declined in prominence by his time. He served as Proconsul of Africa in 17 AD, where he successfully suppressed a major revolt led by the Numidian insurgent Tacfarinas, despite facing significant challenges in guerrilla warfare. For his victory, Emperor Tiberius awarded him triumphal ornaments, a rare honor for someone outside the Julio-Claudian dynasty. Camillus was the father of Livia Medullina Camilla and Lucius Arruntius Camillus Scribonianus, the latter known for leading a failed coup against Emperor Claudius.

trgt: The heute-show ("(the) today show"; stylized in its logo as heute SHOW) is a German late-night satirical television program airing every Friday evening on public broadcasting channel ZDF. A conceptual adaptation of The Daily Show with Jon Stewart, it is presented by German comedian and journalist Oliver Welke. Heute show presents the weekly news with funny or sarcastic and cynical comments, especially focusing on issues that are subject of the latest political discussions in Germany. Welke argues that he might help to make people interested in politics and might help to point out "what goes the wrong way" in parliamentary debates, in ministries, in institutions, and in parties. Sometimes, he simply mocks "people who deserve it" in his show.
mgt: *heute-show* is a German satirical news program that premiered on May 26, 2009, on ZDF. Hosted by Oliver Welke, the show combines comedy and political commentary, parodying current events and media coverage. Initially airing monthly, it transitioned to a weekly format in 2010 due to its popularity. Known for its sharp humor, *heute-show* has achieved high ratings, with its peak episode in 2014 drawing 3.9 million viewers. The program has featured notable guests from politics, journalism, and entertainment, and has received accolades, including being named the best satirical show at the 2017 Goldene Kamera awards. Recorded in Cologne, the show continues to be a prominent fixture in German television satire.

trgt: Tre Motetter (Three Motets), FS 139, Op. 55, is a composition for unaccompanied choir by Carl Nielsen. It is a setting of three quotations in Latin from different psalms chosen by the composer and his wife Anne Marie Carl-Nielsen. The three motets, Afflictus sum, Dominus regit me and Benedictus Dominus were first performed on 11 April 1930 at the Ny Carlsberg Glyptotek by Mogens Wöldike and the Palestrina choir, to whom they are dedicated. Among the composer's last works, they were published in 1931 by the Skandinavisk Musikforlag in Copenhagen.
mgt: *Tre Motetter* (FS 139, Op. 55) is a set of three a cappella motets composed by Carl Nielsen in 1929, with texts drawn from Psalms 38, 23, and 31 in Latin. Dedicated to Mogens Wöldike and the Palestrina Choir, the motets reflect Nielsen's study of Renaissance polyphony and were first performed on April 11, 1930, in Copenhagen. Scored for SATB choir, the works exhibit a "Neo-Renaissance" style, with music composed to evoke the mood of the Biblical texts. They were published in 1931 by Skandinavisk Musikforlag.

trgt:
WNED-FM (94.5 MHz) is a non-commercial radio station licensed to Buffalo, New York. WNED-FM offers a classical music radio format. It is owned by the Western New York Public Broadcasting Association (formerly the Western New York Educational TV Association), doing business as Buffalo Toronto Public Media. The organization also operates PBS network affiliate Channel 17 WNED-TV and FM 88.7 WBFO (which offers a news/talk format and programming from NPR). While WNED-FM airs no commercials, it does conduct periodic pledge drives on the air to seek donations for the station. WNED-FM has local hosts in most day-parts, including weekends.

Programming on WNED-FM is simulcast on WNJA 89.7 FM in Jamestown, New York for listeners in Southwestern New York and some parts of Pennsylvania. WNED-FM's studios and offices are at Horizons Plaza on Lower Terrace in Buffalo, and the transmitter is off Zimmerman Road in Hamburg, New York. WNED-FM and TV also maintain an office in Toronto for listeners and contributors in Canada.
mgt:
WNED-FM (94.5 MHz) is a non-commercial classical music radio station licensed to Buffalo, New York, and serving Western New York and the Toronto area. Owned by Buffalo Toronto Public Media, it operates alongside sister stations WBFO and WNED-TV. The station, branded as "WNED Classical," first signed on as WEBR-FM in 1960 and adopted its current call sign in 1976, transitioning to a classical music format the following year. WNED-FM is a grandfathered "superpower" Class B FM station, broadcasting at 94,000 watts, with a signal that extends into Canada, including Toronto and the Niagara Peninsula.

The station produces extensive local programming, collaborates with the Buffalo Philharmonic Orchestra, and features unique segments like "JoAnn's Classical Corner." It also operates in HD Radio, with its HD2 subchannel airing JazzWorks. In recent years, WNED-FM has engaged listeners through initiatives like its Classical Top 100 playlist, with Beethoven's Symphony No. 9 topping the list in 2020, 2021, and 2022.

trgt:
Bolivar County (/ˈbɒlɪvər/ BOL-i-vər) is a county located on the western border of the U.S. state of Mississippi. As of the 2020 census, the population was 30,985. Its county seats are Rosedale and Cleveland. The county is named in honor of Simón Bolívar, early 19th-century leader of the liberation of several South American colonies from Spain.

The Cleveland, Mississippi, Micropolitan Statistical Area includes all of Bolivar County. It is located in the Mississippi Delta, or Yazoo Basin, of Mississippi. This area was first developed for cotton plantations. Large industrial-scale agricultural operations have reduced the number of farm workers needed, and the population is half of its peak in 1930. Today, soybeans, corn, and rice are also commodity crops.
mgt: Bolivar County is a county located in the Mississippi Delta region of the U.S. state of Mississippi. Founded in 1836 and named after Simón Bolívar, it has two county seats, Rosedale and Cleveland, with Cleveland being the largest city. The county covers 906 square miles, making it the second-largest in Mississippi by land area. Historically, Bolivar County's economy was rooted in agriculture, with a significant enslaved population before the Civil War. It later became a center for African American civil rights activism and remains predominantly Black today. The county faces challenges such as poverty and low life expectancy, with male life expectancy among the shortest in the U.S. It is served by multiple school districts and community colleges.

trgt: Boomplay or Boomplay Music is a media streaming and download service developed by Transsnet Music Limited. The service was first launched in Nigeria in 2015 by TECNO Mobile, Transsion Holdings. Boomplay has a freemium and subscription based service; basic features are free with advertisements or limitations, while additional features, such as download for offline play and ad-free listening are offered via paid subscriptions. The service is currently available for Web via www.boomplay.com, Android and iOS use. As of August 3, 2018, Boomplay Music recorded 10 million installations off the Google Play and App store. It currently has over 70 million monthly users (MAU) with a catalogue of over 85 million songs.
mgt: Boomplay is a music streaming service launched in August 2015 in Lagos, Nigeria, focusing on African and urban music content. Available on web, Android, iOS, and KaiOS, it has over 70 million monthly active users. The platform introduced premium subscriptions in 2016, offering ad-free listening and offline downloads. Boomplay has partnered with major music labels, including Universal Music Group, Warner Music Group, and Sony Music Group, to distribute extensive catalogs of global and local artists. It has expanded operations across several African countries, including Kenya, Ghana, and Tanzania. In 2021, Boomplay's streaming data was integrated into Billboard charts, further solidifying its influence in the music industry.

trgt:
Bristol is a city in the State of Tennessee. Located in Sullivan County, its population was 26,702 at the 2010 census. It is the twin city of Bristol, Virginia, which lies directly across the state line between Tennessee and Virginia. The boundary between the two cities is also the state line, which runs along State Street in their common downtown district. Bristol is a principal city of the Kingsport−Bristol−Bristol, TN-VA metropolitan statistical area, which is a component of the Johnson City−Kingsport−Bristol, TN-VA combined statistical area − commonly known as the "Tri-Cities" region.

Bristol is probably best known for being the site of some of the first commercial recordings of country music, showcasing Jimmie Rodgers and the Carter Family, and later a favorite venue of mountain musician Uncle Charlie Osborne. The U.S. Congress recognized Bristol as the "Birthplace of Country Music" in 1998, and the Birthplace of Country Music Museum is located in Bristol. It also is the birthplace of Tennessee Ernie Ford.

Bristol is also the site of Bristol Motor Speedway, a NASCAR short track that is one of the best-known motorsports facilities in the country.

The world's fifth-largest coal producer, Contura Energy, is based in Bristol.
mgt:
Bristol, Tennessee, is a city in Sullivan County, located in the northeastern corner of the state. Incorporated in 1856 and named after Bristol, England, the city is part of the Tri-Cities region and shares its downtown area with Bristol, Virginia, along State Street. Known as the "Birthplace of Country Music," Bristol was recognized by the U.S. Congress in 1998 for its pivotal role in the early development of country music, particularly through the 1927 Bristol Sessions, which featured artists like the Carter Family and Jimmie Rodgers. The Birthplace of Country Music Museum, opened in 2014, commemorates this legacy.

Bristol is home to the Bristol Motor Speedway, a major NASCAR venue, and hosts the annual Bristol Rhythm & Roots Reunion music festival. The city has a humid subtropical climate with cooler temperatures due to its elevation. As of the 2020 census, Bristol had a population of 27,147. The city operates its own school system, including Tennessee High School, and is served by the Bristol Police Department. Bristol's economy and culture are closely tied to its musical heritage, motorsports, and its unique position straddling the Tennessee-Virginia state line.

trgt:
Caguas (IPA-es, IPA-es) is a city and municipality of Puerto Rico located in the Central Mountain Range of Puerto Rico, south of San Juan and Trujillo Alto, west of Gurabo and San Lorenzo, and east of Aguas Buenas, Cidra, and Cayey. Caguas was founded in 1775. The municipality had a population of 127,244 at the 2020 census.

Caguas is located 20 miles (32 km) from San Juan. It is located in the Valle de Caguas or Caguas Valley (also known as Valle del Turabo or the Turabo Valley), at the eastern ranges of the Central Mountain Range. It is known as La Ciudad Criolla (Criollo City), Valle del Turabo (the Turabo Valley) and La Cuna de los Trovadores (The Cradle of the Trovadores). Its name originates from the Taíno cacique Caguax, who was a regional chief. Caguas is a principal city of both the San Juan-Caguas-Guaynabo Metropolitan Statistical Area and the San Juan-Caguas-Fajardo Combined Statistical Area.
mgt:
Caguas is a city and municipality in central Puerto Rico, located in the Caguas Valley, approximately 30 minutes from San Juan. Founded on January 1, 1775, as San Sebastián del Piñal de Caguax, it was named after a Taíno chief, Caguax. The city played a significant role in Puerto Rico's agricultural and industrial history, particularly in sugarcane production and later as a center for pharmaceutical manufacturing. Caguas is part of the San Juan Metropolitan Area and serves as a commuter town, with a population of 127,244 as of 2020.

Known as "La Ciudad Criolla" (The Creole City) and "La Cuna de los Trovadores" (The Cradle of Trovadores), Caguas is home to cultural landmarks such as the Jardín Botánico William Miranda Marín and the Catedral Dulce Nombre de Jesús. The city also hosts notable sports teams, including the Criollos de Caguas baseball team, and celebrates its patron saint festival in July.

trgt:
Ruby Cabernet is a red Olmo grape variety that is a cross between Cabernet Sauvignon and Carignan, it can produce wines with good colour and a pleasant cherry flavour, but is mostly blended into bulk wines.

The purpose for the creation of the crossing of the grape varieties utilized to produce Ruby Cabernet was to obtain the superior quality of a Cabernet wine, and the resistance to heat of the Carignan combined in an inexpensive table wine. Even though the wine made from these grapes does not possess the distinctive flavor and the overall structure of other types of Cabernet wines, it does carry their fruitful essence.

The grape for this type of red wine was developed for California's hot climate, specially for regions such as the San Joaquin and the Napa Valleys. Ruby Cabernet has improved the quality of the bulk wines produced in these areas thanks to its natural, special acidity.
mgt:
Ruby Cabernet is a red wine grape variety created in 1936 by Dr. Harold Olmo at the University of California, Davis. A cross between Cabernet Sauvignon and Carignan, it was developed to combine the heat tolerance of Carignan with the quality of Cabernet Sauvignon. The variety is primarily grown in warm climates, with notable cultivation in California, South Africa, Australia, and Brazil. In California's San Joaquin Valley, it is often used to add color and structure to bulk wines, though careful vineyard management can yield high-quality wines. 

Ruby Cabernet is also grown in smaller quantities in Argentina, Mexico, Chile, and Israel, where it is typically blended or used in varietal wines. The vine is drought-resistant and wind-tolerant but susceptible to powdery mildew and poor fruit set in some regions. Its adaptability to hot climates has made it a valuable grape for wine production in diverse regions worldwide.

trgt: Fredrik Backman (born 2 June 1981) is a Swedish author, blogger, and columnist. He wrote A Man Called Ove (2012), Things My Son Needs to Know about the World (2012), My Grandmother Asked Me to Tell You She's Sorry (2013), Britt-Marie Was Here (2014), Beartown (2017), Us Against You (2018), Anxious People (2020), and The Winners (2022). The books were number one bestsellers in his home country of Sweden. Backman's books have been published in more than twenty-five languages.
mgt: Fredrik Backman (born 2 June 1981) is a Swedish columnist, blogger, and novelist, best known for his debut novel *A Man Called Ove* (2012), which was adapted into a film in 2015. His works, including *Beartown* and *My Grandmother Asked Me to Tell You She's Sorry*, have been translated into multiple languages and achieved international success. Backman has also written nonfiction, such as *Things My Son Needs to Know about the World*. He lives in Sweden with his wife, Neda Shafti Backman, and their two children.

trgt:
Vellatanjur or Vellatanjoor is a small village in the Thrissur, District of Kerala, India on the banks of Kechery river. This village is located 25 km NorthEast of the Thrissur city, 20 km SouthEast of the Kunnamkulam town and 18 km NorthEast of the Guruvayur township where the famous Guruvayur temple is situated.

The main occupation of the people is farming which you can see from the vast paddy fields there. The main religions there are Hinduism & Christianity. Muslims are not found in Vellatanjur due to a curse that the people there believe has been put by Kootumuchikal Amma the Thatakthamma (Local Goddess) when Tippu Sultan ravished the land during his reign.

The village is very quiet and serene due to its location on the bank of the Kechery river.
mgt:
Vellattanjur is a village in the Thrissur district of Kerala, India, governed by a Grama Panchayat. As of the 2001 Census, it had a population of 6,637, with 3,194 males and 3,443 females. The official languages are Malayalam and English, and the village falls under the Alathur Lok Sabha constituency. 

The village is notable for its religious and cultural heritage, featuring four major temples—Kootumuchi Temple, Kurooramma Srikrishna Temple, Erakad Subramnaswami Temple, and Sree Rama Temple—each hosting annual festivals with traditional processions, music, and fireworks. Vellattanjur is also home to the Fathima Matha Church, the first church in India dedicated to Our Lady of Fatima, which celebrates grand feasts in October and January. The church also operates a dispensary, serving the local community.

trgt:
Woo In-hee (Korean: 우인희, died 1981) was a North Korean actress and a mistress of Kim Jong-il.

Having reached stardom in the 1960s and 1970, Woo, renowned for her beauty, acted in dozens of films. She married Yoo Hosun, a famed film director, but she was involved in affairs outside of her marriage. In the late 1970s, she became a secret mistress of Kim Jong-il. After Woo began an affair with another man, Kim had her publicly executed in front of 6,000 people.
mgt: Woo In-hee (born late 1930s or early 1940s – 1981) was a North Korean actress renowned for her beauty and talent, earning the prestigious title of People's Actress. Rising to fame with her role in *The Tale of Chunhyang*, she starred in numerous films during the 1960s and 1970s. Woo's personal life, including her marriage to director Yoo Hosun and her affair with Kim Jong-il, led to her downfall. In 1981, she was executed by firing squad for revealing her secret relationship with Kim, and her legacy was erased from North Korean media.

trgt: Pierre Galet (28 January 1921 – 30 December 2019) was a French ampelographer and author who was an influential figure within ampelography in the 20th century and before DNA typing was widely introduced. Beginning in the 1950s, Pierre Galet introduced a system for identifying varieties based on the shape, contours and characteristics of the leaves of the vines, petioles, growing shoots, shoot tips, grape clusters, as well as the colour, size, seed content and flavour of the grapes. The impact and comprehensiveness of his work earned him the consideration as the "father of modern ampelography". He started publishing within ampelography in the 1950s and his Ph.D. thesis was presented in 1967. He has also written popular science books on grape varieties. Galet was active at the École Nationale Supérieure Agronomique de Montpellier.
mgt:
Pierre Galet (1921 – 31 December 2019) was a Monegasque-born French ampelographer and viticulturist, widely regarded as a pioneer in the field of grape identification and classification. He spent much of his career at the University of Montpellier, where he taught from 1946 to 1989 and mentored prominent ampelographers, including Paul Truel. Galet traveled extensively, identifying grape varieties and resolving legal disputes, such as those involving European Union regulations on American grapevines. He identified over 100 Pinot family varieties and corrected misidentifications, including a notable case in California involving Melon de Bourgogne.

Galet authored several influential works, including *Cépages et vignoble de France* and *Dictionnaire encyclopédique des cépages*. For his contributions, he was named an Officier de l'Ordre du Mérite Agricole and received recognition from the French vintners' association.

trgt:
Maximum PC, formerly known as boot, is an American magazine and website published by Future US. It focuses on cutting-edge PC hardware, with an emphasis on product reviews, step-by-step tutorials, and in-depth technical briefs. Component coverage areas include CPUs, motherboards, core-logic chipsets, memory, videocards, mechanical hard drives, solid-state drives, optical drives, cases, component cooling, and anything else to do with recent tech news. Additional hardware coverage is directed at smartphones, tablet computers, cameras and other consumer electronic devices that interface with consumer PCs. Software coverage focuses on games, anti-virus suites, content-editing programs, and other consumer-level applications.

Prior to September 1998, the magazine was called boot. boot and sister magazine MacAddict (now Mac|Life) launched in September 1996, when Future US shut down CD-ROM Today.

In March 2016, Future US announced that the Maximum PC website would be merged with PCGamer.com, appearing as the hardware section of the website from that point forward. The magazine was not affected by this change. As of July 2, 2018, browsing to MaximumPC.com no longer forwards to the Hardware section of PCGamer.com
mgt:
*Maximum PC* is an American monthly computing magazine published by Future US, first launched in August 1996 under the name *boot* before being rebranded as *Maximum PC* in September 1998. Based in San Francisco, the magazine focuses on product reviews, technology news, and computing advice, with a reputation for its straightforward and humorous editorial style. Products are rated on a scale of 1 to 10, with exceptional items receiving a "Kick Ass" award. Notably, *Half-Life 2* received the magazine's only "11" rating in January 2005.

The magazine reached a circulation rate-base of 250,000 in 2010 and provides an archive of back issues in PDF format on its website. It also experimented with a quarterly spinoff, *Maximum Tech*, in 2010, which ceased publication in 2011. An Italian edition was briefly published in 2004 but ended after six issues. *Maximum PC* is known for its engaging reviews, often featuring humorous "Pros and Cons" comparisons, and has included contributions from numerous freelance writers.

trgt:
Tempora is the codeword for a formerly-secret computer system that is used by the British Government Communications Headquarters (GCHQ). This system is used to buffer most Internet communications that are extracted from fibre-optic cables, so these can be processed and searched at a later time. It was tested from 2008 and became operational in late 2011.

Tempora uses intercepts on the fibre-optic cables that serve as the backbone of the Internet to gain access to large amounts of Internet users' personal data, without any individual suspicion or targeting. The intercepts are placed in the United Kingdom and overseas, with the knowledge of companies owning either the cables or landing stations.

The existence of Tempora was revealed by Edward Snowden, a former American intelligence contractor who leaked information about the program to former Guardian journalist Glenn Greenwald in May 2013 as part of his revelations of government-sponsored mass surveillance programs. Documents Snowden acquired showed that data collected by the Tempora program is shared with the National Security Agency of the United States.
mgt:
Tempora is a secret mass surveillance program operated by the British Government Communications Headquarters (GCHQ) and revealed by Edward Snowden in 2013. Launched in 2011, the program intercepts and stores vast amounts of internet and telephone data by tapping into fiber-optic cables. Tempora consists of components such as "Mastering the Internet" (MTI) and "Global Telecoms Exploitation" (GTE), and incorporates the NSA's XKeyscore system. It collects data including emails, phone calls, social media activity, and browsing history, with metadata retained for 30 days and content for three days. Snowden alleged that GCHQ collaborated with commercial companies, some of which were paid, to access data, and that the program lacked distinction between public citizens and targeted suspects.

The program has faced significant criticism and legal challenges. German officials and European Union representatives condemned Tempora for violating privacy rights, and in 2018, the European Court of Human Rights ruled it unlawful. The UK government issued media restrictions on reporting related to Tempora and other surveillance programs.

trgt:
Religious Zionism (Lang-he, translit. Tziyonut Datit) is an ideology that combines Zionism and Orthodox Judaism. Its adherents are also referred to as Dati Leumi (דָּתִי לְאֻומִּי‎ "National Religious"), and in Israel, they are most commonly known by the plural form of the first part of that term Datiim (דתיים "Religious"). The community is sometimes called כִּפָּה סְרוּגָה‎ Kippah seruga, literally, "Knitted kippah", the typical head covering which is worn by Jewish men.

Before the establishment of the State of Israel, most Religious Zionists were observant Jews who supported Zionist efforts to build a Jewish state in the Land of Israel. Religious Zionism revolves around three pillars: the Land of Israel, the People of Israel, and the Torah of Israel.
