The Hardal (חרדי לאומי‎ Ḥaredi Le'umi; lit., "Nationalist Haredi") are a sub-community, stricter in its observance, and more statist in its politics. Those Religious Zionists, who are less strict in their observance but not necessarily more liberal, are informally referred to as "dati lite".
mgt:
Religious Zionism is an ideology that combines Zionism with Orthodox Judaism, advocating for the establishment and support of a Jewish state in the Land of Israel as a fulfillment of biblical prophecy and divine will. Rooted in the teachings of Rabbi Zvi Hirsch Kalischer and later developed by Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook, Religious Zionism views the return of Jews to their ancestral homeland as a step toward ultimate redemption and the coming of the Messiah. Rabbi Kook emphasized that Zionism, even when led by secular Jews, was part of a divine plan to restore Jewish sovereignty and enable the observance of Torah commandments specific to the Land of Israel.

The movement has faced opposition from some Haredi Jews, who view Zionism as premature and contrary to Jewish law. Religious Zionism has influenced political parties, such as the Mizrachi movement and its successors, and established educational institutions, including yeshivot and midrashot. It also promotes military service through programs like Hesder, integrating Torah study with service in the Israel Defense Forces.

trgt:
The 2015–16 Pittsburgh Penguins season was the 49th season for the National Hockey League franchise that was established on June 5, 1967. Their regular season games began on October 8, 2015 against the Dallas Stars. On December 12, the team had a record of 15–10–3. The organization then fired head coach Mike Johnston and replaced him with Mike Sullivan, head coach of the organization's American Hockey League affiliate in Wilkes-Barre.

The Penguins qualified for the playoffs for the tenth consecutive season. They earned second place in the Metropolitan Division with 104 points. They began the 2016 Stanley Cup playoffs on April 13 against the New York Rangers, the team they were eliminated by in the Stanley Cup playoffs both of the previous two seasons. They went on to beat the Rangers in five games. In the second round the Pens defeated the Washington Capitals, the winners of the Presidents'Trophy, in six games. The team then played in their first Eastern Conference Final since 2013 against the Tampa Bay Lightning. The Pens defeated the Lightning in seven games, despite trailing the series 3–2 after Game 5, to earn the franchise's fifth berth in the Stanley Cup Final. In the Final, the Penguins defeated the San Jose Sharks in six games to win the franchise's fourth league title.
mgt:
The 2015–16 Pittsburgh Penguins season was the franchise's 49th season in the National Hockey League (NHL). Under the leadership of general manager Jim Rutherford and head coaches Mike Johnston (October–December) and Mike Sullivan (December–June), the team finished second in the Metropolitan Division and Eastern Conference with a 48–26–8 record. Captain Sidney Crosby led the team in goals (36) and points (85), while Kris Letang led in assists (51) and penalty minutes (66). Marc-André Fleury recorded 35 wins as the starting goaltender, with rookie Matt Murray emerging as a key contributor during the playoffs.

The Penguins secured their fourth Stanley Cup championship, defeating the San Jose Sharks in the Stanley Cup Final. The team wore their alternate jerseys for all home games during the playoffs. Key contributors included Crosby, who won the Conn Smythe Trophy as playoff MVP, and Murray, who posted a 2.00 goals-against average. The Penguins' success was bolstered by strong performances from their core players and mid-season adjustments under Sullivan's coaching.

The season also featured notable transactions and draft picks, as well as high attendance at PPG Paints Arena. The Penguins' championship marked a return to prominence for the franchise, setting the stage for continued success in subsequent seasons.

trgt:
A phantom border (Lang-de) is an informal delineation following the approximate course of an abolished political border, associated with demographic differences on each side as a continuing legacy of historical division, despite official geopolitical union. Not all former political borders are today phantom borders. Factors that may increase the likelihood of a political border becoming a phantom border upon dissolution, include: short time elapsed since the border's dissolution, long existence of the former border, imporousity of the former border, and divergent characteristics of the political entity formerly governing one side of the border.

Phantom borders have many different implications: in Ukraine they are associated with conflict, while in countries such as Romania they play an important part in relations with neighboring countries.
mgt: Phantom borders are historical political boundaries that continue to influence contemporary social, cultural, and political landscapes. The concept, articulated through the Phantomgrenzen project, highlights how these former borders persist or re-emerge as markers of historical conquests and annexations. In Germany, the divide between East and West reflects in voting patterns, economic disparities, and cultural preferences, rooted in the legacy of Prussia and East Germany. In Poland, historical partitions by Prussia, Austria, and Russia have shaped regional political tendencies, with western regions favoring liberal parties and central-southern areas leaning conservative. Romania's phantom borders reflect differences between Ottoman and Habsburg rule, influencing ethnic diversity and political behavior. Similarly, Ukraine's historical divisions under various empires manifest in regional electoral and cultural divides.

trgt:
Common ownership refers to holding the assets of an organization, enterprise or community indivisibly rather than in the names of the individual members or groups of members as common property.

Forms of common ownership exist in every economic system. Common ownership of the means of production is a central goal of communist political movements as it is seen as a necessary democratic mechanism for the creation and continued function of a communist society. Advocates make a distinction between collective ownership and common property as the former refers to property owned jointly by agreement of a set of colleagues, such as producer cooperatives, whereas the latter refers to assets that are completely open for access, such as a public park freely available to everyone.
mgt:
Common ownership refers to the shared ownership of resources, property, or assets by a group or society. It has been practiced historically in various forms, including by early Christian communities and religious groups like the Hutterites and Bruderhof, where property is owned collectively to support members. In capitalist economies, common ownership is found in non-profit organizations, cooperatives, and public bodies. 

In Marxist theory, common ownership is a key feature of a classless, communist society, distinguished from earlier communal systems by its basis in technological and social advancements. The concept also plays a role in antitrust economics, where overlapping ownership of competing firms by large investors can reduce competition. Additionally, contract theory explores the implications of common ownership on investment incentives, with debates on its efficiency depending on the context of human or physical capital investments.

trgt: The original Citroën Rosalie was a light-weight racing car that established a succession of records at the Montlhéry racing circuit. More generally the Rosalie was a range of three models/sizes of automobile that comprised the core of Citroën's model range between 1932 and 1938. The three models were originally designated respectively the 8CV, the 10CV and the 15CV, the numbers defining the cars' fiscal horsepower which in turn defined the approximate engine size of each model. After the introduction of the new Traction Avant, the lineup was modified and at the 1934 Paris Salon the two smaller models became the 7UA and the 11UA, now with the overhead-valve engines from the Traction. Starting with the 1933 model year Citroen offered the 1,766 cc 11UD diesel engine option.
mgt:
The Citroën Rosalie is an executive car produced by the French manufacturer Citroën from 1932 to 1938. It was available in three main versions: the 8CV, 10CV, and 15CV, distinguished by their engine sizes, ranging from a 1.5-liter four-cylinder to a 2.7-liter six-cylinder. The Rosalie replaced the Citroën C4 and C6 models and marked a move upmarket for the brand, featuring more modern styling and benefiting from Citroën's advanced assembly line production techniques. 

In 1934, the Rosalie received a facelift, introducing the "NH" or "B-series" models with updated styling. Despite the launch of its revolutionary successor, the Traction Avant, the Rosalie remained in production until 1938, with over 95,000 units built. The Rosalie is remembered for its competitive pricing, diverse body styles, and role in Citroën's transition to more innovative automotive designs.

trgt: The Battle of Linlithgow Bridge is a battle that took place on 4 September 1526 in the village of Linlithgow Bridge, outside the Scottish town of Linlithgow. It was fought between a force of 10,000 men led by the Earl of Lennox and a force of 2,500 led by James Hamilton, 1st Earl of Arran. The battle was part of a power struggle in Scotland for control of the young Scottish king, James V. The battlefield was added to the national Inventory of Historic Battlefields in Scotland prepared by Historic Scotland under the Scottish Historical Environment Policy of 2009.
mgt: The Battle of Linlithgow Bridge, fought in 1526 near Linlithgow, Scotland, was a conflict during the minority of King James V, who was held under the control of his stepfather, Archibald Douglas, 6th Earl of Angus. The battle arose from an attempt by James' mother, Margaret Tudor, to free her son. The Earl of Lennox led an army to challenge Douglas' forces, but was ultimately defeated by a combined force led by James Hamilton, 1st Earl of Arran, and Douglas, with the reluctant young king present. Lennox was killed, and James remained under Douglas' control until 1528.

trgt: Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) is a technique used in mass spectrometry to visualize the spatial distribution of molecules, as biomarkers, metabolites, peptides or proteins by their molecular masses. After collecting a mass spectrum at one spot, the sample is moved to reach another region, and so on, until the entire sample is scanned. By choosing a peak in the resulting spectra that corresponds to the compound of interest, the MS data is used to map its distribution across the sample. This results in pictures of the spatially resolved distribution of a compound pixel by pixel. Each data set contains a veritable gallery of pictures because any peak in each spectrum can be spatially mapped. Despite the fact that MSI has been generally considered a qualitative method, the signal generated by this technique is proportional to the relative abundance of the analyte. Therefore, quantification is possible, when its challenges are overcome. Although widely used traditional methodologies like radiochemistry and immunohistochemistry achieve the same goal as MSI, they are limited in their abilities to analyze multiple samples at once, and can prove to be lacking if researchers do not have prior knowledge of the samples being studied. Most common ionization technologies in the field of MSI are DESI imaging, MALDI imaging and secondary ion mass spectrometry imaging (SIMS imaging).
mgt:
Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) is an analytical technique that combines mass spectrometry with spatially resolved imaging to visualize the distribution of molecules, such as proteins, lipids, and metabolites, within a sample. Introduced over 50 years ago with secondary ion mass spectrometry (SIMS) for studying surfaces, MSI gained prominence in the late 1990s when Richard Caprioli demonstrated its application to biological tissues using matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI). Today, MSI employs various ionization techniques, including SIMS, MALDI, and desorption electrospray ionization (DESI), with MALDI being the most widely used for clinical and biological research.

MSI operates through two main approaches: microprobe, which scans specific regions of a sample, and microscope, which uses position-sensitive detectors for spatial mapping. It has diverse applications, including cancer diagnosis, pharmacological research, and drug distribution studies. MSI can also be combined with other imaging techniques, such as fluorescence staining and MRI, to generate multimodal or 3D images.

Despite its advantages, such as high selectivity and detailed molecular mapping, MSI faces challenges like resolution limitations and the need for standardized data formats, such as imzML. Advances in software and techniques, including multiplexed ion beam imaging and matrix-enhanced SIMS, continue to enhance its capabilities and expand its applications in scientific and medical research.

trgt: Augustus Jones (c. 1757 – November 16, 1836) was an American-born Upper Canadian farmer, land speculator, magistrate, militia captain and surveyor. Jones trained as a surveyor in New York City, and fled as a United Empire Loyalist to Upper Canada. In Upper Canada, he worked as a crown surveyor in the Nassau District, where he quickly rose to the position of Deputy Surveyor General, the highest position in a district of Upper Canada. He occupied that position from 1789 informally, and 1791 formally, until his retirement in 1799. During that time he laid down many of the township boundaries in the Niagara Peninsula and on the north shore of Lake Ontario. He led various teams that cut many of the first sideroads and concession roads into these areas, facilitating their settlement by European and American immigrants. Jones also surveyed the routes for Dundas Street and Yonge Street, and supervised their construction. After his retirement, Jones farmed first in Saltfleet Township, later moving to Brantford and finally an estate outside Paris named Cold Springs, where he died in 1836.
mgt:
Augustus Jones (c. 1757 – November 16, 1836) was an American-born Canadian surveyor and farmer, known for his significant contributions to the development of Upper Canada (now Ontario). Born in the Hudson River Valley, New York, Jones trained as a land surveyor before fleeing to Canada with his Loyalist family during the American Revolution. He became Deputy Surveyor General for the Nassau District in 1789, overseeing the surveying of numerous townships and major infrastructure projects, including Yonge Street and Dundas Street. His work helped establish the geographic and political boundaries of southern Ontario.

Jones developed strong relationships with Indigenous communities, becoming fluent in their languages and earning the trust of leaders like Joseph Brant. He married Sarah Tekarihogen, daughter of a Mohawk chief, and also had children with Tuhbenahneequay, a Mississauga woman. After retiring in 1800, Jones farmed in Saltfleet Township and later near Paris, Ontario. He died in 1836 and was buried in Brantford. A statue honoring his contributions was unveiled in Stoney Creek, Ontario, in 2005.

trgt:
Kodiak is the second level leadership development course for Venturers in the Boy Scouts of America's Venturing program.

Introduction to Leadership Skills for Crews is recommended but not required. Kodiak is the second course in the overall Venturing youth leadership development program called Nature of Leadership. When it was first being developed, Kodiak was being offered as Nature of Leadership Treks.

This training is one of several programs available within the youth leadership training program. Participants must complete unit-level training, National Youth Leadership Training or a special bridge course that has not yet been defined. This training is an element of the overall leadership training program.
mgt: Kodiak is a leadership training program offered by the Boy Scouts of America, designed as a six-day or two-weekend trek-oriented course. It focuses on seven core leadership concepts, with the first two (Values and Planning) covered before the trek and the remaining five addressed during the trek. The course is intended to be integrated into high-adventure trips rather than conducted as a camp-based retreat. Participants who complete the course receive the Kodiak medal, worn on the left pocket. A follow-up program, Kodiak X, reinforced leadership skills and introduced additional concepts but was retired in 2011. Course director training is available for adults organizing these programs.

page_title: MAZON: A Jewish Response to Hunger
trgt:
MAZON: A Jewish Response to Hunger is an American nonprofit working to end hunger among people of all faiths and backgrounds in the United States and Israel.

MAZON, which means "food" or "sustenance" in Hebrew, practices and promotes a multifaceted approach to hunger relief, recognizing the importance of responding to hungry peoples' immediate need for nutrition and sustenance while also working to advance long-term solutions. This holistic approach - executed through three interrelated strategies of education & advocacy, partnership grant-making and strategic initiatives - symbolizes its desire to embody twin Jewish ideals: tzedakah and tikkun olam.

MAZON has offices in Los Angeles, California (headquarters) and Washington, D.C.
mgt: MAZON: A Jewish Response to Hunger is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization founded in 1985 and headquartered in Los Angeles, California. Inspired by the Ethiopian famine of 1985 and Jewish traditions of feeding the poor, it was established by Leonard Fein to address hunger through a combination of grantmaking, education, advocacy, and strategic initiatives. The organization encourages donations from celebrations like bar and bat mitzvahs and weddings to support its mission. Led by President and CEO Abby J. Leibman since 2011, MAZON works to provide immediate access to nutritious food while advocating for long-term solutions to end hunger. Rabbi Joel Pitkowsky serves as the current board chair.

trgt: Władysław Gizbert-Studnicki, a Polish politician and publicist, was born on 15 November 1867 in Dünaburg, Vitebsk Governorate, Russian Empire (current Latvia), into a Polish szlachta family of the Kresy region. Both his parents fought in the January Uprising. Throughout his life, Studnicki was famous for his strongly pro-German stance, and in the Polish People's Republic all his books were banned from publication. He was the older brother of historian Wacław Studnicki. He died on 10 January 1953 in London.
mgt: Władysław Studnicki (1867–1953) was a Polish politician, writer, and publicist known for his controversial pro-German stance and anti-Soviet views. Initially a socialist, he later became a nationalist and advocated for Polish independence through alliances with Austria-Hungary and Germany. During the interwar period, he authored numerous works on geopolitics, predicting events like the Anschluss and the collapse of Czechoslovakia. In 1939, he opposed Poland's alliance with Britain, warning it would lead to disaster. After World War II, he lived in exile, publishing memoirs defending his positions.

trgt:
The Bernina Railway (Lang-de; Lang-it; Lang-rm) is a single-track 1,000 mm (3 ft 3+3⁄8 in) metre gauge railway line forming part of the Rhaetian Railway (RhB). It links the spa resort of St. Moritz, in the canton of Graubünden, Switzerland, with the town of Tirano, in the Province of Sondrio, Italy, via the Bernina Pass. Reaching a height of 2,253 metres (7,392 ft) above sea level, it is the highest railway crossing in Europe and the third-highest railway in Switzerland. It also ranks as the highest adhesion railway of the continent, and – with inclines of up to 7% – as one of the steepest adhesion railways in the world. The elevation difference on the section between the Bernina Pass and Tirano is 1,824 m (5,984 ft), allowing passengers to view glaciers along the line.

On 7 July 2008, the Bernina Railway and the Albula Railway, which also forms part of the RhB, were recorded in the list of UNESCO World Heritage Sites, under the name Rhaetian Railway in the Albula / Bernina Landscapes. The whole site is a cross-border joint Swiss-Italian heritage area.

Trains operating on the Bernina Railway include the Bernina Express.
mgt:
The Bernina railway line is a high-altitude railway in the Swiss canton of Graubünden, connecting St. Moritz, Switzerland, to Tirano, Italy, via the Bernina Pass. Opened in stages between 1908 and 1910, it was originally built by the Bernina-Bahngesellschaft to provide access to hydroelectric plants and promote tourism in the region. The line, which operates year-round, is notable for its steep gradients, reaching a maximum of 7% without the use of rack-and-pinion technology, making it one of the steepest adhesion railways in the world. It also holds the distinction of being the highest railway in the Alps with year-round service, peaking at 2,253 meters (7,392 feet) above sea level.

Now operated by the Rhaetian Railway, the Bernina line is part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Rhaetian Railway in the Albula / Bernina Landscapes." It is renowned for its engineering marvels, including the Brusio spiral viaduct and the Montebello Curve, as well as its scenic views of glaciers, lakes, and alpine landscapes. The line serves both passenger and freight traffic, with flagship services like the Bernina Express attracting tourists worldwide. It remains a vital link for local communities and a celebrated example of mountain railway engineering.

trgt:
One Nation Labour refers to the theme and branding of the British Labour Party adopted by the party in 2012 under the leadership of Ed Miliband. Miliband described the "One Nation" term as being related to British Conservative Prime Minister Benjamin Disraeli's idea of One Nation conservatism. Disraeli claimed a need for government to reduce economic inequality, which he believed was splitting Britain into two nations of rich and poor people. Miliband stated that the theme of One Nation was shared by Labour Prime Minister Clement Attlee. Miliband has declared inspiration from Disraeli's and Attlee's One Nation theme, in that it challenges social barriers of class and promotes the unity of Britain.

One Nation Labour had been described by its proponents as the successor to New Labour. Miliband both commended and criticized elements of New Labour. He commended New Labour for challenging Old Labour's appeal to sectional interests by widening the party's appeal, but also criticized New Labour, saying that "although New Labour often started with the right intentions, over time it did not do enough to change the balance of power in this country", "New Labour was right to talk about rights and responsibilities but was too timid in enforcing them, especially at the top of society", and that "we have to move on from New Labour".
mgt:
One Nation Labour was a political concept and rebranding of the British Labour Party under the leadership of Ed Miliband, emphasizing social justice, economic rebalancing, and national unity. Introduced in 2012, it sought to address societal inequalities and promote a fairer, more inclusive Britain. Economically, One Nation Labour advocated for long-term investment, support for low- and middle-income earners, and a focus on technical education to enhance mid-level skills, which Miliband argued were essential for sustainable growth. Socially, it emphasized equalizing life chances, corporate responsibility, and addressing income disparities.

Culturally, One Nation Labour celebrated Britain's diversity while advocating for immigration policies that prioritized the economic interests of British workers, particularly those in lower-income brackets. It also acknowledged the challenges of emigration and migration's impact on communities. The platform supported further devolution of powers to local governments in England, mirroring the arrangements in Scotland and Wales.

Ideologically, One Nation Labour built on and diverged from New Labour's Third Way approach. While endorsing a form of capitalism that worked for the people, it embraced a more public use of the term "socialism," advocating for a fairer and more just society. This marked a shift from New Labour's reluctance to use the term, reflecting a renewed focus on ethical socialism and social democratic values.

trgt:
Frentorish "Tori" Bowie (born August 27, 1990) is an American track and field athlete, who primarily competes in the 100 m and the 200 m. She has a personal record of 6.95 m (22 ft 9+1⁄2 in) for the long jump, set in 2014. She is the 2017 100m world champion, a 2016 Olympian and a three-time Olympic medalist.

Bowie competed collegiately for the University of Southern Mississippi and was a two-time NCAA long jump champion, winning indoors and outdoors in 2011. She holds the school records for the long jump and was also NCAA runner-up outdoors in 2012. She came runner-up in the long jump at the 2014 USA Indoor Track and Field Championships and represented her country at the 2014 IAAF World Indoor Championships.

After winning Diamond League races in Eugene, Rome, New York, and Monaco in 2014, Bowie shifted her focus to the sprints beginning in 2015. She holds personal bests of 10.78 seconds for the 100-meter dash, 21.77 seconds for the 200-meter dash, and 7.14 seconds for the 60-meter dash, as well as having a triple jump best of 13.09 m (42 ft 11+1⁄4 in).
mgt:
Tori Bowie (August 27, 1990 – May 2023) was an American track and field athlete specializing in sprints and the long jump. A three-time Olympic medalist, she won gold in the 4×100 meter relay, silver in the 100 meters, and bronze in the 200 meters at the 2016 Rio de Janeiro Olympics. Bowie also claimed gold in the 100 meters at the 2017 World Championships in London, solidifying her status as one of the world's top sprinters.

Born in Sand Hill, Mississippi, Bowie excelled in track and field during high school and earned an athletic scholarship to the University of Southern Mississippi. Competing in NCAA Division I, she won multiple Conference USA titles and two NCAA long jump championships, setting school records in the process.

Turning professional in 2013, Bowie quickly rose to prominence, achieving personal bests of 10.78 seconds in the 100 meters, 21.77 seconds in the 200 meters, and 6.95 meters in the long jump. She competed on the IAAF Diamond League circuit and represented the United States at numerous international competitions, earning a total of six major championship medals during her career.

trgt:
Medelpad (IPA-sv or IPA-sv) is a historical province or landskap in the north of Sweden. It borders Hälsingland, Härjedalen, Jämtland, Ångermanland and the Gulf of Bothnia.

The province is a part of Norrland and as such considered to be Northern Sweden, although the province geographically is located in the middle of Sweden. It is a common misconception that the name Medelpad ("middle land" or "middle ground") reflects this, but the name actually refers to the fact that most of the province lies between its two rivers Ljungan and Indalsälven.
mgt: Medelpad is a historical province in northern Sweden, located in the southern part of Västernorrland County. Covering an area of 7,058 km², it is bordered by the rivers Ljungan and Indalsälven and features a varied landscape with elevations averaging 200–300 meters above sea level. Sundsvall, the province's largest city, was granted city status in 1624 and remains its cultural and economic center. Medelpad is notable for its coastline along the Gulf of Bothnia, part of the UNESCO-listed High Coast, shaped by post-glacial rebound. Its provincial symbols include the spruce, mountain hare, and perch.

trgt:
Wild Child is the debut studio album from American singer and actress E. G. Daily, released by A&M in 1985.

Although the album was not a commercial success, the lead single "Say It, Say It" reached No. 1 on the US Billboard Hot Dance Club Play chart and No. 70 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1986. The second single, "Love in the Shadows", reached No. 6 on the Billboard Hot Dance Club Play Songs Chart.

Wild Child was recorded at Arco Studios in Munich, Oasis, Cherokee and Conway Recording Studios in Los Angeles, and Sigma Sound Studios in New York City.

"Just for You" was dedicated to Jon-Erik Hexum, Daily's boyfriend of the time, an American model and actor who died in late 1984 as a result of a firearms accident on the set of the CBS television series Cover Up in which he played the male lead. "Love In the Shadows" was covered by Celine Dion in French, titled "Délivre-moi", for her 1987 album Incognito.
mgt:
*Wild Child* is the debut studio album by American singer and actress E. G. Daily, released in 1985 by A&M Records. The album blends pop rock with elements of dance-pop and features production by Harold Faltermeyer, Keith Forsey, Rick Ramirez, and Daily herself. It includes the singles "Say It, Say It," a Madonna-esque R&B/pop hit, and "Love in the Shadows." The album also features a cover of Donna Summer's "Sunset People."

Critics praised *Wild Child* for Daily's vocal versatility and its mix of pop and rock styles. Cash Box highlighted it as a "respectable debut," while Billboard noted her distinctive vocal range. The *Los Angeles Times* compared her sound to Pat Benatar, and the *Star Tribune* likened the album to Cyndi Lauper's debut, emphasizing Daily's potential as a pop/rock artist. Retrospectively, AllMusic described the album as a "promising debut," showcasing Daily's ability to balance dance-oriented tracks with straight-up pop rock.

trgt:
Police Academy is a 1984 American comedy film directed by Hugh Wilson in his directorial debut, and distributed by Warner Bros. Pictures. Its story follows a new recruitment policy for an unnamed police department's academy that is required to take in any recruit who wishes to try out to be a police officer. The film stars Steve Guttenberg, Kim Cattrall, and G.W. Bailey.

The film was produced by The Ladd Company. It premiered on March 23, 1984. It grossed $8.5 million in its opening weekend and over $149 million worldwide, against a budget of $4.5 million, and remains the most successful film of the series as of 2022. The film spawned six sequels in the Police Academy franchise.
mgt: *Police Academy* is a 1984 American comedy film directed by Hugh Wilson and written by Neal Israel, Pat Proft, and Wilson. The film follows a group of misfit recruits who join a police academy after the mayor mandates the acceptance of all applicants, leading to comedic chaos. Starring Steve Guttenberg, Kim Cattrall, Bubba Smith, and George Gaynes, the film blends slapstick humor with a satirical take on law enforcement training. Despite mixed critical reviews, *Police Academy* was a commercial success, grossing $149.8 million worldwide against a $4.5 million budget, making it the sixth highest-grossing American film of 1984. Its success spawned a franchise, including six sequels, a television series, and an animated series.

trgt:
Cine City (originally named the Scala Cinema) was a cinema in Withington, Manchester, England located at 494 Wilmslow Road, Withington, Manchester, M20 3BG. It opened in 1912 as The Scala, and was the third cinema to open in Britain. When the popularity of picture houses reached its peak in the 1930s, The Scala was one of 109 cinemas in Manchester.

During the Second World War, the cinema escaped with minor damage when the road outside was hit by a small bomb in 1940. After the war, television led to a decline in cinema attendances, and by 1965, only 40 cinemas remained in Manchester. Cine City closed in July 2001, making it the third-longest running cinema in England.

By 2005 the building was in a bad state of repair, and was threatened with demolition. Although heritage groups won a stay of execution, the cinema was demolished in spring 2008. A new residential building has been constructed on the site.
mgt:
Cine City, originally known as The Scala, was a historic cinema located on Wilmslow Road in Withington, Manchester. Opened around 1912, it was one of the earliest cinemas in Britain and operated for nearly 90 years before closing in 2001. The cinema, designed in the Tudor Revival style, featured a single-screen auditorium with 675 seats and hosted notable figures such as Sir John Barbirolli and Violet Carson as resident pianists during the silent film era. Over the decades, it underwent several transformations, including being renamed Cine City in the 1970s and dividing its auditorium into three screens.

Despite its cultural significance, Cine City faced declining popularity due to competition from television and multiplex cinemas. After its closure, redevelopment plans were repeatedly proposed but faced challenges. The building was ultimately demolished in 2008, though its Grade II-listed stone piers remain. In 2018, the site was redeveloped into the Scala Apartments, named in homage to the original cinema.

trgt: In medicine, an avulsion is an injury in which a body structure is torn off by either trauma or surgery (from the Latin avellere, meaning "to tear off"). The term most commonly refers to a surface trauma where all layers of the skin have been torn away, exposing the underlying structures (i.e., subcutaneous tissue, muscle, tendons, or bone). This is similar to an abrasion but more severe, as body parts such as an eyelid or an ear can be partially or fully detached from the body.
mgt: An avulsion injury occurs when tissue is forcibly detached from its normal position, ranging in severity from minor skin flaps to complete amputations. Common types include skin avulsions, often caused by motor vehicle collisions, and nail avulsions, where the nail is torn from the nail bed. Specific forms include ear, eyelid, and tooth avulsions, as well as brachial plexus avulsions, which affect nerve function. Treatment varies by type and severity, involving sutures, grafts, or surgical reattachment. Avulsions can also be performed surgically to address medical conditions, such as varicose veins or ingrown nails.

trgt: In Eastern Orthodox and Byzantine Catholic churches, an entrance is a procession during which the clergy enter into the sanctuary through the Holy Doors. The origin of these entrances goes back to the early church, when the liturgical books and sacred vessels were kept in special storage rooms for safe keeping and the procession was necessary to bring these objects into the church when needed. Over the centuries, these processions have grown more elaborate, and nowadays are accompanied by incense, candles and liturgical fans. In the liturgical theology of the Eastern Orthodox Church, the angels are believed to enter with the clergy into the sanctuary, as evidenced by the prayers which accompany the various entrances.
mgt: The "Entrance" in liturgical practice refers to ceremonial processions during Christian worship, particularly in the Eastern Orthodox and Byzantine Rite traditions. These processions, which include the Little Entrance and the Great Entrance, are integral to the Divine Liturgy. The Little Entrance occurs during the Liturgy of the Catechumens and involves the Gospel Book, symbolizing Christ's Incarnation and baptism. The Great Entrance, part of the Liturgy of the Faithful, involves the presentation of the Eucharistic Gifts and symbolizes Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem. Similar entrances occur during Great Vespers and the Liturgy of the Presanctified Gifts, with variations in ritual and symbolism. These entrances hold deep theological and liturgical significance.

trgt:
Germinated wheat (Lang-fa) or sprouted wheat, wheat sprout is a product of germinating wheat seeds in a wet and relatively warm environment in a process called sprouting. It is sometimes used instead of barley in the form of malt (early stage sprout) for making beer.

It is used in numerous Persian pastries, dishes, and desserts. Also, it has been used traditionally in the New Year celebration's Haft-Seen table decoration, in the form of Sabzeh (wheat grass), and a dessert called Samanu, as symbols of blessings from Ahura-Mazda. This custom has been observed by most of the people on Iranian Plateau and some of the populations in beyond (e.g. some parts of India, Yemen, Lebanon, Russia, and China) for thousands of years maybe as old as emerging of Zoroaster.

One of the confections called Sohan is made up of mainly wheat sprout flour. It is a traditional Persian saffron brittle toffee and made in the holy city of Qom.
mgt: Germinated wheat, known as *samani* in Azerbaijan, is a symbolic and sacred element of the Novruz celebration, marking the arrival of spring. The sprouting of *samani* represents sowing, abundance, and a rich harvest, embodying grain and bread as essential symbols of life and sustenance. Traditionally grown from wheat, barley, peas, lentils, or other grains in copper dishes, *samani* has been revered for generations as a sign of renewal and prosperity. This practice is deeply rooted in Azerbaijani culture, as reflected in the proverb "Səməni, ay səməni, hər il göyərdərəm səni" ("*Samani*, o *samani*, I try to make you grow every year"), which highlights the annual renewal of nature.

trgt: Andrée Geulen-Herscovici (6 September 1921 – 31 May 2022) was a Belgian teacher and member of the resistance during the German occupation of Belgium during World War II. After becoming aware of the persecution of Belgian Jews through her employment, she became involved in the Committee for the Defence of Jews (Comité de Défense des Juifs, or CDJ; Joods Verdedigingscomité, JVC) in 1943 and actively assisted with the organisation and protection of "hidden children" amid the Holocaust in Belgium. In the aftermath of the war, she was recognised as Righteous among the Nations by the Israeli institute Yad Vashem and received several other honours in Belgium and Israel.
mgt: Andrée Geulen (6 September 1921 – 31 May 2022) was a Belgian teacher and member of the resistance during World War II, known for her work with the Committee for the Defence of Jews (CDJ) in Belgium. She helped rescue and hide around 300 Jewish children, placing them with Catholic families and institutions under false identities to protect them from Nazi persecution. After the war, she worked to reunite hidden children with their families and supported Jewish survivors. Recognized as Righteous Among the Nations in 1989, she received honorary Israeli citizenship in 2007. Geulen remained an advocate for pacifist and anti-racist causes throughout her life.

trgt: Supersingular isogeny Diffie–Hellman key exchange (SIDH or SIKE) is an insecure proposal for a post-quantum cryptographic algorithm to establish a secret key between two parties over an untrusted communications channel. It is analogous to the Diffie–Hellman key exchange, but is based on walks in a supersingular isogeny graph and was designed to resist cryptanalytic attack by an adversary in possession of a quantum computer. Before it was broken, SIDH boasted one of the smallest key sizes of all post-quantum key exchanges; with compression, SIDH used 2688-bit public keys at a 128-bit quantum security level. SIDH also distinguishes itself from similar systems such as NTRU and Ring-LWE by supporting perfect forward secrecy, a property that prevents compromised long-term keys from compromising the confidentiality of old communication sessions. These properties seemed to make SIDH a natural candidate to replace Diffie–Hellman (DHE) and elliptic curve Diffie–Hellman (ECDHE), which are widely used in Internet communication. However, SIDH is vulnerable to a devastating key-recovery attack published in July 2022 and is therefore insecure.
mgt:
The supersingular isogeny key exchange (SIDH) is a post-quantum cryptographic protocol designed to provide secure key exchange resistant to attacks by quantum computers. Introduced in 2011 by Luca De Feo, David Jao, and Jérôme Plût, SIDH is based on the mathematics of supersingular elliptic curves and isogenies between them. It offers perfect forward secrecy, ensuring long-term security of encrypted communications, and has applications in contexts with strict space requirements, such as Bitcoin and Tor.

SIDH operates by leveraging isogeny graphs of supersingular elliptic curves, where two parties exchange information to compute a shared secret. Its security relies on the difficulty of finding isogenies between elliptic curves, with classical and quantum attack complexities of \(O(p^{1/4})\) and \(O(p^{1/6})\), respectively. However, in 2022, a key-recovery attack exploiting auxiliary points in SIDH public keys was demonstrated, breaking the SIKE protocol, a SIDH-based implementation.

Despite its vulnerabilities, SIDH has been noted for its efficiency, small key sizes, and potential as a post-quantum cryptographic solution.

trgt:
Harrington Raceway & Casino is a harness racing track and casino (a "racino") located on the fairgrounds of the Delaware State Fair, just south of Harrington, Delaware, 16 miles (26 km) south of Dover. The casino, formerly known as Midway Slots, has over 1,800 slot machines, table games, simulcasting, and sports betting. In addition, the venue promotes and hosts concerts and other similar entertainment revues. Multiple dining options including a buffet are available as well.

The racino is owned and operated by Harrington Raceway, Inc., which is majority-owned by the Delaware State Fair, a non-profit organization.
mgt: Harrington Raceway & Casino is a racino located in Kent County, Delaware, United States. Established in 1946 as a harness racing track, it expanded into a casino in 1996 following the legalization of slot machines in Delaware. Originally named Midway Slots, the facility was rebranded as Harrington Raceway & Casino in 2007 after significant renovations and expansions. The property features a half-mile racetrack, live racing events, and a casino with slot machines, table games, and dining options. It is owned and operated by Harrington Raceway, Inc., and remains a prominent entertainment venue in the region.

trgt:
Anthony Waddington (9 November 1924 – 21 January 1994) was an English football manager at both Crewe Alexandra and Stoke City.

Waddington had a seven-year playing career with Crewe Alexandra before becoming a coach at Stoke City. He progressed to assistant manager to Frank Taylor and took his position in June 1960. He set about staving off the threat of relegation before bringing back club legend Stanley Matthews in an effort to rekindle the club's supporter base. It worked well and he had enough money to bring in a number of established veterans as Stoke took the Football League Second Division title in 1962–63 and reached the 1964 Football League Cup Final, losing out to Leicester City.

More fine signings followed as Stoke enjoyed great success at the beginning of the 1970s reaching two FA Cup semi-finals, playing in the UEFA Cup twice and winning their first major trophy, the Football League Cup in 1972. Stoke then nearly won the First Division in 1974–75 but after the Butler Street Stand roof fell off in a strong storm at the Victoria Ground Stoke had to sell their best players to cover the repair costs and a despondent Waddington quit in March 1977 with Stoke heading for relegation. He later had a two-year spell at Crewe Alexandra before becoming associate director of Stoke in 1991 until his death in 1994.
mgt:
Tony Waddington (9 November 1924 – 21 January 1994) was an English football player and manager, best known for his 17-year tenure as manager of Stoke City from 1960 to 1977. Born in Manchester, he began his playing career as a wing half with Crewe Alexandra, making 193 league appearances before transitioning into coaching. After joining Stoke City as a coach in 1952, he rose to become manager in 1960, tasked with revitalizing the struggling club.

Waddington is celebrated for transforming Stoke City, notably signing legendary players such as Stanley Matthews, Gordon Banks, and George Eastham. Under his leadership, Stoke achieved promotion to the First Division in 1963 and won their first major trophy, the League Cup, in 1972. His tenure also saw the club reach two FA Cup semi-finals and enjoy one of the most successful periods in its history. However, financial difficulties following storm damage to the Victoria Ground in 1976 led to the sale of key players and Waddington's departure in 1977.

After a brief managerial stint at Crewe Alexandra (1979–1981), Waddington returned to Stoke as an associate director in 1991, a role he held until his death in 1994. He is widely regarded as Stoke City's greatest manager, leaving a lasting legacy in English football.

trgt:
The 2021 Little League World Series was held from August 19 to August 29 at the Little League headquarters complex in South Williamsport, Pennsylvania. Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the event was limited to US-based teams only. The most recent prior edition of the tournament contested without international teams had been 1975. Taylor North Little League of Taylor, Michigan, defeated West Side Little League of Hamilton, Ohio, in the championship by a 5–2 score. It was the first championship for a team from Michigan since 1959.

On August 13, it was announced that tickets would not be distributed to the public due to concerns over the Delta variant, with spectators limited to invited friends and family of teams, and other high-level supporters.
mgt: The 2021 Little League World Series was held from August 19 to August 29 in South Williamsport, Pennsylvania, featuring 16 U.S.-based teams due to COVID-19 restrictions. The tournament was divided into two brackets, the Tom Seaver and Hank Aaron championship brackets. Taylor North Little League from Taylor, Michigan, won the championship, defeating West Side Little League from Hamilton, Ohio, 5–2 in the final, a rematch of the Great Lakes regional final. Ella Bruning of Abilene, Texas, became one of the few girls to compete in the series. The event also included the 2021 MLB Little League Classic, where the Cleveland Indians defeated the Los Angeles Angels 3–0. This was the fourth edition of the Classic and the first between American League teams.

trgt:
The Cheese and the Worms (Lang-it) is a scholarly work by the Italian historian Carlo Ginzburg, published in 1976. The book is a notable example of cultural history, the history of mentalities and microhistory. It is "probably the most popular and widely read work of microhistory".

The study examines the unique religious beliefs and cosmogony of Menocchio (1532–1599), also known as Domenico Scandella, who was an Italian miller from the village of Montereale, twenty-five kilometers north of Pordenone. He was from the peasant class, and not a learned aristocrat or man of letters; Ginzburg places him in the tradition of popular culture and pre-Christian naturalistic peasant religions. Due to his outspoken beliefs he was declared a heresiarch (heretic) and burned at the stake during the Roman Inquisition.
mgt: *The Cheese and the Worms* (*Il formaggio e i vermi*) is a 1976 microhistory by Italian historian Carlo Ginzburg, first published in English in 1980. The book examines the life and beliefs of Domenico Scandella, known as Menocchio, a 16th-century miller from Friuli, Italy, who was tried and executed for heresy during the Counter-Reformation. Drawing on trial records, Ginzburg explores Menocchio's unorthodox cosmology—symbolized by his metaphor of the universe as "the cheese and the worms"—and his reinterpretation of religious texts, which challenged Catholic doctrine. The work highlights Menocchio's literacy, shaped by local schools and access to popular books, and his tendency to reduce religion to morality. *The Cheese and the Worms* is a seminal work in microhistory and cultural history, offering insights into popular religion and dissent in early modern Europe.

trgt: Jean Messiha (IPA-fr; born Hossam Boutros Messiha, Lang-ar‎, 10 September 1970) is an Egyptian-born French economist, politician and former senior civil servant. He was appointed Deputy Undersecretary of Management at the Ministry of Defence in 2014 and joined the National Front (FN) in 2016, when he became spokesman of Horaces, a group of high-ranking civil servants and business executives who meet once a month to discuss the party platform. Messiha stood as a candidate in the 2017 legislative election in the 4th constituency of Aisne. In 2020, he left the party to assume the presidency of the Apollon Institute, a far-right think tank. In 2022, Messiha joined Éric Zemmour's newly-founded Reconquête party and became its spokesman, but he since has left the party afterwards.
mgt: Jean Messiha (born Hossam Boutros Messiha on 10 September 1970) is a French economist, civil servant, political advisor, and commentator of Egyptian origin. Born in Cairo to a Coptic Christian family, he moved to France at the age of 8 and became a naturalized French citizen in 1990, changing his first name to Jean. A graduate of Sciences Po and the École nationale d'administration, he began his career as a high-ranking civil servant and later became an advisor to Marine Le Pen and a prominent figure in the National Rally. Known for his controversial views, including support for the Great Replacement theory, Messiha left the National Rally in 2020 and joined Éric Zemmour's Reconquête party in 2022.

trgt:
I Feel Pretty is a 2018 American comedy film written and directed by Abby Kohn and Marc Silverstein. The film stars Amy Schumer, Michelle Williams, Emily Ratajkowski, Rory Scovel, Aidy Bryant, Busy Philipps, Tom Hopper, Naomi Campbell, and Lauren Hutton. The plot involves an insecure woman who, after suffering a head injury, gains extreme self-confidence in her appearance.

The film was released in the United States on April 20, 2018, by STX Entertainment, and grossed over $94 million worldwide. It received mixed reviews from critics, with some saying it did not fully commit to its premise or tone, although the performances of both Schumer and Williams were praised.
mgt: *I Feel Pretty* is a 2018 American comedy film written and directed by Abby Kohn and Marc Silverstein, starring Amy Schumer, Michelle Williams, Rory Scovel, and Emily Ratajkowski. The film follows Renee Bennett, a woman struggling with low self-esteem, who gains newfound confidence after a head injury leads her to believe she is stunningly beautiful. Her transformation impacts her personal and professional life, culminating in a message of self-acceptance. Produced on a $32 million budget, the film grossed $94.5 million worldwide. While it received mixed reviews, with praise for its premise but criticism for its execution, it sparked discussions on body image and inspired a 2021 French television remake, *Belle, belle, belle*.

trgt:
Valsartan, sold under the brand name Diovan among others, is a medication used to treat high blood pressure, heart failure, and diabetic kidney disease. It belongs to a class of medications referred to as angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). It is a reasonable initial treatment for high blood pressure. It is taken by mouth. Versions are available as the combination valsartan/hydrochlorothiazide, valsartan/amlodipine, valsartan/amlodipine/hydrochlorothiazide, or valsartan/sacubitril.

Common side effects include feeling tired, dizziness, high blood potassium, diarrhea, and joint pain. Other serious side effects may include kidney problems, low blood pressure, and angioedema. Use in pregnancy may harm the baby and use when breastfeeding is not recommended. It is an angiotensin II receptor antagonist and works by blocking the effects of angiotensin II.

Valsartan was patented in 1990, and came into medical use in 1996. It is available as a generic medication. In 2020, it was the 123rd most commonly prescribed medication in the United States, with more than 5 million prescriptions.
mgt:
Valsartan, sold under the brand name Diovan among others, is a medication primarily used to treat high blood pressure, heart failure, and to reduce mortality in individuals with left ventricular dysfunction following a heart attack. It belongs to the class of drugs known as angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), which work by blocking the effects of angiotensin II to lower blood pressure and improve heart function. Valsartan is also used in managing diabetic kidney disease by slowing the progression of albuminuria and kidney damage.

The drug is available in combination with other medications, such as amlodipine or hydrochlorothiazide, for enhanced treatment of hypertension. Valsartan has been associated with side effects like dizziness, low blood pressure, and kidney-related issues, and carries a black box warning for fetal toxicity.

In 2018, valsartan faced global recalls due to contamination with carcinogenic impurities during manufacturing, leading to shortages. Efforts to address these issues have included regulatory guidance and the approval of additional generic versions.

trgt: The Sleeping Tiger is a 1954 British film noir directed by Joseph Losey and starring Alexis Smith, Dirk Bogarde and Alexander Knox. It was Losey's first British feature, which he directed under the pseudonym of Victor Hanbury due to being blacklisted in the McCarthy Era. It was shot at Walton Studios and on location in London. The film's sets were designed by the art director John Stoll. It was released by Anglo-Amalgamated while in America it was distributed by Astor Pictures.
mgt: *The Sleeping Tiger* is a 1954 British psychological drama film directed by Joseph Losey, under the pseudonym Victor Hanbury due to Hollywood blacklisting. Based on Maurice Moiseiwitsch's novel, the film stars Dirk Bogarde, Alexis Smith, and Alexander Knox. It follows a criminal who becomes a psychiatrist's experimental subject, leading to a tense love triangle and psychological revelations. The film marked Losey's first British feature and began his collaboration with Bogarde. Produced during the McCarthy era, it reflects the challenges of blacklisted filmmakers working abroad.

trgt:
Gracefield is a city in La Vallée-de-la-Gatineau Regional County Municipality in the Outaouais region of Quebec, Canada. It was reorganized on 13 March 2002 when three former municipalities (Gracefield, Northfield, and Wright) were merged into one.

Its name comes from Patrick Grace who opened there the first business and was mayor of Wright from 1885 to 1890.

Gracefield is located along the Gatineau River and Picanoc River. Its area is dotted with many lakes, most of which are lined with numerous summer camps and cottages.
mgt: Gracefield is a city in the Outaouais region of western Quebec, Canada, located within the La Vallée-de-la-Gatineau Regional County Municipality. Established on March 13, 2002, through the merger of Wright, Northfield, and Gracefield, it was renamed Gracefield in 2003. The city has a population of 2,376 as of the 2021 Census. Historically, Gracefield experienced significant events such as the 1974 Gatineau River flood and a 2010 earthquake that caused extensive damage. Once home to mica mining, the area has seen interest in zinc exploration.

trgt: The February 2016 Ankara bombing killed at least 30 people and injured 60 in the capital of Turkey. According to Turkish authorities, the attack targeted a convoy of vehicles carrying both civilian and military personnel working at the military headquarters during the evening rush hour as the vehicles were stopped at traffic lights at an intersection with İsmet İnönü Boulevard close to Kızılay neighborhood. Several ministries, the headquarters of the army and the Turkish Parliament are located in the neighbourhood where the attack occurred. The Kurdistan Freedom Hawks (TAK) took responsibility for the attack and said they targeted security forces. Censorship monitoring organization Turkey Blocks reported nationwide internet restrictions beginning approximately one hour after the blast pursuant to an administrative order. The attack killed 14 military personnel, 14 civilian employees of the military, and a civilian (as well as the perpetrator).
mgt:
The February 2016 Ankara bombing was a car bombing and suicide attack that occurred on February 17, 2016, in Ankara, Turkey, targeting military personnel. The explosion, which took place near military buses waiting at a traffic light, killed 30 people, including 14 soldiers, 14 civilian employees of the Turkish Armed Forces, one civilian, and the perpetrator, and injured 60 others. The Kurdistan Freedom Hawks (TAK) claimed responsibility for the attack, identifying the bomber as Abdulbaki Sömer, a Turkish citizen, though initial government reports had incorrectly named a Syrian Kurd as the assailant.

The bombing occurred amidst heightened tensions in the Kurdish–Turkish conflict and ongoing Turkish military actions against Kurdish groups. The attack drew widespread condemnation domestically and internationally, with Turkish leaders vowing retaliation. The incident also intensified debates over Turkey's policies toward Kurdish groups and its relations with NATO allies.

trgt:
Daniel Isn't Real is a 2019 American psychological horror film directed by Adam Egypt Mortimer, from a screenplay by Mortimer and Brian DeLeeuw, based upon the novel In This Way I Was Saved by DeLeeuw. It stars Miles Robbins, Patrick Schwarzenegger, Sasha Lane, Mary Stuart Masterson, Hannah Marks, Chukwudi Iwuji and Peter McRobbie.

Daniel Isn't Real had its world premiere at South by Southwest on March 9, 2019. It was released on December 6, 2019, by Samuel Goldwyn Films in select theaters and digitally.
mgt: *Daniel Isn't Real* is a 2019 American psychological horror film directed by Adam Egypt Mortimer and based on Brian DeLeeuw's novel *In This Way I Was Saved*. The film stars Miles Robbins, Patrick Schwarzenegger, Sasha Lane, and Mary Stuart Masterson. It follows a troubled young man who reconnects with his childhood imaginary friend, only to discover the friend is a malevolent supernatural entity. Premiering at South by Southwest on March 9, 2019, and released on December 6, 2019, the film received critical acclaim for its stylish direction, exploration of mental illness, and commentary on toxic masculinity.

trgt:
Speshow is the fourth Mandarin studio album of Taiwanese Mandopop artist Show Lo (Chinese: 羅志祥). It was released on 17 November 2006 by Avex Taiwan. The name Speshow is a pun of 'Special' and his name 'Show', as he hoped it will be special for his fans. This is the last studio album of Show's, released by Avex Taiwan before his move to EMI Music Taiwan.

It was available on pre-order and four editions were released, which includes Speshow (Happiness Hunter MV Celebration Edition) (Speshow 幸福獵人影音慶功版), Speshow (Corner with Love Special Edition) (Speshow 愛*轉角 冠軍特別版) and Speshow (King's Game Live Gold Edition) (Speshow 國王遊戲LIVE黃金版) with 60 minutes of highlight live footage from Show Lo BenQ Speshow Concert at Taipei NanGang101.
mgt: *Speshow* is the fourth studio album by Taiwanese Mandopop artist Show Lo, released on November 17, 2006, by Avex Taiwan. The album achieved significant commercial success, debuting at number one on Taiwan's G-Music Weekly Top 20 Mandarin and Combo Charts, as well as the Five Music Chart, and maintaining strong chart performance for several weeks. Notable tracks include "好朋友" (Good Friends) and "精舞門" (Dance Gate), both of which were featured on Hit Fm Taiwan's Annual Top 100 Singles Chart for 2006 and received nominations for Songs of the Year at the 2007 Metro Radio Mandarin Music Awards. "精舞門" also won a Top 10 Song of the Year award at the 2007 HITO Radio Music Awards.

trgt: The 1945 Katsuyama killing incident was the murder of three African-American United States Marines in Katsuyama near Nago, Okinawa after the Battle of Okinawa on July 10, 1945 to August 13, 1946. Residents of Katsuyama had reportedly killed the three Marines for their repeated rape of village women during occupation of Okinawa and hid their bodies in a nearby cave out of fear for retaliation. The Katsuyama incident was kept secret until August 16, 1997 when the bodies and identities of the Marines were discovered.
mgt: The 1945 Katsuyama killing incident occurred in the village of Katsuyama, Okinawa, during the U.S. occupation following the Battle of Okinawa. Three African-American Marines were killed by villagers, with the assistance of two Japanese soldiers, in response to repeated rapes of local women. Their bodies were hidden in a cave, and the incident remained a secret for over 50 years. The remains were discovered in 1997 and identified through dental records. The incident highlights tensions and misconduct during the U.S. military presence in postwar Okinawa.

trgt: Threat to Survival is the fifth studio album by American rock band Shinedown. It was announced on August 7, 2015 that the album would be released on September 18, 2015. Four singles were released from the album, "Cut the Cord", "State of My Head", "Asking for It", and "How Did You Love". "Black Cadillac" was also released as a promotional song prior to the album's release as well. In February 2018, the album was certified Gold by the RIAA, indicating 500,000 copies sold in the US.
mgt: *Threat to Survival* is the fifth studio album by American rock band Shinedown, released on September 18, 2015, through Atlantic Records. Produced by Dave Bassett, Pete Nappi, Eric Bass, and Scott Stevens, the album features a mix of hard rock, pop rock, and alternative rock. It includes four singles: "Cut the Cord," "State of My Head," "Asking for It," and "How Did You Love." Reception to the album was mixed, with critics praising "Cut the Cord" but criticizing the album's lack of substance and memorable melodies.

trgt:
Petr Rada (born 21 August 1958) is a Czech football coach and former player. He represented Czechoslovakia internationally in the 1980s and managed the Czech Republic national football team between 2008 and 2009. He is well known for his arguments with fans or trainer colleagues and also for impulsive style of coaching.

Before being named national team manager in July 2008, Rada worked as an assistant for then national team coach Karel Brückner from 2006 to 2008, and also as an assistant to Jozef Chovanec from 1998 to 2001 when Chovanec was manager of the Czech national team. Rada has also been head manager of eight various Czech clubs: FK Teplice (on four separate occasions), FC Viktoria Plzeň, FK Jablonec, FC Slovan Liberec, SK Slavia Prague, FC Vysočina Jihlava, Příbram and Sparta Prague.
mgt:
Petr Rada (born 21 August 1958) is a Czech football manager and former player who is currently the manager of Dukla Prague. As a player, he was a defender, spending the majority of his career at Dukla Prague, where he won the Czechoslovak First League in 1979 and 1982. He also earned 11 caps for the Czechoslovakia national team.

Rada transitioned to management in 1998, leading various clubs in the Czech Republic, including Slavia Prague, Teplice, and Jablonec. He also managed the Czech Republic national team from 2008 to 2009. Notably, he guided Jablonec to the final of the 2006–07 Czech Cup. Despite mixed success, Rada has remained a prominent figure in Czech football. He is the father of goalkeeper Filip Rada.

trgt: The Koza riot (コザ暴動, Koza bōdō) was a violent and spontaneous protest against the US military presence in Okinawa, which occurred on the night of December 20, 1970, into the morning of the following day. Roughly 5,000 Okinawans clashed with roughly 700 American MPs in an event which has been regarded as symbolic of Okinawan anger against 25 years of US military occupation. In the riot, approximately 60 Americans and 27 Okinawans were injured, 80 cars were burned, and several buildings on Kadena Air Base were destroyed or heavily damaged.
mgt: The Koza riot was a violent civil disturbance that occurred on December 20, 1970, in Koza, Okinawa, during the U.S. military occupation of the island. Sparked by a traffic accident involving a U.S. serviceman and an Okinawan pedestrian, the riot escalated due to long-standing tensions between Okinawans and the American military presence. Thousands of Okinawans participated, resulting in the destruction of over 70 vehicles, injuries to 56–60 American servicemen and 27 Okinawans, and 82 arrests. The event highlighted local resentment ahead of Okinawa's 1972 reversion to Japanese sovereignty.

trgt: Bill Sizemore (born June 2, 1951) is an American political activist and writer in Happy Valley, Oregon, United States. Sizemore has never held elected office, but has nonetheless been a major political figure in Oregon since the 1990s. He is considered one of the main proponents of the Oregon tax revolt, a movement that seeks to reduce taxes in the state. Oregon Taxpayers United, a political action committee he founded in 1993, has advanced numerous ballot initiatives limiting taxation, and has opposed spending initiatives. Sizemore made an unsuccessful run for Governor of Oregon in 1998. He also announced his intention to run for governor in 2010, but was indicted by the state on charges of tax evasion. The charges were later amended to failure to file tax returns.
mgt:
Bill Sizemore (born June 2, 1951) is an American political activist, former gubernatorial candidate, and ballot initiative advocate in Oregon. A member of the Republican Party, he is best known for founding Oregon Taxpayers United in 1993 and spearheading numerous ballot measures, including the successful Measure 47 in 1996, which rolled back property taxes and introduced a double majority requirement for tax increases. Sizemore ran unsuccessfully for Governor of Oregon in 1998 and 2010.

His career has been marked by legal controversies, including a 2000 civil racketeering case against his organizations and a 2011 guilty plea for failing to file state income tax returns. Despite these challenges, Sizemore has remained a prominent figure in Oregon politics, advocating for tax reform and consumer rights.

trgt:
An air pump is a pump for pushing air. Examples include a bicycle pump, pumps that are used to aerate an aquarium or a pond via an airstone; a gas compressor used to power a pneumatic tool, air horn or pipe organ; a bellows used to encourage a fire; a vacuum cleaner and a vacuum pump. All air pumps contain a part that moves (vane, piston, impeller, diaphragm etc.) which drives the flow of air. When the air gets moved, an area of low pressure gets created which fills up with more air.

Pumps and compressors use very similar mechanisms, and basically perform the same action, but in different fluid regimes. At some point there is a crossover point in terminology, but here are some stereotypes:

• Compressors operate on compressible fluids, typically gases. Pumps operate on fluids, typically liquids, approximated as in-compressible.

• Compressors are intended to develop a very high pressure rise against a closed system; pumps are designed to develop relatively little pressure against a free-flowing system with minimal back-pressure.

• Pumps are often used in continuous-flow operation, while many lower-end compressors must have intermittent duty cycles.

• Compressors usually have a feedback sensor to shut off when they reach a desired pressure; pumps have a fixed design and operate freely across their performance curve as conditions change
mgt:
An air pump is a device used to move air, often by creating pressure or a vacuum, for various applications. The concept dates back to 1649 when Otto von Guericke invented the spool vacuum air pump, which utilized leather washers to minimize leaks. In 1658, Robert Hooke constructed the first effective air pump in England for Robert Boyle's scientific experiments. Later, in 1705, Francis Hauksbee developed a double-barrelled air pump for creating vacuums in scientific research.

Air pumps have significantly improved human life, playing a vital role in HVAC systems, food preservation, data center operations, and ensuring indoor air quality in homes, healthcare facilities, and offices. 

There are several types of air pumps, including air compressors, bellows, bicycle pumps, diaphragm pumps, engine-driven tire pumps, reciprocating pumps, and rotary pumps. Each operates on different principles, such as positive displacement or compression, to move air. For example, diaphragm pumps use a flexible chamber to expel and refill air, while rotary pumps use meshing gears to pressurize air. These pumps are widely used in various industries and everyday applications, from inflating tires to powering tools and maintaining air circulation.

trgt: This is a comprehensive discography of Face to Face, a Southern California-based punk rock band. The band has released eleven full-length studio albums (counting a covers album), eight EPs, two split albums, one official live recording, one compilation album, eleven music videos and twelve singles. This list is not intended to include material performed by members or former members of Face to Face that was recorded with Legion of Doom, Me First and the Gimme Gimmes, No Use for a Name, The Offspring, Pulley, Saves the Day, 22 Jacks, Viva Death, Red Faced, or Real Space Noise.
mgt: The discography of Face to Face, an American punk rock band formed in 1991, includes 10 studio albums, 8 EPs, 3 live albums, 2 compilation albums, 12 singles, and 11 music videos. The band gained prominence with their debut album *Don't Turn Away* (1992) and achieved further success with *Big Choice* (1995), featuring the hit "Disconnected." After releasing several albums and undergoing lineup changes, the band disbanded in 2004 but reunited in 2008. They have since continued to perform and release new music, including their 2011 comeback album *Laugh Now, Laugh Later.*

trgt:
In particle physics, annihilation is the process that occurs when a subatomic particle collides with its respective antiparticle to produce other particles, such as an electron colliding with a positron to produce two photons. The total energy and momentum of the initial pair are conserved in the process and distributed among a set of other particles in the final state. Antiparticles have exactly opposite additive quantum numbers from particles, so the sums of all quantum numbers of such an original pair are zero. Hence, any set of particles may be produced whose total quantum numbers are also zero as long as conservation of energy and conservation of momentum are obeyed.

During a low-energy annihilation, photon production is favored, since these particles have no mass. High-energy particle colliders produce annihilations where a wide variety of exotic heavy particles are created.

The word "annihilation" takes use informally for the interaction of two particles that are not mutual antiparticles – not charge conjugate. Some quantum numbers may then not sum to zero in the initial state, but conserve with the same totals in the final state. An example is the "annihilation" of a high-energy electron antineutrino with an electron to produce a W boson-.

If the annihilating particles are composite, such as mesons or baryons, then several different particles are typically produced in the final state.
mgt:
Annihilation is a fundamental process in particle physics where a particle and its corresponding antiparticle collide and convert their mass into energy, often producing other particles. The simplest form of annihilation occurs between elementary particles, such as an electron and a positron, which typically results in the creation of photons. At low energies, this process conserves momentum and energy, producing two photons with a combined energy of 1.022 MeV. At higher energies, additional particles can be produced.

Proton–antiproton annihilation is more complex due to the composite nature of protons, consisting of quarks and gluons. This interaction produces a variety of mesons, which decay into stable particles like photons, electrons, and neutrinos. In certain cases, such as annihilation within a heavy atomic nucleus, the process can release significant energy, potentially disrupting the nucleus and triggering secondary reactions.

Annihilation also plays a role in high-energy collisions, such as those at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC), where quark and gluon interactions contribute to the production of the Higgs boson. In 2012, the discovery of the Higgs boson at CERN highlighted the importance of annihilation processes in understanding fundamental particles and forces. These reactions are central to both theoretical physics and practical applications, including potential uses in energy generation and propulsion systems.

trgt:
Royalty Exchange is an American company that operates an online platform for buying and selling royalty assets of any type, mostly music, where royalty owners can sell their future payments to investors as alternative assets. The company hosts a centralized marketplace and online auction platform that connects a community of over 22,500+ investors with owners of royalty-based assets.

All types of assets can be monetized and invested in including music royalties, film royalties, and tax credits, patent and intellectual property royalties, trade secret royalties, renewable energy land leases, technology licensing royalties, book publishing royalties, franchise royalties, copyright royalties and agriculture royalties.
mgt: Royalty Exchange is an American online marketplace founded in 2011 in Raleigh, North Carolina, that connects investors with royalty owners seeking to sell their income streams. The company facilitates auctions and private syndicates for music royalties and other intellectual property. Acquired by private investors in 2015, Royalty Exchange relocated to Denver, Colorado, and expanded its operations under new leadership. In 2017, it launched Royalty Flow, a subsidiary aimed at acquiring high-profile music catalogs, though its IPO plans were later canceled. The platform has also introduced innovative investment models, such as multi-unit auctions and private syndicates, attracting accredited investors to music royalty assets.

trgt: The Cadets were an American doo-wop group, formed in Los Angeles, California, United States. The group began as a gospel group, the Santa Monica Soul Seekers, in the late 1940s. The members were Lloyd McCraw, Willie Davis, Austin "Ted" Taylor, Aaron Collins, Glendon Kingsby, and Will "Dub" Jones. In 1955, the group auditioned for Modern Records, and were accepted. The group decided to switch to the popular R&B style, with the exception of Kingsby, who left to continue in gospel music.
mgt: The Cadets were an American doo-wop and R&B group from Los Angeles, California, active primarily in the 1950s. Known for their energetic style and dual identity as "The Jacks" on RPM Records, they achieved success with hits like "Stranded in the Jungle." The group underwent several lineup changes and released music under both names, often creating confusion among fans. After disbanding in the late 1950s, they reformed in the 1990s, performing on PBS and other venues before members passed away in later years.

trgt:
Martyrdom in Judaism is one of the main examples of Jews doing a kiddush Hashem, a Hebrew term which means "sanctification of [the] name". An example of this is public self-sacrifice in accordance with Jewish practice and identity, with the possibility of being killed for no other reason than being Jewish. There are specific conditions in Jewish law that deal with the details of self-sacrifice, be it willing or unwilling.

The opposite or converse of kiddush Hashem is chillul Hashem ("Desecration [of] God's Name" in Hebrew) and Jews are obligated to avoid it according to Halakha (Jewish religious law). There are instances, such as when they are faced with forced conversion to another religion, when Jews should choose martyrdom and sacrifice their lives rather than commit a chillul Hashem which desecrates the honor of God. Martyrdom in Judaism is thus driven by both the desire to Sanctify God's Name concurrently and the wish to avoid the Desecration of God's Name.

In Hebrew a martyr is known as a kaddosh which means "[a] holy [one]", and martyrs are known as kedoshim meaning "holy [ones]". Thus the six million Jews who were murdered in the Holocaust are known as the Kedoshim.

Jewish history is replete with many episodes in which Jews who lived in different times and places chose to become individual and mass martyrs.
mgt:
Martyrdom in Judaism refers to the act of sacrificing one's life for the sanctification of God's name (al Kiddush Hashem) and has been a significant concept throughout Jewish history. Rooted in the Hebrew Bible, the notion of martyrdom is exemplified by the Binding of Isaac, where Abraham and Isaac demonstrate ultimate devotion to God. Biblical figures such as Samson, King Saul, and the Ten Martyrs are revered for their sacrifices, while events like the Maccabean Revolt and Jewish-Roman Wars highlight collective martyrdom in defense of faith and identity.

Throughout history, Jews have faced persecution and martyrdom under various regimes, including the Crusades, the Spanish Inquisition, and pogroms in Europe and the Russian Empire. The Holocaust marked the most devastating period of Jewish martyrdom, with six million Jews murdered, remembered as kedoshim ("holy ones"). Modern observances, such as Yom HaShoah and Yom HaZikaron, honor these martyrs, including victims of antisemitic violence and the Arab-Israeli conflict. Martyrdom remains a central theme in Jewish memory, symbolizing unwavering faith and resilience in the face of oppression.

trgt:
The 2014 WNBA Finals was the playoff series for the 2014 season of the Women's National Basketball Association (WNBA), and the conclusion of the season's playoffs. The Phoenix Mercury, champions of the Western Conference, faced the Chicago Sky, champions of the Eastern Conference.

The WNBA Finals were under a 2–2–1 rotation. The Mercury held home-court advantage as they had a better regular season record (29–5) than the Sky (15–19). The Mercury swept the Sky in three games to win their third title in franchise history.
mgt: The 2014 WNBA Finals was a best-of-five championship series between the Phoenix Mercury and the Chicago Sky, held from September 7 to 12, 2014. The Phoenix Mercury, led by Finals MVP Diana Taurasi, defeated the Sky in a three-game sweep to claim their third WNBA title. The Mercury entered the Finals with the league's best regular-season record (29–5), while the Sky, finishing fourth in the Eastern Conference (15–19), reached the Finals for the first time in franchise history.

trgt: Henry I of Cyprus, nicknamed the Fat (Lang-fr; 3 May 1217 – 18 January 1253 at Nicosia) was King of Cyprus from 1218 to 1253. He was the son of Hugh I of Cyprus and Alice of Champagne. When his father Hugh I died on January 10, 1218, the 8-month-old Henry became king. His mother was the official regent, but handed off the actual governing to her uncle, Philip of Ibelin. When Philip died, the effective regency passed to his brother, John of Ibelin, the Old Lord of Beirut.
