mgt: Henry I of Cyprus (3 May 1217 – 18 January 1253) was King of Cyprus from 1218 until his death. Crowned at the age of eight, his early reign was marked by political struggles, including the intervention of Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor, and the subsequent War of the Lombards. Henry assumed full control of the kingdom in 1232 and maintained strong ties with the Ibelin family. He also served as Regent of Jerusalem from 1246 to 1253. Henry was married three times and was succeeded by his son, Hugh II.

trgt:
Backes & Strauss is an English company that designs and produces high-end timepieces and jewellery. Founded in 1789 in Hanau, Germany, it is the oldest diamond company in the world.

The company's London office was established in 1814, and the company hallmark was registered at Goldsmith's Hall in London in 1878. In November 2006, Backes & Strauss entered into a strategic partnership with the Swiss watchmaker Franck Muller Group, launching its flagship collections of Regent, Berkeley and Piccadilly watches.
mgt: Backes & Strauss, founded in 1789 in Hanau, Germany, by Georg Carl Backes, is the world's oldest diamond company. Initially a goldsmithing business, it expanded under the leadership of Backes's son and later Max Strauss, becoming renowned for its diamond and jewelry craftsmanship. In 2006, the company partnered with Swiss watchmaker Franck Muller to produce luxury diamond-encrusted timepieces. Headquartered in London and Geneva, Backes & Strauss is known for its high-quality diamonds and signature "Jewel in the Crown" design in its watches.

trgt:
Acacia parramattensis, commonly known as Parramatta wattle, is a tree of the family Fabaceae native to the Blue Mountains and surrounding regions of New South Wales. It is a tall shrub or tree to about 15 m (49 ft) in height with phyllodes (flattened leaf stalks) instead of true leaves. These are finely divided bipinnate. The yellow flowers appear over summer. It generally grows in woodland or dry sclerophyll forest on alluvial or shale-based soils, generally with some clay content.

A fast-growing plant, it regenerates after bushfire by seed or suckering and can quickly colonise disturbed areas. Likewise it adapts readily to cultivation and is used in revegetation projects.
mgt: *Acacia parramattensis*, commonly known as the Parramatta wattle or Sydney green wattle, is a fast-growing shrub or tree native to central New South Wales, Australia. It typically grows 2 to 15 meters tall and features bipinnate leaves, yellow spherical flowers blooming from November to February, and flat, grey-black seed pods. Described in 1962 by botanist Mary Tindale, its name references Parramatta, near Sydney. Found in dry sclerophyll forests and woodlands, it thrives on various soil types and elevations up to 900 meters. Ecologically, it supports wildlife, including birds, caterpillars, and beetles, and regenerates after fire. It holds cultural significance in Dharawal stories and is used in revegetation projects.

trgt: Liparis, commonly known as widelip orchids, sphinx orchids or 羊耳蒜属 (yang er suan shu) is a cosmopolitan genus of more than 350 species of orchids in the family Orchidaceae. Plants in this genus are terrestrial, lithophytic or epiphytic herbs with a wide range of forms. The flowers are usually resupinate and small to medium sized, yellow, yellow-green or purplish with spreading sepals and petals. The labellum is usually larger than the sepals and petals and is lobed, sometimes with a toothed or wavy margin and one or two calli at its base.
mgt: *Liparis* is a genus of orchids in the family Orchidaceae, comprising terrestrial, lithophytic, or epiphytic herbs with small to medium-sized flowers. The flowers, often dull yellow, yellow-green, or purplish, are resupinate and may emit an unpleasant odor. The genus is characterized by its distinctive labellum, which is larger than the sepals and petals and often lobed with a toothed or wavy edge. First described in 1817 by Louis Claude Richard, *Liparis* is named after the Greek word *liparos*, meaning "oily" or "shiny," referring to its smooth leaves. The genus is distributed worldwide, except in Antarctica, with notable diversity in tropical Asia, the Americas, and Africa.

trgt:
The IRT Third Avenue Line, commonly known as the Third Avenue Elevated, Third Avenue El, or Bronx El, was an elevated railway in Manhattan and the Bronx, New York City. Originally operated by the New York Elevated Railway, an independent railway company, it was acquired by the Interborough Rapid Transit Company (IRT) and eventually became part of the New York City Subway system.

The first segment of the line, with service at most stations, opened from South Ferry to Grand Central Depot on August 26, 1878. Service was extended to Harlem in Manhattan on December 30. In 1881 this line already began the 24/7 service. Service in Manhattan was phased out in the early 1950s and closed completely on May 12, 1955. The remaining service in the Bronx was designated as part of the 8 route until it was discontinued on April 29, 1973.

The Third Avenue El was the last elevated line to operate in Manhattan, other than the 1 train on the IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line (which has elevated sections between 122nd and 135th Streets and north of Dyckman Street), and was a frequent backdrop for movies. Service on the Second, Sixth and Ninth Avenue elevated lines were terminated in 1942, 1938, and 1940, respectively.
mgt:
The IRT Third Avenue Line, commonly known as the Third Avenue El, was an elevated rapid transit line in New York City, operated by the Interborough Rapid Transit Company (IRT). Opened in 1878, it initially ran from South Ferry in Manhattan to 129th Street and was later extended into the Bronx. The line was electrified and expanded under the Dual Contracts, with a third track added to allow express service. It served as a vital transit route but faced criticism for its noise and perceived obsolescence as subway lines were developed.

The line was gradually closed between 1950 and 1973, with the Manhattan section ceasing operations in 1955 and the Bronx section closing in 1973. Its removal spurred real estate development in Manhattan but contributed to economic decline in parts of the Bronx. The line was replaced by bus services, including the Bx55, which offered limited-stop service along the former route. Plans to replace the line with the Second Avenue Subway were delayed due to financial constraints, leaving the East Side underserved for decades. The Third Avenue El remains a significant part of New York City's transit history, symbolizing the transition from elevated railways to subways and buses.

trgt:
Eclecticism is a conceptual approach that does not hold rigidly to a single paradigm or set of assumptions, but instead draws upon multiple theories, styles, or ideas to gain complementary insights into a subject, or applies different theories in particular cases. However, this is often without conventions or rules dictating how or which theories were combined.

It can sometimes seem inelegant or lacking in simplicity, and eclectics are sometimes criticized for lack of consistency in their thinking. It is, however, common in many fields of study. For example, most psychologists accept certain aspects of behaviorism, but do not attempt to use the theory to explain all aspects of human behavior.

Eclecticism in ethics, philosophy, politics and religion is also known as syncretism.
mgt:
Eclecticism is a philosophical and artistic approach that involves selecting and combining elements from various systems, styles, or ideas to create a cohesive whole. Originating in ancient Greek and Roman philosophy, it was practiced by figures such as Cicero, Antiochus of Ascalon, and the Stoics, who synthesized doctrines from multiple schools of thought. The term derives from the Greek word *eklektikos*, meaning "choosing the best." 

In architecture and art, eclecticism refers to the blending of historical styles, particularly prominent in 19th-century design. It also appears in fields like martial arts, philology, psychology, and modern philosophy, where it emphasizes adaptability and integration. Notably, eclecticism has been both praised for its inclusivity and criticized for lacking originality, reflecting its complex role across disciplines.

trgt: The Taguig Integrated Terminal Exchange (TITX), (IPA-tl) originally conceptualized as the South Integrated Transport System (ITS-South), is a proposed intermodal transport terminal in Arca South, Taguig. It is primarily a bus terminal for buses going to and from southern Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao but connected to the FTI station and the under-construction Metro Manila Subway. Originally scheduled for completion in 2020, as of July 2022, construction has yet to begin as pre-construction activities were temporarily suspended due to the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic.
mgt: The Taguig Integrated Terminal Exchange (TITX) is a planned intermodal transport terminal located in Arca South, Western Bicutan, Taguig, Philippines. Owned by Arca South Integrated Terminal, Inc., the project was awarded to Ayala Land in November 2015 through a public-private partnership. Construction began with a groundbreaking ceremony in January 2018, with an estimated cost of ₱5.20 billion. Initially expected to open in 2020, the terminal's completion was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic. It is projected to accommodate 160,000 passengers daily.

trgt: Hazel Susan Hawke, AO (née Masterson, 20 July 1929 – 23 May 2013) was the first wife of Bob Hawke, the 23rd Prime Minister of Australia. She married him in 1956, and supported him throughout his prime ministership (1983–1991); they divorced in 1995. She worked in social policy areas, and was an amateur pianist and a patron of the arts. After she was diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease, she made public appearances in order to raise awareness of the disease.
mgt: Hazel Susan Hawke AO (20 July 1929 – 23 May 2013) was an Australian advocate, author, and pianist, best known as the wife of Bob Hawke, the 23rd Prime Minister of Australia, serving as Spouse of the Prime Minister from 1983 to 1991. A pro-choice advocate, she drew on her personal experiences to support social causes and wrote several books. Later in life, she became a prominent public figure for Alzheimer's awareness after her diagnosis, establishing a dementia care fund and raising public understanding of the disease.

trgt:
The Redan Football Netball Club (nicknamed the Lions) is an Australian rules football and netball club located in the city of Ballarat, Victoria. The football team currently competes in the Ballarat Football League. Redan has a history of 59 Premierships including 11 Senior Football Premierships and was named the Victorian Country Football League Most Disciplined Club in 2004 & 2005.

Redan players have also won the Henderson Medal (Ballarat Football League's best player award) on 14 occasions with two players, Michael Smith (1994, 1994) and Jarrod Edwards (2006, 2007, 2008 and 2009) winning the award in consecutive years. Edwards is the first player in BFL history to win four consecutive Henderson Medals.

The club also has four winners of the Tony Lockett Medal, for the leading goalscorer in the Ballarat Football League. Redan currently play their home matches at City Oval in Ballarat and play in a predominantly maroon strip with a maroon top band and a lion logo on the chest.

The first Redan game was reported in The Ballarat Star Saturday September 2, 1871 against the Ballarat Football Club. The Redan team was made up from miners from the Band of Hope mine and their average weight was 14 Stone (90 kg). The Star reported that the Redan team were everywhere, kicking and tripping regardless of the consequences.

Former Adelaide Crows coach, the late Phil Walsh, played for Redan prior to be being recruited by Collingwood.
mgt:
The Redan Football Netball Club, nicknamed the Lions, is an Australian rules football and netball club based in Ballarat, Victoria. Established in 1871, it is one of the oldest football clubs in the region and competes in the Ballarat Football League (BFL). The club has a rich history of success, having won 11 senior football premierships, with notable victories in 1946, 1952, and a dominant period in the 2000s, including titles in 2002, 2003, 2006, 2007, 2009, and 2011. The Lions play their senior matches at City Oval, while junior and female football games are held at Western Oval.

Redan has also produced several individual award winners, including multiple Henderson Medal recipients, such as Jarrod Edwards, who won the medal four times between 2006 and 2009, and Nathan Horbury in 2017. The club has also celebrated Tony Lockett Medal winners, with W. Wells achieving the feat in 1952 and 1953, and other players like D. Atkinson and G. Cahir earning the honor in later years. Known for its strong community presence and competitive spirit, Redan continues to be a prominent club in the Ballarat region.

trgt:
At 150.4 miles (242.0 km), Lechuguilla Cave is the eighth-longest explored cave in the world and the second deepest (1,604 feet or 489 meters) in the continental United States. It is most famous for its unusual geology, rare formations, and pristine condition.

The cave is named for the canyon through which it is entered, which is named for Agave lechuguilla, a species of plant found there. Lechuguilla is in Carlsbad Caverns National Park, New Mexico. Access to the cave is limited to approved scientific researchers, survey and exploration teams, and National Park Service management-related trips.
mgt: Lechuguilla Cave, located in Eddy County, New Mexico, is one of the world's most significant caves, known for its extreme depth, length, and unique geological features. Discovered in 1986 after cavers broke through rubble, it has since been mapped to over 138 miles (222 km) of passages, making it the eighth-longest cave in the world and the deepest in the continental United States until 2014. The cave is renowned for its rare speleothems, including gypsum chandeliers and sulfur deposits, and its unique sulfuric acid-based formation. It also hosts rare bacteria with potential medicinal properties.

trgt:
New Zealand has 1157 described spider species, with an estimated total fauna of 2000 species. Over 97 per cent are endemic, and the rest have been introduced through human activities or were natural wind-borne introductions.

The New Zealand spider with the largest leg span is the Nelson cave spider (Spelungula cavernicola), with a leg span of up to 13 centimetres (5.1 in) and a 3 centimetres (1.2 in) body length.

The Australian white-tailed spider, first recorded in New Zealand in 1886, has been falsely attributed as the cause of many necrotising spider bites.

The flat huntsman spider (Delena cancerides), also from Australia, and called the Avondale spider in New Zealand, was accidentally introduced in the early 1920s, possibly in shipments of hardwood logs used for railway sleepers. The huntsman spiders, which are considered harmless to humans, have been collected for use in at least two films.

Very few New Zealand spiders have bites that can cause significant injury to humans, and of these, only one – the katipō – is endemic. Katipō bites have been known to cause systemic effects, such as hypertension, seizure, or coma, though no deaths as a result of katipō bites have been recorded for over 200 years. Its more dangerous close relative, the venomous Australian redback spider, has established a foothold in some parts of New Zealand, notably in Taranaki and Central Otago.
mgt: The spiders of New Zealand include a diverse range of species, with notable examples being the fishing spiders of the genus *Dolomedes*. These spiders have adapted to hunt on the surface of water and are represented by four species in New Zealand. Three of these species are widespread across the two main islands. *Dolomedes aquaticus* inhabits open, unforested riverbanks and shelters under rocks near rivers, while *Dolomedes dondalei* is found along forested riverbanks. The most common species, *Dolomedes minor*, also known as the nursery web spider, is not restricted to rivers and constructs white nursery webs on shrubs, though it retains its fishing abilities. The largest fishing spider, *Dolomedes schauinslandi*, is found exclusively on South East and Mangere islands in the Chatham Islands. These spiders exemplify the unique adaptations and ecological niches occupied by New Zealand's arachnid fauna.

trgt:
William J. Maddox Jr (May 22, 1921 – January 5, 2001) was a United States Army Aviator and a major general in the United States Army. During his career he amassed over 10,500 flying hours, almost 4,000 of those in combat over Korea, Vietnam and Cambodia.

He served three tours in Vietnam, the last as the commander of an armored division. In 1976, he commanded the United States Army Aviation Center. Maddox was instrumental in identifying the need for air mobility, developing a management structure that ensured the Army met its aviation objectives. Maddox is one of army's most highly decorated aviators.
mgt: William J. Maddox Jr. (May 22, 1921 – January 5, 2001) was a highly decorated United States Army major general and aviator who served from 1943 to 1976. A veteran of World War II, the Korean War, and the Vietnam War, he held numerous command positions, including leadership of the United States Army Aviation School and the 164th Aviation Group. Known as the "Grey Ghost," Maddox earned numerous awards, including the Army Distinguished Service Medal, four Silver Stars, and eight Distinguished Flying Crosses. After retiring, he advised King Hussein of Jordan and held leadership roles in aviation organizations in the Middle East and Asia.

trgt:
Melanie Susan Oxley is an Australian musician, singer-songwriter and primary school teacher. Oxley was a member of the dance pop group, The Sparklers (from 1985 to 1989), which in October 1988 released their debut album, Persuasion.

Since 1989 Oxley teamed with keyboardist, Chris Abrahams to perform and record as a soul pop duo, Melanie Oxley & Chris Abrahams. They have released four studio albums, Welcome to Violet, Coal, Jerusalem Bay and Blood Oranges. At the ARIA Music Awards of 1993 Welcome to Violet was nominated for ARIA Award for Best Independent Release.
mgt: Melanie Susan Oxley is an Australian musician, singer-songwriter, and primary school teacher, known for her work in rock, dance pop, and soul pop genres. She began her music career in the 1980s, performing with bands such as Paris Green and The Sparklers, before forming a long-standing duo with Chris Abrahams in 1989. Together, they released several critically acclaimed albums, including *Welcome to Violet* (1992), which was nominated for Best Independent Release at the ARIA Music Awards. Outside of music, Oxley is a primary school teacher and mother of three, residing in Sydney, Australia.

trgt:
Mongolia competed at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, Greece, from 13 to 29 August 2004. This was the nation's tenth appearance at the Olympics, except the 1984 Summer Olympics in Los Angeles, because of its partial support to the Soviet boycott.

The Mongolian National Olympic Committee sent a total of 20 athletes, 13 men and 7 women, to compete in 7 different sports, tying its delegation count with Sydney four years earlier. In the wake of Munkhbayar Dorjsuren's transfer to the German team, three Mongolian athletes had previously competed in Sydney, including shooter Otryadyn Gündegmaa in the women's pistol events. Judoka and world champion Damdinsürengiin Nyamkhüü was appointed by the committee to become Mongolia's flag bearer in the opening ceremony, dressed in a traditional costume.

After failing to achieve a single Olympic medal from Sydney, Mongolia left Athens with only a bronze from twenty-year-old judoka Khashbaataryn Tsagaanbaatar on the first day of the Games.
mgt:
Mongolia competed at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, Greece, from August 13 to 29, with a delegation of 20 athletes participating in seven sports. The team was led by flag bearer Damdinsürengiin Nyamkhüü. Mongolia won one bronze medal, placing 71st in the overall medal rankings. 

The nation’s sole medal was earned in judo by Khashbaataryn Tsagaanbaatar, who competed in the lightweight division. Tsagaanbaatar advanced to the semifinals but lost to his Japanese opponent in under thirty seconds. In the bronze medal match, he secured victory during an additional period, marking Mongolia's only podium finish at the Games. 

Mongolia also competed in athletics, boxing, shooting, and weightlifting, with judo being the largest contingent, featuring eight athletes. The 2004 Games continued Mongolia's consistent participation in the Summer Olympics since its debut in 1964. The Mongolian National Olympic Committee oversaw the team’s participation.

trgt:
Candyman 3: Day of the Dead (sometimes titled just Candyman: Day of the Dead) is a 1999 American supernatural slasher film directed by Turi Meyer and starring Tony Todd and Donna D'Errico. It is the third installment in the Candyman series, and a direct sequel to the 1995 film Candyman: Farewell to the Flesh. Its plot follows Caroline, the daughter of Annie Tarrant and a descendant of the Candyman, the powerful spirit of the murdered son of a slave who kills those who invoked him, who finds herself targeted by him on the eve of the Day of the Dead.

Unlike the first two installments, the film did not get a theatrical release and was instead released direct-to-video on July 9, 1999. It was followed by a fourth film in 2021, titled Candyman, a direct sequel to the original 1992 film of the same name which returns the storyline to Chicago, Illinois.
mgt:
*Candyman 3: Day of the Dead* is a 1999 American supernatural slasher film directed by Turi Meyer and written by Meyer and Al Septien. It is the third installment in the *Candyman* film series, based on characters created by Clive Barker. The film stars Tony Todd as the titular Candyman, alongside Donna D'Errico, Jsu Garcia, and Wade Williams. Set in Los Angeles during the Day of the Dead celebrations, the story follows Caroline McKeever, a descendant of the Candyman, as she confronts the vengeful spirit and attempts to end his reign of terror by destroying his myth.

The film was shot in Boyle Heights, Los Angeles, on a budget of $3 million. It received overwhelmingly negative reviews, holding a 7% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes. Tony Todd later expressed disappointment with the film. Plans for a fourth installment were canceled, but the franchise was revived with a 2021 sequel produced by Jordan Peele.

page_title: Abd al-Malik ibn Umar ibn Marwan
trgt: Abd al-Malik ibn Umar ibn Marwan ibn al-Hakam (Lang-ar; c. 718– c. 778), also known as al-Marwani, was an Umayyad prince, general and governor of Seville under the first Umayyad emir of al-Andalus (Islamic Spain), Abd al-Rahman I (r. 756–788). He led two major campaigns in 758 and 774, the first against the previous ruler of al-Andalus Yusuf ibn Abd al-Rahman al-Fihri and the second against the rebellious troops of Seville and Beja. His victories solidified the Umayyad emirate's control of western al-Andalus. His descendants continued to play important political and military roles in the Emirate well into the 10th century.
mgt: Abd al-Malik ibn Umar ibn Marwan (c. 718–c. 778) was an Umayyad noble and general who played a pivotal role in establishing the Umayyad Emirate in al-Andalus (Islamic Spain). A grandson of Caliph Marwan I, he fled to al-Andalus following the Abbasid overthrow of the Umayyad Caliphate in 750. Abd al-Malik became a trusted advisor and general under Emir Abd al-Rahman I, leading key military campaigns to consolidate Umayyad control over rebellious factions. His victories, including the suppression of a major revolt in 774, secured western al-Andalus for the emirate. His descendants held prominent positions in the region for generations.

trgt:
En Avant Guingamp (Lang-br, Lang-en), commonly referred to as EA Guingamp, EAG, or simply Guingamp (IPA-fr), is a professional football club based in the commune of Guingamp in France's Brittany region. The club was founded in 1912 and play in Ligue 2, the second tier of French football. The club has appeared in the Ligue 1, the top flight of French football, for 13 seasons, and is known for its relative success given Guingamp's small population of only 7,000 people.

Guingamp are one of only two clubs who have won the Coupe de France while not being in the first division, doing so in 2009, by defeating Rennes, 2–1. They won the same competition in 2014, again with a victory against Rennes, 2–0.
mgt: En Avant Guingamp, commonly referred to as Guingamp or EAG, is a French professional football club based in Guingamp, Brittany. Founded in 1912, the club plays its home matches at the Stade de Roudourou, which has a capacity of 19,033, significantly larger than the commune's population. Currently competing in Ligue 2, Guingamp has a storied history, including three promotions to Ligue 1, with their longest top-flight stint lasting from 2013 to 2019. The club's major achievements include winning the Coupe de France twice, in 2009 and 2014, and the 1996 UEFA Intertoto Cup. Known for developing notable players like Didier Drogba and Florent Malouda, Guingamp also fields a women's team in Division 1 Féminine.

trgt:
Richard Franz Stöhr (11 June 1874 – 11 December 1967) was an Austrian composer, music author and teacher.

Born in Vienna, Stöhr studied composition with Robert Fuchs at the Vienna Conservatory. After working there as a repetiteur and choral instructor from 1900, he taught music (theory of harmony, counterpoint, form) from 1903 to 1938, being professor from 1915. Among his students were Herbert von Karajan, Rudolf Serkin, Erich Leinsdorf, Samuel Barber, Erich Zeisl, Louis Horst, Marlene Dietrich, Alois Hába, Hellmut Federhofer, and Mimi Wagensonner. Fired from the Vienna Conservatory due to his Jewish heritage in 1938, he emigrated to the US in 1939 and taught at the Curtis Institute of Music in Philadelphia. His students there included Leonard Bernstein and Eugene Bossart. From 1941 to 1950 he taught at St. Michael's College in Vermont, where he maintained emeritus status until 1960. He died in Montpelier, Vermont in the United States.
mgt:
Richard Stöhr (1874–1967) was an Austrian-American composer, music theorist, and educator. Born in Vienna to a Jewish family, he initially pursued medicine before studying composition under Robert Fuchs at the Vienna Academy of Music. Stöhr became a prominent professor of music theory at the Academy, publishing influential textbooks and composing extensively in the tonal tradition of the 19th century. His works included symphonies, chamber music, choral pieces, and operas, which were widely performed in Europe before his exile.

Following the Nazi annexation of Austria in 1938, Stöhr was dismissed from his position due to his Jewish heritage and emigrated to the United States in 1939. He taught at the Curtis Institute of Music and later at Saint Michael's College in Vermont, where he continued composing. Despite his prolific output, his later works remained unpublished. Stöhr's legacy includes his extensive diary, preserved compositions, and recordings of his music, which have seen renewed interest in recent years.

trgt:
Latino literature is literature written by people of Latin American ancestry, often but not always in English, most notably by Mexican Americans, Puerto Ricans, Cuban Americans, and Dominican Americans, many of whom were born in the United States.

Notable writers include: Elizabeth Acevedo, Julia Alvarez, Gloria Anzaldua, Rudolfo Anaya, Giannina Braschi, Julia de Burgos, Ana Castillo, Sandra Cisneros, Junot Diaz, Cristina García, Oscar Hijuelos, Judith Ortiz Cofer, Piri Thomas, Rudy Ruiz, Denise Chavez, Cherrie Moraga, Kathleen Alcalá, Carmen Maria Machado, and Edmundo Paz Soldan, among others.
mgt: Latino literature encompasses the diverse literary contributions of Latino authors in the United States, reflecting the experiences, identities, and struggles of Latino communities. Emerging alongside the Civil Rights Movement and ethnic pride movements of the late 20th century, it gained prominence through the establishment of Latino Studies programs in American universities. Latino literature addresses themes such as immigration, identity, social justice, and cultural duality, with notable contributions from Chicano, Puerto Rican, Dominican-American, Cuban, and Central American authors. It also includes growing genres like speculative fiction, fantasy, and coming-of-age narratives, expanding the scope of American literature.

trgt:
Capsule hotel (Lang-ja), also known in the Western world as a pod hotel, is a type of hotel developed in Japan that features many small bed-sized rooms known as capsules. Capsule hotels provide cheap, basic overnight accommodation for guests who do not require or who cannot afford larger, more expensive rooms offered by more conventional hotels.

The first capsule hotel in the world opened in 1979 and was the Capsule Inn Osaka, located in the Umeda district of Osaka, Japan and designed by Kisho Kurokawa. From there, it spread to other cities within Japan. Since then, the concept has further spread to various other territories, including Belgium, China, Hong Kong, Iceland, India, Indonesia, Poland and Canada.
mgt: A capsule hotel is a type of affordable, minimalist accommodation featuring small, enclosed sleeping spaces called capsules, which are roughly the size of a single bed. These capsules, often made of fiberglass or plastic, are stacked in rows and include basic amenities such as a small television, air conditioning, and power outlets. Shared facilities, including bathrooms, lounges, and dining areas, are common, and some hotels offer additional features like communal baths, saunas, and vending machines. Capsule hotels, which vary in size and often cater primarily to men, are popular in Japan for their low cost and convenience, particularly among salarymen and travelers. However, they have been criticized for their cramped spaces and lack of privacy.

trgt: Euprymna tasmanica, also known as the southern dumpling squid or southern bobtail squid, is a bobtail squid that lives in the shallow (0.5 m to at least 80 m) temperate coastal waters of southern Australia's continental shelf. It lives for between 5 and 8 months and the adults can grow up to 6 or 7 cm long with a mantle length of 3 to 4 cm. They are found in seagrass beds or areas with soft silty or muddy bottoms from Brisbane on the east coast to Shark Bay on the west, as well as around Tasmania. Southern dumpling squid are nocturnal and during the day hide in sand or mud covered in a mucus-lined coat of sediment. If disturbed acid glans can quickly remove this coat as an additional decoy to ink squirting.
mgt:
*Euprymna tasmanica*, commonly known as the southern dumpling squid or southern bobtail squid, is a species of bobtail squid in the family Sepiolidae. Native to Australian waters, it is characterized by its iridescent green or yellow coloration with brown spots, large semi-circular fins, and a butterfly-shaped light organ in its mantle cavity, which uses bioluminescent bacteria to counteract its silhouette. Its arms and tentacles are equipped with toothed suckers.

Reproduction involves females laying multiple batches of eggs, with hatchlings emerging highly developed but lacking the bacteria needed for their light organ, which they acquire from the water. Adults die shortly after reproduction. The species is listed as Data Deficient by the IUCN, and its common name is sometimes confused with a smaller, unnamed Australian bobtail squid species.

trgt:
Clidastes is an extinct genus of marine lizard belonging to the mosasaur family. It is classified as part of the Mosasaurinae subfamily, alongside genera like Mosasaurus and Prognathodon. Clidastes is known from deposits ranging in age from the Coniacian to the early Campanian in the United States.

Clidastes means "locked vertebrae", which originates from the Greek noun κλειδί, or kleid meaning key (akin to Latin claudere meaning to shut). This refers to how the vertebral processes allow the proximal heads of the vertebrae to interlock for stability and strength during swimming.

It was one of the earliest hydropedal mosasaurs, representing one of the first properly marine predatory forms alongside other early hydropedal genera like Tylosaurus and Platecarpus. It was likely an agile swimmer that preyed upon cephalopods, fish and other small vertebrates in shallow water. Isotopic analysis on teeth specimens has suggested that this genus and Platecarpus may have entered freshwater occasionally, just like modern sea snakes.
mgt:
*Clidastes* is an extinct genus of small mosasaurs that lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 85 to 80 million years ago. Belonging to the subfamily Mosasaurinae, *Clidastes* was one of the smallest mosasaurs, typically measuring 2–4 meters (6.6–13.1 feet) in length, with some specimens reaching up to 6.2 meters (20 feet). Known for its streamlined body and interlocking vertebrae, *Clidastes* was a highly agile swimmer adapted for hunting fast-moving prey in shallow marine environments. Its teeth and tooth replacement system were similar to other mosasaurs, featuring thecodont implantation and specialized resorption pits.

The genus was first described by Edward Drinker Cope in 1868, with *Clidastes propython* later designated as the type species. Fossils of *Clidastes* have been found primarily in the United States, with additional remains reported from Sweden and other regions. *Clidastes* is considered one of the most basal hydropedal mosasaurines, bridging the evolutionary gap between earlier and more derived mosasaurs.

trgt:
Echo Lawn Estate, also known as Stonegate after the Great War, is a historic estate located at Balmville in Orange County, New York. The main house was built about 1860 and is a two-story brick dwelling in the Second Empire style. It features sweeping concave mansard-type roofs. Also on the property is a cluster of mid-19th-century service buildings, an early 20th-century formal garden, and a substantial set of Arts and Crafts inspired gateposts and stone walls.

It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2009.
mgt: Echo Lawn Estate is a historic property located in Balmville, New York, notable for its mid-19th-century Hudson River Valley estate design and Second Empire architectural style. Built around 1848, the estate features a brick mansion with a mansard roof, complemented by Picturesque-style service buildings, including a carriage barn, gatehouse, stable, and ice house. The property also includes restored gardens, a Japanese pond, and Arts & Crafts-inspired gates. Once home to notable families like the Knowltons and Beals, it remains a significant example of 19th-century estate design.

page_title: The ships Anne and Little James
trgt:
In 1623 the ships Anne and Little James were the third and fourth ships financed by the London-based Merchant Adventurers to come out together in support of Plymouth Colony, as were Mayflower in 1620 and Fortune in 1621. Anne carried mostly passengers, and the much smaller Little James carried primarily cargo, albeit with a few passengers. After a stormy three-month voyage from London, Anne arrived at New Plymouth in early July 1623, with Little James a week or so later.

Between them the ships brought 90-odd new settlers along with about thirty others who were not part of the core emigrant group. Many of this emigrant contingent would serve the colony well in the coming years, while others would be judged unfit for the hardships of colony life and be sent back to England.
mgt: The *Anne* and *Little James* were two ships that arrived at Plymouth Colony in 1623, bringing new settlers and supplies. The *Anne*, a 140-ton supply ship captained by William Peirce, carried the majority of the passengers and arrived on July 10, 1623. The *Little James*, a 44-ton pinnace captained by Emmanuel Altham, arrived weeks later and was intended to remain in New England for trade and fishing. However, the *Little James* faced numerous challenges, including a mutinous crew, inadequate trade goods, a shipwreck, and eventual capture by Barbary pirates. Despite efforts to salvage and repair the ship, it failed to fulfill its intended purpose, contributing to the financial struggles of the colony. The combined passenger list of the two ships included approximately 60 settlers, though exact records remain unclear.

trgt:
Paleontology in New Hampshire refers to paleontological research occurring within or conducted by people from the U.S. state of New Hampshire. Fossils are very rare in New Hampshire because so much of the state's geology is highly metamorphic. The state's complicated geologic history has made dating its rocks and the fossils they contain "a difficult task." The state's Devonian rocks are especially metamorphosed, yet its Mississippian rocks formed too recently to have been subject to the same metamorphism. Nevertheless, despite the geologic complications some fossils have been discovered in the state.

During the early Paleozoic, New Hampshire was covered by a warm, shallow sea that would come to be inhabited by creatures like brachiopods, bryozoans, corals, crinoids, and trilobites. Geologic forces raised the local elevation during the Devonian, and local sediments were eroded away from the state rather than deposited, leading to a gap in the local rock record. Erosion continued through the Mesozoic and the early to mid Cenozoic, leaving few rocks of that age, and no fossils. By the Ice Age, the state was being worked over by glaciers and had similar wildlife to the modern Canadian Arctic.

New Hampshire fossils had attracted scientific attention by the late 1800s when C. H. Hitchcock reported local invertebrate remains preserved near Littleton. Other notable finds include fossils preserved in metamorphic rock that are still recognizable despite the extreme geologic forces exerted on these rocks.
mgt:
Paleontology in New Hampshire explores the state's limited but notable fossil record, which spans from the Silurian period to the Quaternary. Due to the predominance of igneous and heavily metamorphosed rocks, New Hampshire lacks fossils from the early Paleozoic and Mesozoic eras. However, Silurian fossils, including brachiopods, bryozoans, corals, crinoids, and trilobites, have been discovered in the Fitch Formation near Littleton and other sites along the Ammonoosuc River. Devonian fossils, such as brachiopods, crinoids, and gastropods, have been found at locations like Tip Top Farm and Mormon Hill.

The state's fossil record is sparse for the Carboniferous, Permian, and Mesozoic due to extensive erosion and geological uplift. During the Quaternary, glaciers covered much of New Hampshire, leaving behind plant fossils, pollen, and remains of megafauna like woolly mammoths and mastodons. Notable discoveries include a mastodon fossil near the Isles of Shoals in 2013 and a mammoth tooth in 2014.

The study of New Hampshire's paleontology began in 1873 with C. H. Hitchcock's report on Silurian fossils. Later contributions include F. H. Lahee's documentation of Devonian fossils in the early 20th century and the discovery of a unique brachiopod-rich horizon in the Clough Formation in 1958. Despite its limited fossil record, New Hampshire provides valuable insights into ancient marine and glacial environments.

trgt:
Fatima Bhutto (Lang-ur; Lang-sd, born 29 May 1982) is a Pakistani writer and columnist. Born in Kabul, she is the daughter of politician Murtaza Bhutto, sister of Zulfikar Ali Bhutto Jr, niece of former Pakistani Prime Minister Benazir Bhutto and granddaughter of former Prime Minister and President of Pakistan, Zulfiqar Ali Bhutto. She was raised in Syria and Karachi, and received her bachelor's degree from Barnard College, followed by a master's degree from the SOAS University of London.

Bhutto is a critic of her aunt Benazir Bhutto and her husband Asif Ali Zardari, whom she accused of being involved in her father's murder. Her non-fiction book, Songs Of Blood And Sword (2010), is about her family. Bhutto has written for The News and The Guardian among other publications.
mgt:
Fatima Bhutto (born May 29, 1982) is a Pakistani writer, columnist, and member of the prominent Bhutto political family. Born in Kabul, Afghanistan, to Murtaza Bhutto and Fauzia Fasihudin, she grew up stateless, moving frequently with her father after her parents' divorce. Bhutto is the granddaughter of former Pakistani Prime Minister Zulfikar Ali Bhutto and niece of Benazir Bhutto. She was educated at Barnard College, Columbia University, and SOAS, University of London, where she studied Middle Eastern and South Asian cultures.

Bhutto has published several works, including the memoir *Songs of Blood and Sword* (2010) and novels such as *The Shadow of the Crescent Moon* (2013) and *The Runaways* (2019). While politically active, she has chosen to focus on writing and activism rather than pursuing elected office, citing concerns about dynastic politics in Pakistan.

trgt:
Lu Rongjing (Chinese: 卢荣景; born August 1933) is a Chinese politician who served as governor of Anhui from 1988 to 1989, party secretary of Anhui from 1988 to 1996, and chairman of the Anhui Provincial Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference from 1996 to 2000.

He was a delegate to the 7th National People's Congress. He was a member of the Standing Committee of the 9th and 10th Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference. He was a member of the 13th, 14th and 15th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party.
mgt: Lu Rongjing (born August 1933) is a Chinese politician who held several prominent positions in Anhui province. He served as the Communist Party Secretary of Anhui, the province's top political position, from 1988 to 1996, and as Governor of Anhui from 1988 to 1989. He later became Chairman of the Anhui Provincial Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference from 1996 to 2000. A member of the Chinese Communist Party since 1954, Lu held various leadership roles throughout his career, including in industrial and economic administration, before rising to provincial leadership.

trgt: Uyirmei is a Tamil medical soap opera that aired on Zee Thamizh. The series is apparently inspired by Grey's Anatomy and House. The plot revolves around the lives of some doctors, their lives, families and patients. The show premiered on 18 August 2014.First the director was Bhushan but after a couple of episodes Sundhar.K.Vijayan started to direct. Starting from Monday 24 November 2014, the show was shifted to 7pm time Slot. Its episodes ended on 30 January 2015 with 112 episodes.
mgt: *Uyirmei* is a Tamil-language medical soap opera that aired on Zee Tamil from August 18, 2014, to January 30, 2015. Directed by Bhushan and later Sundar K. Vijayan, the series starred Amala, Praveen, and Vineeth in lead roles. The story follows Kavitha, who struggles to manage a hospital after the death of its founder, Dr. Dayanand, while facing opposition from his children. Produced by Global One Studios, the show featured a hospital set designed by Thota Tharani and ran for 122 episodes.

page_title: A Walk in the Sun (1945 film)
trgt:
A Walk in the Sun is a 1945 American war film based on the novel by Harry Brown, who was a writer for Yank, the Army Weekly based in England. The book was serialized in Liberty Magazine in October 1944.

The film was directed by Lewis Milestone, stars Dana Andrews and features Richard Conte, George Tyne, John Ireland, Lloyd Bridges, Sterling Holloway, Norman Lloyd, Herbert Rudley and Richard Benedict, with narration by Burgess Meredith.

In 2016, the film was deemed "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant" by the United States Library of Congress, and selected for preservation in its National Film Registry.
mgt: *A Walk in the Sun* is a 1945 American war film directed by Lewis Milestone and based on Harry Brown's 1944 novel of the same name. The film follows a platoon of American soldiers during the Allied invasion of Italy in World War II, focusing on their mission to destroy a bridge near a farmhouse while grappling with the psychological and physical toll of combat. Starring Dana Andrews, Richard Conte, and John Ireland, the film is notable for its realistic portrayal of soldiers and its use of ballads by Millard Lampell and Earl Robinson to narrate the story. It was critically acclaimed upon release.

trgt:
The mountain ground squirrel (Geosciurus princeps) is a rodent that is native to southwestern Angola, western Namibia, and western South Africa. It is also known as the Kaoko ground squirrel or the Damara ground squirrel.

It is the closest relative of the Cape ground squirrel (Latin name Geosciurus inauris), which is so similar in appearance that the two are difficult to distinguish in the field. Both species have long bushy black and white tails with a white stripe from the shoulder towards the rump. Geosciurus princeps is slightly larger, on average, than G. inauris, although there is considerable overlap in body size. Differences in skull morphology also distinguish the two species, and the incisors are yellow to orange rather than white as in G. inauris.
mgt:
The mountain ground squirrel (*Geosciurus princeps*), also known as *Xerus princeps*, is a species of ground squirrel found in the arid regions of southwestern Africa, ranging from southern Angola to southern Namibia and the Richtersveld National Park in South Africa. It is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN. 

This large-bodied squirrel measures 23–29 cm (9.1–11.4 in) in body length, with a tail of 21–28 cm (8.3–11.0 in), and weighs 490–710 grams (1.1–1.6 lb). Its pale cinnamon-brown fur is marked by a white belly, facial markings, and a stripe from shoulders to hips. Mountain ground squirrels are diurnal and primarily solitary, with females sometimes forming small family groups. They inhabit burrows in sparsely vegetated areas and forage up to 1 km (0.6 mi) from their burrows.

trgt:
Yaser Abdel Said (Lang-ar; born January 27, 1957) is an Egyptian-American former taxi driver and convicted murderer. For 12 years, Said evaded arrest for the January 1, 2008, fatal shootings of his two daughters, Amina (18) and Sarah (17). Their bodies were found in his abandoned taxi cab in Irving, Texas, on the property of the Omni Mandalay Hotel (now the Omni Las Colinas Hotel).

Said disappeared after the killings. He also remained a fugitive from law enforcement for 12 years, with six of those years being on the FBI Ten Most Wanted Fugitives List for the fatal shootings.

Said, who was very controlling over his family, felt that his daughters Amina and Sarah dishonored the family. On January 1, 2008, he lured them into his taxi cab on the pretense of taking them out to eat, where instead, he fatally shot them.

Said was captured on August 26, 2020 in the town of Justin, Texas. His son, Islam, and Said's brother, Yassein, were both arrested in Euless, Texas, for aiding a fugitive. Said was taken into federal custody by the FBI, and was then transferred to Dallas County where he was held until his trial, which commenced in August 2022. Said was found guilty on August 9, after a week-long trial.
mgt:
Yaser Abdel Said (born January 27, 1957) is an Egyptian-American former taxi driver convicted of the 2008 murders of his daughters, Amina and Sarah Said, in Irving, Texas. The murders, widely regarded as honor killings, were motivated by Said's anger over his daughters' perceived defiance of his strict control, including their relationships and independence. After the killings, Said evaded capture for 12 years, becoming one of the FBI's Ten Most Wanted Fugitives in 2014. He was apprehended in August 2020 in Justin, Texas, following years of investigation and assistance from his son, Islam Said, and brother, Yassein Said, who were later convicted of harboring a fugitive.

Said was convicted of capital murder in 2022 and sentenced to life imprisonment without parole. The case has drawn significant media attention and has been the subject of a 2014 documentary, *The Price of Honor*, and a 2015 episode of *The Hunt with John Walsh*. The murders and Said's subsequent flight have been cited as a high-profile example of honor-based violence in the United States. Said is currently incarcerated at the McConnell Unit in Texas, while his son and brother are serving federal prison sentences for their roles in aiding his evasion of justice.

trgt:
The Congo Pedicle (at one time referred to as the Zaire Pedicle; in French la botte du Katanga, meaning 'Katanga boot') is the southeast salient of the Katanga Province of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, which divides neighbouring Zambia into two lobes. In area, the pedicle is similar in size to Wales or New Jersey. 'Pedicle' is used in the sense of 'a little foot'. 'Congo Pedicle' or 'the Pedicle' is also used to refer to the Congo Pedicle road which crosses it.

The Congo Pedicle is an example of the arbitrary boundaries imposed by European powers on Africa in the wake of the Scramble for Africa, which were set by European interests and usually did not consider pre-existing political or tribal boundaries.
mgt:
The Congo Pedicle is a salient of the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) that extends into Zambia, separating Zambia's Luapula Province and part of the Northern Province from the Copperbelt, the country's industrial hub. Its origins date back to the late 19th century during the Scramble for Africa, when territorial disputes between the British South Africa Company and King Leopold II's Congo Free State were resolved through negotiations and arbitration. The Pedicle was formalized in the Anglo-Belgian agreements, with its borders finalized by an Anglo-Belgian Boundary Commission in 1914.

The Pedicle has posed strategic challenges for Zambia, particularly during the Congo Crisis (1960–63) and subsequent conflicts in Katanga. It complicates transportation and exposes Zambia to cross-border crime and instability. Despite its proximity to the Copperbelt, the Pedicle's impact on mineral resources has been minimal due to the natural division of ore deposits.

trgt:
Mark V. Campbell (born 1978) is a Canadian academic, disc jockey and writer. He was raised in Scarborough, a suburb of Toronto, Canada. Currently, he is an assistant professor and Associate Chair of the Department of Arts, Culture and Media, University of Toronto Scarborough. He is the founder of the Northside Hip Hop Archive, an online archive that digitizes oral histories, event flyers, posters and analog recordings that document the beginnings of Canadian hip hop. Campbell is the 2020-21 Jackman Humanities Institute UTSC Fellow and a Connaught Early Career Fellow at the University of Toronto. Campbell was formerly Director of FCAD Forum for Cultural Strategies and adjunct professor at the RTA School of Media, Ryerson University.

Campbell was a Banting postdoctoral fellow in the Department of Fine Arts at the University of Regina. He is a fellow and former postdoctoral fellow with the International Institute for Critical Studies in Improvisation. Campbell became a disc jockey in 1994 and co-hosted the Bigger than Hip-Hop Show on community radio from 1997 to 2015.

Campbell's work focuses on new modalities of being human, sonic innovations within Black music, and the knowledge production of digital archives.
mgt:
Mark V. Campbell (born 1978) is a Canadian musicologist and scholar specializing in Afrosonic innovation, Black Studies, and hip hop archives. He is the author of *Afrosonic Life* (2022), which examines how sonic innovations within the African diaspora articulate methodologies for navigating the afterlife of slavery. Campbell's academic work explores themes of relationality, notions of the human, and the cultural significance of sound.

In 2010, Campbell founded the Northside Hip Hop Archive, dedicated to preserving the history of Canada's hip hop community, particularly its emergence in the 1980s and 1990s. He has curated numerous exhibitions showcasing Canadian hip hop culture, including *Still Tho: Aesthetic Survival in Hip Hop's Visual Art* at the Canada Council for the Arts, *Everything Remains Raw: Photographing Toronto’s Hip Hop Culture from Analogue to Digital* at the McMichael Art Collection, and *For the Record: ‘An Idea of the North’* at the Toronto Reference Library. Other notable exhibitions include *Mixtapes: Hip Hop’s Lost Archive* and the *T-Dot Pioneers* series, which highlight the evolution and legacy of Toronto's hip hop scene. Campbell's work bridges academic inquiry and cultural preservation, emphasizing the importance of hip hop as a site of artistic and historical significance.

trgt:
The National Hockey Stadium was a sports stadium in Milton Keynes with a nominal capacity of around 4,000 seats: this was temporarily increased to 9,000 between 2003 and 2007. It was used by England Hockey as their national stadium from 1995 to 2003 and then as a professional football stadium by Wimbledon FC, rebranded as Milton Keynes Dons in 2004.

In summer 2007, the Dons relocated to the new Stadium MK, near Bletchley, leaving the hockey stadium without a tenant. It was unused for two years and, in late 2009, demolition began in preparation for the redevelopment of the site into the new Network Rail headquarters which became operational in July 2012.
mgt: The National Hockey Stadium, located in Milton Keynes, England, was a multi-purpose sports venue built in 1995. Originally designed as a national field hockey stadium with a synthetic pitch, it hosted national and international hockey events, including the 1997 FIH Men's Junior World Cup and the 2000 Olympic Women's Qualifying Tournament. In 2003, the stadium was leased to Wimbledon F.C., later rebranded as Milton Keynes Dons, who converted the pitch to grass and expanded its capacity to 9,000 for football matches. The Dons used the stadium until 2007, after which it was deemed unviable for hockey. Demolition began in December 2009, and the site was redeveloped as Network Rail's headquarters.

trgt:
Edward Walter "Ed" Jones (March 11, 1950 – December 14, 2012) was a North Carolina Democratic politician who represented the state's 4th Senate district (including Bertie, Chowan, Gates, Halifax, Hertford, Northampton, and Perquimans counties) in the North Carolina Senate.

Jones served in the U.S. Army's 82nd Airborne Division and graduated from Western Piedmont Community College with a degree in police science. He served for 30 years as a state trooper with the North Carolina Highway Patrol and after his retirement served as chief of police in Enfield, North Carolina.
mgt: Edward Walter Jones (March 11, 1950 – December 14, 2012) was an American politician and law enforcement officer who served as a Democratic member of the North Carolina General Assembly. He represented the 7th district in the North Carolina House of Representatives from 2005 to 2007 and the 4th district in the North Carolina Senate from 2007 until his death in 2012. Jones also served as the mayor of Enfield, North Carolina. A portion of US Highway 301 was renamed in his honor in 2018.

trgt:
Yana Peel (born June 1974) is a Canadian executive, businesswoman, children's author and philanthropist who is currently global head of arts and culture at Chanel. She was CEO of the Serpentine Galleries from 2016 to 2019, and was previously a board member.

Peel is a co-founder of the Outset Contemporary Art Fund (with Candida Gertler), and Intelligence Squared Asia, and was CEO of Intelligence Squared Group from 2013 to 2016.

Peel has several advisory positions including the Tate International Council, V-A-C Foundation, and the NSPCC therapeutic board. She has been an advisor to the British Fashion Council, Asia Art Archive, Lincoln Center, Para Site and the Victoria and Albert Museum, where she founded the design fund.
mgt: Yana Peel (born June 1974) is a Canadian businesswoman, philanthropist, and arts advocate, currently serving as the Global Head of Arts and Culture at Chanel. Born in Leningrad (now St. Petersburg), Russia, she emigrated to Canada in 1978 and later studied at McGill University and the London School of Economics. Peel began her career at Goldman Sachs before co-founding the Outset Contemporary Art Fund in 2003 and Intelligence Squared Asia in 2009. She served as CEO of the Serpentine Galleries from 2016 to 2019, where she advanced digital initiatives and global architectural projects. Peel is also active in philanthropy, supporting contemporary art, design, and child welfare initiatives.

trgt: Silent Sejm (Lang-pl; Lang-lt), also known as the Mute Sejm, is the name given to the session of the Sejm parliament of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth of 1 February 1717 held in Warsaw. A civil war in the Commonwealth was used by the Russian Tsar Peter the Great as an opportunity to intervene as a mediator. It marked the end of Augustus II of Poland's attempts to create an absolute monarchy in Poland, and the beginning of the Russian Empire's increasing influence and control over the Commonwealth.
mgt: The Silent Sejm, also known as the Muted or Dumb Sejm, was a session of the Polish parliament held on February 1, 1717, during the reign of Augustus II the Strong. It was convened to resolve the conflict between the king and the Polish nobility (szlachta) following the Tarnogród Confederation, a civil war influenced by Russian intervention. Under the watch of Russian troops, the Sejm lasted only six hours, with most deputies silenced. The settlement, largely dictated by Tsar Peter the Great, curtailed royal power, reduced the Commonwealth's military, and unofficially established Russia's dominance over Polish affairs. It marked the end of Augustus II's absolutist ambitions and is seen as a precursor to the partitions of Poland.

trgt:
Yossi Gross (Lang-he; born February 5, 1947) is an Israeli engineer, inventor and entrepreneur. He is a founding partner of Rainbow Medical, an operational investment company, established to launch companies based on the technological ideas and inventions of Gross. Yossi Gross first started his professional career in the 20th century as a project manager of the Lavi (IAI Lavi) program for the Israel Air Force. From the 1990s through to the beginning of the 21st century, Gross started 27 medical device companies based on his various inventions in electronics, signal processing, nanotechnology, drug delivery and neurostimulation. Gross's various companies have developed or are currently developing treatments for diabetes, gastroenterology, stroke, ophthalmology, asthma, congestive heart failure, and urology. Gross has 567 filed patents.

Gross is a conference speaker. He spoke at the American Israel Chamber meeting on the topic: Accelerating Medical Device Start-ups the Israeli Way and at the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania
mgt:
Yossi Gross (Hebrew: יוסי גרוס; born February 5, 1947) is an Israeli entrepreneur, inventor, and engineer known for his contributions to the medical device industry. Born in Budapest, Hungary, he immigrated to Israel at the age of three with his Holocaust-survivor parents. Gross earned an MSc in aeronautical engineering from the Technion – Israel Institute of Technology in 1976 and began his career as a flight test engineer at Israel Aircraft Industries, where he worked on the Lavi fighter jet project.

Gross transitioned to entrepreneurship in 1985, founding General Ideas and Products Ltd. and later focusing on medical devices. He has launched over 27 medical device companies, many based on his patents, and collaborated with major investors and corporations. In 2007, he co-founded Rainbow Medical, a venture fund and incubator supporting early-stage medical technologies. Gross is a member of the Israeli Life Science Association and continues to influence the medical innovation landscape.
